topics.title: Design Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Process (KTU 2024 Definition):** The *Design Process* is a structured, iterative, and multi-phase engineering methodology used to conceptualize, develop, prototype, validate, and refine a product, system, or process — with **safety considerations explicitly integrated at every phase of the lifecycle** rather than being retrofitted at the end.

In the context of **PESFT412 – Design for Safety**, the design process is not merely a sequence of technical steps; it is a **safety-embedded decision-making framework** governed by the principle that *"prevention through design"* is more efficient, ethical, and economical than downstream safety retrofits.

### Key Characteristics of the Design Process

| # | Characteristic | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Iterative** | Cycles back through phases as new information emerges |
| 2 | **Hierarchical** | Moves from concept → detail with progressive refinement |
| 3 | **Multi-disciplinary** | Inputs from safety, ergonomics, reliability, manufacturing |
| 4 | **Risk-driven** | Each decision is evaluated against safety/risk criteria |
| 5 | **Documented** | Produces Design History File (DHF) for traceability |
| 6 | **Stakeholder-aware** | Considers users, operators, regulators, environment |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Kitchen Renovation"**
>
> Imagine you are **renovating a kitchen** for a family with elderly members and a toddler:
>
> - **Phase 1 (Concept):** You decide the broad idea — open layout, cooking island, accessible storage.
> - **Phase 2 (Preliminary Design):** You sketch layouts, choose gas-vs-induction, pick cabinet dimensions.
> - **Phase 3 (Detailed Design):** You finalize *which stove model*, *anti-slip tile grade*, *rounded counter edges*, *fire-suppression hood*, and *child-lock placements*.
> - **Phase 4 (Review/Validation):** You walk through the kitchen *pretending* to be the toddler, asking: *"Can I pull a pot off the stove? Is the stove knob within reach?"*
> - **Phase 5 (Iteration):** You go back, install a **gas-cutoff sensor** because you realized a leak could poison the family.
>
> This is exactly the **Design Process** in engineering. In *Design for Safety*, you are *deliberately* hunting for what could hurt, fail, or kill — *before* a single brick is laid.

## 1.3 Why "Design Process" is the First Topic in Module 1

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Design Process** is the *spine* of all safety engineering. Without a disciplined, documented process, safety cannot be:
> - **Planned** (you cannot schedule a safety review if you don't know the design phases),
> - **Traced** (you cannot prove due diligence),
> - **Verified** (you cannot test what was never specified),
> - **Improved** (you cannot learn what was never measured).

### Physical & Standard Constants Referenced

The following international/standard metrics are central to the Design Process:

- **ISO 12100:2010** — *Safety of machinery — General principles for design — Risk assessment and risk reduction*
- **IEC 61508** — *Functional Safety of E/E/PE Systems* (basis for **SIL 1–4**)
- **ISO 14971:2019** — *Application of risk management to medical devices*
- **ALARP** — *As Low As Reasonably Practicable* (UK HSE / global standard)
- **PFD** — *Probability of Failure on Demand* (target values: **10⁻¹ to 10⁻⁵**)
- **MTTF** — *Mean Time To Failure*
- **Risk Score = Severity × Probability × Exposure** (per ISO 12100)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Canonical Phases of the Design Process (with Safety Integration)

### Phase 1 — Concept / Planning Phase
**Goal:** Define the *problem*, identify *users*, *hazards*, and *constraints*. No physical design yet.

**Safety Activities:**
- Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)
- Stakeholder & use-context analysis
- Regulatory landscape mapping
- Definition of safety goals and target risk levels

**Output:** Concept Design Report, Preliminary Hazard List (PHL), Safety Plan.

### Phase 2 — Preliminary / Embodiment Design
**Goal:** Develop **alternative design concepts**, perform trade-offs, and select the safest feasible option.

**Safety Activities:**
- Concept Hazard Analysis (CHA)
- Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA) at component level
- Design for Inherently Safer Design (ISD) principles:
  - **Minimize** (use less hazardous material)
  - **Substitute** (replace hazardous with benign)
  - **Moderate** (use less hazardous conditions)
  - **Simplify** (fewer parts = fewer failures)

**Output:** Selected design concept, FMEA worksheet, risk register.

### Phase 3 — Detailed Design
**Goal:** Specify every component, dimension, material, software behavior, and tolerance.

**Safety Activities:**
- Detailed FMEA / DFMEA (Design FMEA)
- Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) for critical subsystems
- HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)
- Safety integrity level (SIL) allocation
- Verification of safety requirements (test plans drafted)

**Output:** Engineering drawings, software specifications, safety requirements specification (SRS), verification matrix.

### Phase 4 — Validation, Verification & Review
**Goal:** Prove by test/analysis/inspection that the **built artifact** meets the **safety requirements**.

**Safety Activities:**
- Design Verification (are we building the design right?)
- Design Validation (are we building the *right* design?)
- Safety review boards (SRR, PDR, CDR)
- Type testing, certification (CE, UL, BIS, etc.)
- Failure analysis of test failures

**Output:** V&V Reports, Test Certificates, Design History File (DHF).

### Phase 5 — Production, Operation & Continuous Improvement
**Goal:** Manufacture, deploy, monitor, and **learn** from field data.

**Safety Activities:**
- Process FMEA (PFMEA)
- Field monitoring & incident reporting
- Post-market surveillance
- Corrective and Preventive Actions (CAPA)
- Lessons-learned feedback to next-generation design

**Output:** Field data logs, CAPA database, design updates.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The five phases above are the *answer* to the most common 14-mark question — *"Explain the phases of the design process with safety integration."* Memorize the **safety activity in each phase**.

## 2.2 Design Process Models (Engineering Lifecycle Models)

Different industries use different lifecycle models. KTU expects awareness of the principal models:

| Model | Use Case | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Waterfall** | Simple, regulated (medical, defense) | Clear phase gates | Late discovery of defects |
| **V-Model** | Safety-critical (aerospace, nuclear) | Each design step has a corresponding test | Rigid, expensive |
| **Spiral** | Complex R&D projects | Built-in risk analysis at each loop | Costly documentation |
| **Agile** | Software / fast-iteration products | Flexible, fast feedback | Weak formal safety gating |
| **Stage-Gate** | New Product Development (NPD) | Clear go/kill decisions | Stage-gate may bypass safety if rushed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** The **V-Model** is the most-tested lifecycle model in safety engineering questions. Always draw it as a **V-shape** with the *left arm* = decomposition/specification and *right arm* = integration/verification.

## 2.3 Risk Assessment — The Heart of the Design Process

Safety in the design process is operationalized through **risk assessment**, executed *at every phase*.

### Core Risk Equation (ISO 12100 / ISO 14971)

$$
R = S \times P \times E
$$

Where:
- $R$ = **Risk** (qualitative score or numerical)
- $S$ = **Severity** of harm (typically 1–4 or 1–5)
- $P$ = **Probability of occurrence** of harm
- $E$ = **Exposure / frequency** of being in the hazard zone

If the design uses a 5-point scale for all three, $R$ ranges from **1 to 125**, and acceptance thresholds are predefined (e.g., $R \le 4$ is "acceptable," $4 < R \le 15$ is "ALARP," $R > 15$ is "unacceptable — must redesign").

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Range | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic Risk | $R = S \times P$ | Score 1–25 | Qualitative FMEA |
| 2 | Extended Risk (ISO 12100) | $R = S \times P \times E$ | Score 1–125 | Machinery safety |
| 3 | Risk Priority Number (FMEA) | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | 1–1000 | FMEA worksheets |
| 4 | Probability of Failure on Demand | $PFD_{avg}$ | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-5}$ | SIL determination |
| 5 | Safety Integrity Level (target) | $SIL = -\log_{10}(PFD)$ | 1, 2, 3, 4 | IEC 61508 |
| 6 | Failure Rate (λ) | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTTF}$ | per hour ($h^{-1}$) | Reliability |
| 7 | ALARP Boundary | $\text{Cost of further reduction} \gg \text{Residual risk reduction}$ | Cost-benefit ratio | UK HSE / global |
| 8 | Hazard Rate (Bathtub curve region 2) | $h(t) = \lambda \approx \text{constant}$ | per hour | Reliability engineering |
| 9 | Series Reliability | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | 0–1 | Redundancy analysis |
| 10 | Parallel Redundancy Reliability | $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | 0–1 | Safety systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on the Table:** I have used `\times` (LaTeX) instead of the vertical pipe symbol to avoid breaking markdown table syntax, and the $R$ values are written with LaTeX math wrappers. The $\vert$ and $\mid$ constructs are reserved for absolute-value expressions, e.g., $\vert R \vert$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Design Process is not academic — it is the **legal, ethical, and operational backbone** of safety-critical industries:

- **Aerospace (FAA / EASA):** The V-Model is mandated; missing a phase = airworthiness certificate denied.
- **Medical Devices (FDA / EU MDR):** The Design Controls of **21 CFR 820.30** and **ISO 13485** mirror the 5-phase structure.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262):** Hazard Analysis and Risk Assessment (HARA) drives **ASIL A–D** allocation — directly analogous to SIL.
- **Industrial Machinery (ISO 12100):** The 3-step method (determine limits → identify hazards → reduce risk) *is* the design process for safety.
- **Process Industry (IEC 61511):** The Safety Lifecycle has 12 stages, each mapped to a design phase.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Section Objective:** KTU frequently asks derivation-style questions such as *"Compute RPN from a given FMEA table"* or *"Determine SIL for a given PFD"*. This section walks through the **complete, exhaustive** solution to two representative problems — the most common 14-markers.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Risk Priority Number (RPN) Computation

### Problem Statement
A hydraulic press in a stamping line has the following FMEA ratings for the failure mode *"Sudden pressure spike → hose burst"*:

- Severity $S = 8$ (High — amputation possible)
- Occurrence $O = 5$ (Moderate — occurs ~1 in 2,000 operations)
- Detection $D = 6$ (Low — no pressure sensor at the operator station)

**Compute the Risk Priority Number (RPN) and classify the risk.**

### Step 1 — Recall the FMEA RPN definition

By definition in FMEA methodology (AIAG-VDA Handbook, 2019), the RPN is the product of three ordinal ratings:

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the given values

$$
RPN = 8 \times 5 \times 6
$$

### Step 3 — Perform the multiplication explicitly

$$
\begin{aligned}
8 \times 5 &= 40 \\
40 \times 6 &= 240
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{RPN = 240}
$$

### Step 4 — Classify the risk

Using a standard 1–10 ordinal scale for each parameter, the maximum RPN is $10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000$.

Typical risk classification bands (AIAG-VDA-aligned):

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN \le 40 &\Rightarrow \text{Low Risk — Monitor} \\
41 \le RPN \le 100 &\Rightarrow \text{Moderate Risk — Action Plan (Low Priority)} \\
101 \le RPN \le 300 &\Rightarrow \text{High Risk — Action Plan (High Priority)} \\
RPN > 300 &\Rightarrow \text{Critical Risk — Stop Shipment / Redesign}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $RPN = 240$ falls in the band $101$–$300$, the classification is:

$$
\boxed{\text{High Risk — High-Priority Action Plan Required}}
$$

### Step 5 — Recommended Action (design improvement)

A *Design for Safety* action would be to **install a redundant pressure sensor at the operator station**, raising $D$ from $6$ to $2$ (since now the failure is detected). The new RPN becomes:

$$
RPN_{new} = 8 \times 5 \times 2 = 80
$$

This drops the risk from "High" to "Moderate" — illustrating **how design changes quantitatively reduce risk**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — SIL Determination from Target PFD

### Problem Statement
A safety instrumented function (SIF) in a chemical reactor must achieve a **target average Probability of Failure on Demand** of $PFD_{avg} = 10^{-3}$.

**Determine the required Safety Integrity Level (SIL).**

### Step 1 — Recall the SIL–PFD mapping (IEC 61508)

The Safety Integrity Level is defined (for low-demand mode of operation) as:

$$
SIL = -\log_{10}\bigl(PFD_{avg}\bigr)
$$

The standard mapping table is:

| SIL | Target $PFD_{avg}$ | Range |
|---|---|---|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | $10^{-1} \ge PFD > 10^{-2}$ |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | $10^{-2} \ge PFD > 10^{-3}$ |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | $10^{-3} \ge PFD > 10^{-4}$ |
| SIL 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | $10^{-4} \ge PFD > 10^{-5}$ |

### Step 2 — Compute the logarithm

Given $PFD_{avg} = 10^{-3}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SIL &= -\log_{10}\bigl(10^{-3}\bigr) \\
&= -(-3) \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{SIL = 3}
$$

### Step 3 — Cross-verify with the range table

Since $10^{-3} \le PFD_{avg} = 10^{-3} < 10^{-2}$ does NOT hold (the upper bound is strict $< 10^{-2}$), but the lower bound $PFD_{avg} \le 10^{-3}$ *is* satisfied. The SIL 3 range is $10^{-3} \ge PFD > 10^{-4}$, i.e., the SIF must be more reliable than $10^{-3}$ but not better than $10^{-4}$. Our value sits **exactly at the upper edge of SIL 3**, confirming:

$$
\boxed{SIL = 3}
$$

### Step 4 — Engineering implication

A SIL-3 requirement means the SIF must typically use:
- **Redundant (1oo2 or 2oo3) sensor architecture** (parallel redundancy),
- **Hardware Fault Tolerance ≥ 1**,
- **Systematic Capability SC 3** (prooftest interval typically 1–2 years),
- **Documented verification** per IEC 61508-3.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Risk Reduction Through Design (Layered Protection)

The **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** framework (CCPS / IEC 61511) is a direct extension of the design process. It is one of the most-tested concepts in KTU 2024.

For each layer, the reduction in event frequency is:

$$
f_{n+1} = f_n \times (1 - PFD_{layer})
$$

| Layer # | Independent Protection Layer (IPL) | Typical $PFD$ | Frequency Reduction |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Process Design (Inherently Safe) | $10^{-1}$ | 10× |
| 1 | Basic Control System (BPCS) | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 10–100× |
| 2 | Operator Intervention (Alarms) | $10^{-1}$ | 10× |
| 3 | Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ | 100–10,000× |
| 4 | Mechanical Relief (Rupture disc) | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ | 100–1,000× |
| 5 | Physical Barrier (Bund) | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 10–100× |
| 6 | Emergency Response | $10^{-1}$ | 10× |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The *inherent safety* layer (Layer 0) is the **cheapest and most reliable** — it cannot fail, be bypassed, or malfunction. Every 14-mark answer should *recommend inherent-safety design* as the first line of defense, not add-on controls.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the term "Design Process" as applied in Design for Safety. (3 marks)

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Design Process** is a structured, phased engineering methodology that transforms a *concept* into a *validated, safe product or system* by integrating safety analysis, risk assessment, and verification activities into every stage — from concept planning, through preliminary and detailed design, to validation, production, and post-market feedback. It is iterative, hierarchical, multi-disciplinary, and documented, ensuring that safety is *designed-in* rather than *added-on*.

> *Allot 1 mark each for: (a) structured/phased nature, (b) safety integration at every phase, (c) iterative/feedback nature.*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] List any three safety activities that are performed during the detailed design phase. (3 marks)

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three safety activities during the **Detailed Design Phase** are:

1. **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** — to identify and rank failure modes of every component and interface.
2. **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** — to model the logical combination of failures that could cause a top-level hazard.
3. **Safety Integrity Level (SIL) Allocation** — to assign the required safety reliability to each safety function per IEC 61508.

*(Alternatively acceptable: HAZOP, Safety Requirements Specification, Verification Matrix preparation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: Either A or B — 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

> **(a)** Explain the various phases of the design process with safety integration at each phase. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the V-Model as a lifecycle model for safety-critical systems. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A, Part (a) — Phases of Design Process (7 marks)

The design process consists of **five principal phases**, each embedding specific safety activities:

**Phase 1 — Concept / Planning (1.5 marks)**
- Define user needs, operating context, and regulatory scope.
- Conduct **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)**.
- Establish **safety goals**, **risk acceptance criteria**, and the **Safety Plan**.
- Output: Concept Design Report, Preliminary Hazard List.

**Phase 2 — Preliminary / Embodiment Design (1.5 marks)**
- Develop multiple design alternatives; perform trade-off studies.
- Apply **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** principles: *Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify*.
- Conduct **Concept Hazard Analysis (CHA)** and component-level **FMEA**.
- Output: Selected concept, risk register, design review minutes.

**Phase 3 — Detailed Design (1.5 marks)**
- Specify every component, material, software module, and tolerance.
- Perform **Detailed FMEA (DFMEA)**, **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**, **HAZOP**.
- Allocate **Safety Integrity Levels (SIL)** per IEC 61508.
- Generate the **Safety Requirements Specification (SRS)** and **Verification Matrix**.
- Output: Engineering drawings, software specs, SRS, V&V plan.

**Phase 4 — Validation & Verification (1.5 marks)**
- Conduct **Design Verification** (building the design *right*).
- Conduct **Design Validation** (building the *right* design).
- Hold **Safety Review Boards (SRR, PDR, CDR)**.
- Perform type tests, certification (CE, UL, BIS).
- Output: V&V Reports, certificates, Design History File (DHF).

**Phase 5 — Production, Operation & Improvement (1 mark)**
- Conduct **Process FMEA (PFMEA)**.
- Implement **field monitoring**, **post-market surveillance**, and **CAPA**.
- Feed lessons learned back to next-generation design.
- Output: Field-data logs, CAPA records, design updates.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A, Part (b) — V-Model for Safety-Critical Systems (7 marks)

The **V-Model** is a lifecycle model in which **each design step on the descending (left) arm has a corresponding test step on the ascending (right) arm**. It is the **preferred model in safety-critical industries** (aerospace, nuclear, medical, automotive) because it forces early specification of test procedures.

**Structure (4 marks):**

| Left Arm (Decomposition) | Right Arm (Verification) |
|---|---|
| User Needs & Use Cases | Operation & Field Performance |
| System Safety Requirements | System Validation / Site Acceptance |
| Subsystem Safety Requirements (SRS) | Integration Testing |
| Hardware/Software Detailed Design | Unit & Module Testing |
| Implementation & Coding | Implementation & Coding (self-check) |

**Why V-Model is preferred for safety-critical design (3 marks):**
1. **Testability-by-design:** Test procedures are developed *in parallel* with specifications.
2. **Traceability:** Every safety requirement can be traced downward (decomposition) and upward (verification).
3. **Early defect detection:** Defects are caught at the level they were introduced, reducing rework cost.
4. **Compliance:** Aligns with IEC 61508, ISO 26262, and DO-178C requirements for "verification per requirement."
5. **Discipline:** The model forces design *freeze* at each level before verification begins.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students draw the V-Model but **forget to label the verification arrows**. Without the explicit correspondence between "User Needs ↔ Validation" and "Subsystem Reqs ↔ Integration Test," you lose **2 marks**. Always label the dashed/verification arrows.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

> **(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **Hierarchy of Controls** used in design for safety. Discuss why elimination is the most effective level. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A safety instrumented function (SIF) is required to have an average Probability of Failure on Demand of $3 \times 10^{-4}$. Determine the required SIL and list two design implications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B, Part (a) — Hierarchy of Controls (7 marks)

The **Hierarchy of Controls** is a ranked, top-down approach to risk reduction used in occupational safety and process safety design.

**Levels (top → bottom): (4 marks)**

1. **Elimination** — *Remove the hazard entirely.* (e.g., remove a toxic solvent from the process; do not use lead).
2. **Substitution** — *Replace with less hazardous.* (e.g., use water-based paint instead of solvent-based).
3. **Engineering Controls** — *Physical guards, interlocks, sensors, ventilation, containment.* (e.g., light curtains on a press, gas detectors).
4. **Administrative Controls** — *Procedures, training, signage, supervision.* (e.g., lock-out tag-out, hot-work permits).
5. **PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)** — *Gloves, helmets, respirators.* (e.g., hard hats, ear plugs).

**Why Elimination is most effective (3 marks):**
- It **removes the hazard at the source** — no hazard = no risk.
- It **cannot fail, be bypassed, or malfunction** (unlike interlocks or PPE).
- It **does not require human compliance or maintenance** (unlike administrative controls or PPE).
- It is **always the cheapest in lifecycle cost** (no recurring training, inspection, or replacement).
- Inherent safety principle: *what isn't there cannot hurt you.*

*(See the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.4 for the visual hierarchy.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B, Part (b) — SIL Determination (7 marks)

**Given:** $PFD_{avg} = 3 \times 10^{-4}$.

**Step 1 — Use the SIL definition:**

$$
SIL = -\log_{10}\bigl(PFD_{avg}\bigr)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and compute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg} &= 3 \times 10^{-4} \\
SIL &= -\log_{10}\bigl(3 \times 10^{-4}\bigr) \\
&= -\bigl[\log_{10}(3) + \log_{10}(10^{-4})\bigr] \\
&= -\bigl[0.4771 + (-4)\bigr] \\
&= -\bigl[-3.5229\bigr] \\
&= 3.5229
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Round down to the nearest integer SIL (per IEC 61508):**

$$
\boxed{SIL = 3}
$$

**Reasoning:** The computed value $3.5229$ falls in the range of SIL 3 ($10^{-3} \ge PFD > 10^{-4}$), and our $PFD = 3 \times 10^{-4}$ indeed satisfies $10^{-3} \ge 3 \times 10^{-4} > 10^{-4}$. Hence **SIL 3** is required.

**Step 4 — Design implications (any two of the following) (2 marks):**

1. **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT) ≥ 1:** The SIF must use a redundant architecture (1oo2 or 2oo3 voting), so that a single hardware fault does not disable the function.
2. **Systematic Capability SC 3:** The development process must follow rigorous methods including structured design, formal reviews, modular design, and use of certified components.
3. **Proof Test Interval (PTI):** Typically 1–2 years; the system must be tested periodically to reveal undetected failures.
4. **Documentation:** A full **Safety Case** including FMEA, FTA, operating procedures, and failure-rate data per IEC 61508-2/3 must be maintained.
5. **Operator Interface:** Diagnostic coverage ≥ 90%; clear alarms and bypass control to prevent dangerous failures during maintenance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks-Loss Zones:**
>
> - ❌ **Rounding Error in SIL:** Students round $3.5229$ to 4 — *wrong*. IEC 61508 mandates **floor** (round down). The required SIL is **3**, not 4.
> - ❌ **Confusing SIL with ASIL:** SIL is from IEC 61508 (process/general); ASIL is from ISO 26262 (automotive). Do not mix them in an exam answer.
> - ❌ **Forgetting to mention "documentation"** as a safety activity. The Design History File (DHF) is a regulatory deliverable, not optional.
> - ❌ **PPE-only answers:** Recommending "give workers gloves" without engineering controls in a 14-mark answer will fetch **zero marks**. Always climb the hierarchy.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section. Read it 5 minutes before the exam.**

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Keywords & Phrases
- **Design Process** = structured + iterative + safety-embedded + multi-phase + documented
- **5 Phases:** Concept → Preliminary → Detailed → V&V → Production/Operation
- **ISD Principle:** Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify (mnemonic: **M-S-M-S**)
- **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE (top is most effective)
- **RPN = S × O × D** (max 1000); RPN > 300 = critical
- **SIL = −log₁₀(PFD)**
- **PFD targets:** SIL1 = 10⁻¹, SIL2 = 10⁻², SIL3 = 10⁻³, SIL4 = 10⁻⁴ (one order of magnitude tighter per level)

### 🔑 Must-Recall Standards
- **ISO 12100** — Machinery safety risk assessment
- **IEC 61508** — Functional safety (SIL origin)
- **ISO 14971** — Medical device risk management
- **ISO 26262** — Automotive (ASIL)
- **21 CFR 820.30** — FDA design controls

### 🔑 Must-Draw Diagrams (Practice Before Exam)
- The 5-phase iterative flowchart with safety activities
- The V-Model with verification arrows
- The Hierarchy of Controls pyramid

### 🔑 Common 14-Mark Question Templates
1. *"Explain the phases of design process with safety integration."* → Use Section 2.1 framework.
2. *"Discuss the V-Model / lifecycle model for safety-critical design."* → Use Section 5.2 Q-A(b) answer.
3. *"Compute RPN / SIL / Risk."* → Use Section 3 derivations.
4. *"Explain Hierarchy of Controls and why elimination is best."* → Use Section 5.2 Q-B(a) answer.

### 🔑 One-Line Exam-Ready Definition
> *"The Design Process is the safety-embedded, iterative, five-phase engineering methodology that transforms a need into a validated, safe product — integrating hazard analysis, risk assessment, and verification at every stage."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conceptual Design and Detail Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Conceptual Design and Detail Design # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Conceptual Design — The Engineering Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Design** is the earliest phase of the engineering design lifecycle where the *functional requirements*, *operating environment*, *basic operating principles*, and *broad safety philosophy* of a product, system, or process are established. At this stage, multiple design alternatives are generated, evaluated against performance, cost, and safety criteria, and the most promising configuration is selected for further development. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **EN ISO 12100 (Safety of Machinery)**, conceptual design is the phase in which **inherent safety** decisions are made — the decisions that determine whether a machine or process will be *safe by its very nature* rather than relying on add-on protective devices. **Core activities of Conceptual Design include:** - Defining the **intended use** and **reasonably foreseeable misuse** - Identifying **energy sources** (electrical, mechanical, thermal, chemical, hydraulic, pneumatic) - Conducting **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** - Applying the **Hierarchy of Controls** (Eliminate → Substitute → Engineer → Admin → PPE) - Establishing **design constraints** (regulatory, ergonomic, environmental) ## 1.2 Detail Design — The Engineering Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Detail Design** is the subsequent phase in which the selected concept is transformed into *fully defined engineering deliverables*: precise geometric dimensions, material specifications, tolerances, surface finishes, fasteners, wiring diagrams, control logic, and **explicit safety provisions** such as guards, interlocks, emergency stops, and warning labels. Detail design answers the question: *"How exactly will this product be built so that it is safe, reliable, and compliant with every applicable standard?"* ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The House-Building Metaphor | Phase | Real-World Analogy | Output | |---|---|---| | **Conceptual Design** | The architect sketches the *shape* of a house, decides the *number of floors*, places the *kitchen away from the children's bedrooms* (inherent safety), and chooses *wood-frame vs steel-frame* construction. | A *concept sketch* + *design philosophy* | | **Detail Design** | The structural engineer specifies *every beam size, every bolt torque, every electrical wire gauge, the exact location of smoke detectors, and the fire-escape geometry*. | A complete *construction blueprint* ready for the builder | > [!NOTE] > The single most important insight: **safety decisions made in Conceptual Design cost 10× to 100× less than the same decisions made in Detail Design**, and 1000× less than retrofitting a built product. ## 1.4 The Safety-Critical Distinction Conceptual design determines **WHAT** the system is and **WHICH HAZARDS** exist. Detail design determines **HOW** the system is constructed and **HOW HAZARDS ARE CONTROLLED**. | Aspect | Conceptual Design | Detail Design | |---|---|---| | **Focus** | Function & Hazard Identification | Specification & Risk Control | | **Hazards** | Identify energy sources & harmful situations | Specify guards, interlocks, distances | | **Decisions** | Choice of operating principle | Choice of materials, tolerances, redundancy | | **Freedom to change** | Very high (low cost) | Low (expensive to modify) | | **Safety standard** | EN ISO 12100 — Clause 5 (Risk assessment) | EN ISO 12100 — Clause 6 (Risk reduction) | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six-Stage Conceptual Design Workflow According to the **KTU 2024 PESFT412 syllabus (Module 1)** and aligned with classical systems engineering methodology (Blanchard & Fabrycky), conceptual design proceeds through six logical steps: 1. **Define the Problem** — Establish the need, the user, the operational context. 2. **Establish Design Functions** — Use a *function structure* (black-box model) to break the system into sub-functions: $F = \{F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_n\}$. 3. **Search for Design Concepts** — Apply creativity techniques: brainstorming, morphological analysis, TRIZ, analogy. 4. **Select Concept Using Safety Criteria** — Use **weighted scoring** with explicit *Safety Weight* ($w_s$). 5. **Develop Selected Concept** — Refine into a workable embodiment. 6. **Evaluate Against Constraints** — Check regulatory compliance, ergonomics, lifecycle cost. > [!NOTE] > Step 4 is where **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** is performed. The KTU examiner frequently asks: *"At which design stage is PHA performed?"* — Answer: **Conceptual Design**. ## 2.2 The Detail Design Workflow Once the concept is frozen, detail design proceeds as: 1. **Decomposition** — Create a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS). 2. **Component Specification** — For each component: *geometry, material, tolerance, surface finish, supplier*. 3. **Safety Subsystem Design** — Guards (fixed, interlocked, adjustable), two-hand controls, light curtains, emergency stops, safety PLCs. 4. **Integration of Standards** — Map every hazard to a specific clause in the relevant standard. 5. **Design Verification** — FMEA, fault tree analysis, calculations. 6. **Design Validation** — Prototype testing, user trials, third-party certification. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Symbol | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | $SF$ | $SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{yield}}}{\sigma_{\text{applied}}}$ | Static Safety Factor (Strength-based) | dimensionless | | $SF_{\text{fatigue}}$ | $SF_{\text{fatigue}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{e}}{\sigma_{a}}$ | Fatigue Safety Factor (using endurance limit) | dimensionless | | $R(t)$ | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability at time $t$ (constant failure rate) | dimensionless | | $\lambda$ | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Failure rate | failures/hour | | $R_{\text{system,series}}$ | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Series reliability (weakest-link) | dimensionless | | $R_{\text{system,parallel}}$ | $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | Parallel (redundant) reliability | dimensionless | | $\text{CAR}$ | $CAR = \dfrac{P_{\text{product failure}}}{P_{\text{system failure}}}$ | Conditional Adjustment Rate (accident severity ratio) | dimensionless | | $R_{\text{risk}}$ | $R_{\text{risk}} = P \times C$ | Risk (Probability × Consequence) | qualitative / score | | $ALARP$ | $\dfrac{\text{Cost of further risk reduction}}{\text{Risk reduction benefit}} \geq \text{threshold}$ | As Low As Reasonably Practicable test | monetary ratio | | $H$ | $H = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot C_i$ | Expected Harm per unit time (using IEC 61508) | harm-units / hr | > [!IMPORTANT] > **For the KTU board exam**, memorize the *static safety factor* formula, the *series reliability* formula, and the *risk = probability × consequence* equation. These three appear in nearly every PESFT412 question paper. ## 2.4 The Hierarchy of Safety Controls in Design This is a **direct KTU syllabus topic**. The hierarchy, applied during *both* conceptual and detail design, ranks controls from most effective to least effective: 1. **Elimination** — Remove the hazard entirely (Conceptual Design domain). 2. **Substitution** — Replace with a less hazardous material or process (Conceptual Design). 3. **Engineering Controls (Inherent Safe Design)** — Design the energy out: reduce voltages, lower temperatures, limit stored energy (Conceptual & Detail). 4. **Guarding / Enclosure** — Physical barriers, interlocks (Detail Design). 5. **Administrative Controls** — Procedures, training, signs (Detail Design supplement). 6. **PPE** — Personal Protective Equipment — the *last* resort (Operational, not design). > [!TIP] > Examiner mnemonic: **E-S-E-G-A-P** — *Elephants Sit Elegantly Guarding Adorable Pandas*. Always **eliminate first**, **PPE last**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Static Safety Factor From First Principles The safety factor quantifies the *margin* between the load-carrying capacity of a material and the *actual* stress imposed by operating loads. **Step 1 — Define the strength property.** The ultimate strength of the material is the maximum stress it can sustain: $\sigma_{\text{ultimate}}$. **Step 2 — Define the applied stress.** The applied stress is the actual load divided by the cross-sectional area: $$\sigma_{\text{applied}} = \dfrac{F}{A}$$ **Step 3 — Define the safety factor as a ratio.** By convention, the safety factor is the ratio of strength to stress: $$\text{Step 3a:} \quad SF \triangleq \dfrac{\text{Strength}}{\text{Stress}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the relevant strength criterion.** For ductile materials under static loading, yielding is the relevant failure mode. Hence the strength used is the **yield strength**: $$\text{Step 4a:} \quad \sigma_{\text{strength}} = \sigma_{\text{yield}}$$ **Step 5 — Combine Steps 3a and 4a.** $$\text{Step 5a:} \quad SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{yield}}}{\sigma_{\text{applied}}}$$ **Step 6 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 5a.** $$\text{Step 6a:} \quad SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{yield}}}{F/A} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{yield}} \cdot A}{F}$$ **Conversion logic for Step 6a:** Dividing by a fraction is the same as multiplying by its reciprocal; hence $\dfrac{\sigma_{\text{yield}}}{F/A}$ becomes $\dfrac{\sigma_{\text{yield}} \cdot A}{F}$. **Step 7 — Generalization to multiple load cases.** If $k$ independent loads act simultaneously, the *worst-case* applied stress is the **scalar sum** (conservative): $$\text{Step 7a:} \quad \sigma_{\text{applied,total}} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sigma_j$$ **Step 8 — Final working form.** $$SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{yield}}}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{k} \sigma_j}$$ **Step 9 — Interpretation.** A safety factor $SF \geq 1$ means the component *will not yield* under the assumed loads. KTU and Indian Standards (e.g., IS 800 for steel, IS 456 for concrete) prescribe **minimum $SF$ values** ranging from **1.5 to 4.0** depending on the consequence of failure, load uncertainty, and material variability. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Series System Reliability for Safety-Critical Systems Many safety systems (e.g., a *dual-channel* emergency-stop circuit) consist of components that must *all* function for the system to be safe. We derive the reliability of such a series system. **Step 1 — Define component reliability.** Let $R_i(t)$ be the probability that component $i$ functions correctly at time $t$. For exponential failure distribution, $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$. **Step 2 — State the independence assumption.** Assume component failures are *statistically independent* (no common-cause failures). **Step 3 — Apply the multiplication rule of probability.** For independent events, the joint probability of *all* events occurring is the product of individual probabilities: $$\text{Step 3a:} \quad R_{\text{system}} = P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \ldots \cap A_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the exponential form.** $$R_{\text{system}}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} = e^{-t \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}$$ **Conversion logic for Step 4:** The product of exponentials is the exponential of the sum because $e^{a} \cdot e^{b} = e^{a+b}$. The factor $-t$ is common to every exponent, so it factors out. **Step 5 — Identify the equivalent system failure rate.** $$\text{Step 5a:} \quad \lambda_{\text{system}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$$ **Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.** The system failure rate is the **sum** of component failure rates. A system with 5 components, each having $\lambda = 10^{-5}$/hr, has $\lambda_{\text{sys}} = 5 \times 10^{-5}$/hr — *worse than every individual component*. > [!IMPORTANT] > This is why **redundancy (parallel reliability)** is essential for safety-critical systems — see Section 3.3. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Parallel Redundancy and the SIL Concept For a *parallel* system of $n$ identical components, the system fails **only if all $n$ components fail simultaneously**. **Step 1 — Probability of a single component failing.** $$P(\text{component fails}) = 1 - R_i$$ **Step 2 — Probability of all $n$ components failing simultaneously** (independence): $$P(\text{all fail}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$$ **Step 3 — Probability that at least one component works** (system success): $$\text{Step 3a:} \quad R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$$ **Step 4 — Simplify for $n = 2$ (dual redundancy, common in safety circuits).** $$R_{\text{parallel,2}} = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2)$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $R_1 = R_2 = R$ for identical components.** $$R_{\text{parallel,2}} = 1 - (1 - R)^2 = 2R - R^2$$ **Step 6 — Numerical illustration (KTU-style problem).** Let $R = 0.99$ for each channel. Then: $$R_{\text{parallel,2}} = 2(0.99) - (0.99)^2 = 1.98 - 0.9801 = 0.9999$$ This **four-9s reliability** corresponds to **SIL 3** under IEC 61508 — the level required for *high-demand* safety instrumented functions. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Conceptual vs Detail Design — Safety Lens | Safety Criterion | Addressed in Conceptual Design? | Addressed in Detail Design? | |---|---|---| | Hazard identification | ✅ **Yes (PHA performed here)** | Refined, but no new hazards should appear | | Energy isolation | ✅ **Choice of energy source** | ✅ Physical isolation devices (breakers, valves) | | Inherent safe design | ✅ **Maximum freedom to apply** | Limited (geometry mostly fixed) | | Guards & interlocks | Not yet | ✅ **Primary deliverable** | | Material selection | Broad families considered | ✅ Specific grade, heat treatment, certificate | | Tolerance & clearances | Not yet | ✅ **Safety distances per EN ISO 13857** | | Ergonomic safety | High-level anthropometry | Detailed reach, posture, force analysis | | Standards compliance | Standards identified | ✅ **Each clause mapped and verified** | | Cost of safety change | **Lowest** (₹100 order) | High (₹10,000) | Very high (₹1,00,000+) | > [!NOTE] > This matrix is **the single most important table** for Module 1. The KTU examiner often asks students to *place* a given safety activity into either the conceptual or detail design column. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Weighted Concept Selection (KTU Style) **Problem (KTU University Exam — May 2024 style):** A student team must select between **three** conceptual designs (A, B, C) for a portable grinding machine. The weighted criteria and scores are given. The *Safety* criterion has a weight of **5**, which is the highest. | Criterion | Weight | Design A | Design B | Design C | |---|---|---|---|---| | Safety | 5 | 8 | 6 | 9 | | Cost | 3 | 7 | 9 | 5 | | Manufacturability | 2 | 6 | 7 | 7 | | Ergonomics | 4 | 5 | 6 | 8 | | Energy efficiency | 3 | 6 | 7 | 8 | **Step 1 — Compute weighted score for Design A:** $$S_A = (5 \times 8) + (3 \times 7) + (2 \times 6) + (4 \times 5) + (3 \times 6)$$ $$S_A = 40 + 21 + 12 + 20 + 18 = 111$$ **Step 2 — Compute weighted score for Design B:** $$S_B = (5 \times 6) + (3 \times 9) + (2 \times 7) + (4 \times 6) + (3 \times 7)$$ $$S_B = 30 + 27 + 14 + 24 + 21 = 116$$ **Step 3 — Compute weighted score for Design C:** $$S_C = (5 \times 9) + (3 \times 5) + (2 \times 7) + (4 \times 8) + (3 \times 8)$$ $$S_C = 45 + 15 + 14 + 32 + 24 = 130$$ **Step 4 — Rank the designs.** $$\text{Rank: } C \; (130) > B \; (116) > A \; (111)$$ **Step 5 — Selection.** **Design C** is selected for detail design. Its *Safety* score (9/10) is the highest, which is the most heavily weighted criterion — consistent with the KTU *Design for Safety* philosophy. > [!WARNING] > A common student error: *forgetting to multiply* the weight and the score. The KTU examiner will deduct **2 marks** for this mistake. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Design-for-Safety Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart TD A[phase1: Need & Problem Definition] --> B[phase2: Conceptual Design] B --> B1[Hazard Identification - PHA] B1 --> B2[Generate Design Alternatives] B2 --> B3[Apply Hierarchy of Controls] B3 --> B4[Safety-Weighted Concept Selection] B4 --> C{Concept Frozen?} C -- No --> B C -- Yes --> D[phase3: Detail Design] D --> D1[Component Specification] D --> D2[Safety Subsystem Design: Guards and Interlocks] D --> D3[FMEA and Fault Tree Analysis] D --> D4[Standards Mapping: EN ISO 12100] D4 --> E[phase4: Verification and Validation] E --> E1[Prototype Testing] E --> E2[Third-Party Certification] E2 --> F[phase5: Production and Operation] F --> G[phase6: Continuous Safety Improvement] G -.Feedback Loop.-> A ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Hierarchy of Safety Controls in Design ```mermaid flowchart TD L1[level1: ELIMINATION
Remove the hazard entirely] --> L2[level2: SUBSTITUTION
Use a less hazardous material or process] L2 --> L3[level3: ENGINEERING CONTROLS
Design the energy out, inherently safer] L3 --> L4[level4: GUARDING and ENCLOSURE
Physical barriers, interlocks, two-hand controls] L4 --> L5[level5: ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS
Procedures, training, signage, supervision] L5 --> L6[level6: PPE
Personal Protective Equipment as last resort] style L1 fill:#ffd700,color:#000 style L2 fill:#ffcc00,color:#000 style L3 fill:#ff9900,color:#000 style L4 fill:#cc6600,color:#fff style L5 fill:#993300,color:#fff style L6 fill:#660000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Conceptual vs Detail Design Decision Boundary ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CD[CONCEPTUAL DESIGN PHASE] CD1[Define Function] --> CD2[Identify Hazards - PHA] CD2 --> CD3[Establish Safety Philosophy] CD3 --> CD4[Choose Operating Principle] CD4 --> CD5[Select Concept - Weighted Scoring] end CD5 --> FT[FROZEN CONCEPT GATE] subgraph DD[DETAIL DESIGN PHASE] DD1[Decompose into Subsystems] --> DD2[Specify Materials and Tolerances] DD2 --> DD3[Design Guards and Interlocks] DD3 --> DD4[FMEA and Risk Reduction Verification] DD4 --> DD5[Final Safety Validation] end FT --> DD1 DD5 --> OUT[Output: Certified Safe Product] style FT fill:#ff0000,color:#fff style CD fill:#e6f7ff style DD fill:#fff7e6 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Risk = Probability × Consequence Matrix (KTU Tool) ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Probability of Harm Occurring] -->|Multiply| C[Severity of Consequence] C --> R[RISK SCORE] R --> R1[LOW RISK - 1 to 4: Acceptable, monitor] R --> R2[MEDIUM RISK - 5 to 12: Reduce by design if possible] R --> R3[HIGH RISK - 13 to 20: Must reduce before proceeding] R --> R4[VERY HIGH RISK - 21 to 25: Stop, redesign concept] style R1 fill:#90ee90 style R2 fill:#ffff00 style R3 fill:#ffa500 style R4 fill:#ff0000,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > These four diagrams are the **visual answer key** for Module 1 of PESFT412. A labelled Mermaid block counts as a *valid schematic* in the KTU answer script — students are encouraged to use them in the examination for clarity and partial-mark protection. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Conceptual Design and state any two of its activities.** **Model Answer:** Conceptual Design is the earliest phase of the engineering design process in which the functional requirements, operating principles, and broad safety philosophy of a system are established and multiple design alternatives are generated. *Two activities:* (i) Identification of hazards through Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA). (ii) Application of the Hierarchy of Controls to select inherently safer design options. *(Any two valid activities accepted — 1.5 marks each.)* ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between Conceptual Design and Detail Design in four points.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Conceptual Design | Detail Design | |---|---|---| | Phase | Earliest, pre-detailed engineering | Later, after concept is frozen | | Output | Concept sketch, hazard list, design philosophy | Full engineering drawings, BOM, specs | | Safety activity | Inherent safety, PHA | Guards, interlocks, safety PLC logic | | Cost of change | Very low | High | *(1 mark for each correct difference; 4 differences required for full 3 marks.)* --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]` **(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **six-stage Conceptual Design workflow** for a safety-critical product. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A hydraulic press has a maximum working pressure of **$P = 25 \text{ MPa}$** acting on a piston of diameter **$d = 80 \text{ mm}$**. The piston material is alloy steel with yield strength **$\sigma_y = 650 \text{ MPa}$**. Calculate: (i) The applied stress on the piston. (ii) The static safety factor. (iii) Comment on whether the design is safe, given that the KTU / IS code minimum $SF$ for hydraulic systems is **2.0**. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — Six-Stage Conceptual Design Workflow (7 marks):** > The student should draw (or describe) the following six stages, each carrying 1 mark for description and 0.5 mark for safety relevance: 1. **Define the Problem** — Identify user, function, environment, constraints. 2. **Establish Function Structure** — Black-box decomposition: $F = \{F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_n\}$. 3. **Search for Design Concepts** — Brainstorming, TRIZ, morphological analysis. 4. **Select Concept Using Safety Criteria** — Weighted scoring with **Safety Weight = highest**. 5. **Develop Selected Concept** — Preliminary embodiment, layout sketches. 6. **Evaluate Against Constraints** — Regulatory check, ergonomics, lifecycle cost. > The schematic should show these six stages as a *cyclic* flow with feedback arrows indicating iteration. The KTU examiner expects the student to *circle* or *highlight* the stages where **PHA** and the **Hierarchy of Controls** are applied (stages 4 and 5). **Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks):** **Step (i) — Applied stress on the piston (3 marks).** **Step 1:** Compute the cross-sectional area of the piston. $$A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (80 \text{ mm})^2}{4}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute. $$A = \dfrac{\pi \times 6400}{4} = 1600\pi \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 3:** Compute the applied force from pressure. $$F = P \times A = 25 \text{ MPa} \times 1600\pi \text{ mm}^2 = 25 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 5026.55 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 4:** Evaluate. $$F = 125663.7 \text{ N} \approx 125.66 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 5:** Compute the applied compressive stress. $$\sigma_{\text{applied}} = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{125663.7}{5026.55} = 25 \text{ MPa}$$ > **Conversion logic:** Since pressure is force per unit area, the *applied stress* on a piston in direct contact with hydraulic fluid *equals the working pressure*. Hence $\sigma_{\text{applied}} = P = 25$ MPa. This short-cut is acceptable to the KTU examiner. **Step (ii) — Static safety factor (2 marks).** **Step 1:** Write the safety factor formula. $$SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{yield}}}{\sigma_{\text{applied}}}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute. $$SF = \dfrac{650 \text{ MPa}}{25 \text{ MPa}} = 26.0$$ **Step (iii) — Safety comment (2 marks).** The computed $SF = 26.0$ is **far greater** than the KTU / IS minimum of $2.0$. The piston is therefore *grossly over-designed*. While the design is technically safe, the engineer may reconsider: - Reducing the piston diameter to save material and weight. - Using a cheaper material (e.g., cast iron with $\sigma_y \approx 250$ MPa → $SF \approx 10$). - Optimising for cost without compromising the $SF \geq 2.0$ requirement. > The *design is safe* but **not optimized** — a classic KTU 14-mark answer conclusion that scores full marks. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]` **(a)** Explain the **Hierarchy of Controls** as applied in the design for safety of a CNC machining centre. List each level and give **one engineering example** for each level. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A safety-critical control system uses **two identical relays in parallel** (1-out-of-2 redundancy). Each relay has reliability $R = 0.95$ at the end of a 1000-hour mission. (i) Calculate the **parallel system reliability**. (ii) If the same two relays were connected in **series** instead, what would the system reliability be? (iii) Which configuration is appropriate for a *safety instrumented function* and why? *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) — Hierarchy of Controls for a CNC Machining Centre (7 marks):** > The student should produce a six-level list, with 1 mark per level + 0.5 mark per example. | Level | Strategy | CNC Machining Centre Example | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Elimination** | Use a *laser-based* non-contact cutting process to eliminate the rotating cutter hazard entirely. | | 2 | **Substitution** | Substitute the *hydraulic oil* (flammable) with a *water-glycol* emulsion. | | 3 | **Engineering Controls (Inherent)** | Use *tool-speed limiters* in the controller to prevent spindle runaway. | | 4 | **Guarding / Enclosure** | Fit a *fixed perimeter guard* with *interlocked access door* — door-open disables spindle. | | 5 | **Administrative Controls** | Issue *lock-out / tag-out* (LOTO) procedure and train every operator. | | 6 | **PPE** | Operators must wear *safety glasses and safety shoes* in the cell. | **Part (b) — Reliability Calculation (7 marks):** **Step (i) — Parallel system reliability (3 marks).** **Step 1:** Apply the parallel reliability formula. $$R_p = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2)$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $R_1 = R_2 = 0.95$. $$R_p = 1 - (1 - 0.95)(1 - 0.95) = 1 - (0.05)(0.05)$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate. $$R_p = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975$$ **Conversion logic:** Both relays must *fail simultaneously* for the system to fail. The probability that *one* specific relay fails is $1 - 0.95 = 0.05$. The probability that *both* fail is $0.05 \times 0.05 = 0.0025$ (independence). Hence the system succeeds with probability $1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975$. **Step (ii) — Series system reliability (2 marks).** **Step 1:** Apply the series reliability formula. $$R_s = R_1 \times R_2$$ **Step 2:** Substitute. $$R_s = 0.95 \times 0.95 = 0.9025$$ **Step (iii) — Configuration recommendation (2 marks).** The **parallel (1-out-of-2) configuration** with $R_p = 0.9975$ is appropriate for a *safety instrumented function* because: - It is **fault-tolerant** — the system continues to operate even if one relay fails. This is a *fail-safe* design. - It meets the **SIL 2 / SIL 3** reliability band required by IEC 61508 for high-demand safety functions. - A series configuration ($R_s = 0.9025$) would fail the safety function the moment *either* relay fails, which violates the **single-point-of-failure** prohibition in safety instrumented systems. > **Answer:** Parallel redundancy is correct; series is unacceptable for a safety function. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in PESFT412:** > 1. **Confusing "hazard" with "risk":** A *hazard* is the source of harm; *risk* is the probability × consequence. Examiners deduct 1 mark for interchange. > 2. **Forgetting to multiply weight × score** in concept selection — 2 marks lost. > 3. **Stating "PPE is the most important control"** — it is the *last* resort; examiners will mark you down. > 4. **Drawing concept design activities that belong to detail design** (e.g., "selecting bolt torque") in the conceptual design answer — common 2-mark error. > 5. **Writing $R = 1 - R^2$ for two parallel identical components but *forgetting to expand* to $2R - R^2$** — partial credit lost. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Conceptual Design and Detail Design** - [x] **Conceptual Design** = *function + hazard identification + choice of operating principle*. Performed **before** any geometry is finalized. - [x] **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** is the *defining* safety activity of conceptual design. - [x] **Detail Design** = *specification + guards + interlocks + FMEA + standards mapping*. - [x] **Hierarchy of Controls** mnemonic: **E-S-E-G-A-P** (Eliminate, Substitute, Engineering, Guarding, Administrative, PPE). - [x] **Safety Factor** formula: $SF = \sigma_{\text{yield}} / \sigma_{\text{applied}}$ — minimum 1.5 to 4.0 depending on consequences. - [x] **Series reliability**: $R_s = \prod R_i$ — *multiplication*; *weakest link* dominates. - [x] **Parallel reliability**: $R_p = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$ — used for *redundant* safety circuits. - [x] **Risk equation**: $R_{\text{risk}} = P \times C$ — qualitative or numerical score. - [x] **Cost of safety change increases ~10× per design phase** (Concept → Detail → Production → Retrofit). - [x] **Standard of reference**: EN ISO 12100 — Clause 5 (Risk assessment) for conceptual; Clause 6 (Risk reduction) for detail. - [x] **Fail-safe** = *predictably safe failure state*; **Foolproof** = *cannot be misused by a lay operator*. - [x] **FMEA** is a detail-design activity; **PHA** is a conceptual-design activity. - [x] **Kt = Kt + Kg**: *Knowledge taught = Knowledge gained* — the KTU examiner's mantra for conceptual + detail design. > **Closing Note:** Mastering the *distinction* between conceptual and detail design — and the *flow of safety decisions* between them — is the single most important learning outcome (CO1) of Module 1 of PESFT412.

topics.title: Assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the framework of **Design for Safety (DfS)** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Assessment** refers to the systematic, structured, and rigorous engineering evaluation process used to identify, analyze, quantify, and judge the acceptability of hazards and associated risks inherent in the design, construction, operation, and decommissioning of engineered systems, processes, plants, and products.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *Safety Assessment is the process of evaluating the safety of a system or process through hazard identification, risk estimation, consequence analysis, and the determination of whether the residual risk is acceptable or requires further mitigation.*

The **Assessment** phase is one of the four pillars of the safety management lifecycle:

1. **Identification** (What can go wrong?)
2. **Assessment** (How bad and how likely?)
3. **Control** (What can be done to reduce it?)
4. **Review** (Are the controls working?)

Assessment sits at the **core analytical heart** of this cycle — without proper assessment, design safety becomes a matter of guesswork rather than engineering.

---

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Safety Assessment

Safety assessment is fundamentally built on three complementary analytical pillars:

| Pillar | Core Question | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Hazard Identification** | *What hazards exist?* | Inventory of potential threats |
| **Consequence Analysis** | *How severe is the outcome?* | Damage radii, fatality zones, environmental impact |
| **Probability Analysis** | *How likely is it to occur?* | Frequency of occurrence (events/year) |

The combination of consequence and probability yields **Risk**, the central currency of safety engineering.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Medical Check-Up

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Going to the Doctor**
>
> Imagine you visit a cardiologist before running a marathon. The doctor performs three actions:
>
> 1. **History & Screening** (Hazard Identification) — *"Do you have a family history of heart disease? Any chest pain?"* — Identifies potential *threats* to your health.
>
> 2. **Diagnostic Tests** (Consequence Analysis) — *"If something goes wrong, how severe would it be?"* — An ECG, blood tests, stress test. Quantifies *magnitude of harm*.
>
> 3. **Probability Estimation** (Risk Estimation) — *"Based on your age, lifestyle, and genetics, what is your statistical chance of a cardiac event during the marathon?"* — Quantifies *likelihood*.
>
> 4. **Prescription** (Risk Evaluation & Control) — *"Given your risk, here is the safe action: avoid the marathon, or run with a pace-setter and an AED on-site."* — Decides if risk is *acceptable*.
>
> This is exactly what an engineer does during **Safety Assessment** — but for a chemical plant, a building, a bridge, or a software-controlled system. The doctor treats *one patient*; the safety engineer treats *the system, the operators, the public, and the environment* all at once.

---

## 1.4 Why Assessment is Indispensable in Design for Safety

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU 2024 Core Insight:**
> *"You cannot control what you have not measured, and you cannot measure what you have not identified."*

- **Quantitative Decision-Making**: Assessment converts subjective fear into **objective numbers** that can be compared against acceptance criteria.
- **Resource Optimization**: Engineers have finite budgets. Assessment directs money to the *highest-risk* problems first.
- **Regulatory Compliance**: Statutory bodies (OSHA, EPA, OISD, PESO, ONGC standards) mandate documented safety assessments before commissioning.
- **Liability Protection**: Documented assessments demonstrate **Due Diligence** in court, shielding the design engineer and the organization from negligence claims.
- **ALARP Demonstration**: Assessment provides the mathematical evidence that risks have been reduced *As Low As Reasonably Practicable*.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics in Safety Assessment

The following quantitative metrics form the **vocabulary** of safety assessment. Memorize these — they appear in almost every KTU question:

- **Individual Risk (IR)** = **10⁻⁶ per year** (acceptable fatality probability for workers)
- **Societal Risk** = **F-N Curve** (cumulative frequency vs. number of fatalities)
- **Fatal Accident Rate (FAR)** = **4.0 fatalities per 10⁸ person-hours** (benchmark)
- **Tolerable Risk** = **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ per year**
- **Acceptable Risk** = **≤ 10⁻⁶ per year** for public, **≤ 10⁻⁵ per year** for workers

---

## 1.6 Scope of Assessment in Module 1

In Module 1 of PESFT412, **Assessment** typically covers the following techniques:

- **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)**
- **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)**
- **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**
- **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**
- **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)**
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)**
- **ALARP Principle**
- **Risk Matrix & Bow-Tie Analysis**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Assessment Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats Assessment as a **six-stage systematic process**. Understanding the logical sequence is critical for both theory and exam answers.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six Stages of Safety Assessment (KTU Standard):**
> 1. **Define the System Boundary** — What is being assessed? What is in-scope, out-of-scope?
> 2. **Hazard Identification** — Systematic search for credible threats.
> 3. **Consequence Analysis** — Modeling the physical outcome if a hazard is released.
> 4. **Frequency / Probability Analysis** — Estimating how often the initiating event occurs.
> 5. **Risk Estimation & Evaluation** — Combining consequence × frequency → comparing with criteria.
> 6. **Documentation & Recommendation** — Producing the Safety Assessment Report (SAR).

---

## 2.2 Pillar 1 — Hazard Identification Techniques

### 2.2.1 Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)

PHA is a **qualitative, early-stage** technique applied during the conceptual design phase.

- **Logic**: For each system component, ask *What are the potential hazards, causes, and effects?*
- **Output**: A ranked list of hazards with preliminary risk levels.
- **Strength**: Cheap, fast, requires limited data.
- **Weakness**: Highly dependent on team experience.

### 2.2.2 Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)

HAZOP is the **gold-standard qualitative technique** for chemical process plants.

- **Core Method**: Apply guidewords (`NO`, `MORE`, `LESS`, `AS WELL AS`, `PART OF`, `REVERSE`, `OTHER THAN`) to process parameters (`Flow`, `Pressure`, `Temperature`, `Level`, `Composition`).
- **Output**: Deviations, causes, consequences, safeguards, and actions.
- **Logic Step**: Each node of the process is interrogated with every (guideword × parameter) combination.

### 2.2.3 Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)

FMEA evaluates **component-level failures** and propagates them to system-level effects.

- **Logic**: List every component → identify every failure mode → trace the effect → assess severity.
- **Key Metric**: **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** calculated later in this section.

### 2.2.4 What-If Analysis / Checklist Analysis

- **What-If**: Brainstorming "What if this fails?" scenarios.
- **Checklist**: Use pre-existing industry checklists (OSHA, API, IS standards).

---

## 2.3 Pillar 2 — Consequence Analysis

Consequence analysis estimates the **physical damage** if a hazardous material or energy is released. Standard models include:

- **Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)** — TNT-equivalent method
- **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion (BLEVE)**
- **Jet Fire & Pool Fire** — Thermal radiation distance
- **Toxic Gas Dispersion** — Gaussian plume (Pasquill-Gifford model)
- **Flammable Gas Dispersion** — Lower Flammable Limit (LFL) contour

The **damage criteria** are typically expressed as overpressure (for explosions) or thermal radiation dose (for fires).

---

## 2.4 Pillar 3 — Frequency & Probability Analysis

Two principal techniques:

### 2.4.1 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)

A **top-down deductive** method that uses Boolean logic to compute the probability of a top event (e.g., reactor explosion).

- **Key Operators**: `AND` gate (independent), `OR` gate (either).
- **Output**: Minimum Cut Sets, Top Event Probability.

### 2.4.2 Event Tree Analysis (ETA)

A **bottom-up inductive** method that traces outcomes from an initiating event through successive safety barriers.

- **Output**: Branch probabilities leading to various end-states (Safe, Fire, Explosion, Toxic Release).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** The following formulas appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on Assessment. Master them.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Variable | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Individual Risk (IR)** | `IR = f × C` | fatalities/year | f = frequency, C = consequence (0–1 probability of fatality) |
| 2 | **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** | `RPN = S × O × D` | dimensionless | S=Severity (1–10), O=Occurrence (1–10), D=Detection (1–10) |
| 3 | **Top Event Probability (AND gate)** | `P(AND) = P₁ × P₂ × … × Pₙ` | dimensionless | Events are statistically independent |
| 4 | **Top Event Probability (OR gate)** | `P(OR) = 1 − ∏(1 − Pᵢ)` | dimensionless | For small Pᵢ: `P(OR) ≈ Σ Pᵢ` |
| 5 | **Fatal Accident Rate (FAR)** | `FAR = (N × 10⁸) / (T × H)` | fatalities / 10⁸ person-hr | N=fatalities, T=exposure years, H=hours/year |
| 6 | **TNT Equivalent Mass** | `W_TNT = (η × m × ΔH_c) / E_TNT` | kg TNT | η = empirical yield factor (1–10%), m = mass, ΔHc = heat of combustion, E_TNT = 4520 kJ/kg |
| 7 | **Consequence Distance (Probit)** | `Y = a + b·ln(V)` | probit units | V = intensity (heat dose, concentration, overpressure) |
| 8 | **ALARP Equation** | `Risk ∝ Cost of Further Reduction` | monetary | Reduction must be "grossly disproportionate" to cost |
| 9 | **F-N Curve Criterion** | `F × Nᵏ ≤ C` | dimensionless | k = 1 (UK HSE), k = 2 (Netherlands) |
| 10 | **Lethal Dose 50% (LCt₅₀)** | `C^n × t = k` | constant | Haber's Law for toxic exposure |

---

## 2.6 ALARP — The Cornerstone of Risk Evaluation

> [!NOTE]
> **ALARP** = **As Low As Reasonably Practicable**

ALARP is the **decision-making framework** used after assessment to determine if further risk reduction is required.

**The Three Zones of Risk:**

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│   UNACCEPTABLE RISK   →   Must reduce regardless of cost    │
│  (IR > 10⁻⁴ per year)                                      │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   ALARP / TOLERABLE    →   Reduce if "reasonably            │
│  (10⁻⁶ ≤ IR ≤ 10⁻⁴)   practicable" (cost-benefit analysis) │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   BROADLY ACCEPTABLE  →   No further action required        │
│  (IR < 10⁻⁶ per year)                                      │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Practical Test for ALARP**: Would the *gross disproportion* test be passed? — *"Is the cost of further risk reduction grossly disproportionate to the safety benefit gained?"*

---

## 2.7 Risk Matrix (Standard 5×5)

| ↓ Likelihood / Severity → | **1 - Negligible** | **2 - Minor** | **3 - Moderate** | **4 - Major** | **5 - Catastrophic** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **5 - Frequent** | Medium | High | High | Extreme | Extreme |
| **4 - Probable** | Low | Medium | High | High | Extreme |
| **3 - Occasional** | Low | Medium | Medium | High | Extreme |
| **2 - Remote** | Low | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **1 - Improbable** | Low | Low | Low | Medium | Medium |

**Decision Rules:**
- **Extreme** → Stop operation, immediate redesign.
- **High** → Mandatory controls before commissioning.
- **Medium** → Procedural controls + monitoring.
- **Low** → Accept with documentation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Risk Priority Number (RPN) Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** A hydraulic press component has been identified with three failure modes. Compute RPN for each and rank them.

**Given Data:**

| Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1: Seal rupture | 8 | 5 | 4 |
| F2: Pump cavitation | 6 | 7 | 5 |
| F3: Sensor drift | 4 | 8 | 7 |

**Step 1 — Recall the formula:**

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

where `S` = Severity, `O` = Occurrence, `D` = Detection difficulty (all rated 1–10).

**Step 2 — Compute for F1:**

$$
\text{RPN}_{F1} = 8 \times 5 \times 4 = 160
$$

**Step 3 — Compute for F2:**

$$
\text{RPN}_{F2} = 6 \times 7 \times 5 = 210
$$

**Step 4 — Compute for F3:**

$$
\text{RPN}_{F3} = 4 \times 8 \times 7 = 224
$$

**Step 5 — Rank by RPN (higher = more critical):**

$$
\text{F3 (224)} > \text{F2 (210)} > \text{F1 (160)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action:** The sensor drift (F3) is the highest priority for corrective action, even though it has the lowest severity — because detection is hard and occurrence is high.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fault Tree Analysis: Top Event Probability

**System Description:**
A reactor overheating top event T occurs if:
- Either pump A fails **OR** pump B fails (cooling failure), **OR**
- Both temperature sensors (T1 **AND** T2) fail simultaneously.

**Fault Tree Structure:**

```
                 ┌── P(A) ──┐
         ┌── OR ─┤          ├──
         │       └── P(B) ──┘   T (Top Event)
T ───────┤
         │       ┌── P(T1) ──┐
         └── AND ─┤           ├──
                 └── P(T2) ──┘
```

**Given Failure Probabilities:**
- `P(A) = 0.01`
- `P(B) = 0.02`
- `P(T1) = 0.005`
- `P(T2) = 0.005`

**Step 1 — Compute the AND gate (sensor failure):**

$$
P(\text{AND}) = P(T_1) \times P(T_2) = 0.005 \times 0.005 = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the OR gate (cooling failure):**

$$
P(\text{OR}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - P_i)
$$

Substituting:

$$
P(\text{OR}) = 1 - (1 - 0.01)(1 - 0.02) = 1 - (0.99)(0.98)
$$

$$
P(\text{OR}) = 1 - 0.9702 = 0.0298
$$

**Step 3 — Total Top Event Probability (OR of both branches):**

$$
P(T) = 1 - (1 - P_{\text{OR}})(1 - P_{\text{AND}})
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - (1 - 0.0298)(1 - 2.5 \times 10^{-5})
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - (0.9702)(0.999975)
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - 0.970176 = 2.98 \times 10^{-2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The probability of reactor overheating ≈ 1 in 33.6 events per year. This exceeds typical tolerable risk (10⁻³ to 10⁻⁴), so additional safety interlocks are mandated.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — TNT Equivalent for Vapor Cloud Explosion

**Problem:**
A 500 kg propane cloud detonates. Heat of combustion of propane is 46,000 kJ/kg. Yield factor (empirical) = 4%. Compute the TNT equivalent mass.

**Step 1 — State the formula:**

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{\eta \times m \times \Delta H_c}{E_{\text{TNT}}}
$$

where `E_TNT = 4520 kJ/kg`.

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{0.04 \times 500 \times 46{,}000}{4520}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**

$$
0.04 \times 500 = 20
$$

$$
20 \times 46{,}000 = 920{,}000 \ \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by E_TNT:**

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{920{,}000}{4520} = 203.5 \ \text{kg TNT}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 500 kg propane release yields the explosive equivalent of **~204 kg of TNT**. Using standard overpressure-distance tables, this corresponds to ~50% structural damage at approximately 30–40 meters from the epicenter.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Individual Risk Calculation

**Problem:**
A relief valve fails once in 50 years. When it fails, there is a 10% chance of a fatality in the immediate vicinity. Compute the Individual Risk.

**Step 1 — State the formula:**

$$
\text{IR} = f \times C
$$

**Step 2 — Compute frequency:**

$$
f = \frac{1}{50} = 0.02 \ \text{per year}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
\text{IR} = 0.02 \times 0.10 = 2 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{fatalities/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with acceptance criteria:**

$$
\text{IR} = 2 \times 10^{-3} > 10^{-4} \ (\text{Tolerable Upper Limit})
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** The risk is **UNACCEPTABLE**. Mandatory redesign or additional safeguards are required before commissioning.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Fatal Accident Rate (FAR)

**Problem:**
Over a 20-year industry history, there were 3 fatalities and 250 workers exposed for 2000 hours/year. Compute FAR.

**Step 1 — Formula:**

$$
\text{FAR} = \frac{N \times 10^{8}}{T \times H_{\text{total}}}
$$

where `H_total = T × (workers × hours/year)`.

**Step 2 — Compute total person-hours:**

$$
H_{\text{total}} = 20 \times 250 \times 2000 = 10{,}000{,}000 \ \text{person-hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
\text{FAR} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{10^{7}} = 30 \ \text{fatalities per } 10^{8} \ \text{person-hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with industry benchmarks:**

- **Chemical industry average**: 5 per 10⁸ person-hours
- **Our result**: 30 per 10⁸ person-hours → **6× higher than industry average** → Unacceptable performance; requires intervention.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — F-N Curve Criterion Check

**Problem:**
A plant analysis yields the following data:

| Scenario | Frequency (per year) | Fatalities |
|---|---|---|
| S1 | 1 × 10⁻³ | 1 |
| S2 | 1 × 10⁻⁴ | 10 |
| S3 | 1 × 10⁻⁵ | 100 |

Check against UK HSE criterion: `F × N ≤ 10⁻²`.

**Step 1 — Compute F × N for each scenario:**

$$
S_1: \ 10^{-3} \times 1 = 10^{-3} \ (\leq 10^{-2}) \ \checkmark
$$

$$
S_2: \ 10^{-4} \times 10 = 10^{-3} \ (\leq 10^{-2}) \ \checkmark
$$

$$
S_3: \ 10^{-5} \times 100 = 10^{-3} \ (\leq 10^{-2}) \ \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Compare cumulative F-N:**

- All individual points lie below the criterion line.
- No scenario violates the ALARP envelope.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The plant meets the UK HSE societal risk criterion. ALARP demonstration is complete.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Assessment Technique

| Technique | Stage of Design | Data Required | Output Type | Cost | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PHA** | Conceptual | Low | Qualitative | Low | Early screening |
| **Checklist** | All stages | Low | Qualitative | Low | Routine audits |
| **What-If** | Preliminary | Medium | Qualitative | Low-Med | Brainstorming |
| **HAZOP** | Detailed design | Medium-High | Qualitative | Medium | Process plants |
| **FMEA** | Detailed design | Medium | Semi-quantitative | Medium | Mechanical/Electronic |
| **FTA** | Detailed design | High | Quantitative | High | Complex systems |
| **ETA** | Detailed design | High | Quantitative | High | Sequence of events |
| **QRA** | All stages | Very High | Quantitative | Very High | Major hazard facilities |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Master Assessment Workflow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Define System Boundary"] --> B["Hazard Identification"]
    B --> C1["PHA"]
    B --> C2["HAZOP"]
    B --> C3["FMEA"]
    B --> C4["Checklist"]
    C1 --> D["Select Critical Hazards"]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E["Consequence Analysis"]
    D --> F["Frequency Analysis"]
    E --> G["Risk Estimation R equals f times C"]
    F --> G
    G --> H{"Compare with Acceptance Criteria"}
    H -->|Below Criteria| I["Acceptable Risk"]
    H -->|Above Criteria| J["ALARP Analysis"]
    J --> K{"Further Reduction Practicable?"}
    K -->|Yes| L["Implement Controls"]
    K -->|No| M["Document Residual Risk"]
    L --> N["Re-assess System"]
    N --> E
    M --> O["Safety Assessment Report"]
    I --> O
```

## 4.2 Fault Tree Structure — Reactor Overheat

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T["Top Event: Reactor Overheat"]
    A["Cooling Pump A Fails"]
    B["Cooling Pump B Fails"]
    S1["Temp Sensor T1 Fails"]
    S2["Temp Sensor T2 Fails"]

    T --> G1["OR Gate"]
    G1 --> A
    G1 --> B
    G1 --> G2["AND Gate"]
    G2 --> S1
    G2 --> S2
```

## 4.3 Event Tree — LPG Release Scenario

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IE["Initiating Event: LPG Leak"]
    IE --> S1["Ignite Immediately?"]
    S1 -->|Yes| F["Jet Fire"]
    S1 -->|No| S2["Cloud Forms?"]
    S2 -->|No| SD["Safe Dispersion"]
    S2 -->|Yes| S3["Delayed Ignition?"]
    S3 -->|Yes| VCE["Vapor Cloud Explosion"]
    S3 -->|No| SD
```

## 4.4 Bow-Tie Diagram — Generic Safety Assessment View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T["TOP EVENT: Loss of Containment"]
    T --> P1["Threat: Pipe Rupture"]
    T --> P2["Threat: Pump Failure"]
    T --> P3["Threat: Human Error"]

    T --> C1["Consequence: Fire"]
    T --> C2["Consequence: Explosion"]
    T --> C3["Consequence: Toxic Release"]

    P1 -.->|Prevented by| B1["Barrier: Inspection"]
    P2 -.->|Prevented by| B2["Barrier: Maintenance"]
    P3 -.->|Prevented by| B3["Barrier: Training"]

    C1 -.->|Mitigated by| M1["Mitigation: Fire Water System"]
    C2 -.->|Mitigated by| M2["Mitigation: Blast Walls"]
    C3 -.->|Mitigated by| M3["Mitigation: Scrubber"]
```

## 4.5 ALARP Decision Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Computed Risk Level"]
    R --> Z1{"Risk Greater than 10 power -4?"}
    Z1 -->|Yes| U["UNACCEPTABLE: Mandatory Reduction"]
    Z1 -->|No| Z2{"Risk Between 10 power -6 and 10 power -4?"}
    Z2 -->|Yes| AL["ALARP ZONE: Apply Cost-Benefit"]
    Z2 -->|No| A["ACCEPTABLE: Document and Monitor"]
    AL --> Q{"Cost Grossly Disproportionate?"}
    Q -->|No| RD["Implement Further Reduction"]
    Q -->|Yes| A
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. Define Safety Assessment. List any four hazard identification techniques.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Safety Assessment** is the systematic engineering process of evaluating a system to identify hazards, analyze their consequences and frequencies, estimate the associated risks, and determine whether those risks are acceptable, tolerable, or require further reduction through the ALARP principle.

**Four Hazard Identification Techniques:**

1. **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** — Early-stage qualitative review of system hazards.
2. **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)** — Systematic application of guidewords to process parameters.
3. **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** — Component-level failure analysis with RPN ranking.
4. **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** — Top-down deductive method using Boolean logic for probability computation.
