*(Other acceptable answers: Checklist Analysis, What-If Analysis, Event Tree Analysis)*

---

### **Q2. What is the ALARP principle? Why is it used in safety assessment?** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)** is the risk management principle that requires all risks to be reduced to a level *as low as reasonably practicable* — meaning that further reduction is required unless the cost (in money, effort, or time) is *grossly disproportionate* to the safety benefit gained.

**Why ALARP is Used:**

- Provides a **balanced decision-making framework** between absolute safety and economic reality.
- Forms the **legal and regulatory basis** for compliance in the UK, EU, and many other jurisdictions.
- Demonstrates **due diligence** to regulatory bodies and courts.
- Avoids the extremes of *"zero risk at any cost"* (impossible) and *"accept all risks"* (negligent).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Assessment Problem** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A chemical reactor has a cooling system with two redundant pumps (A and B) and two redundant temperature sensors (T1 and T2). The reactor overheats (Top Event) if:
- **Either** Pump A **OR** Pump B fails, **OR**
- **Both** T1 **AND** T2 fail simultaneously.

The failure probabilities per year are: `P(A) = 0.02`, `P(B) = 0.03`, `P(T1) = 0.04`, `P(T2) = 0.05`.

**(a)** Draw the fault tree and compute the probability of the top event.

**(b)** If the overheat event leads to a fatality with probability 0.2, compute the Individual Risk (IR) per year. Comment on its acceptability using the ALARP framework.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Fault Tree Structure (drawn as in Section 4.2):**

```
                 ┌── P(A) ──┐
         ┌── OR ─┤          ├──
         │       └── P(B) ──┘   T (Top Event)
T ───────┤
         │       ┌── P(T1) ──┐
         └── AND ─┤           ├──
                 └── P(T2) ──┘
```

**Step 2 — Compute AND gate (both sensors fail):**

$$
P(\text{AND}) = P(T_1) \times P(T_2) = 0.04 \times 0.05 = 0.0020
$$

**Step 3 — Compute OR gate (either pump fails):**

$$
P(\text{OR}) = 1 - (1 - P_A)(1 - P_B)
$$

$$
P(\text{OR}) = 1 - (1 - 0.02)(1 - 0.03) = 1 - (0.98)(0.97)
$$

$$
P(\text{OR}) = 1 - 0.9506 = 0.0494
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Top Event Probability (top-level OR):**

$$
P(T) = 1 - (1 - P_{\text{OR}})(1 - P_{\text{AND}})
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - (1 - 0.0494)(1 - 0.0020)
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - (0.9506)(0.9980)
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - 0.9487 = 0.0513 \ \text{per year}
$$

> **Result:** The reactor overheat frequency is approximately **0.0513 events/year** (≈ 1 event every 19.5 years).

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute Individual Risk:**

$$
\text{IR} = f \times C = 0.0513 \times 0.2 = 0.01026 \ \text{fatalities/year}
$$

Converting:

$$
\text{IR} \approx 1.03 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{per year}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with ALARP criteria:**

- **Tolerable upper limit** (worker) ≈ `10⁻⁴ per year`
- **Computed IR** = `1.03 × 10⁻² per year`

$$
\text{IR}_{\text{computed}} > \text{IR}_{\text{tolerable}}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> - Forgetting to multiply by the consequence probability (0.2) — writing only `0.0513` as the final answer.
> - Stating "risk is high" without referencing numerical ALARP criteria.
> - Failing to mention that the design must be modified.

> **The risk is UNACCEPTABLE.** The system lies in the **unacceptable region** of the ALARP framework. Mandatory design modifications are required, such as:
> - Adding a third redundant pump.
> - Installing a diverse backup sensor (different technology).
> - Implementing an automatic emergency cooling system.
> - Re-designing to bring IR below `10⁻⁴ per year`.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

A chemical plant has identified three failure scenarios:

| Scenario | Frequency (per year) | Number of Fatalities |
|---|---|---|
| S1 | 1 × 10⁻³ | 1 |
| S2 | 1 × 10⁻⁴ | 10 |
| S3 | 1 × 10⁻⁵ | 100 |

**(a)** Plot the F-N curve and verify whether the plant meets the **UK HSE criterion** (F × N ≤ 10⁻²). Discuss the significance of societal risk.

**(b)** Using the ALARP principle, recommend whether the plant should be allowed to operate. What further risk reduction measures would you suggest?

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute F × N for each scenario:**

$$
S_1: \ 10^{-3} \times 1 = 10^{-3} \ (\leq 10^{-2}) \ \checkmark
$$

$$
S_2: \ 10^{-4} \times 10 = 10^{-3} \ (\leq 10^{-2}) \ \checkmark
$$

$$
S_3: \ 10^{-5} \times 100 = 10^{-3} \ (\leq 10^{-2}) \ \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — F-N Curve Description:**

The F-N curve is plotted with:
- **X-axis**: N = Number of fatalities (logarithmic scale: 1, 10, 100)
- **Y-axis**: F = Cumulative frequency per year (logarithmic scale)

The three points plot as a straight line on log-log paper with slope `−1`, since `F × N = 10⁻³` for all points.

```
F (frequency/year)
  │
  │ ● S1
10⁻³│       ● S2
  │              ● S3
10⁻⁴│
  │
10⁻⁵└────┬─────┬─────► N (fatalities)
        1    10   100
```

**Step 3 — Comparison with UK HSE Criterion:**

The criterion line is `F = 10⁻² / N`. All three points lie *below* this line, meaning the plant **meets** the UK HSE societal risk criterion.

**Step 4 — Significance of Societal Risk:**

> Societal risk captures the **collective impact** on the public, not just individual workers. A single event with 100 fatalities is weighted more heavily than 100 separate events with 1 fatality each — because communities, emergency services, and political systems cannot easily absorb mass-casualty events.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — ALARP Decision:**

Although all three scenarios meet the UK HSE criterion, ALARP requires that risks be reduced *as low as reasonably practicable* until the cost of further reduction is *grossly disproportionate* to the safety gain.

**Step 2 — Recommended Risk Reduction Measures:**

1. **Inherent Safer Design (ISD)**:
   - Reduce inventory of hazardous materials.
   - Substitute with less hazardous chemicals where possible.
   - Attenuate process conditions (lower T, P).

2. **Engineering Controls**:
   - Install additional pressure relief valves.
   - Add a third independent cooling system.
   - Use diverse (different-vendor) sensors to prevent common-mode failure.

3. **Procedural Controls**:
   - Strict Permit-to-Work systems.
   - Operator training and competency certification.
   - Emergency response drills every 6 months.

4. **Mitigation Systems**:
   - Blast walls and fire-water deluge.
   - Toxic gas detection with automatic isolation.
   - Safe haven / control room with positive-pressure ventilation.

**Step 3 — Final Recommendation:**

> The plant **may operate** subject to implementation of the above ALARP measures, with annual re-assessment and documentation in the **Safety Assessment Report (SAR)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> Students often write *"the plant meets the criteria, so it is safe"* — this is WRONG. Meeting the criterion is **necessary but not sufficient**. ALARP must always be demonstrated through cost-benefit analysis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION CHECKLIST — ASSESSMENT (MODULE 1)**

- **Assessment** is the analytical core of safety engineering — it converts qualitative concerns into quantitative risk metrics.
- The **three pillars** are: Hazard Identification → Consequence Analysis → Frequency Analysis → Risk.
- **Risk = Frequency × Consequence**. Memorize this master equation.
- **RPN = S × O × D** (Severity × Occurrence × Detection). Always rank by RPN descending.
- **FTA AND gate**: `P = product of all inputs`. **OR gate**: `P = 1 − product of (1 − Pᵢ)`.
- **ALARP** has three zones: Unacceptable (>10⁻⁴), ALARP/Tolerable (10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁴), Acceptable (<10⁻⁶).
- **TNT equivalent formula**: `W_TNT = η × m × ΔH_c / 4520` — yields kg of TNT for explosion modeling.
- **Individual Risk (IR)** = `f × C` and must be compared to `10⁻⁶/year` (public) or `10⁻⁵/year` (worker).
- **F-N Curve** plots cumulative frequency vs. fatalities on log-log paper; UK HSE criterion is `F × N ≤ 10⁻²`.
- **HAZOP** uses **guidewords** (`NO`, `MORE`, `LESS`, etc.) × **process parameters** (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, etc.).
- **PHA** is the *first* technique applied — during conceptual design — and is qualitative.
- **QRA** = Quantitative Risk Assessment — the most comprehensive, most expensive, and most data-hungry technique.
- **Bow-Tie diagrams** visualize the path from threats → top event → consequences with barriers on each side.
- **Meet the criterion ≠ Safe** — ALARP demonstration is always mandatory after quantitative evaluation.
- The **Safety Assessment Report (SAR)** is the final deliverable and serves as the legal record of due diligence.
- **Risk Matrix** (5×5) is used for rapid screening when full quantitative data is unavailable.
- **Haber's Law** `C^n × t = k` is the foundation of toxic dose-response assessment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inherently Safer Design - Chemical Reactor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inherently Safer Design - Chemical Reactor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** is a philosophy of process safety engineering that seeks to **permanently eliminate or significantly reduce hazards** by changing the process design itself, rather than by adding external protective systems (such as alarms, interlocks, relief valves, or containment dikes) to manage them. The term is formally defined by the **Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS)** as: *"A condition in which the hazards associated with materials and operations used in the process have been reduced or eliminated through design choices that reduce the potential for human, equipment, or environmental errors to result in accidents."*

Within the specific context of a **Chemical Reactor**, ISD focuses on manipulating the chemistry, thermodynamics, and inventory such that a potential runaway reaction, over-pressurization, or toxic release becomes physically, kinetically, or thermodynamically impossible — or at least orders of magnitude less severe.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Child-Proof Medicine Bottle"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a dangerous medicine that is toxic to children. Two safety philosophies exist:
> - **Add-On Safety (Conventional):** Buy a medicine bottle, then add a child-resistant cap, then add a warning label, then add a lock-box, and warn the parents.
> - **Inherently Safer Design:** Re-formulate the medicine so it tastes bitter, or package it in single-dose blisters that a child cannot open in bulk. The hazard is reduced by *the design itself*, not by accessories stacked on top.
>
> A chemical reactor follows the same logic. Rather than installing alarms, rupture discs, and emergency scrubbers, an inherently safer reactor *is built in a way that the runaway cannot occur in the first place.*

### 1.3 Foundational Context — The Hierarchy of Hazard Control

In the **KTU 2024 syllabus for PESFT412**, ISD sits at the **most preferred (top) tier** of the *Hierarchy of Controls* used in occupational and process safety:

| Priority | Control Level | Nature |
|----------|---------------|--------|
| 1 (Highest) | **Inherent Safety** | Eliminate hazard by design |
| 2 | Passive | Fail-safe physical barriers |
| 3 | Active | Engineered controls (alarms, trips) |
| 4 | Procedural | SOPs, training |
| 5 (Lowest) | PPE / Emergency Response | Last line of defense |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Course Outcome (CO1) of PESFT412 explicitly requires students to *“analyze process hazards and apply inherently safer design principles during the early design phase.”* Reactor design is the most frequently asked application area.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants Used in Reactor ISD

The following constants and parameters govern the analysis throughout this topic and must be memorized:

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \; \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \; \text{m} \cdot \text{s}^{-2}$
- **Atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \; \text{kPa}$
- **Kinematic viscosity of water (reference):** $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$
- **Standard reference temperature for ΔT_ad:** $T_{ref} = 298.15 \; \text{K}$
- **Design basis for toxic inventory threshold (TIH):** $\geq 100 \;\text{kg}$ as per **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119**
- **Tame / Deter / Train concept** (Kletz, 1978) — cornerstone of ISD vocabulary
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Inherently Safer Design (Kletz's Framework)

The foundation of ISD was formalized by **Trevor Kletz (1978)** in his seminal paper *"What You Don't Have, Can't Leak."* The four strategies, remembered with the acronym **MS²** (Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify), form the analytical backbone of every KTU question on this topic.

#### 2.1.1 MINIMIZE (Intensification)

- **Operational meaning:** Reduce the *quantity* of hazardous material present in the reactor at any given instant.
- **Reactor-level application:**
  - Use **continuous stirred-tank reactors (CSTR)** or **plug flow reactors (PFR)** instead of large batch reactors.
  - Decrease reactor volume ($V$) — for a given volumetric flow rate $Q$, this increases the space velocity.
  - Store intermediates in smaller, distributed tanks (one large tank → several small ones).
- **Why it works mathematically:** Consequence of a release scales with the inventory raised to a fractional power (typically $1/3$ for vapor cloud explosions, or $1/2$ for spills). Therefore, halving the inventory may reduce consequence by **30–40%**.

#### 2.1.2 SUBSTITUTE (Substitution)

- **Operational meaning:** Replace a hazardous reactant, solvent, or intermediate with a *less-hazardous* chemical that performs the same function.
- **Reactor-level application:**
  - Use water-based slurries instead of flammable organic solvents.
  - Replace phosgene ($COCl_2$) with diphosgene or solid triphosgene in synthesis.
  - Use solid catalysts instead of soluble toxic metal complexes (e.g., heterogeneous $\text{MoS}_2$ instead of homogeneous $\text{Ni(CO)}_4$).
  - Use **hydrogen peroxide** ($H_2O_2$) instead of **chlorine** for oxidation when chemistry permits.

#### 2.1.3 MODERATE (Attenuation)

- **Operational meaning:** Operate the reactor under conditions (temperature, pressure, concentration, phase) that are *less hazardous*, even if the intrinsic chemistry remains the same.
- **Reactor-level application:**
  - **Refrigerated / cryogenic storage** of acrylonitrile (BP = $77.3 \; °C$) — vapor pressure is suppressed.
  - Use **dilute aqueous solutions** of acids (e.g., <70% HNO₃) instead of fuming nitric acid.
  - Operate **at lower pressure** (atmospheric or slight vacuum) instead of high pressure.
  - Use **dilution with inert carrier gas** to stay below Lower Flammability Limit (LFL).

#### 2.1.4 SIMPLIFY (Error Tolerance)

- **Operational meaning:** Design the reactor and piping so that operating errors, maintenance mistakes, and misconnections are *difficult to commit* and *easy to recover from*.
- **Reactor-level application:**
  - Eliminate dead legs and stagnant branches where exothermic polymerization can accumulate.
  - Use **distinct flange sizes/gaskets** for different services (so caustic cannot be hooked to acid).
  - Design **gravity-fed** coolant tanks above the reactor (no pump required for emergency cooling).
  - Use **one-way valves** (check valves) and **visible flow indicators** to prevent reverse flow of reactants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world case study — Bhopal Disaster (1984):** Methyl Isocyanate (MIC), an extremely toxic intermediate, was stored in **three large refrigerated tanks of ~41 tons each**. Had the plant applied **MINIMIZE**, only a few hundred kilograms would have been on-site at any time, reducing the death toll from ~3,800 to a handful. The disaster is the textbook justification taught in every KTU module on ISD.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table must be memorized verbatim for the End Semester Examination (ESE). Marks are frequently awarded (5–7 marks) for correctly identifying the right formula and its application context.

| # | Formula | Description / Use Case | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\Delta T_{ad} = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{rxn}}{\sum_i n_i \, C_{p,i}}$ | **Adiabatic temperature rise** of a reactor with loss of cooling. Used to assess runaway severity. | $\Delta H_{rxn}$ = heat of reaction (J/mol), $n_i$ = moles of species $i$, $C_{p,i}$ = heat capacity (J/mol·K) |
| 2 | $Q_{gen} = V \cdot k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a/RT} \cdot C_A^n$ | **Heat generation rate** in an exothermic reactor. When $Q_{gen} > Q_{rem}$, runaway begins. | $V$ = volume (m³), $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol), $C_A$ = concentration (mol/m³) |
| 3 | $Q_{rem} = U \cdot A \cdot (T - T_c)$ | **Heat removal rate** through the jacket/coil. | $U$ = overall HTC (W/m²·K), $A$ = area (m²), $T_c$ = coolant T (K) |
| 4 | $TMR_{ad} = \dfrac{C_p \cdot R \cdot T^2}{q_r \cdot E_a}$ | **Time to Maximum Rate** under adiabatic conditions — measures how fast runaway accelerates. | $q_r$ = rate of heat generation (W/kg), $T$ in K |
| 5 | $V_{vent} = \dfrac{Q_{max} \cdot \alpha}{G \cdot \sqrt{P_{set} - P_{atm}}}$ | **DIERS vent sizing equation** (homogeneous system) for two-phase relief. | $Q_{max}$ = max heat gen rate, $\alpha$ = void fraction, $G$ = discharge mass flux |
| 6 | $\text{Risk} = \text{Frequency} \times \text{Consequence}$ | **Fundamental risk equation** — ISD lowers Consequence directly. | Frequency in /yr, Consequence in damage units |
| 7 | $t_{mix} \propto \left(\dfrac{P}{N^3 \cdot D^5 \cdot \rho}\right)^{1/3}$ | **Mixing time** in CSTR — critical for thermal homogeneity. | $P$ = impeller power, $N$ = rpm, $D$ = impeller diameter |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{release} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \cdot \Delta P}$ | **Mass release rate** through a hole — used to quantify inventory reduction benefit. | $C_d \approx 0.61$, $A$ = hole area (m²), $\Delta P$ (Pa) |

### 2.3 The Tame – Deter – Train Hierarchy (Kletz Extended)

A complementary ISD framework that appears in KTU Part A questions:

- **TAME:** Prevent the hazard from creating an incident (e.g., inventory limits, intrinsically safe instruments).
- **DETER:** Prevent an incipient incident from propagating (e.g., barricades, foam injection, secondary containment).
- **TRAIN:** Mitigate the consequences through emergency response (e.g., PPE, escape routes, fire brigade).

> **Inherent safety is the deepest form of "taming"** — it eliminates the need to deter and often the need to train.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ISD philosophy is mandated in major international standards:

- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** (Process Safety Management): "shall consider inherently safer design."
- **CCPS Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis (CPQRA), 2nd Ed.**
- **AIChE/CCPS Inherently Safer Chemical Processes — A Life Cycle Approach (2nd Ed., 2009).**

In engineering practice, ISD is applied during **Conceptual Design (Front-End Loading, FEL-1, FEL-2)** when >80% of the lifecycle safety cost is still modifiable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides the full mathematical derivation of the key reactor safety equations. **Each algebraic step is shown explicitly** — students should reproduce this style in their answer scripts to earn full marks.

### 3.1 Derivation: Adiabatic Temperature Rise ($\Delta T_{ad}$)

**Starting assumption (closed, adiabatic reactor):**
Under loss of cooling, the entire heat of reaction is retained within the reaction mass, raising its temperature.

**Step 1 — Apply First Law of Thermodynamics to the closed, adiabatic system:**

$$
\Delta U = Q - W
$$

**Step 2 — For an adiabatic reactor, $Q = 0$. Assuming negligible PV work at liquid-phase reactor conditions, $W \approx 0$:**

$$
\Delta U = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the heat generated by reaction to the heat absorbed by the reaction mass:**

$$
-\Delta H_{rxn} \cdot (-\xi) = \int_{T_0}^{T_{final}} \sum_i n_i \, C_{p,i} \, dT
$$

where $\xi$ is the extent of reaction (mol). For complete consumption of limiting reactant, the released heat equals $\vert \Delta H_{rxn} \vert \cdot n_{limiting}$.

**Step 4 — Assuming constant heat capacities over the temperature range:**

$$
\int_{T_0}^{T_{final}} \sum_i n_i \, C_{p,i} \, dT = \left(\sum_i n_i \, C_{p,i}\right) \cdot (T_{final} - T_0)
$$

**Step 5 — Define the adiabatic temperature rise as $\Delta T_{ad} = T_{final} - T_0$, and solve:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta T_{ad} = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{rxn}}{\sum_i n_i \, C_{p,i}}}
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** If $\Delta T_{ad}$ exceeds the **Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) onset temperature** minus operating temperature, the reaction is classified as **HIGHLY HAZARDOUS** and an inherently safer alternative (e.g., dilution or substitution) must be sought.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Time to Maximum Rate ($TMR_{ad}$) under Adiabatic Conditions

This is a **Kletz-CCPS gold standard** formula that quantifies "how much time do we have to act before the runaway becomes uncontrollable."

**Step 1 — Define the adiabatic induction time.** The time required for the temperature of an adiabatic mass to rise from initial $T_0$ to a value where the self-heating rate equals some critical value (often $0.01 \; °C$/min or the exotherm onset).

**Step 2 — Write the rate of heat generation as a function of temperature only (after the limiting reactant is partially consumed):**

$$
q_r(T) = k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} \cdot \Delta H_{rxn} \cdot C_A^n
$$

**Step 3 — Express the temperature rise rate in adiabatic conditions:**

$$
\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{q_r(T)}{C_p} = \frac{\Delta H_{rxn} \cdot k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} \cdot C_A^n}{C_p}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to obtain time as a function of temperature:**

$$
dt = \frac{C_p \, dT}{\Delta H_{rxn} \cdot k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} \cdot C_A^n}
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $T_0$ to $T_{final}$ (the runaway peak). Using the approximation that the Arrhenius term dominates:**

$$
\int_0^{TMR} dt = \int_{T_0}^{T_{final}} \frac{C_p \, e^{E_a/RT}}{\Delta H_{rxn} \cdot k_0 \cdot C_A^n} \, dT
$$

**Step 6 — Use the dominant-term approximation (since $E_a / RT \gg 1$ near the runaway), giving the Kletz form:**

$$
\boxed{TMR_{ad} = \dfrac{C_p \cdot R \cdot T^2}{q_r \cdot E_a}}
$$

> **Note for examiners:** When students are asked to assess ISD options, a larger $TMR_{ad}$ means **more time for intervention** — this is a measurable inherently safer property.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Mass Release Rate (Used to Quantify the "MINIMIZE" Benefit)

For a hole of cross-sectional area $A_h$ in a vessel containing pressurized liquid:

**Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's equation between the bulk fluid and the hole exit:**

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_1^2}{2} = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^2}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Inside the vessel, $v_1 \approx 0$ (large reservoir). Pressure outside is $P_{atm}$:**

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{\rho} = \frac{v_2^2}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the exit velocity:**

$$
v_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the discharge coefficient $C_d$ (which accounts for vena contracta and friction losses, typically $C_d \approx 0.61$ for sharp-edged orifice):**

$$
v_{eff} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}
$$

**Step 5 — Mass flow rate through the hole:**

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{release} = C_d \cdot A_h \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \cdot \Delta P}}
$$

> **Inherently safer design link:** If the inventory $m$ is reduced by a factor of 2 (and vessel geometry scales accordingly), the release duration halves, and for a constant hole size, the consequence footprint reduces roughly as the cube root of mass for vapor cloud dispersion:

$$
\text{Consequence} \propto m^{1/3} \quad \text{(explosion overpressure)}
$$

Thus, halving the inventory reduces the blast radius by **~21%**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Applying ISD to a Real Reactor Problem

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **KTU Part B model structure** — students should draw and fill this matrix in the exam to score the 7-mark design question.

**Scenario:** A batch reactor for nitration of benzene uses a mixture of **fuming nitric acid (98%)** and **sulfuric acid** at 60 °C. The $\Delta T_{ad}$ is measured to be 220 °C, $TMR_{ad} = 4 \; \text{min}$, and the on-site inventory is 12 tonnes of mixed acid.

| ISD Strategy | Proposed Change | Quantitative Effect | Net ISD Score (1–5) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Minimize** | Replace 12-t batch with three 4-t semi-batch reactors | $TMR_{ad}$ in each unit **~7 min**; blast footprint $\downarrow 21\%$ | 4 |
| **Substitute** | Use 70% nitric acid (denatured) instead of fuming 98% | $\Delta T_{ad}$ drops to ~90 °C | 5 |
| **Moderate** | Operate at 35 °C with continuous acid addition | $k_{\text{reaction}}$ drops 4×; $TMR_{ad}$ rises to 25 min | 5 |
| **Simplify** | Install **separate, color-coded charging lines** for HNO₃ and H₂SO₄ | Eliminates mis-charge risk; near-zero CAPEX | 3 |
| **Status Quo** | — | Baseline: $TMR_{ad} = 4$ min, $\Delta T_{ad} = 220 \; °C$ | 1 |

> **Best combined solution:** Substitute + Moderate + Simplify. Minimize is desirable but economically constrained (multiple reactors).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Inherently Safer Design Strategy Wheel for a Chemical Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef pillar fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef tactic fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#0a1d33
    classDef outcome fill:#f4d35e,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#0a1d33,stroke-width:2px
    classDef root fill:#7d0a0a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff,stroke-width:3px

    rootHazard([Hazardous Reactor Operation]):::root
    goal[/Inherently Safer Reactor/]:::outcome

    rootHazard --> M1
    M1["MINIMIZE\nInventory"]:::pillar
    M2["SUBSTITUTE\nChemicals"]:::pillar
    M3["MODERATE\nConditions"]:::pillar
    M4["SIMPLIFY\nGeometry"]:::pillar

    M1 --> tA["Use CSTR or PFR\nSmaller tanks\nLower holdup"]:::tactic
    M1 --> tB["On-demand generation\nJust-in-time feed"]:::tactic
    M2 --> tC["Aqueous vs organic\nSolid vs gas catalyst\nLess toxic reagent"]:::tactic
    M2 --> tD["Replace phosgene\nwith diphosgene"]:::tactic
    M3 --> tE["Refrigerated storage\nAtmospheric pressure\nDilute solutions"]:::tactic
    M3 --> tF["Operate below LEL\nUse inert diluent"]:::tactic
    M4 --> tG["Eliminate dead legs\nColor-coded lines\nOne-way valves"]:::tactic
    M4 --> tH["Gravity emergency\ncoolant feed"]:::tactic

    tA --> goal
    tB --> goal
    tC --> goal
    tD --> goal
    tE --> goal
    tF --> goal
    tG --> goal
    tH --> goal
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Risk Reduction Hierarchy Showing ISD Dominance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef layer fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef top fill:#7d0a0a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef mid fill:#f4d35e,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#000
    classDef low fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#000

    R1["Tier 1\nINHERENT SAFETY\nEliminates hazard by design\nHIGHEST PRIORITY"]:::top
    R2["Tier 2\nPASSIVE SAFETY\nBaffles, dikes, blast walls"]:::mid
    R3["Tier 3\nACTIVE CONTROLS\nAlarms, interlocks, trips, SIS"]:::low
    R4["Tier 4\nPROCEDURAL\nSOPs, training, permits"]:::layer
    R5["Tier 5\nEMERGENCY RESPONSE\nPPE, evacuation, fire brigade\nLOWEST PRIORITY"]:::layer

    R1 ==> R2
    R2 ==> R3
    R3 ==> R4
    R4 ==> R5

    note["KTU 2024 Mandate:\nOSHA 29 CFR 1910.119\nrequires ISD consideration\nbefore add-on controls"]:::top
    R1 -.- note
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Heat Balance Failure Logic in a Chemical Reactor (When ISD Prevents Runaway)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef box fill:#f4f4f4,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef fail fill:#7d0a0a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef success fill:#0a5c2e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff

    A[Reactor Operating Normally\nQgen less than Qrem]:::success
    B[Coolant Pump Fails\nor Loss of Agitation]:::fail
    C{Qgen greater than Qrem?}:::box
    D[Temperature Rises\nExponentially]:::fail
    E{ISD TMRad is sufficient?\nIs more than 10 minutes?}:::box
    F[Operator Intervention Possible\nAdd-on Safety Layer]:::box
    G[Reactor Reaches DSC Onset\nRunaway Initiated]:::fail
    H[Inherently Safer Design Active:\nLow Inventory and\nHigh Cp Solvent]:::success
    I[Runaway Self-Quenched\nTmax below Decomposition]:::success
    J[Catastrophic VCE or BLEVE\nRupture Disk Fails]:::fail

    A --> B --> C
    C -- No --> A
    C -- Yes --> D --> E
    E -- Yes --> F --> I
    E -- No --> G --> J
    H -. pre-empts .-> C
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Schematic — Replacing a Batch Reactor with a Continuous One (Minimize)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef old fill:#f4d35e,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#000
    classDef new fill:#0a5c2e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff

    OLD["BATCH REACTOR\nV equals 12 m3\nInventory 12 t\nTMRad 4 min\nHIGH CONSEQUENCE"]:::old
    NEW["CSTR TRAIN in PARALLEL\n3 units of 1.5 m3 each\nTotal Inventory 4.5 t\nTMRad 9 min per unit\nLOWER CONSEQUENCE"]:::new

    FEED1[Reagent Feed]:::old
    FEED2[Reagent Feed]:::new
    PROD1[Batch Product]:::old
    PROD2[Continuous Product]:::new

    FEED1 --> OLD --> PROD1
    FEED2 --> NEW --> PROD2

    OLD -- ISD Minimize --> NEW
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. Writing the four ISD strategies in the wrong order — they must be **M-S² (Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify)**.
> 2. Confusing **Inherent Safety** with **Passive Safety** (e.g., a bund wall is *passive*, not *inherent*).
> 3. Stating *“ISD eliminates risk”* — it **reduces** risk, never fully eliminates it.
> 4. Forgetting the units of $\Delta T_{ad}$ (Kelvin or °C — must be specified).
> 5. In derivation questions, skipping the *assumption statement* (closed system, no PV work) — examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 6. Misnaming **TMR** as "time to maximum rate" without specifying *adiabatic* — wrong answer.

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define Inherently Safer Design (ISD). List and briefly explain the four strategies of ISD as proposed by Trevor Kletz.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Inherently Safer Design (ISD) is a process safety approach that permanently eliminates or reduces hazards by modifying the process design itself, rather than by adding external protective equipment or procedures. Proposed by **Trevor Kletz (1978)**, the four strategies are:

1. **Minimize** — Reduce the quantity of hazardous material in the reactor (e.g., use CSTR instead of large batch).
2. **Substitute** — Replace a hazardous chemical with a less hazardous one (e.g., water-based solvents for organics).
3. **Moderate** — Operate under milder conditions (e.g., refrigerated storage, lower pressure).
4. **Simplify** — Design the system to be error-tolerant (e.g., eliminate dead legs, color-coded lines).

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four strategies with one example each.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*With the help of a suitable example, explain how the principle of "Substitute" is applied to make a chemical reactor inherently safer.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Substitute** strategy replaces a hazardous reactant, solvent, or intermediate with a less dangerous chemical that performs the same chemical function.

**Example:** In the industrial synthesis of polycarbonates, the highly toxic gas **phosgene ($COCl_2$, TLV = 0.1 ppm)** was historically used. Through ISD substitution, it has been replaced with **diphosgene** (a liquid) or with **solid triphosgene**, or with the non-phosgene **melt transesterification process using diphenyl carbonate**. The new process:

- Operates at near-atmospheric pressure instead of high-pressure phosgene cylinders.
- Eliminates the Bhopal-type catastrophic release potential.
- Reduces the inventory of toxic gas from tonnes to grams.

This substitution is a *permanent* hazard reduction, not an add-on control. *(3 marks — 1 for principle, 2 for example with quantitative/qualitative benefit.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of Inherently Safer Design (ISD) for a chemical reactor. Discuss in detail the four strategies of ISD and give **two specific reactor-level applications** of each.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
ISD, as defined by Kletz and CCPS, is the design philosophy that seeks to remove or reduce process hazards at source, making the process *intrinsically* less dangerous rather than relying on engineered add-on controls. For reactors, this is critical because runaway exothermic reactions and toxic releases are the leading causes of major chemical incidents.

**Strategy 1 — Minimize (1.5 marks):**
Reducing the inventory of hazardous material.
- *Application 1:* Replace a 10 m³ batch reactor with three parallel 1 m³ CSTRs — total inventory drops 70%.
- *Application 2:* Use *on-demand* generation of an intermediate (e.g., phosgene generated and consumed in situ).

**Strategy 2 — Substitute (1.5 marks):**
Replacing a hazardous chemical.
- *Application 1:* Use **hydrogen peroxide** (30% aqueous) instead of liquid chlorine for pulp bleaching.
- *Application 2:* Use **water as solvent** instead of methanol in Grignard-free organometallic synthesis.

**Strategy 3 — Moderate (1.5 marks):**
Operating under less severe conditions.
- *Application 1:* Store **acrylonitrile at 4 °C** (vs. ambient) to suppress vapor pressure and reduce release concentration.
- *Application 2:* Operate the **oxidation reactor at 1.5 atm** instead of 10 atm, with corresponding temperature reduction.

**Strategy 4 — Simplify (1.5 marks):**
Designing for error tolerance.
- *Application 1:* Use **distinct flange sizes** for acid vs. alkali charging lines (different services cannot be cross-connected).
- *Application 2:* Position the **emergency coolant tank on the roof** above the reactor — gravity-fed, no pump required during power failure.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A nitration reactor operates with 5,000 kg of mixed acid (HNO₃ + H₂SO₄). The reaction is highly exothermic with $\Delta H_{rxn} = -1.20 \times 10^5 \; \text{J/mol}$. The total heat capacity of the reaction mass is $C_{p,\text{total}} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \; \text{J/K}$. Calculate:
   1. The **adiabatic temperature rise** $\Delta T_{ad}$.
   2. If the operating temperature is $T_0 = 333 \; \text{K}$ and the **DSC onset temperature** is $T_{onset} = 473 \; \text{K}$, classify the reactor's runaway severity.
   3. Propose **two inherently safer modifications** and justify which one is preferred.

**Model Solution:**

**(b)(1) Calculation of $\Delta T_{ad}$ (2 marks):**

The adiabatic temperature rise is the maximum temperature change possible if all heat is retained.

$$
\Delta T_{ad} = -\frac{\Delta H_{rxn}}{C_{p,\text{total}}}
$$

The total heat released by 5,000 kg of reaction mass (using molar basis — given $\Delta H_{rxn}$ is already normalized per mole, but we use the simplified form here for the energy balance using total heat capacity, which is the standard KTU approach):

$$
\Delta T_{ad} = -\frac{(-1.20 \times 10^5)}{1.5 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta T_{ad} = 0.08 \; \text{K per J of heat release per kg}}
$$

> *Note: For the exam, the simplified problem statement uses total heat capacity directly. Re-evaluating using mass-specific heat:*

$$
\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{\vert \Delta H_{rxn} \vert}{C_{p,\text{total}}} = \frac{1.20 \times 10^5}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 0.08 \; \text{K}
$$

> This value seems low for a real nitration; therefore, in a real interpretation, the **total heat released by the entire inventory** is:

$$
Q_{total} = m \cdot \vert \Delta H_{rxn,\text{specific}} \vert
$$

> Assuming $\Delta H_{rxn}$ in the problem is on a *total* basis (per 5,000 kg batch):

$$
\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{1.20 \times 10^5 \; \text{J}}{1.5 \times 10^6 \; \text{J/K}} = 0.08 \; \text{K}
$$

> If the $\Delta H_{rxn}$ value is per kg of acid (more realistic for industrial nitration), then for 5,000 kg:

$$
Q_{total} = 5{,}000 \times 1.20 \times 10^5 = 6.0 \times 10^8 \; \text{J}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^8}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 400 \; \text{K}}
$$

**(b)(2) Severity Classification (2 marks):**

Maximum temperature reached:

$$
T_{max} = T_0 + \Delta T_{ad} = 333 + 400 = 733 \; \text{K}
$$

Since $T_{max} = 733 \; \text{K} \gg T_{onset} = 473 \; \text{K}$, the reactor will **violently cross the decomposition boundary** during loss-of-cooling. **Severity classification: HIGH / CATASTROPHIC (CCPS Level 5).**

**(b)(3) Two ISD Modifications (3 marks):**

| Modification | Quantitative Benefit | Preferred? |
|---|---|---|
| **Substitute:** Replace 98% HNO₃ with 70% HNO₃ | $\Delta T_{ad}$ drops by ~50% (less active reactant); $T_{max} \approx 533 \; K$, still above onset but margin improves | Good, but chemistry may slow |
| **Moderate:** Operate at semi-batch with controlled acid feed | Effective $C_p$ doubles due to lower instantaneous conversion; $\Delta T_{ad}$ halved | **Preferred — no chemistry change** |
| **Minimize:** Reduce inventory to 1,000 kg (1/5) | $\Delta T_{ad}$ unchanged *per unit mass*, but $Q_{total} \downarrow$ 5×; lower total heat release | Combine with above |

**Preferred solution: MODERATE (semi-batch) + MINIMIZE**, because they require minimal capital change and permanently lower the runaway potential without altering the chemistry.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Bhopal Disaster (1984)** as a case study. Explain how each of the four ISD strategies (Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify) could have prevented or drastically reduced the catastrophe.

**Model Answer:**

**Background (1 mark):**
On the night of December 2–3, 1984, in Bhopal, India, ~41 tonnes of **Methyl Isocyanate (MIC)** — an extremely toxic intermediate in pesticide production — leaked from a Union Carbide plant. The gas cloud drifted over densely populated shantytowns, killing ~3,800 people immediately and causing long-term injury to hundreds of thousands. Water ingress into an MIC tank triggered a violent exothermic reaction, generating HCN, CO, and other toxic gases.

**Minimize (1.5 marks):**
- Original design stored ~41 t of MIC in three large refrigerated tanks. 
- *ISD proposal:* Store only the MIC required for the next 8–12 hours of operation (~1–2 t), generated on-demand from the phosgene-MMA precursor.
- *Effect:* Maximum credible release drops by ~95%; lethal vapor cloud radius shrinks from kilometers to a few hundred meters.

**Substitute (1.5 marks):**
- *ISD proposal:* Replace MIC-based synthesis with the **alternative non-MIC routes** that were already known (e.g., the carbamate route using methyl carbamoyl chloride, or enzymatic processes).
- *Effect:* Eliminates MIC from the plant entirely — the Bhopal accident becomes *physically impossible*.

**Moderate (1.5 marks):**
- *ISD proposal:* Maintain MIC at **refrigerated conditions (0–4 °C) AND at low pressure** in smaller, distributed storage.
- *Effect:* Vapor pressure of MIC is suppressed ~5×; the toxic vapor cloud cannot form rapidly even if the tank fails.

**Simplify (1.5 marks):**
- *ISD proposal:* 
  - Install a **physical barrier** between the MIC storage and the water washing line to prevent cross-contamination.
  - Use **dedicated, isolated** MIC storage with one-way valves and a separate vent scrubbing system that is independent of the production vent.
  - Eliminate "stale" MIC inventory (the actual leak was from a tank that had been stored for weeks).
- *Effect:* Eliminates the water-MIC contact that triggered the runaway.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The Bhopal disaster is the canonical case study used globally to justify **mandatory ISD consideration** in process design. A combination of all four strategies — particularly Substitute and Minimize — would have prevented the loss of life entirely.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pharmaceutical batch reactor contains **800 kg of a pyrophoric Grignard reagent** in diethyl ether. The reactor has a **$TMR_{ad} = 6 \; \text{min}$**, operating temperature 35 °C, and a heat of reaction $\Delta H_{rxn} = -3.5 \times 10^4 \; \text{J/mol}$. The total heat capacity of the contents is $C_{p,\text{total}} = 6.4 \times 10^5 \; \text{J/K}$. 

(i) Calculate the **adiabatic temperature rise** $\Delta T_{ad}$.  
(ii) If the **ether flash point is $-45 \; °C$** and the **decomposition temperature is $180 \; °C$**, evaluate whether a runaway will cause a fire/explosion or a decomposition.  
(iii) Suggest **two inherently safer design changes** for this reactor.

**Model Solution:**

**(b)(i) Adiabatic temperature rise (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta T_{ad} = -\frac{\Delta H_{rxn}}{C_{p,\text{total}}}
$$

Assuming $\Delta H_{rxn}$ is the total heat release for the 800-kg batch:

$$
\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^4}{6.4 \times 10^5}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta T_{ad} = 0.055 \; \text{K}}
$$

> *Correction:* In typical Grignard problems, $\Delta H_{rxn}$ is on a per-mole basis. Assume the given value is the *total* for the 800 kg batch (as KTU problems often simplify):

**Maximum temperature under runaway:**

$$
T_{max} = T_0 + \Delta T_{ad} = 308 + 0.055 \approx 308.05 \; \text{K}
$$

> Since this value is too small, the problem is clearly using $\Delta H_{rxn}$ *per mole* or per kg. Recalculating with $\Delta H_{rxn}$ interpreted as **per kg** of Grignard reagent (industry-typical value):

$$
Q_{total} = 800 \times 3.5 \times 10^4 = 2.8 \times 10^7 \; \text{J}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{2.8 \times 10^7}{6.4 \times 10^5} = 43.75 \; \text{K}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta T_{ad} \approx 44 \; \text{K}}
$$

**(b)(ii) Consequence evaluation (2 marks):**

$$
T_{max} = 308 + 44 = 352 \; \text{K} = 79 \; °C
$$

Compare with the two thresholds:
- Ether flash point: $-45 \; °C$ — **FAR EXCEEDED** (by 124 °C).
- Decomposition temperature: $180 \; °C$ — **NOT EXCEEDED** (margin of 101 °C remaining).

**Conclusion:** The runaway will **definitely cause a fire/explosion of the ether solvent** long before the Grignard decomposes. The dominant hazard is **flash fire / VCE**, not decomposition. This is a **Severity Level 4 (Severe)** event per CCPS.

**(b)(iii) Two ISD changes (3 marks):**

| # | ISD Change | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **SUBSTITUTE — Replace diethyl ether with diisopropyl ether or 2-MeTHF** (higher flash point: $-20 \; °C$ or $11 \; °C$ respectively) | Pushes the solvent above the runaway $T_{max}$ region — solvent no longer flashes. |
| 2 | **MINIMIZE — Replace 800-kg batch with a 50-kg continuous-flow reactor (CFR) with in-situ Grignard generation** | Inventory drops 16×; $TMR_{ad}$ rises; flash-fire consequence radius shrinks 2.5×. |
| 3 (bonus) | **MODERATE — Operate at 0–5 °C with $N_2$ blanket and intrinsically safe instruments** | Slower kinetics + oxygen exclusion eliminates ignition source. |

**Best combined solution:** SUBSTITUTE (2-MeTHF) + MINIMIZE (CFR) = inherently safer plant.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **ISD = eliminate hazard at source**, not bolt-on controls. (CCPS Definition)
- [x] **Four strategies (M-S²):** **M**inimize, **S**ubstitute, **M**oderate, **S**implify. *(Kletz, 1978)*
- [x] **Kletz's maxim:** *"What you don't have, can't leak."*
- [x] **Hierarchy of Controls:** Inherent > Passive > Active > Procedural > PPE.
- [x] **Tame – Deter – Train** is the *extended* Kletz hierarchy.
- [x] **Key formula:** $\Delta T_{ad} = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{rxn}}{\sum n_i C_{p,i}}$ — must state the **assumptions** (closed, adiabatic, constant $C_p$).
- [x] **Key formula:** $TMR_{ad} = \dfrac{C_p R T^2}{q_r E_a}$ — used to assess *time available for intervention*.
- [x] **DIERS vent sizing** uses two-phase flow $G$ parameter — refer to DIERS Project Manual.
- [x] **Bhopal 1984** is the *canonical case study* — expect it in every ESE.
- [x] **Inventory reduction benefit:** consequence $\propto m^{1/3}$ for VCE; halving $m$ gives ~21% radius reduction.
- [x] **Minimize ≠ Eliminated:** even with ISD, residual risk needs management.
- [x] **OSHA 1910.119** *mandates* ISD consideration — students should quote this in long answers.
- [x] **Reactor-specific ISD tools:** semi-batch operation, in-situ generation, intensified heat transfer (microreactors, spinning disk).
- [x] **Mistakes to avoid in exam:** confusing *inherent* with *passive*; forgetting to convert K/°C; missing units in $\Delta T_{ad}$.
- [x] **CCPS Levels of Inherent Safety:** Order-of-magnitude reduction in consequence = +1 level on the inherent safety index.
- [x] **Kletz's law of simplicity:** *"A device that is simple is easier to operate, easier to maintain, and harder to make a mistake with."*

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Does "Types" Mean in Design for Safety (DfS)?

In **Design for Safety (PESFT412)**, the term **"Types"** refers to the **classification of safety design strategies** (also called the **hierarchy of controls** or **layers of protection**). A safety engineer does not have a single tool to prevent accidents; instead, he or she has an *arsenal* of techniques that are classified into four fundamental "types" or categories. Each type represents a different philosophy of how safety is achieved in an engineered system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Design for Safety Types* are the **classified engineering strategies** used to eliminate, reduce, or control hazards throughout the lifecycle of a product, process, or system. They are ordered in a hierarchy of effectiveness, from the most effective (inherent) to the least effective (procedural).

## 1.2 The Four Canonical Types of Safety Design

According to internationally accepted process and product safety frameworks (CCPS, Kletz, Trevor Kletz's Inherent Safety principles, IEC 61511, ISO 12100), the four types are:

| # | Type | One-Line Essence |
|---|------|------------------|
| 1 | **Inherent Safety Design** | Remove the hazard itself — it cannot cause harm because it is not present. |
| 2 | **Passive Safety Design** | Add a permanent physical feature that contains or mitigates the hazard without needing to "act." |
| 3 | **Active Safety Design** | Install a sensor + logic + actuator system that detects the hazard and triggers a response. |
| 4 | **Procedural Safety Design** | Rely on human operators, training, SOPs, signage, and administrative controls. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why four and not one?** Because in real engineering (chemical plants, robotics, automotive, aerospace, civil structures), no single method is enough. A modern car, for example, uses **all four** types simultaneously: crash-worthy chassis (passive), ABS brakes (active), lane-keep assist (active), and a driver's manual telling you to wear a seatbelt (procedural).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Burglar" Metaphor

Imagine you want to protect your house from a burglar. The **four types** of protection look like this:

- **Inherent (Type 1):** You simply **do not own anything worth stealing** (the hazard — "valuable item" — is removed). The burglar has no motive. *This is the most powerful form of safety.*
- **Passive (Type 2):** You build a **10-foot concrete wall** around the house. The wall does nothing actively, but by its mere presence, it blocks the burglar. (Bumpers, blast walls, fire insulation, fuses are passive.)
- **Active (Type 3):** You install a **motion sensor, alarm, and automatic door lock**. The system *acts* the instant the burglar steps in. (Smoke detectors, SCADA trips, airbags, sprinklers are active.)
- **Procedural (Type 4):** You hire a **guard and write a rulebook** saying "lock the door at 10 PM." It works — but only if the human follows it. (SOPs, PPE, Permit-to-Work systems are procedural.)

> [!TIP]
> The order **Inherent → Passive → Active → Procedural** is the **Inherently Safer Design (ISD) hierarchy** of Trevor Kletz, the "Father of Inherent Safety." This order is **exam-critical** for KTU.

## 1.4 The Type of Hazard vs. The Type of Safety Strategy — Don't Confuse!

A student new to DfS often confuses **types of safety** with **types of hazards**. They are related but distinct:

- **Types of Hazards:** Physical, Chemical, Biological, Ergonomic, Psychological, Environmental (these are *what can hurt you*).
- **Types of Safety Design:** Inherent, Passive, Active, Procedural (these are *how you prevent it from hurting you*).

The exam question "Discuss the **types** of safety design" almost always means the **four design strategies above**, not the hazard list.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL):** Defined by **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511** as a discrete level (1 to 4) for specifying the safety integrity requirements of the safety instrumented functions. **SIL-4 is the highest** integrity (probability of dangerous failure on demand ≤ 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁸).
- **Hazard Rate (λ):** Failures per hour, typically **10⁻⁶ /hr** for a standard industrial instrument.
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF):** $\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$, units: hours.
- **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF):** The factor by which a safety system reduces risk, dimensionless.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners in 2024 Scheme frequently ask: *"Arrange the types of safety in order of effectiveness"* or *"Why is inherent safety the most preferred?"* — answer is that **each successive layer in the hierarchy costs more to install, more to maintain, and is less reliable** than the layer above it.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Each Type

The classification of safety design into four types is rooted in the **hierarchy of controls** doctrine, formalized by the **National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH)** and codified in **ISO 12100:2010 — Safety of machinery — General principles for design — Risk assessment and risk reduction**. The central idea is that **elimination of the hazard is always more robust than managing the hazard**. Below we dissect each of the four types.

---

### 2.1.1 TYPE 1 — Inherent Safety Design

**Philosophy:** *Make the hazard incapable of causing harm.* This is sometimes called **"primary"** or **"first-principle"** safety.

**Why "inherent"?** Because the safety property is built into the very physics, chemistry, or geometry of the system — it is **intrinsic** and cannot be "turned off" or "forgotten."

**Core Logic Steps (Kletz's 4 Minimization Principles):**

1. **Minimize (Intensify):** Use smaller inventories of hazardous materials. A tank with 100 L of gasoline is inherently safer than one with 100,000 L, *for the same reason a small fire is easier to survive than a large one.*
2. **Substitute:** Replace the hazardous substance with a less hazardous one. Example: water-based paints instead of solvent-based; CO₂ extinguishers instead of halogenated ones.
3. **Moderate (Attenuate):** Use the hazardous material under less hazardous conditions — lower temperature, lower pressure, lower concentration. Chlorine stored as a liquid under its own vapor pressure is more dangerous than chlorine stored as a hydrate or dilute solution.
4. **Simplify:** Eliminate unnecessary complexity. Fewer components, fewer pipes, fewer interlocks mean fewer failure modes. *"You can't leak from a pipe that doesn't exist."*

**Engineering Examples:**
- Replacing benzene (carcinogen) with cyclohexane in a chemical process.
- Designing a battery with solid-state electrolyte instead of liquid lithium-ion (eliminates fire risk).
- Using gravity-fed water supply instead of pumped, high-pressure systems for firefighting.

**Real-world utility:** Inherent safety is the **most cost-effective over the lifecycle** of a plant — you save on instrumentation, maintenance, training, insurance, and shutdown costs. However, it is often **the hardest to retrofit** into an existing design.

---

### 2.1.2 TYPE 2 — Passive Safety Design

**Philosophy:** *Add a permanent, static, fail-safe physical feature that does not need to "act" to provide protection.*

**Why "passive"?** Because the safety feature requires **no sensor, no logic, no power supply, and no human intervention** during the event. It works by virtue of its physical properties alone.

**Core Logic Steps:**

1. Identify a credible hazard scenario (e.g., overpressure in a vessel).
2. Select a passive physical principle that counters it (e.g., a relief device, a rupture disc, a crash structure, a fire-resistant coating).
3. Size and verify the feature using codes and standards (ASME BPVC, ISO 4126, etc.).
4. Confirm that the feature is fail-safe (it transitions to a safe state upon failure).

**Engineering Examples:**
- **Rupture disc** on a pressure vessel — bursts at a preset pressure, venting the overpressure.
- **Reinforced concrete containment** around a nuclear reactor (the "third barrier").
- **Crumple zones** in automobiles — they absorb kinetic energy during a crash.
- **Fire-resistant insulation** on structural steel — it conducts heat away and slows collapse.
- **Earthquake-resistant base isolators** in buildings.

**Real-world utility:** Passive safety is the **backbone of fail-safe engineering**. It is preferred over active safety wherever the *availability of power, sensors, or logic cannot be guaranteed* (e.g., during a power failure, a fire, or an explosion that destroys cables).

---

### 2.1.3 TYPE 3 — Active Safety Design

**Philosophy:** *Use sensors, logic solvers, and actuators to detect an unsafe condition and automatically take corrective action.*

**Why "active"?** Because the safety system must **perceive, decide, and act** in real time. It is dynamic and depends on the proper functioning of every component in the chain.

**Core Logic Steps (the "sense–decide–act" loop):**

1. **Sense:** A sensor (pressure transmitter, smoke detector, radar, accelerometer) measures a process variable.
2. **Decide:** A logic solver (PLC, SIS controller, ECU, microcontroller) compares the measurement against a safe threshold.
3. **Act:** An actuator (solenoid valve, relay, brake caliper, airbag inflator) modifies the process to bring it to a safe state.

**Engineering Examples:**
- **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** in a chemical plant: pressure transmitter → SIS controller → emergency shut-down valve. Rated **SIL 1 to SIL 3** per IEC 61511.
- **Antilock Braking System (ABS):** wheel-speed sensor → ECU → hydraulic modulator.
- **Airbag system:** accelerometer → airbag control unit → inflator.
- **Adaptive cruise control, automatic emergency braking, electronic stability program** in modern cars.

**Real-world utility:** Active safety handles **dynamic, fast, or complex** hazards that passive design cannot fully mitigate. However, it carries the risk of **common-cause failure** (e.g., the same fire that triggers the alarm can also destroy the controller). This is why active systems are usually backed up by passive systems (defense in depth).

---

### 2.1.4 TYPE 4 — Procedural Safety Design

**Philosophy:** *Control the hazard through human behavior, training, instructions, and administrative rules.*

**Why "procedural"?** Because the safety function is delegated to a **human operator or administrator** following a written procedure.

**Core Logic Steps:**

1. Identify the hazard and the human tasks that interact with it.
2. Write a clear, concise, validated Standard Operating Procedure (SOP).
3. Train, certify, and periodically re-train the operators.
4. Provide **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** as a last line of defense (helmets, gloves, goggles, respirators).
5. Enforce compliance with audits, permits, and management systems.

**Engineering Examples:**
- **Lockout–Tagout (LOTO)** procedures for maintenance of electrical/mechanical equipment.
- **Hot Work Permit** systems for welding in flammable atmospheres.
- **Confined Space Entry** permits with a stand-by attendant.
- **Hard hat, safety harness, ear plugs** — all procedural/PPE.

**Real-world utility:** Procedural safety is **the least reliable** of the four types because humans make mistakes, get fatigued, take shortcuts, and can be bypassed. However, it is **the only practical option** in many short-duration, non-routine, or unpredictable activities (e.g., emergency response, one-off maintenance, field inspections).

> [!WARNING]
> KTU students often list PPE as a "type." Strictly speaking, PPE is the **last layer** of procedural safety. If your answer only mentions PPE, you have lost 60% of the marks. Always frame procedural safety as **SOPs + Training + Permits + PPE** together.

---

## 2.2 The Quantitative Foundation — Risk Equation

The entire **"Types"** discussion is built on the idea that each type *reduces* the risk of an event. The basic risk equation is:

$$R = f \times C \times P$$

where:
- $R$ = Risk (a measure of the combination of harm frequency and severity).
- $f$ = Frequency of the hazardous event (events per year).
- $C$ = Consequence (severity) of the event, usually expressed in monetary loss, fatalities, or injury days.
- $P$ = Probability of the person (or asset) being present when the event occurs.

Each **type of safety design** attacks a different term in this equation:

| Type | Risk Term Reduced | Mechanism |
|------|-------------------|-----------|
| Inherent | $f$ and $C$ | Removes the hazard → frequency → 0, consequence → 0 |
| Passive | $C$ | Contains the energy/material → limits consequence |
| Active | $f$ | Prevents the event from occurring (e.g., shutdown before release) |
| Procedural | $P$ or $f$ (partial) | Reduces human exposure or operating errors |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **high-yield summary** for KTU board exams. Memorize the formulas and the boundaries.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Range | Type |
|---|---------|----------------------|--------------|------|
| 1 | Risk | $R = f \times C \times P$ | events·$/yr | All |
| 2 | Mean Time To Failure | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | hours | Active |
| 3 | Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) | $\text{PFD} = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \approx \lambda t$ for small $\lambda t$ | dimensionless, 0 to 1 | Active |
| 4 | Safety Integrity Level (SIL) | $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIL1}} \in [10^{-2}, 10^{-1}]$, $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIL2}} \in [10^{-3}, 10^{-2}]$, $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIL3}} \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-3}]$, $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIL4}} \in [10^{-5}, 10^{-4}]$ | dimensionless | Active |
| 5 | Risk Reduction Factor | $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}}$ | dimensionless | Active |
| 6 | Hazard Rate of standard instrument | $\lambda \approx 10^{-6}$ | per hour | Active |
| 7 | Available risk after n independent barriers | $R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{initial}} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_i$ | $/yr | All |
| 8 | Inherently Safer Design Order (effectiveness) | Inherent > Passive > Active > Procedural | — | Hierarchy |
| 9 | Kletz's 4 ISD Principles | Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify | — | Inherent |
| 10 | As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP) | $R \leq \text{“Gross disproportion” cost-benefit boundary}$ | — | Decision |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TRAP:** Never confuse **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** — which is a property of an **active** safety system — with the **Type of safety design**. SIL applies *only* to Type 3 (Active). Writing "this passive valve is SIL-2" is a guaranteed mark loss.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **Types** classification is not academic decoration. It is used daily by:

- **Chemical Engineers** to design inherently safer plants (Kletz's principles after the Bhopal disaster).
- **Mechanical Engineers** to choose between a passive guard (Type 2) and an interlocked door (Type 3) for a CNC machine (ISO 12100 / ANSI B11.19).
- **Civil/Structural Engineers** to design earthquake-resistant buildings: passive base isolation (Type 2) plus active dampers (Type 3) plus evacuation procedures (Type 4).
- **Automotive Engineers** to integrate crumple zones (Type 2), ABS (Type 3), and seatbelt laws (Type 4).
- **Software Engineers** to apply "secure-by-design" — eliminating vulnerabilities at the code level (inherent) rather than patching them later (procedural).
- **Aerospace Engineers** to use the **"design for safety" triangle** of inherent → passive → active → procedural when certifying aircraft per FAR Part 25.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Risk Reduction by Layered (Type-by-Type) Defense in Depth

In real engineering, a hazard is rarely protected by a single safety type. We **stack** types. This concept is called **defense in depth** (or the **Swiss-cheese model** by James Reason). The residual risk after $n$ independent safety layers is the product of the individual PFDs.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Let:
- $R_0$ = Initial (unmitigated) risk.
- $\text{PFD}_i$ = Probability of Failure on Demand of layer $i$.
- $n$ = number of independent safety layers (one per Type, in general).

The probability that **all** $n$ independent layers fail simultaneously is the product of their individual failure probabilities (probability of intersection of independent events).

$$
P(\text{all layers fail}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_i
$$

The **residual risk** is therefore:

$$
R_{\text{residual}} = R_0 \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_i
$$

**Numerical Example (a typical 14-mark KTU-style problem):**

A reactor has an unmitigated overpressure event frequency of $f_0 = 10^{-1}$ /year (once every 10 years). It is protected by three independent safety layers:
- Type 1 (Inherent): a process modification that lowers $f_0$ to $f_1$ (PFD effectively $\text{PFD}_1 = 0.1$).
- Type 2 (Passive): a rupture disc with PFD $= 10^{-2}$.
- Type 3 (Active): a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) at SIL 2 with PFD $= 10^{-2}$.
- Type 4 (Procedural): an operator inspection with PFD $= 10^{-1}$.

Calculate the residual frequency of a catastrophic overpressure event.

**Step 1:** Identify the PFDs of each layer.

$$
\text{PFD}_1 = 10^{-1}, \quad \text{PFD}_2 = 10^{-2}, \quad \text{PFD}_3 = 10^{-2}, \quad \text{PFD}_4 = 10^{-1}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the product of PFDs.

$$
\prod_{i=1}^{4} \text{PFD}_i = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1} = 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the residual risk (here = residual frequency).

$$
R_{\text{residual}} = f_0 \times \prod_{i=1}^{4} \text{PFD}_i = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-7} \;\text{events/year}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to a return period for intuition.

$$
T = \frac{1}{R_{\text{residual}}} = \frac{1}{10^{-7}} = 10^{7} \;\text{years}
$$

**Interpretation:** The reactor, originally expected to have a catastrophic event every 10 years, is now protected to a return period of 10 million years — a 6-order-of-magnitude risk reduction achieved by stacking the four types.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reliability of a Type 3 (Active) Safety System

For an **active** safety system, the probability of failure on demand over a time interval $t$ for a constant failure rate $\lambda$ is derived from the exponential reliability function.

**Step 1:** The reliability (probability of survival) of a component with constant failure rate $\lambda$ at time $t$ is:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 2:** The probability of failure on demand, assuming a single proof test at the end of the interval $t$, is:

$$
\text{PFD} = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 3:** For typical industrial instruments with $\lambda t \ll 1$ (e.g., $\lambda = 10^{-6}$ /hr, $t = 8760$ hr → $\lambda t = 8.76 \times 10^{-3}$), use the Taylor series expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x + \frac{x^2}{2!} - \dots$ and retain only the first two terms:

$$
e^{-\lambda t} \approx 1 - \lambda t
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the PFD equation.

$$
\text{PFD} = 1 - (1 - \lambda t) = \lambda t
$$

**Step 5:** Insert numerical values.

$$
\text{PFD} = (10^{-6}) \times (8760) = 8.76 \times 10^{-3} \approx 8.8 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 6:** Compare against the SIL bands from Section 2.3.

$$
\text{SIL 1}: \text{PFD} \in [10^{-2}, 10^{-1}]
$$
$$
\text{SIL 2}: \text{PFD} \in [10^{-3}, 10^{-2}]
$$

Since $8.8 \times 10^{-3}$ lies in $[10^{-3}, 10^{-2}]$, this single instrument qualifies as a **SIL 2** safety function.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Kletz's Hierarchy in Terms of Cost and Reliability

A useful analytical exercise is to express the **relative reliability** and **relative lifecycle cost** of each type. The table below is derived from industry data (CCPS, AIChE) and is a common KTU exam illustration.

| Type | Approx. Failure Probability (1 – reliability) | Approx. Relative Lifecycle Cost | Reason |
|------|----------------------------------------------|--------------------------------|--------|
| Inherent | $\approx 0$ (by design) | Lowest | Hazard removed; no system to fail |
| Passive | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ | Low | Static, no power, low maintenance |
| Active | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$ (SIL 1 to SIL 3) | High | Sensors, logic, actuators, proof testing |
| Procedural | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ | Variable (training, supervision) | Depends on human factors |

The **analytical insight** is that as you move down the hierarchy (from Inherent to Procedural), both the **failure probability rises** and the **lifecycle cost rises**. This is the mathematical justification for the slogan *"Inherent safety is the cheapest insurance you will ever buy."*

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Type

A safety engineer choosing among the four types for a given hazard uses the following decision matrix (also called the **"design safety wheel"**):

| Question | If YES → preferred type | If NO → fallback type |
|----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Can the hazard be eliminated at source? | **Inherent (Type 1)** | Go to next question |
| Can a static, fail-safe physical feature contain it? | **Passive (Type 2)** | Go to next question |
| Can sensors + logic + actuators reliably detect & respond? | **Active (Type 3)** | Go to next question |
| Is human intervention the only feasible option? | **Procedural (Type 4)** | Reject the design |

> [!TIP]
> Always exhaust the higher types first. The matrix above is the **exact algorithm** KTU examiners use to award full marks in design-for-safety case-study questions.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Inherently Safer Design (ISD) Hierarchy — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **interactions and dependencies** between the four types of safety design. It is a *functional architecture* (rather than a physical drawing), showing how each type is built on top of, and is *less reliable* than, the type above it.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Hazard Identified]) --> InherentCheck{Eliminate<br/>Hazard at Source?}
    InherentCheck -- YES --> Type1[Type 1<br/>Inherent Safety<br/>Substitute or Minimize]
    InherentCheck -- NO --> PassiveCheck{Add Static<br/>Physical Containment?}
    Type1 --> End1([Hazard Cannot Exist])

    PassiveCheck -- YES --> Type2[Type 2<br/>Passive Safety<br/>Rupture Disc Crumple Zone<br/>Blast Wall]
    PassiveCheck -- NO --> ActiveCheck{Sensor Logic Actuator<br/>Available and Reliable?}
    Type2 --> End2([Hazard Contained<br/>Without Power])

    ActiveCheck -- YES --> Type3[Type 3<br/>Active Safety<br/>SIS ABS Airbag<br/>SCADA Trip]
    ActiveCheck -- NO --> ProcCheck{Human Operator<br/>with SOP PPE Permit?}
    Type3 --> End3([Hazard Detected<br/>and Mitigated Automatically])

    ProcCheck -- YES --> Type4[Type 4<br/>Procedural Safety<br/>LOTO SOP Hot Work<br/>PPE and Training]
    ProcCheck -- NO --> Reject([Reject Design<br/>or Re-engineer])
    Type4 --> End4([Hazard Mitigated<br/>by Human Action])
```

**How to read this diagram (KTU examiner's tip):**
- Flow is **top-down**. Top boxes are the most preferred types.
- The diamond shapes are **decision nodes**.
- Each rectangular box represents a **Type of Safety Design**.
- The "End" rounded boxes represent **successful safety mitigation** of increasing (but still incomplete) residual risk.
- The "Reject" box is the **last resort** — if no type is feasible, the design is fundamentally unsafe and must be re-engineered.

---

## 4.2 The Swiss-Cheese Model — Mapping Types to Layers of Defense

A second, complementary way to visualize the four types is **James Reason's Swiss-Cheese Model**, where each slice of cheese is one type of safety design, and the holes represent the failures of that type. An accident occurs only when holes in **all** slices align.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Layer1[Layer 1 Inherent Safety]
        L1H[Hole 1<br/>Residual Hazard]
    end
    subgraph Layer2[Layer 2 Passive Safety]
        L2H[Hole 1<br/>Hole 2]
    end
    subgraph Layer3[Layer 3 Active Safety]
        L3H[Hole 1]
    end
    subgraph Layer4[Layer 4 Procedural Safety]
        L4H[Hole 1<br/>Hole 2<br/>Hole 3]
    end
    Accident([Accident]) --> Layer1 --> Layer2 --> Layer3 --> Layer4 --> Outcome([Safe Outcome])
    L1H -.-> L2H -.-> L3H -.-> L4H -.-> Accident
```

**Reading guide:** The dashed line shows a hypothetical accident trajectory. It "passes" through the holes of every layer, meaning **every type failed simultaneously**. The probability of this is the product of the individual layer PFDs, as derived in Section 3.1.

---

## 4.3 The Kletz 4-Principle Sub-Diagram (for Inherent Safety)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Inherent[Type 1 Inherent Safety]
    Inherent --> P1[Minimize<br/>Use Less Inventory]
    Inherent --> P2[Substitute<br/>Use Safer Material]
    Inherent --> P3[Moderate<br/>Use Milder Conditions]
    Inherent --> P4[Simplify<br/>Reduce Complexity]
```

This sub-diagram is the **complete ISD toolkit** that KTU Module 1 expects you to know.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**"List the four types of safety design strategies. Which one is considered the most effective, and why?"** **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The four types of safety design, in the order of their effectiveness (from most to least preferred), are:

1. **Inherent Safety Design** — eliminates the hazard at source.
2. **Passive Safety Design** — uses static, fail-safe physical features.
3. **Active Safety Design** — uses sensors, logic, and actuators (e.g., SIL-rated SIS).
4. **Procedural Safety Design** — relies on human operators, SOPs, training, and PPE.

**Inherent safety is the most effective** because it removes the hazard *physically, chemically, or geometrically* from the system. Since the hazard no longer exists, it cannot fail to be controlled — it has zero residual failure probability. It also has the lowest lifecycle cost, as no safety instrument, maintenance, or training is required. As Trevor Kletz famously stated, *"What you don't have, can't leak."*

*(Word count: ~95 words. Allocate ~1 mark per type + 1 mark for the explanation of why inherent is best. Total: 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**"Differentiate between Passive and Active safety design with one example each."** **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Passive Safety Design | Active Safety Design |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Power requirement | None — works by static physical principle | Requires power for sensors, logic, actuators |
| Trigger | No trigger needed; works continuously | Triggered by detection of an unsafe condition |
| Failure mode | Fail-safe (defaults to safe state) | Can fail dangerously if power/sensor is lost |
| Maintenance | Very low | Periodic proof testing required |
| Example | Rupture disc on a pressure vessel | Antilock Braking System (ABS) in a car |

*(1 mark for definition of passive, 1 mark for definition of active, 1 mark for one example each. Total: 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Model)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Model, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**(a)** With neat sketches/schematics, explain the four types of safety design strategies in detail. For each type, give **one industrial example** and state the **principle** on which it works. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A chemical reactor has an unmitigated runaway-reaction frequency of $5 \times 10^{-2}$ /year. It is protected by: (i) an inherent process modification with PFD $= 10^{-1}$; (ii) a passive rupture disc with PFD $= 10^{-2}$; and (iii) an active Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) at SIL 2 with PFD $= 10^{-3}$. Assuming the three layers are independent, compute the **residual event frequency** and the **return period** of the catastrophic event. Comment on whether the design is acceptable. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

The four types of safety design strategies, in the order of decreasing effectiveness, are:

**1. Inherent Safety Design** *(2 marks)*

- **Principle:** Eliminate the hazard at its source, so that it cannot cause harm. *“What you don’t have, can’t leak.”*
- **Industrial Example:** Replacing benzene (a carcinogen) with cyclohexane in a chemical extraction process; or storing chlorine as a dilute solution (moderation).
- **Sub-principles (Kletz’s 4):** Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify.

**2. Passive Safety Design** *(1.5 marks)*

- **Principle:** Use a permanent, static, fail-safe physical feature that mitigates the hazard without external power or human action.
- **Industrial Example:** A rupture disc on a pressure vessel — it bursts at a preset pressure, venting the overpressure before the vessel ruptures.

**3. Active Safety Design** *(1.5 marks)*

- **Principle:** Use a *sense → decide → act* chain. A sensor measures a process variable, a logic solver decides if it is unsafe, and an actuator corrects the process.
- **Industrial Example:** A Safety Instrumented System (SIS) in an oil refinery: pressure transmitter → SIL-2 controller → emergency shutdown valve.

**4. Procedural Safety Design** *(1.5 marks)*

- **Principle:** Control the hazard through human behavior — SOPs, training, permits, and PPE.
- **Industrial Example:** Hot Work Permit system before welding in a flammable atmosphere; operator wears fire-resistant coveralls and uses a fire watch.

*Conclusion:* The hierarchy of effectiveness is **Inherent > Passive > Active > Procedural**. Modern plants use all four in layered defense.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Unmitigated frequency: $f_0 = 5 \times 10^{-2}$ /year
- PFD of layer 1 (Inherent): $\text{PFD}_1 = 10^{-1}$
- PFD of layer 2 (Passive): $\text{PFD}_2 = 10^{-2}$
- PFD of layer 3 (Active): $\text{PFD}_3 = 10^{-3}$

**Step 1:** State the layered defense formula.

$$
R_{\text{residual}} = f_0 \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_i
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the product of PFDs.

$$
\prod_{i=1}^{3} \text{PFD}_i = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-3} = 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the residual event frequency.

$$
R_{\text{residual}} = (5 \times 10^{-2}) \times 10^{-6} = 5 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{events/year}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the return period.

$$
T = \frac{1}{R_{\text{residual}}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-8}} = 2 \times 10^{7} \;\text{years}
$$

**Step 5:** Comment on acceptability.

The design is **highly acceptable**. The catastrophic event is reduced from once every 20 years (unmitigated) to once every 20 million years (with the three layers). This exceeds typical industrial tolerability criteria (e.g., $10^{-6}$ /year for major-hazard plants per UK HSE), so the design is **ALARP-compliant** and the residual risk is in the *broadly acceptable* region of the risk-tolerability matrix.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice) — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Explain **Kletz’s four principles of Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** with one industrial example for each principle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A SIL-2 Safety Instrumented Function uses a single pressure transmitter with a failure rate $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ /hour. The proof-test interval is $T = 1$ year = **8760 hours**. Compute the **PFD** of the transmitter and verify that it falls in the SIL-2 range per **IEC 61511**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks**

Kletz’s four principles of Inherently Safer Design are:

1. **Minimize (Intensify)** — *(1.5 marks)* Use the smallest possible inventory of hazardous material. *Example:* Reducing the solvent inventory in a batch reactor from 10,000 L to 500 L by using a continuous-flow reactor. This reduces the maximum credible release by 20×.

2. **Substitute** — *(1.5 marks)* Replace a hazardous substance with a less hazardous one. *Example:* Replacing toluene (toxic, flammable) with a water-based cleaning agent in a paint-stripping process.

3. **Moderate (Attenuate)** — *(1.5 marks)* Use the hazardous substance under milder conditions (lower T, P, concentration). *Example:* Storing liquefied chlorine at sub-atmospheric pressure and refrigerated temperature, instead of pressurized ambient storage, so that a leak disperses as a gas cloud rather than a flashing liquid.

4. **Simplify** — *(1.5 marks)* Eliminate unnecessary complexity. *Example:* Replacing a complex network of swing-arm pipes, valves, and hoses at a tank-truck loading bay with a single bottom-loading arm and dry-break coupling — fewer leak paths, fewer operator errors.

*(Half-mark reserved for the introductory sentence and the concluding remark on why inherent safety is preferred.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Failure rate: $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr
- Proof-test interval: $T = 8760$ hr (1 year)
- Standard: IEC 61511

**Step 1:** State the PFD formula for a single component with constant failure rate, single proof test.

$$
\text{PFD} = 1 - e^{-\lambda T}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$
\text{PFD} = 1 - e^{-(5 \times 10^{-6}) \times (8760)}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the exponent.

$$
\lambda T = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 4.38 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the exponential.

$$
e^{-0.0438} \approx 0.9572
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the PFD.

$$
\text{PFD} = 1 - 0.9572 = 0.0428 \approx 4.28 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 6:** Verify the SIL band per IEC 61511.

| SIL | PFD Range |
|-----|-----------|
| 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ |
