The computed PFD $= 4.28 \times 10^{-2} = 4.28 \times 10^{-2}$ lies in the range $[10^{-2}, 10^{-1}]$, which corresponds to **SIL 1**, **not SIL 2**.

**Step 7:** Conclusion.

A **single** transmitter with $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$/hr is **not adequate for SIL 2** at a 1-year proof-test interval. To achieve SIL 2, the engineer must either:
- Use a **1oo2 (one-out-of-two) redundant** architecture, which lowers the effective PFD by an order of magnitude, or
- Reduce the proof-test interval to $T \leq 2000$ hr, or
- Select a transmitter with a lower failure rate ($\lambda \leq 10^{-6}$ /hr).

*(1 mark for each of the 7 steps, including the conclusion.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks on "Types of Safety Design" questions:**
>
> 1. **Listing only PPE as Type 4.** You must include SOPs, training, and permits — PPE is only the *last* layer of procedural safety. *(Lose up to 2 marks.)*
> 2. **Confusing SIL with the "Type".** SIL is a *property* of an active safety system; it is not a separate "type." Writing "SIL design" as a fifth type will be marked wrong. *(Lose up to 1 mark.)*
> 3. **Stating the hierarchy backwards.** The order is **Inherent → Passive → Active → Procedural** (most to least effective). Reversing it is a fundamental conceptual error. *(Lose up to 2 marks.)*
> 4. **Skipping the example.** For each type, you must give *at least one* industrial example. A definition without an example is half an answer. *(Lose up to 1 mark per type.)*
> 5. **Mixing hazard types with design types.** "Biological hazard" is a *hazard type*, not a *design type*. KTU Module 1 "Types" refers to **design strategies**, not hazards. *(Lose up to 2 marks.)*
> 6. **Calculation errors in PFD.** The Taylor approximation $\text{PFD} \approx \lambda T$ is valid only for $\lambda T \ll 1$. Always state the approximation and verify $\lambda T < 0.1$. *(Lose up to 1 mark in numerical problems.)*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-second revision for KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 1, Topic: Types**

- The **four types of safety design** are: **Inherent, Passive, Active, Procedural** — in that order of effectiveness.
- **Inherent safety** removes the hazard; **Kletz's 4 principles** are Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify.
- **Passive safety** is a static, fail-safe, no-power feature (rupture disc, crumple zone, blast wall).
- **Active safety** is the *sense → decide → act* loop (SIS, ABS, airbag, sprinkler).
- **Procedural safety** is SOP + Training + Permit + PPE (in that order, with PPE last).
- The **basic risk equation** is $R = f \times C \times P$ — each type attacks a different term.
- **Defense in depth** stacks independent layers: $R_{\text{residual}} = R_0 \times \prod \text{PFD}_i$.
- **SIL** is a property of *active* systems only — **SIL 1 to SIL 4**, PFD ranges are $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-5}$.
- **PFD for a single component:** $\text{PFD} = 1 - e^{-\lambda T} \approx \lambda T$ (for $\lambda T \ll 1$).
- **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable — the decision rule for accepting residual risk.
- Always **start with inherent, end with procedural.** A design that relies *only* on procedural safety is unsafe and will fail the design review.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Batch Reactors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Batch Reactors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Batch Reactor** is a closed, well-mixed, transient (unsteady-state) vessel in which all reactants are charged at the beginning of the cycle, allowed to react for a specified residence time under controlled conditions, and then discharged as products at the end of the cycle. From the **Design for Safety** perspective, a batch reactor is treated as an **adiabatic-to-isothermal spectrum hazard unit**, where the entire inventory of reactive material resides in the vessel simultaneously, making it the most accident-prone reactor configuration in the process industry.

Mathematically, for a single irreversible reaction $A \rightarrow Products$, the operating constraint is expressed as:

$$
t = C_{A0} \int_{0}^{X_{Af}} \frac{dX_{A}}{-r_{A}}
$$

where $-r_A$ is the rate of disappearance of $A$, $X_{Af}$ is the final conversion, $C_{A0}$ is the initial molar concentration, and $t$ is the batch cycle time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:** In safety design literature, batch reactors are classified as **"High Inventory / Low Throughput"** units. The complete inventory of unreacted material is exposed to potential runaway conditions at all times, which fundamentally differentiates them from continuous or semi-batch systems.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **pressure cooker on a domestic gas stove**:
- All ingredients (reactants) are placed inside at once and the lid is sealed shut.
- Cooking proceeds for a fixed time (the "batch time") under heat input.
- If the heat is too high or the reaction (cooking) is strongly exothermic, temperature can rise uncontrollably — the lid pops off (a relief event) or the vessel explodes.
- Once cooking is done, the lid is opened and the contents (products) are removed; then the cycle begins again.

A batch chemical reactor behaves identically: charge → react → discharge. The **closed lid** represents the safety challenge because energy and pressure cannot easily escape — this is precisely why batch reactors dominate thermal runaway incident statistics globally (e.g., the Bhopal, Flixborough, and Seveso incidents all involved batch or semi-batch reactors).

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold):**
> - Typical industrial batch size: **1 m³ to 50 m³**
> - Standard design pressure rating: **Vacuum to 40 bar**
> - Batch cycle duration: **2 hours to 48 hours**
> - Heat transfer area-to-volume ratio: **0.5 to 5 m²/m³**

## Three Defining Features for Safety Analysis

1. **Closed Boundary (No Inlet/Outlet during reaction):** All mass is confined. A runaway cannot be "diluted out" by adding cold reactants.
2. **Transient Operation:** Composition, temperature, and pressure change with time. The system continuously approaches unsafe states if not monitored.
3. **Total Inventory Exposure:** The entire charge of unconverted reactants is available to release energy — making the **Maximum Credible Accident (MCA)** potentially catastrophic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Batch Reactor Behaviour

### Step 1 — Why Batch Reactors Are Inherently Riskier
- The full charge of reactants is present simultaneously.
- No mass-flow cooling effect (unlike CSTR) and no spatial cooling effect (unlike PFR).
- For exothermic reactions, the **adiabatic temperature rise** can drive the system into a thermal runaway, where reaction rate accelerates faster than heat can be removed.

### Step 2 — Energy Generation vs. Energy Removal
A runaway occurs when:

$$
q_{\text{gen}} > q_{\text{rem}}
$$

where:
- $q_{\text{gen}} = (-\Delta H_r) \cdot (-r_A) \cdot V$ — heat released by the reaction
- $q_{\text{rem}} = U \cdot A \cdot (T - T_{\text{coolant}})$ — heat removed through the jacket

If the temperature rises, both sides increase, but $q_{\text{gen}}$ rises **exponentially** (Arrhenius) while $q_{\text{rem}}$ rises only **linearly**. The crossover defines the **Semenov ignition temperature**.

### Step 3 — Why We Quantify the Adiabatic Temperature Rise
The **$\Delta T_{\text{ad}}$** tells the safety engineer the *worst-case* temperature achievable if all cooling fails. It is the single most important safety metric for batch reactors.

### Step 4 — Why TMR is the Most Critical Time Metric
**Time to Maximum Rate (TMR)** is the time available for human intervention before runaway becomes uncontrollable. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats TMR as the central safety time parameter.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula No. | Equation | Description | Units |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|
| F-1 | $t = C_{A0} \int_{0}^{X_{Af}} \frac{dX_{A}}{-r_{A}}$ | Design equation for constant-volume batch reactor | s |
| F-2 | $-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -r_A = k\,C_A^n$ | Rate law (general $n$-th order) | mol/(L·s) |
| F-3 | $k = A\,e^{-E/RT}$ | Arrhenius rate constant | s⁻¹ or L/(mol·s) |
| F-4 | $\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \frac{(-\Delta H_r)\,C_{A0}}{\rho\,C_p}$ | Adiabatic temperature rise (per mole basis) | K |
| F-5 | $\text{TMR} = \frac{C_p\,R\,T_0^{\,2}}{q_0\,E}$ | Time to Maximum Rate (Townsend approximation) | s |
| F-6 | $q_0 = (-\Delta H_r)\,k_0\,C_{A0}\,V$ | Initial heat generation rate | W |
| F-7 | $T_{\text{crit}} = T_0 + \Delta T_{\text{ad}}$ | Maximum reachable temperature under total cooling failure | K |
| F-8 | $P_{\text{crit}} = P_0 \exp\!\left[\frac{\Delta H_v}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_b} - \frac{1}{T_{\text{crit}}}\right)\right]$ | Worst-case vessel pressure (Antoine-based) | Pa |
| F-9 | $\theta = \frac{t_{\text{cool}}}{t_{\text{reaction}}}$ | Heat removal effectiveness ratio | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These nine equations are sufficient to size a relief device, justify a safe charge limit, and define the safe operating envelope for nearly any exothermic batch reactor encountered in KTU examination questions or industrial practice.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

In the chemical process industry, batch reactor safety calculations govern:
- **Pharmaceutical API synthesis** (high-potency exothermic reactions)
- **Polymerization reactors** (radical runaway risk — e.g., methyl methacrylate)
- **Nitration processes** (TNT, nitroglycerin production)
- **Fine chemicals & dyes** (multi-step batch sequences)

The same equations are used by **OSHA Process Safety Management (PSM)** auditors to verify the **Thermal Hazard Assessment** of every covered process.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1 — Design Equation for a Constant-Volume Batch Reactor

**Step 1: Write the mass balance on component A.**

For a closed batch system, there is no inlet or outlet flow. By conservation of mass:

$$
\text{Rate of accumulation} = \text{Rate of generation}
$$

$$
\frac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A \cdot V
$$

**Step 2: Convert moles to concentration using constant volume.**

Since $V$ is constant, $N_A = C_A V$, so:

$$
V \frac{dC_A}{dt} = r_A \cdot V
$$

$$
\frac{dC_A}{dt} = r_A
$$

**Step 3: Introduce the definition of fractional conversion.**

By definition of conversion:

$$
X_A = \frac{C_{A0} - C_A}{C_{A0}} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)
$$

**Step 4: Differentiate and substitute.**

Differentiating $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ with respect to time:

$$
\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -C_{A0}\,\frac{dX_A}{dt}
$$

Substituting into the mass balance:

$$
-C_{A0}\,\frac{dX_A}{dt} = r_A
$$

**Step 5: Separate variables and integrate.**

$$
dt = -C_{A0}\,\frac{dX_A}{r_A} = C_{A0}\,\frac{dX_A}{-r_A}
$$

Integrating from $t = 0$ (where $X_A = 0$) to $t$ (where $X_A = X_{Af}$):

$$
\boxed{\;t = C_{A0} \int_{0}^{X_{Af}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}\;}
$$

This is the **fundamental batch reactor design equation** used throughout chemical reaction engineering and safety design.

## Derivation 2 — Adiabatic Temperature Rise ($\Delta T_{\text{ad}}$)

**Step 1: Write the energy balance on the batch reactor.**

For an adiabatic system ($Q = 0$) at constant volume:

$$
V \rho C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = (-\Delta H_r)\,(-r_A)\,V
$$

**Step 2: Introduce the conversion variable to convert time integration into a temperature function.**

From the mass balance, $dt = C_{A0}\,dX_A / (-r_A)$. Substitute:

$$
V \rho C_p \frac{dT}{C_{A0}\,dX_A / (-r_A)} = (-\Delta H_r)\,(-r_A)\,V
$$

Simplify:

$$
\frac{\rho C_p}{C_{A0}}\,\frac{dT}{dX_A} = (-\Delta H_r)
$$

**Step 3: Separate and integrate.**

$$
dT = \frac{(-\Delta H_r)\,C_{A0}}{\rho C_p}\,dX_A
$$

Integrating from $T_0$ to $T$ as $X_A$ goes from 0 to $X_A$:

$$
T - T_0 = \frac{(-\Delta H_r)\,C_{A0}}{\rho C_p}\,X_A
$$

**Step 4: Define the adiabatic temperature rise at complete conversion ($X_A = 1$).**

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \frac{(-\Delta H_r)\,C_{A0}}{\rho\,C_p}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** $\Delta T_{\text{ad}}$ is the **maximum possible temperature excursion** if all cooling fails and the reaction goes to completion. It is independent of kinetics — only thermodynamic and loading properties matter. This is why safety engineers compute it **first**, before any kinetic analysis.

## Derivation 3 — Time to Maximum Rate (TMR)

**Step 1: Recognize that the maximum rate occurs at the temperature where $dq_{\text{gen}}/dT = dq_{\text{rem}}/dT$.**

For an adiabatic batch (no removal), $q_{\text{rem}} = 0$. The rate of temperature rise is:

$$
\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{(-\Delta H_r)\,k\,C_A}{\rho C_p}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Arrhenius and recognize the structure.**

$$
\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{(-\Delta H_r)\,A\,e^{-E/RT}\,C_A}{\rho C_p}
$$

The "maximum rate" point is the **inflection** where $d(dT/dt)/dT = 0$:

$$
\frac{d^2T}{dt^2} = \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{dT}{dt}\right) = 0
$$

**Step 3: Carry out the differentiation using the chain rule.**

Let $q = dT/dt$. Then:

$$
\frac{dq}{dT} = \frac{(-\Delta H_r)\,A\,C_A}{\rho C_p}\cdot\frac{d}{dT}\!\left(e^{-E/RT}\right) + \frac{(-\Delta H_r)\,A\,e^{-E/RT}}{\rho C_p}\cdot\frac{dC_A}{dT}
$$

Compute each derivative:

$$
\frac{d}{dT}\!\left(e^{-E/RT}\right) = e^{-E/RT}\!\left(\frac{E}{RT^2}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{dC_A}{dT} = \frac{dC_A/dt}{dT/dt} = \frac{r_A}{q} = \frac{-A\,e^{-E/RT}\,C_A}{q}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the condition $dq/dT = 0$.**

After substituting and simplifying:

$$
q = \frac{(-\Delta H_r)\,A\,e^{-E/RT}\,C_A}{\rho C_p}
$$

The condition $dq/dT = 0$ yields, for an adiabatic system, the **Townsend relation**:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{TMR} = \frac{\rho\,C_p\,R\,T^{\,2}}{(-\Delta H_r)\,A\,e^{-E/RT}\,C_A\,E}\;}
$$

**Step 5: Practical approximation using initial conditions.**

For an early estimate (using $T_0$ and $C_{A0}$):

$$
\boxed{\;\text{TMR} \approx \frac{\rho\,C_p\,R\,T_0^{\,2}}{q_0\,E}\;}
$$

where $q_0 = (-\Delta H_r)\,A\,e^{-E/RT_0}\,C_{A0}\,V$ is the initial heat generation rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** A TMR greater than **24 hours** is generally considered "safe to handle"; between **8 and 24 hours** requires active monitoring; below **8 hours** demands automated interlocks and emergency cooling; below **1 hour** mandates a complete redesign toward semi-batch or continuous operation.

## Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A batch reactor runs an irreversible first-order liquid-phase reaction at the following conditions:

$$
C_{A0} = 3.0 \;\text{kmol/m}^3, \quad (-\Delta H_r) = 8.0 \times 10^4 \;\text{kJ/kmol}
$$

$$
\rho = 950 \;\text{kg/m}^3, \quad C_p = 3.0 \;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}, \quad k_0 = 7.0 \times 10^{10} \;\text{s}^{-1}
$$

$$
E = 7.5 \times 10^4 \;\text{J/mol}, \quad T_0 = 320 \;\text{K}
$$

**Step 1 — Adiabatic temperature rise:**

$$
\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \frac{(8.0 \times 10^4)\,(3.0)}{(950)\,(3.0)} = 84.2 \;\text{K}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum reachable temperature:**

$$
T_{\text{crit}} = 320 + 84.2 = 404.2 \;\text{K}
$$

**Step 3 — Initial heat generation rate (per unit volume):**

$$
q_0 = (8.0 \times 10^4)\,(7.0 \times 10^{10})\,e^{-75000/(8.314 \times 320)}\,(3.0)
$$

Compute the exponential:

$$
e^{-75000/(8.314 \times 320)} = e^{-28.20} \approx 5.81 \times 10^{-13}
$$

Then:

$$
q_0 \approx (8.0 \times 10^4)\,(7.0 \times 10^{10})\,(5.81 \times 10^{-13})\,(3.0) \approx 9.76 \;\text{kJ/(m}^3\!\cdot\!\text{s)}
$$

**Step 4 — Time to Maximum Rate:**

$$
\text{TMR} \approx \frac{(950)\,(3.0)\,(8.314)\,(320)^2}{(9.76)\,(75000)} \approx 3.32 \times 10^{4} \;\text{s} \approx 9.2 \;\text{hours}
$$

**Conclusion:** The reactor has a TMR of ~9 hours — within the "active monitoring" zone but **below** the 24-hour safe-handling threshold, requiring engineered safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Figure 4.1 — Batch Reactor Operational Cycle (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start of Cycle] --> B1[Charge Reactant A and B]
    B1 --> C1[Heat to Reaction Temperature]
    C1 --> D1[Hold under Controlled T and P]
    D1 --> E1{Reaction Complete?}
    E1 -- No --> F1[Continue Heating or Cooling]
    F1 --> D1
    E1 -- Yes --> G1[Cool to Discharge Temperature]
    G1 --> H1[Discharge Product]
    H1 --> I1[Clean Vessel in Place CIP]
    I1 --> A1
    D1 -.-> J1{Safety Trigger}
    J1 -- Hi Temp --> K1[Emergency Cooling On]
    J1 -- Hi Press --> L1[Relief Valve Opens]
    J1 -- TMR Breach --> M1[Quench with Solvent]
```

## Figure 4.2 — Hazard Hierarchy: Batch vs. Semi-Batch vs. CSTR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BAT[Batch Reactor]
        BA1[Full inventory charged at t=0]
        BA2[No inlet cooling feed]
        BA3[High runaway potential]
    end
    subgraph SEMI[Semi-Batch Reactor]
        SB1[One reactant added gradually]
        SB2[Feed acts as controlled dilution]
        SB3[Lower peak temperature]
    end
    subgraph CSTRR[CSTR Continuous]
        CS1[Steady-state composition]
        CS2[Only small holdup exposed]
        CS3[Safest under normal control]
    end
    BAT -->|Inherently less safe| SEMI
    SEMI -->|Inherently safer| CSTRR
```

## Figure 4.3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Batch Reactor Safety Interlocks

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[Reactor Vessel with Jacket] --> S1[Temperature Sensor T1]
    IN1 --> S2[Pressure Sensor P1]
    IN1 --> S3[Level Sensor L1]
    S1 --> PLC1[PLC / SIS Logic Solver]
    S2 --> PLC1
    S3 --> PLC1
    PLC1 --> ACT1[Jacket Cooling Valve]
    PLC1 --> ACT2[Emergency Dump Valve]
    PLC1 --> ACT3[Relief Device Rupture Disc]
    PLC1 --> ACT4[Quench Solvent Valve]
    PLC1 --> ALM1[Audible and Visual Alarm]
    PLC1 --> HMI1[Operator HMI Display]
```

## Figure 4.4 — Adiabatic Runaway Trajectory (Qualitative Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Normal Operating Point T0, X0] --> P2[Loss of Cooling Event]
    P2 --> P3[Rising Temperature along Adiabatic Curve]
    P3 --> P4{TMR Reached}
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Rapid Acceleration Beyond Self-Cooling Capacity]
    P5 --> P6[Thermal Runaway and Possible Rupture]
    P4 -- No with intervention --> P7[Restored Cooling Path]
    P7 --> P1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Each node in the diagrams above corresponds to a real physical component or decision point. In a KTU examination, students are expected to label these blocks, identify which one represents the **Inherent Safety Improvement** opportunity, and explain why a semi-batch or continuous configuration shifts the system from Node P4 toward Node P7.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *Why is a batch reactor considered more hazardous than a continuous reactor?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A batch reactor charges the **entire inventory** of reactive material at the beginning of the cycle, leaving the full mass exposed to potential runaway throughout the reaction. There is no inlet dilution effect (as in CSTR) and no continuous removal of unreacted material. Consequently, if cooling fails, the adiabatic temperature rise $\Delta T_{\text{ad}}$ is realized in full, leading to thermal runaway. The TMR may fall below safe intervention thresholds. Continuous reactors, by contrast, operate at steady state with only a small in-vessel holdup, drastically reducing the maximum credible accident energy.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *Define Time to Maximum Rate (TMR) and state its safety significance.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
TMR is the time elapsed between the loss of cooling in an adiabatic batch reactor and the moment the reaction rate reaches its peak. It is calculated from the Townsend equation:

$$
\text{TMR} = \frac{\rho\,C_p\,R\,T_0^{\,2}}{q_0\,E}
$$

**Safety significance:** TMR defines the window available for human or automated intervention. A short TMR (< 1 hour) signals an inherently unsafe process; a long TMR (> 24 hours) indicates the process can be safely managed with standard controls. TMR is the cornerstone of **thermal hazard assessment** under KTU 2024 safety design practice.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Derive the design equation for a constant-volume batch reactor, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Assumptions:** (i) Perfect mixing; (ii) Constant density and volume; (iii) Single liquid-phase reaction; (iv) No inlet or outlet flow during reaction.
2. **Mass balance on A:**

$$
\frac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A V
$$

3. **Substitute $N_A = C_A V$ and use constant volume:**

$$
\frac{dC_A}{dt} = r_A
$$

4. **Introduce conversion $X_A = (C_{A0} - C_A)/C_{A0}$:**

$$
\frac{dX_A}{dt} = \frac{-r_A}{C_{A0}}
$$

5. **Integrate from $X_A = 0$ to $X_{Af}$:**

$$
t = C_{A0}\int_{0}^{X_{Af}}\frac{dX_A}{-r_A}
$$

6. **For first-order kinetics** $-r_A = k C_A = k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$:

$$
t = \frac{-\ln(1 - X_{Af})}{k}
$$

**(b)** A liquid-phase batch reactor carries out a first-order exothermic reaction with the following data: $C_{A0} = 2.5$ kmol/m³, $k = 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$ s⁻¹ at 350 K, $E = 8.0 \times 10^4$ J/mol, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $C_p = 3.5$ kJ/(kg·K), $(-\Delta H_r) = 9.0 \times 10^4$ kJ/kmol. Calculate (i) the adiabatic temperature rise, (ii) the maximum reachable temperature, and (iii) the initial heat generation rate per unit volume. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Adiabatic Temperature Rise:**

$$
\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \frac{(9.0 \times 10^4)\,(2.5)}{(1000)\,(3.5)} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^5}{3500} = 64.3 \;\text{K}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Temperature:**

$$
T_{\text{crit}} = T_0 + \Delta T_{\text{ad}} = 350 + 64.3 = 414.3 \;\text{K}
$$

**(iii) Initial Heat Generation Rate:**

$$
q_0 = (-\Delta H_r)\,k\,C_{A0} = (9.0 \times 10^4)\,(4.0 \times 10^{-3})\,(2.5) = 900 \;\text{kJ/(m}^3\!\cdot\!\text{s)}
$$

**Inference:** With $q_0 = 900$ kJ/(m³·s), this is an extremely energetic reaction requiring vigorous cooling and engineered interlocks.

### Question B (Choice 2) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled diagram, explain the operational cycle of a batch reactor and identify the THREE stages at which the highest accident probability occurs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A batch reactor operates in a closed cycle: **Charge → Heat-up → Reaction Hold → Cool-down → Discharge → Cleaning**. The three stages of highest accident probability are:

1. **Charge Stage:** Mixing of reactants in incorrect order can trigger uncontrolled exotherms (e.g., water into acid).
2. **Reaction Hold Stage:** If cooling fails, runaway develops within the residence time window.
3. **Discharge Stage:** Unreacted material may still be present; exposure to air or moisture can cause secondary reactions.

A labelled schematic was expected — refer to **Figure 4.1** above. Full marks require identification of all three stages plus a labelled sketch.

**(b)** Using the Townsend relation, explain how **TMR scales with temperature**. A reaction has $E = 7.0 \times 10^4$ J/mol. If the TMR is 8 hours at 330 K, estimate the TMR at 340 K using the sensitivity approximation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**TMR Sensitivity Relation:**

Differentiating the Townsend relation logarithmically:

$$
\ln(\text{TMR}) \approx \text{const} + \frac{E}{RT}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{\text{TMR}_2}{\text{TMR}_1}\right) = \frac{E}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)
$$

**Plug in values:**

$$
\frac{E}{R} = \frac{7.0 \times 10^4}{8.314} = 8420 \;\text{K}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{340} - \frac{1}{330} = 2.941 \times 10^{-3} - 3.030 \times 10^{-3} = -8.96 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{\text{TMR}_2}{8}\right) = 8420 \times (-8.96 \times 10^{-5}) = -0.754
$$

$$
\frac{\text{TMR}_2}{8} = e^{-0.754} = 0.471
$$

$$
\text{TMR}_2 \approx 8 \times 0.471 = 3.77 \;\text{hours}
$$

**Inference:** A 10 K rise in operating temperature **roughly halves** the TMR — demonstrating the extreme temperature sensitivity of batch reactor safety margins.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units Mixing:** Students frequently substitute $C_p$ in J/(kg·K) while $(-\Delta H_r)$ is in kJ/kmol — leading to a 1000-fold error in $\Delta T_{\text{ad}}$. Always normalise units **before** substitution.
> 2. **Confusing TMR with Half-Life:** TMR is **not** a kinetic half-life; it is the time to the inflection point of $dT/dt$ under adiabatic conditions.
> 3. **Forgetting Volume Constancy:** The batch design equation $t = C_{A0}\int dX_A / (-r_A)$ is valid **only** when $V$ is constant. For gas-phase non-ideal batches, the volumetric change must be included.
> 4. **Skipping the Sign Convention:** The reaction rate $-r_A$ is positive by convention. Forgetting the negative sign in $dC_A/dt = -r_A$ is the single most common mistake in KTU scripts.
> 5. **Ignoring $\rho C_p$ vs $C_p$:** The formula $\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = (-\Delta H_r)C_{A0}/C_p$ applies only when the heat capacity is expressed **on a per-mole-of-mixture basis**; otherwise the volumetric form with $\rho C_p$ must be used.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A batch reactor is a closed, unsteady-state vessel where all reactants are charged simultaneously.
- **Why unsafe:** Full inventory exposure + no feed dilution + transient operation = high runaway probability.
- **Design Equation:** $t = C_{A0}\int_{0}^{X_{Af}} dX_A / (-r_A)$ — derived from the mass balance under constant volume.
- **Adiabatic Temperature Rise:** $\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = (-\Delta H_r)C_{A0}/(\rho C_p)$ — the worst-case temperature if cooling fails.
- **Critical Temperature:** $T_{\text{crit}} = T_0 + \Delta T_{\text{ad}}$ — sets the relief device sizing temperature.
- **Time to Maximum Rate (TMR):** TMR $= \rho C_p R T_0^{2}/(q_0 E)$ — defines the safety intervention window.
- **TMR Sensitivity:** TMR roughly halves for every 10 K rise in operating temperature (for typical activation energies).
- **Safety Thresholds:** TMR > 24 h (safe); 8–24 h (monitor); 1–8 h (interlocks required); < 1 h (redesign to semi-batch).
- **Inherent Safety Hierarchy:** Batch → Semi-Batch → CSTR — moving from least safe to most safe configuration.
- **Practical Rule:** Always compute $\Delta T_{\text{ad}}$ **first**; if it exceeds 50 K or the TMR is below 8 h, the design requires engineered safeguards (relief, dump, quench, or semi-batch conversion).
- **KTU Favourite Topics for Batch Reactors:** adiabatic temperature rise derivation, TMR calculation, and batch-vs-semi-batch hazard comparison.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reaction Hazard Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Reaction Hazard Evaluation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reaction Hazard Evaluation (RHE)** is the systematic, scientific, and engineering assessment of the *potential undesirable chemical reactions* (decomposition, runaway polymerization, gas evolution, side reactions, thermal instability) that a process stream, intermediate, or product may undergo under realistic processing, storage, and transport conditions. It quantifies the *thermal, pressure, and kinetic severity* of these reactions so that safe operating limits, relief sizing, and emergency procedures can be designed. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance (PESFT412 – Design for Safety), Reaction Hazard Evaluation sits at the intersection of **Process Safety Management (PSM)** and **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)**. It is the *first analytical step* before any Process Flow Diagram (PFD), Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID), or Pressure Relief Device (PRD) sizing is finalised. The three pillars of Reaction Hazard Evaluation are: 1. **Thermal Hazard** – energy released (exotherms), runaway potential, SADT. 2. **Pressure Hazard** – gas/vapour generation, closed-vessel pressure rise. 3. **Gas Hazard** – flammable, toxic, or asphyxiant gas evolution. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy — "The Pressure Cooker That Forgot to Whistle"** > > Imagine a pressure cooker on a stove. As long as the safety whistle releases steam steadily, energy escapes and the temperature stays bounded — *no runaway*. Now imagine the whistle is *blocked*. Heat keeps arriving. Inside, water boils faster, vapour pressure soars, and the cooker wall heats up. The vessel itself now stores more thermal energy (because steel has mass and heat capacity). This stored energy makes the system "remember" past heat input and continues reacting even if you turn the stove off. **That "memory" is the thermal inertia (φ-factor)**, and **the moment the heat-in equals heat-out balance breaks is runaway**. > > Reaction Hazard Evaluation is the engineering discipline that *predicts* when the whistle will block — i.e., when a stirred-tank reactor, a storage drum, or a shipping container of an unstable chemical will depart from controlled operation and enter runaway. ### 1.3 Why RHE is Mandatory in KTU / Process Industries > [!IMPORTANT] > **Global incident lessons (industry gold standards):** > - **Bhopal, India (1984)** – Methyl Isocyanate (MIC) runaway with water → thousands of fatalities. > - **Seveso, Italy (1976)** – 2,4,5-trichlorophenol exothermic decomposition. > - **AZF Toulouse, France (2001)** – Ammonium nitrate decomposition. > - **Texas City Refinery (2005)** – HF alkylation unit hydrocarbon runaway. > > Each of these disasters had, in retrospect, *predictable* thermal/pressure hazards that a proper Reaction Hazard Evaluation would have flagged. ### 1.4 Key Quantitative Metrics (bolded as KTU syllabus constants) - **Adiabatic Temperature Rise (ΔT_ad)** — the maximum temperature a closed reacting mass can reach if no heat is removed. - **Time to Maximum Rate (TMR_ad)** — the time, after loss of cooling, for the runaway reaction to reach its peak rate. - **Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature (SADT)** — the lowest ambient temperature at which self-accelerating decomposition of a substance occurs in a typical commercial package. - **Reaction Hazard Index (RHI)** — a dimensionless severity score combining ΔT_ad and the heat of reaction. - **Phi Factor (φ)** — ratio of total heat capacity to reactant heat capacity; measures "thermal inertia" of the vessel+contents. - **Activation Energy (E)** — energy barrier for the runaway reaction. - **Universal Gas Constant R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Logic Chain of a Reaction Hazard Evaluation A proper RHE is performed in **six sequential logical steps**: 1. **Reaction Identification** — list all possible reactions (desired, side, decomposition). 2. **Calorimetric Measurement** — obtain ΔH (heat of reaction), onset temperature T_onset, and kinetics using DSC, ARC, or RSD. 3. **Adiabatic Extrapolation** — correct laboratory data to plant scale using the φ-factor. 4. **Severity Estimation** — compute ΔT_ad and adiabatic pressure rise (ΔP_ad). 5. **Kinetic / Probability Estimation** — compute TMR_ad at process temperature. 6. **Risk Ranking & Decision** — plot on a criticality diagram, choose mitigation (cooling, venting, ISD, dilution). > [!NOTE] > **Why all six steps?** > Severity (Steps 4) tells you *how bad* it gets; Probability (Step 5) tells you *how likely* it is. **Risk = Severity × Probability**. Industry typically plots severity on the x-axis and probability (TMR_ad) on the y-axis to decide if a process is *acceptable, marginal, or critical*. ### 2.2 The Six Calorimetric Techniques Used in RHE | # | Technique | Full Form | What It Measures | KTU Tag | |---|-----------|-----------|------------------|---------| | 1 | **DSC** | Differential Scanning Calorimetry | ΔH and T_onset (small sample, mg) | Screening | | 2 | **DTA** | Differential Thermal Analysis | Exotherm/endotherm identification | Screening | | 3 | **ARC** | Accelerating Rate Calorimetry | Adiabatic self-heat rate vs. T | Detailed | | 4 | **RSD** | Reaction System Diagnosing / Dewar Calorimetry | φ-corrected adiabatic data | Detailed | | 5 | **VSP** | Vent Sizing Package | Adiabatic pressure rise for relief | Detailed | | 6 | **Phi-Tec** | Phi-Tec adiabatic calorimeter | Small-φ adiabatic kinetics | Detailed | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Meaning / Application | Standard Units | |---|----------|----------------------|----------------| | 1 | $\Delta T_{ad} \;=\; -\dfrac{\Delta H_{rxn}}{C_{p,\text{mix}}}$ | Adiabatic temperature rise of reacting mass | K | | 2 | $\phi \;=\; \dfrac{M_{s}\,C_{p,s} \;+\; M_{r}\,C_{p,r}}{M_{r}\,C_{p,r}}$ | Thermal inertia (vessel + contents vs. contents alone) | dimensionless | | 3 | $\Delta T_{ad,\text{plant}} \;=\; \phi \,\cdot\, \Delta T_{ad,\text{lab}}$ | Plant-corrected adiabatic rise | K | | 4 | $\ln(\text{TMR}_{ad}) \;=\; a \;+\; \dfrac{b}{T}$ | Empirical TMR correlation (linear in 1/T) | s or min | | 5 | $\text{TMR}_{ad} \;=\; \dfrac{C_{p}\,R\,T^{2}}{Q\,A\,e^{-E/RT}}$ | Theoretical TMR (Towsend & Tou) | s | | 6 | $\Delta P_{ad} \;\approx\; \dfrac{\Delta T_{ad}}{T_{p}}\,\cdot\,P_{p}$ | Ideal-gas adiabatic pressure rise | Pa | | 7 | $\text{RHI} \;=\; \dfrac{T_{\max} \;-\; T_{p}}{\Delta T_{ad,\text{plant}}}$ | Reaction Hazard Index (Townsend-style) | dimensionless | | 8 | $\text{SADT} \;=\; T_{p,\text{crit}} \;\text{where}\; \text{TMR}_{ad} \;=\; 7\,\text{days}$ | Definition by UN/IMO | K | | 9 | $k(T) \;=\; A\,e^{-E/RT}$ | Arrhenius rate constant | s⁻¹ | | 10 | $\dfrac{dT}{dt} \;\bigg\vert_{\text{adiabatic}} \;=\; \dfrac{-\Delta H}{C_{p,\text{total}}}\,k(T)\,C_{A}$ | Adiabatic self-heat rate equation | K/s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU note on the φ-factor:** > - In a laboratory RSD/Dewar test, φ ≈ **1.05–1.5** (very close to ideal adiabatic). > - In a small pilot reactor, φ ≈ **1.2–2.0**. > - In a full-scale plant reactor or storage tank, φ can be **3–10** because the *vessel mass* and *internal fittings* dominate the heat capacity. > - A *larger φ* means more stored thermal energy → **MORE dangerous**, not less. This is counter-intuitive to students! ### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Reaction Hazard Evaluation - **Pressure Relief Device (PRD) sizing** — API 521 / API 520 require adiabatic runaway data as inputs. - **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** — Kletz's principle: *attenuate* (dilute), *limit* (inventory), *substitute*, *moderate* (temperature). - **HAZOP & LOPA support** — RHE provides the *initiating-event frequency* and *consequence severity*. - **Transportation safety** — SADT determines whether a chemical is classified as a Class 1 (self-reactive) for shipping (UN TDG, IMDG). - **Reactor scale-up** — identifies whether pilot plant conditions can be linearly scaled to commercial size. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Adiabatic Temperature Rise (ΔT_ad) **Starting principle:** The first law of thermodynamics for a closed, adiabatic, constant-volume system with a single exothermic reaction is: $$ \sum_{i} n_{i}\,C_{p,i}\,(T_{f}-T_{i}) \;=\; -\Delta H_{rxn}\,\xi $$ where **ξ** is the extent of reaction (mol) and **n_i** is the moles of species i. **Step 1 — Assume complete conversion of the limiting reactant** (worst case): $$ \xi \;=\; n_{\text{limiting}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute**: $$ n_{\text{total}}\,C_{p,\text{mix}}\,(T_{f}-T_{i}) \;=\; -\Delta H_{rxn}\,n_{\text{limiting}} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for the temperature rise**: $$ \Delta T_{ad} \;=\; T_{f}-T_{i} \;=\; \dfrac{-\Delta H_{rxn}\,n_{\text{limiting}}}{n_{\text{total}}\,C_{p,\text{mix}}} $$ **Step 4 — Use specific heat per mass instead of molar basis** (industrial form): $$ \Delta T_{ad} \;=\; -\dfrac{\Delta H_{rxn}}{C_{p,\text{mix}}} $$ > **Logic:** The denominator C_p is the *thermal buffer*. A solvent with high C_p (e.g., water = 4.18 kJ/kg·K) absorbs heat and reduces ΔT_ad. This is why *dilution with high-C_p solvents* is a key Inherently Safer Design strategy. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Plant-Corrected Adiabatic Rise (φ-factor) **Starting principle:** The total thermal mass of the system at plant scale is the *vessel + contents*, while the heat is generated only by the *reacting mass*. **Step 1 — Total heat capacity**: $$ C_{p,\text{total}} \;=\; M_{s}\,C_{p,s} \;+\; M_{r}\,C_{p,r} $$ where subscript *s* = vessel structure, *r* = reacting contents. **Step 2 — Energy balance on plant scale**: $$ C_{p,\text{total}}\,\Delta T_{ad,\text{plant}} \;=\; -\Delta H_{rxn} $$ **Step 3 — Laboratory balance** (φ ≈ 1): $$ C_{p,r}\,\Delta T_{ad,\text{lab}} \;\approx\; -\Delta H_{rxn} $$ **Step 4 — Divide the two equations**: $$ \Delta T_{ad,\text{plant}} \;=\; \underbrace{\left(\dfrac{M_{s}\,C_{p,s} + M_{r}\,C_{p,r}}{M_{r}\,C_{p,r}}\right)}_{\phi} \cdot \Delta T_{ad,\text{lab}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Take-away:** Because φ > 1 always, the *plant ΔT_ad is ALWAYS larger than the laboratory ΔT_ad*. Laboratory data systematically **underestimates** plant severity — a critical pitfall KTU examiners test! --- ### 3.3 Derivation of the TMR_ad Equation (Towsend & Tou) **Starting principle:** Under adiabatic conditions, the self-heat rate is: $$ \dfrac{dT}{dt} \;=\; \Delta T_{ad,\text{plant}}\,\cdot\, k(T) \quad \text{(zero-order assumption, max simplification)} $$ with Arrhenius form $k(T) = A\,e^{-E/RT}$. **Step 1 — Separate variables**: $$ dt \;=\; \dfrac{dT}{\Delta T_{ad,\text{plant}}\,A\,e^{-E/RT}} $$ **Step 2 — Integrate from process temperature T_p to TMR endpoint** (T_p + small ΔT, treating T ≈ T_p in the exponent for short time): $$ \text{TMR}_{ad} \;\approx\; \dfrac{1}{\Delta T_{ad,\text{plant}}\,A\,e^{-E/RT_{p}}} $$ **Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $\dfrac{R\,T_{p}^{2}}{E}$ and apply the first-order thermal-runaway approximation**: $$ \text{TMR}_{ad} \;=\; \dfrac{C_{p,\text{total}}\,R\,T_{p}^{2}}{Q_{0}\,A\,e^{-E/RT_{p}}} $$ where $Q_{0}$ is the heat released per unit mass at T_p. **Step 4 — Linearise by taking natural log**: $$ \ln(\text{TMR}_{ad}) \;=\; \underbrace{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_{p,\text{total}}\,R}{Q_{0}\,A}\right)}_{a} \;+\; \underbrace{\dfrac{E}{R}}_{b}\,\dfrac{1}{T_{p}} $$ > **Logic:** This linear-in-(1/T) form is the basis of the **TMR-vs-T plot** that every KTU question on runaway kinetics expects you to draw or interpret. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem (typical KTU 14-mark style):** A pilot reactor contains 200 kg of reaction mass with C_p = 2.5 kJ/kg·K. The reaction has ΔH = -150 kJ/kg, A = 1×10¹² s⁻¹, E = 90 kJ/mol, and the vessel mass is 800 kg of steel (C_p = 0.5 kJ/kg·K). Process temperature T_p = 80 °C = 353 K. R = 8.314 J/mol·K. **Step 1 — Compute φ-factor:** $$ \phi \;=\; \dfrac{800\cdot 0.5 + 200\cdot 2.5}{200\cdot 2.5} \;=\; \dfrac{400 + 500}{500} \;=\; 1.80 $$ **Step 2 — Compute lab and plant ΔT_ad:** $$ \Delta T_{ad,\text{lab}} \;=\; \dfrac{150}{2.5} \;=\; 60\,K $$ $$ \Delta T_{ad,\text{plant}} \;=\; 1.80 \cdot 60 \;=\; 108\,K $$ **Step 3 — Compute total C_p:** $$ C_{p,\text{total}} \;=\; 400 + 500 \;=\; 900\,kJ/K $$ **Step 4 — Compute TMR_ad using Towsend-Tou equation (in seconds):** $$ \text{TMR}_{ad} \;=\; \dfrac{900 \times 10^{3} \cdot 8.314 \cdot (353)^{2}}{150 \times 10^{3} \cdot 1\times10^{12} \cdot e^{-90000/(8.314 \cdot 353)}} $$ Compute the exponent: $$ \dfrac{E}{R\,T_{p}} \;=\; \dfrac{90000}{8.314 \cdot 353} \;=\; 30.67 $$ $$ e^{-30.67} \;\approx\; 4.74\times10^{-14} $$ Numerator: $$ 900 \times 10^{3} \cdot 8.314 \cdot (353)^{2} \;=\; 9.31\times10^{11} $$ Denominator: $$ 150 \times 10^{3} \cdot 1\times10^{12} \cdot 4.74\times10^{-14} \;=\; 7.11\times10^{3} $$ Therefore: $$ \text{TMR}_{ad} \;=\; \dfrac{9.31\times10^{11}}{7.11\times10^{3}} \;\approx\; 1.31\times10^{8}\,s \;\approx\; 4.15\,\text{years} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** At 80 °C, the runaway would take ~4 years. This is **safe** — TMR_ad > 24 h is a typical KTU / industry "safe" benchmark. But if T_p rises to 100 °C (373 K), TMR_ad drops *exponentially* and crosses into the hazardous regime. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Criticality Bands (Townsend Diagram) | TMR_ad at T_p | Severity (ΔT_ad, plant) | Risk Band | Typical KTU Verdict | |----------------|------------------------|-----------|---------------------| | > 24 h | ΔT_ad < 50 K | **Low** | Acceptable, routine monitoring | | 8 – 24 h | 50 – 150 K | **Medium** | Engineering controls (cooling, alarms) | | 1 – 8 h | 150 – 300 K | **High** | Venting / SIS / interlocks mandatory | | < 1 h | > 300 K | **Critical** | Inherently safer redesign required | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Sequential Logic of Reaction Hazard Evaluation ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1: Reaction Identification
Desired Side Decomposition] --> B[Step 2: Calorimetric Measurement
DSC ARC RSD VSP] B --> C[Step 3: Adiabatic Extrapolation
Apply Phi Factor Correction] C --> D[Step 4: Severity Estimation
Delta T ad and Delta P ad] D --> E[Step 5: Probability Estimation
TMR ad vs Process Temperature] E --> F{Step 6: Risk Ranking
Townsend Diagram} F -->|Low Risk| G[Acceptable Process
Routine Monitoring] F -->|Medium Risk| H[Engineering Controls
Cooling Alarms SIS] F -->|High Risk| I[PRD Design
Venting Interlocks] F -->|Critical| J[Inherently Safer Design
Dilute Substitute Moderate] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Calorimetric Technique Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Screening[Stage 1 Screening Calorimetry] A1[DSC Differential Scanning Calorimetry] A2[DTA Differential Thermal Analysis] end subgraph Detailed[Stage 2 Detailed Adiabatic Calorimetry] B1[ARC Accelerating Rate Calorimeter] B2[RSD Dewar Reaction System Diagnosing] B3[VSP Vent Sizing Package] B4[Phi Tec Adiabatic Calorimeter] end subgraph ScaleUp[Stage 3 Plant Scale Correction] C1[Compute Phi Factor] C2[Apply Adiabatic Extrapolation] C3[Generate Townsend Diagram] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 B3 --> B4 B4 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C2 --> C3 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Energy Flow in an Adiabatic Runaway ```mermaid flowchart TB In[Reaction Mass
M_r C_p_r] -->|Heat Release| Mix[Combined System
M_s C_p_s + M_r C_p_r] Ext[External Heat Loss
Q_loss] -->|Normally Sinks| Mix Mix -->|If Q_loss Fails| Ramp[Self Heating Ramp
dT dt phi Delta T ad k T] Ramp --> Critical[Exotherm Accelerates
TMR ad Crossed] Critical --> Outcome{Vessel Outcome} Outcome -->|PRD Sized| Vent[Safe Venting] Outcome -->|PRD Undersized| Rupture[Catastrophic Rupture] ``` ### 4.4 Schematic — Townsend Criticality Diagram (text-rendered for clarity) ``` TMR_ad (log scale) ^ 24h | • Acceptable • Acceptable | (Low T) (High T) 8h | • Marginal • High Risk | (Low T) (High T) 1h | • High Risk • CRITICAL | (Low T) (High T) +----------------------------------> 50K 150K 300K ΔT_ad (plant) ``` > **Reading rule:** Vertical axis = probability (low TMR_ad = high probability). Horizontal axis = severity (high ΔT_ad = severe). Aim to keep your process in the *upper-left* quadrant. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the **Time to Maximum Rate under adiabatic conditions (TMR_ad)** and state its industrial significance in two points. **Model Answer (3 marks):** TMR_ad is defined as the time interval between the instant cooling is lost on a runaway-prone reacting system at a given initial temperature T_p, and the time at which the reaction reaches its maximum rate (i.e., the inflection point of the adiabatic self-heat curve). *(2 marks)* Industrial significance: *(i)* It is used to categorise the probability of a runaway (e.g., TMR_ad > 24 h is "low probability"), and *(ii)* it provides the *available response time* for operators and the Safety Instrumented System (SIS) before runaway peaks. *(1 mark)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the **φ-factor** in Reaction Hazard Evaluation, and why is it always greater than 1? **Model Answer (3 marks):** The φ-factor is defined as the ratio of the total heat capacity of the system (vessel + internal fittings + reactants) to the heat capacity of the reacting mass alone: $\phi = (M_s C_{p,s} + M_r C_{p,r})/(M_r C_{p,r})$. *(2 marks)* It is always greater than 1 because the vessel and fittings have additional thermal mass (M_s C_{p,s} > 0) that absorbs the heat of reaction, so the temperature rise is smaller per unit energy but stored over a *larger* thermal reservoir — leading to a *larger* plant-scale ΔT_ad after runaway consumes the entire mass. *(1 mark)* --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Choice A]** **(a)** Describe in detail the **six sequential steps of a Reaction Hazard Evaluation** and explain how each step contributes to the overall risk picture. *(7 marks)* **(b)** With the help of a **neat sketch of a Townsend-type criticality diagram**, explain how ΔT_ad (plant) and TMR_ad are combined to rank reaction hazards into Low, Medium, High, and Critical categories. Give one example mitigation strategy for each band. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a)** *Step 1 — Reaction Identification*: All desired, side, and decomposition reactions are listed using chemical knowledge, DSC screening, and literature review (e.g., Bretherick's Handbook of Reactive Chemical Hazards). Output: an exhaustive reaction matrix. *(1 mark)* *Step 2 — Calorimetric Measurement*: DSC gives ΔH and T_onset on mg samples; ARC/RSD/VSP give adiabatic self-heat data on 1–100 g samples. Output: ΔH, kinetics (A, E), onset temperature, gas evolution profile. *(1 mark)* *Step 3 — Adiabatic Extrapolation*: Laboratory data is corrected to plant scale using the φ-factor: $\Delta T_{ad,\text{plant}} = \phi \cdot \Delta T_{ad,\text{lab}}$. Output: realistic plant ΔT_ad and ΔP_ad. *(1 mark)* *Step 4 — Severity Estimation*: $\Delta T_{ad,\text{plant}}$ and the corresponding adiabatic pressure rise $\Delta P_{ad} \approx (\Delta T_{ad}/T_p) \cdot P_p$ are computed. Output: how *hot* and how *pressurised* the system becomes. *(1 mark)* *Step 5 — Probability Estimation*: $\ln(\text{TMR}_{ad}) = a + b/T_p$ is plotted. The process T_p is marked to read off TMR_ad. Output: probability / available response time. *(1 mark)* *Step 6 — Risk Ranking & Decision*: Plot (ΔT_ad, plant) on the x-axis and TMR_ad on the y-axis of a Townsend diagram. Decide on mitigation: monitoring, SIS, PRD, or ISD redesign. *(2 marks)* **(b)** Sketch description: *(3 marks for diagram + 1 for logic)* ``` TMR_ad (log scale) ↑ 24 h ────────────• Safe • Marginal 8 h ────────────• Marginal • High 1 h ────────────• High • CRITICAL └──────────────────────→ 50 K 150 K 300 K ΔT_ad (plant) ``` Mitigation per band: *(3 marks)* - **Low**: Routine monitoring; SOP-level controls. - **Medium**: Engineering controls — cooling redundancy, T_high alarms at 90 % of T_onset. - **High**: Safety Instrumented System (SIS) with independent cooling, pressure relief valve sized for runaway fire case (API 521). - **Critical**: Inherently Safer Design — *attenuate* (dilute in high-C_p solvent), *substitute* (less energetic reagent), *moderate* (lower T_p), or *limit inventory*. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Choice B]** **(a)** With appropriate equations, derive the **adiabatic temperature rise (ΔT_ad)** and the **plant-corrected ΔT_ad using the φ-factor**. Why does plant-scale runaway severity always exceed laboratory data? *(7 marks)* **(b)** A pharmaceutical batch reaction has the following data: reaction mass = 500 kg with C_p = 2.0 kJ/kg·K; steel vessel mass = 2000 kg with C_p = 0.5 kJ/kg·K; ΔH = -200 kJ/kg; T_p = 90 °C; A = 5×10¹⁴ s⁻¹; E = 100 kJ/mol. Compute (i) φ, (ii) ΔT_ad,plant, and (iii) TMR_ad at T_p. Comment on whether the reaction is "safe" by the industry TMR_ad > 24 h benchmark. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a)** Derivation — see Sections 3.1 and 3.2 above. Award marks as: *(i)* statement of energy balance = 2 marks; *(ii)* solving for ΔT_ad = 2 marks; *(iii)* defining and applying φ-factor = 2 marks; *(iv)* qualitative reason for plant > lab = 1 mark. **(b) Numerical solution:** *Step (i) — φ-factor:* $$ \phi \;=\; \dfrac{2000 \cdot 0.5 + 500 \cdot 2.0}{500 \cdot 2.0} \;=\; \dfrac{1000 + 1000}{1000} \;=\; 2.00 $$ *Step (ii) — Plant ΔT_ad:* $$ \Delta T_{ad,\text{lab}} \;=\; \dfrac{200}{2.0} \;=\; 100\,K $$ $$ \Delta T_{ad,\text{plant}} \;=\; 2.00 \cdot 100 \;=\; 200\,K $$ *Step (iii) — TMR_ad (T = 90 °C = 363 K):* $$ \dfrac{E}{RT} \;=\; \dfrac{100000}{8.314 \cdot 363} \;=\; 33.13 $$ $$ e^{-33.13} \;\approx\; 1.78\times10^{-14} $$ Total C_p: $$ C_{p,\text{total}} \;=\; 2000\,\text{kJ/K} $$ Towsend-Tou formula: $$ \text{TMR}_{ad} \;=\; \dfrac{2.0\times10^{6} \cdot 8.314 \cdot (363)^{2}}{200 \cdot 500 \cdot 5\times10^{14} \cdot 1.78\times10^{-14}} $$ Numerator: $$ 2.0\times10^{6} \cdot 8.314 \cdot 131769 \;\approx\; 2.19\times10^{12} $$ Denominator: $$ 200 \cdot 500 \cdot 5\times10^{14} \cdot 1.78\times10^{-14} \;=\; 8.90 $$ Wait — careful: heat released per kg at complete conversion is 200 kJ/kg, total mass is 500 kg, so total heat $Q_0 = 100\,000$ kJ. Re-do denominator: $$ Q_0 \cdot A \cdot e^{-E/RT} \;=\; 1.0\times10^{8} \cdot 5\times10^{14} \cdot 1.78\times10^{-14} \;=\; 890 $$ Thus: $$ \text{TMR}_{ad} \;\approx\; \dfrac{2.19\times10^{12}}{890} \;\approx\; 2.46\times10^{9}\,s \;\approx\; 78\,\text{years} $$ **Comment (1 mark):** TMR_ad ≈ 78 years >> 24 h ⇒ **SAFE at 90 °C** by industry standards. However, ΔT_ad,plant = 200 K means that *if* runaway occurs, severity is *high* (Medium-to-High band). Therefore, the reaction is **probability-safe but severity-significant** — engineered controls (independent cooling, alarms) are still recommended. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert ΔH sign convention.** ΔH is *negative* for exotherms; use $-\Delta H_{rxn}$ in the ΔT_ad formula. Many students write $+\Delta H$ and get a *negative* ΔT_ad → lose 1 mark. > 2. **Reporting TMR_ad in inconsistent units.** Convert seconds/minutes/hours consistently. KTU examiners check units strictly. > 3. **Using laboratory ΔT_ad instead of plant ΔT_ad.** This is the *single most common error*. Always apply the φ-factor before drawing any conclusion. > 4. **Confusing SADT with TMR_ad.** SADT is the *ambient temperature* at which TMR_ad = 7 days; TMR_ad is a *time* at a given T_p. > 5. **Drawing Townsend diagram on linear axes.** TMR_ad must be on a *logarithmic* y-axis — this is a frequently-tested KTU trap. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ Reaction Hazard Evaluation = **systematic quantification** of thermal + pressure + gas hazards before process design. - ✅ Three pillars: **thermal, pressure, gas** — never evaluate just one in isolation. - ✅ Six-step logic chain: identify → measure → extrapolate (φ!) → severity → probability → rank. - ✅ **Adiabatic ΔT_ad** uses heat of reaction and heat capacity of the reacting mass. - ✅ **Plant ΔT_ad = φ × lab ΔT_ad**; φ is *always* > 1 → plant is *always* worse than lab data suggests. - ✅ **TMR_ad** uses the Arrhenius equation and the Towsend–Tou formula; linear in (1/T_p). - ✅ **SADT** is defined as the package temperature at which TMR_ad = 7 days (UN/IMO transport regulation). - ✅ **Townsend diagram** plots ΔT_ad (x, severity) vs TMR_ad (y, probability on log scale) to rank risk. - ✅ Four risk bands — Low, Medium, High, Critical — each with prescribed mitigation. - ✅ Key techniques: DSC (screening) → ARC/RSD (adiabatic detail) → VSP (relief sizing) → Φ-Tec (small-φ lab). - ✅ **Inherently Safer Design** is the *final* resort for the Critical band; engineered controls come *before*. - ✅ Always work in SI units; **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**; convert kJ to J carefully in TMR calculations.

topics.title: Assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the framework of **Design for Safety (DfS)** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Assessment** refers to the systematic, structured, and rigorous engineering evaluation process used to identify, analyze, quantify, and judge the acceptability of hazards and associated risks inherent in the design, construction, operation, and decommissioning of engineered systems, processes, plants, and products.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *Safety Assessment is the process of evaluating the safety of a system or process through hazard identification, risk estimation, consequence analysis, and the determination of whether the residual risk is acceptable or requires further mitigation.*

The **Assessment** phase is one of the four pillars of the safety management lifecycle:

1. **Identification** (What can go wrong?)
2. **Assessment** (How bad and how likely?)
3. **Control** (What can be done to reduce it?)
4. **Review** (Are the controls working?)

Assessment sits at the **core analytical heart** of this cycle — without proper assessment, design safety becomes a matter of guesswork rather than engineering.

---

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Safety Assessment

Safety assessment is fundamentally built on three complementary analytical pillars:

| Pillar | Core Question | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Hazard Identification** | *What hazards exist?* | Inventory of potential threats |
| **Consequence Analysis** | *How severe is the outcome?* | Damage radii, fatality zones, environmental impact |
| **Probability Analysis** | *How likely is it to occur?* | Frequency of occurrence (events/year) |

The combination of consequence and probability yields **Risk**, the central currency of safety engineering.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Medical Check-Up

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Going to the Doctor**
>
> Imagine you visit a cardiologist before running a marathon. The doctor performs three actions:
>
> 1. **History & Screening** (Hazard Identification) — *"Do you have a family history of heart disease? Any chest pain?"* — Identifies potential *threats* to your health.
>
> 2. **Diagnostic Tests** (Consequence Analysis) — *"If something goes wrong, how severe would it be?"* — An ECG, blood tests, stress test. Quantifies *magnitude of harm*.
>
> 3. **Probability Estimation** (Risk Estimation) — *"Based on your age, lifestyle, and genetics, what is your statistical chance of a cardiac event during the marathon?"* — Quantifies *likelihood*.
>
> 4. **Prescription** (Risk Evaluation & Control) — *"Given your risk, here is the safe action: avoid the marathon, or run with a pace-setter and an AED on-site."* — Decides if risk is *acceptable*.
>
> This is exactly what an engineer does during **Safety Assessment** — but for a chemical plant, a building, a bridge, or a software-controlled system. The doctor treats *one patient*; the safety engineer treats *the system, the operators, the public, and the environment* all at once.

---

## 1.4 Why Assessment is Indispensable in Design for Safety

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU 2024 Core Insight:**
> *"You cannot control what you have not measured, and you cannot measure what you have not identified."*

- **Quantitative Decision-Making**: Assessment converts subjective fear into **objective numbers** that can be compared against acceptance criteria.
- **Resource Optimization**: Engineers have finite budgets. Assessment directs money to the *highest-risk* problems first.
- **Regulatory Compliance**: Statutory bodies (OSHA, EPA, OISD, PESO, ONGC standards) mandate documented safety assessments before commissioning.
- **Liability Protection**: Documented assessments demonstrate **Due Diligence** in court, shielding the design engineer and the organization from negligence claims.
- **ALARP Demonstration**: Assessment provides the mathematical evidence that risks have been reduced *As Low As Reasonably Practicable*.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics in Safety Assessment

The following quantitative metrics form the **vocabulary** of safety assessment. Memorize these — they appear in almost every KTU question:

- **Individual Risk (IR)** = **10⁻⁶ per year** (acceptable fatality probability for workers)
- **Societal Risk** = **F-N Curve** (cumulative frequency vs. number of fatalities)
- **Fatal Accident Rate (FAR)** = **4.0 fatalities per 10⁸ person-hours** (benchmark)
- **Tolerable Risk** = **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ per year**
- **Acceptable Risk** = **≤ 10⁻⁶ per year** for public, **≤ 10⁻⁵ per year** for workers

---

## 1.6 Scope of Assessment in Module 1

In Module 1 of PESFT412, **Assessment** typically covers the following techniques:

- **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)**
- **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)**
- **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**
- **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**
- **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)**
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)**
- **ALARP Principle**
- **Risk Matrix & Bow-Tie Analysis**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Assessment Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats Assessment as a **six-stage systematic process**. Understanding the logical sequence is critical for both theory and exam answers.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six Stages of Safety Assessment (KTU Standard):**
> 1. **Define the System Boundary** — What is being assessed? What is in-scope, out-of-scope?
> 2. **Hazard Identification** — Systematic search for credible threats.
> 3. **Consequence Analysis** — Modeling the physical outcome if a hazard is released.
> 4. **Frequency / Probability Analysis** — Estimating how often the initiating event occurs.
> 5. **Risk Estimation & Evaluation** — Combining consequence × frequency → comparing with criteria.
> 6. **Documentation & Recommendation** — Producing the Safety Assessment Report (SAR).

---

## 2.2 Pillar 1 — Hazard Identification Techniques

### 2.2.1 Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)

PHA is a **qualitative, early-stage** technique applied during the conceptual design phase.

- **Logic**: For each system component, ask *What are the potential hazards, causes, and effects?*
- **Output**: A ranked list of hazards with preliminary risk levels.
- **Strength**: Cheap, fast, requires limited data.
- **Weakness**: Highly dependent on team experience.

### 2.2.2 Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)

HAZOP is the **gold-standard qualitative technique** for chemical process plants.

- **Core Method**: Apply guidewords (`NO`, `MORE`, `LESS`, `AS WELL AS`, `PART OF`, `REVERSE`, `OTHER THAN`) to process parameters (`Flow`, `Pressure`, `Temperature`, `Level`, `Composition`).
- **Output**: Deviations, causes, consequences, safeguards, and actions.
- **Logic Step**: Each node of the process is interrogated with every (guideword × parameter) combination.

### 2.2.3 Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)

FMEA evaluates **component-level failures** and propagates them to system-level effects.

- **Logic**: List every component → identify every failure mode → trace the effect → assess severity.
- **Key Metric**: **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** calculated later in this section.

### 2.2.4 What-If Analysis / Checklist Analysis

- **What-If**: Brainstorming "What if this fails?" scenarios.
- **Checklist**: Use pre-existing industry checklists (OSHA, API, IS standards).

---

## 2.3 Pillar 2 — Consequence Analysis

Consequence analysis estimates the **physical damage** if a hazardous material or energy is released. Standard models include:

- **Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)** — TNT-equivalent method
- **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion (BLEVE)**
- **Jet Fire & Pool Fire** — Thermal radiation distance
- **Toxic Gas Dispersion** — Gaussian plume (Pasquill-Gifford model)
- **Flammable Gas Dispersion** — Lower Flammable Limit (LFL) contour

The **damage criteria** are typically expressed as overpressure (for explosions) or thermal radiation dose (for fires).

---

## 2.4 Pillar 3 — Frequency & Probability Analysis

Two principal techniques:

### 2.4.1 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)

A **top-down deductive** method that uses Boolean logic to compute the probability of a top event (e.g., reactor explosion).

- **Key Operators**: `AND` gate (independent), `OR` gate (either).
- **Output**: Minimum Cut Sets, Top Event Probability.

### 2.4.2 Event Tree Analysis (ETA)

A **bottom-up inductive** method that traces outcomes from an initiating event through successive safety barriers.

- **Output**: Branch probabilities leading to various end-states (Safe, Fire, Explosion, Toxic Release).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** The following formulas appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on Assessment. Master them.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Variable | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Individual Risk (IR)** | `IR = f × C` | fatalities/year | f = frequency, C = consequence (0–1 probability of fatality) |
| 2 | **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** | `RPN = S × O × D` | dimensionless | S=Severity (1–10), O=Occurrence (1–10), D=Detection (1–10) |
| 3 | **Top Event Probability (AND gate)** | `P(AND) = P₁ × P₂ × … × Pₙ` | dimensionless | Events are statistically independent |
| 4 | **Top Event Probability (OR gate)** | `P(OR) = 1 − ∏(1 − Pᵢ)` | dimensionless | For small Pᵢ: `P(OR) ≈ Σ Pᵢ` |
| 5 | **Fatal Accident Rate (FAR)** | `FAR = (N × 10⁸) / (T × H)` | fatalities / 10⁸ person-hr | N=fatalities, T=exposure years, H=hours/year |
| 6 | **TNT Equivalent Mass** | `W_TNT = (η × m × ΔH_c) / E_TNT` | kg TNT | η = empirical yield factor (1–10%), m = mass, ΔHc = heat of combustion, E_TNT = 4520 kJ/kg |
| 7 | **Consequence Distance (Probit)** | `Y = a + b·ln(V)` | probit units | V = intensity (heat dose, concentration, overpressure) |
| 8 | **ALARP Equation** | `Risk ∝ Cost of Further Reduction` | monetary | Reduction must be "grossly disproportionate" to cost |
| 9 | **F-N Curve Criterion** | `F × Nᵏ ≤ C` | dimensionless | k = 1 (UK HSE), k = 2 (Netherlands) |
| 10 | **Lethal Dose 50% (LCt₅₀)** | `C^n × t = k` | constant | Haber's Law for toxic exposure |

---

## 2.6 ALARP — The Cornerstone of Risk Evaluation

> [!NOTE]
> **ALARP** = **As Low As Reasonably Practicable**

ALARP is the **decision-making framework** used after assessment to determine if further risk reduction is required.

**The Three Zones of Risk:**

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│   UNACCEPTABLE RISK   →   Must reduce regardless of cost    │
│  (IR > 10⁻⁴ per year)                                      │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   ALARP / TOLERABLE    →   Reduce if "reasonably            │
│  (10⁻⁶ ≤ IR ≤ 10⁻⁴)   practicable" (cost-benefit analysis) │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   BROADLY ACCEPTABLE  →   No further action required        │
│  (IR < 10⁻⁶ per year)                                      │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Practical Test for ALARP**: Would the *gross disproportion* test be passed? — *"Is the cost of further risk reduction grossly disproportionate to the safety benefit gained?"*

---

## 2.7 Risk Matrix (Standard 5×5)

| ↓ Likelihood / Severity → | **1 - Negligible** | **2 - Minor** | **3 - Moderate** | **4 - Major** | **5 - Catastrophic** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **5 - Frequent** | Medium | High | High | Extreme | Extreme |
| **4 - Probable** | Low | Medium | High | High | Extreme |
| **3 - Occasional** | Low | Medium | Medium | High | Extreme |
| **2 - Remote** | Low | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **1 - Improbable** | Low | Low | Low | Medium | Medium |

**Decision Rules:**
- **Extreme** → Stop operation, immediate redesign.
- **High** → Mandatory controls before commissioning.
- **Medium** → Procedural controls + monitoring.
- **Low** → Accept with documentation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Risk Priority Number (RPN) Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** A hydraulic press component has been identified with three failure modes. Compute RPN for each and rank them.

**Given Data:**

| Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1: Seal rupture | 8 | 5 | 4 |
| F2: Pump cavitation | 6 | 7 | 5 |
| F3: Sensor drift | 4 | 8 | 7 |

**Step 1 — Recall the formula:**

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

where `S` = Severity, `O` = Occurrence, `D` = Detection difficulty (all rated 1–10).

**Step 2 — Compute for F1:**

$$
\text{RPN}_{F1} = 8 \times 5 \times 4 = 160
$$

**Step 3 — Compute for F2:**

$$
\text{RPN}_{F2} = 6 \times 7 \times 5 = 210
$$

**Step 4 — Compute for F3:**

$$
\text{RPN}_{F3} = 4 \times 8 \times 7 = 224
$$

**Step 5 — Rank by RPN (higher = more critical):**

$$
\text{F3 (224)} > \text{F2 (210)} > \text{F1 (160)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action:** The sensor drift (F3) is the highest priority for corrective action, even though it has the lowest severity — because detection is hard and occurrence is high.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fault Tree Analysis: Top Event Probability

**System Description:**
A reactor overheating top event T occurs if:
- Either pump A fails **OR** pump B fails (cooling failure), **OR**
- Both temperature sensors (T1 **AND** T2) fail simultaneously.

**Fault Tree Structure:**

```
                 ┌── P(A) ──┐
         ┌── OR ─┤          ├──
         │       └── P(B) ──┘   T (Top Event)
T ───────┤
         │       ┌── P(T1) ──┐
         └── AND ─┤           ├──
                 └── P(T2) ──┘
```

**Given Failure Probabilities:**
- `P(A) = 0.01`
- `P(B) = 0.02`
- `P(T1) = 0.005`
- `P(T2) = 0.005`

**Step 1 — Compute the AND gate (sensor failure):**

$$
P(\text{AND}) = P(T_1) \times P(T_2) = 0.005 \times 0.005 = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the OR gate (cooling failure):**

$$
P(\text{OR}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - P_i)
$$

Substituting:

$$
P(\text{OR}) = 1 - (1 - 0.01)(1 - 0.02) = 1 - (0.99)(0.98)
$$

$$
P(\text{OR}) = 1 - 0.9702 = 0.0298
$$

**Step 3 — Total Top Event Probability (OR of both branches):**

$$
P(T) = 1 - (1 - P_{\text{OR}})(1 - P_{\text{AND}})
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - (1 - 0.0298)(1 - 2.5 \times 10^{-5})
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - (0.9702)(0.999975)
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - 0.970176 = 2.98 \times 10^{-2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The probability of reactor overheating ≈ 1 in 33.6 events per year. This exceeds typical tolerable risk (10⁻³ to 10⁻⁴), so additional safety interlocks are mandated.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — TNT Equivalent for Vapor Cloud Explosion

**Problem:**
A 500 kg propane cloud detonates. Heat of combustion of propane is 46,000 kJ/kg. Yield factor (empirical) = 4%. Compute the TNT equivalent mass.

**Step 1 — State the formula:**

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{\eta \times m \times \Delta H_c}{E_{\text{TNT}}}
$$

where `E_TNT = 4520 kJ/kg`.

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{0.04 \times 500 \times 46{,}000}{4520}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**

$$
0.04 \times 500 = 20
$$

$$
20 \times 46{,}000 = 920{,}000 \ \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by E_TNT:**

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{920{,}000}{4520} = 203.5 \ \text{kg TNT}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 500 kg propane release yields the explosive equivalent of **~204 kg of TNT**. Using standard overpressure-distance tables, this corresponds to ~50% structural damage at approximately 30–40 meters from the epicenter.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Individual Risk Calculation

**Problem:**
A relief valve fails once in 50 years. When it fails, there is a 10% chance of a fatality in the immediate vicinity. Compute the Individual Risk.

**Step 1 — State the formula:**

$$
\text{IR} = f \times C
$$

**Step 2 — Compute frequency:**

$$
f = \frac{1}{50} = 0.02 \ \text{per year}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
\text{IR} = 0.02 \times 0.10 = 2 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{fatalities/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with acceptance criteria:**

$$
\text{IR} = 2 \times 10^{-3} > 10^{-4} \ (\text{Tolerable Upper Limit})
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** The risk is **UNACCEPTABLE**. Mandatory redesign or additional safeguards are required before commissioning.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Fatal Accident Rate (FAR)

**Problem:**
Over a 20-year industry history, there were 3 fatalities and 250 workers exposed for 2000 hours/year. Compute FAR.

**Step 1 — Formula:**

$$
\text{FAR} = \frac{N \times 10^{8}}{T \times H_{\text{total}}}
$$

where `H_total = T × (workers × hours/year)`.

**Step 2 — Compute total person-hours:**

$$
H_{\text{total}} = 20 \times 250 \times 2000 = 10{,}000{,}000 \ \text{person-hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
\text{FAR} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{10^{7}} = 30 \ \text{fatalities per } 10^{8} \ \text{person-hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with industry benchmarks:**

- **Chemical industry average**: 5 per 10⁸ person-hours
- **Our result**: 30 per 10⁸ person-hours → **6× higher than industry average** → Unacceptable performance; requires intervention.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — F-N Curve Criterion Check

**Problem:**
A plant analysis yields the following data:

| Scenario | Frequency (per year) | Fatalities |
|---|---|---|
| S1 | 1 × 10⁻³ | 1 |
| S2 | 1 × 10⁻⁴ | 10 |
| S3 | 1 × 10⁻⁵ | 100 |

Check against UK HSE criterion: `F × N ≤ 10⁻²`.

**Step 1 — Compute F × N for each scenario:**

$$
S_1: \ 10^{-3} \times 1 = 10^{-3} \ (\leq 10^{-2}) \ \checkmark
$$

$$
S_2: \ 10^{-4} \times 10 = 10^{-3} \ (\leq 10^{-2}) \ \checkmark
$$

$$
S_3: \ 10^{-5} \times 100 = 10^{-3} \ (\leq 10^{-2}) \ \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Compare cumulative F-N:**

- All individual points lie below the criterion line.
- No scenario violates the ALARP envelope.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The plant meets the UK HSE societal risk criterion. ALARP demonstration is complete.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Assessment Technique

| Technique | Stage of Design | Data Required | Output Type | Cost | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PHA** | Conceptual | Low | Qualitative | Low | Early screening |
| **Checklist** | All stages | Low | Qualitative | Low | Routine audits |
| **What-If** | Preliminary | Medium | Qualitative | Low-Med | Brainstorming |
| **HAZOP** | Detailed design | Medium-High | Qualitative | Medium | Process plants |
| **FMEA** | Detailed design | Medium | Semi-quantitative | Medium | Mechanical/Electronic |
| **FTA** | Detailed design | High | Quantitative | High | Complex systems |
| **ETA** | Detailed design | High | Quantitative | High | Sequence of events |
| **QRA** | All stages | Very High | Quantitative | Very High | Major hazard facilities |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Master Assessment Workflow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Define System Boundary"] --> B["Hazard Identification"]
    B --> C1["PHA"]
    B --> C2["HAZOP"]
    B --> C3["FMEA"]
    B --> C4["Checklist"]
    C1 --> D["Select Critical Hazards"]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E["Consequence Analysis"]
    D --> F["Frequency Analysis"]
    E --> G["Risk Estimation R equals f times C"]
    F --> G
    G --> H{"Compare with Acceptance Criteria"}
    H -->|Below Criteria| I["Acceptable Risk"]
    H -->|Above Criteria| J["ALARP Analysis"]
    J --> K{"Further Reduction Practicable?"}
    K -->|Yes| L["Implement Controls"]
    K -->|No| M["Document Residual Risk"]
    L --> N["Re-assess System"]
    N --> E
    M --> O["Safety Assessment Report"]
    I --> O
```

## 4.2 Fault Tree Structure — Reactor Overheat

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T["Top Event: Reactor Overheat"]
    A["Cooling Pump A Fails"]
    B["Cooling Pump B Fails"]
    S1["Temp Sensor T1 Fails"]
    S2["Temp Sensor T2 Fails"]

    T --> G1["OR Gate"]
    G1 --> A
    G1 --> B
    G1 --> G2["AND Gate"]
    G2 --> S1
    G2 --> S2
```

## 4.3 Event Tree — LPG Release Scenario

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IE["Initiating Event: LPG Leak"]
    IE --> S1["Ignite Immediately?"]
    S1 -->|Yes| F["Jet Fire"]
    S1 -->|No| S2["Cloud Forms?"]
    S2 -->|No| SD["Safe Dispersion"]
    S2 -->|Yes| S3["Delayed Ignition?"]
    S3 -->|Yes| VCE["Vapor Cloud Explosion"]
    S3 -->|No| SD
```

## 4.4 Bow-Tie Diagram — Generic Safety Assessment View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T["TOP EVENT: Loss of Containment"]
    T --> P1["Threat: Pipe Rupture"]
    T --> P2["Threat: Pump Failure"]
    T --> P3["Threat: Human Error"]

    T --> C1["Consequence: Fire"]
    T --> C2["Consequence: Explosion"]
    T --> C3["Consequence: Toxic Release"]

    P1 -.->|Prevented by| B1["Barrier: Inspection"]
    P2 -.->|Prevented by| B2["Barrier: Maintenance"]
    P3 -.->|Prevented by| B3["Barrier: Training"]

    C1 -.->|Mitigated by| M1["Mitigation: Fire Water System"]
    C2 -.->|Mitigated by| M2["Mitigation: Blast Walls"]
    C3 -.->|Mitigated by| M3["Mitigation: Scrubber"]
```

## 4.5 ALARP Decision Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Computed Risk Level"]
    R --> Z1{"Risk Greater than 10 power -4?"}
    Z1 -->|Yes| U["UNACCEPTABLE: Mandatory Reduction"]
    Z1 -->|No| Z2{"Risk Between 10 power -6 and 10 power -4?"}
    Z2 -->|Yes| AL["ALARP ZONE: Apply Cost-Benefit"]
    Z2 -->|No| A["ACCEPTABLE: Document and Monitor"]
    AL --> Q{"Cost Grossly Disproportionate?"}
    Q -->|No| RD["Implement Further Reduction"]
    Q -->|Yes| A
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. Define Safety Assessment. List any four hazard identification techniques.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Safety Assessment** is the systematic engineering process of evaluating a system to identify hazards, analyze their consequences and frequencies, estimate the associated risks, and determine whether those risks are acceptable, tolerable, or require further reduction through the ALARP principle.

**Four Hazard Identification Techniques:**

1. **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** — Early-stage qualitative review of system hazards.
2. **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)** — Systematic application of guidewords to process parameters.
3. **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** — Component-level failure analysis with RPN ranking.
4. **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** — Top-down deductive method using Boolean logic for probability computation.

*(Other acceptable answers: Checklist Analysis, What-If Analysis, Event Tree Analysis)*

---

### **Q2. What is the ALARP principle? Why is it used in safety assessment?** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)** is the risk management principle that requires all risks to be reduced to a level *as low as reasonably practicable* — meaning that further reduction is required unless the cost (in money, effort, or time) is *grossly disproportionate* to the safety benefit gained.

**Why ALARP is Used:**

- Provides a **balanced decision-making framework** between absolute safety and economic reality.
- Forms the **legal and regulatory basis** for compliance in the UK, EU, and many other jurisdictions.
- Demonstrates **due diligence** to regulatory bodies and courts.
- Avoids the extremes of *"zero risk at any cost"* (impossible) and *"accept all risks"* (negligent).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Assessment Problem** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A chemical reactor has a cooling system with two redundant pumps (A and B) and two redundant temperature sensors (T1 and T2). The reactor overheats (Top Event) if:
- **Either** Pump A **OR** Pump B fails, **OR**
- **Both** T1 **AND** T2 fail simultaneously.

The failure probabilities per year are: `P(A) = 0.02`, `P(B) = 0.03`, `P(T1) = 0.04`, `P(T2) = 0.05`.

**(a)** Draw the fault tree and compute the probability of the top event.

**(b)** If the overheat event leads to a fatality with probability 0.2, compute the Individual Risk (IR) per year. Comment on its acceptability using the ALARP framework.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Fault Tree Structure (drawn as in Section 4.2):**

```
                 ┌── P(A) ──┐
         ┌── OR ─┤          ├──
         │       └── P(B) ──┘   T (Top Event)
T ───────┤
         │       ┌── P(T1) ──┐
         └── AND ─┤           ├──
                 └── P(T2) ──┘
```

**Step 2 — Compute AND gate (both sensors fail):**

$$
P(\text{AND}) = P(T_1) \times P(T_2) = 0.04 \times 0.05 = 0.0020
$$

**Step 3 — Compute OR gate (either pump fails):**

$$
P(\text{OR}) = 1 - (1 - P_A)(1 - P_B)
$$

$$
P(\text{OR}) = 1 - (1 - 0.02)(1 - 0.03) = 1 - (0.98)(0.97)
$$

$$
P(\text{OR}) = 1 - 0.9506 = 0.0494
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Top Event Probability (top-level OR):**

$$
P(T) = 1 - (1 - P_{\text{OR}})(1 - P_{\text{AND}})
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - (1 - 0.0494)(1 - 0.0020)
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - (0.9506)(0.9980)
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - 0.9487 = 0.0513 \ \text{per year}
$$

> **Result:** The reactor overheat frequency is approximately **0.0513 events/year** (≈ 1 event every 19.5 years).

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute Individual Risk:**

$$
\text{IR} = f \times C = 0.0513 \times 0.2 = 0.01026 \ \text{fatalities/year}
$$

Converting:

$$
\text{IR} \approx 1.03 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{per year}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with ALARP criteria:**

- **Tolerable upper limit** (worker) ≈ `10⁻⁴ per year`
- **Computed IR** = `1.03 × 10⁻² per year`

$$
\text{IR}_{\text{computed}} > \text{IR}_{\text{tolerable}}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> - Forgetting to multiply by the consequence probability (0.2) — writing only `0.0513` as the final answer.
> - Stating "risk is high" without referencing numerical ALARP criteria.
> - Failing to mention that the design must be modified.

> **The risk is UNACCEPTABLE.** The system lies in the **unacceptable region** of the ALARP framework. Mandatory design modifications are required, such as:
> - Adding a third redundant pump.
> - Installing a diverse backup sensor (different technology).
> - Implementing an automatic emergency cooling system.
> - Re-designing to bring IR below `10⁻⁴ per year`.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

A chemical plant has identified three failure scenarios:

| Scenario | Frequency (per year) | Number of Fatalities |
|---|---|---|
| S1 | 1 × 10⁻³ | 1 |
| S2 | 1 × 10⁻⁴ | 10 |
| S3 | 1 × 10⁻⁵ | 100 |
