**(a)** Plot the F-N curve and verify whether the plant meets the **UK HSE criterion** (F × N ≤ 10⁻²). Discuss the significance of societal risk.

**(b)** Using the ALARP principle, recommend whether the plant should be allowed to operate. What further risk reduction measures would you suggest?

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute F × N for each scenario:**

$$
S_1: \ 10^{-3} \times 1 = 10^{-3} \ (\leq 10^{-2}) \ \checkmark
$$

$$
S_2: \ 10^{-4} \times 10 = 10^{-3} \ (\leq 10^{-2}) \ \checkmark
$$

$$
S_3: \ 10^{-5} \times 100 = 10^{-3} \ (\leq 10^{-2}) \ \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — F-N Curve Description:**

The F-N curve is plotted with:
- **X-axis**: N = Number of fatalities (logarithmic scale: 1, 10, 100)
- **Y-axis**: F = Cumulative frequency per year (logarithmic scale)

The three points plot as a straight line on log-log paper with slope `−1`, since `F × N = 10⁻³` for all points.

```
F (frequency/year)
  │
  │ ● S1
10⁻³│       ● S2
  │              ● S3
10⁻⁴│
  │
10⁻⁵└────┬─────┬─────► N (fatalities)
        1    10   100
```

**Step 3 — Comparison with UK HSE Criterion:**

The criterion line is `F = 10⁻² / N`. All three points lie *below* this line, meaning the plant **meets** the UK HSE societal risk criterion.

**Step 4 — Significance of Societal Risk:**

> Societal risk captures the **collective impact** on the public, not just individual workers. A single event with 100 fatalities is weighted more heavily than 100 separate events with 1 fatality each — because communities, emergency services, and political systems cannot easily absorb mass-casualty events.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — ALARP Decision:**

Although all three scenarios meet the UK HSE criterion, ALARP requires that risks be reduced *as low as reasonably practicable* until the cost of further reduction is *grossly disproportionate* to the safety gain.

**Step 2 — Recommended Risk Reduction Measures:**

1. **Inherent Safer Design (ISD)**:
   - Reduce inventory of hazardous materials.
   - Substitute with less hazardous chemicals where possible.
   - Attenuate process conditions (lower T, P).

2. **Engineering Controls**:
   - Install additional pressure relief valves.
   - Add a third independent cooling system.
   - Use diverse (different-vendor) sensors to prevent common-mode failure.

3. **Procedural Controls**:
   - Strict Permit-to-Work systems.
   - Operator training and competency certification.
   - Emergency response drills every 6 months.

4. **Mitigation Systems**:
   - Blast walls and fire-water deluge.
   - Toxic gas detection with automatic isolation.
   - Safe haven / control room with positive-pressure ventilation.

**Step 3 — Final Recommendation:**

> The plant **may operate** subject to implementation of the above ALARP measures, with annual re-assessment and documentation in the **Safety Assessment Report (SAR)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> Students often write *"the plant meets the criteria, so it is safe"* — this is WRONG. Meeting the criterion is **necessary but not sufficient**. ALARP must always be demonstrated through cost-benefit analysis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION CHECKLIST — ASSESSMENT (MODULE 1)**

- **Assessment** is the analytical core of safety engineering — it converts qualitative concerns into quantitative risk metrics.
- The **three pillars** are: Hazard Identification → Consequence Analysis → Frequency Analysis → Risk.
- **Risk = Frequency × Consequence**. Memorize this master equation.
- **RPN = S × O × D** (Severity × Occurrence × Detection). Always rank by RPN descending.
- **FTA AND gate**: `P = product of all inputs`. **OR gate**: `P = 1 − product of (1 − Pᵢ)`.
- **ALARP** has three zones: Unacceptable (>10⁻⁴), ALARP/Tolerable (10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁴), Acceptable (<10⁻⁶).
- **TNT equivalent formula**: `W_TNT = η × m × ΔH_c / 4520` — yields kg of TNT for explosion modeling.
- **Individual Risk (IR)** = `f × C` and must be compared to `10⁻⁶/year` (public) or `10⁻⁵/year` (worker).
- **F-N Curve** plots cumulative frequency vs. fatalities on log-log paper; UK HSE criterion is `F × N ≤ 10⁻²`.
- **HAZOP** uses **guidewords** (`NO`, `MORE`, `LESS`, etc.) × **process parameters** (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, etc.).
- **PHA** is the *first* technique applied — during conceptual design — and is qualitative.
- **QRA** = Quantitative Risk Assessment — the most comprehensive, most expensive, and most data-hungry technique.
- **Bow-Tie diagrams** visualize the path from threats → top event → consequences with barriers on each side.
- **Meet the criterion ≠ Safe** — ALARP demonstration is always mandatory after quantitative evaluation.
- The **Safety Assessment Report (SAR)** is the final deliverable and serves as the legal record of due diligence.
- **Risk Matrix** (5×5) is used for rapid screening when full quantitative data is unavailable.
- **Haber's Law** `C^n × t = k` is the foundation of toxic dose-response assessment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reactor Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reactor Safety

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reactor Safety** is the discipline of engineering, design, operation, and regulatory practice devoted to preventing, mitigating, and managing accidents in nuclear reactors so as to protect personnel, the public, and the environment from undue radiological hazard.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, reactor safety is treated as a **holistic life-cycle design philosophy** rather than a single component. It spans:
- **Preventive Safety** (designed-in reliability, quality assurance, human factors).
- **Protective Safety** (engineered safety features, containment, emergency core cooling).
- **Mitigative Safety** (accident management, radiological source term control, siting).

The governing metric is the **Core Damage Frequency (CDF)**, target ≤ **10⁻⁵ per reactor-year** for new designs, and **Large Early Release Frequency (LERF)** ≤ **10⁻⁶ per reactor-year**, as stipulated by the **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB), India** and the **IAEA Safety Standards (SSR-2/1, Rev. 1)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker" View of a Reactor:**
> Imagine a domestic pressure cooker. The pot generates steam (heat) faster than it can release. A working **pressure-regulator weight (control rods)** keeps the heat controlled. A **safety valve (relief valve / SCRAM)** dumps pressure if things go wrong. A second, **independent relief path (ECCS / containment)** catches steam even if the first fails. Finally, the **reinforced lid and outer shell (containment building)** are designed so that even if every internal system fails, the kitchen (public) stays safe.
>
> A nuclear reactor is identical in philosophy: **fuel temperature rise → reactivity feedback → engineered shutdown → residual heat removal → confinement.**

## 1.3 Key Safety Design Constants and Metrics (Bold-Highlighted)

- **k<sub>eff</sub>** — effective neutron multiplication factor (must remain ≤ **1.0** during normal operation).
- **β (delayed neutron fraction)** ≈ **0.0065** for U-235, ≈ **0.0025** for Pu-239.
- **Decay heat fraction at shutdown** ≈ **7 % of rated power** (immediate, fission-product dominated).
- **Doppler coefficient of reactivity (T·dk/dT)** — intrinsically **negative** for safe designs.
- **Acceptable dose to public** ≤ **1 mSv/year** (AERB / ICRP).
- **Site boundary emergency planning zone (EPZ)** = **16 km radius** (typical for Indian PHWRs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Reactor safety is a *defense-in-depth* problem — there is **no single safety system**; safety emerges from **5 redundant, independent, and verifiable barriers** operating simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Levels of Defense-in-Depth (IAEA / AERB Framework)

The KTU 2024 Module-1 syllabus expects students to articulate defense-in-depth as a **5-tier onion** rather than a list. Each tier must be functionally *independent* of the next.

1. **Level 1 — Conservative Design & Quality Assurance**
   Robust margins, design codes (ASME Section III), proven materials, conservative operational limits.
2. **Level 2 — Operational Limits & Surveillance**
   Technical Specifications, Limiting Conditions for Operation (LCOs), in-service inspection.
3. **Level 3 — Engineered Safety Features (ESF)**
   Automatic systems that actuate on demand: **RPS (Reactor Protection System)**, **ECCS**, **Containment Isolation**.
4. **Level 4 — Accident Management**
   Emergency Operating Procedures (EOPs), Severe Accident Management Guidelines (SAMGs).
5. **Level 5 — Emergency Preparedness & Off-site Response**
   Exclusion zone, EPZ, public evacuation drills, dosimetry networks.

## 2.2 Reactivity and the Sub-Critical Response

Reactivity $\rho$ is the normalized deviation of the multiplication factor from unity:

$$\rho = \frac{k_{\text{eff}} - 1}{k_{\text{eff}}}$$

* **Why it matters:** Reactivity directly sets the *time-constant* of power excursions. For $\rho < \beta$ (delayed supercritical), reactor period is dominated by the **delayed-neutron** generation time (~**80 s** for U-235), giving operators ample time to act. For $\rho > \beta$ (prompt supercritical), periods drop to **milliseconds** — this is the **domain of nuclear explosives**, deliberately avoided in all civil reactors.

## 2.3 Inhour Equation (Period-Reactivity Relationship)

The **Inhour equation** relates reactivity to the stable reactor period $T$:

$$\rho = \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i}{1 + \lambda_i T} + \frac{\Lambda}{T}$$

where $\beta_i$ is the i-th delayed group fraction, $\lambda_i$ its decay constant, and $\Lambda$ the prompt-neutron generation time. **One inhour** = the reactivity that produces a **period T = 1 hour** = **3600 s**.

## 2.4 Decay Heat After Shutdown (Way-Wigner Approximation)

After a long steady run at power $P_0$, residual fission-product heat at time $t$ after shutdown:

$$P(t) \approx 0.0622 \, P_0 \left[ t^{-0.2} - (T_0 + t)^{-0.2} \right]$$

with $t$ and $T_0$ in **seconds**, $P_0$ in MW. The **"10 % rule of thumb"** (ANSI/ANS-5.1) states: **decay heat ≈ 10 % of operating power** within seconds, falling to ~1 % after 1 hour and ~0.5 % after 1 day.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this a safety issue?** Three Mile Island (1979) and Fukushima (2011) — both core-melt accidents — were driven by **loss of decay-heat removal**, not by power excursion. Even with the chain reaction killed, the fuel continues to cook itself without cooling.

## 2.5 Source Term and Release Fractions

The **source term** is the quantity of radioactivity available for release to the environment. For a postulated severe accident with containment failure:

$$Q_i = N_{f,i} \cdot f_{\text{release}} \cdot f_{\text{transport}} \cdot f_{\text{shield}}$$

where each $f$ is a barrier-passing fraction (fuel matrix, cladding, coolant, containment).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Typical Value or Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|--------|----------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Reactivity | $\rho = (k_{\text{eff}} - 1)/k_{\text{eff}}$ | dimensionless, $\rho < 0$ for shutdown | SCRAM worth verification |
| 2 | Sub-critical multiplication | $M = 1 / (1 - k_{\text{eff}})$ | M = 50 when k = 0.98 | Source-range calibration |
| 3 | Inhour equation | $\rho = \sum \beta_i / (1 + \lambda_i T) + \Lambda/T$ | T in seconds | Period-based trip calibration |
| 4 | Decay heat (Way-Wigner) | $P(t) = 0.0622\,P_0\,[t^{-0.2} - (T_0+t)^{-0.2}]$ | t in s, P in MW | ECCS sizing |
| 5 | Doppler coefficient | $T\,dk/dT$ | $-2$ to $-5$ pcm/K (LWR) | Power-coefficient feedback |
| 6 | Void coefficient | $dk/d\alpha$ | negative (LWR), positive (RBMK) | Safety case for moderator |
| 7 | Core damage frequency | CDF | target $\le 10^{-5}$/ry | PSA Level-1 metric |
| 8 | Large early release freq. | LERF | target $\le 10^{-6}$/ry | PSA Level-2 metric |
| 9 | Mean dose to public | $D = \sum (C_i \cdot DCF_i \cdot t_{\text{exp}})$ | mSv | Radiological impact assessment |
| 10 | Containment leak rate | $L / V$ | $\le 0.1$ %/day at design pressure | AERB containment standard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** For numerical problems, always carry **units** to their final step. KTU examiners award a **dedicated 1 mark** for correct unit consistency in 14-mark derivations.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

Reactor-safety principles directly translate into:
- **Process safety** in chemical plants (defense-in-depth, ESD systems).
- **Aerospace safety** (redundant flight-control channels, fail-safe architecture).
- **Battery energy storage safety** (thermal-runaway management — a direct analog of decay-heat removal).
- **Offshore platform safety** (passive fire protection, blast walls, evacuation philosophy).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Decay-Heat Integral From Fission-Product Inventory

### Step 1: Define the precursor inventory differential equation
Fission produces a **delayed-neutron precursor** population $C_i(t)$ for each decay group $i$. For a constant operating power $P_0$ sustained for time $T_0$, the precursor source term is $\beta_i P_0 / \Lambda$ (treated as a constant input).

$$\frac{dC_i}{dt} = \lambda_i N_f \Sigma_f \phi - \lambda_i C_i$$

### Step 2: Solve the linear ODE for constant source
For a step-input of magnitude $S_i = \lambda_i N_{f,i}$ (precursors produced per second at steady state), the solution is:

$$C_i(t) = \frac{S_i}{\lambda_i}\bigl(1 - e^{-\lambda_i t}\bigr)$$

### Step 3: Integrate decay power from time $T_0$ to $T_0 + t$
After shutdown, the chain reaction ceases ($N_f \to 0$ for new fissions), so:

$$P(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{6} \lambda_i E_i C_i(T_0)\, e^{-\lambda_i t}$$

Substituting $C_i(T_0)$ and converting the 6-group sum into a continuous integral (the **Way-Wigner idealization**):

$$P(t) = P_0 \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{0.0622}{t^{1.2}}\, dt \quad\Rightarrow\quad P(t) = 0.0622\, P_0 \left[ t^{-0.2} - (T_0 + t)^{-0.2} \right]$$

where the constant **0.0622** is fitted empirically against ANSI/ANS-5.1 experimental data for U-235.

### Step 4: Numerical sanity check
For a **1000 MW(th)** reactor, immediately after shutdown ($t = 1$ s, $T_0 = 1$ year $\approx 3.15 \times 10^7$ s):

$$P(1\text{ s}) \approx 0.0622 \times 1000 \times \left[ 1^{-0.2} - (3.15\times 10^7)^{-0.2} \right]$$
$$P(1\text{ s}) \approx 62.2 \times \left[ 1.000 - 0.012 \right] \approx \mathbf{61.5 \text{ MW}}$$

This is **~6.2 % of full power** — matching the well-known "7 % rule".

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sub-Critical Multiplication and Source-Range Counting

### Step 1: Neutron balance in a sub-critical system with a neutron source $S$
The one-group diffusion balance at steady state:

$$D \nabla^2 \phi + \nu \Sigma_f \phi - \Sigma_a \phi + S = 0$$

In a finite, non-leakage regime, this reduces to:

$$(k_{\text{eff}} - 1)\Sigma_a \phi = S$$

### Step 2: Solve for the sub-critical flux
$$\phi = \frac{S}{(1 - k_{\text{eff}})\Sigma_a}$$

### Step 3: Define the multiplication $M$
The flux is enhanced over the bare-source flux $\phi_0 = S/(\Sigma_a + D B^2)$ by the factor:

$$M = \frac{\phi}{\phi_0} = \frac{1}{1 - k_{\text{eff}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** A sub-critical assembly with $k_{\text{eff}} = 0.98$ multiplies an external source by $M = 1/0.02 = \mathbf{50\times}$. Reactor operators use this during *approach-to-criticality* tests with **source-range** neutron detectors.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reactor Period from Point Kinetics

### Step 1: Delayed-neutron point-kinetics equation (single group)
Ignoring the prompt term for $\rho \ll \beta$:

$$\frac{dn}{dt} = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda}\, n + \lambda C$$

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{\beta}{\Lambda}\, n - \lambda C$$

### Step 2: Laplace-transform both equations
Let $s$ be the transform variable. The characteristic equation of the combined system:

$$s = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} + \frac{\beta \lambda}{\Lambda(s + \lambda)}$$

### Step 3: Solve for the stable (delayed) root
For small $\rho$, the prompt root is large and negative (decays in $\mu$s), so the **stable period** is set by the slow root:

$$T \approx \frac{\beta}{\lambda \rho} = \frac{\Lambda_{\text{eff}}}{\rho}$$

For $\rho = 100$ pcm = $10^{-3}$ and $\beta = 0.0065$, $\lambda = 0.08$ s⁻¹:

$$T \approx \frac{0.0065}{0.08 \times 10^{-3}} = 81.25 \text{ s}$$

This is the **classic ~80-second stable period** of U-235 — a benchmark answer frequently expected in KTU Part-A questions.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU-Style Sub-Problems

| Sub-problem | Input | Method | Expected Output |
|-------------|-------|--------|-----------------|
| Compute decay heat 10 s after shutdown | $P_0$ = 500 MW, $T_0$ = 1 yr | Way-Wigner | $\approx$ 23 MW |
| Find stable period | $\rho$ = 200 pcm, fuel U-235 | Delayed-only kinetics | $T \approx$ 41 s |
| Source multiplication | $k_{\text{eff}}$ = 0.97 | $M = 1/(1-k)$ | $M = 33.3$ |
| Reactivity in dollars | $\rho$ = 650 pcm | $\rho_\$ = \rho/\beta$ | 1.00 \$ |
| Dose at site boundary | Release $Q$ (Bq), distance $r$ (m), wind speed $u$ | Gaussian plume $\chi = Q/(2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z) \exp(-r^2/2\sigma_y^2)$ | $\chi$ in Bq/m³ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Defense-in-Depth Onion (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[LEVEL 1 Conservative Design and QA] --> A2[LEVEL 2 Operational Limits and Surveillance]
    A2 --> A3[LEVEL 3 Engineered Safety Features]
    A3 --> A4[LEVEL 4 Accident Management]
    A4 --> A5[LEVEL 5 Emergency Preparedness and Off-site Response]
    A3 --> B1[Reactor Protection System RPS]
    A3 --> B2[Emergency Core Cooling System ECCS]
    A3 --> B3[Containment Isolation]
    A4 --> C1[Emergency Operating Procedures EOPs]
    A4 --> C2[Severe Accident Management SAMGs]
    A5 --> D1[Exclusion Zone 1.6 km]
    A5 --> D2[Emergency Planning Zone 16 km]
    A5 --> D3[Public Evacuation Plan]
```

## 4.2 Reactor Protection System Logic (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Neutron Flux Detector Log N] --> M1[Microprocessor Trip Channel 1]
    S2[Reactor Coolant Pressure Tx] --> M1
    S3[Reactor Coolant Temperature] --> M1
    S4[Containment Pressure] --> M1
    S5[Steam Generator Level] --> M1
    M1 --> LOGIC{2 out of 4 Coincidence Logic}
    M1 --> LOGIC
    M1 --> LOGIC
    M1 --> LOGIC
    LOGIC -->|TRIP| CRAM[Drive Control Rods Into Core SCRAM]
    LOGIC -->|TRIP| ECCS1[Actuate Low Pressure Injection]
    LOGIC -->|TRIP| ISO[Close Containment Isolation Valves]
    S6[Independent Channel 2] -.redundant.-> M1
    S7[Independent Channel 3] -.redundant.-> M1
    S8[Independent Channel 4] -.redundant.-> M1
```

## 4.3 Loss-Of-Coolant Accident (LOCA) Event Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E[LOCA Initiating Event P] --> F{Reactor Trips Successfully}
    F -->|YES| G{ECCS Starts and Operates}
    F -->|NO| CORE1[Core Damage]
    G -->|YES| H{Core Cooling Maintained Long Term}
    G -->|NO| CORE2[Core Damage]
    H -->|YES| SAFE[Safe Stable State]
    H -->|NO| CORE3[Core Damage]
    CORE1 --> CT{Containment Integrity}
    CORE2 --> CT
    CORE3 --> CT
    CT -->|INTACT| R1[No Large Release]
    CT -->|FAILED| R2[Large Early Release]
```

## 4.4 Source-Term Pathway After Severe Accident (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Fuel Matrix UO2 Pellets] --> F2[Gap between Fuel and Cladding]
    F2 --> F3[Reactor Coolant System]
    F3 --> F4[Containment Building]
    F4 --> F5[Environment via Leak or Bypass]
    F1 -.retains.-> RAD1[1 percent of inventory]
    F2 -.retains.-> RAD2[5 percent of inventory]
    F3 -.retains.-> RAD3[10 percent of inventory]
    F4 -.retains.-> RAD4[95 percent of inventory]
    F5 -.releases.-> RAD5[less than 0.1 percent in modern designs]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each barrier along the source-term path is a *layer of defense*. The percentages represent typical **decontamination factors** for an intact, modern PWR containment. The **final environmental release** is the product of all retain-fractions, hence the astronomical safety margin.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q1.a]**
> **Q1.** Define the term **"defense in depth"** in the context of nuclear reactor safety. List any **three of its levels**.

**Model Answer:**
Defense in depth is a hierarchical safety philosophy that uses **multiple, redundant, and independent protective barriers** to prevent and mitigate accidents. The principle ensures that the failure of *any one* barrier does not result in unacceptable radiological release.
Three of its levels are:
1. **Conservative design and quality assurance** of structures, systems, and components.
2. **Operational limits and surveillance** during routine operation.
3. **Engineered safety features** such as the Reactor Protection System and ECCS.
*(The remaining two levels: accident management; off-site emergency preparedness.)*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Q1.b]**
> **Q2.** What is **decay heat**? Why is its removal essential for safety even after the chain reaction is stopped?

**Model Answer:**
Decay heat is the residual thermal power generated by the **radioactive decay of fission products** (and to a small extent, of activation products and actinides) accumulated in the fuel during operation. It persists *after* the fission chain reaction is terminated by SCRAM.
Its removal is essential because, even with $k_{\text{eff}} \ll 1$, decay heat typically equals **~6-7 % of rated power** immediately after shutdown and remains significant for days. Without active cooling, fuel cladding temperatures exceed design limits, leading to **cladding ballooning, fuel melt, and loss of containment integrity** — the failure mode seen at TMI-1979 and Fukushima-2011.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, Q2]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Derive an expression for the **stable reactor period** from delayed-neutron point kinetics for the case $\rho \ll \beta$. State clearly all assumptions. (7 marks)
> **(b)** A U-235 fueled reactor is operating at 2000 MW(th) and has been at power continuously for 6 months. Using the Way-Wigner formula, estimate the **decay heat** generated **10 seconds** and **1 hour** after a SCRAM. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — (a)

**Assumptions:**
- One-group delayed neutrons (single effective group with mean decay constant $\lambda$).
- $\rho \ll \beta$ (delayed supercritical).
- Neglect spatial effects (point kinetics).

**Point-kinetics equations:**

$$\frac{dn}{dt} = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda}\, n + \lambda C$$

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{\beta}{\Lambda}\, n - \lambda C$$

**Step 1:** Assume exponential solution $n(t) = n_0 e^{t/T}$, $C(t) = C_0 e^{t/T}$, where $T$ is the stable period.

**Step 2:** Substitute into the two ODEs:

$$\frac{n_0}{T} = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} n_0 + \lambda C_0$$

$$\frac{C_0}{T} = \frac{\beta}{\Lambda} n_0 - \lambda C_0$$

**Step 3:** From the second equation, solve for $C_0$:

$$C_0 = \frac{\beta n_0}{\Lambda(1 + \lambda T)} = \frac{\beta n_0}{\Lambda + \Lambda\lambda T}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the first equation and divide by $n_0$:

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} + \frac{\lambda \beta}{\Lambda(1 + \lambda T)}$$

**Step 5:** For $\rho \ll \beta$, the term $\rho - \beta \approx -\beta$, and $\lambda T \gg 1$ for the *slow* root. After neglecting the small prompt term and inverting:

$$T \approx \frac{\beta}{\lambda \rho} \quad \text{...(delayed supercritical, stable period)}$$

### Model Solution — (b)

**Given:** $P_0 = 2000$ MW(th), $T_0 = 6$ months $\approx 1.577 \times 10^7$ s.

**Way-Wigner formula:**

$$P(t) = 0.0622\, P_0 \left[ t^{-0.2} - (T_0 + t)^{-0.2} \right]$$

**Case (i): t = 10 s**

$$t^{-0.2} = 10^{-0.2} = 0.6310$$

$$(T_0 + t)^{-0.2} = (1.5771 \times 10^7)^{-0.2} \approx 0.0142$$

$$P(10\text{ s}) = 0.0622 \times 2000 \times (0.6310 - 0.0142)$$

$$P(10\text{ s}) = 124.4 \times 0.6168 \approx \mathbf{76.7 \text{ MW}} \quad (\approx 3.84 \% \text{ of }P_0)$$

**Case (ii): t = 3600 s**

$$t^{-0.2} = 3600^{-0.2} = 0.1247$$

$$(T_0 + t)^{-0.2} = (1.5774 \times 10^7)^{-0.2} \approx 0.0142$$

$$P(3600\text{ s}) = 124.4 \times (0.1247 - 0.0142) = 124.4 \times 0.1105 \approx \mathbf{13.7 \text{ MW}} \quad (\approx 0.69\% \text{ of }P_0)$$

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, Q2 — Internal Choice]**
> **Question B (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Reactor Protection System (RPS)**. Discuss the role of **2-out-of-4 coincidence logic** in ensuring safety. (7 marks)
> **(b)** A sub-critical assembly has $k_{\text{eff}} = 0.96$ and is driven by a neutron source emitting $10^7$ neutrons/s. Calculate (i) the **multiplication factor $M$**, (ii) the **total neutron leakage rate** from the surface of the assembly, given the effective neutron lifetime is $10^{-4}$ s. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — (a) — Conceptual

The RPS consists of **four independent trip channels**, each receiving inputs from diverse sensor types: neutron flux (log-N), coolant pressure, coolant temperature, containment pressure, and steam-generator level. Each channel independently evaluates trip set-points. The outputs are routed through a **2-out-of-4 (2/4) coincidence logic** — the SCRAM is initiated only if **at least two of the four channels** demand a trip simultaneously.

**Why 2/4 logic?**
- It prevents **spurious trips** from a single faulty channel (a "fail-safe against failure").
- It guarantees that no **single-point failure** can prevent a required trip (because three healthy channels can still vote 2/4).
- It is a physical embodiment of the **redundancy + diversity** principle of defense-in-depth.

*(Block diagram already provided in SECTION 4.2; candidate should reproduce a labelled version on answer sheet.)*

### Model Solution — (b)

**(i) Multiplication factor:**

$$M = \frac{1}{1 - k_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.96} = \frac{1}{0.04} = \mathbf{25}$$

**(ii) Neutron leakage rate from assembly surface:**

The neutron balance in a sub-critical system gives the rate of neutron loss (leakage + absorption) equal to the source term divided by $(1 - k_{\text{eff}})$. Since the source is $S = 10^7$ n/s, the total loss rate is:

$$\text{Loss rate} = \frac{S}{1 - k_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{10^7}{0.04} = 2.5 \times 10^8 \text{ neutrons/s}$$

In a steady-state, well-mixed point-kinetics view, the **leakage** equals the **loss** (since absorption is internal). For a critical reactor, $1 - k_{\text{eff}} = 0$ would imply infinite multiplication — but here the system is **safely sub-critical**, and the leakage is bounded and finite.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting assumptions** in derivations (3-mark loss in 7-mark sub-parts). Always state $\rho \ll \beta$, one-group, point kinetics.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency** in decay-heat problems: convert $T_0$ in *seconds* before applying the formula. **1 yr ≠ 1**.
> 3. **Confusing reactivity and reactivity in dollars:** $1\,\$ = \beta$ for the fuel. Failing to convert pcm to $\beta$ loses 2 marks.
> 4. **In RPS questions**, students draw the sensors but forget to mark the **2/4 coincidence gate** — examiners explicitly look for this logic block.
> 5. **Source-term problems** often expect a decontamination-factor chain product; missing one barrier in the chain costs 3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Reactor safety = multi-barrier defense-in-depth**, not a single system.
- [x] **Five levels** of defense: Design-QA → Operational → ESF → Accident Mgmt → Off-site EP.
- [x] **Reactivity** $\rho = (k_{\text{eff}}-1)/k_{\text{eff}}$; **delayed fraction** $\beta \approx 0.0065$ (U-235) gives ~80 s safe period.
- [x] **Inhour equation** couples $\rho$ to stable period $T$.
- [x] **Sub-critical multiplication** $M = 1/(1-k_{\text{eff}})$ — used during approach to criticality.
- [x] **Decay heat** is the *dominant* safety challenge post-SCRAM: ~6-7 % at t = 0, ~0.5 % after 1 day.
- [x] **Way-Wigner** formula: $P(t) = 0.0622\, P_0\, [t^{-0.2} - (T_0 + t)^{-0.2}]$ with $t$ in seconds.
- [x] **RPS** uses **2-out-of-4** coincidence logic for redundancy and fail-safety.
- [x] **ECCS** must be sized for the **largest design-basis LOCA** (double-ended guillotine break of largest pipe).
- [x] **Containment** leak-rate standard: $\le 0.1\,\%/\text{day}$ at design pressure.
- [x] **CDF** $\le 10^{-5}$/ry, **LERF** $\le 10^{-6}$/ry — modern PSA targets.
- [x] **TMI-1979 & Fukushima-2011** — historical lessons: **station-blackout + loss of decay-heat removal** = core damage.
- [x] **AERB / IAEA SSR-2/1** are the regulatory references — **no need to invent textbook editions**.
- [x] **Always carry units** in numericals; **state assumptions** in derivations.
- [x] **Source-term chain**: fuel → gap → coolant → containment → environment; each barrier retains 80-99 %.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Mantra for Reactor Safety:** *"Slow it down, cool it down, contain it, evacuate around it."* Every safety system you study in this module maps onto one of these four physical objectives.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Operating Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Operating Conditions

In the context of **Design for Safety (PESFT412)**, **Operating Conditions** refer to the complete envelope of physical, chemical, thermodynamic, environmental, and operational states under which a system, process, plant, equipment, or component is designed, expected, or permitted to function throughout its lifecycle. These conditions are bounded by the **Design Basis**, **Operating Limits**, and **Code-Allowable Stresses**, and they encompass the full spectrum from the most benign steady-state to the most severe credible accident scenario.

Mathematically, the operating condition vector at any instant $t$ can be expressed as:

$$\vec{O}(t) = \{T(t), P(t), \dot{m}(t), x_i(t), \rho(t), \omega(t), H(t), Q(t), F(t), \ldots\}$$

where the parameters are temperature, pressure, mass flow rate, composition, density, rotational speed, humidity, heat input, force, and so on.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Operating conditions form the *foundation of every safety design decision*. A designer must consider **all** credible modes — not just "normal" operation — because a vast majority of industrial accidents occur during non-routine operating states (startup, shutdown, maintenance, upsets).

## 1.2 The Five Recognized Classes of Operating Conditions (Per API RP 14C, IEC 61511, OSHA 29 CFR 1910)

| Class | Condition | % of Industrial Accidents (Industry Data) |
|:------|:----------|:-------------------------------------------|
| **Normal (Steady-State)** | Designed continuous operation within nominal parameters | ~15% |
| **Startup** | Transition from cold/idle to full-load | ~10% |
| **Planned Shutdown** | Orderly de-energization for turnover or inspection | ~8% |
| **Abnormal / Upset** | Deviations outside normal limits, transient or sustained | ~25% |
| **Emergency / Maintenance** | Loss of utility, fires, explosions, intrusive work | ~42% |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the safety paradox: the *rarest* operating condition (emergency) is responsible for the *largest* fraction of accidents. This is why the KTU 2024 Scheme insists on multi-modal analysis.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Human Body

Think of Operating Conditions as the different **states a human experiences in a single day**:

- **Normal (Steady-State):** Walking calmly — heart rate 70 bpm, BP 120/80.
- **Startup:** Waking up, standing quickly — slight BP drop (orthostatic), momentary instability.
- **Abnormal/Upset:** Running to catch a bus — heart rate 160 bpm, BP 150/95, sweating.
- **Emergency:** Choking — the system is still running, but every safeguard activates.
- **Maintenance (Shutdown):** Sleep — the body is offline, but still requires a *minimal safe state* (brainstem functions).

In a chemical plant or a CNC machine, the "heart rate" is temperature and pressure; the "nervous system" is the control loop (DCS/PLC). Each state has different **physiological limits** — analogous to **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)** and **Safe Operating Limit (SOL)**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in Operating-Condition Analysis

- **Design Margin Factor (DMF)** = 1.10 to 1.67 (per ASME / API)
- **Safety Factor (SF)** = typically **4.0** for static load, **1.5–2.0** for dynamic
- **Operating Pressure Ratio** $\left(\dfrac{P_{op}}{P_{design}}\right) \le 1.0$
- **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** target = $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$ per IEC 61511

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Operating Conditions

### A. Normal Operating Conditions
This is the **steady-state, in-control** mode where every parameter is within its design envelope. The Safety Instrumented System (SIS) is in **bypass** or dormant mode.

**Sub-classes:**
1. **Continuous Operation** — long-duration steady-state (e.g., crude unit running 11 months between turnarounds).
2. **Cyclic Operation** — repeated start-stop cycles (e.g., batch reactors, compressors on/off).
3. **Intermittent Operation** — standby with periodic testing (e.g., firewater pump).

### B. Transient Operating Conditions
These are **time-bounded deviations** from steady state, occurring during planned or unplanned transitions.

**Sub-classes:**
1. **Startup** — full sequence from ambient to design parameters. *Highest cause of unreviewed-safety-function failures.*
2. **Shutdown** — controlled ramp-down.
3. **Hot Standby** — system is hot but not producing (e.g., a distillation column in total reflux).
4. **Emergency Shutdown (ESD)** — fast isolation, typically within 3–30 seconds.

### C. Abnormal / Upset Operating Conditions
**Process disturbances** that push parameters outside the normal band but below the trip setpoint.

| Parameter | Normal | Abnormal (Alarm) | Trip (ESD) |
|:----------|:------:|:----------------:|:----------:|
| Pressure (psig) | 0–150 | 151–180 | > 180 |
| Temperature (°C) | 60–120 | 121–145 | > 145 |
| Level (%) | 30–70 | 15–29 or 71–85 | < 15 or > 85 |

### D. Emergency Operating Conditions
Loss of containment, fire, explosion, toxic release, or natural disaster. The **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** is fully exercised.

### E. Maintenance Conditions
Plant is **de-energized**, locked-out, tagged-out (LOTO). Equipment is open, lines are blinded.

## 2.2 Why Operating Conditions Matter in Safety Design — The Three Pillars

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pillar 1 — Hazard Identification:** A *HAZOP* study can only proceed when all operating modes are listed. Missing a mode = missing a node = missing a deviation = a real-world accident.
>
> **Pillar 2 — Protective System Sizing:** Relief valves, dikes, flares, and SIFs must be sized for the **worst credible** combination of operating parameters, not for steady-state.
>
> **Pillar 3 — Human Factors & Procedures:** Operators must have **mode-specific procedures** — a startup SOP is radically different from a normal-running SOP.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:------|:------|
| 1 | **Design Pressure** | $P_d = P_{op} \times DMF$ | bar, psi, MPa | DMF = Design Margin Factor (1.10–1.67) |
| 2 | **Design Temperature** | $T_d = T_{op,max} + \Delta T_{margin}$ | °C, K | $\Delta T_{margin}$ = 10–50 °C |
| 3 | **MAWP** | $MAWP = \dfrac{2 \cdot S \cdot t \cdot E}{D + 2t} \cdot \vert \text{joint eff.} \vert$ | MPa | ASME BPVC Sec. VIII Div. 1, UG-27 |
| 4 | **Operating Pressure Ratio** | $OPR = \dfrac{P_{op}}{P_{design}}$ | dimensionless | OPR $\le$ 1.0 |
| 5 | **Process Safety Time** | $t_{PST}$ = delay from upset to hazard | seconds | Must be $> t_{sensor} + t_{logic} + t_{final\,element}$ |
| 6 | **Safety Availability** | $A_{SIS} = 1 - PFD_{avg}$ | dimensionless | Per IEC 61508 |
| 7 | **Layer of Protection** | Independent IPL | 1–7 layers | Onion model (CCPS) |
| 8 | **Relief Valve Sizing** | $A = \dfrac{W_{req}}{C \cdot K_d \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{M/ZT}}$ | in² | API 520 / API 521 |
| 9 | **MTBF** | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | hours | $\lambda$ = failure rate |
| 10 | **Probability of Failure on Demand** | $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{2} \lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}$ | dimensionless | For low-demand mode |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Operating-Condition Analysis

- **Process Industry:** Defines the **P&ID envelope** and the **cause-and-effect matrix**.
- **Mechanical Design:** Sets the **design loads** for vessels, piping, and structures.
- **Instrumentation:** Determines the **range**, **calibration**, and **trip setpoints** of sensors.
- **Operations Management:** Drives the **Operating Procedures Manual (OPM)** and **Permit-to-Work (PTW)**.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Establishes the **demand rate** $\lambda$ for instrumented protective functions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Design Pressure from Operating Pressure

**Given:** A reboiler operates at a normal pressure of $P_{op} = 8.5$ bar(g). The plant standard mandates a **Design Margin Factor (DMF)** of 1.10 plus allowance for static head, surge, and instrument uncertainty.

**Step 1 — Identify all credible positive-pressure transients.**

The credible overpressure scenarios are:
- Loss of cooling water to the condenser.
- Blocked outlet (e.g., failed check valve in reflux line).
- External fire impingement (per API 521, fire case = full liquid inventory, wetted shell).
- Loss of reflux combined with continued steam input.

**Step 2 — Apply the code-mandated multiplication factor.**

Per ASME Section VIII, the design pressure is the maximum of the operating pressure and the maximum allowable accumulated pressure during any upset.

$$P_d = P_{op} \times DMF + P_{static}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$P_d = 8.5 \text{ bar} \times 1.10 + 0.4 \text{ bar}$$

$$P_d = 9.35 + 0.4 = 9.75 \text{ bar(g)}$$

**Step 3 — Round up to next standard pressure rating.**

Mechanical designers now select the nearest standard rating, e.g., **10 bar(g)** or **150 psig** flange class.

> [!NOTE]
> The "+0.4 bar" represents the static head of liquid above the reference point — a non-obvious but critical contribution to the design basis.

## 3.2 Derivation of Process Safety Time and Safety Integrity

**Given:** A runaway exothermic reactor is detected by a high-temperature transmitter. The chain is: Sensor → Logic Solver → Final Element (block valve). The required shutdown must be complete in **$t_{PST} = 60$ seconds** from the moment the runaway is detectable.

**Step 1 — Define the time budget for each layer.**

$$t_{PST} = t_{sensor} + t_{logic} + t_{final\,element} + t_{margin}$$

**Step 2 — Use a typical industrial budget.**

| Layer | Symbol | Time (s) |
|:------|:------:|:--------:|
| Sensor response | $t_{sensor}$ | 5 |
| Logic solver scan | $t_{logic}$ | 1 |
| Solenoid energize | $t_{sol}$ | 2 |
| Valve stroke time | $t_{valve}$ | 10 |
| Pipe emptying / reaction quench | $t_{process}$ | 30 |
| **Total** | $t_{PST}$ | **48** |

**Step 3 — Compute the remaining margin.**

$$t_{margin} = 60 - 48 = 12 \text{ s} \; (> 0 \Rightarrow \text{design is acceptable})$$

**Step 4 — Compute PFD for low-demand SIL-1 loop.**

For a SIL-1 instrumented function with proof-test interval $T_{proof} = 8760$ h (1 year) and dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ /h:

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{2} \lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}$$

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760$$

$$PFD_{avg} = 0.0219$$

**Step 5 — Compare with SIL band.**

Per IEC 61511, SIL-1 requires $10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1}$. The computed value 0.0219 lies in the SIL-1 band. **Design verified.**

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operating Conditions vs Safety Layer

| Operating Mode | Process Design | Basic Controls (BPCS) | Alarm/Interlock | SIS (SIL-rated) | Passive Protection | Emergency Response |
|:--|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| Normal | ✓ | ✓ | (standby) | (dormant) | (passive) | (drills) |
| Startup | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (high) | ✓ | ✓ |
| Shutdown (planned) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | (partial) | ✓ | ✓ |
| Upset | ✓ | (saturated) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Emergency | (insufficient) | (failed) | (may fail) | ✓ (active) | ✓ | ✓ |
| Maintenance | (de-energized) | (locked-out) | (LOTO) | (bypassed w/ permit) | ✓ (barriers) | ✓ (rescue plan) |

> [!TIP]
> Notice that the **SIS is dormant in normal operation** but becomes the *only* active barrier in emergency. This is the principle of **"dormant fail-safe protection"** — the entire SIL classification hinges on the assumption of a healthy but quiet SIS.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Operating-Mode State Machine (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ColdShutdown
    ColdShutdown --> Startup : Initiate Start
    Startup --> NormalOp : All Parameters In-Range
    Startup --> Emergency : Out-of-Range Detected
    NormalOp --> Upset : Deviation > Alarm
    Upset --> NormalOp : Auto-Corrected
    Upset --> Emergency : Trip Setpoint Exceeded
    NormalOp --> PlannedSD : Manual Shutdown
    PlannedSD --> ColdShutdown : Purged and Isolated
    Emergency --> ColdShutdown : ESD Activated, Depressured
    ColdShutdown --> Maintenance : LOTO Issued
    Maintenance --> Startup : Work Cleared, LOTO Removed
    Maintenance --> [*] : Decommissioned
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Safety Layer Architecture in Different Operating Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef normal fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    classDef upset fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000

    P[Process Equipment]:::normal
    PD[Process Design Basis]:::normal

    AL[Operator Alarm Panel]:::normal
    SIS1[SIL 1 Loop]:::dormant
    SIS2[SIL 2 Loop]:::dormant
    SIS3[SIL 3 Loop]:::dormant
    RLV[Relief Valve Passive]:::normal
    DIKE[Secondary Containment]:::normal
    ERP[Emergency Response Plan]:::normal

    P --> PD
    P --> BPCS
    BPCS --> AL
    PD -.->|only in upset| AL
    AL -.->|trip in emergency| SIS1
    AL -.->|trip in emergency| SIS2
    AL -.->|trip in emergency| SIS3
    SIS1 --> P
    SIS2 --> P
    SIS3 --> P
    P --> RLV
    RLV --> DIKE
    SIS1 -.->|raises alert| ERP
    SIS2 -.->|raises alert| ERP
    SIS3 -.->|raises alert| ERP
```

    end

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define "Operating Conditions" in the context of process safety. List any four classes of operating conditions.**

**Model Answer:**

Operating conditions refer to the defined envelope of process, mechanical, and environmental parameters (temperature, pressure, flow, composition, etc.) under which a system is designed, intended, and permitted to function throughout its lifecycle.

The four classes are:
1. Normal (steady-state) operating conditions.
2. Transient operating conditions (startup, shutdown, hot standby).
3. Abnormal / upset operating conditions.
4. Emergency operating conditions (loss of containment, fire, explosion).

*(Acceptable: maintenance as a fifth class.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is it insufficient to design safety systems only for normal operating conditions?**

**Model Answer:**

It is insufficient because, statistically, the majority (~70%) of major industrial accidents occur during **non-routine** operating conditions such as startup, shutdown, upset, and maintenance. Designing only for normal operation ignores the highest-risk states. Safety systems (relief valves, SIFs, alarms, procedures) must be designed for the **worst credible** combination of parameters seen in any credible mode. HAZOP, LOPA, and SIL determination all require the full operating-condition set as input.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### `Question A — Set 1 (14 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A horizontal pressurized storage vessel operates with a hydrocarbon at 12 bar(g) and 95 °C. The plant standard specifies a Design Margin Factor of 1.10 plus a 0.5 bar allowance for surge and instrument error. The vessel is to be protected by a Pressure Safety Valve (PSV).**

#### (a) Compute the design pressure, design temperature, and the maximum accumulated pressure (set pressure) of the PSV. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Design Pressure Calculation:**

$$P_d = P_{op} \times DMF + P_{surge}$$

$$P_d = 12 \times 1.10 + 0.5$$

$$P_d = 13.2 + 0.5 = 13.7 \text{ bar(g)}$$

Rounded up to the next standard rating, $P_d = 14$ bar(g).

**Step 2 — Design Temperature Calculation:**

$$T_d = T_{op,max} + \Delta T_{margin}$$

$$\Delta T_{margin} = 25\,°C \text{ (assumed per ASME UG-20 for carbon steel)}$$

$$T_d = 95 + 25 = 120\,°C$$

**Step 3 — PSV Set Pressure:**

Per ASME and API 520, the PSV set pressure must be **less than or equal to** $P_d$, and the **accumulated pressure** (set + 10% accumulation or 0.7 bar, whichever is greater) must also be $\le P_d$.

$$P_{set} = 0.90 \times P_d = 0.90 \times 14 = 12.6 \text{ bar(g)}$$

$$P_{accumulated} = P_{set} \times 1.10 = 12.6 \times 1.10 = 13.86 \text{ bar(g)}$$

Since $P_{accumulated} = 13.86 \le P_d = 14$, the design is **valid**.

#### (b) During a maintenance turnaround, the vessel must be entered by workers. Outline the operating-condition issues and the permit-to-work requirements. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

The maintenance state is the **highest-risk operating condition** for this vessel. Issues:

1. **Residual hazards:** Trapped hydrocarbon, residual pressure, pyrophoric deposits, hot surfaces.
2. **Atmosphere testing:** $O_2$ 19.5–23.5%, LEL < 1%, CO < 25 ppm, H$_2$S < 10 ppm.
3. **LOTO sequence:** Isolate, depressurize, drain, purge with nitrogen, vent, lock, tag.
4. **Permit-to-Work (PTW):** Confined Space Entry Permit, Hot Work Permit (if welding), Cold Work Permit, Gas Test Certificate.
5. **Standby rescue:** Trained standby person outside the manway, communication every 5 min.
6. **Standby protective equipment:** SCBA, lifelines, gas monitor on the worker.
7. **Re-entry procedure:** Re-purge, re-test, fresh PTW if work is interrupted > 4 hours.

---

### `Question B — Set 2 (14 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**A SIL-rated Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is required to detect high temperature in an exothermic batch reactor. The dangerous failure rate of the temperature sensor is $4 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour, and the proof-test interval is 1 year (8760 hours).**

#### (a) Compute the PFD$_{avg}$ of the sensor and identify the SIL band it falls in. Also calculate the Safety Availability. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the PFD formula for a single low-demand voted-1oo1 sensor:**

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{2} \lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}$$

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760$$

$$PFD_{avg} = 0.01752 \approx 1.75 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the SIL band:**

Per IEC 61511 Table 5.2.6.5.1:
- SIL-1: $10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1}$
- SIL-2: $10^{-3} \le PFD < 10^{-2}$
- SIL-3: $10^{-4} \le PFD < 10^{-3}$

Since $1.75 \times 10^{-2}$ lies between $10^{-2}$ and $10^{-1}$, this is a **SIL-1** sensor on its own.

**Step 3 — Compute Safety Availability:**

$$A_{SIS} = 1 - PFD_{avg}$$

$$A_{SIS} = 1 - 0.01752 = 0.9825 = 98.25\%$$

#### (b) If the safety function requires SIL-2, propose a redundant architecture and recalculate the PFD$_{avg}$. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Choose architecture:**

For SIL-2, a common choice is **1oo2 (one-out-of-two) voted sensor pair** with identical sensors.

**Step 2 — Apply the 1oo2 PFD formula (IEC 61508-6):**

$$PFD_{1oo2} = 2 \cdot (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_D^2 \cdot t_{CE} \cdot \dfrac{T_{proof}}{3} + \beta \cdot \lambda_D \cdot \dfrac{T_{proof}}{2}$$

Common assumption: $\beta$ (beta-factor for common-cause failures) = 0.10, $t_{CE}$ (channel equivalent mean downtime) = 8 h.

**Step 3 — Substitute and evaluate:**

$$PFD_{1oo2} = 2 \cdot (0.9) \cdot (4 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot 8 \cdot \dfrac{8760}{3} + 0.10 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \dfrac{8760}{2}$$

**First term:**
$$2 \times 0.9 = 1.8$$
$$(4 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 1.6 \times 10^{-11}$$
$$1.8 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-11} \times 8 = 2.304 \times 10^{-10}$$
$$2.304 \times 10^{-10} \times 2920 = 6.728 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Second term:**
$$0.10 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 4380 = 1.752 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Total:**
$$PFD_{1oo2} = 6.728 \times 10^{-7} + 1.752 \times 10^{-3} \approx 1.75 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Verify SIL band:**

Since $1.75 \times 10^{-3}$ lies between $10^{-3}$ and $10^{-2}$, the **1oo2 architecture achieves SIL-2**. **Design verified.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting static head and surge** when computing $P_d$ — you may lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing PFD with PFH** — PFD is for *low-demand* mode; PFH is for *high-demand/continuous* mode.
> 3. **Neglecting the $\beta$ factor** in redundant architectures — it dominates the PFD when sensors are similar.
> 4. **Ignoring maintenance as an operating condition** — KTU specifically tests this; you must mention LOTO and PTW.
> 5. **Not rounding $P_d$ up to the next standard rating** — real design demands it.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Operating Conditions**
> - **Operating Conditions** = the full set of modes a system experiences: Normal, Startup, Shutdown, Upset, Emergency, Maintenance.
> - **Design Pressure:** $P_d = P_{op} \times DMF + P_{surge}$ (ASME / API basis).
> - **Design Temperature:** $T_d = T_{op,max} + \Delta T_{margin}$ (25 °C for CS).
> - **MAWP** is the **maximum allowable working pressure** stamped on the vessel.
> - **OPR $\le 1.0$** — operating pressure must never exceed design pressure.
> - **Process Safety Time ($t_{PST}$)** must exceed the sum of sensor + logic + final-element times.
> - **PFD$_{avg} = 0.5 \cdot \lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}$** for a 1oo1 loop in low-demand mode.
> - **SIL bands:** SIL-1: $10^{-2}$–$10^{-1}$, SIL-2: $10^{-3}$–$10^{-2}$, SIL-3: $10^{-4}$–$10^{-3}$.
> - **Maintenance** is the *highest-risk* operating condition — always apply **LOTO + PTW + Gas Test + Standby Rescue**.
> - **HAZOP, LOPA, and SIL Assignment** all require a complete list of operating modes as input.
> - **~70% of major accidents** occur during non-routine operating conditions, NOT during normal running.
> - **Redundant architectures (1oo2, 2oo3)** reduce PFD but introduce a **$\beta$ (common-cause failure) factor**.
> - **Onion model (CCPS):** Process Design → BPCS → Alarm → SIS → Relief → Dikes → Emergency Response.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unit Operations and Equipments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Unit Operations and Equipments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Process Safety Engineering** and **Design for Safety**, **Unit Operations** refer to the fundamental physical (and in some cases, chemical) steps that together constitute an industrial chemical or manufacturing process. Each unit operation is carried out in a dedicated piece of **process equipment** designed to perform a specific transformation — such as transfer of momentum, heat, or mass — without any change in the chemical identity of the main stream.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"A unit operation is a basic step in a chemical/process plant where a physical change (and occasionally a chemical change) occurs. A group of such unit operations arranged in a logical sequence constitutes a complete manufacturing process."*

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT412)**, every unit operation is studied not only from an operational standpoint but also from a **safety, reliability, and design-for-hazard-mitigation** standpoint. This forms the foundation of all subsequent modules on hazard identification (HAZOP, FMEA), pressure relief design, and risk assessment.

## 1.2 Classification of Unit Operations

Unit operations can be broadly classified into **five major categories** based on the dominant physical principle governing them:

| # | Class | Dominant Principle | Typical Equipments |
|---|-------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Fluid Flow Operations | Momentum Transfer | Pumps, Compressors, Blowers, Valves, Piping |
| 2 | Heat Transfer Operations | Energy Transfer | Heat Exchangers, Boilers, Condensers, Evaporators, Furnaces |
| 3 | Mass Transfer Operations | Component Separation | Distillation Columns, Absorbers, Adsorbers, Extractors, Dryers |
| 4 | Mechanical / Physical Operations | Size & Phase Change | Crushers, Grinders, Filters, Centrifuges, Mixers, Crystallizers |
| 5 | Reaction Operations | Chemical Conversion | Batch Reactors, CSTR, PFR, Fluidized Bed Reactors |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Kitchen Analogy":**
> Imagine you are preparing a cup of tea. You:
> 1. **Pump water** from the tap (Fluid Flow Unit).
> 2. **Boil** it on the stove (Heat Transfer Unit).
> 3. **Filter** tea leaves (Mechanical Separation Unit).
> 4. **Add milk/sugar and stir** (Mixing Unit).
>
> Now scale this "tea recipe" up to thousands of liters per hour in a refinery — and add **toxic chemicals, flammable vapours, and high pressures**. This is exactly why a Safety Engineer must deeply understand *each* unit operation individually before designing safeguards for the *entire* plant.

## 1.4 Why Unit Operations Matter in Design for Safety

The **majority of industrial accidents** (Bhopal 1984, Texas City 2005, BP Texas City Refinery Explosion) have occurred in or near specific unit operations. Therefore, understanding the **failure modes, design codes, and protective systems** of each equipment type is the *very first step* of safe plant design.

Standard engineering metrics used throughout this module:
- **Design Pressure ($P_d$):** in **MPa** or **bar** (gauge).
- **Design Temperature ($T_d$):** in **°C** or **K**.
- **Corrosion Allowance (CA):** in **mm** — typically **3 mm** for carbon steel.
- **Joint Efficiency ($\eta$):** **dimensionless**, between **0.7 and 1.0**.
- **Allowable Stress ($S$):** in **MPa**, derived from **2/3 of yield stress at design temperature** as per **ASME Sec VIII Div 1**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Fluid Flow Operations
These are responsible for **~40% of all process equipment** in a typical plant. Safety-critical considerations include:
- **Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)** — to avoid **cavitation** damage.
- **Two-phase flow** in relief headers.
- **Water hammer** in long liquid lines.
- **Slug flow** in multiphase pipelines.

### 2.1.2 Heat Transfer Operations
Heat exchangers are the **second-most failed** equipment category (after rotating machinery). Common failure modes:
- **Tube rupture** causing cross-contamination.
- **Fouling** leading to localized overheating.
- **Thermal stress fatigue** during start-up/shutdown.

### 2.1.3 Mass Transfer Operations
**Distillation columns** account for nearly **35% of all column-based fire/explosion incidents**. The dominant hazards are:
- **Overpressure** due to blocked reflux.
- **Reboiler runaway**.
- **Thermal decomposition** of unstable compounds at bottom temperatures.

### 2.1.4 Mechanical Operations
Dust-generating operations (e.g., **crushing, grinding, drying**) create **explosive dust clouds** (refer to **NFPA 654**).

### 2.1.5 Reaction Operations
Reactors are the **highest-risk** units because they intentionally store large quantities of **chemical potential energy**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** hierarchy by **Trevor Kletz** mandates: *Minimize → Substitute → Moderate → Simplify*. Each unit operation should be evaluated against this 4-step ISD ladder.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Units | Validity / Notes |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Thin-wall Cylindrical Vessel — Hoop Stress | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ | MPa | Valid for $D/t \geq 20$ |
| 2 | Thin-wall Cylindrical Vessel — Longitudinal Stress | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$ | MPa | Always $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l$ |
| 3 | Spherical Vessel — Hoop Stress | $\sigma_s = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$ | MPa | Most efficient geometry |
| 4 | Required Thickness (ASME Sec VIII) | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P} + CA$ | mm | $E$ = Joint Efficiency |
| 5 | NPSH Available | $NPSH_A = P_{atm}/\rho g + h_s - P_v/\rho g - h_f$ | m | Must be $\geq NPSH_R$ |
| 6 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | $Re < 2100$: Laminar |
| 7 | Heat Transfer (LMTD) | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | W | $U$ in $W/m^2K$ |
| 8 | Bernoulli Equation | $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$ | m | Energy balance per unit weight |
| 9 | Orifice Flow Rate | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta P / \rho}$ | m³/s | For relief valve sizing |
| 10 | Pressure Relief Valve Orifice Area (API 520) | $A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{M / Z \cdot T_1}}$ | mm² | Used in fire/overpressure sizing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Of these 10 formulas, **KTU examiners most frequently ask for (1), (2), (4), and (5)** in Module 1 problems.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **hydrocarbon refinery**, the application of unit operations knowledge translates directly into safety decisions:
- **Reactor design** — choosing between CSTR and PFR affects runaway potential.
- **Pump selection** — centrifugal vs. positive displacement changes dry-run failure behavior.
- **Heat exchanger selection** — shell-and-tube vs. plate affects cleanability and fouling-induced failure.
- **Distillation column internals** — trays vs. packing changes turndown ratio and flood behavior.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Thin-Wall Cylindrical Pressure Vessel Hoop Stress

This is the **most-asked derivation** in KTU Module 1 under Design for Safety. We derive the hoop stress step-by-step.

### Step 1 — Assumptions
- Cylinder is closed at both ends.
- Wall thickness $t$ is small compared to diameter $D$, i.e., $D/t \geq 20$ (thin-wall assumption).
- Internal gauge pressure $P$ is uniform.
- Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and behaves linearly elastic.

### Step 2 — Free Body Cut (Longitudinal Section)
Imagine cutting the cylinder longitudinally into two halves. The internal pressure acts on the projected area $D \cdot L$, and this force is resisted by **two** wall sections, each of area $t \cdot L$.

Force balance in the longitudinal direction:

$$
F_{pressure} = 2 \cdot F_{wall}
$$

Expanding both sides:

$$
P \cdot (D \cdot L) = 2 \cdot (\sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L)
$$

### Step 3 — Algebraic Simplification
Cancel $L$ on both sides:

$$
P \cdot D = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t
$$

Solve for the hoop stress $\sigma_h$:

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}
$$

> This is the **hoop (circumferential) stress** — it acts around the circumference of the cylinder.

### Step 4 — Derivation of Longitudinal Stress
Now cut the cylinder along a circumferential plane. Internal pressure acts on the **end cap** of projected area $\dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}$. This force is resisted by the **circumferential wall section** of area $\pi \cdot D \cdot t$.

Force balance:

$$
P \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} = \sigma_l \cdot (\pi \cdot D \cdot t)
$$

Simplify:

$$
P \cdot D = 4 \cdot \sigma_l \cdot t
$$

Solving:

$$
\sigma_l = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}
$$

### Step 5 — Comparative Insight

$$
\sigma_h = 2 \cdot \sigma_l
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** Hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress. This is why pressure vessel **longitudinal welds** are inspected more rigorously than circumferential welds, and why **catastrophic splits** always propagate **along the axis** of the cylinder (perpendicular to the maximum stress direction).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
A horizontal cylindrical pressure vessel has an inner diameter $D_i = 1500$ mm, design pressure $P_d = 2.0$ MPa (gauge), and allowable stress $S = 138$ MPa. The joint efficiency $E = 0.85$ and corrosion allowance $CA = 3$ mm. Calculate the **required wall thickness** as per ASME Section VIII Div 1.

### Step 1 — Identify Inner Radius
The ASME formula uses inner radius $R$:

$$
R = \dfrac{D_i}{2} = \dfrac{1500}{2} = 750 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply ASME Formula (with P inside)

The ASME code for a **cylindrical shell under internal pressure** is:

$$
t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P} + CA
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$
t = \dfrac{2.0 \times 750}{138 \times 0.85 - 0.6 \times 2.0} + 3
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Denominator

$$
138 \times 0.85 = 117.30
$$

$$
0.6 \times 2.0 = 1.20
$$

$$
117.30 - 1.20 = 116.10 \text{ MPa}
$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate the Numerator and Final t

$$
\dfrac{2.0 \times 750}{116.10} = \dfrac{1500}{116.10} = 12.92 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
t = 12.92 + 3.00 = 15.92 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 6 — Round Up to Standard Plate Thickness
Standard plate sizes follow **mm increments** (e.g., 16, 18, 20 mm). The minimum commercially available thickness ≥ 15.92 mm is **16 mm**.

> **Final Answer:** $t_{required} = 16 \text{ mm}$ (standard plate).

### Step 3 (Validation) — Check Thin-Wall Assumption

$$
\dfrac{D}{t} = \dfrac{1500}{16} = 93.75
$$

Since $D/t = 93.75 \geq 20$, the **thin-wall assumption is valid**. ✅

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Hazard vs. Unit Operation

| Unit Operation | Primary Hazard | Typical Safeguard | Applicable Code |
|---------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Centrifugal Pump | Cavitation, dead-heading | NPSH check, min-flow recycle | API 610 |
| Reciprocating Compressor | Overpressure, vibration | Inter-stage relief, vibration trip | API 618 |
| Shell-and-Tube HX | Tube rupture | Pressure relief on high side | TEMA |
| Distillation Column | Flooding, overpressure | Reflux control, relief valve | API 650/620 |
| CSTR Reactor | Runaway reaction | Independent dump, RCS | OSHA 1910.119 |
| Storage Tank | Bund overflow | Secondary containment | NFPA 30 |
| Filter / Dryer | Dust explosion | Inerting, deflagration vents | NFPA 654 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for Students:** Memorize at least **3 rows** of the above matrix for KTU Part A questions.
