# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** — *Define the term "Unit Operation" with two examples. Explain its relevance in Design for Safety.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **unit operation** is a basic physical (or chemical) step in a chemical process plant where a change in **composition, energy content, or phase** occurs without change in the chemical identity of the bulk stream. *(1 mark)*

**Examples:** *(1 mark)*
- **Distillation** — separation of liquids based on boiling point.
- **Heat exchange** — transfer of thermal energy between two streams.

**Relevance to Design for Safety:** *(1 mark)*
Every unit operation has distinct failure modes (e.g., pump cavitation, reactor runaway, column flooding). Understanding these per-unit failure modes is the **first step** of process safety design, enabling the engineer to apply targeted safeguards like relief valves, interlocks, and Inherently Safer Design (ISD) choices.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** — *List any three safety hazards associated with centrifugal pumps used in chemical industries.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Cavitation** — due to insufficient NPSH, causing impeller pitting and loss of flow. *(1 mark)*
2. **Dead-heading** — block flow leading to rapid temperature rise and seal failure. *(1 mark)*
3. **Mechanical seal failure** — leading to toxic/flammable leakage to atmosphere. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** — *14 Marks*

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the classification of **unit operations** in a chemical process plant. Discuss the safety implications of **mass transfer operations**. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A cylindrical pressure vessel has an inner diameter of **1200 mm**, design pressure of **1.5 MPa (gauge)**, allowable stress of **120 MPa**, joint efficiency of **0.80**, and corrosion allowance of **2 mm**. Compute the required wall thickness as per **ASME Sec VIII Div 1**. *(7 Marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### (a) Classification of Unit Operations (7 Marks)

Unit operations are classified into **five major categories** based on the dominant physical principle:

| S.No | Category | Governing Principle | Examples | Safety Implication |
|------|----------|--------------------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Fluid Flow | Momentum Transfer | Pumps, Compressors, Pipes | Cavitation, water hammer |
| 2 | Heat Transfer | Energy Transfer | HX, Boilers, Condensers | Tube rupture, thermal stress |
| 3 | Mass Transfer | Component Separation | Distillation, Absorption, Drying | Overpressure, fire |
| 4 | Mechanical | Size & Phase Change | Crushing, Filtration, Mixing | Dust explosion |
| 5 | Reactive | Chemical Conversion | CSTR, PFR, Batch | Runaway, detonation |

*(3 marks for the classification table and 4 marks for elaboration.)*

**Safety Implications of Mass Transfer Operations:** *(Specifically 2 marks within the 7)*
- Distillation columns handle **large inventories of flammable liquids** at elevated temperatures.
- Hazards include **flooding, weeping, overpressure due to loss of reflux**, and **thermal decomposition** in the reboiler.
- Safeguards include **pressure relief valves, level instrumentation, reflux pumps with standby**, and **emergency dump systems**.

---

#### (b) Numerical Solution — Wall Thickness (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $D_i = 1200$ mm
- $P = 1.5$ MPa
- $S = 120$ MPa
- $E = 0.80$
- $CA = 2$ mm

**Step 1 — Inner Radius**

$$
R = \dfrac{D_i}{2} = \dfrac{1200}{2} = 600 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — ASME Sec VIII Formula**

$$
t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P} + CA
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Denominator**

$$
S \cdot E = 120 \times 0.80 = 96.0
$$

$$
0.6 \cdot P = 0.6 \times 1.5 = 0.9
$$

$$
96.0 - 0.9 = 95.1 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Pressure Term**

$$
\dfrac{P \cdot R}{95.1} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 600}{95.1} = \dfrac{900}{95.1} = 9.46 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Add Corrosion Allowance**

$$
t = 9.46 + 2.00 = 11.46 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Round Up to Next Standard Plate Thickness**

$$
t_{selected} = 12 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Validation**

$$
D/t = 1200 / 12 = 100 \geq 20 \quad (\text{Thin-wall assumption valid}) \checkmark
$$

**Final Answer:** $t_{required} = 12$ mm (standard plate). *(1 mark reserved for the final conclusion.)*

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** — *14 Marks*

**(a)** Describe the principle of operation and **major safety considerations** of any two of the following: *(i)* Centrifugal pump *(ii)* Shell-and-tube heat exchanger *(iii)* CSTR reactor. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A spherical pressure vessel of inner diameter **2.0 m** stores a liquefied gas at **3.5 MPa**. If allowable stress is **150 MPa** and joint efficiency is **1.0**, calculate the **minimum wall thickness** without corrosion allowance. Is it a thin-wall vessel? *(7 Marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### (a) Principle & Safety of Two Equipments (7 Marks)

**Equipment 1: Centrifugal Pump** *(3.5 marks)*
- **Principle:** Converts rotational kinetic energy from an impeller into hydrodynamic energy, generating flow through centrifugal force.
- **Safety Considerations:** NPSH margin verification, minimum flow recirculation line, mechanical seal integrity, vibration monitoring, and dead-head protection.

**Equipment 2: Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger** *(3.5 marks)*
- **Principle:** Heat transfer between two fluids separated by a tube bundle; one fluid flows through tubes, the other across them.
- **Safety Considerations:** Pressure relief on high-pressure side, material compatibility to prevent galvanic corrosion, leak detection between shell and channel, thermal expansion allowance, and regular cleaning to prevent fouling-induced hot spots.

---

#### (b) Spherical Pressure Vessel Thickness (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $D_i = 2.0$ m = 2000 mm
- $P = 3.5$ MPa
- $S = 150$ MPa
- $E = 1.0$
- $CA = 0$ (neglected)

**Step 1 — Inner Radius**

$$
R = \dfrac{D_i}{2} = \dfrac{2000}{2} = 1000 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Spherical Vessel Formula**

$$
\sigma_s = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{2 \cdot t} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{2 \cdot S \cdot E}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**

$$
t = \dfrac{3.5 \times 1000}{2 \times 150 \times 1.0} = \dfrac{3500}{300} = 11.67 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Select Standard Plate**

$$
t_{selected} = 12 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Thin-Wall Check**

$$
D/t = 2000 / 12 = 166.67 \geq 20
$$

**Yes**, the vessel qualifies as a **thin-wall pressure vessel**. ✅

**Engineering Note:** *(1 mark for the discussion)*
Spherical vessels are **twice as efficient** as cylindrical vessels (only half the wall thickness is needed for the same pressure and diameter), which is why **LNG bullets and Horton spheres** are widely used for pressurized storage of LPG, propane, and butane.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Most-Common Mark Losers in Module 1:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 0.6P** in the ASME denominator — losing up to **2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up hoop and longitudinal stress formulas** — losing up to **2 marks**.
> 3. **Not validating the thin-wall assumption** ($D/t \geq 20$) — losing **1 mark**.
> 4. **Forgetting to round up** the calculated thickness to the next standard plate size — losing **1 mark**.
> 5. **Wrong joint efficiency** — full penetration butt welds = 1.0; spot-examined lap welds = 0.55.
> 6. **Using internal radius vs. outer radius** inconsistently — always use **inner radius** $R = D_i/2$ unless the question explicitly states otherwise.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 1 — Unit Operations & Equipments:**

- **Definition** — Unit Operation = physical/chemical step in a process plant; studied for safety, design, and operation.
- **Five Classes** — Fluid Flow, Heat Transfer, Mass Transfer, Mechanical, Reactive. *(Memorize examples for each.)*
- **ASME Thin-Wall Cylinders:**
  - Hoop: $\sigma_h = P D / (2 t)$
  - Longitudinal: $\sigma_l = P D / (4 t)$
  - Hoop is **always twice** longitudinal.
- **ASME Required Thickness:**
  $t = (P R) / (S E - 0.6 P) + CA$
- **Thin-wall condition:** $D/t \geq 20$. Always validate.
- **Spherical vessel stress:** $\sigma_s = P R / (2 t)$ — **half** of cylinder hoop stress.
- **Safety Hierarchy:** ISD by Kletz — *Minimize → Substitute → Moderate → Simplify.*
- **Common hazards per equipment:**
  - Pumps → cavitation, dead-heading.
  - Compressors → surge, vibration, overpressure.
  - HX → tube rupture, fouling.
  - Distillation → flooding, overpressure.
  - Reactors → runaway, decomposition.
  - Tanks → bund overflow, BLEVE.
- **Standard codes:** ASME Sec VIII, API 610, API 520, NFPA 30, NFPA 654, TEMA.
- **Design metrics** to memorize: Allowable stress $S$, Joint Efficiency $E$, Corrosion Allowance $CA = 3$ mm typical, NPSH margin ≥ **1.5 m** minimum.
- **Always round UP** calculated thickness to next standard plate size.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Unit Operations and Equipments.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Utilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Utilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚙️ Utilities – Foundation Concepts for Process Safety Design

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor – PESFT412 / Module 1**
> Utilities form the **lifeline infrastructure** of any chemical / process plant. They are the auxiliary services (water, steam, air, nitrogen, electricity, fuel, refrigeration, inert gas, flare, drain) whose continuous and *reliable* availability is a **prerequisite** for safe startup, normal operation, controlled shutdown, and emergency response. Loss of a utility is one of the most common **root causes** of major process incidents in the chemical industry.

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Board-Exam Friendly)

A **Utility** in process plant safety engineering is defined as any off-process, support service that provides energy, material, or conditioning to one or more process units, and whose **failure, deviation, or contamination** can directly initiate or escalate a hazardous event.

Mathematically, the **availability (A)** of a utility system is expressed as:

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

where **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures and **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair. KTU examiners expect you to remember the inverse relationship between *availability* and *downtime risk*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight – "Utilities as a Process Safety Element"**
> Under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM) and CCPS guidelines, utilities are explicitly treated as **Process Safety Elements** because a single utility failure can trip multiple independent process units — a classic **common-cause / common-mode failure** scenario.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – The Hospital Analogy

Think of a process plant as a **hospital ICU**:
- **Electricity** = Heartbeat (everything stops without it).
- **Cooling water** = Blood circulation (removes metabolic heat = reaction heat).
- **Instrument air** = Nervous system (drives control valves, dampers).
- **Nitrogen / Inert gas** = Anesthesia (puts flammable sections to sleep).
- **Steam** = Fever reducer (provides heat for endothermic reactions & tracing).
- **Flare / Drain** = Kidney dialysis (removes unwanted / vented hydrocarbons).

If **any one** utility fails, the patient (plant) goes into shock → uncontrolled reaction → fire / explosion / toxic release.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Plant Utilities (KTU Board Table)

| Category | Examples | Primary Safety Role |
|----------|----------|---------------------|
| Energy | Steam, Electric power, Fuel gas | Heat input, motive power |
| Cooling | Cooling water, chilled water, refrigerant | Heat removal |
| Purging / Inerting | Nitrogen, CO₂, flue gas | Flammability control |
| Actuation | Instrument air, hydraulic oil | Valve / damper movement |
| Emergency | Firewater, foam, deluge, sprinkler | Fire suppression |
| Effluent | Sewer, flare, scrubber, blowdown | Safe disposal of reliefs |
| Support | Service water, potable water, lube oil | Auxiliary operations |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked *"Why are utilities studied under safety?"* — answer with the keywords **Common-Cause Failure, Interdependency, Loss of Containment trigger**. Examiners reward these exact phrases.

---

## 1.4 Why Utilities Are a Safety Concern – The "Domino" View

A process unit rarely fails alone. Consider a **reactor cooling water failure**:
1. Cooling water pump trips → loss of heat removal.
2. Exothermic reaction temperature rises.
3. Pressure relief valve opens → load on flare system.
4. Flare system unavailability → hydrocarbon vented to atmosphere.
5. Vapor cloud → ignition → BLEVE / Explosion.

This **single root cause** cascaded through **5 interdependent utilities**. This is precisely why KTU devotes an entire module to utilities.

---

## 1.5 Key Engineering Metrics (Must-Memorize for KTU Viva)

- **Nitrogen Purity for Purging**: ≥ **99.5%** for hydrocarbon service; ≥ **99.99%** (O₂ < 100 ppm) for cryogenic / reactive service.
- **Instrument Air Dew Point**: ≤ **-40 °C** (atmospheric) — prevents valve freezing.
- **Steam Quality (for safety reliefs)**: ≥ **99.5% dry saturated** to prevent water hammer in relief headers.
- **Emergency Power Transfer Time**: ≤ **30 seconds** for PSM-covered facilities.
- **Firewater Duration**: ≥ **4 hours** continuous at maximum demand (NFPA / OISD standard).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

## 2.1 The Utility Failure Cascade – Logic Decomposition

Step-by-step reasoning KTU expects:

1. **Identify Utility Criticality** → Map each utility to each process unit using a **Utility Flow Diagram (UFD)** and a **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)**.
2. **Determine Failure Mode** → Loss (total), Degradation (partial), Contamination (wrong quality), Over-pressure, Under-pressure.
3. **Evaluate Consequence** → Use HAZOP nodes; every utility supply line is a HAZOP node in itself.
4. **Assign Safeguards** → Independent backup (e.g., diesel-driven pump), Trip interlock, or Diverse protection.
5. **Quantify Risk** → Likelihood × Consequence → Decide if **SIL-rated** protection layer is needed (per IEC 61511).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Why?" matters in KTU answers:**
> Examiners give 1–2 marks for the *cause*, 2 marks for the *consequence chain*, and 2 marks for the *mitigation*. Skipping any layer = losing marks.

---

## 2.2 Utility Reliability Concepts

### 2.2.1 Availability vs. Reliability
- **Reliability (R)** = Probability the utility performs its function for a specified interval without failure.
- **Availability (A)** = Fraction of *total* time the utility is *actually* ready.

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

$$
A = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

where $\lambda$ = failure rate (failures/hr) and $\mu$ = repair rate (repairs/hr).

### 2.2.2 Common-Cause / Common-Mode Failure (CCF)
For two redundant utility trains A and B:

$$
\beta\text{-factor model: } \lambda_{CCF} = \beta \cdot \lambda_{D}
$$

Typical industrial $\beta$ for instrumented utilities ≈ **2% to 10%**. KTU Board loves this number.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Units | Remarks |
|---|---------|----------|-------|---------|
| 1 | System Availability (1-out-of-2 redundant) | $A_{1oo2} = 1 - (1-A_A)(1-A_B)$ | dimensionless | Higher than single |
| 2 | Unavailability | $U = 1 - A$ | dimensionless | Used in LOPA |
| 3 | Failure Rate | $\lambda = 1 / MTBF$ | hr⁻¹ | Constant failure rate regime |
| 4 | Repair Rate | $\mu = 1 / MTTR$ | hr⁻¹ | Maintenance engineering |
| 5 | Mean Downtime | $MDT = \lambda \cdot MDT_{single}$ (approx.) | hr | For redundant pair |
| 6 | Heat Removal Duty | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kW | Cooling water sizing |
| 7 | Steam Quality (mass basis) | $x = \dfrac{m_{vapor}}{m_{total}}$ | — | ≥ 0.99 for safety reliefs |
| 8 | Nitrogen Purge Volume | $V_{N_2} = k \cdot V_{system}$ | Nm³ | $k = 3$–$5$ for hydrocarbon |
| 9 | Firewater Flow | $Q_{fw} = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{\tau_{extinguish}}$ | m³/hr | NFPA 11 |
| 10 | Instrument Air Consumption | $Q_{air} = \sum Q_{actuator} \cdot f_{usage}$ | Nm³/hr | Sum of valve strokes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Tables:** All absolute values and dividers in this table use LaTeX commands `\mid` / `\vert` / `\cdot` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. KTU PDF renders handle these perfectly.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility Safety Engineering Examples

- **Deepwater Horizon (2010) — Loss of Blowout Preventer hydraulic utility → Uncontrolled hydrocarbon release → Marine fire & explosion.** This single utility failure caused the largest marine oil spill in history.
- **Bhopal (1984) — Loss of refrigeration utility for MIC storage → Runaway exothermic reaction → Toxic gas release.** A textbook utility-failure catastrophe.
- **Texas City Refinery (2005) — Loss of level instrumentation air → Raffinate splitter overfill → Explosion.** Common-mode failure of instrument air.

Each of these reinforces: **Utility design ≠ auxiliary engineering. It is a primary safety function.**

---

## 2.5 Hierarchy of Utility Protection Layers (KTU "Onion Model")

1. **Inherent Safe Design** — Use less hazardous utility (e.g., nitrogen instead of steam for purging where water hammer is a risk).
2. **Passive Protection** — Gravity-driven firewater tanks, self-actuating pressure reliefs.
3. **Active Protection** — Diesel-driven pumps, UPS-backed instrument air.
4. **Instrumented Protection (IPL)** — SIL-rated trips on utility parameters.
5. **Procedural** — Operator action on utility alarms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Availability of a 1-out-of-2 Redundant Utility System

We begin with the **fundamental reliability assumption** that two independent utility trains A and B operate in parallel redundancy, and either one alone can satisfy the process demand.

**Step 1 — Failure Probability of a Single Train**

By definition of availability, the probability that a *single* train is unavailable at any random instant is:

$$
U_A = 1 - A_A
$$

**Step 2 — Probability Both Trains Fail Simultaneously**

Because the trains are **independent** (no common-cause assumed for this derivation), the joint probability is the product:

$$
U_{1oo2} = U_A \cdot U_B = (1 - A_A)(1 - A_B)
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Availability of the Combined System**

$$
A_{1oo2} = 1 - U_{1oo2}
$$

Substituting the result from Step 2 into Step 3 yields:

$$
A_{1oo2} = 1 - (1 - A_A)(1 - A_B)
$$

**Step 4 — Symmetric Case Simplification**

If both trains are *identical* ($A_A = A_B = A$), then:

$$
A_{1oo2} = 1 - (1 - A)^2 = 2A - A^2
$$

This is the standard result the KTU Board expects in Part B 14-mark problems.

> **Real-World Meaning:** A single utility pump with A = 0.95 → $A_{1oo2} = 2(0.95) - (0.95)^2 = 0.9975$. **Redundancy nearly quadrupled the safety margin.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cooling Water Sizing for Heat Removal

A reactor releases $Q$ kW that must be removed by cooling water.

**Step 1 — Energy Balance**

$$
Q = \dot{m}_{cw} \cdot C_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T_{cw}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Mass Flow Rate**

$$
\dot{m}_{cw} = \frac{Q}{C_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T_{cw}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Volumetric Flow**

$$
\dot{V}_{cw} = \frac{\dot{m}_{cw}}{\rho_w}
$$

Substituting the mass-flow expression:

$$
\dot{V}_{cw} = \frac{Q}{\rho_w \cdot C_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T_{cw}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)**

Given: $Q = 500$ kW, $C_{p,w} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K, $\Delta T_{cw} = 10$ K (allowed rise), $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³.

Step 4a — Compute $\dot{m}_{cw}$:

$$
\dot{m}_{cw} = \frac{500}{4.18 \times 10} = 11.96 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Step 4b — Convert to $\dot{V}_{cw}$:

$$
\dot{V}_{cw} = \frac{11.96}{1000} = 0.01196 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 43.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

Step 4c — Apply 1.25 **safety / fouling factor** (KTU Board expects this multiplier):

$$
\dot{V}_{design} = 1.25 \times 43.05 = 53.81 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This design flow ensures that even with 20% tube fouling (the typical operating condition after 12–18 months), the cooling duty is still met — preventing reactor runaway.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Nitrogen Purge Volume for Confined-Space Entry

For purging a vessel of volume $V$ from initial $C_{O_2,i}$ to final $C_{O_2,f}$:

**Step 1 — Mass Balance on Oxygen**

Assuming perfect mixing, oxygen concentration after $n$ vessel-volumes of nitrogen:

$$
C_{O_2,f} = C_{O_2,i} \cdot e^{-n}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Number of Purges**

$$
n = \ln \left( \frac{C_{O_2,i}}{C_{O_2,f}} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Total Nitrogen Volume**

$$
V_{N_2} = n \cdot V_{vessel}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example**

Purge a 50 m³ tank from $21\%$ to $1\%$ O₂:

$$
n = \ln \left( \frac{0.21}{0.01} \right) = \ln(21) = 3.045
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{N_2} = 3.045 \times 50 = 152.2 \text{ Nm}^3
$$

Apply 1.5 contingency → **228 Nm³ nitrogen** required. This matches standard plant practice.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Utility Failure Modes & Safety Responses

| Utility | Failure Mode | Immediate Consequence | Independent Protection Layer (IPL) | KTU Board Frequency |
|---------|--------------|------------------------|-----------------------------------|----------------------|
| Cooling water | Loss of flow | Reaction runaway | Independent backup pump + high-temp trip | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Instrument air | Loss of pressure | Valves fail open/closed as designed | Nitrogen backup / hand-operated valves | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Steam | Over-pressure | Heat exchanger rupture | Rupture disc + safety valve | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Electric power | Total blackout | Control system collapse | UPS + Emergency Diesel Generator | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Nitrogen | Contamination (O₂ ingress) | Flammable atmosphere formed | Continuous O₂ analyzer + auto block | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Flare | Loss of header | Relief valves can't discharge | Dual flare tips + backup flare KO drum | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Firewater | Pump failure | Fire spreads uncontrolled | Diesel-driven jockey + main pump | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Schematics – Utility Architecture Flow

## 4.1 Plant Utility Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **interdependencies** between major plant utility systems and the process units they serve, including the emergency backup paths. This is the exact kind of schematic KTU examiners draw on the answer sheet to test whether a student can *trace* a utility-failure cascade.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MAIN POWER GRID] -->|33 kV Incoming| B[UNIT SUBSTATION A1]
    A -->|Independent Feeder| C[UNIT SUBSTATION B1]
    B --> D[MCC SWITCHBOARD D1]
    C --> E[MCC SWITCHBOARD E1]
    F[DIESEL GENERATOR] -.->|Emergency Backup| D
    F -.->|Emergency Backup| E
    G[STEAM HEADER] -->|High Pressure| H[PROCESS HEATERS]
    G -->|Medium Pressure| I[TRACING AND REBOILERS]
    J[BOILER FEED WATER] --> G
    K[COOLING WATER PUMP A] --> L[COOLING WATER HEADER]
    M[COOLING WATER PUMP B] -.->|Standby| L
    L --> N[REACTOR JACKETS]
    L --> O[CONDENSERS]
    P[INSTRUMENT AIR COMPRESSOR A] --> Q[IA HEADER]
    R[INSTRUMENT AIR COMPRESSOR B] -.->|Standby| Q
    Q --> S[CONTROL VALVES]
    Q --> T[SHUTDOWN VALVES]
    U[NITROGEN STORAGE VESSEL] --> V[N2 DISTRIBUTION HEADER]
    V --> W[PURGING STATIONS]
    V --> X[BLANKETING SYSTEMS]
    Y[FIRE WATER PUMP ELECTRIC] --> Z[FIRE WATER RING MAIN]
    AA[FIRE WATER PUMP DIESEL] -.-> Z
    Z --> AB[HYDRANTS AND MONITORS]
    Z --> AC[DELUGE VALVES]
    AD[FLARE KO DRUM] --> AE[FLARE STACK]
    AF[PROCESS RELIEF VALVES] --> AD
    H --> AG[PROCESS UNIT ALPHA]
    N --> AG
    S --> AG
    W --> AG
    AF --> AG
    AG --> AH[SAFE PLANT OPERATION]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram for the exam:**
> - Solid arrows → normal (primary) supply path.
> - Dotted arrows (-.->) → **emergency / standby / redundant** path.
> - The central convergence point **AG [PROCESS UNIT ALPHA]** shows how *multiple utilities* intersect at one process node. Loss of any single utility can disable this node.

---

## 4.2 Utility Failure Cascade Logic Diagram

This diagram explains *why* utility failures propagate. It mirrors the **Bow-Tie** logic of loss-of-containment events.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[UTILITY FAILURE ROOT CAUSE] --> M2[LOSS OF PROTECTION LAYER]
    M2 --> M3[PROCESS DEVIATION]
    M3 --> M4[UNSAFE CONDITION]
    M4 --> M5[LOSS OF CONTAINMENT]
    M5 --> M6[FIRE EXPLOSION TOXIC RELEASE]
    M5 --> M7[ENVIRONMENTAL DAMAGE]
    M5 --> M8[PERSONNEL INJURY]
    M6 --> M9[MITIGATION BY EMERGENCY UTILITIES]
    M7 --> M9
    M8 --> M9
    M9 --> M10[INCIDENT CONTAINED]
    M6 --> M11[MAJOR ACCIDENT IF MITIGATION FAILS]
    M7 --> M11
    M8 --> M11
    M11 --> M12[CATASTROPHIC OUTCOME]
```

---

## 4.3 Redundancy Topology — 1-out-of-2 vs. 2-out-of-3

For higher-criticality utilities (e.g., firewater, instrument air on emergency valves), KTU expects familiarity with **voting logic**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph R1[1oo2 REDUNDANCY]
    UA1[Utility Train A] --> V1[Voting Logic: OR]
    UB1[Utility Train B] --> V1
    V1 --> P1[Process Demand Met]
    end
    subgraph R2[2oo3 REDUNDANCY]
    UA2[Utility Train A] --> V2[Voting Logic: 2 of 3]
    UB2[Utility Train B] --> V2
    UC2[Utility Train C] --> V2
    V2 --> P2[Process Demand Met]
    end
```

**Engineering meaning:**
- **1-out-of-2 (1oo2):** Higher availability, lower SIL. Used for general plant utilities.
- **2-out-of-3 (2oo3):** Higher *spurious trip* tolerance, higher SIL. Used for **safety instrumented utility systems** per IEC 61511.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> All questions are mapped to **PESFT412 – Module 1 – Utilities** and follow the **2024 Scheme ESE pattern** (Part A = 3 marks × short; Part B = 14 marks with internal choice, typically sub-parts of 7+7 marks).

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Define the term 'utility' in the context of process plant safety. Why are utilities classified as a process safety element under PSM?"**

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)

A **utility** is any support service (electricity, steam, cooling water, instrument air, nitrogen, firewater, flare system) that provides energy, material, or environmental conditioning to one or more process units **(1 mark)**.

Utilities are a PSM element because their **failure constitutes a common-cause / common-mode initiator** that can simultaneously disable multiple process units, bypassing independent protection layers **(1 mark)**. Standards like OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 explicitly list "utility systems" as one of the fourteen process safety elements to be audited **(1 mark)**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"List any four utilities in a process plant and state one safety-critical function of each."**

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)

1. **Cooling water** — Removes exothermic reaction heat; failure → reactor runaway **(0.75 mark)**.
2. **Instrument air** — Powers control & shutdown valves; failure → valves fail as designed, but process control lost **(0.75 mark)**.
3. **Nitrogen** — Provides inert atmosphere for hydrocarbon storage; failure → flammable mixture forms **(0.75 mark)**.
4. **Firewater** — Suppresses and controls fires; failure → fire spreads uncontrolled **(0.75 mark)**.

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice – Choose ONE)

---

### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**"Discuss the role of utilities as common-cause failure initiators in process plants. With reference to a cooling-water failure scenario, explain how a single utility failure can cascade into a major incident. Suggest minimum three Independent Protection Layers (IPLs) to mitigate this."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Conceptual Discussion: Utilities as Common-Cause Failures**

A common-cause failure (CCF) is one in which a *single* root cause simultaneously disables multiple, otherwise independent, safety functions **(1 mark)**.

Utilities are *inherently* common-cause because **every process unit typically draws from the same utility header** (shared cooling-water main, shared instrument-air header, shared power bus) **(1 mark)**. Therefore, a single pump trip, a header rupture, or a contamination event can disable many process units at once **(1 mark)**.

Statistical consequence: a CCF event bypasses the independence assumption that the $\beta$-factor model tries to quantify (with $\beta$ typically 2–10% for instrumented utilities) **(1 mark)**. Thus, redundancy without *diversity* (e.g., two electric pumps on the same bus) provides a *false sense* of safety **(1 mark)**.

**Cooling-Water Failure Cascade:**

1. **Initiation** — Main cooling-water pump trips (cause: power failure, mechanical seizure, or operator isolation) **(0.5 mark)**.
2. **Process Deviation** — Reactor outlet temperature rises at a rate of $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{Q_{rxn}}{\sum m_i C_{p,i}}$ **(0.5 mark)**.
3. **Pressure Build-Up** — Vapor pressure rises; relief valve opens → load on flare system **(0.5 mark)**.
4. **Escalation** — If flare unavailable, hydrocarbon release → vapor cloud → ignition → BLEVE **(0.5 mark)**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Minimum Three Independent Protection Layers (IPLs):**

**IPL 1 — Diverse Backup Pump with Independent Driver (0–1 mark)**
A *diesel-driven* (electrically independent) cooling-water pump on a *separate* power bus, automatically started on low header pressure **(1 mark)**. This breaks the common electrical cause **(0.5 mark)**. Sized for 100% design flow + 25% margin **(0.5 mark)**.

**IPL 2 — High-Temperature Trip Interlock (SIL-2 rated) (0–2 marks)**
Independent temperature transmitter → Safety PLC → closes feed valve + opens dump valve when reactor outlet T exceeds MAH (Maximum Allowable Hot) **(1 mark)**. Per IEC 61511, this is an Instrumented Protection Layer with target PFD ≤ 10⁻² **(1 mark)**.

**IPL 3 — Depressurization to Atmospheric Dump Tank (1 mark)**
A passive dump route to a remotely located, large-volume, water-cooled dump tank; opens on high-high temperature **(1 mark)**. **No utility required** — fully passive, hence highest reliability in the IPL hierarchy **(1 mark)**.

**IPL 4 (Bonus) — Mechanical Relief Device (1 mark)**
Rupture disc in series with safety valve — fails open on overpressure, providing *last-line* mechanical protection regardless of utility status **(1 mark)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students *very commonly* list four IPLs but **repeat the same category** (e.g., two electric pumps). Examiners deduct up to **3 marks** because they are NOT *independent* — both can fail from the same electrical cause.
> - Always use the phrase **"Independent of the initiating cause"** in your answer. KTU Board examiners are trained to look for this exact phrase.
> - Do NOT confuse IPL with **layer of protection** in the LOPA onion. They are the same conceptually but require distinct verbiage.

---

### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**"Explain the concept of availability and reliability for a utility system. Two identical cooling-water pumps operate in 1-out-of-2 parallel redundancy. Each pump has MTBF = 4000 hours and MTTR = 50 hours. Calculate (i) the availability of each pump, (ii) the combined availability of the 1oo2 system, and (iii) the unavailability per year (in hours). Comment on the safety significance."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):**

- **Reliability (R)** = Probability the utility performs its intended function without failure for a specified interval. Quantified as $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Availability (A)** = Fraction of *total* time the system is *actually* ready. $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Unavailability (U)** = $1 - A$; represents the *fraction of time the utility is down*, the direct driver of process risk. **(1 mark)**
- For redundant utility systems, **availability rises sharply** because both must fail simultaneously to disable the process. **(1 mark)**
- Safety significance: utilities with **U > 10⁻³ per year** typically require additional IPLs (per CCPS Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis). **(1 mark)**
- The **β-factor model** is then introduced to account for the small but non-zero common-cause component. **(1 mark)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks — Numerical Solution

**Given Data:**
- MTBF = 4000 hr
- MTTR = 50 hr

**Step (i) — Availability of Each Pump**

Using $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$:

$$
A_{single} = \frac{4000}{4000 + 50} = \frac{4000}{4050}
$$

$$
A_{single} = 0.98765 \quad \text{(or } 98.765\%\text{)}
$$

**Marks:** 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 0.5 for value **(2.5 marks total)**.

**Step (ii) — Combined Availability of 1oo2 System**

Using $A_{1oo2} = 2A - A^2$:

$$
A_{1oo2} = 2(0.98765) - (0.98765)^2
$$

$$
A_{1oo2} = 1.97530 - 0.97545 = 0.99985
$$

**Marks:** 1 for correct formula, 1 for substitution, 0.5 for value **(2.5 marks total)**.

**Step (iii) — Unavailability per Year in Hours**

$$
U_{1oo2} = 1 - A_{1oo2} = 1 - 0.99985 = 0.00015
$$

Hours per year = 8760 hours.

$$
t_{down} = 8760 \times 0.00015 = 1.314 \text{ hr/year}
$$

**Marks:** 1 for formula, 0.5 for computation **(2 marks total)**.

**Safety Significance Comment (Bonus but valued):**
A 1oo2 system reduces downtime from ~**108 hr/yr** (single pump) to **~1.3 hr/yr** — a **~80-fold improvement**. This is why KTU board expects redundancy with diversity (diesel + electric, not electric + electric).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Forgetting to convert **fraction to hours per year by multiplying by 8760** → **−1.5 marks**.
> - Writing $A = MTBF / MTTR$ (forgetting the denominator $+ MTTR$) → **−2 marks**.
> - Treating $A_{1oo2}$ as simply $A_1 + A_2$ → **−1 mark** (common error).
> - **Omitting the safety significance comment** → **−1 mark** (examiner specifically tests engineering judgment).

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1 / Utilities**

- ✅ A **utility** is any support service whose failure can initiate or escalate a hazardous event.
- ✅ Utilities are a **PSM element** because they cause **common-cause failures** affecting multiple units.
- ✅ **MTBF ↑ ⇒ A ↑ ; MTTR ↑ ⇒ A ↓.**
- ✅ **1oo2 availability** = $2A - A^2$ for identical trains.
- ✅ **Cooling water failure** → exothermic runaway → relief valve → flare load → potential vapor cloud.
- ✅ **Instrument air dew point** ≤ **−40 °C** for safe pneumatic valve operation.
- ✅ **Nitrogen purity** ≥ **99.5%** for purging hydrocarbons; ≥ **99.99%** for reactive service.
- ✅ **Firewater storage** = minimum **4-hour** supply at peak demand.
- ✅ **Emergency power transfer** ≤ **30 seconds** for PSM-covered units.
- ✅ **β-factor** for common-cause quantification in redundant utility trains ≈ **2–10%**.
- ✅ **Steam quality** ≥ **99.5%** for safety relief headers (no water hammer).
- ✅ Always design IPLs **independent of the initiating utility cause** — examiner's favorite trap.
- ✅ **Passive protection > active protection > instrumented > procedural** (Safety Layer Ranking).
- ✅ Always apply **1.25 fouling / safety factor** to cooling-water design flows.
- ✅ Reference **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119**, **CCPS Guidelines**, **IEC 61511**, **NFPA 11/15/30**, and **OISD** standards wherever possible in descriptive answers — examiners reward citations.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Advice:**
> For a 14-mark question, structure your answer as:
> **(1) Definition / Concept** → **(2) Cause-Effect Chain** → **(3) Numerical / IPL Listing** → **(4) Safety Significance + Standard Reference**.
> This is the *exact* structure the KTU Board Examiner marking scheme uses — it directly hits all four Bloom's levels: **Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyze → Evaluate**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pressure System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Pressure System** is defined as a system comprising one or more pressure vessels, the associated piping, fittings, pumps, compressors, safety devices, and pressure-containing accessories, designed to operate at pressures substantially different from atmospheric pressure. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme and aligned with international standards (ASME Boiler & Pressure Vessel Code, PD 5500, and Indian Standard **IS 2825**), a pressure system includes any arrangement where the internal pressure can exceed **0.5 bar gauge** above atmospheric pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition (High-Yield for KTU Board Exams):**
> A pressure system is an integrated network of pressure-containing components (vessels, pipes, valves, fittings, safety devices, and instrumentation) in which the fluid medium is confined and maintained at a working pressure significantly different from ambient atmospheric pressure.

### 1.2 Classification by Operating Pressure

| Category | Pressure Range | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Low Pressure** | 0.5 – 4 bar | Domestic water heaters, air receivers |
| **Medium Pressure** | 4 – 40 bar | LPG cylinders, autoclaves |
| **High Pressure** | 40 – 100 bar | Hydraulic presses, ammonia plants |
| **Ultra-High Pressure** | > 100 bar | Supercritical boilers, synthesis reactors |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **pressure cooker** in your kitchen. When you heat water inside it, steam builds up and creates high internal pressure. The cooker has a **safety valve** (the weighted whistle) that releases excess steam to prevent an explosion. Now scale this idea up by a factor of one thousand — replace the cooking pot with a giant cylindrical steel tank, the burner with an industrial furnace, and the whistle with a precisely calibrated spring-loaded safety relief valve. **That is essentially a pressure system**.

The fundamental engineering problem is the same: contain a high-energy fluid safely, manage the stress on the container walls, and provide a reliable escape route for excess pressure if things go wrong.

### 1.4 Essential Terminology (Mandatory KTU Vocabulary)

> [!NOTE]
> These terms appear almost every KTU exam. Memorize the exact wording.

- **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP):** The maximum gauge pressure permissible at the top of the vessel under normal operating conditions.
- **Design Pressure:** The pressure used in the design equations, typically **MAWP + 10%** (or as per code) to provide a margin of safety.
- **Test Pressure:** The hydrostatic pressure applied during fabrication testing, usually **1.3 × Design Pressure**.
- **Gauge Pressure ($P_g$):** Pressure measured relative to atmospheric pressure.
- **Absolute Pressure ($P_a$):** $P_a = P_g + P_{atm}$
- **Set Pressure:** The inlet pressure at which a safety relief valve opens.
- **Blowdown:** The drop in pressure between the set pressure and the reseating pressure of a relief valve.

### 1.5 Why Pressure Systems Demand Dedicated Safety Engineering

Pressure systems concentrate enormous potential energy. A small vessel failing at high pressure releases energy equivalent to an explosion. According to historical incident data, pressure vessel failures account for a significant portion of major industrial accidents globally. This justifies the entire field of *Design for Safety*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Complete Pressure System (Block-Level Architecture)

A complete pressure system consists of **five integrated functional layers**:

1. **Pressure Generation Layer:** Pumps, compressors, boilers, reactors.
2. **Pressure Containment Layer:** Vessels, pipes, tanks, headers.
3. **Pressure Control Layer:** Regulators, control valves, instrumentation.
4. **Pressure Protection Layer:** Safety relief valves, rupture discs, vents.
5. **Pressure Monitoring Layer:** Pressure gauges, transmitters, alarms, trips.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"List the major components of a pressure system and explain the role of the protection layer."* — Always emphasize the **Protection Layer** as the *last line of defense*.

### 2.2 Classification of Pressure Vessels

#### A) By Wall Thickness

- **Thin-Walled Vessels:** Wall thickness $t \le \dfrac{D}{20}$ (where $D$ is the internal diameter). Membrane stress theory applies.
- **Thick-Walled Vessels:** $t > \dfrac{D}{20}$. Radial stress is no longer negligible; **Lamé's equations** apply.

#### B) By Geometry

- **Cylindrical** (most common in industry — easy to manufacture, internally pressurizable).
- **Spherical** (best stress distribution but expensive to fabricate).
- **Conical and Torispherical** (used as transition pieces and heads).

#### C) By End Closure

- **Flat ends** (high stress concentration — rarely used for high pressure).
- **Hemispherical ends** (lowest stress — ideal but costly).
- **Ellipsoidal ends** (2:1 ratio is standard per ASME code).
- **Torispherical ends** (knuckle + crown — most common in industry).

### 2.3 Design Philosophy — Why the Safety Factor Exists

The design is driven by a three-tier safety logic:

- **Tier 1 — Primary Containment:** Walls thick enough to withstand design pressure with elastic stress below yield.
- **Tier 2 — Protective Devices:** Relief valves sized to discharge the maximum possible inflow.
- **Tier 3 — Procedural Safety:** Inspection, testing, and maintenance protocols.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important study artifact** for this topic. Memorize every row.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Validity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hoop (Circumferential) Stress — Cylinder | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \, d}{2 \, t}$ | Thin-walled only |
| 2 | Longitudinal (Axial) Stress — Cylinder | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p \, d}{4 \, t}$ | Thin-walled only |
| 3 | Equivalent Stress — Cylinder | $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_h - \sigma_l = \dfrac{p \, d}{4 \, t}$ | Maximum shear stress |
| 4 | Stress — Spherical Vessel | $\sigma = \dfrac{p \, d}{4 \, t}$ | Thin-walled sphere |
| 5 | Required Wall Thickness — Cylinder | $t = \dfrac{p \, d}{2 \, \sigma_{allow} \, E_j} + c$ | ASME Sec VIII Div 1 |
| 6 | Required Wall Thickness — Sphere | $t = \dfrac{p \, d}{4 \, \sigma_{allow} \, E_j} + c$ | ASME Sec VIII Div 1 |
| 7 | Lamé — Radial Stress | $\sigma_r = A - \dfrac{B}{r^2}$ | Thick cylinder |
| 8 | Lamé — Hoop Stress | $\sigma_\theta = A + \dfrac{B}{r^2}$ | Thick cylinder |
| 9 | Lamé Constants | $A = \dfrac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$ | Internal & external pressure |
| 10 | Lamé Constants | $B = \dfrac{(p_i - p_o) \, r_i^2 \, r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$ | Internal & external pressure |
| 11 | Absolute Pressure Relation | $P_a = P_g + P_{atm}$ | Always |
| 12 | Test Pressure | $P_{test} = 1.3 \times P_{design}$ | Hydrostatic test |
| 13 | Relief Valve Orifice Area | $A = \dfrac{W}{C \, K \, P \, \sqrt{M/ZT})}$ | API 520 / API 521 |
| 14 | Spherical vs Cylinder Thickness Ratio | $t_{sphere} = \dfrac{t_{cylinder}}{2}$ | Same pressure & diameter |

Where the symbols denote:
- $p$ = internal design pressure (gauge)
- $d$ = internal diameter
- $t$ = wall thickness
- $r_i$ = inner radius, $r_o$ = outer radius
- $\sigma_{allow}$ = allowable stress (from code tables)
- $E_j$ = joint efficiency (weld quality factor)
- $c$ = corrosion allowance
- $W$ = required relieving capacity (mass flow)
- $C, K$ = discharge coefficient & capacity correction
- $M$ = molecular weight, $Z$ = compressibility, $T$ = temperature

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pressure system design governs:
- **Power generation:** Boilers, superheaters, steam drums (operating at 100–300 bar).
- **Oil & Gas:** Refinery distillation columns, hydrocrackers.
- **Chemical industry:** Ammonia synthesis (200 bar), urea reactors.
- **Pharmaceutical:** Sterilization autoclaves.
- **Aerospace:** Rocket motor casings (ultra-high pressure).
- **Cryogenics:** LNG storage tanks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hoop Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Vessel

**Engineering Setup:**
Consider a thin cylindrical pressure vessel of internal diameter $d$, wall thickness $t$, and internal pressure $p$. We slice the cylinder longitudinally along its axis and apply equilibrium to one half of the cylinder.

**Step 1 — Identify Forces**

Consider a cylinder of length $L$. The internal pressure acts on the projected (diametrical) area.

$$
A_{projected} = d \cdot L
$$

**Step 2 — Pressure Force Trying to Burst the Cylinder**

The net bursting force acting on the longitudinal section is the pressure times the projected area.

$$
F_{pressure} = p \cdot d \cdot L
$$

**Step 3 — Resisting Force from the Wall**

The wall resists this bursting force across two wall sections (one on each side of the cut). Each section has area $t \cdot L$. So total resisting area is $2 t L$.

$$
F_{resist} = \sigma_h \cdot (2 \, t \, L)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Equilibrium ($F_{pressure} = F_{resist}$)**

$$
p \cdot d \cdot L = \sigma_h \cdot 2 \, t \, L
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify (Cancel $L$ and Solve)**

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \, d}{2 \, t}
$$

This is the **hoop (circumferential) stress**. Note that hoop stress is the **dominant stress** and is twice the longitudinal stress.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Longitudinal Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylinder

**Engineering Setup:**
Now consider a transverse (cross-sectional) cut of the same cylinder. The pressure pushes the two end caps apart.

**Step 1 — Force on the End Cap**

The pressure acts on the full circular cross-section.

$$
A_{cross} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d^2
$$

**Step 2 — Net Axial Force**

$$
F_{axial} = p \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d^2
$$

**Step 3 — Resisting Wall Area**

The wall carries this load through its annular cross-section (mean circumference times thickness).

$$
A_{wall} = \pi \, d \, t
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Equilibrium**

$$
p \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, d^2 = \sigma_l \cdot \pi \, d \, t
$$

**Step 5 — Solve**

$$
\sigma_l = \dfrac{p \, d}{4 \, t}
$$

This is the **longitudinal (axial) stress** — exactly half the hoop stress.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thick Cylinder via Lamé's Equations

**Engineering Setup:**
For a thick cylinder ($t > D/20$), the assumption of uniform stress across the wall thickness is invalid. We use Lamé's method.

**Step 1 — Assume Stress Distribution Form**

Lamé proposed that the stress varies with radius in the form:

$$
\sigma_r = A - \dfrac{B}{r^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta = A + \dfrac{B}{r^2}
$$

Here, $A$ and $B$ are constants determined from boundary conditions.

**Step 2 — Apply Inner Boundary Condition ($r = r_i$, $\sigma_r = -p_i$)**

$$
-p_i = A - \dfrac{B}{r_i^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Outer Boundary Condition ($r = r_o$, $\sigma_r = -p_o$)**

$$
-p_o = A - \dfrac{B}{r_o^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract to Find $B$**

$$
p_o - p_i = B \left( \dfrac{1}{r_i^2} - \dfrac{1}{r_o^2} \right)
$$

$$
B = \dfrac{(p_i - p_o) \, r_i^2 \, r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Add to Find $A$**

$$
-p_i - p_o = 2A - B \left( \dfrac{1}{r_i^2} + \dfrac{1}{r_o^2} \right)
$$

Solving gives:

$$
A = \dfrac{p_i \, r_i^2 - p_o \, r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Lamé Equations (for closed-end thick cylinder)**

$$
\sigma_r = A - \dfrac{B}{r^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta = A + \dfrac{B}{r^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_z = \dfrac{p_i \, r_i^2 - p_o \, r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}
$$

The **maximum hoop stress** occurs at the inner radius ($r = r_i$) and the **maximum radial stress** is zero (compressive) at the inner wall and equals $-p_o$ at the outer wall.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of **500 mm** and a wall thickness of **10 mm**. It is subjected to an internal gauge pressure of **2 MPa**. Compute (a) hoop stress, (b) longitudinal stress, (c) whether the vessel is safe if the allowable stress is **100 MPa**.

**Given Data:**

$$
d = 500 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \text{ m}, \quad t = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.01 \text{ m}, \quad p = 2 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute Hoop Stress**

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \, d}{2 \, t} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.5}{2 \times 0.01}
$$

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{1.0}{0.02} = 50 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Longitudinal Stress**

$$
\sigma_l = \dfrac{p \, d}{4 \, t} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.5}{4 \times 0.01}
$$

$$
\sigma_l = \dfrac{1.0}{0.04} = 25 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Allowable Stress**

Since $\sigma_h = 50 \text{ MPa} < 100 \text{ MPa}$ (allowable), the vessel is **safe under normal operation**.

**Step 4 — Compute Test Pressure**

$$
P_{test} = 1.3 \times P_{design} = 1.3 \times 2 = 2.6 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Stress at Test Pressure (Verification)**

$$
\sigma_h^{test} = \dfrac{2.6 \times 0.5}{2 \times 0.01} = 65 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $65 \text{ MPa} < 100 \text{ MPa}$, the vessel also passes the hydrostatic test.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** Define a pressure system and list its major components.

**Model Answer:**
A pressure system is an assembly of pressure vessels, piping, fittings, pumps or compressors, safety devices, and instrumentation in which a fluid is contained at a pressure substantially different from atmospheric. Its major components are: **(1)** Pressure generation equipment (pumps, compressors, boilers), **(2)** Pressure containment equipment (vessels, pipes), **(3)** Pressure control equipment (regulators, valves), **(4)** Pressure protection devices (safety relief valves, rupture discs), and **(5)** Monitoring devices (gauges, transmitters, alarms).

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** Differentiate between **design pressure** and **maximum allowable working pressure (MAWP)**.

**Model Answer:**
**MAWP** is the maximum permissible gauge pressure at the top of the vessel during normal operation, derived from the thinnest section and code-allowable stress. **Design pressure** is the value used in the thickness calculation and is set slightly higher than MAWP (typically MAWP + 10% or the pressure at the most severe operating condition) to provide a safety margin. The test pressure is then computed as $1.3 \times$ design pressure.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
A thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of **600 mm** and is required to withstand an internal pressure of **3 MPa**. The allowable stress for the material is **120 MPa**. Determine:
1. The required wall thickness (neglecting corrosion allowance).
2. The longitudinal and hoop stresses in the vessel.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**

$$
d = 600 \text{ mm} = 0.6 \text{ m}, \quad p = 3 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_{allow} = 120 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 1 — Required Wall Thickness**

Using hoop stress formula and setting $\sigma_h \le \sigma_{allow}$:

$$
t = \dfrac{p \, d}{2 \, \sigma_{allow}} = \dfrac{3 \times 0.6}{2 \times 120}
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{1.8}{240} = 0.0075 \text{ m} = 7.5 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt **standard thickness = 8 mm**.

**Step 2 — Actual Hoop Stress (with 8 mm)**

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{3 \times 0.6}{2 \times 0.008} = \dfrac{1.8}{0.016} = 112.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Longitudinal Stress**

$$
\sigma_l = \dfrac{\sigma_h}{2} = \dfrac{112.5}{2} = 56.25 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Conclusion:** Both stresses are below the allowable 120 MPa. **Vessel is safe.**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
Explain the role of **safety relief valves** and **rupture discs** in a pressure system. How are they sized?

**Model Answer:**

A **safety relief valve (SRV)** is an automatic pressure-protection device designed to open when the system pressure exceeds the set pressure, discharge fluid, and thereby prevent overpressure. It is the **primary** protective device.

A **rupture disc** is a non-reclosing pressure-relieving device that bursts at a predetermined pressure. It is used either alone (when even small leaks are unacceptable, e.g., toxic fluids) or in combination with an SRV (installed below the SRV) to protect the SRV from corrosive media.

**Sizing (API 520 / API 521 method):**

The required orifice area is:

$$
A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K \cdot P \cdot \sqrt{M / (Z \cdot T)}}
$$

Where $W$ is the required relieving rate, $C$ and $K$ are discharge coefficients, $P$ is the set pressure, $M$ is molecular weight, $Z$ is compressibility, and $T$ is temperature. The SRV is then selected from manufacturer catalogs based on the computed orifice area.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Derive the expression for **hoop stress** and **longitudinal stress** in a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel. Show that the hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress.

**Model Solution:**

*(See detailed derivation in Section 3.1 and 3.2 of these notes.)*

From equilibrium of the longitudinal section:

$$
p \cdot d \cdot L = \sigma_h \cdot 2 \, t \cdot L \implies \sigma_h = \dfrac{p \, d}{2 \, t}
$$

From equilibrium of the cross-section:

$$
p \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \sigma_l \cdot \pi \, d \, t \implies \sigma_l = \dfrac{p \, d}{4 \, t}
$$

**Ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = \dfrac{p d / 2t}{p d / 4t} = \dfrac{4t}{2t} = 2
$$

Hence $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l$. (QED)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
Discuss the design considerations for pressure vessels according to **ASME Section VIII**. Mention the significance of joint efficiency and corrosion allowance.

**Model Answer:**

ASME Section VIII (Division 1) governs the design of unfired pressure vessels. Key design considerations are:

1. **Allowable Stress:** Drawn from ASME Section II material tables, typically the lower of $\frac{1}{4}$ ultimate tensile strength or $\frac{2}{3}$ yield strength.

2. **Joint Efficiency ($E_j$):** Accounts for the quality of the weld. Categories are 100% (full radiography), 85% (spot radiography), and 70% (no radiography). It appears in the denominator of the thickness formula — better welds reduce required thickness.

3. **Corrosion Allowance ($c$):** An extra thickness added to compensate for material loss due to corrosion during service life. Typical value is 1.6 mm to 3.2 mm depending on the fluid.

4. **Design Pressure:** Typically the **MAWP + 10%** margin or the maximum expected pressure under any operating scenario (including blocked outlet, fire case, etc.).

5. **Design Temperature:** The maximum expected metal temperature.

6. **Loadings:** Internal/external pressure, weight, wind, seismic, thermal expansion, nozzle loads.

The general thickness formula (ASME) is:

$$
t = \dfrac{p \, d}{2 \, \sigma_{allow} \, E_j - 1.2 \, p} + c
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm and m in the same calculation. *Always convert all dimensions to SI before substituting.*
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** Using $\dfrac{p d}{t}$ instead of $\dfrac{p d}{2 t}$ for hoop stress. This is the **#1 mistake** flagged by KTU examiners.
> 3. **Confusing gauge and absolute pressure:** The formulas use **gauge pressure**. Adding atmospheric pressure is incorrect.
> 4. **Ignoring corrosion allowance:** Not adding the corrosion term $c$ in design thickness — a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Thin-wall assumption violation:** Applying thin-walled formulas when $t > D/20$ — invalid and penalized.
> 6. **Diagram Skipping:** Even in derivation questions, KTU examiners award **0.5 to 1 mark** for a labeled free-body diagram. *Always draw the FBD.*

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **pressure system** contains fluid at pressure substantially different from atmospheric (typically $> 0.5$ bar gauge).
- [x] Five layers: **Generation, Containment, Control, Protection, Monitoring**.
- [x] **Hoop stress** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p d}{2 t}$ — dominant stress, governs thickness.
- [x] **Longitudinal stress** $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p d}{4 t}$ — half of hoop stress.
- [x] **Spherical vessel** has half the wall thickness of a cylinder for the same pressure and diameter — most efficient geometry.
- [x] **Thin-walled assumption:** $t \le D/20$. Outside this, use **Lamé's equations**.
- [x] Lamé constants: $A = \dfrac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$ and $B = \dfrac{(p_i - p_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$.
- [x] **MAWP** = normal operating maximum. **Design Pressure** = MAWP + margin (typically +10%). **Test Pressure** = 1.3 × Design.
- [x] ASME thickness formula includes **joint efficiency** ($E_j$) and **corrosion allowance** ($c$).
- [x] **Safety relief valves** are primary protection; **rupture discs** are used for toxic/corrosive or as SRV back-up.
- [x] Always convert to SI units before any numerical substitution.
- [x] Always draw a **free-body diagram** for derivation questions — examiners reward it.
- [x] Relief valve sizing uses **API 520**; orifice area formula is $A = \dfrac{W}{C K P \sqrt{M/(ZT)}}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Vessel Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pressure Vessel Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Pressure Vessel** is defined as a closed, leak-tight container designed to hold fluids (gases or liquids) at a pressure substantially different (higher or lower) from the ambient atmospheric pressure, with the pressure differential acting as the primary design load.

According to KTU 2024 scheme design-safety terminology, a pressure vessel is **any enclosure whose internal or external operating pressure exceeds 0.5 bar gauge**, irrespective of size, shape, or service application.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **MAWP (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure):** The maximum gauge pressure permissible at the top of the vessel under normal operating conditions.
> - **Design Pressure ($p_d$):** The pressure used in stress calculations, usually **MAWP + 10%** or **MAWP + 1 bar**, whichever is higher.
> - **Design Temperature ($T_d$):** The expected metal temperature corresponding to design pressure.
> - **Corrosion Allowance ($c$):** Extra thickness provided to compensate for material loss during service life.
> - **Joint Efficiency ($\eta$):** A factor ($0 \leq \eta \leq 1$) accounting for the weakening effect of welded/ riveted joints.

> [!NOTE]
> **Statutory Exclusion:** Vessels with internal diameter $\leq 150$ mm, or vessels used for domestic water supply up to **10 bar**, are typically exempt from formal pressure vessel codes (per Indian Boiler Regulations).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine blowing up a **balloon** 🎈. The thin rubber membrane stretches to contain the pressurized air inside. The tension in the rubber is analogous to the **hoop stress** in a cylindrical pressure vessel.

Now imagine a **soft drink can** 🥤. It is a thin-walled cylindrical shell with domed ends. Why are the ends dome-shaped? Because a curved surface distributes pressure-induced stress more uniformly than a flat surface. If the ends were flat, the can would bulge outward and burst immediately. The domes (called **torispherical** or **ellipsoidal heads**) use geometry to minimize material and maximize strength.

> A pressure vessel is essentially a **structural skin that converts internal pressure into tensile membrane stress**, where the designer's job is to ensure the skin (wall) is thick enough to withstand that tensile stress with an adequate margin of safety.

**Real-world examples** students must recognize for KTU viva:
- LPG cylinders (cylindrical + spherical end caps)
- Compressed air receivers
- Boiler shells (fire-tube, water-tube)
- Nuclear reactor pressure vessels (RPV)
- Aircraft fuselage sections (treated as thin pressure cylinders)

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 1.01325 \text{ bar} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Typical safety factor for pressure vessels:** $n = 3.5$ to $4.0$ (on ultimate tensile strength)
- **Joint efficiency ranges (weld):** $\eta = 0.70$ to $1.00$ based on NDE level

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Pressure Vessels (Hierarchical Logic)

Pressure vessels are categorized along three independent axes:

**Axis 1 — Wall Thickness Ratio:**
- **Thin-walled vessels:** Ratio $\frac{D_o}{D_i} \leq 1.2$ (or $\frac{t}{D_i} \leq 0.1$). Stress is assumed uniform across wall thickness. Membrane theory applies.
- **Thick-walled vessels:** Ratio $\frac{D_o}{D_i} > 1.2$. Stress varies significantly across thickness. Lamé equations are mandatory.

**Axis 2 — Geometric Shape:**
- **Cylindrical** (most common in industry — boilers, reactors, storage tanks)
- **Spherical** (highest strength-to-weight ratio — used for LPG, large storage)
- **Conical** (used as transition pieces / hoppers)
- **Torispherical / Ellipsoidal / Hemispherical** (used as end closures — "heads")

**Axis 3 — Operating Temperature:**
- **Cold / Non-fired vessels** (ambient to ~200 °C) — IS 2825
- **Hot / Fired vessels** (boilers) — Indian Boiler Regulations (IBR)

## 2.2 Thin-Walled Cylindrical Pressure Vessel — Membrane Stress Theory

The two principal stresses developed in a thin cylindrical shell under internal pressure $p$ are:

1. **Hoop (Circumferential) Stress $\sigma_h$** — acts along the circumference, trying to split the cylinder along its length.
2. **Longitudinal (Axial) Stress $\sigma_l$** — acts along the axis, trying to pull the cylinder apart lengthwise.

### Why two stresses? (Intuitive 'How' explanation)

When internal pressure pushes outward on the cylindrical wall:
- The wall resists by stretching **around the circumference** (creates hoop stress) — like a stretched rubber band.
- The wall simultaneously resists by stretching **along the axis** (creates longitudinal stress) — like a stretched string.
- The radial stress $\sigma_r \approx 0$ on the outer surface and $\sigma_r = -p$ on the inner surface, so for thin walls, $\sigma_r$ is neglected.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Applicable For |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | Hoop Stress | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ | N/mm² | Thin Cylinder |
| 2 | Longitudinal Stress | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$ | N/mm² | Thin Cylinder |
| 3 | Spherical Vessel Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$ | N/mm² | Thin Sphere |
| 4 | Maximum Shear Stress (cyl) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{pD}{8t}$ | N/mm² | Thin Cylinder |
| 5 | Thin-wall validity | $\dfrac{t}{D_i} \leq 0.1$ | — | Membrane theory |
| 6 | Required thickness (cyl) | $t = \dfrac{pD}{2\eta\sigma_{allow} - p}$ | mm | Design eqn. (ASME) |
| 7 | Required thickness (sph) | $t = \dfrac{pD}{4\eta\sigma_{allow} - p}$ | mm | Design eqn. (ASME) |
| 8 | Lamé radial stress | $\sigma_r = A - \dfrac{B}{r^{2}}$ | N/mm² | Thick cylinder |
| 9 | Lamé hoop stress | $\sigma_\theta = A + \dfrac{B}{r^{2}}$ | N/mm² | Thick cylinder |
| 10 | Lamé constant A (int. p only) | $A = \dfrac{p_i r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$ | N/mm² | Thick cylinder |
| 11 | Lamé constant B (int. p only) | $B = \dfrac{p_i r_i^{2} r_o^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$ | N/mm² | Thick cylinder |
| 12 | Volumetric strain (sphere) | $\epsilon_v = \dfrac{3pD}{4tE}(1-\nu)$ | — | Spherical dilation |
| 13 | Change in volume (cyl) | $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{pD}{4tE}(5-4\nu)$ | — | Cylindrical dilation |
| 14 | Design pressure | $p_d = p_{MAWP} + \text{safety margin}$ | bar | Pre-design |
| 15 | Hoop strain | $\epsilon_h = \dfrac{\sigma_h}{E} - \nu\dfrac{\sigma_l}{E}$ | — | Cylindrical |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas above are **mathematically verifiable from first principles of solid mechanics** and match standard KTU reference data.

## 2.4 Failure Theories Used in Pressure Vessel Design (Ktu 2024 Module-1 Emphasis)

Since pressure vessel walls are in **biaxial stress state** (hoop + longitudinal), simple uniaxial yield criteria must be extended:

**1. Maximum Principal Stress Theory (Rankine's):**
$$\sigma_1 \leq \dfrac{\sigma_{ut}}{n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_h \leq \dfrac{\sigma_{ut}}{n}$$
- Conservative for ductile materials — overdesigns in the longitudinal direction.

**2. Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca's):**
$$\dfrac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} \leq \dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{2n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_h - \sigma_l \leq \dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{n}$$

**3. Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises') — Preferred for ductile materials:**
$$\sigma_h^{2} - \sigma_h \sigma_l + \sigma_l^{2} \leq \left(\dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{n}\right)^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Preferred Choice:** Von Mises (DE) theory is the standard for ductile pressure vessel materials because it gives the most economical (least conservative) safe design.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pressure vessel design theory underpins:
- **Power generation:** Boiler drums, nuclear RPVs, steam headers.
- **Oil & gas:** Separators, hydrocrackers, LNG storage spheres.
- **Aerospace:** Rocket motor casings, LOX tanks.
- **Pharmaceutical:** Autoclaves, sterilizers.
- **Process industries:** Distillation columns, reactors.

> Every "Design for Safety" decision in such vessels ultimately reduces to controlling the **stress ratio** $\dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}} \leq \dfrac{1}{n}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Hoop Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Pressure Vessel

**Problem Setup:** Consider a thin cylindrical shell of internal diameter $D$, wall thickness $t$ (where $t \ll D$), length $L$, subjected to internal gauge pressure $p$.

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram Construction:**
Cut the cylinder along a longitudinal diametral plane. The internal pressure $p$ acts on the projected area $D \cdot L$. This pressure is resisted by the hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acting on the two cut wall sections, each of cross-sectional area $t \cdot L$.

**Step 2 — Force Balance Equation (Equilibrium):**
The total pressure force pushing the two halves apart must equal the total resisting force from the wall material.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pressure Force} &= \text{Resisting Wall Force} \\[4pt]
p \cdot (D \cdot L) &= \sigma_h \cdot (2 \cdot t \cdot L) \\[4pt]
p \cdot D \cdot L &= 2 \sigma_h t L \\[4pt]
\sigma_h &= \frac{pD}{2t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Alternative Expression using radius:**
Since $D = 2r$ (where $r$ is internal radius):

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{p(2r)}{2t} = \frac{pr}{t}
$$

**Step 4 — Validity Check (Sanity):**
- Doubling pressure doubles stress ✓
- Doubling diameter doubles stress ✓
- Doubling thickness halves stress ✓

> **Conclusion:** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ — the **maximum principal stress** in a thin cylindrical pressure vessel.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Longitudinal (Axial) Stress

**Step 1 — Section the cylinder perpendicular to its axis** (imagine cutting it into two short cups).

**Step 2 — Identify the force from pressure acting on the end cap:**
Projected area on the end cap = $\dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$

$$
F_{pressure} = p \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the resisting force in the wall:**
The thin wall forms a ring of cross-sectional area $\pi D t$ at the cut section. The longitudinal stress $\sigma_l$ acts uniformly over this ring.

$$
F_{wall} = \sigma_l \cdot (\pi D t)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply force equilibrium:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_l \cdot (\pi D t) &= p \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \\[4pt]
\sigma_l &= \frac{pD}{4t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparative Observation:**
The longitudinal stress is exactly **half** the hoop stress:
$$
\frac{\sigma_l}{\sigma_h} = \frac{pD/4t}{pD/2t} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

This means **the cylinder will always split along its length first**, never across its width. This is the physical reason why boilers are often seen to fail by longitudinal cracks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Thick-Walled Cylinder Stresses — Lamé's Equations

**Step 1 — Assumptions:**
- Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic.
- Plane sections remain plane (generalized plane strain).
- Axisymmetric loading; deformation is axisymmetric.

**Step 2 — Compatibility / Equilibrium in polar coordinates:**
For an axisymmetric problem, equilibrium reduces to:

$$
\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Assume polynomial form for $\sigma_r$:**
A solution of the form $\sigma_r = A - B/r^2$ is a classical integrable form. Differentiating:

$$
\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} = \frac{2B}{r^3}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the equilibrium equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{2B}{r^3} + \frac{(A - B/r^2) - \sigma_\theta}{r} &= 0 \\[4pt]
\frac{2B}{r^3} + \frac{A - B/r^2 - \sigma_\theta}{r} &= 0 \\[4pt]
A - B/r^2 - \sigma_\theta &= -\frac{2B}{r^2} \\[4pt]
\sigma_\theta &= A + B/r^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply boundary conditions (Internal pressure $p_i$, External pressure $p_o = 0$):**

At $r = r_i$ (inner radius): $\sigma_r = -p_i$ (compressive)
At $r = r_o$ (outer radius): $\sigma_r = 0$ (free surface)

$$
\begin{aligned}
-p_i &= A - \frac{B}{r_i^2} \\[4pt]
0 &= A - \frac{B}{r_o^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve the simultaneous equations:**

From the second equation: $A = \dfrac{B}{r_o^2}$

Substituting into the first:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-p_i &= \frac{B}{r_o^2} - \frac{B}{r_i^2} = B \left( \frac{1}{r_o^2} - \frac{1}{r_i^2} \right) = -B \left( \frac{r_o^2 - r_i^2}{r_o^2 r_i^2} \right) \\[4pt]
B &= \frac{p_i r_i^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
A = \frac{B}{r_o^2} = \frac{p_i r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}
$$

**Step 7 — Final Lamé Equations (Internal pressure only):**

$$
\sigma_r = \frac{p_i r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} \left( 1 - \frac{r_o^2}{r^2} \right)
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta = \frac{p_i r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} \left( 1 + \frac{r_o^2}{r^2} \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** At $r = r_i$ (inner surface), $\sigma_\theta$ is **maximum**. This is why pressure vessels fail from the **inside** first — fatigue cracks initiate at the inner wall.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Theory

| Vessel Type | Wall Ratio | Governing Theory | Failure Theory | KTU Frequency |
|-------------|------------|------------------|----------------|---------------|
| Storage tank | $t/D < 0.05$ | Membrane (thin) | DE (von Mises) | High |
| Boiler drum | $0.05 < t/D < 0.1$ | Membrane (thin) | Tresca | High |
| High-pressure cylinder | $t/D > 0.1$ | Lamé (thick) | DE | Medium |
| Spherical LPG | $t/D \approx 0.02$ | Membrane (thin) | DE | High |
| Nuclear RPV | $t/D \approx 0.15$ | Lamé (thick) | ASME Code | Low |
| Rocket casing | $t/D \approx 0.05$ | Membrane + thermal | DE | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pressure Vessel — Functional Architecture Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow of pressure vessel design for safety**, showing how inputs (loads, codes, materials) are transformed into outputs (final wall thickness, safety factors) through the KTU 2024 design process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA["DESIGN INPUTS\nInternal Pressure p\nDiameter D\nDesign Temperature Td\nService Life L"]:::in
    inputB["CODE SELECTION\nASME Section VIII Div 1\nIS 2825\nIBR 1950"]:::code
    inputC["MATERIAL DATA\nYield Strength Sy\nUltimate Strength Su\nModulus of Elasticity E\nPoisson Ratio nu"]:::mat

    decision1{"Thin or Thick\nWall Check\nt over Di <= 0.1"}:::dec

    pathA["THIN WALL MEMBRANE THEORY\nHoop Stress sigma h equals pD over 2t\nLongitudinal Stress sigma l equals pD over 4t"]:::thin
    pathB["THICK WALL LAME EQUATIONS\nRadial sigma r equals A minus B over r squared\nHoop sigma theta equals A plus B over r squared"]:::thick

    failSelect{"FAILURE THEORY\nDuctile or Brittle"}:::dec2

    pathC["VON MISES DE THEORY\nsigma h squared minus sigma h times sigma l plus sigma l squared"]:::vm
    pathD["TRESCA MAX SHEAR\nsigma h minus sigma l equals Sy over n"]:::tresca
    pathE["RANKINE MAX STRESS\nsigma h equals Sut over n"]:::rankine

    thicken["REQUIRED THICKNESS CALCULATION\nt equals pD over 2 eta sigma allow minus p"]:::thick2
    safetyCheck{"SAFETY FACTOR CHECK\nn actual greater than n design"}:::dec3

    pathC --> thicken
    pathD --> thicken
    pathE --> thicken
    thicken --> safetyCheck
    safetyCheck -->|Pass| pass
    safetyCheck -->|Fail| reject
    reject --> inputA
```

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **stress components and their relative magnitudes** along a longitudinal cross-section of a thin cylindrical pressure vessel.
