# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Spring-Loaded Conventional PRV

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *internal functional architecture* of a spring-loaded Pressure Relief Valve. It identifies the eight critical sub-assemblies and their functional interactions during normal, lift, and reseat modes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InA[Inlet Flanged Nozzle] --> BodyA[Valve Body Pressure Boundary]
    BodyA --> SeatA[Nozzle Seat Geometry]
    SeatA --> DiscA[Disc and Disc Holder]
    DiscA --> StemA[Stem and Guide Surfaces]
    StemA --> SpringA[Helical Compression Spring]
    SpringA --> AdjA[Spring Adjusting Screw]
    AdjA --> BonnetA[Bonnet and Cap]
    DiscA --> LiftA[Lift Adjustment Hanger Pin]
    OutletA[Outlet Flanged Nozzle] --> BodyA
    BonnetA -.-> LevA[Lever Optional for Weight Loaded]
    BodyA --> GaskA[Gasket and Bolting Assembly]
```

**Functional Reading of the Diagram:**

- The **Inlet Flanged Nozzle** channels process fluid into the valve body.
- The **Nozzle Seat** defines the *effective discharge area* (orifice size).
- The **Disc and Disc Holder** form the *moving barrier*; disc lift creates the flow path.
- The **Stem** transfers force from spring to disc through guide surfaces.
- The **Helical Spring** provides the *closing force*; it is preloaded by the **Adjusting Screw**.
- The **Bonnet** encloses the spring and is sealed against fugitive emissions.
- The **Outlet** discharges the relieved fluid to the safe disposal system (flare, vent, or atmospheric tailpipe).
- The **Gasket and Bolting Assembly** ensures pressure boundary integrity per ASME B16.34.

## 4.2 Functional Process Flow — Sizing & Selection Algorithm

This flowchart outlines the **decision logic** an engineer follows when sizing a pressure relief device per API 520 / API 521.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    St1[Step 1 Identify Relief Scenario] --> St2{Step 2 Fluid Phase Vapor or Liquid}
    St2 -->|Vapor or Gas| St3[Step 3a Compute W and P1]
    St2 -->|Liquid| St4[Step 3b Compute Q and dP]
    St2 -->|Multiphase| St5[Step 3c Use HEM or Omega Method]
    St3 --> St6{Step 4 Check Critical Flow P2 over P1 less than nu sub c}
    St4 --> St7[Step 4b Liquid Single Phase Equation]
    St5 --> St8[Step 4c Two Phase Method]
    St6 -->|Yes Critical| St9[Step 5a Apply Vapor Critical Equation]
    St6 -->|No Subcritical| St10[Step 5b Apply Subcritical Vapor Equation]
    St9 --> St11[Step 6 Compute A and Round Up to Standard Orifice]
    St10 --> St11
    St7 --> St11
    St8 --> St11
    St11 --> St12[Step 7 Select Body Size and End Connections]
    St12 --> St13[Step 8 Compute Inlet and Outlet Pipe Pressure Drop]
    St13 --> St14[Step 9 Specify Set Pressure CDTP and Blowdown]
    St14 --> St15[Step 10 Final Documentation per ASME Section VIII]
```

**Operational Reading of the Algorithm:**

- **Step 1:** Identify *why* relief is required (fire case, blocked outlet, control valve failure, runaway reaction).
- **Steps 3a/3b/3c:** Determine the *relief rate* — this is the most judgement-intensive step.
- **Step 4:** The critical vs. subcritical decision is purely a function of pressure ratio and $k$.
- **Step 6:** *Always round up* the calculated area to the next standard orifice. Undersizing is unsafe; oversizing by 25% is acceptable, but excessive oversizing leads to *simmer leak* or *chatter*.
- **Step 8:** The inlet pipe pressure drop at relief flow must be **< 3% of set pressure** per API 520 to prevent *chatter* (rapid cycling) and *opening lag*.

## 4.3 Functional State Diagram — PRV Operating States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    Closed: State 1 Closed at Normal Pressure
    Lift: State 2 Lifting at Set Pressure
    FullOpen: State 3 Fully Open at Overpressure
    Discharging: State 4 Discharging at Relieving Pressure
    Reseat: State 5 Reseating as Pressure Drops
    ClosedAgain: State 6 Reclosed System Re-pressurizes
    Closed --> Lift : P1 reaches Pset
    Lift --> FullOpen : P1 increases to 1.1 Pset
    FullOpen --> Discharging : Steady relieving flow established
    Discharging --> Reseat : P1 drops below reseat pressure
    Reseat --> ClosedAgain : Disc contacts seat
    ClosedAgain --> Closed : System normal
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Differentiate between a Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) and a Pressure Safety Valve (PSV). Mention the service for which each is typically used.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Attribute | Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) | Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) |
|---|---|---|
| Service fluid | **Liquids** (single-phase) | **Gases, vapors, steam** |
| Opening characteristic | Opens *proportionally* to pressure (modulating / proportional lift) | Opens *abruptly* with a popping action at set pressure |
| Set pressure definition | Pressure at which the valve begins to open (simmer) | Pressure at which the first *measurable* lift occurs after popping |
| Typical use | Pump discharge, hydrocarbon liquid lines, hydrostatic test bypass | Steam boilers, distillation columns, gas receivers, compressor discharge scrubbers |

*(1 mark each: service fluid, opening behavior, typical application)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Define the following terms with reference to pressure relief devices: (i) Blowdown, (ii) Cold Differential Test Pressure (CDTP), (iii) Built-up Backpressure.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks, 1 each):**

**(i) Blowdown:** The difference between the set pressure and the reseat pressure of a relief valve, expressed as a percentage of set pressure. It represents the *hysteresis* between opening and closing. Typical values: 3%–7% for conventional PRVs and < 2% for pilot-operated valves. *(1 mark)*

**(ii) Cold Differential Test Pressure (CDTP):** The pressure at which a relief valve is adjusted and tested on a shop test rig at ambient temperature. It is set *higher* than the hot set pressure to compensate for the relaxation of the spring modulus at elevated operating temperature. *(1 mark)*

**(iii) Built-up Backpressure:** The pressure that develops at the *outlet* of the relief valve *only during* the discharge event, as a result of flow through the downstream piping, headers, and disposal system. It is distinguished from *superimposed* backpressure, which exists in the discharge system even when the valve is closed. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a neat sketch the construction and working of a *balanced-bellows* pressure relief valve. Explain how the bellows isolate the backpressure effects on the disc. Why is this construction preferred for services involving **toxic, corrosive, or polymerizing fluids**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A saturated steam relief valve is required to discharge **W = 12,000 kg/hr** of saturated steam at a set pressure of **8 bar (g)**. The valve discharges to atmosphere. Calculate the *required effective orifice area* in mm², and select the *next standard API 520 orifice*. Use $K_{d} = 0.975$, $K_{b} = 1.0$, $K_{c} = 1.0$.

**Given Data:**
- Saturated steam temperature at 8 bar(g) ≈ **170.4 °C = 443.55 K**
- Molecular weight of steam M = 18.02
- 1 bar(g) = 14.504 psig; 8 bar(g) = 116.03 psig
- $P_{1} = 8 + 1.01325 = 9.013$ bar(a) = **130.7 psia**
- $T = 170.4 + 273.15 = 443.55$ K = **443.55 × 1.8 = 798.4 °R**
- Conversion: 1 kg/hr = **2.2046 lb/hr**
- W = 12,000 × 2.2046 = **26,455 lb/hr**

---

#### **Solution (a) — Balanced Bellows PRV**

**Construction (3 Marks):**
- A **metallic bellows** is welded onto the *upper* face of the disc, sealing the spring chamber from the process fluid.
- The lower half of the bellows is exposed to the *inlet* pressure; the upper half to the *outlet* (back) pressure.
- The stem passes *through* the bellows and is connected to the spring retainer outside the bellows.
- The bonnet, spring, and adjusting screw remain *outside* the bellows, in atmospheric environment.

**Working (2 Marks):**
- The hydraulic force on the disc has two components: (i) area exposed to *inlet* pressure, and (ii) area exposed to *outlet* pressure.
- For a *conventional* valve, both areas are subjected to *outlet* pressure, so increasing backpressure reduces the net opening force → valve opens late, at higher pressure.
- For a *balanced-bellows* valve, the bellows **isolates the backpressure** from the spring chamber. The net area seeing backpressure is now only the *effective bellows area*, which is *very small*.
- Hence, set pressure becomes *independent* of backpressure — this is the **backpressure-compensating** effect.

**Why preferred for toxic / polymerizing fluids (2 Marks):**
- The bellows acts as a *secondary* pressure boundary, preventing process fluid from leaking past the stem into the atmosphere — **fugitive emission control**.
- This is mandatory under **Clean Air Act (US EPA)** and **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.169** for toxic or carcinogenic services.
- For polymerizing fluids (e.g., styrene, butadiene), the bellows prevents the process fluid from reaching the spring chamber and *gumming up* the moving parts, ensuring reliable operation.
- Suitable for **hydrogen fluoride (HF), chlorine, vinyl chloride monomer (VCM), phosgene**, and similar hazardous services.

---

#### **Solution (b) — Steam Sizing (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute C for steam (k = 1.3, Z = 1):**

$$C = 520 \cdot \sqrt{1.3 \cdot \left(\frac{2}{1.3+1}\right)^{\frac{1.3+1}{1.3-1}}}$$

$$C = 520 \cdot \sqrt{1.3 \cdot (0.8696)^{7.667}$$

$$C = 520 \cdot \sqrt{1.3 \cdot 0.3233} = 520 \cdot \sqrt{0.4203} \approx 520 \cdot 0.6483 \approx 337.1$$

**Step 2 — Account for 10% overpressure (ASME standard for non-fire case):**

$$P_{1} = 1.1 \times 130.7 = 143.77 \text{ psia}$$

**Step 3 — Apply API 520 Saturated Steam Equation:**

$$A = \frac{W}{C \cdot K_{d} \cdot P_{1} \cdot K_{b} \cdot K_{c}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T \cdot Z}{M}}$$

$$A = \frac{26{,}455}{337.1 \cdot 0.975 \cdot 143.77 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.0} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{798.4 \cdot 1.0}{18.02}}$$

$$A = \frac{26{,}455}{47{,}254.6} \cdot \sqrt{44.31}$$

$$A = 0.5599 \cdot 6.656 \approx 3.727 \text{ in}^2$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mm²:**

$$A = 3.727 \times 645.16 \approx 2405 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Select Next Standard Orifice:**

From API 520 standard orifice sizes:
- Orifice **M** = 3.600 in² (2322 mm²) → *smaller*, not acceptable
- Orifice **N** = 4.340 in² (2800 mm²) → *next larger*, **selected**

**Final Answer:** **Orifice N (4.34 in²)** on a valve body of nominal size **6" × 8"** or **8" × 10"** (selected based on body pressure rating and piping layout).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **layer of protection analysis (LOPA)** concept in the context of pressure relief devices. Why is a PRD considered a *passive* safeguard, and what is its **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** in a typical SIL classification per **IEC 61511**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A vertical cylindrical storage tank of diameter 4 m and height 6 m is protected by a weight-loaded pressure-vacuum relief valve (PVRV). The tank contains **gasoline** (specific gravity $G_{f} = 0.72$, vapor molecular weight M = 66, $C_{p}/C_{v} = 1.10$). The **out-breathing** (thermal) flow rate is **3000 Nm³/hr** at a relieving pressure of **+50 mmH₂O (gauge)**. **In-breathing** (vacuum) occurs at **−30 mmH₂O (gauge)**. Compute the **required vent area** for *out-breathing* (in cm²) using a discharge coefficient of $K_{d} = 0.65$ for the weight-loaded pallet. Assume subcritical flow.

---

#### **Solution (a) — LOPA & PRD Classification (7 Marks)**

**Layer of Protection Analysis (3 Marks):**
- LOPA is a semi-quantitative methodology used to evaluate the **risk reduction** contributed by independent protection layers (IPLs) against a hazardous scenario.
- Layers typically include: (1) **Inherent Safe Design**, (2) **Process Control Systems (BPCS)**, (3) **Alarms and Operator Intervention**, (4) **Safety Instrumented Functions (SIF/SIS)**, (5) **Physical Protection** (relief devices, dikes, blast walls), (6) **Emergency Response**.
- The cumulative *Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)* of all layers must be less than the tolerable frequency of the event.

**PRD as Passive Safeguard (2 Marks):**
- A PRD is **passive** because it requires **no external energy** (no air, no power, no signal) to function. It activates purely on the *thermodynamic* event of overpressure.
- Per **IEC 61511**, a *passive* device typically has a **PFD between 1×10⁻² and 1×10⁻¹** (i.e., 1% to 10% probability of failing to operate on demand).
- This places a properly maintained pressure relief valve at approximately **SIL 1 / SIL 2** equivalence for *reactive* risk reduction.
- A rupture disc, being a *one-shot* device with no moving parts, has a *better* PFD of around **1×10⁻³ to 1×10⁻²**.

**Engineering Implication (2 Marks):**
- A PRD is the *last* layer; if it fails, the consequence is usually *catastrophic* (vessel rupture, BLEVE, fireball).
- Hence, ASME Section VIII requires *periodic bench testing* of PRD, typically every 12 months for process service, to ensure the PFD remains within bounds.
- A *single* PRD cannot reduce the SIL requirement by 2 levels — generally, one PRD layer = 1 order of magnitude risk reduction.

---

#### **Solution (b) — PVRV Sizing for Out-Breathing (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify Subcritical Flow Condition (1 Mark):**

Compute the critical pressure ratio for gasoline vapor ($k = 1.10$):

$$\nu_{c} = \left(\frac{2}{1.10+1}\right)^{\frac{1.10}{1.10-1}} = \left(\frac{2}{2.10}\right)^{11} = (0.9524)^{11} \approx 0.583$$

Since the tank pressure is at 50 mmH₂O (gauge) and discharges to atmosphere, the backpressure is essentially zero; the ratio $P_{2}/P_{1} \approx 0$, which is well below 0.583. So technically, the flow is *critical*. However, the problem *explicitly* says to use subcritical. We proceed with a subsonic approach (Orifice equation).

**Step 2 — Use a Subsonic Air-Equivalent Empirical Formula (3 Marks):**

For low-pressure storage tank venting, **API 2000** is the governing standard. The out-breathing flow $Q_{a}$ is converted to *standard air* equivalent:

$$Q_{a} = Q \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M_{air}}{M_{vapor}} \cdot \frac{T_{vapor}}{T_{air}} \cdot \frac{Z_{air}}{Z_{vapor}}}$$

Where $M_{air} = 28.97$, $T_{air} = 288$ K, $Z_{air} = 1.0$.

$$Q_{a} = 3000 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{28.97}{66} \cdot \frac{T_{vapor}}{288} \cdot 1.0}$$

Assuming gasoline vapor at $T \approx 35 °C = 308$ K (warm tank):

$$Q_{a} = 3000 \cdot \sqrt{0.4389 \cdot 1.0694} = 3000 \cdot \sqrt{0.4694} = 3000 \cdot 0.6851 \approx 2055 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr (air equivalent)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Subcritical Orifice Equation for Low-Pressure Vents (3 Marks):**

For a weight-loaded pallet, the empirical sizing equation per API 2000 is:

$$A = \frac{Q_{a}}{93.6 \cdot K_{d}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{H \cdot G_{f}}}$$

Where:
- $A$ = vent area in cm²
- $Q_{a}$ = air-equivalent flow in Nm³/hr
- $H$ = relieving pressure in mmH₂O (gauge) = 50
- $G_{f}$ = vapor specific gravity relative to air

For gasoline vapor:
$$G_{vapor} = \frac{M_{vapor}}{M_{air}} = \frac{66}{28.97} = 2.278$$

Substituting:

$$A = \frac{2055}{93.6 \cdot 0.65} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{50 \cdot 2.278}}$$

$$A = \frac{2055}{60.84} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{113.9}} = 33.78 \cdot \sqrt{0.00878}$$

$$A = 33.78 \cdot 0.0937 \approx 3.165 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Final Selection:**

The calculated area of **3.17 cm²** is *very small*, and standard PVRV pallet sizes are typically *larger* (e.g., 50 cm², 100 cm², etc.). In practice, the *in-breathing* (vacuum) requirement would be calculated separately, and the *larger* of the two values would govern. The selected PVRV would be of size **6" × 8"** or **8" × 8"** nominal with a pallet area in the range of **80–120 cm²** to handle *both* in- and out-breathing plus a margin for *pallet open by 5% set pressure* tolerance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 10% overpressure** in Step 3 of the saturated steam example. Without it, $P_{1}$ is wrong and the area calculation carries a 10% error.
> 2. **Mixing critical and subcritical flow constants**: The constant $C = 356$ for steam is *only* valid for $k = 1.3$. For other gases, recompute $C$ using the $k$-dependent formula.
> 3. **Not rounding up the orifice**: Choosing orifice M when N is required is a *safety violation* and a guaranteed 1-mark loss. The rule is **always round up**, never down.
> 4. **Confusing the cold set pressure with the hot set pressure**: Failing to apply the temperature derating factor when the operating temperature exceeds 200°F will lose 2 marks in design-based questions.
> 5. **Liquid sizing without $K_{w}$**: For viscous liquids, the simple liquid equation over-predicts flow. Use API 520 Fig. 11 to find $K_{w} < 1$ and re-evaluate.
> 6. **Forgetting the backpressure factor $K_{b}$**: For *balanced-bellows* valves discharging into a header with non-zero superimposed backpressure, $K_{b} < 1$ must be read from API 520 Fig. 8.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination:**

- [x] A **Pressure Relief Device** is a *passive* safety device protecting vessels from overpressure; the *last* line of defense per ASME Section VIII.
- [x] **Set pressure** = pressure at first measurable lift; **Blowdown** = set pressure − reseat pressure (3–7% typical).
- [x] **Cold set pressure** must be set *higher* than the *hot set pressure* to compensate for spring relaxation at elevated temperature.
- [x] **Critical flow** condition: $P_{2}/P_{1} \le \nu_{c} = (2/(k+1))^{k/(k-1)}$. Critical flow depends only on $P_{1}$ — independent of $P_{2}$.
- [x] **Standard orifice designations** (API 520): D through T, ranging from **0.110 in² to 26.00 in²**.
- [x] **Sizing equations** to memorize: vapor critical (with $C$-function), saturated steam (constant 51.5), liquid (constant 38.0 or 35.0), and combined with $K_{d}, K_{b}, K_{c}$.
- [x] **Rupture Disc** is used in *series* with a PRV for toxic, corrosive, or polymerizing service — it isolates the PRV from process fluid.
- [x] **Balanced Bellows** PRV isolates *backpressure* from the spring chamber — used when superimposed backpressure exceeds 10% of set pressure.
- [x] **Inlet pipe pressure drop** at relief flow must be **< 3% of set pressure** to prevent *chatter* and *opening lag* (API 520, Section 4.4.5).
- [x] **Orifice area must always be rounded UP** to the next standard size — undersizing is unsafe.
- [x] **PFD** of a well-maintained PRD = **1×10⁻² to 1×10⁻¹** per IEC 61511 (SIL 1 / SIL 2 equivalent).
- [x] **Codes & Standards** to remember for the exam: ASME Section VIII (mandatory), API 520 (sizing), API 521 (scenarios), API 2000 (low-pressure storage), IEC 61511 (SIF/SIL), OSHA 1910.169 (testing).
- [x] **Fire case overpressure allowance**: 21% for unfired vessels under external fire exposure (per ASME Section VIII, Appendix M).
- [x] **Block valve** is permitted *only upstream* of a PRV, never downstream (to prevent seat damage and ensure open flow path).
- [x] **Test interval**: PRVs in *normal* service — every 12 months; *corrosive* service — every 6 months (per API 576).
- [x] For **flashing liquids** (hot water, molten sulfur), use **API 520 Part II Omega Method** or **HEM** — the simple liquid equation is invalid.
- [x] **Sizing always for the *worst-case scenario***: blocked outlet, external fire, utility failure, or runaway reaction — whichever demands the *largest* relief rate.

> **End of Topic Notes — Pressure Relief Devices and Design (Module 1)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Relief
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fire Relief

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fire Relief** is a critical engineering design provision in pressure vessels, storage tanks, and process equipment that ensures controlled and safe discharge of internal fluid (vapor, gas, or liquid) when the equipment is exposed to an **external fire engulfment** or **adjacent fire** scenario. The primary objective is to prevent catastrophic **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion (BLEVE)** or vessel rupture caused by uncontrolled pressure rise when the contained liquid is heated beyond its saturation temperature at the relieving pressure.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, fire relief is studied as a subset of **Pressure Relief Device (PRD) Sizing** under the **API 521 / API 520 / IS 3233** framework, where students are expected to size relief areas for **wetted** (liquid-covered) and **unwetted** (vapor-space) vessel surfaces during pool fire and jet fire scenarios.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Fire relief is **scenario-based design**, not normal-operation design. The designer must assume the worst-case fire exposure (i.e., the entire wetted area is engulfed) and ensure the relief device has enough capacity to vent the vapor generated from this heat input.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **pressure cooker** left on a gas stove with the safety whistle (weight) jammed. As the burner transfers heat, the water inside boils faster than steam can escape. Pressure builds, the metal stretches, and eventually the cooker explodes catastrophically — this is exactly what **fire engulfment** does to an industrial vessel.

**Fire relief** is the engineered equivalent of a perfectly functioning pressure cooker whistle — but sized for thousands of kilograms of hydrocarbon and flames of 1000 °C. The relief valve acts as a "controlled escape hatch," continuously venting vapor so the internal pressure never exceeds the vessel's **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Metric:** The standard design heat flux used by API 521 for pool fires on uninsulated wetted steel surfaces is **20,000 BTU/(hr·ft²)** ≈ **63.1 kW/m²**. This is a benchmark value students must memorize for KTU problems.

### 1.3 Why Fire Relief is Mandatory in Process Safety

- **Thermal Expansion:** Liquids (especially water) expand ~1,700 times when vaporized. Even a 1% full vessel becomes highly dangerous under fire.
- **BLEVE Prevention:** A BLEVE occurs when a flame impinges on a vessel wall above the liquid level (vapor space), weakening the steel and causing violent rupture with shrapnel projection up to hundreds of meters.
- **Regulatory Mandate:** Per the Indian Factories Act, OISD standards, and API 521, all hydrocarbon service pressure vessels operating above **15 psig (1.03 barg)** must have fire relief analysis documented.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physics of Fire Relief — Step-by-Step Logic

The fire relief sizing problem follows a clean four-step logic chain:

1. **Identify the Fire Scenario**
   - **Total Fire Engulfment** → assume the entire wetted surface is exposed.
   - **Adjacent Fire / Partial Engulfment** → only a fraction of the wetted area receives radiation.
   - **Jet Fire vs. Pool Fire** → jet fires have higher localized heat flux.

2. **Calculate the Effective Wetted Surface Area (A_w)**
   - Wetted surface = internal vessel surface in contact with liquid below the normal liquid level.
   - For horizontal cylindrical tanks: include cylindrical shell up to the liquid level + both heads (2/3 of head area for elliptical, 0.542 of head area for torispherical).

3. **Compute the Total Heat Input (Q)**
   - API 521 empirical correlation for hydrocarbon pool fires:
   - **For wetted areas ≤ 280 ft² (26 m²):**
     Q = 20,000 × A_w  BTU/hr
   - **For wetted areas > 280 ft²:**
     Q = 20,000 × (A_w)^0.82  BTU/hr
   - Adjustments:
     - **Unwetted area contribution:** Q_unwetted = 20,000 × A_unwetted × F
       where F is the environmental factor (typically 1.0 if no drainage, 0.5 with proper drainage).
     - **Insulation credit:** Apply a Credit Factor (CF) of **0.05 / inch of insulation thickness** (cementitious or mineral wool) up to a maximum reduction of **95%**.
     - **Water spray / Deluge credit:** Q can be reduced by up to 50% if approved water spray systems exist.

4. **Calculate the Required Relief Area (A)**
   - Use the **API 521 isothermal discharge equation** for vapor relief:
   - A = Q / (K × √(P₁ − P₂))
   - K is the gas-specific coefficient (function of vapor specific heat ratio k, molecular weight M, and inlet temperature T₁).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | Q = 20,000 × A_w | Heat input for A_w ≤ 280 ft² | BTU/hr |
| 2 | Q = 20,000 × (A_w)^0.82 | Heat input for A_w > 280 ft² | BTU/hr |
| 3 | Q_insulated = Q × (1 − CF) | With credit factor CF = 0.05 × thickness(in) | BTU/hr |
| 4 | Q_total = Q_wetted + Q_unwetted | Sum of all heat contributions | BTU/hr |
| 5 | A = Q ÷ [K × √(P₁ − P₂)] | Relief area (API 520 / 521) | in² |
| 6 | K_d = 0.975 × √(k × (2/(k+1))^((k+1)/(k−1))) | Discharge coefficient for gas | unitless |
| 7 | K = K_d × √(M ÷ (Z × T)) | Coefficient K (gas property) | (lb·mol·°R)^0.5 / (psi·in²·min) |
| 8 | A_w = π × D × L_liquid + heads | Wetted area (cylindrical tank) | ft² |
| 9 | t_min = (P × R) ÷ (S × E − 0.6 × P) | Minimum required wall thickness | in |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** In all LaTeX expressions, use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values and divisions to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. For example, write `\sqrt{P_1 \mid - \mid P_2}` or simply `√(P₁ − P₂)`.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Refineries & Petrochemical Plants:** Sizing PSVs (Pressure Safety Valves) on hydrocracker reactors, distillation columns, and product storage tanks.
- **LNG Storage:** Cryogenic tanks require sophisticated fire relief to handle both boil-off and external fire simultaneously.
- **Ammonia Refrigeration:** Fire on an ammonia receiver is a textbook KTU problem — the latent heat of ammonia (≈ 590 kJ/kg) governs the vapor generation rate.
- **Pharmaceutical Vessels:** Solvent receivers handling acetone, methanol, etc., require API 521 fire case analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the API 521 Relief Area Equation

The fundamental basis is the **mass flow rate through an orifice** under choked (sonic) flow conditions for vapor relief.

**Step 1 — Mass Flow Equation (Choked Flow for Ideal Gas):**

The general compressible flow equation through a relief valve orifice is:

$$\dot{m} = C \cdot A \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k \cdot M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T_1} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\frac{k+1}{k-1}}}$$

where:
- C = 0.0404 (unit conversion constant for US units)
- A = relief area (in²)
- P₁ = upstream relieving pressure (psia)
- k = specific heat ratio Cp/Cv
- M = molecular weight (lb/lb-mol)
- Z = compressibility factor
- R = 1545 ft·lbf/(lb-mol·°R)
- T₁ = relieving temperature (°R)

**Step 2 — Rearranging to Express in Terms of Heat Input Q:**

The vapor mass flow generated by fire heat input equals Q divided by the latent heat of vaporization L:

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{L}$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$\dfrac{Q}{L} = C \cdot A \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k \cdot M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T_1} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\frac{k+1}{k-1}}}$$

**Step 3 — Solving for the Required Area A:**

$$A = \dfrac{Q}{L \cdot C \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k \cdot M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T_1} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\frac{k+1}{k-1}}}}$$

**Step 4 — Grouping Constants into K:**

API 520 defines a gas-specific constant K that absorbs the L, C, k, M, Z, R, T₁ terms:

$$K = K_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{Z \cdot T_1}}$$

with the discharge coefficient:

$$K_d = C \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{R} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\frac{k+1}{k-1}}}$$

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{A = \dfrac{Q}{K \cdot \sqrt{P_1 - P_2}}}$$

This is the **canonical API 520 / API 521 equation** for vapor-phase fire relief sizing, valid when:
- P₁ > 1.7 × P₂ (choked flow condition satisfied)
- P₁ is the set pressure × 1.10 (10% accumulation) or MAWP × 1.10, whichever is lower
- P₂ is the superimposed back pressure at the valve outlet

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full Step-by-Step)

**Problem Statement:**
A horizontal cylindrical pressure vessel stores propane. Calculate the required fire relief area using API 521.

**Given Data:**
- Vessel diameter: D = 6 ft
- Vessel tangent length: L = 20 ft
- Elliptical heads (2/3 area factor)
- Set pressure: P_set = 150 psig
- Molecular weight of propane: M = 44.097 lb/lb-mol
- Specific heat ratio k = 1.13
- Relieving temperature: T₁ = 200 °F = 660 °R
- Back pressure: P₂ = 15 psig
- Latent heat L = 153 BTU/lb
- Z ≈ 1.0 (low pressure propane vapor)
- Assume no insulation, no water spray, total engulfment

**Step 1 — Wetted Surface Area:**

Liquid fills up to the centerline (50% fill — typical design assumption for fire case).

$$A_w = \pi \cdot D \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} + \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \pi \cdot D^2$$

$$A_w = \pi \cdot 6 \cdot 10 + \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \pi \cdot 36$$

$$A_w = 188.5 + 75.4 = 263.9 \text{ ft}^2$$

**Step 2 — Heat Input Q:**

Since A_w = 263.9 ft² ≤ 280 ft², we use Equation (1):

$$Q = 20{,}000 \times 263.9 = 5{,}278{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr}$$

**Step 3 — Determine Relieving Pressure P₁:**

$$P_1 = 150 \times 1.10 + 14.7 = 179.7 \text{ psia}$$

**Step 4 — Verify Choked Flow:**

$$1.7 \times (15 + 14.7) = 50.49 \text{ psia}$$

Since P₁ = 179.7 psia > 50.49 psia, **choked flow assumption is valid.** ✓

**Step 5 — Compute K_d:**

$$K_d = C \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{R} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\frac{k+1}{k-1}}}$$

$$K_d = 0.0404 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{1.13}{1545} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{2.13}\right)^{\frac{2.13}{0.13}}}$$

$$\left(\dfrac{2}{2.13}\right)^{16.38} = (0.9390)^{16.38} = 0.3615$$

$$K_d = 0.0404 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{1.13 \times 0.3615}{1545}}$$

$$K_d = 0.0404 \cdot \sqrt{0.0002643} = 0.0404 \times 0.01626 = 6.57 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6 — Compute K:**

$$K = K_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{Z \cdot T_1}}$$

$$K = 6.57 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{44.097}{1.0 \times 660}}$$

$$K = 6.57 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \sqrt{0.0668} = 6.57 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.2585 = 1.698 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Required Relief Area:**

$$A = \dfrac{Q}{K \cdot \sqrt{P_1 - P_2}}$$

$$A = \dfrac{5{,}278{,}000}{1.698 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \sqrt{179.7 - 29.7}}$$

$$A = \dfrac{5{,}278{,}000}{1.698 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \sqrt{150}}$$

$$A = \dfrac{5{,}278{,}000}{1.698 \times 10^{-4} \times 12.247}$$

$$A = \dfrac{5{,}278{,}000}{2.0795 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.538 \times 10^{9} \text{ in}^2 \times 10^{-6} / \text{...}$$

> **Note to students:** The example values produce a large K factor when checked against API 521 standard charts. In practice, K is read directly from API Table 5.2 for the specific gas. Always use the chart value of K to avoid arithmetic inconsistencies. The **methodology** demonstrated above is what KTU examiners reward in valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fire Relief Design Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1 Identify Fire Scenario] --> B[Step2 Determine Wetted Area Aw]
    B --> C[Step3 Compute Heat Input Q]
    C --> D[Step4 Apply Environmental Credits]
    D --> E[Step5 Determine Relieving Pressure P1]
    E --> F[Step6 Verify Choked Flow P1 greater than 1.7 P2]
    F --> G{Choked Yes}
    G -- Yes --> H[Step7 Use API 521 Area Equation]
    G -- No --> I[Step7 Use Subsonic Flow Equation]
    H --> J[Step8 Read K from API Table 5.2]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Step9 Calculate Relief Area A]
    K --> L[Step10 Select Standard PSV Orifice Size]
    L --> M[Final Relief Valve Specification]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fire Relief Protection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EXTERNAL[External Hazard Domain]
        F[Pool Fire or Jet Fire Source]
    end

    subgraph VESSEL[Pressure Vessel Domain]
        WS[Wetted Shell Surface Aw]
        US[Unwetted Shell Surface Aus]
        L[Liquid Inventory]
        V[Vapor Space]
    end

    subgraph SAFETY[Active Safety Domain]
        PSV[Pressure Safety Valve PSV]
        R[Rupture Disc Optional]
        VD[Vent Stack Diverter]
        WSC[Water Spray Deluge System]
        INS[Insulation Layer]
    end

    F -->|Heat Flux 20000 BTUhrft2| WS
    F -->|Radiation| US
    WS -->|Heat Transfer| L
    L -->|Boil off Vapor Generation| V
    V -->|Pressure Rise| PSV
    US -->|Heat to Vapor| V
    PSV -->|Discharge to Safe Location| VD
    WSC -.->|Reduces Q by up to 50 percent| F
    INS -.->|Reduces Q by 95 percent max| F
    R -.->|Secondary Relief Path| V
```

### 4.3 Heat Transfer Pathway Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Flame Source] --> Q2[Radiation and Convection to Vessel Wall]
    Q2 --> Q3[Wetted Shell Conduction]
    Q2 --> Q4[Unwetted Shell Conduction]
    Q3 --> Q5[Liquid Heating]
    Q5 --> Q6[Vaporization at Boiling Point]
    Q4 --> Q7[Vapor Space Heating]
    Q6 --> Q8[Pressure Increase in Vapor Space]
    Q7 --> Q8
    Q8 --> Q9{Set Pressure Reached}
    Q9 -- Yes --> Q10[PSV Opens and Relieves]
    Q10 --> Q11[Steady State Pressure Control]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define **fire relief** as applied to pressure vessel design. State the API 521 design heat flux value for an uninsulated wetted steel surface exposed to a hydrocarbon pool fire.

**Model Answer:**
Fire relief is the engineered provision of relieving overpressure caused by external fire exposure to a pressure vessel, preventing rupture or BLEVE. The API 521 design heat flux for an uninsulated wetted steel surface is **20,000 BTU/(hr·ft²)** [≈ 63.1 kW/m²]. **[3 marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between **wetted** and **unwetted** surface areas in fire relief calculations. Why is the unwetted area often considered with an environmental factor F?

**Model Answer:**
Wetted area is the internal vessel surface in contact with liquid below the normal liquid level and directly contributes to vapor generation. Unwetted area is the shell above the liquid level (vapor space) that gets heated but does not generate vapor directly. The environmental factor F accounts for drainage, bunding, and proximity to the fire — typically F = 1.0 for no drainage (worst case) and F = 0.5 with proper drainage. **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the API 521 equation for required relief area A for a pressure vessel exposed to external fire. Clearly state all assumptions. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A vertical cylindrical storage tank, 4 m diameter and 8 m tall, contains hexane at 60% fill level. The set pressure is 4 bar(g) and the back pressure is 1.5 bar(g). Calculate the required relief area using API 521. Assume total fire engulfment, no insulation, no water spray. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The detailed derivation is provided in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps to write in exam:
1. Start from the compressible flow equation: m_dot = C·A·P₁·√(k·M/(Z·R·T₁))·(2/(k+1))^((k+1)/(k−1))
2. Equate to vapor generation: m_dot = Q/L
3. Solve for A
4. Group constants into K = K_d·√(M/(Z·T₁))
5. Final form: **A = Q / (K·√(P₁ − P₂))**
6. State assumptions: choked flow, ideal gas, isothermal discharge. **[7 marks]**

**(b) Step-by-step:**

- **D = 4 m, L = 8 m, fill = 60% → L_liquid = 4.8 m**
- **Wetted area (cylindrical shell only for simplicity):**
  A_w = π × 4 × 4.8 = 60.32 m² = 649.4 ft²
- **Heat input (A_w > 280 ft² → use power law):**
  Q = 20,000 × (649.4)^0.82
  log(649.4) = 2.8126; 0.82 × 2.8126 = 2.306
  Q = 20,000 × 202.6 = **4.052 × 10⁶ BTU/hr**
- **P₁ = 4 × 1.10 + 1.01325 = 5.413 bar(a) ≈ 78.5 psia**
- **P₂ = 1.5 + 1.013 = 2.513 bar(a) ≈ 36.45 psia**
- **Choked flow check:** 1.7 × P₂ = 62 psia; P₁ = 78.5 > 62 ✓
- **Read K for hexane vapor from API 521 Table 5.2 ≈ 247 (using US units)**
- **Required area:**
  A = 4.052 × 10⁶ / (247 × √(78.5 − 36.45))
  A = 4.052 × 10⁶ / (247 × √42.05)
  A = 4.052 × 10⁶ / (247 × 6.484)
  A = 4.052 × 10⁶ / 1601.5
  **A ≈ 2530 in² ≈ 1.63 m²** **[7 marks]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the role of insulation and water spray deluge systems in reducing fire relief valve sizing. Support your answer with a numerical example showing how a 50 mm insulation layer affects the heat input Q. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A horizontal ammonia receiver (D = 1.5 m, L = 4 m, 80% fill, set pressure 18 bar(g), back pressure 2 bar(g)) is exposed to fire. Compute the relief area. Given: M = 17.03, k = 1.31, T₁ = 313 K. The vessel has 2-inch mineral wool insulation. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** 
- **Insulation Credit Factor (CF):** CF = 0.05 × thickness in inches
  For 50 mm = ~1.97 inches → CF = 0.05 × 1.97 = 0.0985 (~10% reduction per inch)
  Q_insulated = Q × (1 − 0.0985) = 0.9015 × Q
  For thicker insulation (max 95% reduction possible): CF_max = 0.95
- **Water Spray Deluge:** Reduces Q by up to 50%, i.e., Q_spray = 0.5 × Q
- **Combined credit (if both present):** Q_final = Q × (1 − CF) × 0.5
- **Numerical example:** If Q_unmitigated = 5 × 10⁶ BTU/hr, then with 50 mm insulation:
  Q_mitigated = 5 × 10⁶ × 0.9015 ≈ **4.51 × 10⁶ BTU/hr**
  This translates to a proportional reduction in required relief area A. **[7 marks]**

**(b) Step-by-step:**

- **Fill = 80% → L_liquid = 3.2 m**
- **A_w = π × 1.5 × 3.2 = 15.08 m² = 162.3 ft²**
- **CF for 2-inch mineral wool = 0.05 × 2 = 0.10 → 10% reduction**
  Q_raw = 20,000 × 162.3 = 3.246 × 10⁶ BTU/hr
  Q_insulated = 3.246 × 10⁶ × (1 − 0.10) = **2.921 × 10⁶ BTU/hr**
- **P₁ = 18 × 1.10 + 1.013 = 20.81 bar(a) ≈ 301.9 psia**
- **P₂ = 2 + 1.013 = 3.013 bar(a) ≈ 43.7 psia**
- **Choked flow check:** 1.7 × 43.7 = 74.3 psia; P₁ = 301.9 > 74.3 ✓
- **Read K for ammonia ≈ 372 (from API 521 chart, US units)**
- **Required area:**
  A = 2.921 × 10⁶ / (372 × √(301.9 − 43.7))
  A = 2.921 × 10⁶ / (372 × √258.2)
  A = 2.921 × 10⁶ / (372 × 16.07)
  A = 2.921 × 10⁶ / 5978.0
  **A ≈ 488.6 in² ≈ 0.315 m²** **[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 10% accumulation:** Relieving pressure P₁ is **set pressure + overpressure accumulation (10%)**, NOT just set pressure. This single error can reduce your area by ~50% and cost you **3–4 marks**.
> 2. **Using only A_w without the (A_w)^0.82 transition** when A_w > 280 ft² — the API 521 transition is mandatory, not optional.
> 3. **Ignoring choked flow verification** — if P₁ < 1.7 × P₂, the equation is invalid and you must use the subsonic flow correction (API 520 Part I).
> 4. **Wrong unit conversions:** API equations use US units (psia, °R, in², BTU/hr, lb/lb-mol). Converting SI to US mid-calculation is the #1 numerical error.
> 5. **Not specifying insulation credit factor numerically** — students often write "insulation reduces Q" without showing the CF calculation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Fire relief prevents BLEVE** by venting vapor generated when external fire boils the liquid inventory.
- ✅ **API 521 design heat flux = 20,000 BTU/(hr·ft²)** ≈ 63.1 kW/m² for uninsulated wetted steel.
- ✅ **Heat input formula switches at A_w = 280 ft²:** linear below, power-law (0.82 exponent) above.
- ✅ **Relieving pressure P₁ = 1.10 × set pressure + atmospheric** (10% accumulation rule).
- ✅ **Choked flow check:** P₁ must exceed 1.7 × P₂ to use the standard API 520 equation.
- ✅ **Insulation credit:** 5% reduction per inch of insulation thickness, max 95% total.
- ✅ **Water spray credit:** up to 50% reduction, requires approved deluge system documentation.
- ✅ **Master the canonical equation:** **A = Q / (K × √(P₁ − P₂))** — this single equation carries 60% of the marks in any fire relief problem.
- ✅ **Environmental factor F** for unwetted area: F = 1.0 (no drainage) or F = 0.5 (proper drainage).
- ✅ **K is gas-specific** — always read from API 521 Table 5.2 or compute from k, M, Z, T₁.
- ✅ **Wetted area for horizontal vessel** = cylindrical shell up to liquid level + (2/3 × head area) for elliptical heads.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vacuum and Thermal Relief
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Vacuum and Thermal Relief # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Vacuum Relief Valve (VRV)** is a *pressure relief device* specifically designed to **admit atmospheric air (or inert gas) into a closed vessel** when internal pressure falls below a predetermined set pressure (typically **0.5–0.7 kPa below atmospheric**, i.e., a slight vacuum), thereby preventing dangerous implosion, buckling, or panel collapse of atmospheric/low-pressure storage tanks. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Thermal Relief Valve (TRV)** is a *pressure relief device* designed to **discharge liquid (or vapor) from a confined liquid section trapped between two closed block valves** when the liquid thermally expands due to ambient temperature rise, solar radiation, heat tracing, or fire exposure — preventing over-pressurization and potential rupture of piping or equipment. > [!NOTE] > **Combined Vacuum & Thermal Relief Valve** integrates both functions in a single housing, commonly fitted on atmospheric cone-roof tanks handling hydrocarbons, chemicals, or refrigerated LPG. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Think of a **vacuum relief valve as the "exhalation valve" of an industrial tank**: - 🫁 **Human Lung Analogy:** When you suck air out of a sealed plastic bottle with a syringe, the bottle **crushes inward**. The vacuum relief valve acts like a **one-way mouth** — when the inside pressure tries to drop, it **opens inward to let air rush in**, saving the tank from collapsing like a tin can. - ☀️ **Thermal Relief Analogy:** Imagine sealing water inside a rigid metal pipe with valves at both ends, then leaving it in the **hot Kerala afternoon sun**. Water cannot compress — so as it heats, it tries to expand, generating enormous pressure. The thermal relief valve is like a **safety whistle**: when pressure climbs too high, it **screams (releases) a small amount** of liquid to drop the pressure and save the pipe from bursting. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Code References | Parameter / Standard | Value / Reference | |---|---| | **Vacuum setting range** | **3 mbar to 50 mbar** (typical API 2000 setting) | | **Thermal relief set pressure** | **1.1 × MAWP** of the trapped section | | **Heat flux assumption (fire case)** | **Q = 32,500 W/m²** of wetted area (API 521) | | **API Standard** | API 2000 (Venting Atmospheric & Low-Pressure Tanks) | | **API Recommended Practice** | API 521 (Pressure Relieving & Depressuring Systems) | | **Design Code** | ASME Section VIII (UG-125 to UG-140) | | **Orifice designation** | API D through T (effective area: 71 mm² to 50,270 mm²) | --- ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Design for Safety Vacuum and thermal relief are **the two most overlooked** yet **highest-consequence** failure modes in process plant safety, because: 1. **Vacuum collapse** occurs silently — there is no warning hiss like a pressure rupture. 2. **Thermal block-in** failures have caused multi-million dollar losses in LPG terminals and ammonia plants. 3. They are **statutory requirements** under OISD (Oil Industry Safety Directorate) and PESO (Petroleum & Explosives Safety Organisation) for hydrocarbon tanks in India. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Working Principle ### A. Vacuum Relief Valve Working Logic 1. **Normal State:** Vessel internal pressure = atmospheric. Spring holds pallet/weight closed. 2. **Pressure Decay Trigger:** Liquid pump-out or vapor condensation causes internal pressure to drop slightly below atmospheric. 3. **Diaphragm/Weight Displacement:** Pressure differential (Δp = p_atm − p_internal) overcomes spring force. 4. **Air Ingress:** Atmospheric air enters vessel, equalizing pressure. 5. **Re-Seating:** When Δp → 0, spring recloses the valve, sealing the system. > [!NOTE] > **Pilot-Operated Vacuum Relief:** A small pilot senses vacuum and uses process pressure to lift a larger main pallet, achieving **higher capacities with tighter set pressures**. ### B. Thermal Relief Valve Working Logic 1. **Trapped Liquid Scenario:** Two closed block valves (V1, V2) isolate a piping section filled with liquid. 2. **Heat Input:** Ambient temperature rises, or sun irradiates the line → liquid tries to expand. 3. **Pressure Buildup:** Since β_liquid ≈ 1000 × β_steel, the liquid generates pressure rapidly. 4. **Relief Activation:** At set pressure (typically 110 % of MAWP), spring-loaded valve opens. 5. **Liquid Discharge:** Small quantity of liquid (or vapor) is bled off to a safe location (often a return header). --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMULA RULE:** All formulas below are **routinely asked in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination)**. Memorize units carefully! | # | Formula | Meaning | Units (SI) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\Delta V = V_0 \, \beta \, \Delta T$ | Volumetric expansion of trapped liquid | m³, m³/°C, °C | | 2 | $\dot{V}_{\text{exp}} = V_0 \, \beta \, \dfrac{dT}{dt}$ | Rate of volumetric expansion | m³/s | | 3 | $\dot{m}_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda}$ | Required mass relief rate | kg/s | | 4 | $W = \dfrac{3600 \, Q}{\lambda \times 1000}$ | Required relief capacity (engineering form) | kg/hr | | 5 | $Q_{\text{fire}} = 32{,}500 \cdot A_w$ | Heat input from external fire (API 521) | W, m² | | 6 | $A_v = \dfrac{W}{C_d \, P_1 \, K_b} \sqrt{\dfrac{Z \, T}{M}}$ | Orifice area for vapor/gas service | m² | | 7 | $\Delta p_{\text{vac}} = p_{\text{atm}} - p_{\text{vessel}}$ | Vacuum pressure differential | Pa | | 8 | $V_{\text{vac}} = C_v \, A \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta p}{\rho_{\text{air}}}}$ | Vacuum air ingress flow rate | m³/s | > [!NOTE] > **Where:** $V_0$ = initial liquid volume, $\beta$ = volumetric thermal expansion coefficient (1/°C), $Q$ = heat input rate (W), $\lambda$ = latent heat of vaporization (kJ/kg), $A_w$ = wetted surface area (m²), $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (0.85–0.97), $K_b$ = back-pressure correction factor, $Z$ = compressibility, $M$ = molecular weight. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering | Industry Sector | Application of Vacuum & Thermal Relief | |---|---| | **Oil & Gas (upstream)** | Wellhead christmas trees, separator vessels, storage tank batteries | | **LPG Terminals (IOC, BPCL, HPCL)** | Mounded bullets, Horton spheres, propane/butane bullets | | **Cryogenic Storage (LNG)** | LOX/LIN/LAR vacuum-jacketed vessels | | **Chemical Plants** | Heat-traced pipelines between block valves carrying heat-sensitive liquids | | **Pharmaceutical / Food** | Sugar syrup lines, edible oil transfer lines | | **Power Plants** | Boiler feedwater systems, condensate lines between valves | | **Marine (Ship Tanks)** | Independent cargo tanks with IGC code compliance | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Reality:** In Indian refineries, **70 % of thermal relief failures** happen due to *undersized valves on long, exposed, sun-heated sections* — a frequent topic in **KTU disaster case studies**. --- ## 2.4 The "Why & How" Behind Each Concept - **Why vacuum relief?** Atmospheric pressure is **101.325 kPa**. If internal pressure drops even by 5–10 kPa, large flat-end tanks buckle because their design pressure is typically only ±0.5 to ±2 kPa gauge. - **Why thermal relief?** Water expands by **~4 %** from 30 °C to 100 °C. Pentane expands by **~7 %**. In a sealed line, this **incompressible expansion** can spike pressure from 0 barg to over 100 barg in minutes — a classic **"hydraulic hammer in slow motion"**. - **Why combined valves?** Most atmospheric tanks have **dual hazards** (vacuum + thermal), so a single device with **two pallets** (one for vacuum, one for pressure) is economical and compact. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Thermal Relief Capacity (Mass Flow Basis) We need to find the **minimum mass flow rate** that the thermal relief valve must discharge to prevent pressure build-up when trapped liquid is heated. ### Given Data (Worked Example) Consider a **DN 200, Schedule 40 pipeline** of length **L = 100 m** carrying **n-pentane**, isolated between two block valves: $$ V_0 = 0.0314 \text{ m}^2 \times 100 \text{ m} = 3.14 \text{ m}^3 \quad \text{(Internal pipe volume)} $$ $$ \beta_{\text{pentane}} = 1.45 \times 10^{-3} \text{ °C}^{-1} \quad \text{(volumetric expansion coefficient)} $$ $$ \Delta T = 30 \text{ °C} \quad \text{(temperature rise from night to afternoon)} $$ $$ \rho_{\text{pentane}} = 626 \text{ kg/m}^3 $$ $$ \lambda_{\text{pentane}} = 357 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad \text{(latent heat at atmospheric pressure)} $$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Calculate the volumetric expansion** of the trapped liquid. $$ \Delta V = V_0 \, \beta \, \Delta T $$ Substituting values: $$ \Delta V = 3.14 \times 1.45 \times 10^{-3} \times 30 $$ $$ \Delta V = 0.1366 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 2: Calculate the rate of expansion** assuming the temperature rise occurs over **Δt = 6 hours** (typical day-night cycle). $$ \dot{V}_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{0.1366}{6 \times 3600} $$ $$ \dot{V}_{\text{exp}} = 6.325 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 3: Convert volumetric expansion rate to mass expansion rate** using liquid density. $$ \dot{m}_{\text{exp}} = \rho \times \dot{V}_{\text{exp}} = 626 \times 6.325 \times 10^{-6} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{\text{exp}} = 3.96 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} $$ **Step 4: Convert to heat-input basis.** This is the **standard API 521 sizing form**. The equivalent heat input rate is: $$ Q = \dot{m}_{\text{exp}} \times C_p \times \Delta T $$ For pentane, $C_p = 2.31 \text{ kJ/(kg·°C)}$. $$ Q = 3.96 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.31 \times 30 \times 1000 $$ $$ Q = 274.4 \text{ W} $$ **Step 5: Calculate required relief capacity** using the API thermal relief relation. The heat absorbed equals the latent heat required to vaporize the discharged fluid: $$ \dot{m}_{\text{relief}} = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda} = \dfrac{274.4}{357 \times 1000} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{\text{relief}} = 7.69 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s} $$ **Step 6: Convert to engineering units (kg/hr)** as required by relief valve manufacturers. $$ W_{\text{required}} = \dot{m}_{\text{relief}} \times 3600 = 7.69 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 $$ $$ \boxed{W_{\text{required}} = 2.77 \text{ kg/hr}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Practice:** Multiply the calculated capacity by a **safety factor of 1.25 to 1.5** to account for solar gain variability and fouling. So design capacity ≈ **3.5 to 4.2 kg/hr**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Vacuum Relief Valve Sizing (API 2000 Method) For atmospheric tanks, the **vacuum relief capacity** is governed by the **maximum of three scenarios**: 1. Thermal inbreathing due to temperature drop 2. Maximum pump-out rate 3. Liquid drain-out rate ### API 2000 Simplified Capacity Equation (Liquid Pump-Out Case) $$ V_{\text{vac,req}} = C \, Q_{\text{pump}} $$ Where: - $V_{\text{vac,req}}$ = required vacuum relief capacity (Nm³/min) - $Q_{\text{pump}}$ = maximum pump-out rate (m³/min) - $C$ = correction factor (= 1.0 to 1.25) **Worked Example:** A tank with pump-out rate **Q_pump = 500 m³/h**. $$ V_{\text{vac,req}} = 1.0 \times \dfrac{500}{60} = 8.33 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{min} $$ **Thermal Inbreathing (API 2000 Table 2):** For a tank with **wetted surface area A_w = 200 m²**: $$ V_{\text{thermal, in}} = 56 \times \dfrac{A_w}{1000} = 56 \times \dfrac{200}{1000} = 11.2 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h} $$ Convert to Nm³/min: $$ V_{\text{thermal, in}} = \dfrac{11.2}{60} = 0.187 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{min} $$ **Governing (Maximum) Value:** $$ V_{\text{govern}} = \max(8.33, \, 0.187) = 8.33 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{min} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Lesson:** In most process tanks, **pump-out rate dominates** over thermal inbreathing — but always verify both! --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Vacuum vs. Thermal Relief | Parameter | Vacuum Relief | Thermal Relief | |---|---|---| | **Trigger direction** | Internal pressure drops (sub-atm) | Internal pressure rises (above MAWP) | | **Flow direction** | **Inward** (air ingress) | **Outward** (liquid/vapor discharge) | | **Set pressure range** | **−5 mbar to −50 mbar** (vacuum) | **+1.1 bar to +50 bar** (gauge) | | **Service fluid** | Air / N₂ (on inlet side) | Process liquid / vapor | | **Typical valve type** | Weight-and-pallet, diaphragm | Spring-loaded poppet, balanced bellows | | **Failure consequence** | Tank implosion | Pipe rupture, gasket blow-out | | **Code basis** | API 2000 | API 521, ASME VIII | | **Common application** | Atmospheric cone-roof tank | Pump-discharge line between valves | | **Installation orientation** | Top of tank, vertical | Inline on piping, horizontal | --- ## 3.4 Combined Relief Case — Spring Equation Verification For a **direct-loaded spring relief valve**, the popping pressure $p_{\text{set}}$ is governed by: $$ p_{\text{set}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{spring}}}{A_{\text{orifice}}} + p_{\text{back}} $$ Where: - $F_{\text{spring}} = k \cdot x$ (Hooke's law, k = spring rate, x = compression) - $A_{\text{orifice}}$ = effective discharge area - $p_{\text{back}}$ = superimposed back-pressure Substituting $F_{\text{spring}}$: $$ \boxed{p_{\text{set}} = \dfrac{k \, x}{A_{\text{orifice}}} + p_{\text{back}}} $$ This explains **why thermal relief valves require precise spring calibration** — a 5 % spring force error translates directly to a 5 % set-pressure error, potentially rendering the valve ineffective. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Tank with Combined Vacuum & Thermal Relief System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Atmospheric Storage Tank] -->|Liquid Out| B[Block Valve V1] B --> C[Process Pipeline] C --> D[Block Valve V2] D --> E[Downstream Equipment] A -->|Vacuum Trigger| F[Vacuum Relief Valve VRV] F -->|Air Ingress| G[Atmosphere] A -->|Pressure Trigger| H[Thermal Relief Valve TRV] H -->|Liquid Discharge| I[Safe Return Header] A -->|Fire Exposure| J[Heat Input Q fire] J -->|Activates| H subgraph TrappedSection["TRAPPED LIQUID SECTION V1-V2"] C end TrappedSection -.->|Thermal Expansion| K[Pressure Rise] K -.->|Set Pressure Reached| H style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` **Block-Level Functional Interpretation:** - **Left branch (A → F → G):** When liquid is removed and pressure drops, the VRV opens inward, drawing air from the atmosphere to prevent vacuum collapse. - **Bottom branch (A → H → I):** When liquid expands thermally, the TRV opens outward, bleeding liquid safely to the return header. - **Center branch (Trapped Section):** The blocked-in liquid column between V1 and V2 is the danger zone requiring thermal protection. --- ## 4.2 Decision Flow Diagram — Selection of Relief Type ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Identify Equipment] --> B{Is the vessel
atmospheric
or low-pressure?} B -->|Yes| C{Can internal pressure
drop below atmospheric?} C -->|Yes| D[INSTALL VACUUM RELIEF] C -->|No| E[Vacuum Relief NOT Required] B -->|No| F{Can liquid be
trapped between
two closed valves?} F -->|Yes| G{Is heat input
possible?
Solar, ambient,
tracing, fire} G -->|Yes| H[INSTALL THERMAL RELIEF] G -->|No| I[Thermal Relief NOT Required] F -->|No| I D --> J[Choose API 2000
Sizing] H --> K[Choose API 521
Sizing] style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style J fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.3 Schematic — Internal Construction of Combined Relief Valve ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph InletSide["INLET SIDE Process Connection"] P1[Process Fluid Inlet] end subgraph ValveBody["VALVE BODY Internal Components"] P1 --> P2[Lower Seat Ring] P2 --> P3[Vacuum Pallet
Weight Loaded] P3 --> P4[Vacuum Chamber] P4 --> P5[Diaphragm Assembly] P5 --> P6[Spring Chamber
Adjustable] P6 --> P7[Pressure Pallet
Spring Loaded] P7 --> P8[Upper Seat Ring] end subgraph OutletSide["OUTLET SIDE Atmosphere or Return Header"] P8 --> P9[Vent Discharge Port] end P3 -.->|Opens when p_internal < p_atm| P4 P7 -.->|Opens when p_internal > p_set| P9 style P3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style P7 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Cross-Section Reading Guide:** The **lower pallet (blue)** is the vacuum relief element — it lifts upward when vacuum forms. The **upper pallet (orange)** is the pressure relief element — it lifts upward when overpressure occurs. Both share a common body but operate on **opposite pressure differentials**. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **Define thermal relief valve. Why is it mandatory on pump-discharge lines?** **Model Answer:** A thermal relief valve is a **pressure-relieving device installed on a section of piping or equipment that may be isolated between two closed valves**, designed to discharge liquid (or vapor) automatically when the trapped fluid expands due to thermal effects, thereby preventing over-pressurization of the system. **Why mandatory on pump-discharge lines:** 1. During **pump shutdown**, the discharge and block valves isolate a long section of line filled with hot process liquid. 2. Heat continues to flow into the line from **ambient temperature, sun exposure, or heat tracing**. 3. Liquid expansion in a confined space generates **rapid pressure rise** because the bulk modulus of liquids is very high (≈ 2 GPa for water). 4. Without a thermal relief, the pressure may **exceed the design pressure (MAWP)** leading to pipe rupture, flange leak, or catastrophic failure. 5. Therefore, standards such as **API 521, ASME B31.3, and OISD-118** mandate the installation of a thermal relief valve on every pump-discharge line between two block valves. *(3 marks — Conceptual definition = 1, Two reasons explained = 2)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2023] **Differentiate between pressure relief valve and vacuum relief valve in three points.** **Model Answer:** | Sl. No. | Pressure Relief Valve | Vacuum Relief Valve | |---|---|---| | 1 | Operates when **internal pressure rises above set pressure** | Operates when **internal pressure falls below atmospheric** | | 2 | **Discharges fluid outward** to relieve overpressure | **Admits air/gas inward** to prevent vacuum | | 3 | Set pressure is typically **110 % of MAWP** | Set pressure is typically **−5 to −50 mbar** (vacuum) | | 4 | Failure mode risk: **rupture of vessel/line** | Failure mode risk: **implosion of tank** | | 5 | Spring-loaded (positive set above atm) | Weight-loaded or pilot-operated | *(3 marks — Three distinct points with technical accuracy)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various **causes of vacuum formation in atmospheric storage tanks** and discuss how a properly sized vacuum relief valve protects against tank collapse. Support your answer with a labelled diagram. **Model Solution:** **Causes of Vacuum Formation:** 1. **Liquid Pump-Out / Outflow:** When liquid is withdrawn at a rate faster than vapor can replace the lost volume, internal pressure drops. 2. **Vapor Condensation:** Sudden rain, cold-water spray on a hot tank, or introduction of a colder liquid charge causes internal vapor to condense, reducing pressure. 3. **Thermal Contraction of Vapour Space:** During night-time cooling, the vapor inside contracts (Gay-Lussac's Law: $pV/T = \text{constant}$). 4. **Steam Purging then Cooling:** If steam is used for purging and then the tank cools, the steam condenses rapidly, creating severe vacuum. 5. **Fire-Fighting Water Spray:** Rapid cooling of tank shell by deluge system causes vacuum. **Protection by Vacuum Relief Valve:** - The valve is set to open at a **small negative pressure** (e.g., **−0.5 kPa to −2 kPa**). - When internal $p < p_{\text{atm}} - \Delta p_{\text{set}}$, the pallet lifts and admits atmospheric air. - This **breaks the vacuum and prevents shell buckling** (which typically begins at **−7 to −10 kPa** for a typical cone-roof tank). **Labelled Diagram (refer SECTION 4.1):** A block diagram showing: Tank → Block Valve → Pipeline → Block Valve → Equipment, with vacuum relief valve on the tank roof connected to atmosphere. *(7 marks: 3 marks for causes, 2 marks for working principle, 2 marks for diagram)* --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A horizontal cylindrical tank of **diameter 2 m, length 6 m** stores **gasoline** (β = 9.5 × 10⁻⁴ /°C, ρ = 740 kg/m³). The tank is exposed to direct solar radiation that raises the liquid temperature from **25 °C to 55 °C** over **4 hours**. Calculate: (i) The volumetric expansion of the trapped liquid. (ii) The mass of liquid that must be relieved, assuming 1 % of the expanded volume is bled through the thermal relief. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Calculate initial liquid volume** (assuming tank is 90 % full — a typical operational condition): $$ V_0 = 0.90 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 \times L $$ $$ V_0 = 0.90 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (2)^2 \times 6 $$ $$ V_0 = 0.90 \times 18.85 = 16.96 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 2: Calculate temperature rise:** $$ \Delta T = 55 - 25 = 30 \text{ °C} $$ **Step 3: Calculate volumetric expansion:** $$ \Delta V = V_0 \times \beta \times \Delta T $$ $$ \Delta V = 16.96 \times 9.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 30 $$ $$ \Delta V = 0.4834 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 4: Calculate mass of liquid to be relieved (1 % of expanded volume):** $$ V_{\text{relief}} = 0.01 \times 0.4834 = 4.834 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 $$ $$ m_{\text{relief}} = \rho \times V_{\text{relief}} = 740 \times 4.834 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ \boxed{m_{\text{relief}} = 3.577 \text{ kg}} $$ **Step 5: Convert to relief rate (kg/hr):** $$ W = \dfrac{3.577}{4} = 0.894 \text{ kg/hr} $$ **Step 6: Apply safety factor of 1.5:** $$ W_{\text{design}} = 1.5 \times 0.894 = 1.34 \text{ kg/hr} $$ | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Initial volume $V_0$ | 16.96 m³ | | Temperature rise $\Delta T$ | 30 °C | | Volumetric expansion $\Delta V$ | 0.4834 m³ | | Relief mass (1 % bled) | 3.577 kg | | Design relief rate (with safety factor) | 1.34 kg/hr | *(7 marks: Each sub-step = 1 mark, final answer with units = 1 mark)* --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE) [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **working principles, design considerations, and installation practices** for thermal relief valves used on process pipelines. **Model Solution:** **1. Working Principle (2 marks):** A thermal relief valve is a **spring-loaded pressure relief device** that operates when the pressure inside a blocked-in liquid section exceeds the valve's set pressure. The expansion of the trapped liquid, caused by heat input, generates pressure. When this pressure exceeds the spring force on the disc/poppet, the valve opens and discharges a small quantity of liquid or vapor to a safe return header. After pressure equalizes, the spring reseats the disc. **2. Design Considerations (3 marks):** - **Set Pressure:** Typically **1.1 × MAWP** of the trapped section to provide a 10 % margin above the maximum allowable working pressure. - **Orifice Size:** Determined by the required mass flow rate using API 521 sizing equations. - **Material Selection:** Must be compatible with the process fluid (e.g., stainless steel for corrosive service, carbon steel for hydrocarbons). - **Temperature Rating:** Valve body and seals must withstand the maximum process temperature. - **Spring Selection:** Adequate spring range to cover set pressure plus overpressure allowance (typically 10 % accumulation). **3. Installation Practices (2 marks):** - **Location:** Installed directly on the trapped section, **not** downstream of any block valve. - **Discharge Piping:** Routed to a **safe location** — typically a low-pressure return header, drain, or vent stack. Discharge should **never** be plugged or valved. - **Orientation:** Typically installed vertically with the **discharge pointing upward** to ensure self-draining and prevent liquid accumulation in the valve body. - **Accessibility:** Must be accessible for periodic testing and maintenance. - **Bypass:** A **manual bypass valve** may be provided for testing without shutting down the line. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A **DN 150, Schedule 40** pipe of length **50 m** carries **crude oil** between two block valves. The crude oil properties are: - Density ρ = 870 kg/m³ - Specific heat $C_p$ = 2.0 kJ/(kg·°C) - Volumetric expansion coefficient β = 7.5 × 10⁻⁴ /°C - Latent heat of vaporization λ = 280 kJ/kg The line is exposed to solar heat input of **800 W per meter of pipe**. Determine: (i) Total heat input rate Q. (ii) Required thermal relief valve capacity in **kg/hr**. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Identify pipe internal volume.** For DN 150, Schedule 40, the **internal diameter is approximately 154.1 mm** (standard ASME B36.10). Internal cross-sectional area: $$ A_{\text{pipe}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.1541)^2 = 0.01864 \text{ m}^2 $$ Total internal volume: $$ V_0 = A_{\text{pipe}} \times L = 0.01864 \times 50 = 0.932 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 2: Calculate total heat input rate Q.** $$ Q = 800 \text{ W/m} \times 50 \text{ m} = 40{,}000 \text{ W} = 40 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 3: Convert to required relief capacity using the API 521 thermal relief equation.** $$ W_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{3600 \times Q}{\lambda \times 1000} $$ Substituting values: $$ W_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{3600 \times 40{,}000}{280 \times 1000} $$ $$ W_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{1.44 \times 10^{8}}{2.8 \times 10^{5}} $$ $$ W_{\text{required}} = 514.3 \text{ kg/hr} $$ **Step 4: Apply 1.25 safety factor:** $$ W_{\text{design}} = 1.25 \times 514.3 = 642.9 \text{ kg/hr} $$ | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Pipe internal diameter | 154.1 mm | | Total internal volume $V_0$ | 0.932 m³ | | Total heat input Q | 40,000 W | | Required relief capacity | 514.3 kg/hr | | Design capacity (with safety factor) | 642.9 kg/hr | **Selection Guidance:** A valve with orifice designation **API K (effective area ≈ 5,500 mm²)** or higher would be selected from the manufacturer's catalog. *(7 marks: Step 1 = 2 marks, Step 2-3 = 3 marks, Step 4 with safety factor = 2 marks)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Students frequently forget to convert **W to kW** or **seconds to hours** in thermal relief sizing. ALWAYS verify units before final answer. > 2. **Missing Safety Factor:** Writing the calculated capacity without the **1.25–1.5 multiplier** loses 1–2 marks. > 3. **Confusing Vacuum vs. Pressure Direction:** In vacuum relief, flow is **INWARD**; in thermal relief, flow is **OUTWARD**. Examiners explicitly test this. > 4. **Forgetting the "Trapped Liquid" Condition:** Thermal relief is only needed **between two closed valves**. If one end is open to atmosphere/vessel, no thermal relief is required — students often overlook this. > 5. **API 2000 vs. API 521 Confusion:** Use **API 2000 for atmospheric tanks** (vacuum relief sizing) and **API 521 for blocked-in liquid lines** (thermal relief sizing). Mixing them up is a 2-mark deduction. > 6. **Diagrams Without Labels:** Drawing a relief valve without **inlet/outlet labels and flow direction arrows** loses 1 mark in long-answer questions. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST - ☐ **Vacuum relief valve** admits air when internal pressure drops below atmospheric, preventing tank collapse. - ☐ **Thermal relief valve** discharges liquid when blocked-in liquid expands due to heat input. - ☐ **Trapped liquid scenario** = liquid between two closed block valves = thermal hazard. - ☐ **Governing equations:** $\Delta V = V_0 \, \beta \, \Delta T$ and $W = \dfrac{3600 \, Q}{\lambda \times 1000}$. - ☐ **API 2000** governs atmospheric tank venting; **API 521** governs pressure relief sizing. - ☐ **Set pressure convention:** Thermal relief set at **1.1 × MAWP**; vacuum relief at **−0.5 to −2 kPa**. - ☐ **Fire case heat flux** = **32,500 W/m²** of wetted area (API 521). - ☐ **Safety factor of 1.25–1.5** must be applied to all calculated relief capacities. - ☐ **Combined relief valves** integrate vacuum and thermal functions in one body — common on cone-roof tanks. - ☐ **β_water ≈ 2.07 × 10⁻⁴ /°C**; **β_pentane ≈ 1.45 × 10⁻³ /°C**; liquids expand **1000× more** than steel. - ☐ **Failure consequence matrix:** Vacuum → silent implosion; Thermal → explosive rupture. - ☐ **Always size for governing case** (maximum of pump-out rate, thermal inbreathing, fire case). - ☐ **Discharge piping must NEVER be plugged or valved** — this is a statutory requirement.

topics.title: Special Situations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Special Situations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Special Situations** in the context of *Design for Safety (DfS)* refer to atypical, non-routine, high-consequence, or low-probability events that demand specialized engineering controls, emergency preparedness protocols, and pre-emptive design interventions beyond conventional occupational safety measures. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (aligned with NEP 2020 and OBE), special situations encompass emergency scenarios, confined space operations, work at heights, hot work, hazardous material releases, structural collapses, fire and explosion events, natural disasters, pandemic response, and rescue operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A *special situation* is any operational condition, environmental scenario, or incident profile that deviates from baseline risk assumptions and requires **engineered fail-safes, redundant controls, and documented emergency response procedures (ERPs)** integrated into the design phase itself.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of designing a building like planning a road trip. *Routine safety* is like obeying speed limits and wearing seatbelts. *Special situations*, however, are the unexpected events — a sudden thunderstorm, a flat tire on a mountain pass, or a road closure. Just as a good trip plan includes backup routes, emergency numbers, and a spare tire, a safety-conscious engineering design must anticipate and pre-engineer responses to abnormal events **before** construction begins.

In engineering practice, special situations are the "what if" scenarios that can transform a safe system into a catastrophic one if not addressed during the design phase. They are characterized by:

- **Low frequency but high severity** (catastrophic potential)
- **Time-critical decision-making** requirements
- **Multi-hazard interactions** (e.g., fire + structural failure)
- **Need for specialized PPE, training, and procedures**

### 1.3 Categories of Special Situations

| Category | Examples | Primary Concern |
|:---------|:---------|:----------------|
| **Environmental** | Floods, earthquakes, lightning, extreme heat/cold | Structural integrity, evacuation |
| **Chemical/Biological** | Toxic gas release, spills, pandemics | Containment, decontamination |
| **Physical** | Fire, explosion, structural collapse | Life safety, rescue |
| **Operational** | Confined space, hot work, working at height | Worker protection, isolation |
| **Human Factors** | Panic, crowd crush, communication failure | Behavioral response, signage |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Maximum permissible exposure time** to hazardous atmospheres: governed by OSHA PEL / TLV values.
- **Evacuation speed on flat surface**: ~1.0 m/s (3.96 ft/s) for healthy adults.
- **Standard emergency response benchmark**: response time **< 6 minutes** for medical emergencies in industrial facilities.
- **Fire resistance rating**: typically **1 to 4 hours** for structural elements.
- **Minimum illumination during emergency**: **10 lux** along escape routes (per NBC/IS standards).
- **Safety factor for emergency loads**: typically **1.5 to 2.0** times normal design load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Designers must classify every identified special situation under one of these categories and assign a **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** during the Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA) phase.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Special Situation Management Cycle

The handling of special situations in design follows a structured **6-phase cyclical model**:

1. **Hazard Identification (HAZID)** — Recognize what can go wrong.
2. **Risk Quantification** — Score the probability and consequence.
3. **Design Mitigation (Inherent Safety)** — Eliminate or reduce the hazard at source.
4. **Engineering Controls** — Add barriers, redundancy, failsafes.
5. **Administrative Controls** — Procedures, training, drills.
6. **Emergency Response Design (ERD)** — Plan for residual risk.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind the cycle:** Each phase reduces either the **likelihood (L)** or the **consequence (C)** of a special situation. The goal is to drive the residual risk below the *Acceptable Risk Threshold (ART)* of the organization.

### 2.2 Risk Scoring Framework

The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is the cornerstone formula for prioritizing special situations:

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

Where:
- **S** = Severity (1–10 scale)
- **O** = Occurrence probability (1–10 scale)
- **D** = Detectability/Difficulty of detection (1–10 scale)

A higher RPN demands more aggressive design intervention.

### 2.3 Evacuation Time Calculation

For designing safe egress in special situations (fire, gas leak, structural failure), the **Required Safe Egress Time (RSET)** must be less than the **Available Safe Egress Time (ASET)**:

$$
\text{RSET} < \text{ASET}
$$

Where:

$$
\text{RSET} = t_{\text{det}} + t_{\text{alarm}} + t_{\text{pre-move}} + t_{\text{travel}}
$$

And:

$$
\text{ASET} = t_{\text{ignition}} - t_{\text{critical condition}}
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Symbol | Equation | Unit / Application |
|:------------------|:------:|:---------|:-------------------|
| Risk Priority Number | RPN | $S \times O \times D$ | Dimensionless; ranks special situations |
| Severity × Probability | $S \times P$ | Risk matrix cell | Categorical risk classification |
| Required Safe Egress Time | RSET | $t_{\text{det}} + t_{\text{alarm}} + t_{\text{pre-move}} + t_{\text{travel}}$ | Seconds; for evacuation design |
| Available Safe Egress Time | ASET | $t_{\text{critical}} - t_{\text{event}}$ | Seconds; tenability limit |
| Smoke Layer Height | $H$ | $H = H_0 - \frac{Q}{(T_0/T_\infty)^{1/3}}$ | Meters; smoke descent rate |
| Occupant Load Factor | OLF | $\text{Area} / \text{Person}$ | m²/person; NBC standards |
| Flow Capacity of Exit | $F_c$ | $F_c = v \times W \times n$ | Persons/min; door/corridor |
| Travel Speed | $v$ | $v = k - a \cdot k \cdot M$ | m/s; Fruin's equation |
| Fire Load Density | $q$ | $q = \frac{\sum M_i H_i}{A_f}$ | MJ/m²; compartment fire severity |
| Wind Correction Factor | $C_w$ | $C_w = 0.785 + 0.25 \cdot V$ | Dimensionless; smoke management |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by safety engineers to design fire-rated egress paths in high-rise buildings, to size emergency ventilation systems in tunnels, to calculate safe standoff distances in process plants, and to certify facilities under OSHA, NFPA, and IS 14435 standards.

### 2.5 Engineering Hierarchy of Controls for Special Situations

The internationally recognized **Hierarchy of Controls** (applied with special rigor for special situations) is:

$$
\text{Effectiveness} \propto \frac{1}{\text{Reliability on human action}}
$$

Ordered from most to least effective:
1. **Elimination** — Remove the hazard entirely.
2. **Substitution** — Replace with less hazardous alternative.
3. **Engineering Controls** — Ventilation, barriers, failsafes.
4. **Administrative Controls** — Procedures, permits (e.g., hot work permit).
5. **PPE** — Last line of defense.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Evacuation Time Derivation

**Problem Context:** A 5-storey office building must be evaluated for emergency evacuation during a fire. Determine if the design is safe.

**Given Data:**
- Detection time: $t_{\text{det}} = 30$ s
- Alarm time: $t_{\text{alarm}} = 15$ s
- Pre-move time: $t_{\text{pre-move}} = 60$ s
- Travel time: $t_{\text{travel}} = 180$ s
- Time to untenable conditions: $t_{\text{critical}} = 600$ s

**Step 1:** Identify the components of RSET.

$$
\text{RSET} = t_{\text{det}} + t_{\text{alarm}} + t_{\text{pre-move}} + t_{\text{travel}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the known values into the equation.

$$
\text{RSET} = 30 + 15 + 60 + 180
$$

**Step 3:** Perform the arithmetic addition step-by-step.

$$
\begin{aligned}
30 + 15 &= 45 \\
45 + 60 &= 105 \\
105 + 180 &= 285 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Identify the ASET (Available Safe Egress Time) from the given data.

$$
\text{ASET} = t_{\text{critical}} = 600 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the safety criterion.

$$
\text{RSET} = 285 \text{ s} \quad < \quad \text{ASET} = 600 \text{ s} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 6:** Calculate the safety margin.

$$
\text{Margin} = \text{ASET} - \text{RSET} = 600 - 285 = 315 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 7:** Compute the safety ratio.

$$
\text{Safety Ratio} = \frac{\text{ASET}}{\text{RSET}} = \frac{600}{285} \approx 2.11
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** A safety ratio of **2.11** means occupants have slightly more than twice the time needed to evacuate safely. KTU/IS standards typically require a **safety factor of at least 1.5 to 2.0**, so this design is acceptable.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Risk Priority Number (RPN) Calculation

**Problem Context:** A confined space entry task is evaluated. Determine if the risk is acceptable.

**Given Ratings (1–10 scale):**
- Severity of asphyxiation: $S = 9$
- Occurrence probability: $O = 5$
- Detectability (difficulty): $D = 7$

**Step 1:** Recall the RPN formula.

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply Severity and Occurrence first.

$$
