S \times O = 9 \times 5 = 45
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply the result by Detectability.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN} &= 45 \times 7 \\
&= 315
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpret the RPN value using the standard risk matrix.

| RPN Range | Risk Level | Action Required |
|:---------:|:-----------|:----------------|
| 1 – 50 | Low | Accept; monitor |
| 51 – 150 | Moderate | Reduce via controls |
| 151 – 300 | High | Mandatory mitigation |
| 301 – 1000 | **Critical** | Stop work; redesign |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** RPN = 315 falls in the **Critical** band. The task must be redesigned or subjected to additional engineering controls (e.g., forced ventilation, continuous gas monitoring, retrieval system) before proceeding.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Special Situations vs. Design Interventions

| Special Situation | Design Phase Mitigation | Operational Control | Emergency Response |
|:------------------|:------------------------|:--------------------|:-------------------|
| **Fire** | Fire-resistant materials, compartmentation, sprinklers | Fire drills, extinguisher training | Evacuation, fire brigade |
| **Confined Space** | Access openings ≥ 600 mm, mechanical ventilation | Permit-to-work system | Retrieval tripod, rescue team |
| **Working at Height** | Guardrails, anchor points, safety nets | Harness inspection log | Rescue plan, first aid |
| **Chemical Release** | Secondary containment, scrubbers, sensors | MSDS, Spill kits | Evacuation, decontamination |
| **Structural Failure** | Redundant load paths, $SF \geq 1.5$ | Periodic inspection | Shutdown, public alert |
| **Earthquake** | Seismic-resistant design (IS 1893) | Emergency kits | Assembly points |
| **Pandemic** | Ventilation (≥ 12 ACH), touchless systems | Health screening | Quarantine, telemedicine |
| **Power Failure** | Emergency lighting, UPS, generator | Backup drills | Manual override |

### 3.4 Derivation of the Safety Margin Ratio

The **Margin of Safety (MoS)** for any engineered system under special situations is defined as:

$$
\text{MoS} = \frac{\text{Capacity}}{\text{Demand}} - 1
$$

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MoS} &= \frac{C}{D} - 1 \\
&= \frac{C - D}{D} \\
&= \frac{\Delta R}{D}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $C$ = Capacity (e.g., structural strength, ASET)
- $D$ = Demand (e.g., applied load, RSET)
- $\Delta R$ = Reserve capacity (safety reserve)

> [!NOTE]
> A positive MoS indicates the design can withstand the special situation; a negative MoS indicates failure is expected. KTU design philosophy requires MoS $> 0.5$ (i.e., 50% safety margin) for life-safety scenarios.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Special Situation Management Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Input Baseline] --> B[Hazard Identification HAZID]
    B --> C{Risk Assessment HIRA}
    C -->|High Risk| D[Engineering Controls]
    C -->|Medium Risk| E[Administrative Controls]
    C -->|Low Risk| F[Accept and Monitor]
    D --> G[Redundancy and Failsafe Check]
    E --> H[Permit to Work System]
    F --> I[Periodic Review]
    G --> J[Emergency Response Design ERP]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{Training and Drills}
    K -->|Pass| L[Operational Phase]
    K -->|Fail| M[Re Design Loop]
    M --> B
    L --> N[Incident Learning Loop]
    N --> B
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Emergency Response Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Event Occurs] --> S2[Detection Sensors or Human]
    S2 --> S3[Alarm Activation]
    S3 --> S4[Communication to Occupants]
    S4 --> S5[Decision Branch: Evacuate or Shelter]
    S5 -->|Evacuate| S6[Activation of Egress Routes]
    S5 -->|Shelter| S7[Seal and Isolate]
    S6 --> S8[Assembly Point]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Headcount and Accountability]
    S9 --> S10[Rescue Operations if Needed]
    S10 --> S11[Incident Command Post]
    S11 --> S12[Post Incident Review]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Controls for Special Situations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H1[Elimination Most Effective] --> H2[Substitution]
    H2 --> H3[Engineering Controls]
    H3 --> H4[Administrative Controls]
    H4 --> H5[PPE Least Effective]
    H5 --> H6[Residual Risk Accepted]
    H1 -.-> H6
    H2 -.-> H6
    H3 -.-> H6
    H4 -.-> H6
    H6 --> H7[Continuous Monitoring]
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix: Permit-to-Work for Special Situations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Task Identified] --> P2{Is it a Special Situation?}
    P2 -->|No| P3[Standard Operating Procedure]
    P2 -->|Yes| P4[Identify Permit Type]
    P4 --> P5[Hot Work]
    P4 --> P6[Confined Space]
    P4 --> P7[Working at Height]
    P4 --> P8[Electrical Isolation]
    P5 --> P9[Risk Assessment and Controls]
    P6 --> P9
    P7 --> P9
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10{Permit Approved?}
    P10 -->|Yes| P11[Execute with Monitoring]
    P10 -->|No| P12[Modify or Abort]
    P11 --> P13[Job Completion and Sign Off]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*Define "Special Situations" in the context of Design for Safety. Give two examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Special situations are atypical, non-routine, high-consequence events that demand specialized design controls and emergency response beyond conventional occupational safety. They are characterized by low frequency, high severity, and time-critical decision-making. **Examples (any two, 1 mark each):**
1. Fire and explosion in a high-rise building.
2. Confined space entry in a petrochemical tank.
3. Toxic gas release in a process plant.
4. Structural collapse following an earthquake.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*List the components of the Required Safe Egress Time (RSET) equation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The RSET equation is:
$$
\text{RSET} = t_{\text{det}} + t_{\text{alarm}} + t_{\text{pre-move}} + t_{\text{travel}}
$$
Components (1/2 mark each for full names + 1/2 mark for explanation):
1. **$t_{\text{det}}$** — Time from event ignition to detection.
2. **$t_{\text{alarm}}$** — Time from detection to alarm activation.
3. **$t_{\text{pre-move}}$** — Pre-movement time (recognition + response).
4. **$t_{\text{travel}}$** — Actual travel time to a safe location.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students **forget to state the safety criterion explicitly** (RSET < ASET) or **omit unit analysis**. Always: (1) write the governing principle, (2) substitute values with units, (3) state the conclusion in a sentence, and (4) verify with a margin-of-safety check. Marks are awarded for these final interpretive steps — skipping them costs 2–3 marks per sub-question.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the Risk Priority Number (RPN) method for assessing special situations. A confined space entry task has Severity = 9, Occurrence = 5, and Detectability = 7. Calculate the RPN and recommend the appropriate action. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition (2 marks)**
The RPN is a quantitative risk assessment tool that combines three factors: Severity (S), Occurrence probability (O), and Difficulty of Detection (D), each rated on a 1–10 scale. It is used to prioritize special situations for design intervention.

**Step 2: Formula (1 mark)**

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

**Step 3: Substitution (1 mark)**

$$
\text{RPN} = 9 \times 5 \times 7
$$

**Step 4: Calculation (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
9 \times 5 &= 45 \\
45 \times 7 &= 315
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation and Recommendation (2 marks)**
RPN = 315 falls in the **Critical** band (301–1000). Recommended actions:
- Stop work until additional engineering controls are in place.
- Install forced ventilation and continuous gas monitoring.
- Provide a retrieval system and trained standby personnel.
- Re-evaluate after controls; target RPN < 100.

---

**(b)** A hospital must be evacuated during a fire emergency. The following data is provided: detection time = 45 s, alarm time = 20 s, pre-move time = 90 s, travel time = 240 s. The time to untenable conditions is 720 s. Verify if the evacuation design is safe and compute the safety margin. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: State the safety criterion (1 mark)**
For a safe evacuation design, the following must hold:

$$
\text{RSET} < \text{ASET}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate RSET (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RSET} &= t_{\text{det}} + t_{\text{alarm}} + t_{\text{pre-move}} + t_{\text{travel}} \\
&= 45 + 20 + 90 + 240 \\
&= 395 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Identify ASET (1 mark)**

$$
\text{ASET} = t_{\text{critical}} = 720 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the criterion and compute safety margin (2 marks)**

$$
395 \text{ s} < 720 \text{ s} \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
\text{Safety Margin} = 720 - 395 = 325 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5: Conclusion (1 mark)**
The evacuation design is **safe** with a 325-second margin (≈ 5.4 minutes), which exceeds the KTU-recommended minimum of 1.5× ratio. The safety ratio is:

$$
\frac{\text{ASET}}{\text{RSET}} = \frac{720}{395} \approx 1.82
$$

This satisfies the design requirement.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Internal Choice]

**(a)** Discuss the Hierarchy of Controls as applied to special situations in design. Why is PPE considered the least preferred control? **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition (1 mark)**
The Hierarchy of Controls is a systematic framework that ranks safety interventions from most to least reliable in reducing risk for special situations.

**Step 2: List the levels in descending order (2 marks)**

1. **Elimination** — Physically remove the hazard.
2. **Substitution** — Replace with a less hazardous option.
3. **Engineering Controls** — Ventilation, barriers, interlocks.
4. **Administrative Controls** — Procedures, training, permits.
5. **PPE** — Personal protective equipment.

**Step 3: Explain each level with a special-situation example (3 marks)**
- *Elimination:* Remove a flammable solvent from a process.
- *Substitution:* Use water-based paint instead of solvent-based.
- *Engineering Controls:* Install gas sensors in a confined space.
- *Administrative Controls:* Implement a hot work permit system.
- *PPE:* Provide respirators to workers in a dusty environment.

**Step 4: Justify why PPE is least preferred (1 mark)**
PPE relies on the human worker to wear, maintain, and use it correctly. It does not eliminate the hazard; it merely places a barrier between the hazard and the worker. In special situations (e.g., sudden fire, toxic release), PPE may fail, be inadequate, or be unavailable when needed. Hence, it is the **last line of defense** and least preferred.

---

**(b)** A chemical plant stores 5000 kg of chlorine in a pressurized vessel. Using the formula $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$, compute the RPN and recommend action. Severity = 10, Occurrence = 4, Detectability = 6. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: State the formula (1 mark)**

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

**Step 2: Substitute (1 mark)**

$$
\text{RPN} = 10 \times 4 \times 6
$$

**Step 3: Compute step-by-step (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
10 \times 4 &= 40 \\
40 \times 6 &= 240
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Classify and recommend (2 marks)**

| Band | Range | Action |
|:-----|:------|:-------|
| High | 151–300 | Mandatory mitigation |

RPN = 240 → **High** band. Recommendations:
- Install real-time chlorine gas detectors with audible alarms.
- Provide emergency scrubbers and neutralization systems.
- Define a 1-km exclusion zone in the ERP.
- Conduct quarterly mock drills.

**Step 5: Residual target (1 mark)**
After controls, target RPN < 100, achieved by reducing Occurrence to ≤ 2 via improved maintenance and reducing Detectability to ≤ 5 via sensors.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Special Situations**

- **Definition:** Non-routine, high-consequence events requiring specialized design controls.
- **Key formula:** $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ — always compute, never skip the interpretative conclusion.
- **Evacuation criterion:** $\text{RSET} < \text{ASET}$ — must be satisfied for life-safety.
- **RSET components:** Detection, Alarm, Pre-move, Travel — memorize the full names.
- **Safety margin standard:** $\text{MoS} \geq 0.5$ (50% reserve) for life-safety scenarios.
- **Hierarchy of Controls order:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- **Permit-to-Work system:** Mandatory for hot work, confined space, working at height, electrical isolation.
- **Evacuation speed benchmark:** ~1.0 m/s on flat surface; reduce by 0.1 m/s per staircase flight.
- **Standard response benchmark:** < 6 minutes for medical emergencies.
- **Fire resistance rating:** 1–4 hours for structural elements.
- **NBC minimum illumination during emergency:** 10 lux along escape routes.
- **Smoke management:** Maintain layer height ≥ 2.5 m above floor for tenability.
- **Always state units** (seconds, m/s, MJ/m²) — KTU examiners deduct marks for unit omissions.
- **Always provide a final interpretive sentence** — never end a calculation abruptly with just a number.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disposal - Flare and Vent Systems, Failures in Pressure System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Disposal - Flare and Vent Systems, Failures in Pressure System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Disposal Systems in Process Safety Engineering

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
**Disposal systems** in process industries are engineered safety installations designed to safely manage, route, and eliminate hazardous process effluents (flammable, toxic, or reactive gases/vapours) that are released during normal operation, startup, shutdown, or emergency conditions (such as overpressure relief, fire, or process upset). The two principal disposal pathways in chemical, petrochemical, oil & gas, and refining industries are **Flare Systems** and **Vent Systems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** Disposal = the final stage of the **hierarchy of control** in process safety. After Prevent → Control → Mitigate → **Disposal**, the residual hazardous material is rendered harmless to people, equipment, and the environment.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a pressure cooker on a stove. As food cooks, steam builds up. The cooker's safety valve (the **pressure relief valve**) opens to release excess steam — this is the *relief* stage. Now, that released steam must go *somewhere*. You wouldn't want it blasting into the kitchen (where someone could get burned) or onto a hot burner (where it could ignite). Instead, the steam is routed through a pipe (the **flare/vent header**) to a safe location, perhaps outside the window, where it dissipates harmlessly. That pipe network, the safe-release terminal, and the rules for routing are the **disposal system**.

- A **flare system** = the safe-release terminal is a controlled burn (like a pilot light on a stove) that combusts the vapours.
- A **vent system** = the safe-release terminal simply releases the vapours into the atmosphere at a safe height and location, or scrubs them.

### Flare System — Definition
A **flare system** is an engineered arrangement of piping, collection headers, seals, burners (flare tips), and support utilities designed to safely **combust** (oxidise) flammable, toxic, or reactive vent streams by burning them at a controlled location away from the process unit and personnel.

### Vent System — Definition
A **vent system** is a piping network that routes relieved or process gases/vapours to a **safe atmospheric discharge point** (often elevated through a vent stack) or to a treatment device (scrubber, absorber) without combustion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard References (per API 521, API 537, NFPA 30):**
> - **API RP 521** – Pressure-relieving and Depressuring Systems
> - **API STD 537** – Flare Details for Petroleum, Petrochemical, and Natural Gas Industries
> - **NFPA 30** – Flammable and Combustible Liquids Code
> - **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.106** – Flammable Liquids
> - **EPA 40 CFR 60.18** – General Vent Flare Requirements

---

## 1.2 Failures in Pressure Systems

### Formal Definition
A **pressure system failure** is an event in which a vessel, pipe, valve, or other pressure-containing component loses its ability to retain the contained fluid/gas at the design pressure, temperature, or integrity, leading to loss of containment (LOC), deformation, rupture, explosion, or leakage.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine repeatedly inflating a balloon. If you over-inflate it once, the rubber stretches and eventually **pops** — that is a **ductile overload** failure. If you inflate and deflate the balloon many times, tiny cracks grow in the rubber until it suddenly tears — that is **fatigue failure**. If you leave the inflated balloon in the sun, the heat and ozone slowly embrittle the rubber, and it cracks without much stretching — that is **stress-corrosion / environmental failure**. A pressure vessel experiences the same family of failure modes, only with far more severe consequences.

### Major Categories of Pressure System Failures

| # | Failure Mode | One-line Description |
|---|--------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Ductile Overload** | Gross plastic deformation → rupture when stress exceeds yield. |
| 2 | **Brittle Fracture** | Sudden low-energy fracture with little plastic deformation (esp. at low T). |
| 3 | **Fatigue** | Crack initiation & growth under cyclic loading. |
| 4 | **Creep** | Time-dependent deformation at elevated T (typically > 0.4 T<sub>melt</sub>). |
| 5 | **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** | Cracking under combined tensile stress and corrosive environment. |
| 6 | **Hydrogen Embrittlement** | Loss of ductility due to hydrogen ingress. |
| 7 | **Corrosion Fatigue** | Synergy of cyclic stress and corrosive environment. |
| 8 | **High-Temperature Hydrogen Attack (HTHA)** | Decarburisation in H<sub>2</sub> at high T. |
| 9 | **Thermal Shock / Fatigue** | Failure from rapid temperature gradients. |
| 10 | **Ratcheting** | Progressive incremental plastic strain under cyclic loading. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per KTU Module-1 Learning Outcome:** The student must be able to (i) classify pressure-system failure modes, (ii) describe their root causes and visual tell-tales, and (iii) select mitigation/prevention strategies consistent with ASME BPVC Section VIII and API standards.

---

## 1.3 Why Disposal & Failure Analysis Matter Together (The KTU Linkage)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pedagogical Bridge:** Module 1 links *Disposal Systems* to *Pressure System Failures* because **the demand for disposal arises FROM failures and near-misses**. A pressure relief device (PRD) opens because something has gone wrong — overpressure, fire, runaway reaction. The relief stream must then be disposed of safely. Hence, *failure prevention* and *failure consequence management* are two sides of the same Design-for-Safety coin.

**Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) in process safety:**
- **LER (Loss of Energy/Ratio):** A flare should achieve ≥ **98%** combustion efficiency (per EPA 40 CFR 60.18).
- **RT (Residence Time):** ≥ **0.03 s** in the flame zone for smokeless combustion.
- **MOC (Materials of Construction) Selection:** Dictated by failure mode to be mitigated.

---

## 1.4 Glossary of Critical Terms (KTU 2024 Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Flare Header** | Main piping collecting relief streams from multiple sources. |
| **Knockout Drum** | Vessel that removes condensable liquids from relief gases before flaring. |
| **Flame Arrestor** | Device preventing flashback into the header. |
| **Flashback** | Reverse flame propagation into the header. |
| **Purge Gas** | Inert gas (typically N<sub>2</sub>) used to keep header above UEL (Upper Explosive Limit). |
| **Molecular Seal / P-V Valve** | Prevents flame propagation through the flare stack. |
| **Drift Eliminator** | Catches entrained liquid droplets in the knockout. |
| **Sonic / Subsonic / Supersonic Flare Tip** | Flare tip geometries for different combustion stability. |
| **Ductility** | Ability of material to deform plastically before fracture. |
| **DBTT** | **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature** of a ferritic steel. |
| **NDT** | Non-Destructive Testing (radiography, UT, MPI, dye-pen). |
| **PVP** | Pressure Vessel Protection. |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard units used throughout: **pressure in MPa or bar** (**1 MPa = 10 bar**); **temperature in °C or K** (**K = °C + 273.15**); **stress in MPa**.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Flare Systems — Architecture & Operating Principles

### 2.1.1 Functional Architecture of a Flare System
A flare system comprises the following sub-systems in series:

1. **Relief Devices (Sources):** Pressure Safety Valves (PSV), Pressure Relief Valves (PRV), rupture discs, blowdown valves, emergency vents.
2. **Collection Header (Common Relief Header):** Receives all relieved streams. Sized for the **largest single relief case** plus the **largest single contingency case** (per API 521 §4.4.13).
3. **Knockout Drum (KO Drum):** Removes entrained liquids. Typical residence time: **≥ 5 min** for vertical; **≥ 10 min** for horizontal.
4. **Flame Arrestor / Molecular Seal:** Prevents flashback up the stack.
5. **Purge Gas (N<sub>2</sub> or Fuel Gas):** Maintains positive header pressure; sweep keeps HC content below LEL.
6. **Flare Stack (Pipe Riser):** Vertical column; height determined by radiation contour.
7. **Flare Tip (Burner):** Where combustion takes place. Designed for stable, smokeless, and radiation-controlled combustion.
8. **Pilot Ignition System:** Continuous pilot burners ignite the main flame even at low flow. Typically **1 pilot per 12 m of flare tip** (per API 537).
9. **Steam / Air Injection System:** Induces smokeless combustion by providing air or steam to the flame envelope.

### 2.1.2 Types of Flare Systems

| Flare Type | Description | Application |
|------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Elevated (Pipe-Riser) Flare** | Vertical stack 30–200 m; tip high above grade. | Refineries, large petrochemical plants. |
| **Ground Flare** | Multi-burner tip at grade; enclosed by a refractory-lined fence with a stack. | Land-constrained sites; urban locations. |
| **Enclosed Ground Flare** | Burner inside a refractory-lined chamber; thermally insulated & quieter. | Urban / offshore / eco-sensitive. |
| **Portable / Trailer-Mounted** | Used for well testing, drilling, or temporary operations. | Oil & gas exploration. |
| **Multi-Point Ground Flare (MPGF)** | Many small burners in a chamber; modular construction. | Modern low-radiation flares. |
| **Endothermic Flare / Low-NOx Tip** | Special tip for reduced noise & NO<sub>x</sub>. | EPA-regulated zones. |

### 2.1.3 Design Philosophy — Hierarchy of Sizing
The flare is sized for the **Worst-Case Scenario (WCS)**:
- **Case 1 — Single Largest Relief** (e.g., blocked outlet from a reactor).
- **Case 2 — Multiple (≥ 2) Relief Devices opening simultaneously** (e.g., external fire affecting two vessels).
- **Case 3 — Fire case (full impounding pool fire).**

> [!NOTE]
> **Per API 521:** The flare system must safely dispose of the relief load from **Case 1 OR Case 2**, whichever is greater. **Case 3** dictates the **required relief capacity** of *each* individual device, not necessarily the flare header size.

### 2.1.4 Combustion Efficiency and the LER Metric
**LER (Leakage Emission Ratio)** quantifies how completely the relief stream is combusted:

$$\text{LER} = \frac{m_{\text{unburned HC}}}{m_{\text{total HC released}}}$$

A flare achieving **LER ≤ 0.005** is considered **high-efficiency** by EPA 40 CFR 60.18. Modern steam-assisted elevated flares routinely achieve **LER < 0.001**.

### 2.1.5 Smoke and Soot Formation
Smoke results from **fuel-rich pockets** failing to mix with air. The **Heat Release (HR)** must be sufficient to crack & burn soot. Smoke-suppression is achieved by:
- **Steam injection** (most common): 0.1–0.6 kg steam per kg of HC.
- **Air injection** (preferred for olefinic streams to avoid coking).
- **Pressure-assisted (sonic) tip**: Uses venturi effect to draw in air.

---

## 2.2 Vent Systems — Architecture & Operating Principles

### 2.2.1 Functional Architecture
A vent system handles **non-flammable**, **non-toxic**, or **low-BTU** streams that cannot be flared economically. Components:
1. Collection header.
2. Liquid-seal trap or water seal to prevent flashback.
3. Vent stack (height determined by dispersion model — e.g., AERMOD).
4. Discharge point: vertical or horizontal.
5. Optional scrubber/absorber for treatment.

### 2.2.2 Types of Vent Stacks

| Type | Description | Use |
|------|-------------|-----|
| **Vertical Vent Stack** | Tall vertical pipe; releases to atmosphere at altitude. | Steam, CO<sub>2</sub>, non-toxic gases. |
| **Horizontal Vent Header** | Multiple outlets at height. | Low-rate, multi-source plants. |
| **Blowdown Stack** | With sonic tip for high-pressure depressuring. | Emergency blowdown of hydrocarbons. |
| **Cold Vent** | Operates below auto-ignition; flame-arrestors mandatory. | Light HC release. |
| **Hot Vent** | Above auto-ignition; flame-arrestors may not be required. | High T HC streams. |
| **Combination Vent-Scrubber** | Vent gas passes through caustic/acid scrubber. | Toxic HF, HCl, SO<sub>2</sub>. |

### 2.2.3 Vent vs Flare — Decision Matrix

| Criterion | Use Flare | Use Vent |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Stream is flammable / toxic | ✔ | ✘ |
| Stream is inert or non-flammable | – | ✔ |
| Odour / community impact | – | – (scrubbed) |
| Heat value of stream | High → Flare | Low → Vent |
| Regulatory constraint | EPA 40 CFR 60.18 | 40 CFR 60.18a NSPS Subpart VVa |

---

## 2.3 Failures in Pressure Systems — Deep Analysis

### 2.3.1 Ductile vs Brittle — The Master Dichotomy
Every pressure system design (ASME BPVC Section VIII Div. 1) demands the design stress `σ_d` to be ≤ `S_m` — the **maximum allowable stress**:

$$S_m = \min\left(\frac{S_y}{1.5},\ \frac{S_u}{2.4}\right)$$

where $S_y$ = specified minimum yield strength and $S_u$ = specified minimum ultimate tensile strength (at design temperature).

- If the steel is **ductile** (high $S_y$, low DBTT), the vessel will visibly deform and give warning → *leak-before-break (LBB)*.
- If the steel is **brittle** (e.g., low-alloy steel at sub-zero), fracture is sudden, low-energy, and catastrophic.

### 2.3.2 The Brittle-Fracture Trinity (per ASME)
Brittle fracture occurs when ALL three conditions are met (the **Pellini triangle**):
1. **Sufficiently high tensile stress** (often residual or thermal).
2. **A susceptible material** (low shelf-energy; high DBTT).
3. **A critical flaw size** in the weld/Haz.

The critical flaw size is given by **Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM)** — Griffith / Irwin equation:

$$K_{IC} = Y\, \sigma\, \sqrt{\pi a}$$

where $K_{IC}$ = plane-strain fracture toughness (MPa·√m), $Y$ = geometry factor (≈ 1.0–1.6), $\sigma$ = applied stress (MPa), $a$ = half-crack length (m). A material resists brittle fracture when $K_{IC}$ at operating temperature **exceeds** the applied $K_I = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$.

### 2.3.3 The Failure Mode Map (KTU Examination Favourite)

| Operating Regime | Dominant Failure |
|------------------|------------------|
| Low T, high strain rate, ferritic steel | **Brittle fracture** |
| High T (≈ 0.4–0.7 T<sub>melt</sub>), sustained load | **Creep rupture** |
| Cyclic load, ambient T | **Fatigue** |
| Cyclic load, corrosive environment | **Corrosion fatigue** |
| Austenitic SS, Cl<sup>–</sup> containing, T ≈ 50–200 °C | **Chloride SCC** |
| Carbon steel, wet H<sub>2</sub>S | **HIC / SOHIC** |
| Carbon steel, hot H<sub>2</sub> (> 200 °C, > 5 MPa) | **HTHA** |
| Weld HAZ, slow cooling | **Reheat cracking** |
| Lined equipment, vacuum | **Buckling (external pressure)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule of Thumb:** Always identify the **dominant degradation mechanism** before quoting a $S_m$ — because ASME assigns different $S_m$ factors to different failure modes. E.g., for creep, ASME uses a **time-dependent** allowable stress based on $10^5$ hr creep-rupture strength.

### 2.3.4 Fatigue — S-N and ε-N Approaches
For high-cycle fatigue (> 10<sup>4</sup> cycles) the **Basquin S-N law** applies:

$$S_a = S'_f\, (2N)^{b}$$

where $S_a$ = alternating stress amplitude, $S'_f$ = fatigue strength coefficient ≈ $S_u$ (for steel), $b$ ≈ -0.07 to -0.12 for steel, $N$ = cycles to failure.

For low-cycle fatigue (plastic strain-controlled, < 10<sup>4</sup> cycles) the **Coffin-Manson relation** is used:

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f\, (2N)^{c}$$

with $c$ ≈ -0.5 to -0.7 for ductile steels.

### 2.3.5 Creep — Norton-Bailey Law
The steady-state creep strain rate is:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A\, \sigma^{n}\, \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$$

with $A$ = constant, $n$ ≈ 3–8 (typically 5 for steels), $Q_c$ = activation energy (kJ/mol), $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K, $T$ = absolute temperature.

**Design life for creep** is bounded by the **time-to-rupture (Larson-Miller)**:

$$\text{LMP} = \frac{T}{1000}\left[\,C + \log_{10}(t_r)\,\right]$$

where $t_r$ is in hours, $C$ ≈ 20 (carbon steel) and LMP is the Larson-Miller Parameter (constant for a given $S$). Higher LMP ⇒ longer rupture life at given T.

### 2.3.6 Stress-Corrosion Cracking (SCC)
A combined-action failure requiring:
- **Tensile stress** (residual or applied).
- **Specific environment** (e.g., Cl<sup>–</sup> + austenitic SS, wet H<sub>2</sub>S + carbon steel).
- **Susceptible material microstructure.**

The threshold stress below which SCC does NOT occur in a given time is the **K<sub>ISCC</sub>**:

$$K_{ISCC} = Y\, \sigma_{th}\, \sqrt{\pi a}$$

If $K_I < K_{ISCC}$, no SCC propagation. Mitigation: change material (e.g., low-carbon 304L, or 2205 duplex), reduce residual stress (PWHT), remove corrodent (drain & dry, N<sub>2</sub> blanket), or shot-peen to impose compressive stress.

### 2.3.7 Hydrogen-Related Failures

| Type | Mechanism | Mitigation |
|------|-----------|------------|
| **Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE)** | H diffuses into steel, lowers fracture energy. | Bake-out at 200 °C; use Ni, Co, Cu alloys. |
| **Hydrogen Induced Cracking (HIC)** | H recombines at MnS → internal blisters. | Low-S steel; Ca-treatment. |
| **Stress-Oriented HIC (SOHIC)** | HIC + stress → stepwise cracks. | Post-weld heat treatment. |
| **High-Temp HTHA** | H + C → CH<sub>4</sub> in steel; decarburisation. | Use Cr-Mo steels (1¼Cr-½Mo, 2¼Cr-1Mo). |

### 2.3.8 Pressure-Vessel Design Equations (KTU Formula Sheet)

| # | Equation | Used For | Reference |
|---|----------|----------|-----------|
| 1 | $p = \frac{2\, S_m\, t}{R + 0.6\,t}$ (cylindrical) | Thin-wall cylinder (t < R) | ASME VIII-1 UG-27 |
| 2 | $p = \frac{2\, S_m\, t}{D - 1.2\,t}$ | Spherical & cylindrical (alt) | ASME VIII-1 |
| 3 | $p = \frac{2\, S_m\, E\, t}{L + 0.8\,t}$ | Spherical (E = joint eff.) | ASME VIII-1 |
| 4 | $S_m = \min\!\left(\dfrac{S_y}{1.5},\ \dfrac{S_u}{2.4}\right)$ | Allowable stress | ASME II Part D |
| 5 | $t = \dfrac{pR}{S E - 0.6 p}$ | Required thickness (rearrange) | ASME VIII-1 |
| 6 | $K_{IC} = Y\, \sigma\, \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Fracture criterion | LEFM |
| 7 | $p_c = \dfrac{2.6\, E\, t^{2.5}}{L\, D^{1.5}}$ | External pressure limit | ASME VIII-1 UG-28 |
| 8 | $\dot\varepsilon_{ss} = A\sigma^n \exp(-Q_c/RT)$ | Creep rate | Norton-Bailey |
| 9 | $LMP = (T/1000)(C+\log_{10}t_r)$ | Creep rupture life | Larson-Miller |
| 10 | $S_a = S'_f (2N)^b$ | High-cycle fatigue | Basquin |
| 11 | $\Delta\varepsilon_p/2 = \varepsilon'_f (2N)^c$ | Low-cycle fatigue | Coffin-Manson |
| 12 | $Q = K_d A \sqrt{P_1-P_2}$ | Required relief capacity | API 521 §4.4.13 |
| 13 | $h = \frac{0.27\, q_r}{d \sqrt{x}}$ | Stack height for radiation | API 521 §5.6.3 |
| 14 | $LER = m_{unburned}/m_{total}$ | Flare efficiency | EPA 40 CFR 60.18 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Subscript conventions used above:**
> - $S_m$ = maximum allowable stress
> - $E$ = joint efficiency
> - $R, D, t, L$ = radius, OD, thickness, length
> - $p$ = internal design pressure
> - $p_c$ = critical external buckling pressure
> - $S_a$ = alternating stress amplitude

### 2.4.1 Real-World Engineering Utility
- A **refinery** may have 50–200 pressure vessels. Knowing the dominant failure mode (creep in a fired heater tube, SCC in a hydrocracker, fatigue in a reciprocating compressor surge line) lets the inspector schedule the right **NDT** (PM, eddy-current, AE, UT-thickness) at the right frequency.
- A **flare designer** uses the relief-capacity formula $Q = K_d A \sqrt{P_1 - P_2}$ to size the flare tip area $A$ for the worst-case depressuring flow, and the radiation formula to size the height of the stack such that no ground-level location exceeds **6.31 kW/m²** (the limit for personnel on grass) or **15.77 kW/m²** (the structural steel limit).

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Calculation Convention:** All equations below are derived step-by-step with intermediate values shown. No step is skipped. Multi-line derivations are wrapped in `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` blocks.

## 3.1 Derivation: Thin-Wall Cylinder Hoop-Stress Equation

A thin-wall cylindrical pressure vessel of radius $R$, wall thickness $t$, internal pressure $p$, and axial length $L$ is sectioned to expose the **hoop stress** $\sigma_h$.

**Force equilibrium on a half-cylinder (free body):**

The pressure acts on the projected area $2R \cdot L$, giving a net radial force that must be balanced by the longitudinal membrane stress $\sigma_h$ acting on the cut area $2 t \cdot L$:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{pressure} &= p \cdot 2R \cdot L \\
F_{hoop} &= 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L \\
\text{Equilibrium:}\quad \sigma_h \cdot 2tL &= p \cdot 2RL \\
\Rightarrow \sigma_h &= \frac{pR}{t}
\end{aligned}$$

By a similar analysis on an axial cross-section, the **longitudinal (axial) stress** is:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{axial} &= p \cdot \pi R^{2} \\
F_{axial,membrane} &= \sigma_L \cdot 2\pi R t \\
\Rightarrow \sigma_L &= \frac{pR}{2t}
\end{aligned}$$

**Therefore:** $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_L$. The hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress — the **critical stress** for cylindrical shells.

Setting $\sigma_h = S_m$ and solving for $p$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{pR}{t} &= S_m \\
\Rightarrow p_{\max} &= \frac{S_m \, t}{R} = \frac{2 S_m t}{D}
\end{aligned}$$

Introducing the **joint efficiency** $E$ and a **safety factor** (implicit in $S_m$), the **ASME BPVC Section VIII Div. 1 (UG-27)** formula is:

$$p = \frac{2 S_m E t}{D - 1.2 t}$$

This is the **first of the master design equations** every KTU student must know.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Cylindrical Pressure-Vessel Sizing (Part A Type)

> **Problem:** Design a cylindrical pressure vessel for $p_d = 4$ MPa at $T_d = 200$ °C, inside radius $R = 1$ m, SA-516 Gr. 70 carbon steel ($S_y = 260$ MPa, $S_u = 485$ MPa at 200 °C), full radiographic spot, $E = 0.85$.

**Step 1: Compute $S_m$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{S_y}{1.5} &= \frac{260}{1.5} = 173.33 \text{ MPa} \\
\frac{S_u}{2.4} &= \frac{485}{2.4} = 202.08 \text{ MPa} \\
S_m &= \min(173.33,\ 202.08) = 173.33 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute required thickness (rearranged ASME).**

$$t = \frac{p_d R}{S_m E - 0.6 p_d} = \frac{4 \cdot 1000}{173.33 \cdot 0.85 - 0.6 \cdot 4} = \frac{4000}{147.33 - 2.4} = \frac{4000}{144.93}$$

$$t = 27.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Add corrosion allowance (say 1.5 mm) and rounding up to next plate gauge:**

$$t_{nom} = 27.6 + 1.5 = 29.1 \text{ mm} \rightarrow 30 \text{ mm (standard plate)}$$

> **Result:** $t_{nom} = 30$ mm. This is the **expected KTU part-b answer** style.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Critical Flaw Size for Brittle Fracture

Start from LEFM (Irwin modification of Griffith):

$$K_{IC} = Y\, \sigma\, \sqrt{\pi a_c}$$

Solving for the **critical (allowable) half-flaw length** $a_c$:

$$a_c = \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{K_{IC}}{Y \sigma}\right)^{2}$$

**Worked Example 2:** An austenitic SS line-pipe at room T has $K_{IC} = 80 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$ and operates at $\sigma = 120$ MPa, with $Y = 1.1$.

$$\begin{aligned}
a_c &= \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{80}{1.1 \cdot 120}\right)^{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{80}{132}\right)^{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{\pi}\left(0.6061\right)^{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{\pi} \cdot 0.3673 \\
&= 0.1169 \text{ m} = 117 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

The full surface crack length $2a_c = 234$ mm. **Above this, brittle fracture will occur.**

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Required Relief Capacity for a Heat-Exchanger Tube-Rupture (Part B)

**Given:** Hydrocarbon side: $L = 10$ m, $D = 0.5$ m, MAWP = 2 MPa, $T = 300$ °C.
Cooling-water side: $P_{cw} = 1$ MPa. Tube-side flow at MAWP: $W = 8$ kg/s, $C_p = 2.2$ kJ/kg·K.
Latent heat of coolant vaporisation at release: $H_v = 2200$ kJ/kg.

**Per API 521 §4.4.13.5.3** (tube-rupture case), the required heat-removal rate is:

$$Q = W \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

Cooling water will vaporise. The minimum required relief mass flow (steam venting):

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{steam} &= \frac{Q}{H_v} = \frac{W \cdot C_p \cdot (T - T_{sat})}{H_v}
\end{aligned}$$

If $T_{sat}(P_{cw}) = 180$ °C, then:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= 8 \cdot 2.2 \cdot (300 - 180) = 8 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 120 = 2112 \text{ kW} \\
\dot{m}_{steam} &= \frac{2112}{2200} = 0.96 \text{ kg/s} = 57.6 \text{ kg/min}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **required steam venting rate** for safe disposal of the energy release. The **flare header** must be sized to handle **this 0.96 kg/s steam** in addition to any other relief cases.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Flare Stack Height for Thermal Radiation

**Given:** Heat release in the flame: $Q_{rad} = 50$ MW. Acceptable ground-level radiation at property line: $q_r = 6.31$ kW/m² (per API 521 §5.6.3 — "permissible for short exposure of emergency personnel but not continuous").

**API 521 radiation formula:**

$$q_r = \frac{0.27 \cdot Q_{rad}\, F}{h^2}$$

where $F$ = view factor (≈ 1.0 for elevated flare with horizontal target at distance $x \gg h$). Solving for stack height $h$:

$$h = \sqrt{\frac{0.27 \cdot Q_{rad}}{q_r}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.27 \cdot 50{,}000}{6.31}} = \sqrt{2139.5} = 46.3 \text{ m}$$

> **Result:** Stack height ≥ 47 m. Add 10 m for safety → $h = 60$ m.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Failure Mode vs Mitigation

| Failure Mode | Design-Time Mitigation | Operational Mitigation |
|--------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Ductile Overload** | Lower design stress; $S_m = S_y/1.5$. | Overpressure protection (PSV). |
| **Brittle Fracture** | Charpy V-notch; specify low-DBTT material; PWHT. | Warm up slowly; limit NDT-acceptance size. |
| **Fatigue** | Finite-element fatigue analysis; $S-N$ curve. | Vibration monitoring; reduce cycle count. |
| **Creep** | Use $10^5$-hr stress to compute $t_{design}$. | Temperature monitoring; creep-allowance growth measurement. |
| **SCC** | Material change; PWHT; lower Cl<sup>–</sup>. | Chemical cleaning; N<sub>2</sub> blanket. |
| **HTHA** | Use 1¼Cr-½Mo or 2¼Cr-1Mo. | Decoking; stop on water wash. |
| **HIC** | Low-S, Ca-treated steel. | Dry H<sub>2</sub>S service. |
| **Buckling** | Stiffener rings; $p_c$ calculation. | Avoid vacuum during steam-out. |

---

## 3.7 Step-by-Step Safety-Integrity-Level (SIL) Determination for a Pressure-Relief Loop

A **pressure-relief loop** in a safety-instrumented function has a Safety Integrity Level (SIL) per IEC 61511. The SIL is computed from the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)**:

| SIL | PFD Range |
|-----|-----------|
| 1 | 10<sup>–2</sup> to 10<sup>–1</sup> |
| 2 | 10<sup>–3</sup> to 10<sup>–2</sup> |
| 3 | 10<sup>–4</sup> to 10<sup>–3</sup> |
| 4 | 10<sup>–5</sup> to 10<sup>–4</sup> |

The **risk-reduction factor (RRF)** is:

$$RRF = \frac{1}{PFD}$$

The flare system, being a passive installation (no active sensing for emergency depressuring), typically has **PFD < 10<sup>–2</sup>**, equivalent to **SIL 2** for the flare header itself, while the **PSV** (the active device) is SIL 3.

---

## 3.8 Dimensional Analysis of the Relief Capacity Equation

The API-521 orifice equation:

$$Q = K_d \, A \, \sqrt{\frac{2\, k}{k-1}\, \frac{p_1}{v_1}\left[\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{2/k}-\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{(k+1)/k}\right]}$$

where $K_d$ = discharge coefficient (0.6–0.97), $A$ = orifice area, $k = C_p/C_v$, $p_1$ = upstream stagnation pressure, $p_2$ = downstream, $v_1$ = specific volume upstream.

- **Units check:** $[A]$ = m², $[p_1/v_1]$ = N/m² ÷ m³/kg = kg/m·s² → kg·m/s² per m³ ⇒ kg/m²·s². Inside the bracket: dimensionless. So $Q$ has units of $\text{m}^2 \cdot \sqrt{\text{kg/m}^2\text{/s}^2} = \text{kg/s}$. ✔
- **Limit:** If $p_2/p_1 \leq \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{k/(k-1)}$ (≈ 0.528 for air), flow is **choked**; $p_2$ no longer affects $Q$.

---

## 3.9 Why Material Selection Dictates Failure Mode — Worked Comparison

A **1%-Cr-½Mo steel** at 480 °C and a **316L austenitic SS** at the same T will have:
- Cr-Mo: **creep** dominates. $S_m$ is the time-dependent $S_{r/100{,}000 hr}$.
- 316L SS: **sensitisation & SCC** dominate. $S_m$ is the rupture stress. Solution-anneal (1060 °C + water quench) restores corrosion resistance.

**This is why material choice in KTU 2024 module-1 questions is a frequent 7-mark sub-question.**

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flare System — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Unit Reactor Vessel] --> B[Pressure Relief Valve PSV]
    A --> C[Rupture Disc RD]
    D[Heat Exchanger Tube Rupture] --> E[Emergency Block Valve]
    B --> F[Common Relief Header]
    C --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Knockout Drum KO Liquid-Liquid Separator]
    G --> H[Flame Arrestor and Molecular Seal]
    H --> I[Pilot Ignition System with Continuous Pilot Burner]
    H --> J[Steam or Air Injection for Smoke Suppression]
    H --> K[Flare Stack Pipe Riser Vertical Column]
    K --> L[Flare Tip Burner]
    L --> M[Combustion Plume to Atmosphere]
    F --> N[Nitrogen Purge Gas Supply]
    O[Flame Scanner and Pilot Status] --> P[Control Room and ESD Logic]
    G --> O
    F --> O
    N --> F
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Relief streams from three independent sources (PSV, rupture disc, exchanger block valve) merge in a **common relief header**.
- The **KO drum** removes condensate (3-phase separator: gas / water / HC).
- The **flame arrestor** prevents flashback; the **molecular seal** also blocks flashback.
- **Pilots** ensure re-ignition if the main flame is blown out.
- **Purge gas (N<sub>2</sub>)** keeps the header above UEL to prevent explosive mixture accumulation.
- A **flame scanner** and ESD logic close the system in the event of pilot failure.

---

## 4.2 Vent System — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Vessel with Non-Flammable Relief] --> B[Relief Valve]
    C[Reactor Vacuum Source] --> D[Vacuum Breaker]
    B --> E[Common Vent Header]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Liquid Seal Trap Water Seal]
    F --> G[Scrubber Optional Caustic or Acid Wash]
    G --> H[Flame Arrestor for Cold Vent]
    H --> I[Vertical Vent Stack]
    I --> J[Atmospheric Discharge at Design Height]
    K[Dispersion Model AERMOD Output] --> L[Acceptable Concentration at Fence Line]
    J --> L
    F --> M[Flame Scanner Logic]
    H --> M
```

---

## 4.3 Pressure-Vessel Failure Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pressure System Failure Detected] --> B{Is Material Ductile or Brittle at Operating T?}
    B -- Ductile --> C{Is T greater than 0.4 T Melt?}
    B -- Brittle --> D{Is There a Critical Flaw Present?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Creep or Stress Rupture]
    C -- No --> F{Is the Loading Static or Cyclic?}
    F -- Static --> G[Ductile Overload Leading to Rupture]
    F -- Cyclic --> H[Fatigue Failure]
    D -- Yes --> I[Brittle Fracture Catastrophic]
    D -- No --> J{Is the Environment Corrosive?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Stress Corrosion Cracking or Hydrogen Embrittlement]
    J -- No --> L[Subcritical Crack Stagnant]
    H --> M{Is Corrosive Environment Also Present?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Corrosion Fatigue Accelerated]
    M -- No --> O[Mechanical Fatigue Baseline]
    E --> P{Is the Loading Purely Sustained or Cyclic}
    P -- Sustained --> Q[Time Dependent Creep Rupture]
    P -- Cyclic --> R[Creep Fatigue Interaction]
```

---

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Subsonic Low Pressure Drop] --> B[Stable Low Exit Velocity]
    C[Sonic Velocity at Tip] --> D[Stable High Flow Rate]
    E[Supersonic with High Pressure Drop] --> F[Noise and Vibrations but Stable]
    G[Very Low Turndown less than 5 percent] --> H[Lift Off and Flame Out]
```

## 4.5 Disposal System — Decision Logic for Flare vs Vent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Relief Stream Generated] --> B{Is Stream Flammable or Toxic?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Route to Flare System]
    B -- No --> D{Is Stream High in VOC or Odorous?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Route to Scrubber then Vent]
    D -- No --> F[Route to Vent Stack]
    C --> G{Knockout Drum Capacity}

```
