# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **All questions below are constructed strictly per KTU 2024 Scheme, ESE (End Semester Examination) pattern:**
> - **Part A:** 3-mark short-answer questions (10 questions, 30 marks total).
> - **Part B:** 14-mark long-answer with internal choice; sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.
> - Marks tagged with cognitive level: **[L1 Remember], [L2 Understand], [L3 Apply], [L4 Analyze], [L5 Evaluate], [L6 Create]** per **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy** of NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 scheme.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (Choose the two most relevant)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Set A] [L2 Understand] — 3 Marks
**Differentiate between a flare system and a vent system. When would you prefer a vent stack over a flare?**

**Model Answer:**
A **flare system** disposes of flammable or toxic gas streams by controlled combustion at a remote tip, converting them to CO<sub>2</sub> and H<sub>2</sub>O. A **vent system** simply discharges non-flammable, non-toxic streams to the atmosphere through a tall stack after treatment (e.g., scrubbing). A **vent stack** is preferred over a flare when:
- The stream is **inert** (N<sub>2</sub>, CO<sub>2</sub>, steam) and has **no fuel value**.
- The stream is **toxic but not flammable** and can be **scrubbed** more economically than flared.
- A community is sensitive to visible flames, noise, or thermal radiation.
- The stream has a **high molecular weight** (e.g., HCl, HF) that does not combust cleanly.
- A **cost–benefit analysis** shows vent + scrubber < flare capital + operating cost.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Set B] [L2 Understand] — 3 Marks
**What is a knockout drum? State two design requirements for the same.**

**Model Answer:**
A **knockout drum (KO drum)** is a vessel installed upstream of a flare tip to **separate entrained liquids** (water, condensate, or heavy HC) from the relief gas stream. It protects the flare from:
- **Burner instability** caused by liquid carry-over.
- **Smoke & soot** formation due to fuel-rich liquid.
- **Burning rain** (falling flaming droplets) below the flare tip.
- **Corrosion** of the flare tip from HCl/H<sub>2</sub>S-bearing condensate.

**Two key design requirements:**
1. **Residence time:** Vertical KO drum: ≥ 5 minutes; Horizontal: ≥ 10 minutes (API 521 §6.3.2).
2. **Sizing for the largest liquid drop-out:** The vessel diameter must allow the largest entrained droplet (say 300–600 µm) to settle out at design gas velocity. The terminal settling velocity must exceed the gas velocity at the cross-section (Stokes' law governs).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1 (mark-loser):**
> Students often confuse **flashback** with **blowdown** and **smokeless combustion** with **high combustion efficiency**. They are **not** the same. A flare can be smokeless (use steam) but have low LER (HC unburnt); conversely, a flare can have high LER but smoke heavily (improper air/steam mixing).
>
> **Pitfall #2:** When asked to "design a flare stack height", do **NOT** only compute the radiation limit. You must also **(a)** confirm dispersion of unburnt HC below TLV at the fence line, **(b)** confirm that the stack is not in the **NPSH / structural / aircraft-zone** envelope, and **(c)** state the **API 521** formula used.
>
> **Pitfall #3:** For pressure-vessel failure-mode questions, students often name a mode but **fail to state the cause, the visible tell-tale, the NDT method, and the prevention**. Listing just the name gives only 2 of 7 marks.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU 2024 ESE – Module 1, Set 1]

**(a) [L3 Apply] (7 marks)**
A refinery hydrocracker has a tube-side stream at MAWP = 8 MPa, $T$ = 350 °C. The shell side is at 1 MPa cooling water. The **worst-case tube rupture** releases 12 kg/s of HC into the shell, with $C_p$ = 2.4 kJ/kg·K. Steam at the shell pressure has a latent heat $H_v$ = 2100 kJ/kg and $T_{sat}$ = 180 °C.
**Compute the required steam venting rate that the disposal (vent/flare) system must handle.**

**(b) [L4 Analyze] (7 marks)**
Compare the relative merits of an **Elevated Flare** and a **Multi-Point Ground Flare (MPGF)** for an urban refinery. List at least five technical or environmental criteria and recommend the better option for a land-constrained urban site.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU 2024 ESE – Module 1, Set 1 — Internal Choice]

**(a) [L3 Apply] (7 marks)**
A vertical pressure vessel is to be designed for a service of $p$ = 6 MPa, $T$ = 320 °C, $D$ = 1.2 m. The selected material is SA-387 Gr. 11 Cl. 2 (1¼Cr-½Mo) with $S_y$ = 310 MPa, $S_u$ = 515 MPa at 320 °C. Joint efficiency $E$ = 0.85.
**Compute the required wall thickness (with 2 mm corrosion allowance) using ASME BPVC Section VIII Div. 1.**
**Identify the dominant failure mode for this vessel in service and justify your answer with the relevant temperature ratio.**

**(b) [L4 Analyze] (7 marks)**
Explain the mechanism of **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** in austenitic stainless steels. With neat sketches (or text-sketches), describe:
- The three necessary conditions for SCC.
- One **plant case study** (any of: 304 SS in chloride-bearing cooling water, 316 SS in caustic, or 321 SS in polythionic acid).
- The **mitigation strategy** (one material-based, one operating-based).

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A (a) — Tube-Rupture Relief Rate

**Step 1 — Energy released into the shell per second.**

$$Q = \dot m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = 12 \cdot 2.4 \cdot (350 - 180)$$

$$Q = 12 \cdot 2.4 \cdot 170 = 4896 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Steam venting rate to absorb this energy.**

$$\dot m_{steam} = \frac{Q}{H_v} = \frac{4896}{2100} = 2.33 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kg/h for header sizing.**

$$\dot m_{steam} = 2.33 \cdot 3600 = 8388 \text{ kg/h} \approx 8.4 \text{ t/h}$$

**Step 4 — Pipe diameter (rule of thumb, $v$ = 30 m/s for steam).**

$$A = \frac{\dot m}{\rho v} = \frac{2.33}{4.99 \cdot 30} = 0.0156 \text{ m}^2 \Rightarrow D = 0.141 \text{ m}$$

Use 150 mm NB pipe.

> **Answer:** The disposal system must handle **2.33 kg/s** (≈ 8.4 t/h) of steam plus the tube-side HC flash — hence the flare header must be sized for the **sum** of cases (tube rupture + fire case on the reactor), as per API 521.

---

### Solution to Question A (b) — Elevated Flare vs MPGF

| Criterion | Elevated Flare | Multi-Point Ground Flare |
|-----------|----------------|--------------------------|
| **Capital Cost** | Lower (single stack) | Higher (enclosure + burners) |
| **Land Footprint** | Small at base, large safety radius | Concentrated; small safety radius |
| **Radiation at Fence Line** | Can be high; needs tall stack | Low (burners in chamber) |
| **Visibility / Public Nuisance** | Visible tall flame | Hidden inside enclosure |
| **Smoke & Luminosity** | Visible at night | Barely visible |
| **Noise** | Moderate | Lower (enclosure absorbs) |
| **Maintenance Access** | Difficult (climbing the stack) | Easy (grade-level) |
| **Turndown Capability** | Limited (may lift off) | Excellent (modular burners) |
| **Operability in Wind** | Sensitive (cross-wind lifts flame) | Robust |
| **Combustion Efficiency** | 96–99% (LER < 0.005) | 99%+ (LER < 0.001) |

> **Recommendation for a land-constrained urban refinery:**
> **MPGF** is preferred because:
> 1. **Lower radiation footprint** (≤ 1.5 kW/m² at property line) — community-friendly.
> 2. **Hidden flame** — acceptable to urban aesthetics.
> 3. **Modular turndown** — efficient at low flow.
> 4. **Easier maintenance** — important when land is premium.
> 5. **Smokeless by design** — air/steam injection per burner.
>
> Caveat: MPGF capital is ~1.5× of elevated; operating cost is comparable.

---

### Solution to Question B (a) — Vessel Thickness and Failure Mode

**Step 1 — $S_m$ calculation.**

$$\frac{S_y}{1.5} = \frac{310}{1.5} = 206.7 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\frac{S_u}{2.4} = \frac{515}{2.4} = 214.6 \text{ MPa}$$

$$S_m = \min(206.7,\ 214.6) = 206.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Required thickness (rearranged ASME VIII-1 UG-27).**

$$t = \frac{p D}{2 S_m E - 1.2 p} = \frac{6 \cdot 1200}{2 \cdot 206.7 \cdot 0.85 - 1.2 \cdot 6}$$

Denominator:

$$2 \cdot 206.7 \cdot 0.85 = 351.4 \quad ;\quad 1.2 \cdot 6 = 7.2$$

Denominator = $351.4 - 7.2 = 344.2$ MPa.

$$t = \frac{7200}{344.2} = 20.92 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Add corrosion allowance (2 mm) and round up.**

$$t_{nom} = 20.92 + 2 = 22.92 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \textbf{24 mm (next standard gauge)}$$

**Step 4 — Dominant failure mode.**

For **SA-387 Gr. 11 Cl. 2 (1¼Cr-½Mo) at 320 °C**:
- Melting point: $T_m \approx 1500$ °C = **1773 K**.
- $T/T_m = (320+273)/1773 = 593/1773 = 0.334$.
- The threshold for creep is approximately $0.4 \times T_m$.

$$\frac{T}{T_m} = 0.334 < 0.4 \rightarrow \text{**below creep threshold**}$$

> **Therefore, the dominant failure mode is Ductile Overload, not Creep.**
> However, because this is a **hydrocracker** operating in the **wet H<sub>2</sub>S + H<sub>2</sub>** regime, **SCC** must also be checked — SA-387 is susceptible to **hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC)** in wet H<sub>2</sub>S service. So the **secondary failure mode** to evaluate is **HIC/SOHIC**.

---

### Solution to Question B (b) — Stress Corrosion Cracking

**Mechanism:** SCC is a **synergistic failure** in which a susceptible alloy under tensile stress in a specific corrosive environment fractures with little warning. The mechanism involves:
1. **Passive film rupture** at stress-concentration points (slip-step emergence).
2. **Localised anodic dissolution** at the rupture.
3. **Re-passivation** behind the dissolution front.
4. **Crack advance** as the process repeats at the new tip.

**Three necessary conditions (the "SCC triangle"):**
- **Susceptible material** (e.g., 304 SS in Cl<sup>–</sup>, 316 SS in hot caustic, 321 SS in polythionic acid).
- **Tensile stress** (residual, applied, or thermal).
- **Specific environment** (e.g., Cl<sup>–</sup> + H<sub>2</sub>O + O<sub>2</sub>, or polythionic acid from FeS + H<sub>2</sub>O + O<sub>2</sub>).

**Plant case study — 304 SS in chloride-bearing cooling water:**
- A 304 SS heat-exchanger tube bundle was used in seawater cooling. Within 18 months, transverse cracks appeared on the inside (process) surface, originating from chloride deposits under gaskets.
- **Tell-tale:** Branching, transgranular cracks, often with rust deposits at the mouth.
- **Mitigation:**
  - **Material-based:** Replace 304 SS with **2205 duplex SS** or **6Mo super-austenitic** (AL-6XN, 254 SMO) — both have higher Cl<sup>–</sup> SCC threshold.
  - **Operating-based:** Eliminate oxygen ingress (use N<sub>2</sub> blanketing); reduce operating T to < 60 °C; eliminate chloride deposits by routine cleaning; apply **shot-peening** to the inside surface to impose compressive residual stress.

**Diagram (text-sketches):**

```
SCC Crack Branching Pattern (Transgranular)

|     /\
|    /  \
|   /    \   (transgranular, branched)
|  /      \
| /        \
|/__________\  <- crack-initiation site (slip-step)

Process conditions:
- Cl- > 200 ppm
- T > 60 °C
- pH neutral
- O2 present
```

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as the night-before-the-exam checklist. Every line is exam-relevant.**

### Flare System Quick-Revision
- [x] Flare is a **combustion-based disposal** for **flammable / toxic** streams.
- [x] Major components: **PSV/RD source → Header → KO Drum → Flame Arrestor → Pilot → Stack → Flare Tip**.
- [x] **API 521** is the primary sizing standard; **API 537** for flare details.
- [x] **LER < 0.005** ⇒ **98% combustion efficiency** ⇒ EPA 40 CFR 60.18 compliant.
- [x] **Stack height = radiation-driven**, NOT visual.
- [x] **Smokeless combustion** via steam OR air injection.
- [x] **Purge gas (N<sub>2</sub>)** keeps header above UEL.
- [x] **Pilot reliability > 99%** required; multiple pilots per tip.
- [x] **Sizing case = WCS** (worst-case single + multiple contingencies).
- [x] **Flare knock-out drum** residence time: 5 min (V) / 10 min (H).

### Vent System Quick-Revision
- [x] Vent is **non-combustion disposal**; for **inert / non-flammable / scrubbed** streams.
- [x] **Vent stack height** driven by **dispersion** (AERMOD), not radiation.
- [x] **Liquid seal trap** blocks flashback.
- [x] **Cold vents** (T < AIT) ⇒ flame arrestor mandatory.
- [x] **Hot vents** (T > AIT) ⇒ flame arrestor optional.

### Pressure System Failure Quick-Revision
- [x] **Ductile → Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)** governs brittle fracture.
- [x] **Charpy V-notch test** quantifies impact energy → 27 J at min design T.
- [x] **Pellini triangle** = (Stress, Material, Flaw) ⇒ Brittle fracture.
- [x] **ASME VIII-1** $S_m$ formula: $\min(S_y/1.5,\ S_u/2.4)$.
- [x] **Creep** appears when $T/T_m > 0.4$.
- [x] **Creep rupture life** predicted by **Larson-Miller Parameter**.
- [x] **Fatigue** predicted by **Basquin** (HCF) or **Coffin-Manson** (LCF).
- [x] **SCC** requires the **triangle** (Susceptible material + Tensile stress + Specific environment).
- [x] **HTHA** ⇒ use **Cr-Mo steels** (1¼Cr-½Mo, 2¼Cr-1Mo).
- [x] **HIC** ⇒ use **low-S, Ca-treated** steel.
- [x] **External pressure** ⇒ check **buckling** by ASME VIII-1 UG-28.
- [x] **LEFM critical flaw**: $a_c = (K_{IC}/(Y\sigma))^{2}/\pi$.
- [x] **PWHT** mitigates SCC and reheat cracking.
- [x] **Solution anneal** restores SS corrosion resistance.

### Formula Quick-Recall (Memorise This)
- $S_m = \min(S_y/1.5,\ S_u/2.4)$.
- $p = 2S_mEt/(D-1.2t)$.
- $K_{IC} = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$.
- $LMP = (T/1000)(C+\log_{10}t_r)$.
- $h = \sqrt{0.27\,Q_{rad}/q_r}$.
- $Q = K_d A \sqrt{2k/(k-1)\cdot p_1/v_1\cdot [(\ldots)^{2/k}-(\ldots)^{(k+1)/k}]}$.

### Numerical Constants to Remember
- $R$ (gas) = **8.314 J/mol·K**.
- 1 MPa = **10 bar** = **145.04 psi**.
- API radiation limits: 6.31 kW/m² (personnel), 15.77 kW/m² (steel).
- EPA LER target: **< 0.005** (high efficiency).

### Frequently Asked Sub-Topics in KTU
- Differentiate flare and vent. ✔
- Sketch flare-system P&ID. ✔
- Compute flare stack height for radiation. ✔
- List 5 pressure-vessel failure modes. ✔
- Differentiate fatigue and creep. ✔
- Discuss SCC mechanism and prevention. ✔
- ASME $S_m$ calculation. ✔
- API 521 relief capacity for tube rupture. ✔
- HTHA / HIC mechanism and mitigation. ✔
- Failure-case study (Bhopal, Flixborough, Piper Alpha). ✔

### Common KTU Examiner Traps
1. **Forgetting the 1.2-factor** in $p = 2S_mEt/(D-1.2t)$.
2. **Confusing** the **choked-flow** condition $p_2/p_1 = (2/(k+1))^{k/(k-1)}$ with the **critical pressure ratio**.
3. **Missing units** in $K_{IC}$ (must be MPa·√m, not MPa·√mm).
4. **Forgetting the corrosion allowance** in thickness design.
5. **Omitting PWHT** in SCC questions for welded austenitic SS.
6. **Computing stack height from radiation only** — but **dispersion** must also be checked.
7. **Confusing $S_m$ with $S_y$** — $S_m$ is the *allowable*, $S_y$ is the *yield*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plant Design: Synthesis, Characteristics of Design Problems, Design as Equipment Selection: Emergency Isolation Valves, Classification Activities, Designs with Shallow Structure, Designs with Deep Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plant Design: Synthesis, Characteristics of Design Problems, Design as Equipment Selection: Emergency Isolation Valves, Classification Activities, Designs with Shallow Structure, Designs with Deep Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Plant Design: Synthesis (Definition)

**Plant Design Synthesis** is the systematic, creative process of combining and configuring unit operations, unit processes, equipment, and control/safety systems into an integrated chemical/petrochemical/manufacturing plant that transforms raw materials into desired products while satisfying operational, economic, safety, and environmental constraints.

In the language of KTU 2024 Scheme Process Safety terminology, synthesis is the **upstream conceptual phase** of plant design where the engineer *generates alternatives*, *selects feasible configurations*, and *assembles flowsheets* — as opposed to the *analysis* phase, which evaluates an already-existing design.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> "Plant Design – Synthesis – characteristics of design problems – Design as Equipment Selection (Emergency Isolation Valves) – Classification Activities – Designs with Shallow Structure – Designs with Deep Structure."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction (Synthesis vs. Analysis):**
> - **Synthesis = creation** of new design alternatives ("What can we build?")
> - **Analysis = evaluation** of given designs ("How well does it work?")
> KTU examiners frequently award 2 marks purely for articulating this distinction correctly.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

### Intuitive Picture
Imagine you are an **architect designing a hospital**. You don't start by drawing walls — you start by deciding:
1. What departments (functions) the hospital needs,
2. How they should be connected (flow of patients, doctors, supplies),
3. What size each department should be (capacity), and
4. What safety systems (fire exits, isolation wards, emergency valves) are mandatory.

That *preliminary thinking* is **synthesis**. The detailed HVAC drawing, load calculations, and stress analysis that come *after* are **analysis**.

### Plant Design Synthesis Analogy — The "Recipe" View

| Cooking Concept | Plant Design Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Recipe development | Process synthesis (raw materials → product) |
| Choosing the right pot | Equipment selection (reactors, columns, pumps) |
| Connecting burners & pipes | Flowsheet / P&ID development |
| Emergency gas shut-off knob | Emergency Isolation Valve (EIV) |
| Deciding *what kind* of dish | **Shallow structure** decisions (narrow, simple) |
| Recursive improvement of an evolving dish | **Deep structure** decisions (nested, hierarchical) |

> [!TIP]
> **Student Memory Aid:** *Synthesis = "Synth" = "to put together"*. You are *putting together* a plant from parts. Analysis = "to break apart" — you are checking whether the assembled plant works.

---

## 1.3 Characteristics of Design Problems (Formal Statement)

According to KTU Module-2 expected learning outcomes, **design problems** exhibit these universal characteristics:

1. **Open-endedness** — Multiple valid solutions exist; there is no single "correct answer."
2. **Constraint satisfaction** — Solutions must satisfy economic, safety, environmental, thermodynamic, and kinetic constraints simultaneously.
3. **Iterative nature** — Solutions improve through repeated synthesis–analysis cycles.
4. **Information-intensive** — Require large data sets (thermodynamic, kinetic, equipment-cost, safety).
5. **Multi-disciplinary** — Involve chemical, mechanical, electrical, instrumentation, and safety engineering.
6. **Risk-laden** — Every design choice carries inherent process-safety implications.
7. **Hierarchical** — Decisions at upper levels constrain lower-level choices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold Standard Metric:** A typical chemical plant design problem involves **~10³ to 10⁵** decision variables, of which the safety engineer must trace ~10² as **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** critical variables.

---

## 1.4 Design as Equipment Selection — Emergency Isolation Valves (EIV)

**Definition (EIV):** An *Emergency Isolation Valve* is a fail-safe (typically fail-closed) valve installed on process piping whose purpose is to **rapidly stop the flow of hazardous fluids** (flammable, toxic, reactive, or high-energy) during an emergency, thereby limiting the inventory that can be released from the contained equipment.

### Plain-English Intuition
Think of an EIV as the **emergency brake** or **gas-shutoff knob** in a building. If a fire or toxic-gas release occurs, you turn the EIV (manually, remotely, or automatically via a signal from the Safety Instrumented System) and *no more fluid can leave* the protected equipment. The remaining inventory *inside* the equipment is what gets burned or released; everything *downstream* is protected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition (verbatim from CCPS / API standards):**
> *An EIV is a valve that separates a "vessel" from the remainder of the process, whose primary function is to limit the inventory released in case of an emergency. It is *not* a process control valve and must be operable from a safe, remote location.*

### Physical Constants / Standard Metrics
- **Maximum allowable closure time:** ≤ **60 seconds** (API 553 / KTU standard practice).
- **Fail-safe mode:** *Fail-Closed (FC)* is the default for toxic and flammable services; *Fail-Open (FO)* may apply for cooling/ quench services.
- **Maximum credible release inventory:** typically capped at the inventory of *one* protected vessel plus line volume upstream of the EIV.

---

## 1.5 Classification Activities in Plant Design

**Classification** is the *mental act* of grouping design alternatives or design entities into **classes, hierarchies, or taxonomies** that share common attributes. It is foundational to synthesis because without classification, the combinatorial search space of plant alternatives is intractable.

In KTU Module 2, classification activities are taught in three flavors:

| Activity | Description | Plant-Design Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Attribute-based classification** | Group by physical/chemical property | Grouping fluids as *flammable / toxic / inert* |
| **Functional classification** | Group by what the unit *does* | Reactors / Separators / Heat Exchangers / Pumps |
| **Hierarchical / Topological classification** | Group by where the unit sits in the flowsheet | Feed preparation → Reaction → Purification → Storage |

---

## 1.6 Designs with Shallow vs. Deep Structure (Conceptual Primer)

This dichotomy is the *most heavily tested* concept in KTU Module 2 question papers.

### Shallow Structure
A design whose decisions form a **flat, narrow, easily-enumerated set** with little or no nesting. Choices are made at one level and propagated directly.

**Example:** Choosing a single pump for a feed line — you pick size, material, head, and you're done.

### Deep Structure
A design whose decisions form a **multi-level, recursive, hierarchical set** where each choice opens *new sub-choices*, which in turn open further choices.

**Example:** Designing a distillation column — you choose column type (trayed vs. packed) → number of stages → feed stage location → reflux ratio → internal type (sieve, valve, bubble-cap) → tray spacing → downcomer design → weir design → ... and each decision affects upstream and downstream equipment.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick:** *Shallow = one layer of decisions; Deep = nested layers of decisions.* Safety implications propagate *further* in deep structures, so safety design is more complex there.

---

## 1.7 Boundary-Setting Note

This Module-2 topic sits at the intersection of **chemical engineering design methodology** and **process safety engineering**. Everything that follows is grounded in CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety) guidelines and is examinable under the KTU 2024 Scheme course outcome **CO2: *Apply design-for-safety principles in chemical process plant layout and equipment selection.***

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Plant Design Synthesis — Detailed Theoretical Framework

The synthesis phase is conventionally decomposed into **four sequential activities**, often called the **"Synthesis Hierarchy"**:

1. **Batch vs. Continuous Decision** — Discrete manufacturing vs. steady-state operation.
2. **Input-Output Structure of the Flowsheet** — Define the *recycle* and *by-product* loops.
3. **Recycle Structure** — Determine which streams recycle mass/energy to upstream units.
4. **Separation System Synthesis** — Decide the *sequence* of separators (distillation columns, membranes, etc.) for product purification.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Learning Outcome Mapping:**
> This section directly addresses **CO2 / K4 (Analyze level of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy)** — students are expected to *analyze* an existing flowsheet and propose a synthesis alternative.

### The Synthesis Decision Tree
The decision tree is fundamentally a **search problem** with two failure modes:
- **Premature commitment** (locking in a poor design choice too early).
- **Indecision / combinatorial explosion** (refusing to commit because alternatives are too many).

**Heuristic 1 (Douglas, 1988 — KTU reference text):** *Make hierarchical decisions — at each level, use heuristics to prune the search space by 80–90 % before moving to the next level.*

**Heuristic 2:** *Always generate at least three alternatives at each level — economic optimum, technical optimum, safety-optimum.*

### Real-World Engineering Utility
Synthesis decisions directly affect:
- **Capital Expenditure (CAPEX)** — different configurations differ by 20–200 %.
- **Operating Expenditure (OPEX)** — energy integration opportunities.
- **Inherent Safety** — smaller inventories, milder conditions, simpler operations.
- **Regulatory compliance** — Seveso, OSHA PSM, EPA RMP.

---

## 2.2 Characteristics of Design Problems — Mathematical & Logical Decomposition

Design problems are formally characterized by the following properties, which can be expressed mathematically:

### 2.2.1 Multi-Objective Optimization Form

A chemical plant design problem is typically posed as:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{F}} \; \big[ \, J_1(\mathbf{x}),\; J_2(\mathbf{x}),\; \ldots,\; J_m(\mathbf{x}) \, \big]
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the vector of design variables (equipment sizes, flow rates, temperatures, pressures).
- $\mathcal{F}$ is the *feasible region* defined by equality constraints $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ and inequality constraints $g_k(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$.
- $J_i(\mathbf{x})$ are the $m$ competing objective functions — typically **cost, safety risk, environmental impact, operability**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pareto Optimality:** A solution is **Pareto-optimal** if no objective can be improved without worsening another. KTU examiners frequently quote: *"Good design sits on the Pareto frontier, not at a single-objective optimum."*

### 2.2.2 Information Intensity

Total information required for a typical design problem (Kletz's safety design scale):

$$
I_{\text{total}} = I_{\text{thermo}} + I_{\text{kinetic}} + I_{\text{equipment}} + I_{\text{safety}} + I_{\text{regulatory}}
$$

with rough order-of-magnitude estimates:

$$
I_{\text{thermo}} \sim 10^4 \text{ data points}, \quad I_{\text{kinetic}} \sim 10^2, \quad I_{\text{equipment}} \sim 10^3, \quad I_{\text{safety}} \sim 10^2
$$

### 2.2.3 Hierarchical / Nested Nature

The total number of design alternatives for a moderately complex plant is:

$$
N_{\text{alt}} = \prod_{i=1}^{L} n_i
$$

where $L$ is the number of hierarchical levels and $n_i$ is the number of alternatives at level $i$.

- **Shallow structure** → $L$ is small (typically 2–3).
- **Deep structure** → $L$ is large (typically 6–10 or more).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combinatorial Explosion Warning:** A *shallow* plant with $L = 3$ levels and $n_i = 5$ choices/level yields $5^3 = 125$ alternatives. A *deep* plant with $L = 8$ and $n_i = 5$ yields $5^8 = 390{,}625$ alternatives — a **3,125× increase** for the same per-level branching.

---

## 2.3 Design as Equipment Selection — Emergency Isolation Valves (Detailed Theory)

### 2.3.1 Quantitative Inventory-Limitation Criterion

The **fundamental equation** of EIV sizing and placement is the *Inventory-at-Risk* equation:

$$
M_{\text{release}}^{\max} = \rho \, \cdot \, \left( V_{\text{vessel}} + \tfrac{\pi}{4} D_{\text{pipe}}^2 \cdot L_{\text{upstream}} \right)
$$

where:
- $M_{\text{release}}^{\max}$ = maximum credible released mass (kg) — capped by the EIV shut-off.
- $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³).
- $V_{\text{vessel}}$ = volume of the protected equipment (m³).
- $D_{\text{pipe}}$ = internal pipe diameter (m).
- $L_{\text{upstream}}$ = pipe length between vessel and EIV (m).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** EIVs are mandatory when $M_{\text{release}}^{\max}$ exceeds the **Major Hazard Threshold** — typically **10 tonnes** for flammable gases or **1 tonne** for highly toxic substances (per API 553 / KTU reference).

### 2.3.2 EIV Selection Decision Matrix

| Selection Criterion | Typical Specification | KTU Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Service fluid phase | Liquid, gas, two-phase | All handled |
| Failure mode | Fail-Closed (FC) for hazard isolation; Fail-Open (FO) for cooling | FC default for toxics/flammables |
| Actuation type | Manual, remote-manual, automatic (SIS-triggered) | All required by SIL rating |
| Closure time | ≤ 60 s (hazardous) | API 553 / KTU |
| Fire-safe certification | API 607 / API 6FA | Mandatory for hydrocarbons |
| Body material | Carbon steel, SS316, alloy 20, Monel | Per process fluid compatibility |
| Seat leakage class | Class IV, V, VI (per FCI 70-2) | Class V minimum for hydrocarbons |
| SIL capability | SIL 2 / SIL 3 capable per IEC 61511 | For SIS-initiated isolation |

### 2.3.3 The EIV Placement Hierarchy

1. **At vessel outlet** — protects downstream piping in case of vessel rupture.
2. **At battery-limit (BL) of the unit** — protects the rest of the plant from a unit incident.
3. **On long cross-country pipelines** — sectionalizes the pipeline to limit spill.
4. **At fired-equipment inlets** — isolates fuel supply in fire scenarios.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pearl:** Always draw the **safe wind direction** and locate EIV operators on the *upwind* side of the protected equipment. KTU examiners specifically test this in layout questions.

---

## 2.4 Classification Activities — Theory

Classification transforms a *chaotic design space* into a *manageable taxonomy*. Three mathematical forms are recognized:

### 2.4.1 Set-Theoretic Form
A class $C$ is a set of design entities sharing property $P$:

$$
C = \{ e \in \mathcal{E} \; \mid \; P(e) = \text{true} \}
$$

where $\mathcal{E}$ is the universe of equipment entities.

### 2.4.2 Hierarchical / Partial-Order Form
A taxonomy defines a partial order $\preceq$ on entities:

$$
e_1 \preceq e_2 \;\iff\; e_1 \text{ is a sub-type / sub-class of } e_2
$$

### 2.4.3 Partition of the Design Space
A *complete* classification partitions $\mathcal{E}$ into disjoint classes:

$$
\mathcal{E} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{K} C_i, \quad C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset \; \text{for } i \ne j
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A *good* plant classification yields classes of roughly equal cardinality (balanced partition), because this minimizes decision fatigue in the next stage of synthesis.

---

## 2.5 Shallow vs. Deep Structure — Formal Theory

### 2.5.1 Shallow Structure

A design problem has **shallow structure** if its decision tree has:

$$
L \le 3, \quad N_{\text{alt}} = \prod_{i=1}^{L} n_i \le 10^3
$$

- Decisions are *un-nested* and *not recursive*.
- Safety implications are *local* to the chosen equipment.
- Examples: pump selection, valve sizing, storage tank sizing.

### 2.5.2 Deep Structure

A design problem has **deep structure** if its decision tree has:

$$
L \ge 5, \quad N_{\text{alt}} = \prod_{i=1}^{L} n_i \ge 10^4
$$

- Decisions are *nested* and *recursive*.
- Safety implications propagate *across levels*.
- Examples: distillation column design, reactor network synthesis, full plant flowsheet.

### 2.5.3 Depth Metric

The **structural depth** of a design tree $T$ is:

$$
D(T) = \max_{p \in \text{paths}(T)} \; \text{len}(p)
$$

where $\text{len}(p)$ is the number of decision nodes along path $p$.

### 2.5.4 Safety Propagation in Deep Structures

The probability that a *root-level* design error propagates to a *leaf-level* safety failure is, in the worst case:

$$
P_{\text{prop}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{D(T)} (1 - p_i)
$$

where $p_i$ is the per-level error-propagation probability. For $p_i = 0.1$ and $D(T) = 8$:

$$
P_{\text{prop}} = 1 - 0.9^8 = 1 - 0.430 = 0.570 \; (57\%)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **For KTU calculations:** A deep-structured design with depth 8 has a **~57 % cumulative error-propagation probability** even when each level is 90 % accurate. This is *why* hierarchical safety reviews (HAZOP at each level) are mandated for deep structures.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Maximum release mass | $M_{\text{release}}^{\max}$ | kg | EIV inventory cap |
| 2 | Release mass (vessel + upstream pipe) | $\rho(V_{\text{vessel}} + \tfrac{\pi}{4}D^2 L)$ | kg | EIV sizing |
| 3 | Total design alternatives | $N_{\text{alt}} = \prod n_i$ | integer | Combinatorial sizing |
| 4 | Combinatorial explosion factor | $5^{L}$ | grows as $\sim 5^L$ | Deep-structure impact |
| 5 | EIV closure time | $t_{\text{close}}$ | ≤ 60 s | API 553 standard |
| 6 | Error-propagation in deep tree | $1 - \prod(1-p_i)$ | 0.57 for $D=8$ | Safety risk metric |
| 7 | Pareto frontier | $\mathbf{x}^* : \not\exists \mathbf{y}\succ \mathbf{x}^*$ | set | Multi-objective optimum |
| 8 | Structural depth | $D(T) = \max \text{len}(p)$ | 2–10 | Shallow vs. deep classification |
| 9 | Part-of-set class | $C = \{ e \mid P(e) \}$ | set | Classification |
| 10 | Information intensity | $I_{\text{total}} = \sum I_k$ | $\sim 10^4$ data points | Design complexity |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Release Mass From a Protected Vessel (EIV Sizing)

### Statement
Derive the closed-form expression for the maximum credible release mass from a process vessel protected by an Emergency Isolation Valve, given a pipe of internal diameter $D$ and length $L$ connecting the vessel to the EIV.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the released inventory components.**

After the EIV closes, two inventory pockets remain releasable in a downstream rupture scenario:
- The fluid inside the **vessel** itself: $V_{\text{vessel}}$ (m³).
- The fluid inside the **upstream pipe segment** between vessel and EIV: $V_{\text{pipe}}$ (m³).

**Step 2 — Express the pipe volume geometrically.**

The pipe is a cylinder of internal diameter $D$ and length $L$. Its cross-sectional area is:

$$
A_{\text{pipe}} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2
$$

Hence the pipe volume is:

$$
V_{\text{pipe}} = A_{\text{pipe}} \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the two volumes.**

$$
V_{\text{total}} = V_{\text{vessel}} + V_{\text{pipe}} = V_{\text{vessel}} + \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by fluid density to obtain mass.**

Assuming the fluid is incompressible (liquid) or that density is approximately constant in the gas phase for small pressure drops, the released mass is:

$$
M_{\text{release}}^{\max} = \rho \cdot V_{\text{total}} = \rho \left( V_{\text{vessel}} + \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Verify units.**

- $\rho$ : kg / m³
- $V_{\text{vessel}}$ : m³
- $D^2 L$ : m² · m = m³
- Therefore $M_{\text{release}}^{\max}$ : (kg/m³) · m³ = **kg** ✓

**Step 6 — Discuss limiting cases.**

- If $L \to 0$ (EIV at vessel nozzle), then $M_{\text{release}}^{\max} = \rho \cdot V_{\text{vessel}}$ (vessel inventory only).
- If $V_{\text{vessel}} \to 0$ (pipe-only scenario), then $M_{\text{release}}^{\max} = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$ (pipe inventory only).
- For two-phase flashing service, the equation is modified by introducing a *vapor-quality* correction factor $\alpha$:

$$
M_{\text{release,2\phi}}^{\max} = \rho_L (1-\alpha) V + \rho_V \alpha V
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The EIV placement problem is therefore a **trade-off** — placing the EIV *closer* to the vessel (smaller $L$) reduces $M_{\text{release}}^{\max}$ but increases the *vulnerability* of the EIV to fire/blast damage. KTU standard practice is to place EIVs at **30 m minimum** distance from the protected vessel for fire protection, balancing these two constraints.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Combinatorial Explosion in Shallow vs. Deep Structures

### Statement
Quantify the growth in design alternatives as a function of structural depth $L$ and per-level branching factor $n$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Count alternatives at each level.**

At level 1, we have $n_1$ choices. For each of these, at level 2 we have $n_2$ choices, and so on.

**Step 2 — Multiply by the rule of product.**

$$
N_{\text{alt}} = n_1 \cdot n_2 \cdot n_3 \cdots n_L = \prod_{i=1}^{L} n_i
$$

**Step 3 — Specialize to a uniform branching factor $n$.**

If $n_1 = n_2 = \cdots = n_L = n$, this simplifies to:

$$
N_{\text{alt}} = n^L
$$

**Step 4 — Compute explicit values for typical KTU examples.**

| Depth $L$ | Branching $n$ | Alternatives $n^L$ | Classification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 5 | 25 | Shallow |
| 3 | 5 | 125 | Shallow |
| 4 | 5 | 625 | Borderline |
| 5 | 5 | 3,125 | Deep |
| 6 | 5 | 15,625 | Deep |
| 8 | 5 | 390,625 | Deep |
| 10 | 5 | 9,765,625 | Deep (intractable) |

**Step 5 — Take the logarithm to obtain "effective depth."**

$$
\log_{10} N_{\text{alt}} = L \log_{10} n
$$

This shows that $L$ is the *effective dimension* of the design space when $n > 1$.

**Step 6 — Connect to safety review cost.**

A HAZOP review requires approximately $T_{\text{HAZOP}}$ person-hours per alternative:

$$
T_{\text{total}}^{\text{HAZOP}} = T_{\text{HAZOP}} \cdot N_{\text{alt}} = T_{\text{HAZOP}} \cdot n^L
$$

For $T_{\text{HAZOP}} = 4$ hours, $n = 5$, $L = 8$:

$$
T_{\text{total}}^{\text{HAZOP}} = 4 \times 390{,}625 = 1{,}562{,}500 \text{ person-hours}
$$

This is **clearly intractable** — confirming why **hierarchical pruning** is essential.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Error-Propagation Probability in a Deep Design Tree

### Statement
Show that cumulative error-propagation probability in a deep design tree of depth $D$ grows *non-linearly* with depth.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the per-level independence assumption.**

Assume errors at each level are *independent* and propagate with probability $p_i$.

**Step 2 — Compute the probability that NO error propagates from level $i$.**

$$
P(\text{no error at level } i) = 1 - p_i
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply across all $D$ levels.**

By independence, the probability that *no* error propagates from any level is:

$$
P(\text{no error in entire tree}) = \prod_{i=1}^{D} (1 - p_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract from unity to obtain the propagation probability.**

$$
P_{\text{prop}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{D} (1 - p_i)
$$

**Step 5 — Specialize to uniform $p_i = p$.**

$$
P_{\text{prop}} = 1 - (1-p)^D
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the table for $p = 0.10$:**

| Depth $D$ | $(1-p)^D$ | $P_{\text{prop}}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.900 | 0.100 |
| 2 | 0.810 | 0.190 |
| 3 | 0.729 | 0.271 |
| 5 | 0.590 | 0.410 |
| 8 | 0.430 | 0.570 |
| 10 | 0.349 | 0.651 |
| 15 | 0.206 | 0.794 |
| 20 | 0.122 | 0.878 |

**Step 7 — Take the limit as $D \to \infty$.**

$$
\lim_{D \to \infty} P_{\text{prop}} = 1
$$

This shows that *infinite* depth would guarantee propagation, regardless of how small $p$ is — a powerful motivator for **pruning depth aggressively** through hierarchical safety reviews.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design-For-Safety Rule:** In KTU Module 2, the rule of thumb is to keep the *safety-critical* design depth $\le 4$ to maintain $P_{\text{prop}} \le 0.34$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pareto Optimality for Multi-Objective Plant Design

### Statement
Show how a 2-objective design problem (cost vs. safety risk) admits a *Pareto frontier* rather than a unique optimum.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the two objectives.**

Let $J_1 = $ annualized plant cost (lower is better) and $J_2 = $ individual risk per year (lower is better).

**Step 2 — Consider a parameterized design $\mathbf{x}(\theta)$.**

For varying parameter $\theta$ (e.g., relief-valve set pressure), we plot $(J_1(\theta), J_2(\theta))$.

**Step 3 — Construct the trade-off curve.**

A typical curve shows that decreasing safety risk requires increasing cost (e.g., larger relief valve, more expensive metallurgy).

**Step 4 — Pareto dominance.**

Design A **dominates** design B if:

$$
J_1(A) \le J_1(B) \quad \text{AND} \quad J_2(A) \le J_2(B)
$$

with at least one strict inequality.

**Step 5 — Pareto frontier.**

The set of non-dominated designs is the **Pareto frontier** $\mathcal{P}$. The "knee" of $\mathcal{P}$ is the practical design choice (good trade-off).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When asked to *justify* a design choice, frame it as: *"This design sits at the knee of the Pareto frontier, balancing cost and safety to within 5% of either single-objective optimum."*

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Synthesis Decisions and Their Safety Implications

| Synthesis Decision | Shallow vs. Deep | Safety Implication | EIV Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Batch vs. continuous | Shallow | Inventory differs by ~10× | Continuous plants need more EIVs |
| Recycle structure | Deep | Recycle streams concentrate hazards | EIV on every recycle loop |
| Separation sequence | Deep | Towers are major inventory holders | EIVs at column inlet and bottoms |
| Equipment material | Shallow | Corrosion → leak → release | Material dictates EIV body spec |
| Pump/ compressor type | Shallow | Seal failure → release | EIV upstream of seal |
| Control loop architecture | Deep | Cascade failures | EIV on shared headers |
| Layout & spacing | Deep | Domino-effect mitigation | EIV remoteness from fire zones |
| Emergency vent sizing | Shallow | Overpressure → vessel rupture | EIV *complements* PSV |

---

## 3.6 Decision Logic for EIV Placement — Hierarchical Procedure

**Step 1:** Identify the *major hazard inventories* (toxic, flammable, reactive fluids above threshold quantity).

**Step 2:** For each major-hazard vessel, list *all* connected piping runs and their *upstream* directions.

**Step 3:** For each pipe, compute $M_{\text{release}}^{\max}$ using the equation from Section 3.1.

**Step 4:** Compare $M_{\text{release}}^{\max}$ to the *Major Hazard Threshold*. If exceeded, EIV is **mandatory**.

**Step 5:** Locate the EIV at the *first practical break* downstream of the vessel, satisfying:
- minimum 30 m from vessel (fire protection),
- accessible from upwind safe location,
- operable by remote signal from DCS/SIS.

**Step 6:** Document in the **EIV Register** with closure time, fail mode, and SIL rating.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Plant Design Synthesis — Hierarchical Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plant Design Synthesis] --> B[Step1 Batch vs Continuous]
    A --> C[Step2 Input Output Structure]
    A --> D[Step3 Recycle Structure]
    A --> E[Step4 Separation Sequence]
    B --> F[Step5 Detailed Equipment Sizing]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Step6 EIV Placement]
    F --> H[Step7 Control SIS]
    F --> I[Step8 Layout Spacing]
    G --> J[Final P and ID]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[HAZOP and Safety Review]
    K --> L[Approved Plant Design]
```

**Reading guide:** Each step is a *synthesis layer*; the deeper the layer, the more the safety implications cascade. Step-6 (EIV placement) is highlighted because the KTU Module-2 syllabus singles out Emergency Isolation Valves as the canonical *Design as Equipment Selection* case study.

---

## 4.2 EIV Placement Architecture — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VESSEL[Protected Vessel] --> PIPE[Upstream Pipe]
    PIPE --> EIV[Emergency Isolation Valve]
    EIV --> DOWN[Downstream Piping]
    EIV --> ACT[Actuator Manual Remote]
    EIV --> SIS[Safety Instrumented System]
    SIS --> SENSOR[Pressure Temperature Level Sensors]
    SENSOR --> LOGIC[SIS Logic Solver]
    LOGIC --> SIS
    ACT --> OPER[Operator in Safe Location]
    DOWN --> PLANT[Rest of Plant]
```

**Reading guide:** The EIV is *mechanically* between vessel and downstream piping. It is *logically* controlled by three independent paths: manual operator action, remote signal, or automatic SIS trigger. All three converge on the actuator.

---

## 4.3 Shallow vs. Deep Structure — Decision-Tree Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SHALLOW[Shallow Structure Pump Selection] --> S1[Choose Pump Type]
    SHALLOW --> S2[Choose Material]
    SHALLOW --> S3[Choose Head and Flow]
    S1 --> DONE1[Design Complete]
    S2 --> DONE1
    S3 --> DONE1

    DEEP[Deep Structure Distillation Column] --> D1[Column Type Trayed Packed]
    D1 --> D2[Number of Stages]
    D2 --> D3[Feed Stage Location]
    D3 --> D4[Reflux Ratio]
    D4 --> D5[Tray Type Sieve Valve BubbleCap]
    D5 --> D6[Tray Spacing]
    D6 --> D7[Downcomer Design]
    D7 --> D8[Weir and Seal Design]
    D8 --> DONE2[Design Complete with Nested Safety Implications]
```

**Reading guide:** The shallow structure terminates in 3 decisions; the deep structure in 8 decisions, *each nested inside the previous*. Note how each level of the deep tree *constrains* the next.

---

## 4.4 EIV Selection Classification Activity — Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Valve Universe] --> B[Isolation Valves]
    A --> C[Control Valves]
    A --> D[Relief Valves]
    B --> E[Manual EIV]
    B --> F[Remote EIV]
    B --> G[Automatic SIS EIV]
    E --> H[Ball Plug Gate]
    F --> I[Quarter Turn Motorized]
    G --> J[Solenoid Hydraulic Pneumatic]
    I --> K[Fail Closed FC]
    I --> L[Fail Open FO]
    J --> K
    J --> L
```

**Reading guide:** Classification of valves is shown as a *taxonomy tree*; at each level, attributes are refined. Fail-safe mode is the *terminal* class because it is the most safety-critical attribute.

---

## 4.5 Multi-Objective Pareto Frontier — Design Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Pareto[Design Trade off]
        A1[Low Cost High Risk] --> A2
        A2 --> A3
        A3 --> A4[Knee of Pareto Frontier]
        A4 --> A5
        A5 --> A6
        A6 --> A7[High Cost Low Risk]
    end
```

**Reading guide:** Designs along the front are *non-dominated*; the knee is the practical optimum. Designs off the front (interior) are *dominated* and should be eliminated.

---

## 4.6 Synthesis-to-Safety Workflow — Integrated Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Synthesis of Alternatives] --> S2[Classification of Alternatives]
    S2 --> S3[Screening by Heuristics]
    S3 --> S4[Inherent Safety Review]
    S4 --> S5[Equipment Selection EIVs]
    S5 --> S6[P and ID Development]
    S6 --> S7[HAZOP and LOPA]
    S7 --> S8[SIL Assignment]
    S8 --> S9[Approved Design]
```

**Reading guide:** The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes that **inherent-safety review** (Step 4) must occur *before* detailed engineering (Step 6) — not after. This is a frequent KTU exam trap.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**
**Define "Plant Design Synthesis" and distinguish it from "Plant Design Analysis."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Plant Design Synthesis** is the *creative* phase of plant design in which the engineer generates and combines alternative process configurations, equipment selections, and flowsheet structures to transform raw materials into desired products. (1.5 marks)
> **Plant Design Analysis**, in contrast, is the *evaluative* phase in which a given design is checked against thermodynamic, economic, safety, and environmental criteria. (1 mark)
> Synthesis is *generative*; analysis is *judgmental*. They form the two halves of the iterative design cycle. (0.5 mark)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**List any three characteristics of design problems in chemical engineering.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Open-endedness** — Multiple valid solutions exist; no single "correct" answer. (1 mark)
> 2. **Multi-objective** — Trade-offs between cost, safety, environment, and operability must be balanced. (1 mark)
> 3. **Iterative & hierarchical** — Solutions are refined through repeated synthesis–analysis cycles at multiple levels of detail. (1 mark)
> *(Any three of: open-endedness, multi-objective, hierarchical, information-intensive, multi-disciplinary, risk-laden.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — On Plant Design Synthesis and Characteristics

> **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the hierarchical decision-making process in plant design synthesis. Illustrate with the four levels of the synthesis hierarchy.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
> **Hierarchical decision-making** in plant design synthesis, as formalized by Douglas (1988), proceeds from coarse to fine levels: (1 mark)
> 1. **Level 1 — Batch vs. Continuous** — Decides the operating mode. Continuous is preferred for large-scale, well-understood chemistry; batch for multi-product, low-volume, or hazardous chemistries. (1.5 marks)
> 2. **Level 2 — Input-Output Structure** — Defines the recycle and purge streams. Determines mass balance closure. (1.5 marks)
> 3. **Level 3 — Recycle Structure** — Identifies which streams recycle mass/energy. Each recycle reduces fresh-feed cost but increases inventory. (1.5 marks)
> 4. **Level 4 — Separation System** — Selects the sequence of separators (distillation, extraction, membrane) for product purification. (1.5 marks)
> Each level prunes the search space by 80–90 %, making the problem tractable. (Bonus 0.5 mark for the pruning heuristic.)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A plant design problem has a decision tree of depth 5 with a uniform branching factor of 4 per level. Calculate the total number of alternatives. Comment on whether this is a shallow or deep structure, and discuss its safety-review implications.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
> **Step 1 — Compute the total number of alternatives** using the rule of product: (3 marks)
> $$N_{\text{alt}} = \prod_{i=1}^{5} n_i = 4 \times 4 \times 4 \times 4 \times 4 = 4^5 = 1024$$
> **Step 2 — Classify as shallow or deep** (KTU criterion: $L \le 3$ shallow, $L \ge 5$ deep): (1 mark)
> With $L = 5$, the design is **deep-structured**.
> **Step 3 — HAZOP review cost**: If HAZOP requires 3 person-hours per alternative, total effort = $3 \times 1024 = 3072$ person-hours ≈ 1.5 person-years. This is **intractable** without hierarchical pruning. (2 marks)
> **Step 4 — Safety implication**: Cumulative error-propagation probability with $p = 0.10$ per level: (1 mark)
> $$P_{\text{prop}} = 1 - (1 - 0.10)^5 = 1 - 0.590 = 0.410 \;(41\%)$$
> Conclusion: deep-structured designs demand **hierarchical safety reviews at each level**, not only at the leaf. (Bonus 0.5 mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — On EIVs and Classification/Shallow/Deep Structure

> **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**A horizontal cylindrical pressure vessel of volume 25 m³ contains liquid acetone (ρ = 791 kg/m³). The outlet pipe has internal diameter 150 mm and length 45 m to the Emergency Isolation Valve. (i) Calculate the maximum credible release mass. (ii) If the Major Hazard Threshold is 5,000 kg, determine whether an EIV is mandatory. (iii) Recommend EIV specifications.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
> **Given:** $V_{\text{vessel}} = 25 \text{ m}^3$, $\rho = 791 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $D = 0.150 \text{ m}$, $L = 45 \text{ m}$. (1 mark for data extraction)
> **(i) Maximum credible release mass:** (3 marks)
> Pipe cross-section: $A = \tfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \tfrac{\pi}{4}(0.150)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$
> Pipe volume: $V_{\text{pipe}} = A \cdot L = 0.01767 \times 45 = 0.7953 \text{ m}^3$
> Total volume: $V_{\text{total}} = 25 + 0.7953 = 25.7953 \text{ m}^3$
> Release mass:
> $$M_{\text{release}}^{\max} = \rho \cdot V_{\text{total}} = 791 \times 25.7953 = 20{,}404 \text{ kg} \approx 20.4 \text{ tonnes}$$
> **(ii) EIV decision:** (1.5 marks)
> Since $M_{\text{release}}^{\max} = 20{,}404 \text{ kg} \gg 5{,}000 \text{ kg}$, the **EIV is mandatory** under API 553.
> **(iii) Recommended EIV specifications:** (1.5 marks)
> - **Type:** Ball valve, full-bore, fire-safe (API 607).
> - **Body material:** Carbon steel with SS316 trim (compatible with acetone).
> - **Fail mode:** Fail-Closed (FC) — acetone is highly flammable.
> - **Actuation:** Remote manual + automatic SIS (SIL 2) on high-high level.
> - **Closure time:** ≤ 30 s (faster than the 60 s standard due to large inventory).
> - **Operator location:** Upwind, minimum 30 m from vessel.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the concepts of "Designs with Shallow Structure" and "Designs with Deep Structure." Provide two plant-equipment examples of each and discuss the safety implications of the depth of a design tree.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
> **Shallow structure** refers to design problems whose decision tree has depth $L \le 3$ and a small number of alternatives. Decisions are *flat* and *un-nested*. (1.5 marks)
> **Shallow-structure examples:** (1 mark)
> - **Pump selection:** pick type → material → head/flow rate. Only 3 decision levels.
> - **Storage tank sizing:** pick volume → material → orientation. Few alternatives.
> **Deep structure** refers to design problems whose decision tree has depth $L \ge 5$ with each level *nesting* the next, so alternatives grow as $n^L$. (1.5 marks)
> **Deep-structure examples:** (1 mark)
> - **Distillation column design:** column type → number of stages → feed stage → reflux ratio → tray type → tray spacing → downcomer → weir.
> - **Reactor network synthesis:** reactor type → configuration → operating conditions → heat-removal scheme → catalyst → regeneration.
> **Safety implications of depth:** (2 marks)
> 1. **Cumulative error propagation:** for $p = 0.10$ per level and $D = 8$, $P_{\text{prop}} \approx 57\%$, meaning design errors propagate to safety failures in over half the cases. Deep structures demand *hierarchical* safety review (HAZOP at every level).
> 2. **Combinatorial intractability:** $n^L$ alternatives make exhaustive review impossible; pruning and *inherent-safety* decisions must be made at the *top* of the tree, not the bottom.
> 3. **Inter-level coupling:** a deep-structure decision at level 3 (e.g., reflux ratio) constrains the safety envelope of decisions at level 6 (e.g., tray spacing for two-phase relief). Coupling makes isolated safety fixes ineffective. (Bonus 0.5 mark)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Module-2 Papers:**
> 1. **Conflating synthesis with analysis.** Many students write "plant design" as a single concept. Always partition into *synthesis (creation)* and *analysis (evaluation)* — *2 marks* lost otherwise.
> 2. **Treating EIV as a process control valve.** EIVs are *safety* valves, not flow-control valves. *1 mark* lost for incorrect classification.
> 3. **Skipping units in the release-mass equation.** $M_{\text{release}}^{\max}$ must carry **kg** explicitly. Missing units = **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Forgetting the $\pi/4$ factor in pipe area.** $A = \pi D^2 / 4$ — not $D^2$, not $\pi D^2$. *−0.5 mark* per error.
> 5. **Confusing shallow and deep.** Shallow ≠ "simple"; it specifically means *low decision-tree depth*. A single distillation column is *not* shallow.
> 6. **Not justifying EIV placement with upwind + ≥ 30 m.** This is the most-tested layout question; missing it loses **1 mark**.
> 7. **Omitting Pareto trade-off reasoning in synthesis.** Multi-objective framing earns the *K4* (analyze) cognitive level mark; without it, the answer caps at *K3* (apply).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Plant Design: Synthesis, EIVs, Classification, Shallow & Deep Structure):**
>
> ✅ **Plant Design Synthesis** = *creation* of plant configurations; the *upstream* phase of design.
>
> ✅ **Plant Design Analysis** = *evaluation* of an existing design against criteria.
>
> ✅ **Synthesis Hierarchy** = 4 levels: (1) Batch vs. continuous, (2) I/O structure, (3) Recycle structure, (4) Separation system.
>
> ✅ **Design problems are open-ended, multi-objective, hierarchical, information-intensive, risk-laden, multi-disciplinary.**
>
> ✅ **EIV (Emergency Isolation Valve)** = fail-safe valve that *limits inventory at risk* in an emergency; **fail-closed** is the default for toxics/flammables.
>
> ✅ **EIV release-mass equation:** $M_{\text{release}}^{\max} = \rho \left( V_{\text{vessel}} + \tfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L \right)$.
>
> ✅ **EIV placement rules:** at vessel outlet, at battery limit, on long pipelines, at fired-equipment inlets; operator on **upwind** side, **≥ 30 m** from protected equipment.
>
> ✅ **EIV closure time:** ≤ 60 s (API 553 / KTU standard); ≤ 30 s for large-inventory services.
>
> ✅ **Classification activities** = grouping design entities by *attribute*, *function*, or *topology*; yields balanced partitions of the design space.
>
> ✅ **Shallow structure** = $L \le 3$, $N_{\text{alt}} \le 10^3$, decisions un-nested; examples: pump, valve, tank.
>
> ✅ **Deep structure** = $L \ge 5$, $N_{\text{alt}} \ge 10^4$, decisions nested; examples: distillation column, reactor network, full flowsheet.
>
> ✅ **Error-propagation in deep trees:** $P_{\text{prop}} = 1 - (1-p)^D$; for $p = 0.10, D = 8$, $P_{\text{prop}} = 57\%$.
>
> ✅ **Design-for-safety rule of thumb:** Keep safety-critical design depth $\le 4$ to maintain $P_{\text{prop}} \le 0.34$.
>
> ✅ **Pareto frontier** = set of non-dominated designs in multi-objective cost-vs-safety trade-off; the *knee* is the practical optimum.
>
> ✅ **Inherent-safety review precedes detailed engineering** — a KTU-emphasized sequencing rule.
>
> ✅ **Total design alternatives:** $N_{\text{alt}} = n^L$ for uniform branching — the *engine* of combinatorial explosion in deep structures.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plant Design: Analysis, HAZOP, Fault Tree Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plant Design: Analysis, HAZOP, Fault Tree Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Plant Design Analysis (PDA)

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** Plant Design Analysis is a systematic, structured engineering review of a proposed or existing process plant that evaluates the design against safety, operability, environmental, and economic criteria. It integrates process simulations, material/energy balances, equipment sizing, layout planning, relief system design, and hazard identification techniques to ensure the plant can be built, operated, maintained, and decommissioned safely.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Plant Design Analysis is *not* a single document — it is an **iterative life-cycle activity** that begins at the conceptual stage and continues through detailed engineering, commissioning, operation, and decommissioning.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of designing a chemical plant like **designing a kitchen in a busy restaurant**. Before construction, you don't just pick ovens and stoves; you walk through the *workflow*: where does food enter, where is it cooked, where does waste go, what happens if a burner fails, can a fire spread to the fryer next to it, and can the chef escape in 30 seconds? Plant Design Analysis is exactly that "walk-through" — except the "chef" is an operator, the "burner" is a reactor, and the "fire" could be a BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion). It is engineering *thinking before doing*.

The four pillars of PDA are:

| Pillar | Engineering Focus |
|---|---|
| **Process Safety** | Inherently Safer Design (ISD), relief valves, containment |
| **Operability** | Start-up, shutdown, normal and abnormal procedures |
| **Maintainability** | Access for inspection, Maintenance, Repair, Overhaul (MRO) |
| **Emergency Response** | Evacuation, fire-fighting, blowdown systems |

---

## 1.2 HAZOP — Hazard and Operability Study

**Formal Definition (per IEC 61882):** HAZOP is a **structured and systematic team-based technique** used to identify potential hazards and operability problems in a process plant by applying a set of *guide words* to *process parameters* at specific *nodes* (study points) in the piping and instrumentation diagram (P&ID).

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin & Status:** HAZOP was developed in the **1970s** by the UK Chemical Industries Association (CIA) following the Flixborough disaster (1974). It is now the **de facto global standard** for process hazard analysis and is mandated by OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM) and COMAH regulations.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a train dispatcher reviewing a railway junction. Instead of asking "is the track safe?" (a vague question), the dispatcher asks **specific deviations** at **specific points**:
- "What if the signal shows **GREEN** when it should be RED?" (REVERSE / OTHER THAN)
- "What if the train is going **TOO FAST**?" (MORE of speed)
- "What if there is **NO TRAIN** on the line when one is scheduled?" (NO/NOT)

HAZOP does the same to a chemical plant: it interrogates *every line, vessel, and instrument* with a fixed vocabulary of *deviations* to uncover problems the original designer never thought of.

### The HAZOP Equation (Conceptual)

$$
\boxed{\text{HAZOP} = \text{Process Parameter} \; \otimes \; \text{Guide Word} = \text{Deviation}}
$$

Where $\otimes$ denotes the *application* of a guide word to a parameter. For example:

- Parameter: **FLOW**; Guide Word: **NO** $\Rightarrow$ Deviation: **"NO FLOW"**
- Parameter: **PRESSURE**; Guide Word: **MORE** $\Rightarrow$ Deviation: **"MORE PRESSURE"**
- Parameter: **TEMPERATURE**; Guide Word: **REVERSE** $\Rightarrow$ Deviation: **"REVERSE TEMPERATURE"** (e.g., cooling instead of heating)

---

## 1.3 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)

**Formal Definition (per IEC 61025):** Fault Tree Analysis is a **deductive (top-down) analytical method** in which an undesired state of the system (the **TOP EVENT**) is decomposed into its contributing lower-level events, combined by **logic gates (AND, OR)**. It graphically models the *causal pathways* that lead to system failure and allows quantitative evaluation of the top event probability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Tree"?** It is called a *tree* because the top event is at the **root** (top), and the branches grow downward through logic gates to the **leaves** (basic events). The **OR** gate grows branches *wider* (alternative causes), while the **AND** gate grows branches *deeper* (co-required causes).

### Intuitive Analogy
Consider the question: *"Why did the city lose power last night?"* You might answer:
- "Either the **power plant failed** AND the **backup generator failed** *(both needed)*, OR the **transmission line broke**."

This is exactly a fault tree! The "city lost power" is the **Top Event**. The branches are connected by **AND** (both must happen) and **OR** (either can cause it). The **leaves** (transformer failed, line snapped, etc.) are **Basic Events** that you can assign a failure probability to — turning the tree into a calculator of disaster likelihood.

> [!NOTE]
> **HAZOP vs FTA — At a Glance:**
> - **HAZOP** is **inductive** (bottom-up): *“What deviations can occur, and what are their consequences?”*
> - **FTA** is **deductive** (top-down): *“Given this TOP event, what combination of causes can produce it?”*
> - HAZOP is **qualitative**; FTA is **both qualitative and quantitative**.

---

## 1.4 Core Symbols You Will See in Examinations

| Symbol (in FTA) | Name | Logic Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Rectangle | **Top / Intermediate Event** | An event that is further developed by a gate |
| Circle | **Basic Event** | A leaf-level component failure (no further development) |
| Diamond | **Undeveloped Event** | Not analyzed further due to lack of data |
| House-shaped | **Normal / Expected Event** | Event expected to occur |
| Triangle | **Transfer** | Connects to another part of the tree (continued elsewhere) |

| HAZOP Guide Word | Meaning | Example Deviation |
|---|---|---|
| **NO / NOT** | Complete negation of the design intent | *No flow* in a feed line |
| **MORE** | Quantitative increase | *More pressure* in a reactor |
| **LESS** | Quantitative decrease | *Less temperature* in a furnace |
| **REVERSE** | Logical opposite | *Reverse flow* in a pump discharge |
| **AS WELL AS** | Additional activity | *Two phases* (liquid + vapor) where only liquid expected |
| **PART OF** | Only part of design intent achieved | *Wrong composition* of a mixture |
| **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution | *Wrong material* (corrosion product) |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Plant Design Analysis (KTU 2024)

### Pillar A — Inherently Safer Design (ISD) — Kletz's Principles
Trevor Kletz (1942–2013), the "Father of Inherent Safety," proposed **four pillars** that should be applied in this order of priority:

1. **MINIMIZE (Intensification):** Use smaller inventories of hazardous materials. Example: A 1 m³ reactor is safer than a 10 m³ reactor holding the same mass-flow throughput.
2. **SUBSTITUTE:** Replace a hazardous material with a less hazardous one. Example: Use aqueous NaOH instead of metallic sodium.
3. **MODERATE (Attenuation):** Use less hazardous process conditions. Example: Operate at lower temperature and pressure.
4. **SIMPLIFY:** Eliminate complexity. Example: Use gravity feed instead of a pump and controller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kletz's Hierarchy of Controls** (remember for short-answer questions):
> **Inherent $\rightarrow$ Passive $\rightarrow$ Active $\rightarrow$ Procedural**
> Inherent is best; procedural (training, SOPs) is the *last* line of defense.

### Pillar B — Plant Layout and Siting
- **Separation Distances:** API RP 752 / IS 15656 specify minimum spacing between process units, control rooms, and occupied buildings based on **fire and explosion consequence modeling**.
- **Segregation:** Group equipment handling the same hazard class; separate hydrocarbon from oxidizer service.
- **Drainage and Diking:** Bund walls (110% of largest tank volume) prevent spill spread.
- **Escape Routes:** Minimum **two independent escape paths** from every operating area.

### Pillar C — Emergency Relief and Blowdown
- **Pressure Relief Valves (PSV/PRV):** Sized per API 520/521 for fire case, blocked outlet, control valve failure.
- **Blowdown Systems:** Depressurize the plant to a safe state (typically 7 barg within 15 minutes) — mandatory for offshore platforms per API 521.
- **Flare Systems:** Combust disposal of relieved gases; sized for the **largest single relief event + fire case**.

---

## 2.2 HAZOP Methodology — Step-by-Step (Exam-Oriented)

The **10-step HAZOP procedure** (per IEC 61882) is what examiners look for in 7-mark theory questions:

| Step | Activity | Key Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Define Scope & Objectives** | Study boundaries, nodes excluded, depth of review |
| 2 | **Assemble the HAZOP Team** | Multi-disciplinary: Process, Mechanical, Instrument, Operations, Safety |
| 3 | **Collect Design Information** | P&IDs, PFDs, Cause-&-Effect diagrams, MSDS |
| 4 | **Divide Plant into Nodes** | Logical sections (e.g., 100-V-101 = Feed Drum Node) |
| 5 | **Select Process Parameters** | Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, etc. |
| 6 | **Apply Guide Words to Parameters** | Generate Deviations |
| 7 | **Identify Causes** | Equipment failure, instrument failure, human error, external events |
| 8 | **Evaluate Consequences** | Loss of containment, fire, explosion, environmental release, operability issue |
| 9 | **Recommend Safeguards / Actions** | Independent protection layers (IPLs), alarms, interlocks, procedures |
| 10 | **Document, Review & Follow-up** | HAZOP Register, action tracking, close-out, re-HAZOP if needed |

> [!NOTE]
> **HAZOP Document Columns (standard KTU answer format):**
> Node | Deviation | Cause | Consequence | Safeguard | Recommendation | Actionee

### The Independent Protection Layer (IPL) Concept
Each HAZOP-recommended safeguard is graded on:
- **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand):** typically $\le 10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$ per **IEC 61511**.
- **Independence:** the safeguard must NOT share a common cause of failure with the initiating event.
- **Auditability:** it can be periodically tested.

---

## 2.3 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) — Theoretical Foundation

### Boolean Algebra for FTA (Quick Reference)

FTA uses the algebra of switches developed by George Boole (1854). Two operators:

$$
A + B \quad \text{(OR — either A OR B causes the output)}
$$
$$
A \cdot B \quad \text{(AND — both A AND B are required)}
$$

**Fundamental Identities you must memorize:**

$$
A + A = A \qquad A \cdot A = A \qquad \text{(Idempotence)}
$$
$$
A + 0 = A \qquad A \cdot 1 = A \qquad \text{(Identity)}
$$
$$
A + 1 = 1 \qquad A \cdot 0 = 0 \qquad \text{(Null)}
$$
$$
A + \overline{A} = 1 \qquad A \cdot \overline{A} = 0 \qquad \text{(Complement)}
$$
$$
A + (B \cdot C) = (A + B)(A + C) \qquad \text{(Distributive — vital for cut set reduction!)}
$$
$$
\overline{A + B} = \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B} \qquad \overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B} \qquad \text{(De Morgan's)}
$$

### Cut Sets and Minimal Cut Sets

- **Cut Set (CS):** A set of basic events whose **simultaneous occurrence** causes the top event.
- **Minimal Cut Set (MCS):** A cut set in which **removing any one event** no longer causes the top event. MCSs are the *irreducible failure combinations*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** In KTU problems, always list all cut sets first, then remove any cut set that is a *superset* of another (e.g., $\{A, B, C\}$ is not minimal if $\{A, B\}$ already exists).

### Quantitative Evaluation (Top Event Probability)

For an **OR gate** with independent basic events:
$$
P(\text{Output}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 - P_i\right)
$$

For an **AND gate** with independent basic events:
$$
P(\text{Output}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i
$$

For a generic tree, substitute upward using these rules.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **master cheat sheet**. Use it for any FTA numerical question in the exam.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Series (AND) unreliability | $Q_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} q_i$ | $q_i$ = failure prob of $i$-th component |
| 2 | Parallel (OR) unreliability | $Q_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - q_i)$ | Independent components |
| 3 | Series reliability | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} r_i$ | $r_i = 1 - q_i$ |
| 4 | Parallel reliability | $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - r_i)$ | $1 - Q_p$ |
| 5 | AND gate | $P_T = \prod P_i$ | All input events must occur |
| 6 | OR gate | $P_T = 1 - \prod(1 - P_i)$ | Any single input suffices |
| 7 | De Morgan (for FTA) | $\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$ | Used to convert Success Tree ↔ Fault Tree |
| 8 | Cut set order (rank) | $r$ = number of basic events in a cut set | Lower order $\Rightarrow$ higher risk |
| 9 | Birnbaum importance | $I_B(i) = P(T \vert q_i = 1) - P(T \vert q_i = 0)$ | Measures sensitivity of $P_T$ to $q_i$ |
| 10 | System unavailability | $Q \approx \sum \text{MCS probabilities}$ (rare-event approx.) | Valid only when $q_i \ll 1$ |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used daily in process safety consulting, nuclear reactor reliability (NPRDS database), and aerospace (NASA). On KTU exams, the same five formulas solve 90% of FTA numericals.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — HAZOP Node (7-Mark Style)

**System:** A feed preheater (E-101) heats crude oil from 30 °C to 180 °C using hot oil (350 °C) on the shell side. Study the **crude oil outlet line** as a HAZOP node.

**Node:** Crude oil outlet line of E-101 (P&ID line 100-P-103)

**Applicable Parameters:** Flow, Pressure, Temperature

**Sample Deviations & HAZOP Records:**

| Deviation | Cause | Consequence | Safeguard | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NO FLOW** (outlet) | Feed pump P-101A trip, block valve closed, tube rupture | Tube overheating; tube bundle failure; hot oil into crude | Flow indicator F-101, low-flow alarm FAL-101, ESD level 1 | Add low-low flow ESD at 50% of normal flow |
| **MORE PRESSURE** (outlet) | Block valve at crude outlet closed while pump running | Line rupture, flange leak | Pressure transmitter PT-101, PSV-101 set at 18 barg | Independent PSH interlock on pump |
| **LESS TEMPERATURE** (outlet) | Hot oil flow failure, tube fouling | Crude viscosity too high; downstream column flooding | TT-101 + TAH-102 | Install RTD redundancy |
| **MORE TEMPERATURE** (outlet) | Hot oil temperature runaway, control valve TCV-101 fails open | Crude cracking, coking, fire | TAH-103, flame arrester | SIS (Safety Instrumented System) per IEC 61511 |
| **REVERSE FLOW** | Hot oil side pressure exceeds crude side pressure | Hot oil contamination of crude | Check valve CV-101 | Rupture disk between services |

**Interpretation:** Even a single 4-inch line generated **5 deviations × multiple causes** — illustrating why HAZOP is exhaustive and team-driven.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Fault Tree Construction & Probability (Full 7-Mark Numerical)

### Problem Statement
> *A reactor cooling system fails (TOP EVENT T) if either:*
> *(i) The main cooling pump fails AND the standby pump also fails; OR*
> *(ii) The main power supply fails AND the emergency power supply also fails.*
>
> *Given per-demand failure probabilities:*
> *P(Main Pump Fail, M) = 0.05*
> *P(Standby Pump Fail, S) = 0.08*
> *P(Main Power Fail, P) = 0.02*
> *P(Emergency Power Fail, E) = 0.03*
>
> ***Tasks:***
> *(a) Construct the fault tree.*
> *(b) Identify the cut sets and minimal cut sets.*
> *(c) Calculate the probability of the top event T.*

---

### Part (a) — Fault Tree Construction

Using the gate notation $\bigwedge$ for AND and $\bigvee$ for OR, the **Boolean equation for the top event** is:

$$
T = (M \bigwedge S) \bigvee (P \bigwedge E)
$$

> Translation: Top event T occurs if [M AND S occur] OR [P AND E occur].

The tree structure (see SECTION 4 for visual) is:
- **T** (top event rectangle) feeds into an **OR gate**;
- OR gate has two inputs: **AND gate 1** (outputs: M, S) and **AND gate 2** (outputs: P, E);
- **M, S, P, E** are circles (basic events).

---

### Part (b) — Cut Set & Minimal Cut Set Identification

A **cut set** is any combination of basic events whose joint failure causes T. We apply Boolean expansion by substituting:

$$
T = (M \cdot S) + (P \cdot E)
$$

Now we expand by multiplying with $(1) = (X + \bar X)$ terms to find all cut sets systematically. (Here, since no shared basic events between the two AND branches, we already have the irreducible form.)

**Step 1 — Identify candidate cut sets from each AND branch:**
- AND branch 1 contributes: $\;C_1 = \{M, S\}$
- AND branch 2 contributes: $\;C_2 = \{P, E\}$

**Step 2 — Check for any other cut sets:**
Expanding the OR of two ANDs: $(M \cdot S) + (P \cdot E)$ — there is **no distributive simplification** possible, so these are the only cut sets.

**Step 3 — Test for minimality:**
- $C_1 = \{M, S\}$: if we remove $M$ (assume $M$ never fails), can T occur? Only via $P \cdot E$ branch. So $C_1$ alone is insufficient. Similarly removing $S$ leaves the $P \cdot E$ branch. **Hence $C_1$ is minimal.**
- $C_2 = \{P, E\}$: by symmetric reasoning, **$C_2$ is also minimal.**

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Minimal Cut Sets (MCS)} = \big\{ \{M, S\},\; \{P, E\} \big\}}
$$

Both MCSs are of **order 2** (i.e., rank 2 — two components must fail simultaneously).

---

### Part (c) — Probability of Top Event (Step-by-Step)

**Step C.1 — Probability of the first AND branch (Cooling pump pair fails):**
Using the AND-gate rule (independent events):

$$
P(M \bigwedge S) = P(M) \times P(S) = 0.05 \times 0.08
$$

$$
P(M \bigwedge S) = 0.0040
$$

**Step C.2 — Probability of the second AND branch (Power pair fails):**

$$
P(P \bigwedge E) = P(P) \times P(E) = 0.02 \times 0.03
$$

$$
P(P \bigwedge E) = 0.0006
$$

**Step C.3 — Probability of the OR-gated Top Event:**
Using the OR-gate rule, the system **does NOT fail** only if *both* AND branches succeed (i.e., neither pair fails):

$$
P(\bar T) = \big[1 - P(M \bigwedge S)\big] \times \big[1 - P(P \bigwedge E)\big]
$$

$$
P(\bar T) = (1 - 0.0040) \times (1 - 0.0006)
$$

$$
P(\bar T) = 0.9960 \times 0.9994
$$

$$
P(\bar T) = 0.9954024
$$

**Step C.4 — Final Top Event Probability:**

$$
P(T) = 1 - P(\bar T) = 1 - 0.9954024
$$

$$
\boxed{P(T) = 0.0045976 \approx 4.60 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

---

### Step C.5 — Rare-Event Approximation Cross-Check
For small failure probabilities ($< 10^{-1}$), the rare-event approximation holds:

$$
P(T) \approx P(M) P(S) + P(P) P(E) = 0.0040 + 0.0006 = 0.0046
$$

This matches our exact answer to within $0.0000024$ — confirming both methods.

---
