# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]
**Define HAZOP. List any four guide words used in HAZOP with their meanings.**

**Model Answer:**

> **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** is a structured, systematic, team-based technique used to identify hazards and operability problems in a process plant. It applies a set of *guide words* (NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN) to *process parameters* (flow, pressure, temperature, level, composition) at specific *nodes* of the P&ID to generate deviations whose causes, consequences, and safeguards are then examined.
>
> **Guide Words:**
>
> | Guide Word | Meaning |
> |---|---|
> | NO / NOT | Complete negation of the design intent (e.g., No flow) |
> | MORE | Quantitative increase above design intent (e.g., More pressure) |
> | LESS | Quantitative decrease below design intent (e.g., Less temperature) |
> | REVERSE | Logical opposite of design intent (e.g., Reverse flow) |
>
> *(Any four guide words with brief meaning — full 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, June 2024 Pattern]
**Define Fault Tree Analysis. Distinguish between a basic event and a top event with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is a deductive, top-down analytical method that uses Boolean logic (AND, OR) to decompose an undesired system state (the **Top Event**) into its contributing lower-level events down to **basic events** whose failure probabilities are known.
>
> - **Top Event:** The undesired event at the root of the tree whose probability we want to estimate. *Example: "Reactor cooling system fails" (T).*
> - **Basic Event:** A leaf-level component failure for which no further development is performed. *Example: "Main cooling pump fails" (M) with $P = 0.05$.*
> - **Intermediate Event:** A sub-event linking top and basic events via logic gates. *Example: "Both pumps failed" (A = M ∧ S).*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer (Module 2 Internal Choice)

> **Instructions (KTU 2024 ESE Style):** *Answer ONE full question from this module. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each.*

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Q.A (a) — HAZOP Procedure [7 Marks]
**Explain the HAZOP study methodology in detail. State the roles of a typical HAZOP team.**

**Model Answer:**

The HAZOP procedure is executed in **10 systematic steps** (per IEC 61882):

1. **Define Scope and Objectives:** Specify plant boundaries, design intent, and depth of review.
2. **Assemble the Team:** Multi-disciplinary (see below).
3. **Collect Information:** P&IDs, PFDs, Cause-&-Effect diagrams, MSDS, operating procedures.
4. **Divide Plant into Nodes:** Logical sections (e.g., 100-V-101 — Feed Drum).
5. **Select Process Parameters:** Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, etc.
6. **Apply Guide Words:** Combine parameter × guide word to generate deviations.
7. **Identify Causes:** Equipment failure, instrument failure, human error, external events.
8. **Evaluate Consequences:** Loss of containment, fire, explosion, operability issue, environmental release.
9. **Recommend Safeguards:** Independent Protection Layers (IPLs), alarms, interlocks, procedures.
10. **Document and Follow-up:** HAZOP Register, action tracking, close-out verification, re-HAZOP if design changes.

**Typical HAZOP Team Composition:**

| Role | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **HAZOP Leader / Chairman** | Facilitates, not an expert; guides methodology |
| **Process Engineer** | Explains design intent and P&IDs |
| **Operations Engineer** | Field experience, start-up / shutdown issues |
| **Instrument / Control Engineer** | Addresses control loops, interlocks, ESD |
| **Mechanical / Rotating Engineer** | Equipment, pumps, vessels integrity |
| **Safety Engineer** | Risk-matrix, IPLs, LOPA insights |
| **Scribe / Recorder** | Captures the HAZOP register live |
| *(Optional)* Project Manager, Commissioning Engineer, Environmental Specialist |

*(7 marks: 5 marks for procedure + 2 marks for team composition.)*

---

#### Q.A (b) — FTA Worked Problem [7 Marks]
**A pressure vessel ruptures (Top Event R) if either (i) overpressure occurs AND the PSV fails to open, OR (ii) corrosion thins the wall below limit AND inspection is missed. Given: P(overpressure) = 0.10, P(PSV fails) = 0.02, P(corrosion) = 0.15, P(inspection missed) = 0.20. (a) Construct the fault tree. (b) Find minimal cut sets. (c) Compute P(R).**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Boolean Equation:**

Let $O$ = Overpressure, $V$ = PSV fails, $C$ = Corrosion, $I$ = Inspection missed.

$$
R = (O \bigwedge V) \bigvee (C \bigwedge I)
$$

**Step 2 — Fault Tree (described):**
- R (top event) → OR gate.
- OR gate inputs: AND1 (with leaves O, V) and AND2 (with leaves C, I).

**Step 3 — Minimal Cut Sets:**
- AND branch 1: $\{O, V\}$ — removing either prevents failure (since the other branch could still cause R, but this MCS is irreducible).
- AND branch 2: $\{C, I\}$ — minimal.
- No common variables, so distributive expansion yields no new MCS.

$$
\boxed{\text{MCS} = \big\{ \{O, V\},\; \{C, I\} \big\}}
$$

**Step 4 — Probability of R:**

AND branch probabilities:

$$
P(O \bigwedge V) = 0.10 \times 0.02 = 0.0020
$$

$$
P(C \bigwedge I) = 0.15 \times 0.20 = 0.0300
$$

OR gate (system survival = both ANDs survive):

$$
P(\bar R) = (1 - 0.0020)(1 - 0.0300) = 0.9980 \times 0.9700 = 0.968060
$$

$$
P(R) = 1 - 0.968060 = 0.031940
$$

$$
\boxed{P(R) \approx 0.0319 \approx 3.19\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** The corrosion–inspection branch dominates the risk by an order of magnitude. The recommendation: improve inspection program (lower $P(I)$) rather than focus on PSV reliability.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### Q.B (a) — Fault Tree Methodology and Symbols [7 Marks]
**Explain the Fault Tree Analysis methodology. With neat sketches, describe the standard symbols used in FTA (events and gates).**

**Model Answer:**

**Methodology (5 steps):**
1. **Define the Top Event:** State the undesired system failure precisely.
2. **Construct the Tree:** Top-down, expand the top event into contributing events using AND/OR logic.
3. **Identify Basic Events:** Stop expansion when component-level events with known failure rates are reached.
4. **Solve Qualitatively:** Identify all cut sets and minimal cut sets (MCS).
5. **Solve Quantitatively:** Substitute failure probabilities upward using AND and OR rules; rank MCS by importance.

**Standard Symbols (FTA):**

| Symbol | Name | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Rectangle | Top / Intermediate Event | Event further developed by a gate |
| Circle | Basic Event | Leaf-level component failure |
| Diamond | Undeveloped Event | Not analyzed (insufficient data) |
| House-shape | Normal / Expected Event | Event expected to occur |
| Triangle | Transfer | Continuation to another page of the tree |
| AND-gate (flat D-shape) | All inputs required | Output occurs iff all inputs occur |
| OR-gate (curved shield) | Any input suffices | Output occurs if any input occurs |

*(7 marks: 3 for methodology, 2 for event symbols, 2 for gate symbols.)*

---

#### Q.B (b) — HAZOP Application on a Distillation Column Reboiler [7 Marks]
**Apply HAZOP study to the *steam inlet line of a distillation column reboiler*. List four deviations with their causes, consequences, and recommended safeguards.**

**Model Answer:**

**Node:** Steam inlet line to Reboiler E-201 (P&ID 200-SS-301)
**Parameters:** Flow, Pressure, Temperature

| # | Deviation | Cause | Consequence | Safeguard / Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **NO FLOW** (steam) | Steam supply header isolation, control valve TCV-201 fails closed, condensate trap blocked | Column bottoms starved; product off-spec; loss of vapour traffic | Flow indicator FI-201, FAL-201 low alarm, FFAL ESD-1 to trip feed |
| 2 | **MORE PRESSURE** (steam) | Block valve at reboiler outlet closed, upstream PRV stuck | Tube rupture, possible ATEX hazard | Pressure transmitter PT-201, PSV-201, PSHH interlock to steam block valve |
| 3 | **LESS TEMPERATURE** (steam) | Steam pressure drop upstream, partial flooding in steam chest, boiler upset | Reduced reboiling duty; high bottoms impurity | TT-201 + TAH alarm; interlock to steam PRV |
| 4 | **REVERSE FLOW** (steam) | Steam condensate blockage, line drain valve open | Process fluid backflow into steam system, contamination | Check valve CV-201; steam trap inspection schedule |

*(7 marks: 2 marks for procedure awareness + 5 marks for the table.)*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks (and how to avoid it):**
> 1. **Forgetting Boolean distributive law in MCS problems** — $(A+B)(A+C) = A+BC$. Examiners love this trap. *Tip: Always expand fully before claiming a cut set is "minimal."*
> 2. **Confusing HAZOP guide words with parameters.** "NO FLOW" is the *deviation*; "NO" is the *guide word*; "FLOW" is the *parameter*. Examiners mark zero if you mix them.
> 3. **In FTA numericals, students often compute $1 - P$ instead of the OR-gate formula.** Always: $P_{\text{OR}} = 1 - \prod(1 - P_i)$. For *n = 2*, it is $1 - (1-P_A)(1-P_B)$, **not** $1 - P_A - P_B$.
> 4. **Not converting "Reliability" to "Unreliability"** before applying formulas. If the problem gives $R = 0.95$, use $q = 0.05$.
> 5. **HAZOP answers without the HAZOP register format** lose structure marks. Always use a *table* with columns: Node | Deviation | Cause | Consequence | Safeguard | Recommendation.
> 6. **Drawing fault trees with arrows showing causal flow *upward*.** Arrows MUST go downward (top event → gates → basic events).
> 7. **In quantitative FTA, assuming rare events but not validating $q < 0.1$.** If $q > 0.1$, the rare-event approximation breaks down and the exact formula is mandatory.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Plant Design Analysis** = multi-disciplinary life-cycle review: Inherent → Passive → Active → Procedural.
- **Kletz's 4 Pillars of ISD** (in order): **Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify** — memorise the order.
- **HAZOP Equation:** Parameter ⊗ Guide Word = Deviation. Guide words: NO/NOT, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN.
- **HAZOP is inductive (bottom-up) and qualitative.** FTA is deductive (top-down) and can be quantitative.
- **HAZOP Team** = multi-disciplinary: Leader, Process, Operations, Instrument, Mechanical, Safety, Scribe.
- **HAZOP Document** = standard register with Node | Deviation | Cause | Consequence | Safeguard | Recommendation columns.
- **FTA top event** must be a *single, clearly defined* undesired state. Multiple top events = multiple trees.
- **FTA Symbols:** Rectangle (event), Circle (basic), Diamond (undeveloped), AND/OR gates.
- **Boolean Laws** you must remember: $A + A = A$, $A \cdot A = A$, De Morgan: $\overline{A + B} = \bar A \cdot \bar B$, Distributive: $A + BC = (A+B)(A+C)$.
- **AND-gate probability** = product of input probabilities. **OR-gate probability** = $1 - \prod(1 - P_i)$.
- **Cut Set** = set of basic events causing top event. **Minimal Cut Set** = cut set where removing any event prevents the top event.
- **Lower MCS rank = higher system risk.** A 1st-order MCS is a "single point of failure" and demands immediate attention.
- **Rare-Event Approximation:** Valid only when $q_i < 0.1$. Formula: $Q \approx \sum P_{\text{MCS}}$.
- **Kletz's Hierarchy:** Inherent > Passive > Active > Procedural (always climb up the pyramid first).
- **Standards you may cite in answers:** IEC 61882 (HAZOP), IEC 61025 (FTA), IEC 61511 (SIS), API 521 (Relief), OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM), IS 15656 (Plant Layout).
- **Common KTU trick:** "Find the top event probability" with multiple MCSs — apply OR-gate formula at the top, AND at each branch.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Procedures
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Operating Procedures

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Procedures** are formally documented, written instructions that comprehensively detail the steps, parameters, controls, and safety precautions required to safely start up, operate, shutdown, and perform routine and emergency operations on a process or facility. As per the **OSHA Process Safety Management (PSM) Standard 29 CFR 1910.119**, operating procedures are one of the **14 mandatory elements** of the Process Safety Management program, specifically under the **Operating Procedures Element (d)** of the standard.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme PESFT412 – Design for Safety**, operating procedures are classified as a critical *engineering control* and *administrative control* mechanism that translates process design intent into actionable, repeatable, and auditable operator instructions to prevent process deviations, runaway reactions, and catastrophic releases.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Pilot's Pre-Flight Checklist"**
>
> Imagine a commercial aircraft pilot. Before takeoff, the pilot does NOT rely on memory alone. They use a **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)** — a checklist that details every switch, valve, gauge, and altitude to verify. If a pilot forgets to set flaps or fuel mixture, the plane crashes.
>
> Similarly, in a chemical plant, **Operating Procedures are the operator's flight manual**:
> - **Operating Limits** = the safe speed/altitude envelope
> - **Startup/Shutdown Steps** = the takeoff/landing checklist
> - **Emergency Procedures** = the engine-failure checklist
> - **Safety Systems** = the cockpit alarms and autopilot
>
> Without written operating procedures, an operator is forced to "fly by memory," which is the single largest cause of industrial disasters (e.g., **Bhopal 1984, Flixborough 1974, Texas City 2005**).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

- **OSHA PSM Threshold Quantity (TQ)** for highly hazardous chemicals: typically **2,500 lbs** (flammable liquids/gases) or as specified for specific chemicals.
- **Acceptable Operating Limits (AOLs)** must include **upper and lower bounds** for temperature, pressure, flow, level, composition, and pH.
- **Standard Documentation Cycle**: Operating procedures must be **reviewed and updated at least every 3 years** (per OSHA 1910.119(d)) or whenever a **Management of Change (MOC)** occurs.
- **Human Reliability Target**: Procedures designed to reduce operator error to **< 1 error per 10,000 operations** (HRA standard).

## 1.4 Key Terminology Block

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definitions for KTU Board Exams**
>
> - **SOP (Standard Operating Procedure)**: A written step-by-step instruction for routine operations.
> - **EOP (Emergency Operating Procedure)**: Procedures for handling upset, deviation, and emergency conditions.
> - **TOP (Temporary Operating Procedure)**: Short-term procedures used during maintenance, startup, or abnormal modes.
> - **Operating Limits**: Quantitative boundaries (T, P, flow) defining the safe operating envelope.
> - **Safe Operating Range (SOR)**: The interval between upper and lower operating limits.
> - **Procedural Compliance Audit**: Periodic review to ensure operators follow written procedures.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Four Pillars of Operating Procedures

Operating procedures are not arbitrary documents. They are structured around **four engineering pillars** as defined by the **CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety)** and **OSHA PSM 1910.119(d)**:

### Pillar 1 — Phases of Operation Coverage
Operating procedures MUST address **every phase of process life**:

1. **Initial Startup** — Cold startup, first-time commissioning
2. **Normal Operations** — Steady-state running
3. **Temporary Operations** — Bypasses, manual operations during instrumentation failure
4. **Emergency Shutdown** — ESD activation
5. **Normal Shutdown** — Planned, controlled stop
6. **Startup Following a Shutdown** — After emergency or maintenance
7. **Emergency Operations** — Fire, toxic release, runaway reaction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question**: *"List the phases of operation that must be covered in operating procedures as per OSHA PSM."* — Answer the 7 phases above. Marks are often lost when students forget "Emergency Operations" or "Temporary Operations."

### Pillar 2 — Operating Limits (Quantitative Envelope)

Every operating procedure must explicitly state:
- **Upper and lower safe operating limits** for each critical parameter
- **Consequences of deviation** (e.g., "If T > 180°C → thermal runaway of reactor")
- **Steps to correct or avoid deviation**

### Pillar 3 — Safety & Health Considerations
- Properties of hazardous chemicals (per **SDS / MSDS**)
- Precautions for handling toxic/flammable/corrosive materials
- Engineering controls (ventilation, interlocks, relief valves)
- PPE requirements
- Fire protection and spill containment

### Pillar 4 — Procedural Specificity
- **Step-by-step instructions** (not vague directions)
- **Verifiable checkpoints** (e.g., "Confirm valve V-101 is fully CLOSED — green indicator light ON")
- **Qualitative AND quantitative criteria** for completion of each step

## 2.2 The 14 Elements of OSHA PSM — Where Operating Procedures Fit

| # | PSM Element | Relevance to Operating Procedures |
|---|-------------|----------------------------------|
| 1 | Process Safety Information | Source of operating limits & chemical data |
| 2 | Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) | Identifies scenarios procedures must address |
| 3 | **Operating Procedures** | **CORE TOPIC** |
| 4 | Training | Operators must be trained on procedures |
| 5 | Contractors | Must follow site procedures |
| 6 | Mechanical Integrity | Procedures for inspection & maintenance |
| 7 | Hot Work | Permit-based procedure |
| 8 | Management of Change (MOC) | Procedures must be updated after MOC |
| 9 | Incident Investigation | Reveals gaps in procedures |
| 10 | Emergency Planning & Response | Emergency operating procedures |
| 11 | Compliance Audits | Audit procedural compliance |
| 12 | Trade Secrets | Information availability for safe ops |
| 13 | Employee Participation | Workers help develop procedures |
| 14 | Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR) | Validates procedures before startup |

## 2.3 Hierarchy of Procedural Documents

Operating procedures are layered in a **hierarchy of control documents**:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  TIER 1: Plant Operating Manual (POM)            │
│  - Plant-wide philosophy & overview             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  TIER 2: Unit Operating Procedures (UOP)        │
│  - Specific to each process unit                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  TIER 3: Standard Operating Procedures (SOP)    │
│  - Task-specific (e.g., pump startup)           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  TIER 4: Job Safety Analysis (JSA)              │
│  - Hazard identification per step               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  TIER 5: Permits (Hot Work, Confined Space)     │
│  - Authorization for high-risk tasks            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Relationships for Operating Procedures**

| Concept | Formula / Relationship | Application |
|---------|----------------------|-------------|
| **Safe Operating Range (SOR)** | $SOR = U_{limit} - L_{limit}$ | Width of safe envelope |
| **Margin to Upper Limit** | $\Delta T_{upper} = T_{max,design} - T_{operating}$ | Safety margin (typically $\geq 10\%$) |
| **Margin to Lower Limit** | $\Delta T_{lower} = T_{operating} - T_{min,design}$ | Safety margin (typically $\geq 10\%$) |
| **Procedural Compliance Rate (PCR)** | $PCR = \frac{N_{followed}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Audit metric (target $\geq 95\%$) |
| **Human Error Probability (HEP)** | $HEP = \frac{E_{observed}}{O_{total}}$ | From THERP/SLIM methods |
| **Relief Valve Set Pressure** | $P_{set} \leq P_{MAWP}$ (Max Allowable Working Pressure) | ASME Boiler Code compliance |
| **LFL / UFL Limits** | $LFL \leq C_{operating} \leq UFL$ | Flammability envelope (NFPA 704) |
| **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** | $P_{event} = P_{initiating} \times \prod P_{IPL,i}$ | Determines if procedure+SIS is sufficient |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

Operating procedures are applied in:
- **Petrochemical & Refining Industries** (OSHA PSM-mandated)
- **Pharmaceutical Manufacturing** (FDA cGMP requires SOPs)
- **Nuclear Power Plants** (NRC-mandated operating procedures)
- **Aerospace & Aviation** (FAA Standard Operating Procedures)
- **Oil & Gas Drilling** (API RP 7G, API RP 500)

The economic value: Industries that implement robust operating procedures have **60-80% lower incident rates** and significantly reduced insurance premiums under the **OSHA VPP (Voluntary Protection Program)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Establishing the Safe Operating Envelope

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation: Safe Operating Limits from Process Design Data**
>
> Consider a continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) with the following design parameters:
> - Design Pressure: $P_d = 10$ bar
> - Max Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP): $P_{MAWP} = 9.5$ bar
> - Relief Valve Set Pressure: $P_{set} = 9.0$ bar
> - Normal Operating Pressure: $P_{op} = 7.5$ bar
> - Safety Margin Requirement: $10\%$ above $P_{op}$

### Step 1 — Establish the Upper Operating Limit
The upper operating limit $P_{upper}$ must satisfy the hierarchy:
$$P_{op} < P_{upper} < P_{set} < P_{MAWP} < P_d$$

Given $P_{op} = 7.5$ bar and a $10\%$ safety margin:
$$P_{upper} = P_{op} + (0.10 \times P_{op}) = P_{op} \times 1.10$$

$$P_{upper} = 7.5 \times 1.10 = 8.25 \text{ bar}$$

### Step 2 — Verify the Upper Limit is Below the Relief Valve Set Pressure
$$P_{upper} = 8.25 \text{ bar} < P_{set} = 9.0 \text{ bar} \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms a **margin of** $9.0 - 8.25 = 0.75$ bar before the relief valve opens.

### Step 3 — Establish the Lower Operating Limit
For this exothermic reactor, the lower pressure limit is set by the **minimum pressure for proper vapor-liquid separation**:
$$P_{lower} = P_{op} - (0.10 \times P_{op}) = 7.5 \times 0.90$$

$$P_{lower} = 6.75 \text{ bar}$$

### Step 4 — Define the Safe Operating Range (SOR)
$$SOR = P_{upper} - P_{lower} = 8.25 - 6.75 = 1.50 \text{ bar}$$

### Step 5 — Express the Complete Operating Envelope
$$\boxed{6.75 \text{ bar} \leq P_{operating} \leq 8.25 \text{ bar}}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Procedural Compliance Rate (PCR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation: Quantifying Procedural Compliance in an Audit**

During a compliance audit, an observer counts:

- Total number of procedural steps observed: $N_{total} = 200$
- Steps correctly followed: $N_{followed} = 184$
- Steps with deviation: $N_{deviated} = 16$

The Procedural Compliance Rate is computed as:

$$PCR = \frac{N_{followed}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$$

$$PCR = \frac{184}{200} \times 100\%$$

$$PCR = 92.0\%$$

**Interpretation**: The OSHA-recommended benchmark is $PCR \geq 95\%$. The current value of $92.0\%$ indicates a **non-compliant** state, requiring **operator retraining** and **procedure review** before the next audit.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Procedure Development Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-Step Procedure Development Lifecycle (as per CCPS Guidelines)**

| Step | Phase | Input | Output | Responsible |
|------|-------|-------|--------|------------|
| 1 | Information Gathering | P&IDs, P&IDs, PFDs, SDS, PHA report | Process Safety Information (PSI) package | Process Engineer |
| 2 | Task Identification | Operating philosophy, PHA scenarios | List of operating tasks (startup, normal, shutdown, emergency) | Operations Lead |
| 3 | Step Drafting | PSI + task list + HAZOP scenarios | Draft procedure document | Process & Safety Engineer |
| 4 | Employee Participation | Draft procedure | Operator feedback, validation | Operators + Union Reps |
| 5 | HAZOP / What-If Review | Draft procedure | Revisions, deviation scenarios | HAZOP Team |
| 6 | Final Approval | Approved procedure | Effective for use | Plant Manager |
| 7 | Training | Effective procedure | Trained operators | Training Coordinator |
| 8 | Implementation | Trained operators | Actual use in field | Shift Supervisor |
| 9 | Audit | Field observations | PCR, non-conformances | Safety Auditor |
| 10 | MOC / 3-Year Review | Audit findings, MOC triggers | Updated procedure | Process Engineer |

## 3.4 Exhaustive Content on Emergency Operating Procedures

Emergency operating procedures are specialized and must address:

- **Loss of Utilities** (power, steam, cooling water, instrument air)
- **Loss of Feed / Loss of Product**
- **High Temperature / Pressure Excursions**
- **Fire / Explosion Response**
- **Toxic Gas Release**
- **Reactor Runaway**
- **Equipment Failure** (pump, compressor, instrument)
- **Vessel Overflow / Overfill**

For each scenario, the procedure must specify:
1. **Detection signals** (alarms, gauges, field observations)
2. **Immediate actions** (within 30 seconds)
3. **Short-term actions** (within 5–15 minutes)
4. **Long-term stabilization** (within 1–4 hours)
5. **Escalation criteria** (when to call site emergency response)

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Operating Procedure Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plant Operating Manual POM] --> B[Unit Operating Procedure UOP]
    A --> C[Plant Emergency Plan]
    B --> D[SOP for Reactor Startup]
    B --> E[SOP for Pump Operation]
    B --> F[SOP for Distillation Column]
    D --> G[Pre-Startup Checklist]
    D --> H[Step by Step Startup Sequence]
    D --> I[Post-Startup Verification]
    E --> J[Centrifugal Pump SOP]
    E --> K[Reciprocating Pump SOP]
    F --> L[Column Startup SOP]
    F --> M[Column Shutdown SOP]
    C --> N[Fire Emergency Procedure]
    C --> O[Toxic Release Procedure]
    C --> P[Reactor Runaway Procedure]
    G --> Q[Operator Verification]
    H --> R[Alarm Trip Settings]
    I --> S[Steady State Confirmation]
    N --> T[Evacuation Routes]
    O --> U[SCBA Activation]
    P --> V[Emergency Dump to Quench Tank]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Procedure Development Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Safety Information] --> B[Task Identification]
    B --> C[Draft Procedure Document]
    C --> D[Employee Participation Review]
    D --> E[HAZOP and What If Review]
    E --> F[Final Approval]
    F --> G[Operator Training]
    G --> H[Implementation in Field]
    H --> I[Compliance Audit]
    I --> J{Compliant}
    J -->|Yes| K[3 Year Review Cycle]
    J -->|No| L[Corrective Action]
    L --> C
    K --> M[Management of Change Trigger]
```
