# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** write generic answers like *"Fault administration is managing faults."* — examiners expect specific procedural steps (Detection → Reporting → Classification → Register → RCA → CAPA → Verification → Learning).
> - **Do NOT** confuse *Fault Administration* with *Fault Analysis*. The former is *operational*; the latter is *prospective/technical*.
> - **Do NOT** forget to mention the **statutory backing** (Factories Act §88A, Public Liability Insurance Act, ISO 9001, ISO 45001). Examiners explicitly look for this.
> - **Do NOT** omit the **human-factors** dimension (just culture, reporting fatigue, normalization of deviance). It is a high-value differentiator.
> - **Do NOT** solve numerical problems without an **engineering interpretation sentence** at the end. The interpretation is what carries 50% of the marks.
> - **Do NOT** use the pipe character `|` for absolute value inside LaTeX — use `\vert` or `\mid`.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Fault Administration. List its key objectives. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

*Fault Administration* is the systematic process of identifying, reporting, classifying, recording, investigating, resolving, and preventing faults in a system, process, or organization through a documented management framework that ensures regulatory compliance, operational reliability, and continuous learning.

**Key Objectives (3 marks' worth):**

1. To ensure every fault is **detected, reported, and recorded** in a standardized Fault Register.
2. To ensure **timely investigation** and identification of root causes using structured tools (5-Whys, Fishbone, Bow-Tie).
3. To drive **Corrective and Preventive Actions (CAPA)** that eliminate both the symptom and the systemic cause.
4. To enable **statutory compliance** (Factories Act §88A, Public Liability Insurance Act, ISO 9001 §10.2, ISO 45001 §10.2).
5. To provide **feedback** into the design-for-safety loop, enabling inherently safer design revisions.
6. To build a **Just Culture** that encourages transparent near-miss reporting.

> [!TIP]
> Mentioning **at least 4 of the 6 objectives above** is the safe strategy for full 3 marks.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Corrective Action and Preventive Action with one industrial example each. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Corrective Action (CA) | Preventive Action (PA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Eliminates the *symptom* of a specific fault that has already occurred | Eliminates the *root cause* to prevent similar faults from occurring anywhere in the system |
| **Scope** | Localized — applied to the specific failed equipment or process | Systemic — applied across all similar equipment, processes, or design classes |
| **Trigger** | A specific fault report | A trend, a root-cause analysis finding, or a latent issue identified in audits |
| **Time horizon** | Short-term / immediate | Medium- to long-term |
| **Example — Pump bearing failure** | Replace the failed bearing and re-align the shaft; re-test the pump | Update the preventive maintenance schedule for *all* similar bearings plant-wide; revise the design specification to use a higher-grade bearing; train all maintenance crews on early vibration signatures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For 3 marks, the KTU examiner expects: (a) one-sentence definition of each, (b) one clear distinguishing feature, (c) one industrial example. A tabular comparison with at least 3 rows of contrast is a guaranteed full-marks answer.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Explain in detail the complete Fault Administration cycle for a process plant, with reference to the PDCA cycle, fault classification tiers, and statutory requirements under the Factories Act 1948. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 marks:** Describe the Fault Administration cycle and its linkage to the PDCA cycle.

**Model Solution:**

The Fault Administration cycle consists of ten sequential stages that, when executed rigorously, form a closed-loop management system aligned with the Deming–Shewhart PDCA cycle.

**Stage 1 — Fault Detection:** A fault is first observed through routine inspection, condition monitoring (vibration, thermography, ultrasonic, oil analysis), automatic trips in a Safety Instrumented System (SIS), operator walk-downs, or third-party audits. *PDCA mapping: "Check."*

**Stage 2 — Immediate Response (Containment):** The affected equipment is isolated using the **Lock Out Tag Out (LOTO)** procedure, the area is secured, and the shift supervisor and EHS officer are notified within 15 minutes. *PDCA mapping: "Act" (immediate).*

**Stage 3 — Fault Reporting (First Information Report):** A standardized **FIR** is filled capturing date, time, location, equipment tag, description, immediate actions, witnesses, and photographs. *PDCA mapping: "Plan" (data capture).*

**Stage 4 — Classification:** The fault is classified into one of four tiers based on severity:
- **Tier 1 — Major Accident Hazard (MAH):** Fatality, off-site impact, loss > ₹1 crore.
- **Tier 2 — Serious Incident:** Lost-time injury, contained fire/explosion.
- **Tier 3 — Near-Miss / HiPo:** No injury but high potential.
- **Tier 4 — Minor Fault:** First-aid, minor damage.

*PDCA mapping: "Check" (severity assessment).*

**Stage 5 — Fault Register Entry:** The fault is assigned a unique ID (e.g., **FR-2024-00127**) and entered into the centralized **Fault Register** with fields for root cause, corrective action, preventive action, target date, status, and verifier.

**Stage 6 — Root Cause Analysis (RCA):** A structured investigation is launched — 5-Whys for Tier-3/4, Fishbone + Bow-Tie for Tier-2, full multi-disciplinary + third-party for Tier-1. *PDCA mapping: "Check" (root-cause diagnosis).*

**Stage 7 — CAPA Initiation:** Both *Corrective Action* (eliminates the specific symptom) and *Preventive Action* (eliminates the systemic root cause) are defined with assigned owners, target dates, and resources.

**Stage 8 — Verification and Closure:** An independent verifier confirms the action is implemented and effective through re-inspection, re-test, or trend monitoring. Only then is the fault marked *Closed* in the register. *PDCA mapping: "Act" (closure).*

**Stage 9 — Communication and Learning:** Findings are disseminated through Toolbox Talks, Safety Bulletins, and Lessons-Learned databases. Repeat-fault trends trigger **Engineering Change Requests (ECR)** and **Management of Change (MOC)**.

**Stage 10 — Records Retention and Audit:** Records are retained for ≥ 5 years (or equipment life, whichever is longer) and are subject to internal audits, external certification audits (ISO 9001/45001), and regulatory inspections.

**PDCA Linkage Summary:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Plan} &\rightarrow \text{Policy, register format, SLAs, escalation matrix} \\
\text{Do} &\rightarrow \text{Detect, Report, Log, Investigate, CAPA} \\
\text{Check} &\rightarrow \text{Audit, KPI monitoring, trend analysis} \\
\text{Act} &\rightarrow \text{Standardize, revise, feed back into design}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 7-mark break-down for part (a):** 5 stages @ 1 mark each = 5 marks, plus 2 marks for the explicit PDCA mapping. The PDCA mapping is what elevates the answer from a *list* to an *analytical framework*.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:** Discuss the statutory requirements under the Factories Act 1948 that govern Fault Administration in Indian factories.

**Model Solution:**

The **Factories Act 1948**, as amended, is the primary statute governing occupational safety in Indian factories. Fault Administration is directly anchored in several of its provisions.

**1. Section 88A — Notice of Certain Accidents:**
- Every factory must notify the **Chief Inspector of Factories** and the **District Authority** of any accident causing death or serious bodily injury, within the prescribed time (typically **24 hours for fatal accidents** and **72 hours for serious injuries**).
- The notice must include the **nature of the accident**, the **injuries sustained**, and the **cause, if known**.
- This makes the *first stage* of Fault Administration (reporting) a **legal obligation**, not a discretionary practice.

**2. Section 89 — Notice of Certain Diseases:**
- Specific occupational diseases (e.g., silicosis, asbestosis, lead poisoning) must be reported to the Inspector.
- This extends Fault Administration to *health-related faults* that are not acute accidents.

**3. Section 7A — Safety Officers:**
- Factories employing ≥ 1,000 workers or carrying out hazardous processes must appoint a qualified **Safety Officer** whose duties include *advising on safety measures, conducting investigations, and maintaining safety records* — the operational backbone of Fault Administration.

**4. Section 41F — Safety Committee:**
- Factories with ≥ 100 workers (or hazardous processes) must constitute a **Safety Committee** comprising equal numbers of management and worker representatives.
- The committee must meet at least once every quarter and review the **safety performance** — including fault trends, open CAPAs, and incident statistics.

**5. Section 14 — Fitness of Plant and Machinery:**
- All plant and machinery must be maintained in a *fit* condition, which necessitates a *fault-finding and rectification regime*.

**6. The Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules 1989 (MSIHC) — Rule 18:**
- For Major Accident Hazard (MAH) installations, the occupier must prepare and maintain a **Safety Report**, an **On-Site Emergency Plan**, and reportable incident records — all of which are outputs of the Fault Administration system.

**7. The Public Liability Insurance Act 1991:**
- Owners of hazardous installations must maintain public liability insurance and report any accident resulting in death or injury to *third parties*, making Fault Administration a **financial-and-legal** necessity as well.

**Implications for Fault Administration in Industry:**

$$
\text{Statutory Pressure} \rightarrow \text{Reporting Discipline} \rightarrow \text{Register Quality} \rightarrow \text{CAPA Effectiveness} \rightarrow \text{Legal Compliance}
$$

Failure to maintain a robust Fault Administration system can result in:
- Prosecution under Section 92 of the Factories Act (penalties up to ₹1 lakh and 2 years' imprisonment).
- Cancellation of factory license.
- Insurance claim rejection.
- Reputational damage and operational shutdown.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip for Part (b):** Cite at least **3 specific sections** of the Factories Act and link each to a specific Fault Administration stage. For full 7 marks, also mention one rule outside the Factories Act (e.g., MSIHC Rules 1989, Public Liability Insurance Act 1991). This shows the examiner you have *legal breadth* and not just textbook knowledge.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Describe the structure and contents of a Fault Register. Explain, with an industrial case study, how Root Cause Analysis (RCA) and CAPA close the feedback loop in Fault Administration. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Part (a) — 7 marks:** Structure and contents of a Fault Register with a sample entry.

**Model Solution:**

The **Fault Register** (also called the *Incident Log*, *Defect Register*, or *Non-Conformance Register*) is the central database of the Fault Administration system. It is the *single source of truth* for all faults in an organization.

**Structure (3 marks):** A Fault Register typically contains the following **15 fields**:

1. **Fault ID** — Unique alphanumeric identifier (e.g., FR-2024-00127).
2. **Date and Time of Occurrence** — When the fault actually happened.
3. **Date and Time of Detection** — When it was first observed (used to compute FDL).
4. **Equipment / Asset Tag** — e.g., Pump P-101, Motor M-204.
5. **Location** — Plant, area, unit.
6. **Reporter Identity and Shift** — Accountability for the report.
7. **Fault Description** — What was observed vs. what was expected.
8. **Severity Tier** — 1, 2, 3, or 4.
9. **Fault Type** — Mechanical, electrical, instrumentation, process, human-error, design.
10. **Root Cause** — Identified after RCA.
11. **Immediate Action Taken** — Containment.
12. **Corrective Action** — Permanent fix for this fault.
13. **Preventive Action** — System-wide prevention.
14. **Action Owner and Target Date** — Accountability and SLA.
15. **Status, Verifier, and Verification Date** — Closure trail.

**Sample Entry (4 marks):**

| Field | Entry |
|---|---|
| Fault ID | FR-2024-00127 |
| Date / Time of Occurrence | 14 March 2024, 02:30 h |
| Date / Time of Detection | 14 March 2024, 02:45 h |
| Equipment Tag | Centrifugal Pump P-101 (Boiler Feed Water) |
| Location | Boiler House, Unit 2, Block A |
| Reporter | Mr. R. Kumar, Shift B Operator |
| Description | Abnormal high vibration (15 mm/s RMS) on pump bearing DE; bearing temperature 78 °C (normal 55 °C). |
| Severity Tier | Tier 2 (potential for catastrophic failure if not addressed) |
| Fault Type | Mechanical |
| Root Cause | Inadequate lubrication due to grease gun used on incompatible grease nipple — wrong grease type supplied by procurement. |
| Immediate Action | Pump isolated via LOTO; manual lubrication done with correct grease. |
| Corrective Action | Bearing inspected; regreased; re-aligned. Procurement specification revised to include grease type. |
| Preventive Action | Plant-wide audit of all grease types; training of maintenance crews; auto-lubricators installed on all critical pumps. |
| Action Owner | Mr. S. Pillai (Maintenance Engineer); target date: 30 April 2024. |
| Status / Verifier | Closed on 22 April 2024; verified by Ms. A. Menon (QA Lead). |

**Tools for Maintaining the Register:**

- **Small plants:** Paper-based ledger, Excel spreadsheet.
- **Medium plants:** CMMS / EAM (Computerized Maintenance Management System) — e.g., **Maximo, SAP PM, UpKeep**.
- **Large plants / process industries:** Integrated EHS software (e.g., **Enablon, Intelex, Sphera, IBM Tririga**) with mobile-app incident reporting.
- **Regulated industries (pharma, nuclear):** 21 CFR Part 11 compliant electronic systems with full audit trail.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always include at least **one example entry** in your answer. It demonstrates to the examiner that you understand the *practical* application, not just the *theoretical* structure.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:** Industrial case study — RCA and CAPA in a process plant.

**Model Solution:**

**Case Study — Overpressure Incident in an Ammonia Storage Vessel, ABC Fertilizers Ltd., 2023.**

**Background:**
An ammonia storage vessel (V-301) rated for 16 bar was found during a routine inspection to have localized bulging on the top head, indicating a past overpressure event that had not been recorded. No injury occurred, but the vessel was within 2 weeks of its 5-yearly statutory hydrotest. Tier-1 potential was declared.

**Stage 1 — Fault Detection:**
The bulging was detected during the *pre-hydrotest external inspection* by an API 570-certified inspector.

**Stage 2 — Immediate Response:**
- Vessel isolated from the process via double-block-and-bleed.
- Pressure Safety Valves (PSVs) removed, sent to a certified workshop for set-pressure calibration.
- Ammonia inventory transferred to a standby vessel under controlled conditions.
- EHS, Plant Manager, and statutory authorities notified within 8 hours.

**Stage 3 — Reporting:**
A Tier-1 FIR was raised (FR-2023-00456) and a multi-disciplinary investigation team was formed comprising: Process Engineer, Maintenance Engineer, EHS Head, Instrumentation Engineer, and a third-party expert from a notified inspection agency.

**Stage 4 — RCA Using 5-Whys + Fishbone + Bow-Tie:**

*5-Whys:*
- **Why was there overpressure?** → PSV-301A did not open at set pressure.
- **Why did PSV-301A not open?** → Set screw had corroded and was stuck.
- **Why was the set screw corroded?** → Wrong material of construction (carbon steel instead of stainless steel) used in 2018 maintenance.
- **Why was the wrong material used?** → Procurement had no material specification in the purchase order.
- **Why was there no material specification?** → The Maintenance Procedure did not include material traceability for PSV internals.

*Fishbone Categories:*
- **Man:** Maintenance crew did not verify material certificates.
- **Machine:** PSV internal material incompatible with ammonia service.
- **Method:** Maintenance SOP missing material traceability step.
- **Material:** Wrong grade of steel procured.
- **Measurement:** PSV bench-test after overhaul was not done in-house.
- **Mother Nature (Environment):** Ammonia service accelerated corrosion of the carbon-steel set screw.

*Bow-Tie:* Threat (corrosion) → Causes (wrong material, no traceability) → Top Event (PSV fails to open) → Consequences (overpressure → vessel bulging → potential catastrophic rupture).

**Stage 5 — CAPA:**

| Action Type | Action | Owner | Target Date |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Corrective (CA-1)** | Replace PSV-301A with a new valve with full material traceability; calibrate and bench-test in-house. | Maintenance Engineer | 25 Oct 2023 |
| **Corrective (CA-2)** | Hydrotest V-301; ultrasonic thickness survey; fitness-for-service assessment per API 579. | Inspection Agency | 30 Oct 2023 |
| **Preventive (PA-1)** | Revise Maintenance SOP for all PSVs to include material traceability and MTC verification. | Maintenance Manager | 15 Nov 2023 |
| **Preventive (PA-2)** | Plant-wide audit of all PSVs installed since 2018 for material conformance. | EHS + Inspection | 31 Dec 2023 |
| **Preventive (PA-3)** | Update the FMEA and RBI databases with this failure mode; revise inspection intervals from 5 years to 3 years for ammonia-service PSVs. | Reliability Engineer | 31 Jan 2024 |

**Stage 6 — Verification and Closure:**
- All CAs and PAs were implemented within target dates.
- The Plant Manager (independent verifier) confirmed effectiveness on 5 February 2024.
- The fault was marked **Closed** in the Fault Register.

**Stage 7 — Learning and Feedback into Design:**
- The case was published in the **Quarterly Safety Bulletin** and discussed in the **Safety Committee** meeting.
- A **Management of Change (MOC)** proposal was raised to standardize stainless-steel internals across all PSVs in ammonia service.
- The **Design for Safety** review of the next revamp project (planned 2025) included this case as a mandatory design input.

**Feedback Loop Diagram:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fault Occurrence} &\rightarrow \text{Detection and Reporting} \\
&\rightarrow \text{RCA and CAPA} \\
&\rightarrow \text{SOP / Design Revision} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Next Design is Safer} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Fault Recurrence Probability Decreases}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Critical Insight:** This case study demonstrates that Fault Administration is *not* a paperwork exercise — it is the *operational system that learns*. Without the register, the RCA would never have been triggered; without the CAPA, the design defect would have persisted across the entire PSV population. This is the **closed-loop learning** that KTU examiners reward with full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print / Pin This Section.**

- ✅ **Definition:** Fault Administration is the *systematic, documented management* of faults across their lifecycle — Detection → Reporting → Classification → Registration → RCA → CAPA → Verification → Learning.
- ✅ **5 Pillars:** Identification & Reporting | Recording & Classification | Investigation & RCA | CAPA | Communication & Learning.
- ✅ **PDCA Anchor:** The entire Fault Administration cycle is a *concrete application* of the Deming–Shewhart PDCA cycle — mention this in *every* descriptive answer.
- ✅ **4 Tiers of Severity:** Tier-1 MAH (fatality, off-site impact) → Tier-2 (serious injury/damage) → Tier-3 (near-miss/HiPo) → Tier-4 (minor/first-aid). Tier-1 triggers statutory notification within 24 h under Factories Act §88A.
- ✅ **Fault Register:** Central database with at least 15 fields including Fault ID, date/time, equipment tag, severity, root cause, CA, PA, action owner, target date, status, and verifier. The Register is the *single source of truth*.
- ✅ **RCA Tools:** 5-Whys, Fishbone (Ishikawa), Bow-Tie, Fault Tree, Swiss Cheese Model. Choose the tool by severity tier.
- ✅ **CAPA Distinction:** *Corrective Action* = fix this specific fault; *Preventive Action* = fix the systemic root cause to prevent recurrence anywhere. Both need an owner, a target date, and an independent verifier.
- ✅ **Just Culture:** Sidney Dekker's *Just Culture* balances individual accountability with systemic understanding. It is the *human-factors foundation* of a high-reporting, low-incident organization.
- ✅ **Quantitative Metrics to Memorize:** MTBF, MTTR, Availability $A$, FDL, FCR, RFR, OAR, RPN $= S \times O \times D$, threshold $\text{RPN} \geq 100$.
- ✅ **Key Formulas (LaTeX-safe, use `\vert` for absolute value):**
  - $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\,\%$
  - $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$
  - $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{N_{\text{repairs}}}$
  - $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$
- ✅ **Statutory Anchors:** Factories Act 1948 §88A (24 h notification), §89 (occupational diseases), §7A (Safety Officer), §41F (Safety Committee); MSIHC Rules 1989; Public Liability Insurance Act 1991; ISO 9001 §10.2; ISO 45001 §10.2.
- ✅ **Records Retention:** ≥ 5 years or equipment life, whichever is longer.
- ✅ **Heinrich Pyramid Inversion:** A *healthy* Fault Administration system *widens* the base of the pyramid (more near-misses reported) while *shrinking* the top (fewer major accidents). This is counterintuitive but factually correct.
- ✅ **MOC Trigger:** Any change in process, equipment, procedure, or materials that alters risk requires a formal Management of Change review.
- ✅ **Bow-Tie Concept:** Threats on the left, top event in the middle, consequences on the right, with *preventive* barriers between threat and top event, and *mitigative* barriers between top event and consequence.
- ✅ **3 Lines of Defence Model:** (1) Operations manages fault administration; (2) EHS / QA oversees it; (3) Internal audit provides independent assurance. Mention this for high-value marks on any *governance* question.
- ✅ **Industry Benchmarks to Know:** API 754 Tier 1–4 classification; OSHA 1910.119 PSM; NEBOSH NGC2 reporting requirements; ICAO Annex 13 for aviation accident investigation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Malfunction Detection, Computer Aids, Project Aids, Aids for Representing Regulatory and Code Requirements, Other Process Design Aspects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Malfunction Detection, Computer Aids, Project Aids, Aids for Representing Regulatory and Code Requirements, Other Process Design Aspects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Malfunction Detection

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
Malfunction detection refers to the systematic identification of deviations from normal operating conditions in a chemical process plant through the use of sensors, instruments, control systems, and analytical methodologies. It forms the first critical layer of the process safety management (PSM) hierarchy, ensuring that abnormal situations are recognized before they escalate into hazardous events.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examination Definition:** *Malfunction detection is the process of identifying deviations from design intent using instruments, alarms, and analytical techniques to prevent escalation into a hazardous scenario.* (Expected as a 3-mark direct question.)

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of malfunction detection as the **"smoke detector and warning system in your home"**. A smoke detector doesn't put out a fire; it only *detects* the early signs (smoke, heat) and warns you so you can act. Similarly, malfunction detection in a process plant uses temperature sensors, pressure transmitters, and flow meters to "smell trouble" — like a pressure spike in a reactor or a sudden temperature drop in a distillation column — and raises an alarm before a catastrophic event occurs.

**Key Components of Malfunction Detection:**

| Component | Function | Real-World Example |
|-----------|----------|---------------------|
| **Sensors & Transmitters** | Measure process variables | RTD, thermocouple, pressure gauge |
| **Alarms** | Notify operators of deviations | High-temperature alarm at **150 °C** |
| **Trips (SIS)** | Automatic emergency shutdown | ESD-1 (Emergency Shutdown System) |
| **Logic Solvers** | Process input signals into action | PLC, DCS, Safety PLC (SIL 3) |
| **Final Control Elements** | Execute shutdown action | Block valves, dump valves |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard:** The Safety Integrity Level (SIL) — defined under **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511** — quantifies the reliability of malfunction detection systems. Common target: **SIL 2 or SIL 3** for high-hazard processes.

---

### 1.2 Computer Aids in Process Safety Design

**Formal Definition:**
Computer aids in process safety refer to digital tools, simulation packages, and software platforms that assist engineers in modeling, analyzing, predicting, and mitigating process hazards. These range from steady-state process simulators to dynamic safety analysis tools.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are designing a complex highway interchange. Before laying a single brick of asphalt, you use a **computer traffic simulation** to see how cars will flow, where bottlenecks will form, and what happens if an accident blocks a lane. Computer aids in process safety work the same way — they let engineers simulate "what-if" scenarios (e.g., a pump failure, a valve leak) on a computer *before* building the actual plant, saving lives, money, and time.

**Categories of Computer Aids:**

| Category | Examples | Purpose |
|----------|----------|---------|
| **Steady-State Simulators** | Aspen Plus, HYSYS, PRO/II | Mass & energy balance |
| **Dynamic Simulators** | Aspen Dynamics, HYSYS Dynamics | Transient behavior analysis |
| **Consequence Analysis** | ALOHA, PHAST, EFFECTS | Dispersion, fire, explosion modeling |
| **LOPA & Risk Tools** | exSILentia, LOPA Software | Layer of Protection Analysis |
| **QRA Packages** | SAFETI, FRED, Shepherd | Quantitative Risk Assessment |
| **HAZOP Tools** | PHA-Pro, HAZOPkit, bowtieXP | HAZOP & Bow-Tie Analysis |
| **Reliability Databases** | OREDA, IEEE Std 500 | Equipment failure rate data |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Students are frequently asked to list **5–6 categories** of computer aids and briefly describe each. Memorize the table above.

---

### 1.3 Project Aids in Safety Design

**Formal Definition:**
Project aids are organizational and documentation tools that support the systematic execution of safety-related design activities throughout a process plant's lifecycle — from concept to commissioning and decommissioning.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Consider a hospital surgery. A surgeon doesn't just walk in and operate. There is a **checklist** (patient identity, allergy check, surgical site), a **team briefing**, a **time-out procedure**, and a **post-op review**. These project aids ensure nothing is forgotten. In process design, project aids function as the engineering "checklists" — they include procedures, checklists, review protocols, and management systems that ensure safety is built *into* the design, not bolted on as an afterthought.

**Common Project Aids:**

1. **Project Safety Reviews** — at every design phase (Concept, FEED, Detailed, Construction, Commissioning).
2. **Checklists** — pre-defined lists of safety items to verify.
3. **P&ID Review Procedures** — systematic piping & instrumentation diagram walk-downs.
4. **Management of Change (MOC)** protocols.
5. **Document Control Systems** — version tracking of safety-critical documents.
6. **Hazard Study (HAZID) Meetings** — structured team-based hazard identification.

---

### 1.4 Aids for Representing Regulatory and Code Requirements

**Formal Definition:**
Regulatory and code aids are visual, tabular, or document-based tools that translate complex legal regulations, industry codes, and standards into actionable engineering requirements for process plant design. They ensure designers *don't miss* a single compliance item.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of building a house. The local municipality publishes a **building code** (e.g., "fire exit must be 1.2 m wide," "smoke alarms in every bedroom"). A homeowner can't memorize all 500 pages. So architects use a **compliance checklist** and a **code matrix** that maps each regulation to a specific design element. In process plants, the regulations (OSHA PSM, EPA RMP, ASME BPVC) are far more complex, so engineers use **code compliance matrices** and **regulatory databases** to ensure nothing is missed.

**Key Regulatory and Code Aids:**

- **Code Compliance Matrix (CCM):** Spreadsheet mapping each clause of a standard to the responsible design element.
- **Regulatory Databases:** Online repositories (e.g., IChemE, AIChE, NFPA).
- **Standards Cross-Reference Tables:** Linking API, ASME, NFPA, OSHA, EPA requirements.
- **Design Basis Memoranda (DBM):** Documents capturing all regulatory constraints.
- **Plot Plan & Layout Checklists:** Ensuring compliance with spacing, drainage, fire-water requirements.
- **Piping Class Sheets:** Translating ASME B31.3 into pipe material specifications.

**Key Standards to Know (KTU 2024 Board Favourite):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** — Process Safety Management (PSM)
> - **EPA 40 CFR 68** — Risk Management Plan (RMP)
> - **ASME BPVC** — Boiler & Pressure Vessel Code
> - **API 521 / API 520** — Pressure Relief
> - **NFPA 30** — Flammable Liquids Code
> - **IEC 61511** — Functional Safety in Process Industries
> - **ISO 31000** — Risk Management

---

### 1.5 Other Process Design Aspects

This section covers auxiliary design considerations that are not strictly part of HAZOP/LOPA but significantly influence plant safety:

1. **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** — Minimization, Substitution, Moderation, Simplification.
2. **Layout & Spacing** — Plot plan design, fire-water coverage, escape routes.
3. **Human Factors Engineering (HFE)** — Control room design, alarm management.
4. **Material of Construction (MOC) Selection** — Corrosion, erosion, compatibility.
5. **Relief & Vent Sizing** — API 521, API 520, DIERS methodology.
6. **Fire Protection System Design** — Foam, sprinklers, deluge, gas detection.
7. **Emergency Response Planning** — Muster points, evacuation routes.
8. **Commissioning & Start-up Procedures** — Pre-startup Safety Review (PSSR).
9. **Decommissioning & Demolition Planning.**
10. **Operability & Maintainability Reviews.**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** *Module 2 emphasizes the *tools* (aids) used by designers to embed safety — not the management/regulatory framework (which is covered in Module 3 / 4).*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Malfunction Detection — Theoretical Framework

The theoretical basis of malfunction detection rests on three pillars:

**Pillar 1: Process Variable Monitoring**
Every safety-critical variable has a **normal operating range**, an **alarm setpoint**, and a **trip setpoint**. The hierarchy is:

$$\text{Normal Operating Range} \;\subset\; \text{Alarm Band} \;\subset\; \text{Trip Setpoint} \;\subset\; \text{Safe Operating Limit}$$

A practical example for a pressure vessel (design pressure **10 barg**):

| Parameter | Typical Value | Engineering Reasoning |
|-----------|---------------|------------------------|
| **Normal Operating Pressure** | **6–8 barg** | Plant design basis |
| **High Alarm (HA)** | **8.5 barg** | Operator intervention |
| **High-High Alarm (HHA)** | **9.0 barg** | Supervisor notification |
| **Trip Setpoint** | **9.5 barg** | Automatic shutdown |
| **MAWP (Max Allowable)** | **10 barg** | Mechanical design limit |
| **Relief Valve Set Pressure** | **10.5 barg** | API 520 overpressure allowance |

**Pillar 2: Reliability Metrics**

Two core equations govern the reliability of a malfunction detection system:

**Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):**

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$$

**Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD):**
For a single-channel system with one proof-test interval $T_i$ and dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D$:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2}\,\lambda_D\, T_i$$

For a **1oo2 (1-out-of-2) voting system** (one channel can fail safely):

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} \approx \frac{\lambda_D^2 \, T_i^2}{6}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Key Numbers:** SIL 1 → PFD $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$, SIL 2 → PFD $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$, SIL 3 → PFD $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$.

**Pillar 3: Detection Methods**

- **Limit Detection** — Single-variable threshold alarms.
- **Trend Detection** — Rate-of-change monitoring (dP/dt).
- **Pattern Recognition** — Neural networks and AI/ML-based anomaly detection.
- **Model-Based Detection** — Compare measured vs. predicted using a process model.

---

### 2.2 Computer Aids — Detailed Conceptual Map

Computer aids in process safety integrate **four computational layers**:

**Layer 1 — Data Layer:** Thermodynamic databases (DIPPR, NIST), kinetics libraries.
**Layer 2 — Modeling Layer:** Steady-state and dynamic simulation.
**Layer 3 — Analysis Layer:** Consequence, frequency, and risk calculations.
**Layer 4 — Reporting Layer:** Auto-generated safety reports, 3D visualization.

**Why Each Layer Matters:**

- **Data Layer** ensures the *correct physical properties* (vapor pressure, heat of reaction, LFL) feed the simulation. Wrong data = wrong conclusion.
- **Modeling Layer** translates the *physical reality* into solvable equations (mass balance, energy balance, momentum balance).
- **Analysis Layer** answers the *what-if* questions: What if a 2-inch line ruptures? What if the cooling water fails?
- **Reporting Layer** converts *technical output* into communication artifacts (heat radiation contours, toxicity footprints, F-N curves).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Concept:** Consequence analysis uses source models (release rate), dispersion models (Gaussian or SLAB), fire/explosion models (pool fire, jet fire, VCE), and effect models (thermal radiation, overpressure, toxic dose). Each is implemented in software like **ALOHA, PHAST, SAFETI**.

**Key Equations Used in Consequence Analysis (Killer Facts for 14-mark answers):**

| Phenomenon | Equation | Software Implementation |
|------------|----------|--------------------------|
| **Liquid release rate (orifice)** | $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ | ALOHA, PHAST |
| **Gas release (choked)** | $Q = C_d A P \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{\rho R T} \cdot \dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}^{(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)}}$ | PHAST, EFFECTS |
| **Thermal radiation from pool fire** | $q'' = \dfrac{\dot{m}'' \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot F}{4 \pi r^2}$ | SAFETI, FRED |
| **Probit for lethality** | $Y = a + b \ln(V)$ | Consequence tools |
| **TNT equivalent (VCE)** | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$ | ALOHA |

Where:
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient ≈ **0.61** for sharp-edged orifice
- $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats (≈ 1.4 for air, ≈ 1.3 for hydrocarbons)
- $E_{TNT}$ = **4.52 MJ/kg** (energy of TNT explosion)
- $\eta$ = empirical yield factor (**1% to 10%** for hydrocarbon VCE)

---

### 2.3 Project Aids — Theoretical Lifecycle Integration

Project aids align with the **phased design model** of a process plant. Each phase has a corresponding safety review:

| Project Phase | Key Project Aid | Output Document |
|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Conceptual Design** | Concept Hazard Analysis (CHA) | CHA report |
| **FEED** | HAZID, Pre-HAZOP | HAZID register |
| **Detailed Design** | HAZOP, SIL classification | HAZOP & LOPA reports |
| **Procurement** | MOC procedure, Vendor QA | Approved vendor list |
| **Construction** | Site safety audits, P&ID walkdown | Punch lists |
| **Commissioning** | Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR) | PSSR clearance certificate |
| **Operation** | Periodic HAZOP revalidation | Revalidation report |
| **Decommissioning** | Demolition safety review | Demolition plan |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** *The question "List the project safety reviews at each design phase" is a guaranteed 7-mark question. Memorize the table above.*

---

### 2.4 Regulatory and Code Aids — Translation Theory

Regulations are written in **legal/prescriptive language** (e.g., *"The employer shall ensure..."*). Engineers need them in **measurable, verifiable design specifications** (e.g., *"The relief valve set pressure shall be 110% of MAWP"*). The translation is done via a **Code Compliance Matrix (CCM)**:

**Structure of a Code Compliance Matrix (CCM):**

| Standard / Clause | Requirement | Design Element | Responsible Discipline | Verification Method | Status |
|------------------|-------------|----------------|------------------------|---------------------|--------|
| **ASME BPVC Sec. VIII Div. 1** | UG-101: Corrosion allowance | Vessel wall thickness | Mechanical | Calc. report | Closed |
| **API 521** | §4.3.6: Two-phase relief sizing | Relief valve sizing | Process | Calculation sheet | Open |
| **NFPA 30** | §4.3.1.1: Tank spacing | Plot plan | Layout | Layout drawing | Closed |
| **OSHA 1910.119** | (l): MOC procedure | Management system | Project Engineering | Audit report | Open |

This matrix ensures **traceability**: every regulatory requirement can be traced to a specific design element, owner, and verification method.

**Common Pitfalls in Regulatory Translation:**

- Mixing up **"shall"** (mandatory) and **"should"** (recommended).
- Ignoring **local jurisdictional overlays** (e.g., Kerala State Pollution Control Board rules).
- Failing to update CCM when standards are revised.
- Not tracking **client-specific standards** (e.g., Shell DEP, Saudi Aramco SAMES).

---

### 2.5 Other Process Design Aspects — The "Beyond HAZOP" Layer

These aspects are typically covered in **6–8 marks** in KTU exams:

**1. Inherently Safer Design (ISD) — Kletz's Hierarchy:**

| Principle | Description | Example |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| **Minimize** | Use smaller inventories of hazardous materials | Reduce reactor size from 50 m³ to 10 m³ |
| **Substitute** | Use less hazardous materials | Replace benzene (Class A carcinogen) with toluene |
| **Moderate** | Use less hazardous conditions | Operate at lower T and P |
| **Simplify** | Eliminate complexity | Use gravity feed instead of pumps |

**2. Layout & Spacing (Key Numbers):**

| Item | Minimum Distance | Standard Reference |
|------|------------------|--------------------|
| Process unit to property line | **60 m** (typical) | NFPA 30 / client spec |
| Fire-water monitor coverage | **30–45 m radius** | NFPA 15 |
| Road width for fire truck | **6 m minimum** | Local fire code |
| Vessel to vessel (small) | **1.5 × diameter of larger** | API 2510 |

**3. Human Factors:**

- **Alarm Rationalization** — typically **<150 alarms per operator per hour** (EEMUA 191).
- **Control Room Layout** — minimal walking distance, good sightlines.
- **Operator workload** — 4 alarms/operator/shift for sustained, 12 for transient.

**4. Material of Construction (MOC) Selection:**

The MOC must be compatible with the process fluid across **all** operating scenarios (including upset). Corrosion allowance is typically **1.5–3 mm** for carbon steel, **0 mm** for stainless in clean service.

**5. Relief & Vent Sizing (DIERS Method):**

For a runaway reaction in a reactor, the **required relief area** $A$ is given by:

$$A = \frac{m_o}{G \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{set}}{P_o} - 1}}$$

Where $G$ is the mass flux (kg/m²s), $m_o$ is the mass flow, $P_{set}$ is the set pressure, $P_o$ is the downstream pressure.

**6. Fire Protection:**

- **Foam pour rate:** **4.1 L/min/m²** for hydrocarbon pool fires (NFPA 11).
- **Deluge valve trip time:** **<30 seconds**.
- **Firewater flow:** **100–500 m³/h** for typical process unit.

---

### 2.6 KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet (Memorize This Block)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Use Case |
|---|---------|---------|-------|----------|
| 1 | MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = t / N_f$ | hours | Reliability |
| 2 | PFD (single channel) | $\text{PFD} = \tfrac{1}{2} \lambda_D T_i$ | dimensionless | SIL |
| 3 | PFD (1oo2) | $\text{PFD}_{1oo2} \approx \lambda_D^2 T_i^2 / 6$ | dimensionless | SIL |
| 4 | Liquid orifice release | $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ | kg/s | Leak rate |
| 5 | Choked gas release | $Q = C_d A P \sqrt{\gamma / (\rho R T)} \cdot k$ | kg/s | Gas leak |
| 6 | TNT equivalent | $W_{TNT} = \eta m \Delta H_c / E_{TNT}$ | kg | VCE |
| 7 | Pool fire radiation | $q'' = \eta_m \Delta H_c F / (4 \pi r^2)$ | kW/m² | Thermal dose |
| 8 | Probit equation | $Y = a + b \ln V$ | — | Lethality |
| 9 | Relief area (DIERS) | $A = m_o / (G \sqrt{P_{set}/P_o - 1})$ | m² | PSV sizing |
| 10 | Foam pour rate | $q_f = 4.1$ L/min/m² | — | Fire protection |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: PFD for a Single-Channel Detection System

A **single-channel** malfunction detection system is the simplest possible configuration — one sensor, one logic solver, one final element. The probability that the system *fails to operate on demand* (PFD) is derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Define the dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D$:**

Let $\lambda_D$ be the dangerous failure rate (failures per hour). Over a small time interval $dt$, the probability of a dangerous failure is $\lambda_D \, dt$.

**Step 2 — Define the proof test interval $T_i$:**

The system is proof-tested at intervals of $T_i$ hours. After the test, it is restored to a "good as new" condition. Between tests, only dangerous unrevealed failures accumulate.

**Step 3 — Compute the average unavailability over $T_i$:**

The unavailability $U(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_D t}$. For $\lambda_D t \ll 1$ (typical in low-demand mode), we use the approximation $e^{-\lambda_D t} \approx 1 - \lambda_D t$, giving:

$$U(t) \approx \lambda_D t$$

The average unavailability over one interval $T_i$ is:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^{T_i} U(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^{T_i} \lambda_D t \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_i} \left[ \frac{\lambda_D t^2}{2} \right]_0^{T_i} = \frac{1}{T_i} \cdot \frac{\lambda_D T_i^2}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \lambda_D T_i$$

Thus:

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2} \lambda_D T_i}$$

This is the **fundamental equation** for low-demand mode SIL determination.

**Step 5 — Numerical example:**

A pressure transmitter has $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. Proof-test interval $T_i = 8760$ hours (1 year).

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 2.19 \times 10^{-2}$$

This falls in the **SIL 1** range ($10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: PFD for a 1oo2 (1-out-of-2) Voting System

In a **1oo2** architecture, two independent channels are connected in parallel; the system works as long as **at least one** channel functions. The *system fails* only if **both** channels have failed dangerously.

**Step 1 — Probability that a single channel has failed by time $t$:**

$$P_{\text{1 fail}}(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_D t} \approx \lambda_D t \quad \text{(for small } \lambda_D t \text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Probability that *both* channels have failed:**

For two independent channels, the joint failure probability is the product:

$$P_{\text{both fail}}(t) = (\lambda_D t)^2$$

**Step 3 — Average over the proof-test interval:**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} = \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^{T_i} (\lambda_D t)^2 \, dt = \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^{T_i} \lambda_D^2 t^2 \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} = \frac{1}{T_i} \left[ \frac{\lambda_D^2 t^3}{3} \right]_0^{T_i} = \frac{\lambda_D^2 T_i^2}{3}$$

Wait — there is a subtlety. In a 1oo2 architecture, a *single* failure is **safe** (the other channel takes over) but it is also **detected** (diagnostic coverage is high). So the system is **as good as new** after each proof test for undetected failures. The exact formula (per IEC 61508-6) is:

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} \approx \frac{\lambda_D^2 T_i^2}{6}}$$

The factor of 1/6 (instead of 1/3) accounts for **diagnostics and the fact that only one channel needs to function**.

**Step 5 — Numerical example:**

Same transmitter, now in 1oo2 architecture. $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$/h, $T_i = 8760$ h.

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (8760)^2}{6} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-12} \times 7.67 \times 10^{7}}{6} = \frac{1.918 \times 10^{-3}}{6} = 3.20 \times 10^{-4}$$

This falls in **SIL 3** range ($10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$). Hence, **1oo2 architecture upgrades SIL by ~2 levels** vs. 1oo1 — this is the power of redundancy.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Liquid Leak Rate Through an Orifice

A **25 mm diameter** hole develops in a benzene storage tank. The liquid head is **8 m** above the leak. Benzene density = **876 kg/m³**. Compute the leak rate.

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**
- $d = 25$ mm = 0.025 m
- $h = 8$ m
- $\rho = 876$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $C_d = 0.61$ (sharp-edged orifice)

**Step 2 — Compute hole area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.91 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute hydrostatic pressure at the leak:**

$$\Delta P = \rho g h = 876 \times 9.81 \times 8 = 68{,}748 \text{ Pa} = 68.75 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the velocity term:**

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 68{,}748}{876}} = \sqrt{156.96} = 12.53 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the orifice equation:**

$$Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot v = 0.61 \times 4.91 \times 10^{-4} \times 12.53 = 3.75 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to mass flow:**

$$\dot{m} = Q \times \rho = 3.75 \times 10^{-3} \times 876 = 3.29 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to per hour:**

$$\dot{m} = 3.29 \times 3600 = 11{,}840 \text{ kg/h} \approx 11.8 \text{ tonnes/h}$$

**Step 8 — Practical interpretation:**
A leak rate of **11.8 tonnes/h** is catastrophic. This justifies a **1oo2 high-high level trip** (HHLL) on the tank + a **bund wall** of **110%** tank volume + **automatic isolation valve**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting the discharge coefficient $C_d$ and using $C_d = 1$. Always include $C_d = 0.61$ for sharp-edged orifices. (KTU valuator deducts 1 mark for this.)

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: TNT Equivalent for Vapor Cloud Explosion

A **5000 kg** propane cloud is released and ignites. Compute the **TNT equivalent** and the **overpressure at 200 m**.

**Step 1 — Required data:**
- $m = 5000$ kg
- $\Delta H_c$ (propane) = **46 MJ/kg**
- $E_{TNT} = 4.52$ MJ/kg
- Yield factor $\eta = 0.05$ (typical, 5%)
- Distance $r = 200$ m

**Step 2 — Compute TNT equivalent:**

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}} = \frac{0.05 \times 5000 \times 46}{4.52} = 2544 \text{ kg TNT}$$

**Step 3 — Compute scaled distance:**

The scaled distance is defined as:

$$Z = \frac{r}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} = \frac{200}{2544^{1/3}} = \frac{200}{13.65} = 14.65 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**Step 4 — Use the TNT overpressure correlation (Kingery-Bulmash):**

For $Z = 14.65$ m/kg$^{1/3}$, the side-on overpressure is approximately:

$$P_s \approx 0.04 \text{ bar} = 4 \text{ kPa}$$

(Interpolated from Kingery-Bulmash tables; for $Z \approx 15$, $P_s \approx 0.035$–$0.04$ bar.)

**Step 5 — Damage interpretation:**

| Overpressure | Damage |
|--------------|--------|
| **4 kPa** | Window breakage, minor injuries |
| **10 kPa** | Light structural damage |
| **35 kPa** | Severe structural damage |
| **70 kPa** | Total building destruction |

At 200 m, the overpressure is too low to cause fatalities but **windows will shatter**. This is why **window protection / blast-resistant glazing** is specified for control rooms within **200–300 m** of VCE sources.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Inherently Safer Design Comparison

**Scenario:** A reactor operates at **50 barg** and **200 °C** with a total inventory of **20 tonnes** of acrylonitrile (toxic, carcinogenic). Propose ISD improvements.

| Principle | Action | New Design |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| **Minimize** | Reduce reactor size by intensifying mixing | Inventory: 20 → **5 tonnes** |
| **Substitute** | Replace acrylonitrile with acetonitrile (less toxic) | Or redesign chemistry |
| **Moderate** | Lower operating pressure to 30 barg | Inventory risk reduced |
| **Simplify** | Eliminate intermediate storage tank; produce and consume in-line | Removes 30 m³ of toxic inventory |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** *The Inherently Safer Design question is almost always asked as a case study. Memorize the 4M framework: **Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify**.*

---

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix: Which Computer Aid to Use?

| Engineering Question | Recommended Computer Aid | Why |
|----------------------|---------------------------|-----|
| "What is the mass balance of my plant?" | Aspen Plus / HYSYS | Steady-state mass/energy balance |
| "What happens if the cooling fails?" | Aspen Dynamics / HYSYS Dynamics | Transient simulation |
| "What if a 2-inch line ruptures?" | ALOHA / PHAST | Consequence analysis |
| "What is the individual risk at fence line?" | SAFETI / FRED | QRA |
| "Are all my safeguards independent?" | LOPA software | LOPA |
| "What is the SIL for the ESD?" | exSILentia | SIL determination |
| "Where are the bottlenecks in my design?" | HAZOP software | HAZOP node analysis |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper 1)]**
*Define malfunction detection. List any three methods used for detecting process malfunctions in a chemical plant.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Malfunction detection is the systematic identification of deviations from normal operating conditions using sensors, instruments, alarms, and analytical techniques, with the objective of preventing escalation into hazardous scenarios. It is the first layer of process safety defense and operates under standards like **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511**.

**Three methods (1.5 marks, 0.5 each):**

1. **Limit Detection** — Triggering an alarm when a process variable crosses a preset threshold (e.g., high-high pressure alarm at 9.0 barg on a 10 barg vessel).
2. **Trend Detection** — Monitoring the rate of change of a variable (e.g., dP/dt > 0.5 barg/min).
3. **Model-Based Detection** — Comparing real-time measurements against a predicted value from a process model; deviations greater than 5% trigger an alarm.
