### 📕 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2]**

**(a) Explain in detail the different categories of computer aids used in process safety engineering, with at least two examples from each category.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Computer aids in process safety can be organized into **six major categories**:

**1. Steady-State Process Simulators (1 mark)**
- **Examples:** Aspen Plus, HYSYS, PRO/II, ChemCAD.
- **Purpose:** Compute mass and energy balances, determine stream compositions, and size equipment (columns, reactors, heat exchangers).
- **Application in Safety:** Ensures operating points are within safe envelopes; identifies hot spots in heat exchangers.

**2. Dynamic Process Simulators (1 mark)**
- **Examples:** Aspen Dynamics, HYSYS Dynamics, OLGA (multiphase flow).
- **Purpose:** Simulate transient behavior during start-up, shutdown, and upsets.
- **Application in Safety:** Models the response of a column to a cooling water failure; tests whether operator actions or interlocks are timely.

**3. Consequence Analysis Tools (1.5 marks)**
- **Examples:** ALOHA (EPA), PHAST (DNV), EFFECTS (TNO), FRED (Shell).
- **Purpose:** Calculate downwind concentrations, thermal radiation, and overpressure following a release.
- **Application in Safety:** Determines exclusion zones, escape route design, control room blast rating.

**4. Risk Quantification Tools (1 mark)**
- **Examples:** SAFETI (DNV), Shepherd (TNO), RiskSpectrum.
- **Purpose:** Combine consequence with frequency to compute individual and societal risk.
- **Application in Safety:** Plots F-N curves; compares against company risk criteria.

**5. LOPA & SIL Tools (1.5 marks)**
- **Examples:** exSILentia (exida), LOPA Tool (AIChE), RiskgraphPro.
- **Purpose:** Calculate PFD, evaluate layer of protection adequacy, and determine SIL requirements.
- **Application in Safety:** Justifies SIF (Safety Instrumented Function) architecture; verifies IEC 61511 compliance.

**6. HAZOP & Hazard Study Tools (1 mark)**
- **Examples:** PHA-Pro, HAZOPkit, bowtieXP, Studio HAZOP.
- **Purpose:** Facilitate structured team-based hazard identification; maintain a live register of safeguards and recommendations.
- **Application in Safety:** Standardizes deviation nodes; ensures traceability of decisions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip (7-mark question):** *A complete answer must include **6 categories** with **2 examples each** and **purpose/application**. A diagram relating each tool to the corresponding design question earns bonus marks.*

---

**(b) Explain with a neat sketch the architecture of a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) used for malfunction detection. Discuss the role of each component.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture of an SIS (refer to SECTION 4.2 diagram):**

A Safety Instrumented System (SIS) is a dedicated, independent system that takes the process to a safe state when a hazardous condition is detected. The architecture consists of **three layers**:

**Layer 1 — Input / Sensor Layer (2 marks):**
- Process variables (T, P, F, L) are sensed by field instruments.
- Common sensors: RTD/thermocouple (T), pressure transmitter (P), orifice plate (F), displacer/differential pressure level (L).
- These sensors provide 4–20 mA signals (analog) or 24 VDC digital signals.
- **Role:** To accurately measure the process variable and convert it into an electrical signal for the logic solver.
- **Redundancy:** Critical sensors are often 1oo2 or 2oo3 for high SIL.

**Layer 2 — Logic Solver Layer (3 marks):**
- The logic solver receives input signals and applies pre-programmed Boolean logic to determine whether a trip is required.
- Examples: **Safety PLC** (e.g., Triconex, Honeywell FSC), **hard-wired relay logic**, or **DCS-based logic** (less preferred for SIL 3).
- The logic is implemented in fail-safe manner (de-energize to trip).
- **Role:** To make a deterministic decision (trip or not trip) based on inputs.
- **Certification:** Must be TÜV-certified for the target SIL (e.g., SIL 3 per IEC 61508).

**Layer 3 — Final Control Element Layer (2 marks):**
- These elements physically execute the shutdown action.
- Examples: **Block valves** (pneumatically actuated, spring-return), **dump valves** (open on de-energization), **motor starters** (stop pumps), **fire-water pumps** (start on demand).
- **Role:** To convert the logic solver's signal into mechanical action that brings the process to a safe state.
- **Critical Property:** Valves are specified as "fail-close" (FC) or "fail-open" (FO) depending on safety function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Principle: De-energize to Trip (DTT):** SIS design convention is that loss of signal energy (air, power) causes the safe action. This is because wire breaks, air failures, and power outages are *more likely* than spurious operation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing SIS with DCS (Distributed Control System).** SIS is *independent* and *separate* from DCS. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Not stating "de-energize to trip"** convention. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Skipping the role of the logic solver** and going straight to valves. (–1 mark)
> 4. **No diagram** — for a 7-mark question, a sketch is **mandatory**. (–2 marks if absent)
> 5. **Not mentioning SIL certification** of the logic solver. (–1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model Paper 2 — Alternative Choice)]

**(a) Discuss in detail the aids used for representing regulatory and code requirements in process plant design. Explain the structure of a Code Compliance Matrix (CCM) with an example.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Process plants must comply with a vast array of regulations and codes — OSHA PSM, EPA RMP, ASME BPVC, API, NFPA, IEC 61511, ISO 31000, and local jurisdictional laws. Failure to comply results in legal liability, operational shutdown, and safety incidents. **Aids for representing regulatory and code requirements** translate these legal texts into actionable engineering specifications.

**Categories of Regulatory and Code Aids (3 marks, 1.5 each):**

**Category A — Code Compliance Matrix (CCM):**
A CCM is a tabular document that maps every regulatory clause to:
- The corresponding design element.
- The responsible discipline (Process, Mechanical, Electrical, Instrumentation, Civil).
- The verification method (calculation, inspection, test, audit).
- The closure status.

**Example row:**
| Standard | Clause | Requirement | Design Element | Discipline | Verification | Status |
|----------|--------|-------------|----------------|------------|--------------|--------|
| **API 521** | §4.3.6 | Two-phase relief sizing | PSV-101 | Process | Calc. sheet 521-CS-001 | Open |
| **ASME BPVC** | UG-101 | Corrosion allowance 1.5 mm | Vessel V-101 thickness | Mechanical | Mech. calc. report | Closed |
| **NFPA 30** | §4.3.1.1 | Tank-to-tank spacing | Plot plan | Layout | Layout dwg. PL-001 | Closed |
| **OSHA 1910.119** | (l) | MOC procedure | MOC system | Project Eng. | Audit report | Open |

**Category B — Design Basis Memorandum (DBM):**
A DBM captures *all* regulatory and code constraints in a single design document, including:
- Statutory requirements (e.g., factory inspector, pollution control board).
- Client standards (Shell DEP, Saudi Aramco SAMES).
- Insurance requirements (e.g., FM Global).
- Codes by discipline (Piping → ASME B31.3; Pressure vessels → ASME Sec VIII; Electrical → IEC/NEC).

**Category C — Standards Cross-Reference Tables:**
For complex projects, a cross-reference table links standards that *overlap* on a topic. For example, NFPA 30, API 2510, and OSHA 1910.106 all address flammable liquid storage — a cross-reference table identifies which is most stringent in each aspect.

**Why These Aids Are Critical (3 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Traceability:** Every regulatory requirement can be traced to a specific design element and owner. This supports audits and incident investigations.
2. **Completeness:** Engineers cannot "miss" a requirement because the matrix prompts them.
3. **Communication:** Non-technical stakeholders (regulators, clients, auditors) can quickly verify compliance.

**Other Aids (briefly, 1 mark):**
- **Regulatory databases** (IHS, IChemE, NFPA online).
- **Plot plan checklists** (spacing, drainage, fire-water).
- **Piping class sheets** (material spec, ASME B31.3 class).
- **SIS architecture documents** (per IEC 61511).

---

**(b) Explain the concept of Inherently Safer Design (ISD) with reference to Kletz's four principles. Apply the principles to reduce the hazard of a 20-tonne acrylonitrile reactor operating at 50 barg and 200 °C.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of ISD (1 mark):**
Inherently Safer Design (ISD), pioneered by **Trevor Kletz**, is a philosophy of *permanently* and *inherently* reducing hazards by *eliminating* them or *reducing* the amount of hazardous material, energy, or process complexity. Unlike engineered safeguards (alarms, relief valves) that can *fail*, ISD removes the hazard at the design stage.

**Kletz's Four Principles — The 4M Hierarchy (3 marks, 0.75 each):**

| Principle | Meaning | Type of Hazard Reduced |
|-----------|---------|--------------------------|
| **Minimize** | Use the smallest possible inventory of hazardous material | Reduces the consequence of a release |
| **Substitute** | Replace a hazardous material with a less hazardous one | Reduces the intrinsic hazard |
| **Moderate** | Use less hazardous process conditions (lower T, P, concentration) | Reduces the driving force for a release |
| **Simplify** | Eliminate unnecessary complexity and equipment | Reduces human error and equipment failure modes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of Preference:** Kletz's hierarchy is **Minimize > Substitute > Moderate > Simplify > Add-on Safeguards**. ISD should always be *attempted first*, before adding engineered controls.

**Application to the 20-tonne Acrylonitrile Reactor (3 marks, 0.75 each principle):**

| Principle | Applied Action | New Design Outcome |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Minimize** | Intensify mixing and reaction kinetics; redesign with a **continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR)** instead of batch | Reduce reactor inventory from 20 tonnes to **5 tonnes** — risk reduced 4× |
| **Substitute** | Replace acrylonitrile (toxic, carcinogenic, Class A) with **acetonitrile** or modify the chemistry to use a less toxic nitrile | Hazard class reduced |
| **Moderate** | Operate at **30 barg** and **150 °C** instead of 50 barg / 200 °C | Lower driving force for rupture; lower vapor pressure reduces release rate |
| **Simplify** | Eliminate the **intermediate storage tank**; produce and consume acrylonitrile in-line; use **gravity feed** instead of high-pressure pumps | Removes 30 m³ of toxic inventory; reduces pump failure frequency |

**Final Outcome:**
After applying all four principles, the residual hazard inventory is reduced by ~80%, the operating pressure is halved, and the process complexity is reduced. The *need* for engineered safeguards (PSV, ESD, firewater) is *correspondingly reduced*, although they are still recommended as defense-in-depth.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Add Safeguards" with "Inherently Safer."** ISD = remove hazard; safeguards = add protection. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Not memorizing the 4M.** Students who write "Minimization, Substitution, Moderation, Simplification" verbatim earn full credit. (–1 mark)
> 3. **No numerical/quantitative application.** A case study must have *specific numbers* (e.g., 20 → 5 tonnes, 50 → 30 barg). (–1 mark)
> 4. **Not mentioning Kletz by name.** (–0.5 mark)

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 10-Point KTU 2024 Module 2 Rapid-Revision Block:**

1. **Malfunction Detection = Sensor → Logic Solver → Final Element.** Remember **De-energize to Trip (DTT)**. Know the equation:
$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \lambda_D T_i \quad \text{(1oo1)},\quad \text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} \approx \lambda_D^2 T_i^2 / 6$$

2. **SIL Levels:** SIL 1 ($10^{-2}$–$10^{-1}$), SIL 2 ($10^{-3}$–$10^{-2}$), SIL 3 ($10^{-4}$–$10^{-3}$) — per **IEC 61508 / 61511**.

3. **Six Categories of Computer Aids:** Steady-state sim, Dynamic sim, Consequence (ALOHA/PHAST), QRA (SAFETI/FRED), LOPA/SIL, HAZOP tools.

4. **Project Aids = Phased Reviews:** CHA → HAZID → HAZOP → LOPA → SIL → PSSR → Periodic HAZOP. Know the **output document** at each phase.

5. **Regulatory and Code Aids = CCM, DBM, Cross-Reference Tables, Piping Class Sheets.** Memorize the **structure of CCM** (Clause → Design Element → Owner → Verification → Status).

6. **Key Standards (write all 7 in exam):** OSHA 1910.119, EPA 40 CFR 68, ASME BPVC, API 521/520, NFPA 30, IEC 61511, ISO 31000.

7. **ISD = 4M:** **Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify.** Kletz's hierarchy; apply *before* add-on safeguards.

8. **Layout Numbers to Memorize:** Fire-water coverage **30–45 m**; foam pour rate **4.1 L/min/m²**; alarm rate **<150/operator/h** (EEMUA 191).

9. **Consequence Equations:** Liquid leak $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ with **$C_d = 0.61$**; TNT equivalent $W_{TNT} = \eta m \Delta H_c / E_{TNT}$ with **$E_{TNT} = 4.52$ MJ/kg**.

10. **SIS ≠ DCS.** SIS is independent, TÜV-certified, and operates on **de-energize to trip** principle. DCS is for *control*, not *safety*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instrumentation in Safety Applications - Applications of Advanced Equipment and Instruments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Instrumentation in Safety Applications - Applications of Advanced Equipment and Instruments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advanced Safety Instrumentation** refers to the integrated deployment of high-precision, microprocessor-based, and often networked electro-optical, electro-chemical, and electromechanical devices designed to continuously monitor, detect, measure, record, and warn against hazardous physical, chemical, thermal, and radiological conditions within industrial, environmental, and occupational environments.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, this term is **strictly differentiated from "basic" instruments** (like a simple thermometer or hand-held combustible gas indicator) by three mandatory characteristics:

1. **Quantification capability** — Output is a calibrated, traceable numerical value (ppm, °C, dB, mSv, %LEL).
2. **Real-time actuation logic** — Operates within a defined response time (typically **< 30 s** for toxic gases, **< 1 s** for flame).
3. **Integration potential** — Designed to interface with SCADA / DCS / PLC / SIL-rated shutdown systems (per IEC 61508 / IEC 61511).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sensory Nervous System of a Plant"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a modern chemical plant as a human body. The **reactor, vessels, and pipelines are the "organs"**. A *basic safety instrument* (e.g., a pressure gauge) is like a single nerve ending — it tells you something hurts. An **Advanced Safety Instrument** is the **entire nervous system wired to a reflex arc**: pressure sensors (baroreceptors), gas detectors (olfactory nerves), thermal cameras (skin thermoreceptors), and flame detectors (visual cortex), all coordinated to initiate an *automatic protective response* (a hand pulling away from fire) before the conscious "brain" (operator) even knows there's danger.

This analogy is critical for KTU exam answers because the examiners reward students who can articulate *functional integration*, not just list devices.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Performance Indicators

Every advanced safety instrument is evaluated using these **mandatory KTU-recognized metrics**:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Industrial Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lower Explosive Limit | **LEL** | **% by volume** | 0 – 100 % |
| Threshold Limit Value | **TLV** | **ppm** | 0.5 – 1000 ppm |
| Immediately Dangerous to Life & Health | **IDLH** | **ppm** | 10 – 10000 ppm |
| Permissible Exposure Limit | **PEL** | **ppm or mg/m³** | 0.1 – 500 ppm |
| Sound Pressure Level | **SPL** | **dB(A)** | 60 – 140 dB(A) |
| Dose Equivalent (Radiation) | **H** | **mSv** | 0 – 50 mSv/yr (worker) |
| Response Time (T90) | **t₉₀** | **seconds (s)** | 0.5 – 60 s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always write units in **SI base or accepted derived units**. Writing "ppm" alone (dimensionless) is acceptable, but writing "ppm(v)" or "ppm(v/v)" for *volume-to-volume ratio* earns a **½ mark bonus** for precision.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Advanced Safety Instruments

The KTU 2024 syllabus organizes advanced safety instruments into **four functional families**. Understanding this taxonomy is the single most important first step.

### 2.1.1 Family A — Atmospheric Hazard Monitors
These instruments detect **flammable, toxic, or asphyxiant gases/vapours** dispersed in air.

- **Catalytic Bead (Pellistor) Sensor:** A heated platinum coil causes catalytic oxidation of combustible gas; the resulting temperature rise changes coil resistance (Wheatstone bridge imbalance).
- **Infrared (NDIR) Gas Detector:** Uses the **Beer–Lambert Law** of selective molecular absorption at a characteristic IR wavelength (e.g., **4.26 µm** for CO₂, **3.3 µm** for hydrocarbons).
- **Electrochemical Sensor:** Target gas diffuses through a membrane and undergoes a redox reaction at a working electrode, generating a current proportional to concentration (**Faraday's Law**).
- **Photo-Ionization Detector (PID):** A UV lamp (typically **10.6 eV**) ionizes gas molecules; the resulting ion current is measured.
- **Metal Oxide Semiconductor (MOS) Sensor:** Tin dioxide (SnO₂) surface adsorbs oxygen; exposure to reducing gas changes conductivity.

### 2.1.2 Family B — Fire & Thermal Event Detectors
- **Ultraviolet (UV) Flame Detector:** Detects photons in the **185–260 nm** band emitted by hydrocarbon flames.
- **Infrared (IR) Flame Detector:** Detects CO₂ emission band at **4.4 µm** with characteristic flicker frequency (**1–10 Hz**).
- **Dual / Triple Spectrum (IR/IR/UV):** Uses two or three wavelengths for **false-alarm rejection** (solar-blind logic).
- **Aspirating Smoke Detector (ASD):** Actively draws air through a network of pipes to a highly sensitive laser-based scatter chamber (**sensitivity 0.005 %/m obscuration**).

### 2.1.3 Family C — Mechanical Integrity & Process Monitors
- **Vibration Analyser / Accelerometer (IEPE-type):** Measures bearing vibration in **g-RMS or mm/s** per ISO 10816.
- **Acoustic Emission (AE) Sensor:** Detects high-frequency stress waves (**20 kHz – 1 MHz**) from crack propagation.
- **Partial Discharge (PD) Detector:** Detects electrical insulation degradation in HV equipment via UHF or HFCT methods.

### 2.1.4 Family D — Radiological & PPE-Integrated
- **Geiger-Müller (GM) Tube / Scintillation Detector** for ionizing radiation.
- **Electronic Personal Dosimeter (EPD)** for real-time worker dose tracking.
- **Smart PPE** with embedded IoT sensors (heart rate, temperature, fall detection, gas exposure logging).

## 2.2 The Beer–Lambert Law (Foundation of IR & UV Detection)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most-tested equation** in KTU papers for this module. Memorize its derivation and every variable.

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot c \cdot L}$$

Where:
- $I_0$ = Intensity of incident light (**W/m²**)
- $I$ = Intensity of transmitted light (**W/m²**)
- $\alpha$ = Molar absorption coefficient (**m²/mol**)
- $c$ = Concentration of absorbing gas (**mol/m³**)
- $L$ = Optical path length through gas cell (**m**)

**Inverse form (used by detector to compute concentration):**

$$c = -\frac{1}{\alpha \cdot L} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha \cdot L} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

**The Absorbance (A)** is a dimensionless quantity often used in datasheets:

$$A = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot L$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the **decadic molar absorption coefficient** (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹). Note: $\alpha = 2.303 \cdot \varepsilon$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse the symbols $\alpha$ (Napierian coefficient) and $\varepsilon$ (decadic coefficient). Examiners award a **negative mark** for using the wrong base in logs.

| Equation | Application Domain | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha c L}$ | NDIR gas detection | $I, I_0$: W/m², $\alpha$: m²/mol, $c$: mol/m³, $L$: m |
| $c = \frac{1}{\alpha L}\ln(I_0/I)$ | Concentration from IR absorbance | Same as above |
| $\Delta R = R_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T$ | Catalytic bead response | $R$: $\Omega$, $\beta$: K⁻¹ |
| $I = n \cdot z \cdot F$ | Electrochemical current | $I$: A, $n$: mol/s, $z$: electrons, $F$=**96485 C/mol** |
| $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$ | Sound pressure level | $L_p$: dB, $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ |
| $H = D \cdot Q$ | Radiation dose equivalent | $H$: Sv, $D$: Gy, $Q$: quality factor |
| $t_{90} = t_{r(\text{final})} - t_{r(0.9\cdot\text{final})}$ | Sensor response time | $t_{90}$: s |
| $\%\text{LEL} = \frac{C_{\text{measured}}}{C_{\text{LEL}}} \times 100$ | Combustible gas scaling | Dimensionless ratio |
| $A_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\sum 10^{L_i/10}\right)$ | Combined noise from multiple sources | $A_{eq}$: dB(A) |
| $T_{IT} = \frac{8}{2^{n-1}}$ | Time-Intensity Trade (TIT) for alarm | $T_{IT}$: hours, $n$: no. of doublings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Faraday constant F = 96,485 C/mol** is one of the most frequently tested constants in KTU safety papers. Commit it to memory.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical Refineries:** Use NDIR + catalytic bead **dual-technology** detectors on tank farms; catalytic bead detects all combustible gases but is **poisoned by silicones and lead**, so NDIR serves as the redundant check.
- **Semiconductor Cleanrooms:** Use **PIDs (10.6 eV lamp)** to detect photoresist solvents (e.g., PGMEA) at sub-ppb levels.
- **Power Generation:** Use **UHF PD detectors** in Gas-Insulated Switchgear (GIS) to detect insulation defects before catastrophic failure.
- **Mining:** Use **multi-gas detectors** (CH₄, O₂, CO, H₂S) with **wireless mesh telemetry** to protect underground workers.
- **Nuclear Facilities:** Use **whole-body dosimeters + area monitors** networked to a central ALARA database.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Concentration from Beer–Lambert Law for an NDIR Methane Detector

> [!NOTE]
> The following worked derivation is a **direct-style KTU numerical**. Practice writing each step exactly as shown — partial marks are awarded line-by-line.

### Problem Statement
An NDIR detector uses a 0.20 m path length and a 3.31 µm wavelength filter. The molar absorption coefficient of CH₄ at this wavelength is **$\alpha = 12.5\ \text{m}^2/\text{mol}$**. The incident intensity is $I_0 = 100.0$ (arbitrary units), and the measured transmitted intensity is $I = 60.65$. Calculate the methane concentration in **mol/m³** and convert to **ppm(v)** at STP (T = 273.15 K, P = 101.325 kPa).

### Step 1 — Write the governing equation
The intensity of light transmitted through an absorbing gas cell follows:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot c \cdot L}$$

### Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides
Dividing both sides by $I_0$ first:

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\alpha c L}$$

Applying $\ln(\cdot)$ on both sides:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\alpha \cdot c \cdot L$$

### Step 3 — Multiply both sides by $-1$ to isolate the negative sign
$$-\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = \alpha \cdot c \cdot L$$

This is mathematically equivalent to:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \alpha \cdot c \cdot L$$

### Step 4 — Solve algebraically for $c$
$$c = \frac{1}{\alpha \cdot L} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the numerical values
$$c = \frac{1}{(12.5\ \text{m}^2/\text{mol}) \cdot (0.20\ \text{m})} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{100.0}{60.65}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Evaluate the logarithm
$$\ln(100.0 / 60.65) = \ln(1.6488) = 0.5000$$

### Step 7 — Compute denominator
$$\alpha \cdot L = 12.5 \times 0.20 = 2.500\ \text{m}^3/\text{mol}$$

### Step 8 — Final concentration
$$c = \frac{0.5000}{2.500} = 0.200\ \text{mol}/\text{m}^3$$

### Step 9 — Convert mol/m³ to ppm(v)
Using the ideal gas relation at STP, 1 mol occupies **0.02241 m³**. The volumetric fraction in ppm(v) is:

$$\text{ppm(v)} = c \cdot V_m \times 10^6 = 0.200 \times 0.02241 \times 10^6$$

$$\text{ppm(v)} = 4482\ \text{ppm(v)}$$

### Step 10 — Cross-check against LEL of methane
LEL(CH₄) = **5.0 % by volume = 50,000 ppm(v)**. The %LEL reading is:

$$\%\text{LEL} = \frac{4482}{50000} \times 100 = 8.96\ \%\text{LEL}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The detector reading of **8.96 % LEL** is **below the 10 % LEL alarm threshold** (typical low alarm) but **above 5 % LEL** (typical advisory). No immediate shutdown — operator notification only. **This 5-step logic chain is exactly what examiners want to see.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Electrochemical Sensor Current from Faraday's Law

The current produced by an electrochemical gas sensor follows **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis**:

$$I = n \cdot z \cdot F$$

Where:
- $I$ = current (**A**)
- $n$ = molar flow rate of gas to electrode (**mol/s**)
- $z$ = number of electrons transferred per molecule (dimensionless)
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**

**Worked numerical:**
A CO sensor uses the half-reaction $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$. The mass flow rate of CO into the sensor is **$1.04 \times 10^{-9}$ mol/s** (corresponding to 50 ppm exposure).

**Step 1 — Identify $z$:** From the half-reaction, $z = 2$.

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$I = (1.04 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 2 \cdot 96485$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**
$$I = 2.008 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{A} = 200.8\ \mu\text{A}$$

This microampere-level current is then converted by a **transimpedance amplifier** to a 4–20 mA signal for the DCS.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Detector Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** A **2-mark short-answer** may ask: "Compare PID and NDIR for VOC detection." The table below is the **model answer format**.

| Parameter | **Catalytic Bead** | **NDIR** | **PID** | **Electrochemical** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Target | All combustible | Specific IR-active gas | VOCs (ionization < lamp eV) | Specific toxic gas |
| Selectivity | Poor | Excellent | Moderate | Excellent |
| Poison resistance | Low (Si, Pb, S) | Excellent | Good | Moderate |
| O₂ required? | **Yes** | No | No | No |
| Typical t₉₀ | 20–60 s | 5–30 s | 3–10 s | 15–60 s |
| Drift/year | High | Very low | Moderate | Moderate |
| Calibration interval | 1–3 months | 6–12 months | 1 month | 1–3 months |
| Cost | Low | High | High | Moderate |

---

## 3.4 Derived Quantity: LEL Alarm Threshold from Stoichiometry

For a hydrocarbon $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y$ burned in air:
$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**LEL by volume (ideal stoichiometric):**

$$\%\text{LEL} = \frac{1}{1 + 4.773\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)} \times 100$$

**Worked example for propane (C₃H₈):** $x = 3$, $y = 8$, so $x + y/4 = 5$. Theoretical LFL = $100/(1 + 4.773 \times 5) = 4.03\%$. Experimental value = **2.1 %**. The factor 4.773 accounts for the O₂/N₂ ratio in air (21/79 × molar volume ratio).

> [!NOTE]
> **This relationship is asked verbatim** in KTU December 2023 and June 2024 past papers. Know it cold.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Distributed Safety Monitoring System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Field Layer: Hazardous Zone] --> B[Sensor Cluster Layer]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Layer]
    C --> D[Controller Layer]
    D --> E[Actuation Layer]
    E --> F[Final Element Protection]
    D --> G[Operator HMI Layer]
    D --> H[Alarm Management Layer]
    H --> I[Event Logger and Historian]
    G --> J[Emergency Shutdown Logic]

    A1[Gas: CH4 CO H2S] --> A
    A2[Fire: UV IR Flame] --> A
    A3[Process: P T Flow Level] --> A
    A4[Radiation: Gamma Neutron] --> A
    A5[Vibration: Accelerometer] --> A

    B1[NDIR Detector] --> B
    B2[PID Detector] --> B
    B3[Triple IR Flame Detector] --> B
    B4[Scintillation Probe] --> B
    B5[IEPE Accelerometer] --> B

    C1[4-20 mA Transmitter] --> C
    C2[Hart Protocol Modem] --> C
    C3[WirelessHART Gateway] --> C

    D1[SIL 2 Safety PLC] --> D
    D2[SIL 3 Logic Solver] --> D

    E1[Solenoid Valves] --> E
    E2[Motor Contactors] --> E
    E3[Fire Suppression Dampers] --> E
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Multi-Sensor Fire Confirmation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start([Process Running]) --> S1{Sensor 1: IR Flame Triggered?}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2{Sensor 2: UV Confirmed Within 5 s?}
    S1 -- No --> Wait[Wait 100 ms Poll Cycle]
    Wait --> S1
    S2 -- Yes --> S3{Sensor 3: Aspirating Smoke Confirmed?}
    S2 -- No --> Hold[Hold Alarm 30 s for Re-evaluation]
    Hold --> S2
    S3 -- Yes --> Trip[Activate Fire Suppression and Isolation]
    S3 -- No --> Alert[Raise Operator Alert Level 2]
    Trip --> End([Emergency Shutdown Initiated])
    Alert --> Monitor[Continued Monitoring]
    Monitor --> S1
```

## 4.3 Reading the Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU 14-mark answer:** The flow demonstrates the **voting logic** (typically **1-out-of-2 or 2-out-of-3**) demanded by IEC 61508 SIL ratings. A single spurious signal is *insufficient* to trigger shutdown — confirmation from an independent sensor technology (optical + ionization) is required. This **coincidence logic** is the cornerstone of modern Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS).

| Block | Real Hardware | Typical Make/Model (Generic) |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor Cluster | Toxic + Combustible Detector | Single multi-head unit |
| Signal Conditioning | Barrier + Transmitter | Galvanic isolator |
| Controller | Safety PLC | Triple-modular redundant |
| Final Element | Solenoid + SOV | SIL 3 certified actuator |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between catalytic bead and NDIR gas detectors. State two specific advantages of NDIR over catalytic bead."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Catalytic Bead:** Uses catalytic oxidation on a heated platinum coil; resistance change is proportional to gas concentration. Requires **O₂** to function. Output is non-specific (responds to all combustible gases).
- **NDIR:** Uses selective **absorption of infrared radiation** at a gas-specific wavelength (e.g., 4.4 µm for CO₂). Does not require O₂. Output is **highly selective**.
- **Two advantages of NDIR:** **(1)** Immune to **catalytic poisons** (silicones, lead, sulfur); **(2)** Longer **calibration interval** (6–12 months vs. 1–3 months); **(3) bonus point** No risk of **sensor burnout** in lean gas mixtures.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**"Define the term 'Lower Explosive Limit' (LEL). If methane has an LEL of 5.0 % v/v, what concentration corresponds to 25 % LEL alarm setting?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition:** LEL is the **minimum concentration of a flammable gas or vapour in air** below which propagation of a flame does not occur on contact with a source of ignition. **[1 mark]**
- **Conversion formula:** $\%\text{LEL} = (C_{\text{meas}}/C_{\text{LEL}}) \times 100$ **[1 mark]**
- **Calculation:** $C_{\text{meas}} = 0.25 \times 5.0 = 1.25\ \%\text{v/v} = 12{,}500\ \text{ppm(v)}$ **[1 mark]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

> **(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a modern fixed gas detection system** used in a hydrocarbon processing plant. Label all field, control, and actuation layers. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** A photo-ionization detector (PID) with a **10.6 eV lamp** is used to monitor isopropyl alcohol (IPA, **C₃H₈O**, IP = 10.48 eV) vapour in a cleanroom. The measured ion current is **$3.85 \times 10^{-10}$ A**. If the electron yield per molecule is $z = 1$ and the sensor flow rate is **$2.0 \times 10^{-6}$ mol/s of air**, calculate the **mole fraction of IPA** in the sampled air. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):
1. **Field Layer:** Point gas detectors (NDIR for hydrocarbons, EC for H₂S, PID for VOCs) installed at grade level, equipment levels, and ventilation ducts per **IEC 60079-29-2**. **[1 mark]**
2. **Sensor Cluster:** Multiple detectors grouped in zones; each with a **junction box** for power and signal. **[1 mark]**
3. **Signal Conditioning:** **4–20 mA** current loop with **HART** protocol; signals routed via **safety barriers** to control room. **[1 mark]**
4. **Controller Layer:** **Triple-Modular Redundant Safety PLC** (SIL 2/3) implementing voting logic. **[1 mark]**
5. **Actuation Layer:** Output modules trigger **solenoid valves** (gas isolation), **fire dampers**, and **deluge valves**. **[1 mark]**
6. **HMI / Event Logging:** Operator stations display real-time trends, alarms, and **first-up indication** with time-stamped **SOE (Sequence of Events)** recording. **[1 mark]**
7. **Conclusion:** Architecture must follow **defence-in-depth** and **independent layer of protection** (LOPA) principles per **IEC 61511**. **[1 mark]**

#### Model Solution for (b):
**Step 1 — State the governing equation** (Faraday's Law of Electrolysis):

$$I = n_{\text{IPA}} \cdot z \cdot F$$

**Step 2 — Solve for molar flow of IPA:**

$$n_{\text{IPA}} = \frac{I}{z \cdot F} = \frac{3.85 \times 10^{-10}}{1 \times 96485}$$

$$n_{\text{IPA}} = 3.99 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{mol/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total molar flow (air + IPA):** Since IPA is at trace levels, the total flow is dominated by air:

$$n_{\text{total}} \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{mol/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute mole fraction:**

$$x_{\text{IPA}} = \frac{n_{\text{IPA}}}{n_{\text{total}}} = \frac{3.99 \times 10^{-15}}{2.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.995 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to ppm(v):**

$$x_{\text{IPA}} = 1.995 \times 10^{-9} \times 10^6 = 1.995 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{ppm(v)} \approx 2.0\ \text{ppb(v)}$$

**Step 6 — Note ionization feasibility:** Since lamp energy (10.6 eV) > IP of IPA (10.48 eV), ionization is **thermodynamically possible** (margin of 0.12 eV). **[Validation step, ½ mark]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students forget to convert the mole fraction to **ppm(v) or ppb(v)**. The numerical answer `1.995 × 10⁻⁹` (dimensionless) alone earns only **5/7 marks**; the **unit conversion is mandatory** for full marks.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

> **(a)** Describe the **principle, construction, and working of an aspirating smoke detection (ASD) system**. Compare its sensitivity with conventional point smoke detectors. State **two applications** where ASD is preferred. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** A workshop has **three machines** producing sound levels of **85 dB(A)**, **88 dB(A)**, and **90 dB(A)** simultaneously. Calculate the **equivalent continuous sound level L_eq** assuming equal exposure duration. The OSHA permissible exposure limit (PEL) is **90 dB(A) for 8 hours** with a **5 dB exchange rate**. Determine the **maximum allowed daily exposure time** for the measured L_eq. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):
- **Principle (1.5 marks):** ASD actively draws air samples from the protected zone through a network of small-bore pipes (typically PVC, **20–25 mm OD**). Air is filtered and passed through a **laser-based scatter chamber** (using a **laser diode at 650 nm**). Smoke particles scatter light; a **photodiode** detects scattered light; the obscuration level is calculated.
- **Construction (1.5 marks):** Includes **aspirator fan**, **filter**, **detection chamber** (with laser + photodiode), **pipe network** with sampling holes, and **display/control unit**.
- **Working (1 mark):** Air velocity maintained at **0.5–1.5 m/s**; sensitivity adjustable from **0.005 %/m to 20 %/m** obscuration.
- **Comparison (1.5 marks):** Conventional point detector sensitivity = **3–5 %/m** obscuration; ASD sensitivity up to **0.005 %/m** (i.e., **600× more sensitive**). ASD also offers **multiple sampling holes** = one detector covers area of many points.
- **Applications (1.5 marks):** **1.** Cleanrooms and semiconductor fabs (contamination-free). **2.** Cable tunnels / data centers (very early warning). **3.** Cold storage (heater-free sampling). **(any two)**

#### Model Solution for (b):
**Step 1 — Equivalent L_eq from multiple sources:**

$$L_{eq} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} 10^{L_i/10}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Convert each dB to linear power ratio:**

$$10^{85/10} = 10^{8.5} = 3.16 \times 10^{8}$$

$$10^{88/10} = 10^{8.8} = 6.31 \times 10^{8}$$

$$10^{90/10} = 10^{9.0} = 1.00 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the linear ratios:**

$$\sum 10^{L_i/10} = (3.16 + 6.31 + 1.00) \times 10^{8} = 10.47 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 4 — Take log and multiply by 10:**

$$L_{eq} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(10.47 \times 10^{8}) = 10 \cdot (8 + 0.0200) = 80.20\ \text{dB(A)} + 20 \cdot \log_{10}(\sqrt{10.47})$$

Re-computing cleanly:

$$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}(1.047 \times 10^{9}) = 10 \cdot (9 + \log_{10}(1.047)) = 10 \cdot (9 + 0.0200) = 90.20\ \text{dB(A)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the OSHA Time-Intensity Trade (TIT) formula:**

$$T_{allowed} = \frac{T_{ref}}{2^{(L_{eq} - L_{ref})/q}}$$

Where $T_{ref} = 8$ hours, $L_{ref} = 90$ dB(A), and $q = 5$ dB (OSHA exchange rate).

**Step 6 — Substitute:**

$$T_{allowed} = \frac{8}{2^{(90.20 - 90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{0.04}}$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate $2^{0.04}$:**

$$2^{0.04} = e^{0.04 \cdot \ln 2} = e^{0.04 \times 0.6931} = e^{0.02773} = 1.0281$$

**Step 8 — Final exposure time:**

$$T_{allowed} = \frac{8}{1.0281} = 7.78\ \text{hours}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Three common mistakes here: **(1)** Using NIOSH's **3 dB exchange rate** instead of OSHA's **5 dB** — read the question carefully. **(2)** Forgetting to convert dB to **linear scale** before summation (decibel values are *logarithmic* and **cannot be arithmetically added**). **(3)** Misidentifying the formula — students sometimes invert numerator/denominator, producing $T > 24$ h, which is physically impossible.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Print or screenshot this section the night before the exam. Each item is a **standalone, high-yield point**.

- [ ] **Beer–Lambert Law** $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha c L}$ is the foundation of **NDIR** gas detection.
- [ ] **Faraday's constant** $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$ — required for **electrochemical** current calculations.
- [ ] **PID** uses UV lamp energy **(eV)** to ionize gas; ionization **only occurs if** $E_{\text{lamp}} > \text{IP of gas}$.
- [ ] **LEL** is a **combustion property**; **TLV** is a **toxicology property**; they are **not interchangeable**.
- [ ] **OSHA TIT formula** uses **5 dB doubling rate**; **NIOSH / EU** uses **3 dB doubling rate**.
- [ ] **Response time t₉₀** = time to reach 90 % of final reading — **not** time to full response.
- [ ] **4–20 mA** signal: 4 mA = **zero**, 20 mA = **full scale**; **0 mA** = **sensor failure / open loop** (live zero).
- [ ] **SIL ratings** must be verified to **IEC 61508** (general) and **IEC 61511** (process industry).
- [ ] **Voting logic:** 1oo2 = 1 out of 2, 2oo3 = 2 out of 3 — affects PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand).
- [ ] **Catalytic bead** requires **O₂**; **poisoned by Si, Pb, S, P** compounds — choose NDIR if these are present.
- [ ] **IR flame detectors** use **4.4 µm CO₂ emission band**; **UV flame** uses **185–260 nm** band; **dual/triple** spectra for solar-blind operation.
- [ ] **Aspirating Smoke Detection (ASD)** sensitivity **0.005 %/m** is **~600× more sensitive** than conventional point detectors.
- [ ] **Vibration monitoring** follows **ISO 10816-3** for machinery; **> 4.5 mm/s RMS** on rigid foundations = alarm.
- [ ] **Dose equivalent** $H = D \cdot Q$ in **Sieverts (Sv)**; annual worker limit = **20 mSv** (averaged over 5 years).
- [ ] **Smart PPE** integrates **IoT sensors** for **real-time man-down, gas exposure, and heat-stress** monitoring — emerging 2024 syllabus trend.
- [ ] **Acoustic Emission** is **passive** (listens for cracks); **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** is **active** (transmits + receives) — do not confuse.
- [ ] **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) of common gases:** CH₄ = 5.0 %, propane = 2.1 %, hydrogen = 4.0 %, gasoline = 1.4 %.
- [ ] **Industrial Hygiene Hierarchy:** **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative → PPE** — instruments support the *engineering control* tier.

> **End of Notes — Module 3, Topic: Instrumentation in Safety Applications — Applications of Advanced Equipment and Instruments**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermo Calorimetry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermo Calorimetry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermo-Calorimetry** is the branch of thermochemistry concerned with the measurement of heat quantities evolved or absorbed during physical, chemical, or phase-change processes, applied within **Fire Safety Engineering (FSE)** to quantify the **thermal energy release of combustible materials and fires**. It forms the empirical foundation of **fire load calculation, HRR (Heat Release Rate) estimation, and reaction-to-fire classification** used in performance-based safety design.

In the context of **Design for Safety (PESFT412)**, thermo-calorimetry is not merely a chemistry lab exercise — it is a **predictive engineering tool** that quantifies how violently a material will burn, how much energy it will release, and how that energy will threaten life, structure, and egress pathways.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine you are buying firewood. Two piles may *look* identical, but one burns slowly for 8 hours and the other erupts in 20 minutes. You cannot tell the difference by appearance — you need to **measure** the energy each releases. A **calorimeter is the "energy weighing scale"** for fire: it tells you, in joules, exactly how much heat a material will hand over to your building, your occupants, and your suppression system.

Think of calorimetry as answering three fire-safety questions:

1. **How much total heat is locked inside the material?** (Total Heat of Combustion, $\Delta H_c$)
2. **How fast is that heat being released?** (Heat Release Rate, $\dot{Q}$ or HRR)
3. **What is the worst-case temperature rise in the compartment?** (Adiabatic / runaway behaviour)

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard reference pressure:** $P^0 = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- **Standard reference temperature:** $T^0 = 298.15 \text{ K}$ (25 °C)
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Huggett constant (Oxygen Consumption):** $E = 13.1 \text{ MJ/kg O}_2$ (this is the *engineering* constant that makes fire calorimetry possible)
- **Heat of combustion of methane (reference):** $\Delta H_c = -55.5 \text{ MJ/kg}$
- **Specific heat of water:** $c_w = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- **Air supply for complete combustion:** $\approx 5.3 \text{ kg air/kg fuel (stoichiometric for hydrocarbons)}$
- **Thornton’s rule (specific heat of gases):** $c_p \approx \frac{7}{2} R/M$ for diatomics

## 1.4 Classification of Calorimeters (At a Glance)

| Type | What it measures | Fire-safety use |
|---|---|---|
| **Bomb Calorimeter** (constant-volume) | Total $\Delta H_c$ of a fuel | Fire load ($MJ/m^2$) estimation |
| **Cone Calorimeter** (ISO 5660) | HRR, time-to-ignition, smoke, CO | Reaction-to-fire rating, code compliance |
| **Oxygen Consumption Calorimeter** | HRR via O$_2$ depletion (Huggett) | Full-scale room fire tests (ISO 9705) |
| **Adiabatic Calorimeter** (e.g. VSP, ARC) | Self-heating rate, $T_{NR}$ | Runaway reaction / chemical process safety |
| **Differential Scanning Calorimeter (DSC)** | Enthalpy changes vs. $T$ | Material screening, polymer decomposition |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** The **Cone Calorimeter** and **Oxygen Consumption Principle (Huggett's Relation)** are the most frequently tested items in Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physical Principle

Every calorimeter is governed by the **First Law of Thermodynamics (steady-state, closed system):**

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

The calorimeter's job is to either:
- **Trap** all the heat released by a reaction and measure the resulting temperature rise $\Delta T$ of a known medium (water, air, or metal block), or
- **Balance** the heat release against a controlled heat sink (e.g., electric heater compensation in Cone Calorimeter).

## 2.2 The Two Governing Quantities in Fire Calorimetry

### A. Total Heat of Combustion $\Delta H_c$
The absolute thermal energy released when a unit mass of fuel is completely oxidized. Determined in a **Bomb Calorimeter** (constant volume → $\Delta U_c$) and corrected to constant pressure via:

$$\Delta H_c = \Delta U_c + \Delta n_g \cdot R \cdot T$$

where $\Delta n_g$ = change in moles of gaseous products minus reactants.

### B. Heat Release Rate (HRR) — $\dot{Q}$
The **rate** at which heat is released per unit time. This is the single most important variable in fire safety design because **HRR drives temperature, smoke production, and flashover**.

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{dQ}{dt}$$

## 2.3 The Huggett Principle (Oxygen Consumption Calorimetry)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Huggett's Relation (1980):** For most organic fuels, the heat released per unit mass of oxygen consumed is approximately **constant** at $E \approx 13.1 \text{ MJ/kg O}_2$ — with a scatter of less than ±5 % across fuels ranging from methane to polyurethane foam.

This remarkable empirical constant is the foundation of modern fire calorimetry. It allows engineers to compute HRR simply by measuring the **oxygen depletion** in the exhaust duct:

$$\dot{Q} = E \cdot \left( \dot{m}_e \cdot Y_{O_2,e} - \dot{m}_i \cdot Y_{O_2,i} \right)$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_e, \dot{m}_i$ = exhaust and inlet mass flow rates (kg/s)
- $Y_{O_2,e}, Y_{O_2,i}$ = oxygen mass fractions in exhaust and inlet streams

## 2.4 Heat Release Rate & the Power Law of Fire Growth

A KTU-favourite — many past papers ask how HRR is used in **fire-growth modelling**:

$$\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha \cdot t^2$$

where $\alpha$ is the **fire growth coefficient** (kW/s²), classified as:

| Classification | $\alpha$ (kW/s²) | Example material |
|---|---|---|
| **Slow** | 0.00293 | Wood cribs, dense timber |
| **Medium** | 0.01172 | Cotton, PU mattress (early) |
| **Fast** | 0.0469 | Plastic stacked pallets, pool fire |
| **Ultra-fast** | 0.1876 | Methanol pool, gasoline spill |

**Time to flashover** $t_{fo}$ is reached when $\dot{Q}(t_{fo}) = 1000 \text{ kW}$ (NFPA 204M / ISO 9705 convention).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` notation instead of the pipe `|` to keep markdown tables intact.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat in a calorimeter medium | kJ |
| 2 | $\dot{Q} = \alpha \cdot t^2$ | t-squared fire growth | kW |
| 3 | $\dot{Q} = E \cdot \dot{m}_{O_2}$ | Huggett oxygen consumption | kW |
| 4 | $E = 13.1 \text{ MJ/kg O}_2$ | Huggett constant (organic fuels) | MJ/kg O$_2$ |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_{O_2} = \dot{m}_e \cdot \left( Y_{O_2,i} - Y_{O_2,e} \right)$ | Oxygen depletion (duct) | kg/s |
| 6 | $\Delta H_c = \Delta U_c + \Delta n_g \cdot R \cdot T$ | Bomb → constant-pressure correction | kJ/mol |
| 7 | $q^{\prime\prime} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{A_f}$ | HRR per unit area (cone test) | kW/m² |
| 8 | $L = \frac{\sum m_i \cdot \Delta H_{c,i}}{A_{floor}}$ | Fire load density | MJ/m² |
| 9 | $T_{NR} = T_0 + RT^2 / \phi$ | Onset of runaway (SADT) | K |
| 10 | $\phi = \frac{\dot{Q}_{gen}}{hA(T-T_{amb})}$ | Semenov thermal explosion criterion | dimensionless |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in Safety Design

1. **Fire Load Calculation** — Determines the equivalent fire duration $t_{eq}$ for structural fire resistance rating (per Eurocode 1991-1-2).
2. **Means of Egress Design** — ASET (Available Safe Egress Time) depends on HRR-driven smoke layer descent.
3. **Suppression System Sizing** — Sprinkler and gas-suppression design rates depend on the peak HRR.
4. **Process Safety** — Adiabatic calorimetry (ARC/VSP) is used to size relief valves for runaway reactions (DIERS methodology).
5. **Material Selection / Reaction-to-Fire** — Cone calorimeter data ($\dot{Q}^{\prime\prime}_{peak}$, THR, FIGRA) feed Euroclass ratings (A1–F).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — From First Law to the Bomb Calorimeter Equation

The Bomb Calorimeter is a **constant-volume, adiabatic** vessel. The fuel (mass $m_f$) is ignited in pure oxygen; the released energy raises the temperature of:
- the water jacket (mass $m_w$, specific heat $c_w$)
- the bomb + accessories (water equivalent $W$, K)

By the **First Law** (no work at constant volume → $\Delta U_c = Q_v$):

$$Q_v = m_f \cdot (-\Delta U_c) = (m_w \cdot c_w + W) \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step-by-step logical transition:**

1. Combustion is internal to the rigid bomb → boundary work is zero ($W_{bdy}=0$).
2. Therefore the heat evolved equals the internal energy change: $Q_v = -\Delta U_c$.
3. All this energy is absorbed by the surrounding medium at constant pressure (jacket is open to atmosphere).
4. Conservation of energy: heat released = heat gained by water + bomb hardware.
5. Define **water equivalent** $W$ such that the bomb and stirrer behave thermally as an equivalent mass of water.
6. Solve for $\Delta U_c$ per kg of fuel.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{released} &= Q_{absorbed} \\[4pt]
m_f \cdot (-\Delta U_c) &= (m_w c_w + W) \cdot \Delta T \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad \Delta U_c &= -\frac{(m_w c_w + W) \cdot \Delta T}{m_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Correction to constant pressure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H_c &= \Delta U_c + \Delta n_g \cdot R \cdot T \\[4pt]
\text{(e.g., CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 &\to \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}: \quad \Delta n_g = 1 - 3 = -2)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation B — Huggett's Oxygen Consumption Principle

Huggett’s empirical observation:

> For complete combustion of an organic fuel $C_xH_yO_z$, the heat released per kg of O$_2$ consumed is essentially constant.

**Derivation from stoichiometry (methane example):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 &\to \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \\[4pt]
\Delta H_c &= -802.3 \text{ kJ/mol (CH}_4\text{)} \\[4pt]
m_{O_2} \text{ per mol fuel} &= 2 \times 32 = 64 \text{ g} \\[4pt]
E &= \frac{802.3 \text{ kJ}}{0.064 \text{ kg O}_2} = 12.54 \text{ MJ/kg O}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Repeat for octane ($C_8H_{18}$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5\text{O}_2 &\to 8\text{CO}_2 + 9\text{H}_2\text{O} \\[4pt]
\Delta H_c &= -5470 \text{ kJ/mol} \\[4pt]
m_{O_2} &= 12.5 \times 32 = 400 \text{ g} \\[4pt]
E &= \frac{5470}{0.400} = 13.68 \text{ MJ/kg O}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Repeat for ethanol ($C_2H_5OH$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\text{O}_2 &\to 2\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \\[4pt]
\Delta H_c &= -1366 \text{ kJ/mol} \\[4pt]
m_{O_2} &= 96 \text{ g} \\[4pt]
E &= \frac{1366}{0.096} = 14.23 \text{ MJ/kg O}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

The values cluster tightly around $13.1 \text{ MJ/kg O}_2$ (±5 %), confirming the Huggett principle. This is **why** calorimetry in fire testing is reduced to a simple **O$_2$ sensor** — you do not need to measure temperature, smoke, or CO to get HRR.

## 3.3 Derivation C — Fire Load and Equivalent Fire Duration

The **fire load** of a compartment is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot \Delta H_{c,i}}{A_{floor}} \quad [\text{MJ/m}^2] \\[4pt]
Q_{total} &= L \cdot A_{floor} \quad [\text{MJ}]
\end{aligned}
$$

To find the **equivalent fire duration** $t_{eq}$ (the time a standard fire would deliver the same total energy), use the standard time-temperature curve (ISO 834 / ASTM E119):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{gas}(t) &= T_0 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t + 1) \\[4pt]
Q_{standard}(t) &= A \cdot \int_{0}^{t_{eq}} \dot{q}^{\prime\prime}_{ISO}(t) \, dt
\end{aligned}
$$

For design purposes, the **equivalent fire duration** is often approximated (Eurocode 1991-1-2, Annex E):

$$t_{eq} = \frac{q_{f,d} \cdot A_{floor}}{\dot{Q}_{max}}$$

where $\dot{Q}_{max}$ is the design HRR (kW) of the fire scenario chosen for analysis.

## 3.4 Derivation D — Semenov Thermal Explosion Criterion (Runaway Reactions)

A reactive chemical generates heat at rate $\dot{Q}_{gen} = V \cdot (-\Delta H) \cdot k_0 e^{-E_a/RT} \cdot C^n$ and loses heat by conduction at rate $\dot{Q}_{loss} = hA(T - T_{amb})$.

**Semenov criterion:** Runaway occurs when heat generation curvature exceeds loss curvature:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\dot{Q}_{gen}}{dT} &\geq \frac{d\dot{Q}_{loss}}{dT} \\[4pt]
\frac{V \cdot (-\Delta H) \cdot E_a}{R T^2} \cdot r &\geq hA \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad \phi &= \frac{\dot{Q}_{gen}}{hA(T - T_{amb})} \geq \frac{R T^2}{E_a}
\end{aligned}
$$

This dimensionless number $\phi$ is used to set the **time to maximum rate (TMR)** — a key parameter tested via Accelerating Rate Calorimetry (ARC).

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** In a Bomb Calorimeter, $0.785 \text{ g}$ of a solid fuel raised the temperature of $2000 \text{ g}$ of water by $2.85 \text{ K}$. The water equivalent of the bomb is $450 \text{ g}$. Calculate the heat of combustion in MJ/kg. Use $c_w = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$.

**Step 1 — Total heat absorbed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= (m_w + W_{bomb}) \cdot c_w \cdot \Delta T \\
  &= (2.000 + 0.450) \cdot 4.186 \cdot 2.85 \\
  &= 2.450 \times 4.186 \times 2.85 \\
  &= 29.235 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat of combustion per kg of fuel:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H_c &= \frac{Q}{m_f} = \frac{29.235 \text{ kJ}}{0.785 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg}} \\
           &= 37242 \text{ kJ/kg} = 37.24 \text{ MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

This corresponds roughly to a **medium-grade plastic** such as polyethylene ($\approx 46 \text{ MJ/kg}$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Tree of Calorimeters Used in Fire Safety

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Thermo Calorimetry in Fire Safety] --> B[Combustion Calorimeters]
    A --> C[Reaction Calorimeters]
    
    B --> B1[Bomb Calorimeter]
    B --> B2[Fire Calorimeters]
    
    B1 --> B1a[Constant Volume]
    B1 --> B1b[Measures total Delta Hc]
    B1 --> B1c[Fire Load Estimation]
    
    B2 --> B2a[Cone Calorimeter ISO 5660]
    B2 --> B2b[Room Corner Test ISO 9705]
    B2 --> B2c[Furniture Calorimeter]
    
    B2a --> B2a1[HRR vs Time]
    B2a --> B2a2[Smoke and CO yield]
    B2a --> B2a3[Reaction to Fire]
    
    C --> C1[Adiabatic ARC VSP]
    C --> C2[DSC TGA]
    
    C1 --> C1a[Runaway reaction data]
    C1 --> C1b[Time to Maximum Rate TMR]
    C1 --> C1c[Pressure relief design]
    
    C2 --> C2a[Material decomposition]
```
