# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Define the Huggett constant used in fire calorimetry. State its numerical value and the principle behind it.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - The **Huggett constant** $E$ is the amount of heat released per kilogram of oxygen consumed during complete combustion of an organic fuel. **[1 mark]**
> - Numerically, $E \approx 13.1 \text{ MJ/kg O}_2$, with a deviation of less than ±5 % for nearly all organic fuels (hydrocarbons, polymers, alcohols, etc.). **[1 mark]**
> - **Principle (Huggett's Relation, 1980):** Because $E$ is essentially constant, the **HRR of a fire can be determined simply by measuring the rate of oxygen depletion in the exhaust duct**, without needing direct temperature or fuel-mass measurements. **[1 mark]**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**List any three differences between a Bomb Calorimeter and a Cone Calorimeter.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
> | Feature | Bomb Calorimeter | Cone Calorimeter |
> |---|---|---|
> | Operating mode | Constant volume (closed) | Open, under exhaust hood |
> | Outputs | Total heat of combustion $\Delta H_c$ | Heat release rate HRR vs time, smoke yield |
> | Application | Fire load estimation | Reaction-to-fire classification, Euroclass |
> | Sample size | ~1 g pellet | 100 mm × 100 mm slab |

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE PROVIDED — answer EITHER Question A OR Question B]**

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a **Bomb Calorimeter**. Derive the expression used to compute the **heat of combustion** from the observed temperature rise. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** In a Bomb Calorimeter experiment, $0.825 \text{ g}$ of a fuel caused a temperature rise of $2.62 \text{ K}$ in $2000 \text{ g}$ of water. The water equivalent of the bomb is $425 \text{ g}$. Given $c_w = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, determine the heat of combustion in **MJ/kg**. If this material is used as a structural component in a $40 \text{ m}^2$ office with $80 \text{ kg}$ of similar material, calculate the **fire load density** in **MJ/m²**. Comment on the fire-resistance requirement. **[7 marks]**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
- Schematic of Bomb Calorimeter (sample, ignition wires, oxygen port, water jacket, stirrer, thermometer): **2 marks**
- Working: pellet ignited electrically in pure O$_2$ at 3 MPa; released energy absorbed by water + bomb hardware; $\Delta T$ measured precisely: **2 marks**
- Derivation:
  $$Q_v = (m_w c_w + W) \cdot \Delta T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta U_c = -\frac{Q_v}{m_f} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta H_c = \Delta U_c + \Delta n_g R T$$
  : **3 marks**

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Heat released to water + bomb**

$$
Q = (m_w + W) \cdot c_w \cdot \Delta T = (2.000 + 0.425) \times 4.186 \times 2.62 = 2.425 \times 4.186 \times 2.62
$$

$$
Q = 26.595 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 2: Heat of combustion per kg**

$$
\Delta H_c = \frac{26.595 \text{ kJ}}{0.825 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg}} = 32{,}236 \text{ kJ/kg} \approx 32.24 \text{ MJ/kg}
$$

**[2 marks for steps 1–2]**

**Step 3: Total heat in office compartment**

$$
Q_{total} = m \cdot \Delta H_c = 80 \text{ kg} \times 32.24 \text{ MJ/kg} = 2579.2 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Step 4: Fire load density**

$$
L = \frac{Q_{total}}{A_{floor}} = \frac{2579.2 \text{ MJ}}{40 \text{ m}^2} = 64.5 \text{ MJ/m}^2
$$

**[2 marks for steps 3–4]**

**Step 5: Comment on fire resistance**

> A fire load of $64.5 \text{ MJ/m}^2$ falls in the **low-to-moderate** category (NBCC 2015: Low < 250 MJ/m²). Equivalent fire duration $t_{eq} \approx 30\text{–}45$ minutes; therefore a **1-hour fire-resistance rating** is generally sufficient, but active suppression (sprinklers) is strongly recommended because polymeric fuels produce high HRR during early growth. **[3 marks]**

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the **principle of oxygen consumption calorimetry** as applied in the **Cone Calorimeter (ISO 5660)**. Derive the relation used to compute HRR from oxygen depletion in the exhaust duct. State typical ranges of the Huggett constant. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A **medium-growth fire** ($\alpha = 0.01172 \text{ kW/s}^2$) initiates in a $50 \text{ m}^2$ office at $t = 0$. Determine:
- (i) the **time to reach flashover** ($1000 \text{ kW}$ threshold); **[2 marks]**
- (ii) the **total energy released** by flashover; **[2 marks]**
- (iii) the **time to reach 500 °C** layer temperature (use ISO 834 standard curve: $T = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)$). **[3 marks]**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
- Principle: complete combustion of organic fuels releases a near-constant energy per kg of O$_2$ consumed. Therefore, HRR is proportional to the rate of O$_2$ depletion in the exhaust stream. **[2 marks]**
- Schematic: cone heater, spark igniter, exhaust hood, load cell, O$_2$/CO/CO$_2$ analysers: **2 marks**
- Derivation:
  $$\dot{Q} = E \cdot \dot{m}_{O_2} = E \cdot \dot{m}_e \cdot \left( Y_{O_2,i} - Y_{O_2,e} \right)$$
  where $E = 13.1 \text{ MJ/kg O}_2$, $\dot{m}_e$ = exhaust mass flow, $Y$ = oxygen mass fractions. **[2 marks]**
- Typical range $E = 12.5 \text{ to } 14.3 \text{ MJ/kg O}_2$ for hydrocarbons and most common organics. **[1 mark]**

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Time to flashover:**

$$
\dot{Q}(t_{fo}) = \alpha t_{fo}^2 = 1000 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
t_{fo} = \sqrt{\frac{1000}{0.01172}} = \sqrt{85{,}324} = 292.1 \text{ s} \approx 4.87 \text{ min}
$$

**[2 marks]**

**(ii) Total energy released by flashover:**

$$
Q_{fo} = \int_{0}^{t_{fo}} \alpha t^2 \, dt = \frac{\alpha t_{fo}^3}{3} = \frac{0.01172 \times (292.1)^3}{3}
$$

$$
Q_{fo} = \frac{0.01172 \times 2.49 \times 10^{7}}{3} \approx 9.74 \times 10^{4} \text{ kJ} = 97.4 \text{ MJ}
$$

**[2 marks]**

**(iii) Time to 500 °C (ISO 834):**

$$
500 = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)
$$

$$
\log_{10}(8t+1) = \frac{480}{345} = 1.3913
$$

$$
8t + 1 = 10^{1.3913} = 24.65
$$

$$
8t = 23.65 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = 2.96 \text{ s}
$$

**[3 marks]**

This result illustrates the well-known fact that the **standard fire curve reaches 500 °C in less than 5 minutes** — far faster than most medium-growth t² fires reach flashover (~5 min). This is why compartment design must couple HRR-growth and structural heating analyses.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
>
> - **Mistake 1:** Using the *mass of water* only (forgetting the water equivalent $W$ of the bomb). Partial marks (½ out of 2) deducted — examiners explicitly look for the term $(m_w + W)$.
> - **Mistake 2:** Confusing constant-volume $\Delta U_c$ with constant-pressure $\Delta H_c$ — without showing the $\Delta n_g RT$ correction, you lose 1 mark in 7-mark derivations.
> - **Mistake 3:** In HRR problems, using the *wrong* Huggett constant (some students write $44 \text{ MJ/kg}$, confusing it with heat of combustion of methane). **The correct number is $E = 13.1 \text{ MJ/kg O}_2$** — and units must show "per kg O$_2$", not "per kg fuel".
> - **Mistake 4:** Forgetting the units in the final answer (MJ/kg, MJ/m², kW). Unit errors routinely cost ½ to 1 mark even when the arithmetic is right.
> - **Mistake 5:** In t² fire-growth problems, mixing seconds and minutes. **Always keep $t$ in seconds** because $\alpha$ has units of kW/s².

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Calorimetry = measurement of heat** released or absorbed during a process; in fire safety, it is the *quantitative backbone* of fire load and HRR estimation.
- ✅ **Bomb Calorimeter** → constant volume → gives $\Delta U_c$ (corrected to $\Delta H_c$); used for **fire load** $L = \sum m_i \Delta H_{c,i} / A_{floor}$ in MJ/m².
- ✅ **Cone Calorimeter (ISO 5660)** → bench-scale HRR measurement; sample 100 mm × 100 mm; **HRR per unit area** $\dot{Q}^{\prime\prime}$ in kW/m²; key output for Euroclass reaction-to-fire classification.
- ✅ **Oxygen Consumption Principle (Huggett, 1980)** → $E \approx 13.1 \text{ MJ/kg O}_2$; $\dot{Q} = E \cdot \dot{m}_{O_2}$ is the basis of full-scale room calorimetry (ISO 9705).
- ✅ **t-squared fire growth** $\dot{Q} = \alpha t^2$; growth classes: Slow 0.00293, Medium 0.01172, Fast 0.0469, Ultra-fast 0.1876 (kW/s²).
- ✅ **Flashover threshold** is conventionally $\dot{Q}_{fo} = 1000 \text{ kW}$ in many code frameworks.
- ✅ **ISO 834 standard fire**: $T_{gas}(t) = T_0 + 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)$ — use to size structural resistance vs. time.
- ✅ **Adiabatic calorimetry (ARC, VSP)** is for **runaway reactions**; key outputs: $T_{NR}$ (no-return temperature), $\phi$ (Semenov criterion), TMR (time to maximum rate).
- ✅ **Water equivalent** of bomb $W$ must always be added to $m_w$ — common exam trap.
- ✅ **DSC / TGA** are screening tools — not direct HRR devices; they give enthalpy vs. temperature data for material decomposition.
- ✅ **Fire load density** interpretation: < 250 MJ/m² = low; 250–500 = moderate; 500–1000 = high; > 1000 MJ/m² = very high (NBCC / Eurocode indicative ranges).
- ✅ **Engineering use chain:** Calorimetry data → Fire load → Equivalent fire duration → Structural fire resistance → Egress design (ASET) → Suppression sizing.
- ✅ **Master equation set to memorize:** $Q = mc_p\Delta T$, $\dot{Q}=\alpha t^2$, $\dot{Q}=E\dot{m}_{O_2}$, $\Delta H_c=\Delta U_c+\Delta n_g RT$, $L=Q_{total}/A_{floor}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Scanning Calorimeter (DSC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Differential Scanning Calorimeter (DSC)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)** is a thermoanalytical technique in which the **heat flow rate** (dQ/dt) difference between a **sample** and an **inert reference** is measured as a function of temperature (or time) under a controlled atmosphere, as both are subjected to an identical temperature programme. The technique quantifies the energy absorbed (endothermic) or released (exothermic) during physical transitions and chemical reactions, providing direct measurement of enthalpic changes.

The result is plotted as a **DSC thermogram**, plotting **heat flow (mW or W/g) versus temperature (°C or K)** or **versus time (min)**. The area under the peak is directly proportional to the enthalpy change of the transition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PESFT412):**
> "Differential Scanning Calorimeter is a thermal hazard screening tool used in the design of inherently safer chemical processes, used to identify exothermic decomposition, self-accelerating decomposition temperature (SADT), and adiabatic induction times of reactive chemicals."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Twin Pot Experiment

Imagine two identical pressure cookers placed side by side on the same gas stove burner, both with identical lids and identical starting temperatures.

- **Pot A (Reference):** Empty stainless-steel pan — it has nothing inside.
- **Pot B (Sample):** Contains a small quantity of sugar or chocolate that is undergoing melting.

Both burners are set to deliver the *same* heating rate (e.g., 10 °C per minute). A sensitive electronic "difference thermometer" continuously compares the *actual energy delivered* to Pot A versus Pot B.

- When the sugar **melts**, it **absorbs** heat (endothermic). The stove must supply *extra* energy to Pot B to keep the temperature rising at the same rate. The differential energy = the **latent heat of fusion**.
- If the sample **decomposes** and releases heat (exothermic), Pot B begins to *overheat*. The differential sensor detects that *less* energy is required from the stove. The differential energy = the **heat of decomposition**.

This twin-pot, differential energy comparison is exactly the operating philosophy of a DSC instrument — one pan = sample, one pan = reference, and a precisely controlled furnace with twin heaters keeps them in thermal lockstep.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Materials

| Reference Material | Transition | Temperature (°C) | Enthalpy (J/g) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Indium (In)** | Fusion (Melting) | **156.6** | **28.45** |
| Tin (Sn) | Fusion | 231.9 | 60.4 |
| Zinc (Zn) | Fusion | 419.5 | 108.4 |
| Sapphire (Al₂O₃) | Heat Capacity Std. | — | Reference only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Indium is the universal DSC calibration standard** because its melting point (156.6 °C) and enthalpy of fusion (28.45 J/g) are precisely known and reproducible. Every KTU lab question on DSC calibration implicitly assumes the **Indium standard** unless stated otherwise.

## 1.4 The Two Principal DSC Architectures

The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly distinguishes the two operating modes:

**1. Heat-Flux DSC (Single-Furnace, Disc-Type):**
Sample and reference sit on a thermally conductive platform inside *one* furnace. Differential temperature (ΔT) is measured by thermocouples beneath each pan. The ΔT is converted to heat-flow via a prior calibration constant.

**2. Power-Compensation DSC (Twin-Furnace, Calorimetric):**
Sample and reference each have their *own* tiny independent heater. The instrument continuously adjusts the *power difference* (ΔW) supplied to the two heaters to maintain zero temperature difference. ΔW is the direct DSC signal (in mW).

> [!TIP]
> For numerical KTU problems, always identify which mode is being used. **Power-Compensation DSC** gives the most direct quantitative measurement because ΔW equals dQ/dt directly, with no calibration constant required.

## 1.5 Why DSC Matters in *Design for Safety*

DSC is not merely a materials-characterization tool — it is the **first-line hazard-screening instrument** in the chemical-process-safety toolbox:

- Identifies **exothermic runaway reactions** before scale-up.
- Determines the **onset temperature (T_onset)** of decomposition.
- Quantifies the **heat of reaction (ΔH_r)** for vent-sizing calculations (e.g., DIERS methodology).
- Estimates **SADT (Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature)** under UN/IMDG shipping regulations.
- Provides kinetic data (activation energy, pre-exponential factor) for **time-to-runaway** calculations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners consistently frame DSC questions in the context of **process safety and reactive-hazard evaluation**, not merely in pure materials-science terms. Always link DSC outputs (T_onset, ΔH, kinetics) to the corresponding *safety consequence*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step

The DSC operates on the principle of **null-balance thermal comparison**. The sequence of operation is:

1. **Sample Preparation:** A small mass (typically **3–10 mg** for standard pans, up to 100 mg for pressure-tight crucibles) is weighed into a metal crucible (Al, Pt, or stainless steel) and hermetically sealed. A matching empty crucible serves as the **reference**.

2. **Atmosphere Control:** The sample-holder assembly is purged with a controlled gas — typically **inert nitrogen (50 mL/min)** for organic materials, or **air/oxygen (50 mL/min)** for oxidation studies.

3. **Temperature Programme:** The furnace is programmed to ramp at a constant heating rate (β = dT/dt), typically:
   - β = **5, 10, 20, or 40 K/min** for routine scans.
   - Slow scans (1–2 K/min) for kinetic studies.

4. **Sensor Response:** Differential thermocouples (heat-flux mode) or twin heaters (power-compensation mode) continuously measure ΔT or ΔW between sample and reference.

5. **Signal Conversion:** The differential signal is plotted against the programmed temperature. A positive peak = **endothermic** (sample absorbs heat); a negative peak = **exothermic** (sample releases heat).

6. **Calibration:** The temperature axis is calibrated against the melting of **indium (156.6 °C)**; the heat-flow axis (sensitivity) is calibrated using the known enthalpy of indium fusion (**28.45 J/g**).

## 2.2 Reading a DSC Thermogram — Interpretation Logic

A typical DSC trace exhibits several characteristic features:

| Feature | Symbol | Direction | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Glass Transition | T_g | **Step change** (no peak, baseline shift) | Amorphous → rubbery transition |
| Crystallization (cold) | T_c | **Exothermic peak** | Disorder → ordered crystalline |
| Melting / Fusion | T_m | **Endothermic peak** | Solid → liquid |
| Polymorphic transition | T_tr | Endothermic or exothermic | Solid–solid phase change |
| Decomposition | T_d | **Exothermic peak** | Chemical bond breaking |
| Oxidation | T_ox | Exothermic peak (in O₂) | Reaction with O₂ |
| Vaporization | T_vap | Endothermic peak | Liquid → gas |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **onset temperature (T_onset)** is read at the intersection of the **baseline extrapolation** and the **tangent to the rising edge** of the peak. KTU questions on safety will frequently ask: *"What is the safe processing limit for this chemical?"* — the answer is anchored on T_onset minus a **safety margin of 25–50 °C**.

## 2.3 Quantitative Heat-Flow Equations

For an idealized DSC, the differential signal is related to the heat capacity difference:

$$
\Delta \dot{Q} = \beta \cdot (C_{p,\text{sample}} - C_{p,\text{reference}}) + f(T, t)
$$

where:
- $\beta = \dfrac{dT}{dt}$ is the heating rate (K/min).
- $C_p$ is the heat capacity (J/g·K).
- $f(T, t)$ is the heat flow associated with kinetic transitions.

For a peak event (e.g., melting), the integrated area under the peak gives the enthalpy change:

$$
\Delta H = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{\Delta \dot{Q}}{\beta \cdot m} \, dT
$$

where *m* is the sample mass (mg) and ΔH is in **J/g** or **kJ/kg**.

## 2.4 Kinetic Analysis — The Kissinger Method (KTU High-Yield)

The **Kissinger equation** is the standard KTU 2024 tool for extracting the activation energy (E_a) of a decomposition reaction from a series of DSC runs at different heating rates (β₁, β₂, β₃, β₄):

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{\beta}{T_p^{\,2}}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T_p} + \ln\!\left(\frac{A \cdot R}{E_a}\right)
$$

where:
- $\beta$ = heating rate (K/min or K/s)
- $T_p$ = peak temperature of decomposition (K)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol)
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (min⁻¹)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**

Plotting $\ln(\beta/T_p^2)$ versus $1/T_p$ yields a straight line:
- **Slope** = $-E_a/R$
- **Intercept** = $\ln(A R / E_a)$

## 2.5 The Ozawa Method (Alternative)

For validation or when peak shape analysis is difficult:

$$
\log \beta = -0.4567 \cdot \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T_p} + \text{constant}
$$

Plotting $\log \beta$ versus $1/T_p$ gives slope = $-0.4567 \cdot E_a / R$.

## 2.6 Time-to-Maximum-Rate under Adiabatic Conditions (TMR_ad)

Critical for **reactive-hazard safety design**, the **TMR_ad** is the time available before a runaway reaction reaches its maximum rate. It is computed as:

$$
\text{TMR}_{ad} = \frac{C_p \cdot \Delta T_{\text{ad}}}{q_{\text{max}}}
$$

A more rigorous form using activation energy:

$$
\text{TMR}_{ad} = \frac{R T^2}{E_a \cdot q_{\text{max}}}
$$

where $q_{\text{max}}$ is the maximum heat-release rate (W/kg) at temperature T.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Safety Decision Threshold:** If **TMR_ad < 8 hours**, the reaction is considered **highly hazardous** and requires engineered safeguards (inhibitors, emergency cooling, dump systems, or dilution).

## 2.7 Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature (SADT)

The SADT is the lowest ambient temperature at which a self-reactive substance undergoes runaway decomposition within **7 days** in a 50 kg commercial package. DSC data are used to estimate SADT through the **Semenov thermal-explosion model**:

$$
\text{SADT} \approx T_{\text{onset}} - \Delta T_{\text{offset}}
$$

A practical engineering approximation, used in UN/IMDG classification:

$$
\text{SADT} \approx T_{onset,DSC} - 25\,^{\circ}\text{C (safety margin)}
$$

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Consolidated)

| # | Equation | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta H = \int \Delta \dot{Q} / (\beta \cdot m)\, dT$ | Enthalpy from peak area | J/g or kJ/kg |
| 2 | $\ln(\beta/T_p^2) = -E_a/(R T_p) + \text{const}$ | Kissinger kinetics | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\log \beta = -0.4567\,E_a/(R T_p) + \text{const}$ | Ozawa kinetics | dimensionless |
| 4 | $TMR_{ad} = R T^2 / (E_a \cdot q_{max})$ | Runaway time | seconds/minutes |
| 5 | SADT ≈ T_onset − 25 °C | Shipping classification | °C |
| 6 | $\beta = dT/dt$ | Heating rate | K/min |
| 7 | $C_p = \Delta Q / (m \cdot \Delta T)$ | Heat capacity | J/(g·K) |
| 8 | $E_a = -R \cdot \text{slope}$ | Activation energy | J/mol |
| 9 | $A = (\text{slope-coefficient}) \cdot E_a / R$ | Frequency factor | min⁻¹ or s⁻¹ |
| 10 | $\Delta T_{ad} = \Delta H / C_p$ | Adiabatic temperature rise | K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion factors used in KTU problems:**
> - 1 cal = **4.184 J**
> - R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** = 1.987 cal/(mol·K)
> - $T(K) = T(^{\circ}C) + 273.15$
> - 1 kJ/kg = 1 J/g

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

The DSC data feed directly into:

- **Vent Sizing (DIERS):** ΔH, $q_{max}$, and T_onset are inputs to the Leung-Fauske model for two-phase relief.
- **Reactor Design:** TMR_ad dictates the maximum allowable hold time of a reactive mixture.
- **Storage & Transport:** SADT determines packaging, labelling, and quantity limits under UN Class 4 / 5.
- **Inherent Safety:** T_onset > 50 °C above processing temperature = inherently safe; smaller margins demand engineered controls.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the DSC Heat-Flow Equation (Heat-Flux Mode)

**Starting premise:** Sample and reference are mounted on a thermally conductive disc inside a common furnace. Let $T_s$ = sample temperature, $T_r$ = reference temperature, and $T_p$ = programmed (furnace) temperature.

**Step 1 — Heat balance on the sample pan:**

The heat delivered to the sample pan by conduction is:

$$
\dot{Q}_s = K_s (T_p - T_s)
$$

where $K_s$ is the thermal conductance of the sample-pan interface (W/K).

**Step 2 — Heat balance on the reference pan (no sample inside):**

$$
\dot{Q}_r = K_r (T_p - T_r)
$$

Because the pans are identical in a properly designed DSC, $K_s \approx K_r = K$. Therefore:

$$
\Delta \dot{Q} = \dot{Q}_r - \dot{Q}_s = K (T_s - T_r) = K \cdot \Delta T
$$

> This is the foundational linear relation for **Heat-Flux DSC**: the differential heat flow is proportional to the differential temperature, the proportionality constant being the cell's thermal conductance.

**Step 3 — Introducing the sample's thermal response:**

The sample pan has a heat capacity $C_s$ and a kinetic heat-release term $f(T,t)$:

$$
K (T_p - T_s) = C_s \frac{dT_s}{dt} + f(T_s, t)
$$

**Step 4 — Combining with reference equation:**

Subtracting the two heat-balance equations and eliminating $T_p$:

$$
\Delta \dot{Q} = C_s \frac{dT_s}{dt} - C_r \frac{dT_r}{dt} + f(T_s, t)
$$

If $C_s \approx C_r$ (matched pans):

$$
\Delta \dot{Q} = (C_s - C_r) \frac{dT}{dt} + f(T, t) = \Delta C \cdot \beta + f(T, t)
$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form:**

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta \dot{Q} = \beta \cdot \Delta C_p + f(T, t)\;}
$$

This equation is the basis of every quantitative DSC interpretation. The first term gives the **baseline** (heat capacity contribution), and $f(T, t)$ gives the **peak** (transition or reaction contribution).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Kissinger Equation (Reactive Hazard Kinetics)

**Starting premise:** A first-order decomposition reaction with rate constant $k(T) = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ is taking place in the sample. The DSC peak maximum occurs when the *rate of heat release* is maximum, i.e., when $d^2(\text{heat release})/dt^2 = 0$.

**Step 1 — Heat-release rate proportional to reaction rate:**

$$
q(T, t) = \Delta H \cdot k(T) \cdot (1 - \alpha)
$$

where $\alpha$ is the fractional conversion. At peak maximum $T = T_p$:

$$
\frac{d q}{dt} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{d^2 \alpha}{dt^2}\bigg|_{T_p} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Expanding the derivative:**

$$
\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = A e^{-E_a/RT} (1-\alpha)^n
$$

Taking the second derivative and setting it to zero (assuming first-order n = 1):

$$
\frac{E_a}{R T_p^{\,2}} \cdot \beta = A e^{-E_a/RT_p}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearranging:**

$$
\frac{\beta}{T_p^{\,2}} = \frac{A R}{E_a} e^{-E_a/RT_p}
$$

**Step 4 — Taking natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{\beta}{T_p^{\,2}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{A R}{E_a}\right) - \frac{E_a}{R T_p}
$$

**Step 5 — Recognising the linear plot form:**

$$
\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{\beta}{T_p^{\,2}}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R}\cdot \frac{1}{T_p} + \ln\!\left(\frac{A R}{E_a}\right)\;}
$$

This is the **Kissinger equation** in its canonical form. Plotting $\ln(\beta/T_p^2)$ on the y-axis against $1/T_p$ on the x-axis yields a straight line whose slope yields E_a and intercept yields A.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Kissinger Analysis

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern, 14-Mark Level):**
> A reactive monomer is scanned by DSC at four heating rates. The exothermic decomposition peak temperatures are:
>
> | β (K/min) | T_p (°C) | T_p (K) |
> |---|---|---|
> | 2 | 178 | 451.15 |
> | 5 | 198 | 471.15 |
> | 10 | 215 | 488.15 |
> | 20 | 235 | 508.15 |
>
> Determine the activation energy E_a and pre-exponential factor A using the Kissinger method. State whether the material is a reactive-hazard concern (assume a target of E_a > 100 kJ/mol for "low concern").

**Step 1 — Compute auxiliary variables:**

| β (K/min) | T_p (K) | 1/T_p (K⁻¹) | β/T_p² (K⁻¹·min⁻¹) | ln(β/T_p²) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 451.15 | 2.2166 × 10⁻³ | 9.827 × 10⁻⁶ | −11.531 |
| 5 | 471.15 | 2.1225 × 10⁻³ | 2.252 × 10⁻⁵ | −10.703 |
| 10 | 488.15 | 2.0486 × 10⁻³ | 4.196 × 10⁻⁵ | −10.078 |
| 20 | 508.15 | 1.9679 × 10⁻³ | 7.746 × 10⁻⁵ | −9.464 |

**Step 2 — Linear regression of ln(β/T_p²) vs 1/T_p:**

Using two end points for clarity (full regression yields essentially the same answer):

$$
\text{slope} = \frac{-9.464 - (-11.531)}{(1.9679 - 2.2166) \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2.067}{-0.2487 \times 10^{-3}} = -8310\;\text{K}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for E_a:**

$$
E_a = -R \cdot \text{slope} = -8.314 \times (-8310) = 6.91 \times 10^4\;\text{J/mol} = 69.1\;\text{kJ/mol}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for A using one data point (β = 10 K/min, T_p = 488.15 K):**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{A R}{E_a}\right) = \text{intercept} = \ln(\beta/T_p^2) - \text{slope} \cdot (1/T_p)
$$

$$
= -10.078 - (-8310) \times (2.0486 \times 10^{-3}) = -10.078 + 17.024 = 6.946
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{A R}{E_a} = e^{6.946} = 1041
$$

$$
A = \frac{1041 \times 69100}{8.314} = 8.65 \times 10^6\;\text{min}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Safety conclusion:**

Since $E_a = 69.1\;\text{kJ/mol} < 100\;\text{kJ/mol}$, the material is **a reactive-hazard concern** and requires engineered safeguards (e.g., temperature control, inhibitor system, emergency cooling, or dilution) for safe processing.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners award full marks only if the student *interprets* the kinetic parameters in terms of safety consequence. Always conclude with a safety statement.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Enthalpy by Peak Integration

> **Problem:** A DSC run at β = 10 K/min on a 5.20 mg sample produces a melting peak. The peak extends from 158.0 °C to 162.5 °C, and the integrated area is 1.49 J (machine output). Determine the specific enthalpy of fusion ΔH_f in J/g and comment on whether the value matches the known Indium standard (28.45 J/g).

**Solution:**

$$
\Delta H_f = \frac{\text{peak area}}{m \cdot \beta} \times \text{(calibration factor)}
$$

In modern software, peak area (J) is already normalised by sample mass and heating rate, but to demonstrate the algebra:

$$
\Delta H_f = \frac{1.49\;\text{J}}{5.20 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{g}} = 286.5\;\text{J/g}
$$

> Note: This is a value in J/g — for comparison with Indium (28.45 J/g), the answer is **10× higher** because the calculation was performed without the heating-rate normalisation. The correct procedure is:

$$
\Delta H_f = \frac{\text{area}}{m} = \frac{1.49}{5.20 \times 10^{-3}} = 286.5\;\text{J/g}\;\text{(raw, before β-normalisation)}
$$

After dividing by heating rate:

$$
\Delta H_f = 286.5 / 10 = 28.65\;\text{J/g}
$$

This matches the Indium reference (28.45 J/g) within 0.7 % — an acceptable calibration result.

> [!WARNING]
> Always check whether the DSC software output gives **J (raw)**, **J/g (mass-normalised)**, or **W/g (heat-flow)**. Mixing units is the single largest source of error in KTU numerical answers.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — From DSC Output to Safety Decision

| DSC Output | Engineering Translation | Safety Implication |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{onset} < 100\;°C$ | Decomposition near ambient | **Critical hazard** — no heating without engineering controls |
| $T_{onset} = 100–200\;°C$ | Moderate thermal stability | Requires temperature control + relief |
| $T_{onset} > 250\;°C$ | Thermally stable | Inherently safer at moderate processing temperatures |
| $\Delta H_d < 200\;\text{J/g}$ | Mild exotherm | Tolerable in dilute systems |
| $\Delta H_d > 800\;\text{J/g}$ | Powerful exotherm | Requires remote handling, dilution, or substitution |
| $TMR_{ad} < 1\;\text{h}$ | Fast runaway | Catastrophic potential; explosion-proof design required |
| $E_a < 80\;\text{kJ/mol}$ | Highly reactive | Inherently unsafe — search for safer alternative |
| $E_a > 150\;\text{kJ/mol}$ | Kinetically stable | Engineered controls usually sufficient |
| SADT < 50 °C | Self-decomposing at ambient | Class 1 self-reactive substance (UN) |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]
**"Define Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC). List the four characteristic thermal events identified on a DSC thermogram and state the sign convention used for endothermic and exothermic processes."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Differential Scanning Calorimetry is a thermoanalytical technique in which the **difference in heat flow rate (dQ/dt)** between a sample and a reference is measured as a function of temperature under a controlled atmosphere, while both are subjected to the same temperature programme.

**Four characteristic events (1 mark):**
1. **Glass transition (T_g):** a step change in the baseline, no peak.
2. **Crystallisation (T_c):** an exothermic peak.
3. **Melting / fusion (T_m):** an endothermic peak.
4. **Decomposition (T_d):** an exothermic peak (for organic reactive materials).

**Sign convention (1 mark):**
By the IUPAC convention, **endothermic (heat absorbed) events are plotted upward** (positive y-axis) and **exothermic (heat released) events are plotted downward** (negative y-axis), though some software reverses this. Always state the convention explicitly.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]
**"Why is Indium used as a calibration standard for DSC? State its melting temperature and enthalpy of fusion."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Indium is used (2 marks):**
1. Its **melting point (156.6 °C)** lies in the mid-range of typical DSC operation (room temperature to 600 °C), so it calibrates the temperature axis in the most-used region.
2. Its **enthalpy of fusion (28.45 J/g)** is precisely known and reproducible, allowing accurate calibration of the heat-flow axis (sensitivity).
3. Indium does not oxidise, supercool, or decompose near its melting point, ensuring sharp, repeatable peaks.
4. It is commercially available in **99.999 %+ purity**, eliminating impurity-related uncertainty.

**Values (1 mark):** T_m = **156.6 °C**; ΔH_f = **28.45 J/g**.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern, Module 3 Choice 1]**
**(a)** Describe with a neat functional block diagram the **Power-Compensation DSC** and explain how it differs from the **Heat-Flux DSC**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A reactive chemical is analysed by DSC at four heating rates. The decomposition peak temperatures are: β = 2 K/min → T_p = 178 °C; β = 5 K/min → T_p = 198 °C; β = 10 K/min → T_p = 215 °C; β = 20 K/min → T_p = 235 °C. Using the **Kissinger method**, determine the **activation energy (E_a)** and the **pre-exponential factor (A)**. Comment on the safety implications. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Power-Compensation DSC — Operating Principle (4 marks):**
In a Power-Compensation DSC, the **sample and reference are placed in separate, independent micro-furnaces**, each with its own heater and temperature sensor. The instrument continuously compares the temperatures of the two pans and adjusts the power delivered to *each* heater independently through a **PID control loop** to keep both at the *same* temperature (i.e., ΔT = 0 at all times).

The **differential power ΔW** supplied to the two heaters to maintain this null balance is the direct DSC signal:

$$
\Delta W = \frac{dQ_{sample}}{dt} - \frac{dQ_{reference}}{dt} = \frac{dQ}{dt}
$$

The output is in **milliwatts (mW)** and is plotted against temperature to give the DSC thermogram.

**Functional block diagram (2 marks):** A neat block diagram showing — Temperature Programme → Twin Furnace Assembly → Sample and Reference Heaters → Differential Temperature Sensor → PID Controller → Power Difference Calculator → ADC → Data Acquisition → Thermogram Display. *(Draw in the exam; see Section 4.1 of these notes for reference.)*

**Difference from Heat-Flux DSC (1 mark):**
- Power-Compensation: **two separate furnaces**, ΔW is the direct signal, no calibration constant needed.
- Heat-Flux: **one common furnace**, ΔT is measured via thermocouples, and a calibration constant K converts ΔT to heat flow.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert all T_p to Kelvin:
- 178 °C → 451.15 K
- 198 °C → 471.15 K
- 215 °C → 488.15 K
- 235 °C → 508.15 K

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Calculate β/T_p² and ln(β/T_p²):

| β (K/min) | T_p (K) | 1/T_p (×10⁻³ K⁻¹) | β/T_p² (×10⁻⁵) | ln(β/T_p²) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 451.15 | 2.2166 | 0.9827 | −11.531 |
| 5 | 471.15 | 2.1225 | 2.252 | −10.703 |
| 10 | 488.15 | 2.0486 | 4.196 | −10.078 |
| 20 | 508.15 | 1.9679 | 7.746 | −9.464 |

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Linear regression gives slope ≈ −8310 K (using endpoints; full regression yields E_a ≈ 69.1 kJ/mol). Therefore:

$$
E_a = -R \times \text{slope} = -8.314 \times (-8310) = 6.91 \times 10^4\;\text{J/mol} = 69.1\;\text{kJ/mol}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Intercept ≈ 6.95, so:

$$
A = \frac{e^{6.95} \cdot E_a}{R} = \frac{1041 \times 69100}{8.314} \approx 8.65 \times 10^6\;\text{min}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Safety comment:** Since E_a ≈ 69 kJ/mol < 100 kJ/mol threshold, the material is a **reactive-hazard concern**. TMR_ad will be short at any temperature above T_onset, requiring **temperature control, inhibitor addition, and emergency relief** for safe handling.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern, Module 3 Choice 2]**
**(a)** Explain the **operating principle of a Heat-Flux DSC** with a labelled block diagram. List the **five major events** identified on a DSC thermogram, and for each, state whether the peak is **endothermic or exothermic** and the corresponding safety/process implication. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A pharmaceutical intermediate exhibits an exothermic decomposition peak with **T_onset = 142 °C** and **ΔH_d = 425 J/g** at a heating rate of 10 K/min. The estimated activation energy from kinetic studies is **E_a = 95 kJ/mol** and the maximum heat release rate is **q_max = 2.5 W/kg** at 160 °C. Estimate the **TMR_ad** and the **SADT**, and recommend the storage and processing strategy. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Operating Principle (3 marks):**
In a Heat-Flux DSC, sample and reference sit on a **common thermally conductive disc (Constantan platform)** inside a *single* furnace that is programmed to heat at a linear rate β. As the furnace temperature rises, the **differential temperature ΔT** between sample and reference is measured by thermocouples welded beneath each pan. The relationship:

$$
\Delta \dot{Q} = K \cdot \Delta T
$$

gives the heat flow signal after multiplication by the calibration constant K (W/K), which is determined by running an Indium standard.

**Block diagram (1 mark):** Draw the schematic from Section 4.2 of these notes.

**Five thermogram events (3 marks):**

| Event | Endothermic/Exothermic | Safety Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Glass transition (T_g) | Step change, no peak | Indicates amorphous content; affects stability |
| Crystallisation (T_c) | Exothermic | Energy release during processing — important for solid form design |
| Melting (T_m) | Endothermic | Defines upper limit for solid handling; useful for purity assessment |
| Boiling/vaporisation | Endothermic | Indicates volatile impurities |
| Decomposition (T_d) | Exothermic | **Critical safety parameter** — T_onset is the safe-processing limit |

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — TMR_ad calculation (3 marks):**

$$
\text{TMR}_{ad} = \frac{R T^2}{E_a \cdot q_{max}}
$$

Convert: T = 160 °C = **433.15 K**; E_a = 95,000 J/mol; q_max = 2.5 W/kg.

$$
\text{TMR}_{ad} = \frac{8.314 \times (433.15)^2}{95000 \times 2.5} = \frac{8.314 \times 187619}{237500} = \frac{1,560,153}{237500} \approx 6.57\;\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — SADT calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\text{SADT} \approx T_{onset} - 25\;°C = 142 - 25 = 117\;°C
$$

This places the material in the **UN Class 4 self-reactive** category when shipped in 50 kg packages.

**Step 3 — Storage and processing recommendations (2 marks):**
- **Storage:** Keep bulk material below 90 °C (well below SADT) with 27 °C engineering margin. Use temperature-controlled warehousing.
- **Processing:** Maintain reactor temperature **at least 50 °C below T_onset** — i.e., below 92 °C. Install **emergency cooling**, **dump system** to quench tank, and **continuous temperature monitoring** with high-high interlock at 100 °C.
- **Quantity limits:** Limit in-process inventory to < 100 kg to keep potential energy release within manageable bounds.
- **Inherent safety consideration:** Investigate whether a less energetic synthesis route exists.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit errors in TMR_ad:** Failing to convert T to Kelvin or mixing J/mol with kJ/mol in the denominator. KTU strictly awards 0 marks for an answer in wrong units.
> 2. **Confusing E_a and ΔH:** E_a is the *kinetic barrier* (from Kissinger plot), ΔH is the *total heat released* (from peak area). They are different quantities with different units and different safety meanings.
> 3. **Forgetting the safety margin in SADT:** Always subtract the **25 °C engineering margin** — the raw T_onset is NOT the SADT.
> 4. **Missing the safety comment:** A numerical answer without a concluding safety statement loses 1–2 marks. KTU examiners *expect* an engineering interpretation.
> 5. **Confusing heat-flux and power-compensation DSC:** A common error is to give the heat-flux calibration equation $\Delta \dot{Q} = K \Delta T$ in an answer about power-compensation DSC. Match the equation to the architecture.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

### **Definitions & Basics**
- ☐ DSC = measurement of **differential heat flow (dQ/dt)** between sample and reference under controlled temperature programme.
- ☐ Two architectures: **Heat-Flux DSC** (single furnace, ΔT measured) and **Power-Compensation DSC** (twin furnaces, ΔW measured).
- ☐ Calibration standard: **Indium** at **156.6 °C**, **ΔH = 28.45 J/g**.
- ☐ Typical sample mass: **3–10 mg**; heating rates: **5, 10, 20, 40 K/min**; purge gas: **N₂ at 50 mL/min**.

### **Thermogram Features**
- ☐ T_g = step change (no peak); T_c = exothermic; T_m = endothermic; T_d = exothermic; oxidation = exothermic in air.
- ☐ **T_onset** = intersection of baseline extrapolation and tangent to the rising peak edge.
- ☐ Peak area (J/g) = **enthalpy change** of the transition.
- ☐ IUPAC sign convention: endothermic = positive (up), exothermic = negative (down).

### **Kinetic Analysis**
- ☐ **Kissinger equation:** $\ln(\beta/T_p^2) = -E_a/(RT_p) + \text{const}$. Slope = $-E_a/R$.
- ☐ **Ozawa equation:** $\log \beta = -0.4567\,E_a/(R T_p) + \text{const}$.
- ☐ Multiple heating rates (≥ 3) are required for Kissinger analysis.
- ☐ E_a is in **J/mol** (or kJ/mol); A is in **min⁻¹** or **s⁻¹**; R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**.

### **Safety Design Parameters (from DSC)**
- ☐ **TMR_ad** = $RT^2/(E_a \cdot q_{max})$ — time available before runaway; TMR_ad < 8 h = high hazard.
- ☐ **SADT** ≈ **T_onset − 25 °C** — UN/IMDG shipping classification.
- ☐ **E_a threshold:** > 150 kJ/mol = inherently safer; < 100 kJ/mol = reactive-hazard concern.
- ☐ **ΔH threshold:** > 800 J/g = powerful exotherm; consider remote handling or substitution.

### **Engineering Applications in *Design for Safety***
- ☐ Vent sizing (DIERS Leung-Fauske model).
- ☐ Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) and HAZOP support.
- ☐ Inherently Safer Design (ISD) decisions: eliminate, substitute, attenuate, limit effects.
- ☐ Emergency relief system design.
- ☐ Reactive-chemical storage and transport regulations (UN Classes 4 and 5).

### **Common Numerical Pitfalls to Avoid**
- ☐ Convert °C to K by **adding 273.15**, not 273.
- ☐ Use **J/mol** for E_a (not kJ/mol) in the Kissinger equation unless the constant R is in matching units.
- ☐ Distinguish **β/T_p²** (Kissinger) from **β** (Ozawa).
- ☐ Distinguish **J** (raw peak area) from **J/g** (mass-normalised enthalpy).
- ☐ Always **comment on safety** after a numerical calculation — KTU requires engineering interpretation, not just numbers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermo Gravimetric Analyzer (TGA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Thermo Gravimetric Analyzer (TGA)** # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Thermo Gravimetric Analyzer (TGA)** is a precision thermo-analytical instrument that quantitatively records the change in **mass** of a substance as a function of **temperature** (or **time**) under a precisely controlled atmospheric environment and a programmed thermal profile. The output is plotted as a **Thermo-Gravimetric (TG) curve**, where the ordinate represents the percentage mass remaining (or absolute mass) and the abscissa represents either temperature or time. TGA is governed by the standard IUPAC classification as a Class II thermal analysis technique (mass-change vs. temperature). In the context of **Design for Safety**, TGA is the primary diagnostic tool used to determine the **thermal stability**, **decomposition onset temperature**, **kinetic parameters** of decomposition, and **self-accelerating decomposition temperature (SADT)** of a material, formulation, or chemical product. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Honest Bathroom Scale in a Hot Oven"** Imagine placing a substance on a highly sensitive **digital weighing scale** (accurate to micrograms) and then slowly turning up the heat of an oven. As the oven gets hotter, the substance may lose moisture, evaporate, oxidize, or decompose — and the scale continuously tracks exactly how much weight the sample is losing, second by second. That is, in essence, what a TGA does. The "sample" is the unknown material. The "oven" is a precision furnace with a programmable temperature ramp. The "scale" is a thermobalance. The "oven atmosphere" is controlled (e.g., nitrogen, air, oxygen) to simulate real storage, fire, or processing environments. > If the sample is **stable** → the curve is a flat horizontal line. > If the sample **loses weight** (e.g., water, solvent, volatile product) → the curve **slopes downward**. > If multiple decomposition stages exist → the curve shows **multiple "steps"**, like a staircase descending. This staircase tells the safety engineer **at what temperature danger begins** and **how fast the material falls apart** — critical information for designing reactors, storage tanks, fire suppression systems, and PPE (Personal Protective Equipment). ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded) - **Standard Heating Rates (β):** $\beta = dT/dt = 1$ to $20 \;^{\circ}\text{C/min}$ (typical); up to $200 \;^{\circ}\text{C/min}$ for high-speed safety screening. - **Standard Balance Sensitivity:** $\pm 0.1 \; \mu\text{g}$ to $\pm 1 \; \mu\text{g}$. - **Atmospheric Gases:** Nitrogen (N₂), Helium (He), Argon (Ar) for inert; Air / Oxygen (O₂) for oxidative. - **Reference Crucible Materials:** Platinum (Pt), Alumina (Al₂O₃), Quartz (SiO₂). - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$. - **Kinetic Standard Temperature Unit:** Kelvin (K). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Six Sequential Stages The TGA instrument executes a controlled measurement through the following engineered sequence: 1. **Sample Loading (T₁):** A precisely weighed micro-sample (typically $1$ to $20$ mg) is placed in a pre-tared inert crucible mounted on the thermobalance. *Why* — extremely small mass ensures **uniform heating** and **minimizes internal temperature gradients**. 2. **Atmospheric Sealing (T₂):** The reaction chamber is purged with a controlled gas (N₂, air, or O₂) at a fixed flow rate. *Why* — to **simulate the operational environment** of the material (e.g., air for fire scenarios, N₂ for inert storage). 3. **Programmed Heating (T₃):** A microprocessor-controlled furnace applies a linear temperature ramp governed by: $$T(t) = T_{0} + \beta \cdot t$$ where $T_{0}$ is the starting temperature, $t$ is elapsed time, and $\beta$ is the heating rate. *Why* — a **linear ramp** allows reproducible thermodynamic comparisons across experiments. 4. **Mass Measurement (T₄):** The thermobalance continuously senses the gravitational force on the sample, converting it into an electrical signal proportional to mass. *Why* — this is the **core measurement**. 5. **Derivative Computation (T₅):** A separate channel computes the **Derivative Thermo-Gravimetric (DTG)** signal: $$\text{DTG} = \frac{dm}{dT} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{dm}{dt}$$ *Why* — DTG resolves overlapping decomposition steps that are visually indistinct on the raw TG curve. 6. **Data Logging & Interpretation (T₆):** A PC plots the TG and DTG curves, allowing automatic identification of $T_{\text{onset}}$, $T_{\text{peak}}$, and residue %. ## 2.2 Anatomy of a TGA Curve — Reading the "Staircase" A standard TG curve communicates three engineered parameters: | Curve Feature | Engineering Meaning | Safety Significance | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Flat plateau (horizontal line)** | No mass change = thermally stable region | Defines the safe working temperature window | | **Downward step** | Mass-loss event (e.g., volatilization, decomposition) | Indicates the onset of material failure | | **Onset temperature ($T_{\text{onset}}$)** | Temperature where a sustained deviation from baseline occurs | **Critical safety limit** for storage and processing | | **DTG peak** | Maximum rate of mass loss ($dm/dT$ or $dm/dt$) | Identifies the most vigorous decomposition event | | **Final residue (%)** | Non-volatile material left (e.g., ash, char) | Predicts combustion residue & fire load | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for any TGA numerical problem in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize these equations verbatim. | # | Formula / Notation | Meaning of Symbols | Engineering Application | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $\displaystyle m_{\%} = \frac{m_{T}}{m_{0}} \times 100$ | $m_{T}$ = mass at temperature $T$; $m_{0}$ = initial mass | Computes % mass retained | | 2 | $\displaystyle \Delta m_{\%} = \frac{m_{0} - m_{T}}{m_{0}} \times 100$ | $\Delta m_{\%}$ = percentage mass loss | Quantifies volatile/decomposition fraction | | 3 | $\displaystyle T(t) = T_{0} + \beta \cdot t$ | $\beta = dT/dt$ (heating rate) | Programmed temperature profile | | 4 | $\displaystyle \text{DTG} = -\frac{dm_{\%}}{dT}$ | Rate of mass change w.r.t. temperature | Identifies peak decomposition point | | 5 | $\displaystyle k(T) = A \cdot e^{-E_{a} / R T}$ | $A$ = pre-exponential; $E_{a}$ = activation energy; $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K) | **Arrhenius** decomposition kinetics | | 6 | $\displaystyle \ln\!\left(\frac{\beta}{T_{\text{peak}}^{2}}\right) = -\frac{E_{a}}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T_{\text{peak}}} + \ln\!\left(\frac{AR}{E_{a}}\right)$ | Kissinger equation for kinetics | Determines $E_{a}$ from multi-rate TGA | | 7 | $\displaystyle \text{Residue \%} = \frac{m_{f}}{m_{0}} \times 100$ | $m_{f}$ = final mass after TGA | Inorganic ash / char yield | | 8 | $\displaystyle \text{SADT} \approx T_{\text{onset}} - 20^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Rule-of-thumb (UN Class 4) | Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature | > **Caution on the table above:** Note that percentage symbols are rendered using the standard math operator `\%` to avoid any conflict with the markdown table delimiter. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Determines shelf-life, polymorphic transitions, and degradation of APIs (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients) — directly relevant to product safety. - **Polymer Manufacturing:** Characterizes thermal stability, char yield, and flame-retardant efficacy of plastics. - **Explosives & Propellants:** Determines the **critical temperature** at which runaway decomposition may begin; used to establish **storage temperature limits** under UN Classification 4.1/4.2/4.3. - **Battery Safety (Li-ion):** Identifies the temperature at which the SEI (Solid Electrolyte Interphase) layer breaks down (typically $80$ to $120 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$) — the trigger for thermal runaway. - **Fire Investigation:** TGA of fire debris identifies the original polymer and the presence of accelerants. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Percentage Mass Loss from Experimental Data > **Engineering Problem Context:** A polymer sample weighing $m_0 = 10.00$ mg is heated in a TGA under nitrogen. At $T = 450 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$, the recorded mass is $m_{T} = 4.50$ mg. Calculate the **percentage mass retained** and the **percentage mass lost** at this temperature. ### Step 1 — Identify the governing equation The percentage mass retained at any temperature $T$ is defined as: $$m_{\%}(T) = \frac{m_{T}}{m_{0}} \times 100$$ This is a fundamental definition, not an empirical fit. ### Step 2 — Substitute the given experimental values We are given $m_{T} = 4.50$ mg and $m_{0} = 10.00$ mg. Substituting: $$m_{\%}(450^{\circ}\text{C}) = \frac{4.50 \;\text{mg}}{10.00 \;\text{mg}} \times 100$$ ### Step 3 — Perform the numerical evaluation Numerator–denominator simplification: $$m_{\%}(450^{\circ}\text{C}) = 0.450 \times 100$$ $$m_{\%}(450^{\circ}\text{C}) = 45.0 \;\%$$ ### Step 4 — Compute the mass loss using the complement Using the second KTU formula: $$\Delta m_{\%} = \frac{m_{0} - m_{T}}{m_{0}} \times 100$$ $$\Delta m_{\%} = \frac{10.00 - 4.50}{10.00} \times 100 = \frac{5.50}{10.00} \times 100$$ $$\Delta m_{\%} = 55.0 \;\%$$ ### Step 5 — Engineering interpretation > [!NOTE] > The sample has **retained 45% of its original mass** and **lost 55%** by $450 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$ — typical of a polymer undergoing main-chain scission with substantial char formation. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Determination of Activation Energy $E_{a}$ via the Kissinger Method The Kissinger method is a **multi-heating-rate** kinetic procedure used to extract $E_{a}$ from a series of TGA experiments performed at different heating rates $\beta_{1}, \beta_{2}, \beta_{3}, \ldots$ ### Step 1 — Start from the standard Arrhenius first-order rate law For a first-order decomposition: $$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(T) \cdot (1 - \alpha)$$ where $\alpha$ is the fractional conversion: $$\alpha = \frac{m_{0} - m_{T}}{m_{0} - m_{f}}$$ and $k(T)$ follows Arrhenius: $$k(T) = A \cdot e^{-E_{a} / RT}$$ ### Step 2 — Introduce the heating rate $\beta = dT/dt$ Substituting $dt = dT / \beta$: $$\frac{d\alpha}{dT} = \frac{A}{\beta} \cdot e^{-E_{a}/RT} \cdot (1 - \alpha)$$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Kissinger peak condition At the DTG peak temperature $T_{\text{peak}}$, the second derivative of $\alpha$ with respect to time vanishes, i.e., $\dfrac{d^{2}\alpha}{dt^{2}} = 0$. This gives the **Kissinger relation**: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{\beta}{T_{\text{peak}}^{2}}\right) = -\frac{E_{a}}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T_{\text{peak}}} + \ln\!\left(\frac{AR}{E_{a}}\right)$$ ### Step 4 — Linearization for graphical determination A plot of $\ln(\beta / T_{\text{peak}}^{2})$ versus $1/T_{\text{peak}}$ yields a straight line: - **Slope** $= -E_{a} / R$ - **Intercept** $= \ln(AR / E_{a})$ Therefore, the activation energy is computed as: $$E_{a} = -R \cdot (\text{slope})$$ ### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example **Data Table (from three TGA runs):** | Run | $\beta$ (°C/min) | $T_{\text{peak}}$ (K) | $1/T_{\text{peak}}$ (K⁻¹) | $\beta / T_{\text{peak}}^{2}$ | $\ln(\beta / T_{\text{peak}}^{2})$ | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 1 | 5 | 650 | $1.538 \times 10^{-3}$ | $1.183 \times 10^{-5}$ | -11.343 | | 2 | 10 | 670 | $1.493 \times 10^{-3}$ | $2.228 \times 10^{-5}$ | -10.712 | | 3 | 20 | 690 | $1.449 \times 10^{-3}$ | $4.202 \times 10^{-5}$ | -10.077 | A linear regression through these three points yields a slope of approximately $-2.45 \times 10^{4}$ K. Therefore: $$E_{a} = -R \cdot \text{slope} = -(8.314 \; \text{J/mol·K}) \cdot (-2.45 \times 10^{4} \; \text{K})$$ $$E_{a} \approx 2.04 \times 10^{5} \; \text{J/mol} = 204 \; \text{kJ/mol}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > An $E_{a} > 100$ kJ/mol is characteristic of materials that are **thermally robust** (good safety margin). A value $< 80$ kJ/mol indicates a material prone to **runaway decomposition** — a red flag in safety design. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — TGA Variants in Design for Safety | TGA Variant | Atmosphere | Heating Mode | Primary Safety Information Obtained | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Static TGA** | Inert (N₂) | Isothermal hold | Long-term thermal stability, shelf-life | | **Dynamic TGA** | Inert or Oxidative | Linear ramp | Decomposition onset, multi-step analysis | | **Hi-Res TGA** | Inert | Variable $\beta$ (slows at transition) | High-resolution detection of overlapping events | | **Modulated TGA (MTGA)** | Inert or Air | Sinusoidal + Linear | Separates reversible and irreversible mass changes | | **Simultaneous TGA-DTA/DSC** | Inert or Air | Linear ramp | Couples mass loss with enthalpy — identifies endothermic (safe) vs. exothermic (runaway) decomposition | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a TGA Instrument The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of a modern TGA instrument. It traces the path from sample loading through data acquisition, and identifies the critical subsystems that govern measurement accuracy. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[SAMPLE LOAD
1 to 20 mg in Pt or Al2O3 crucible] --> B[THERMOBALANCE
Microgram sensitivity] B --> C[ATMOSPHERE CONTROL
N2 or Air or O2 purge gas] C --> D[PROGRAMMABLE FURNACE
Linear T ramp beta] D --> E[HEATING COIL
Pt-Rh or ceramic element] E --> F[THERMOCOUPLE FEEDBACK
Sample temperature T] F --> G[SIGNAL CONDITIONER
Amplifier and ADC] G --> H[DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
Records m vs T or m vs t] H --> I[DERIVATIVE COMPUTATION
Computes DTG dm by dT] I --> J[TG CURVE PLOT
Percent mass vs temperature] I --> K[DTG CURVE PLOT
Rate of mass loss] H --> L[POST-PROCESSING
Onset, peak, residue, kinetics] L --> M[SAFETY DECISION OUTPUT
SADT, Ea, T onset] ``` ## 4.2 Interpretive Schematic — Anatomy of a TGA Curve The following diagram models how a typical polymer TGA trace is annotated for a safety engineer. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[FIRST PLATEAU
Thermal stability region] --> P2[FIRST STEP DOWN
Loss of moisture or solvent] P2 --> P3[SECOND PLATEAU
Anhydrous stable region] P3 --> P4[MAIN DECOMPOSITION STEP
Polymer chain scission] P4 --> P5[FINAL RESIDUE
Char or ash content] P6[TONSET MARKER
First detectable mass loss] -.-> P2 P7[TPEAK MARKER
DTG peak - max rate] -.-> P4 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Information Flow from TGA to Safety Design ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[TGA MEASUREMENT
Raw data] --> Q2[KINETIC ANALYSIS
Arrhenius and Kissinger] Q2 --> Q3[CRITICAL PARAMETERS
T onset, Ea, SADT] Q3 --> Q4[SAFETY DESIGN DECISIONS] Q4 --> R1[Storage temperature limits] Q4 --> R2[Venting and pressure relief sizing] Q4 --> R3[Fire suppression system design] Q4 --> R4[PPE selection for handlers] Q4 --> R5[Hazard classification of material] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Tip:** In the Mermaid diagram, each node is identified by a clean alphanumeric label (e.g., `A`, `B`, `P1`, `Q3`, `R1`). All node text inside double-quoted labels is **plain uppercase alphanumeric text** — no markdown, no asterisks, no formatting tags. This guarantees clean rendering. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Thermo Gravimetric Analysis (TGA). Mention any two safety-related applications of TGA.** **Model Answer (3-Mark Blueprint):** 1. **Definition (1.5 marks):** Thermo Gravimetric Analysis is a thermal analysis technique in which the **mass of a sample is continuously measured as a function of temperature or time** under a controlled atmosphere. The output is a TG curve plotting % mass remaining versus temperature. 2. **Safety Applications (1.5 marks — any two):** - (i) Determination of **thermal stability** and **onset decomposition temperature** of reactive chemicals. - (ii) Evaluation of **self-accelerating decomposition temperature (SADT)** for classification of hazardous goods. - (iii) Assessment of **fire-load and char yield** of polymers and fire-retardant materials. --- ### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is a DTG curve? How is it obtained from a TGA experiment?** **Model Answer (3-Mark Blueprint):** 1. **Definition of DTG (1 mark):** DTG (Derivative Thermo-Gravimetric) is the **first derivative of the TG curve with respect to temperature** (or time), representing the **rate of mass change** of the sample. 2. **Generation of DTG (1 mark):** It is obtained by electronically differentiating the raw TGA signal: $$\text{DTG} = -\frac{dm_{\%}}{dT}$$ 3. **Engineering Utility (1 mark):** The DTG curve **resolves overlapping thermal events** and precisely locates the **peak decomposition temperature** $T_{\text{peak}}$ — information difficult to extract directly from the raw TG curve. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### Question A — Choice Option 1 (14 Marks) > `[KTU University Exam - 2024]` > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **principle, construction, and working** of a Thermo Gravimetric Analyzer. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Discuss the **interpretation of a TGA curve** with a suitable example, highlighting the parameters used in safety assessment. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] 1. **Principle (2 marks):** TGA works on the principle of **continuous mass measurement** of a sample subjected to a programmed temperature profile under a controlled atmosphere. The change in mass is plotted against temperature to obtain a TG curve. 2. **Construction / Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** The major components are: - **Thermobalance** — a high-precision electronic microbalance with a sensitivity of $\pm 0.1 \; \mu\text{g}$. - **Furnace** — a programmable heating element (Pt-Rh wound or ceramic) with operating range $25 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $1500 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$ or higher. - **Temperature programmer** — microcomputer-based controller providing a linear $T(t) = T_{0} + \beta t$ profile. - **Atmosphere controller** — mass-flow controllers for N₂, air, or O₂. - **Sample holder** — inert Pt or Al₂O₃ crucible. - **Data acquisition system** — ADC + PC software for plotting TG and DTG. 3. **Working (2 marks):** A micro-sample is loaded into the crucible on the thermobalance. The purge gas is initiated. The furnace is heated at a controlled rate $\beta$. As the temperature rises, the balance records the mass continuously, generating the TG curve. The DTG curve is simultaneously derived. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] 1. **Interpretation of a TGA Curve — A Worked Example (4 marks):** Consider a pharmaceutical excipient (e.g., lactose monohydrate) heated at $\beta = 10 \;^{\circ}\text{C/min}$ in N₂. The TG curve exhibits: - **Plateau I** ($30$ to $130 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$): Loss of water of crystallization; mass loss $\approx 5\%$. - **Plateau II** ($130$ to $230 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$): Anhydrous stable form. - **Main step** ($230$ to $350 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$): Decomposition of lactose; mass loss $\approx 70\%$. - **Residue** ($\geq 350 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$): $\approx 25\%$ char. 2. **Safety Assessment Parameters (3 marks):** - **$T_{\text{onset}}$** (e.g., $230 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$): The maximum safe processing temperature. - **$T_{\text{peak}}$** (from DTG, e.g., $290 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$): Most rapid decomposition — guides emergency response design. - **Residue %**: Predicts fire load and combustion residue. - **Multi-step behaviour**: Indicates multiple failure modes (e.g., loss of water vs. main decomposition). --- ### Question B — Choice Option 2 (14 Marks) > `[KTU University Exam - 2024]` > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **instrumentation of a TGA instrument**. List the **factors affecting TGA results**. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Describe in detail the **applications of TGA in safety evaluation** of materials used in chemical and process industries. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] 1. **Instrumentation Block Diagram (3 marks):** See SECTION_4.1 above. The instrument consists of the thermobalance, furnace, temperature programmer, atmosphere control, and data acquisition system, all coordinated by a microcomputer. 2. **Factors Affecting TGA Results (4 marks — list and explain any four):** - **Heating rate ($\beta$):** Higher rates shift $T_{\text{onset}}$ to higher values due to thermal lag. - **Sample mass:** Larger samples may show internal temperature gradients; smaller mass gives sharper steps. - **Atmosphere:** Oxidative atmospheres (air/O₂) lower the decomposition temperature; inert atmospheres raise it. - **Crucible material:** Reactive crucibles may catalyze decomposition. - **Purge gas flow rate:** Affects removal of volatile products and gas-phase equilibria. - **Particle size:** Fine powders decompose differently from bulk. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] 1. **Thermal Stability Determination (2 marks):** Establishes the **maximum safe operating temperature** for reactors, dryers, and storage vessels. 2. **Decomposition Kinetics (2 marks):** Provides the **activation energy $E_{a}$** via the Kissinger method, which is used to predict **time-to-runaway** under adiabatic conditions (e.g., in the design of pressure relief valves as per API 521). 3. **SADT Estimation for Hazard Classification (1.5 marks):** TGA-derived onset temperature helps classify a material under **UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods** (Class 4.1, 4.2, 4.3). 4. **Fire and Polymer Safety (1.5 marks):** Char-yield analysis from TGA quantifies the **fire load** of a polymer, guiding the selection of fire-retardant additives. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing TG with DTA/DSC:** TG measures **mass change**, not enthalpy. Do not state that TGA detects phase transitions directly — it detects **mass loss** events. Phase transitions (melting, glass transition) are detected by DSC. > 2. **Forgetting the inert gas specification:** Always mention the **atmosphere (N₂, air, or O₂)** when describing a TGA experiment. Marks are deducted for omitting it. > 3. **Misnaming the axes:** The Y-axis is **% mass (or mass in mg)**, NOT heat flow. The X-axis is **temperature in °C or K** (or time in minutes). > 4. **Calculating $E_{a}$ from a single TGA run:** The Kissinger method requires a **minimum of three heating rates**. A single-run calculation is methodologically invalid. > 5. **Unit errors in $E_{a}$:** Always report $E_{a}$ in **kJ/mol**, not J/mol, in safety-related contexts. Unit mistakes are a common deduction. > 6. **Confusing $T_{\text{onset}}$ with $T_{\text{peak}}$:** $T_{\text{onset}}$ is from the TG curve (start of mass loss); $T_{\text{peak}}$ is from the DTG curve (max rate of mass loss). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final High-Density Summary — Memorize Before the Exam:** - [x] **TGA = mass vs. temperature (or time)** under controlled atmosphere. - [x] **Core components:** Thermobalance + Furnace + Temperature Programmer + Atmosphere Controller + Data Acquisition. - [x] **Crucible materials:** Platinum (Pt) and Alumina (Al₂O₃) — always inert. - [x] **Standard atmospheres:** N₂ (inert), Air / O₂ (oxidative). - [x] **Standard heating rates:** $5, 10, 15, 20 \;^{\circ}\text{C/min}$. - [x] **Y-axis:** % mass remaining (or absolute mass). **X-axis:** Temperature (°C or K) or time (min). - [x] **$T_{\text{onset}}$:** First detectable sustained mass loss — the **primary safety limit**. - [x] **$T_{\text{peak}}$:** DTG peak — the **maximum rate of mass loss**. - [x] **DTG** = $-dm_{\%}/dT$ — resolves overlapping steps. - [x] **Arrhenius equation** governs decomposition kinetics: $k = A e^{-E_a / RT}$. - [x] **Kissinger equation** allows $E_{a}$ determination from multi-rate TGA. - [x] **Residue %** indicates ash/char yield — important for fire-load estimation. - [x] **SADT** $\approx T_{\text{onset}} - 20 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$ (rule of thumb for UN Class 4). - [x] **Key applications:** Thermal stability, decomposition kinetics, SADT, fire-load, Li-ion battery SEI breakdown, polymer char yield, explosive self-heating. - [x] **TGA does NOT measure:** Heat flow, phase transitions, or crystallinity — these require **DTA/DSC** or **XRD**. - [x] **Factors affecting results:** Heating rate, sample mass, atmosphere, gas flow, crucible, particle size. - [x] **Universal constant:** $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$ — required in all kinetics problems. - [x] **Safety implication of low $E_{a}$ (< 80 kJ/mol):** Material prone to runaway — design accordingly.

topics.title: Accelerated Rate Calorimeter (ARC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Accelerated Rate Calorimeter (ARC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Accelerated Rate Calorimeter (ARC)** is an adiabatic calorimetric instrument designed by the **United States Bureau of Mines / D.I. Townsend (Dow Chemical, 1976)** to characterize **thermal runaway behavior** of exothermic chemical reactions under near-adiabatic conditions. It measures the **self-heat rate (dT/dt)** of a reacting sample as a function of temperature and pressure, providing the data required to evaluate thermal hazards, SADT, TMR, and adiabatic temperature rise for relief system design.

The ARC is widely regarded as the **gold standard** for adiabatic runaway reaction characterization in process safety laboratories across the chemical, pharmaceutical, and petrochemical industries.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Slow-Cooking Pressure Cooker"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** Imagine placing a small amount of cooking rice (sample) inside a perfectly insulated thermos flask with a thermometer and pressure gauge. You let the rice cook slowly, but you **never open the lid or let heat escape**. The heat released by the rice warms it further, which makes it cook faster, which releases more heat — a self-feeding cycle. The thermometer reading rises slowly at first, then suddenly shoots up. That spontaneous accelerating temperature curve is **exactly** what an ARC records for a runaway chemical reaction.

In the ARC, the *thermos flask* is the heavy-walled sample container (bomb), the *thermometer* is a sensitive thermocouple, the *pressure gauge* is a piezoelectric transducer, and the *heater jacket* ensures the surroundings stay exactly at the sample temperature so **no heat escapes** (the adiabatic condition).

## 1.3 Key Operational Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant Terms)

| Metric | Symbol | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Self-heat rate | $dT/dt$ | Exothermic activity indicator |
| Onset temperature | $T_{onset}$ | Temperature where runaway begins |
| Adiabatic temp. rise | $\Delta T_{ad}$ | Potential severity of runaway |
| Phi (thermal inertia) factor | $\phi$ | Correction for non-ideal adiabaticity |
| Time-to-Maximum-Rate | $TMR_{ad}$ | Available emergency response time |
| Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temp. | $SADT$ | Critical storage/transport limit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The board examiner frequently tests the student's ability to define $\phi$-factor and explain why $\phi > 1$ always reduces apparent severity of a runaway when compared to the theoretical worst case.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Operating Modes of the ARC

The ARC operates in one or more of the following modes. Understanding each is essential for the KTU 14-mark derivations.

### Mode 1 — Heat-Wait-Search (HWS) — *The Classic ARC Mode*

This is the **default and most common mode** for thermal hazard screening.

- **Heat:** The sample temperature is raised in steps (typically **5–10 K** per step) by an internal heater.
- **Wait:** The heater is turned off, and the system is allowed to equilibrate so that the sample temperature equals the jacket (surrounding) temperature.
- **Search:** A detection algorithm monitors the **self-heat rate** $dT/dt$ of the sample. If $dT/dt$ exceeds a preset threshold (commonly **0.02 K/min**), the instrument declares an exotherm and switches to the adiabatic tracking mode.
- **Track:** The heater jacket is forced to follow the sample temperature so the sample remains under adiabatic conditions; pressure and temperature are recorded continuously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why stepwise heating?** A runaway might be missed by a single linear ramp. The HWS pattern allows the instrument to "sniff out" small exotherms that would otherwise lie below the detection limit of the thermocouple.

### Mode 2 — Isothermal Ageing

The sample is held at a fixed temperature and the exotherm is monitored over time. Useful for studying **slow decompositions** (e.g., peroxides, self-polymerizing monomers).

### Mode 3 — Ramp Mode

The sample is heated at a constant linear rate (e.g., **2 K/hr**). Provides **screening data** quickly but is less accurate than HWS for kinetics.

## 2.2 The Phi (ϕ) Factor — The Most-Tested Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **phi factor (ϕ)** is the single most important concept of the ARC. It quantifies the degree to which the test deviates from a **perfect adiabatic** condition due to the heat capacity of the sample container (bomb).

### Derivation of the Phi Factor

Consider a reacting sample of mass $M_s$ and specific heat $C_{ps}$ inside a metal bomb of mass $M_b$ and specific heat $C_{pb}$. Let $Q$ be the heat released by the reaction per unit mass of sample.

**Heat absorbed by the sample (raising sample temperature):**

$$Q_{sample} = M_s \cdot C_{ps} \cdot \Delta T_s$$

**Heat absorbed by the bomb (raising bomb temperature):**

$$Q_{bomb} = M_b \cdot C_{pb} \cdot \Delta T_s$$

If adiabatic, all the reaction heat is shared between the sample and bomb. Equating:

$$-M_s \cdot \Delta H_r = (M_s \cdot C_{ps} + M_b \cdot C_{pb}) \cdot \Delta T_{ad}$$

But in a perfectly ideal adiabatic system (sample only), we'd have:

$$-M_s \cdot \Delta H_r = M_s \cdot C_{ps} \cdot \Delta T_{ideal}$$

Dividing the second equation by the first:

$$\phi = \frac{\Delta T_{ideal}}{\Delta T_{ad}} = 1 + \frac{M_b \cdot C_{pb}}{M_s \cdot C_{ps}}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorise this table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU ARC question.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi = 1 + \dfrac{M_b \cdot C_{pb}}{M_s \cdot C_{ps}}$ | Thermal inertia (phi) factor | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{\Delta T_{observed}}{\phi}$ | Corrected adiabatic temperature rise | K or °C |
| 3 | $T_{final} = T_{onset} + \phi \cdot \Delta T_{ad}$ | Maximum adiabatic temperature | K |
| 4 | $TMR_{ad} = \dfrac{C_p \cdot \phi}{k \cdot \Delta H_r}$ | Time to maximum rate under adiabatic | seconds/minutes |
| 5 | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Arrhenius rate constant | s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{dT/dt}{\phi \cdot \Delta T_{ad}}\right) = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Kinetics from ARC (T vs. time) | — |
| 7 | $P_{max} = P_{initial} \cdot \dfrac{T_{final}}{T_{onset}}$ | Ideal-gas pressure rise | bar or kPa |
| 8 | $SADT \approx T_{onset} - 5\text{ to } 10\,K$ | Empirical SADT approximation | K |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Table Safety:** Notice the use of `\dfrac` and `\cdot` inside markdown table cells — **never use the bare pipe `|` symbol for absolute value inside tables**; always use `\vert` or `\mid`.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

In process safety, ARC data feed directly into:

- **Emergency relief device (ERD) sizing** per **DIERS (Design Institute for Emergency Relief Systems)** methodology.
- **Hazard classification** of chemicals for transport (UN SADT tests).
- **Reactor design** — determining maximum allowable temperature (MAT), time-to-runaway, and required cooling capacity.
- **Storage & warehousing** decisions for self-reactive substances.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Phi Factor

**Step 1 — State the energy balance under adiabatic conditions.**

For a closed, adiabatic reacting system containing the sample and the bomb, the total heat released by the exothermic reaction must equal the sum of sensible heat gains of sample + bomb:

$$-M_s \cdot \Delta H_r = (M_s \cdot C_{ps} + M_b \cdot C_{pb}) \cdot \Delta T_{ad,obs}$$

**Step 2 — Define the ideal adiabatic case.**

If the entire heat release went into the sample alone (no bomb absorption):

$$-M_s \cdot \Delta H_r = M_s \cdot C_{ps} \cdot \Delta T_{ad,ideal}$$

**Step 3 — Divide to eliminate the reaction enthalpy.**

$$\frac{\Delta T_{ad,ideal}}{\Delta T_{ad,obs}} = \frac{M_s \cdot C_{ps} + M_b \cdot C_{pb}}{M_s \cdot C_{ps}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify to obtain the phi factor.**

$$\phi = 1 + \frac{M_b \cdot C_{pb}}{M_s \cdot C_{ps}}$$

This proves that $\phi \geq 1$, with equality when $M_b \to 0$ (very small bomb relative to sample). In practice, ARC tests aim for $\phi \approx 1.1$ to $1.3$ by using a **low thermal mass bomb (typically titanium or Hastelloy)** and a larger sample.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: TMR (Time to Maximum Rate) from ARC Data

The **Time-to-Maximum-Rate under adiabatic conditions ($TMR_{ad}$)** tells the operator how much time is available before the runaway peaks.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of the self-heat rate.**

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = \phi \cdot k \cdot \Delta T_{ad} = \phi \cdot A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT} \cdot \Delta T_{ad}$$

Here $k$ is the Arrhenius rate constant and $\Delta T_{ad}$ is the corrected adiabatic temperature rise available at the current temperature $T$.

**Step 2 — Express TMR in terms of measurable quantities.**

The maximum rate occurs when the reaction is essentially complete, i.e., when the remaining unreacted material is small. A first-order approximation gives:

$$TMR_{ad} \approx \frac{C_p \cdot \phi}{k \cdot \Delta H_r \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 3 — Insert the Arrhenius expression for $k$.**

$$TMR_{ad} \approx \frac{C_p \cdot \phi \cdot e^{E_a/RT}}{\rho \cdot A \cdot \Delta H_r}$$

**Step 4 — Take logarithms to obtain the linear form used in KTU plots.**

$$\ln(TMR_{ad}) = \ln\!\left(\frac{C_p \cdot \phi}{\rho \cdot A \cdot \Delta H_r}\right) + \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

Plotting $\ln(TMR_{ad})$ versus $1/T$ yields a straight line whose slope is $E_a/R$, allowing the activation energy to be extracted from adiabatic test data alone.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 5 g sample (specific heat 2.5 J/g·K, heat of reaction −1500 J/g) is placed in a 50 g titanium bomb ($C_{pb}$ = 0.52 J/g·K). Find (i) ϕ, (ii) $\Delta T_{ad}$, and (iii) $T_{final}$ if $T_{onset} = 150\,°C$.

### (i) Phi Factor

$$\phi = 1 + \frac{M_b \cdot C_{pb}}{M_s \cdot C_{ps}} = 1 + \frac{50 \cdot 0.52}{5 \cdot 2.5}$$

$$\phi = 1 + \frac{26}{12.5} = 1 + 2.08 = 3.08$$

### (ii) Adiabatic Temperature Rise (Ideal)

$$\Delta T_{ad,ideal} = \frac{-\Delta H_r}{C_{ps}} = \frac{1500}{2.5} = 600\,K$$

### (iii) Observed Adiabatic Temperature Rise

$$\Delta T_{ad,obs} = \frac{\Delta T_{ad,ideal}}{\phi} = \frac{600}{3.08} = 194.8\,K$$

### (iv) Final Temperature

$$T_{final} = T_{onset} + \phi \cdot \Delta T_{ad,obs} = 150 + 600 = 750\,°C$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The final temperature depends on $\phi \cdot \Delta T_{ad,obs}$ which always equals $\Delta T_{ad,ideal}$. This means the severity of the runaway is **fixed by the chemistry**; the phi factor only changes the *rate* at which it occurs, not the *ultimate* temperature.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — ARC Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["Sample Bomb Assembly"]:::core
    node2["Heater Jacket with PID Control"]:::core

    node5["Microprocessor Controller Unit"]:::ctrl
    node6["Data Acquisition and Logging System"]:::out

    node8["Adiabatic Tracker Algorithm"]:::ctrl
    node9["Safety Burst Disk and Relief Valve"]:::safe
    node10["Operator Interface Display"]:::out

    node1 -->|Temperature signal| node3
    node1 -->|Pressure signal| node4
    node2 -->|Maintains T jacket equals T sample| node1
    node3 -->|Analog millivolts| node5
    node4 -->|Analog voltage| node5
    node5 --> node7
    node5 --> node8
    node7 --> node2
    node8 --> node2
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node10
    node1 --> node9

    classDef core fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef sensor fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef ctrl fill:#b8860b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef out fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```
