
## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Heat-Wait-Search (HWS) Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Start: Load sample into bomb"]
    stepB["Seal bomb and pressurize if required"]
    stepC["Heat sample to next temperature step"]
    stepD["Wait for thermal equilibrium"]
    stepE{"Measure self heat rate dT by dt"}
    stepF{"Is dT by dt greater than 0.02 K per min"}
    stepG["Continue heating to next step"]
    stepH["Switch to adiabatic tracking mode"]
    stepI["Record T, P, dT by dt vs time"]
    stepJ["Reach end of run and cool safely"]
    stepK["Download data and analyze kinetics"]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF
    stepF -- No --> stepG --> stepC
    stepF -- Yes --> stepH --> stepI --> stepJ --> stepK
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — ARC Data Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    raw["Raw ARC Data: T, P, dT by dt vs t"]
    phi["Phi Factor from mass and Cp of bomb"]
    corr["Corrected dT by dt and dT ad"]
    onset["Onset Temperature T onset"]
    adrise["Adiabatic Temp Rise delta T ad"]
    tmr["Time to Maximum Rate TMR ad"]
    sadt["SADT for storage and transport"]
    erd["ERD Sizing per DIERS"]

    raw --> onset
    raw --> adrise
    raw --> tmr
    phi --> corr
    raw --> corr
    corr --> adrise
    corr --> tmr
    onset --> sadt
    adrise --> erd
    tmr --> erd
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]`
**Define the Phi (ϕ) factor of an ARC and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer:**
The phi factor is the ratio of the total heat capacity of sample + bomb to the heat capacity of the sample alone:

$$\phi = 1 + \frac{M_b C_{pb}}{M_s C_{ps}}$$

It is **dimensionless**, always $\geq 1$, and represents the **degree of departure from ideal adiabaticity**. A phi factor close to 1 (typically 1.05–1.25) ensures realistic runaway simulation. Higher phi factors (≥ 2) mean the bomb absorbs a lot of reaction heat, slowing the apparent runaway and underestimating the true severity. Hence minimising bomb mass and using low-Cp materials (titanium) is critical.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]`
**Briefly describe the Heat-Wait-Search (HWS) mode of ARC operation.**

**Model Answer:**
HWS is the standard screening mode of the ARC. The instrument (i) **heats** the sample to a set temperature in increments of 5–10 K, (ii) **waits** for thermal equilibrium between sample and jacket, and (iii) **searches** for an exotherm by measuring the self-heat rate $dT/dt$. If $dT/dt$ exceeds the threshold (typically 0.02 K/min), adiabatic tracking begins. This stepwise pattern allows detection of small exotherms that would be invisible in a single linear ramp.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Set A]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat functional block diagram, describe the construction and working of an Accelerated Rate Calorimeter (ARC). Explain each subsystem's role.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An exothermic decomposition has the following ARC data at a phi factor of 1.6: $T_{onset} = 130\,°C$ and observed $\Delta T_{ad} = 250\,K$. Compute (i) the true adiabatic temperature rise, (ii) the maximum final temperature, and (iii) the ideal-gas pressure rise if the initial pressure at $T_{onset}$ was 5 bar.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Sample Bomb:** A spherical or tube-shaped vessel of low thermal mass (titanium/Hastelloy, ~ 5–50 g) housing the reactant.
2. **Heater Jacket:** Surrounds the bomb; PID-controlled to track sample temperature to within ±0.1 K, ensuring adiabaticity.
3. **Thermocouple:** Type K or J inserted in the sample, providing the primary feedback signal.
4. **Pressure Transducer:** Piezoelectric sensor (0–200 bar typical) monitors gas evolution.
5. **Controller:** Microprocessor orchestrates HWS, sets heater power, logs data, and triggers tracking mode.
6. **Safety:** Burst disc rated above test pressure, vent line to scrubber, and software interlocks.

Working: Sample is loaded → bomb sealed → HWS sequence begins → on detection of exotherm, jacket tracks sample adiabatically → T, P, dT/dt logged → kinetics extracted.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) True adiabatic temperature rise:**

$$\Delta T_{ad,true} = \phi \cdot \Delta T_{ad,obs} = 1.6 \times 250 = 400\,K$$

**(ii) Maximum final temperature:**

$$T_{final} = T_{onset} + \Delta T_{ad,true} = 130 + 400 = 530\,°C$$

**(iii) Ideal-gas pressure rise:**

$$P_{max} = P_{initial} \cdot \frac{T_{final}}{T_{onset}} = 5 \times \frac{(530 + 273)}{(130 + 273)} = 5 \times \frac{803}{403} = 5 \times 1.993 = 9.96\,bar$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define TMR, SADT, and $\Delta T_{ad}$. Show graphically (sketch) how they are obtained from a typical ARC temperature-versus-time curve.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 2 g sample with $C_{ps} = 2.0$ J/g·K is placed in a 30 g stainless-steel bomb ($C_{pb} = 0.5$ J/g·K). The observed onset is 160 °C and the observed adiabatic rise is 180 K. Calculate (i) the phi factor, (ii) the corrected $\Delta T_{ad}$, and (iii) the final temperature. Comment on whether this phi factor is acceptable for reliable hazard assessment.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

- **TMR (Time-to-Maximum-Rate):** Time between onset ($T_{onset}$) and the maximum of the dT/dt curve under adiabatic conditions. Indicates **how long an operator has to respond**.
- **SADT (Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature):** Lowest ambient temperature at which a substance of mass ≥ 50 kg will self-heat by ≥ 6 K in 7 days, often approximated as $T_{onset} - 5$ to $10$ K from ARC.
- **$\Delta T_{ad}$:** Total temperature rise available from the heat of reaction divided by the heat capacity, corrected by phi.

The ARC curve plots T vs. t: an initial slow ramp, a sudden inflection at $T_{onset}$, exponential rise, and peak at $T_{final}$. TMR is read on the time axis from $T_{onset}$ to peak. $\Delta T_{ad}$ is the vertical distance from $T_{onset}$ to $T_{final}$. SADT is read from the temperature axis 5–10 K below $T_{onset}$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Phi factor:**

$$\phi = 1 + \frac{30 \times 0.5}{2 \times 2.0} = 1 + \frac{15}{4} = 1 + 3.75 = 4.75$$

**(ii) Corrected adiabatic temperature rise:**

$$\Delta T_{ad,obs} = 180\,K \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta T_{ad,true} = \phi \cdot \Delta T_{ad,obs} = 4.75 \times 180 = 855\,K$$

**(iii) Final temperature:**

$$T_{final} = 160 + 855 = 1015\,°C$$

**Comment:** A phi factor of 4.75 is **unacceptably high** for hazard assessment. The bomb mass is too large relative to the sample; the runaway observed is grossly slowed and underestimates the true rate. The test should be repeated with a smaller bomb (e.g., 8 g titanium) or a larger sample to bring $\phi$ closer to 1.2–1.5.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $\phi \cdot \Delta T_{ad,obs}$ with $\Delta T_{ad,obs}$.** Many students add $\phi \cdot \Delta T_{ad,obs}$ to $T_{onset}$ *again*, double-counting. Remember: $\phi \cdot \Delta T_{ad,obs} = \Delta T_{ad,ideal}$ (the true severity), so $T_{final} = T_{onset} + \phi \cdot \Delta T_{ad,obs}$ already gives the correct final temperature.
> 2. **Using °C in the gas law instead of K.** Always convert to Kelvin for $P_{max} = P_i \cdot T_f/T_i$.
> 3. **Stating phi = 1 always.** This is the idealised case; in practice $\phi$ must be calculated from the actual bomb and sample masses.
> 4. **Forgetting units in the heat-capacity ratio.** $C_p$ must be in the **same units** for bomb and sample; $M$ must be in the **same units**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **ARC = Adiabatic Calorimeter** that mimics worst-case thermal runaway.
- [x] **HWS mode** is the standard: Heat → Wait → Search → Track.
- [x] **Phi factor** $\phi = 1 + \dfrac{M_b C_{pb}}{M_s C_{ps}}$ is the **most important** derived quantity.
- [x] **Ideal $\phi$ ≈ 1.0–1.3**; values above ~ 2 are unreliable for kinetics.
- [x] $\Delta T_{ad,obs} = \dfrac{\Delta T_{ad,ideal}}{\phi}$ — the test under-records severity as $\phi$ rises.
- [x] $T_{final} = T_{onset} + \phi \cdot \Delta T_{ad,obs} = T_{onset} + \Delta T_{ad,ideal}$.
- [x] **TMR** decreases sharply with rising temperature — log-linear Arrhenius behaviour.
- [x] **SADT** ≈ $T_{onset} - (5\text{ to }10)$ K for transport classification.
- [x] **Pressure rise** $P_{max} = P_i \cdot T_f / T_i$ (ideal gas approximation).
- [x] **ARC outputs feed** DIERS relief sizing, SADT testing, MAT/MTT determination, and runaway severity ranking.
- [x] **Typical materials:** Titanium or Hastelloy low-thermal-mass bombs; Type K thermocouples; piezoelectric pressure transducers.
- [x] **Common mistakes:** Mixing °C with K; ignoring the bomb's Cp; double-counting $\phi$ when computing $T_{final}$; assuming $\phi = 1$.
- [x] **KTU Board Pattern:** Question on $\phi$ derivation (7 marks) + numerical application (7 marks) is the **most frequent 14-mark pattern** for ARC.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reactive Calorimeter (RC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reactive Calorimeter (RC) # 1. Reactive Calorimeter (RC) — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Reactive Calorimeter (RC)** is a precision laboratory instrument used in process safety engineering to quantitatively measure the **heat released or absorbed** during a chemical reaction under precisely controlled thermal conditions. It simultaneously determines reaction kinetics, heat of reaction ($\Delta H_r$), specific heat capacity ($c_p$), and overall heat transfer characteristics (UA value) of a reaction mixture at plant-relevant scales (typically 0.5 L to 2 L). In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with NEP 2020 outcome-based education, the RC is categorized as a **bench-scale calorimetric technique** that bridges laboratory characterization and plant-scale safety design. It is a direct, experimental tool for acquiring data needed to: - Establish the **MTSR** (Maximum Temperature of Synthesis Reaction) - Compute the **adiabatic temperature rise** ($\Delta T_{ad}$) - Calibrate runaway reaction severity using the **TMRad** (Time to Maximum Rate under adiabatic conditions) - Validate thermal data obtained from accelerating rate calorimetry (ARC) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A reactive calorimeter is distinct from a DSC or an ARC because it operates at **near-plant conditions** (real concentrations, real solvents, real stirring, real dosing) rather than under the highly idealized, milligram-scale conditions of a DSC pan. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a **chemical reaction as a "patient"** in an Intensive Care Unit (ICU): - The **reactor vessel** is the patient's body. - The **temperature probe (Tr)** is the clinical thermometer continuously reading the body's temperature. - The **jacket (Tj)** is the cooling blanket or heating pad applied externally. - The **heat flow sensor (qr)** is the cardiograph reading the "metabolic heat" produced by the patient's internal chemistry. - The **control system** is the nurse monitoring both the body and the blanket to keep the patient stable. Just as an ICU isolates the patient's internal metabolism from the outside environment while measuring vital signs, a reactive calorimeter **isolates the chemical reaction's heat from the surroundings** while measuring how much heat is generated. If the "patient" (reaction) suddenly produces excessive heat — analogous to a fever — the RC immediately detects it, just as a runaway reaction would in a real plant. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Conditions for RC Operation:** > - **Operating Pressure:** **0.001 MPa to 1.0 MPa** (vacuum to ~10 bar, depending on vessel rating) > - **Operating Temperature Range:** **−80 °C to +300 °C** > - **Typical Vessel Volume:** **0.5 L, 1.0 L, 1.8 L, 2.5 L** > - **Stirring Speed Range:** **0 to 2000 rpm** (typically 50–500 rpm for kinetics work) > - **Standard Reference:** **ASTM E698** for thermal stability kinetics; manufacturer-specific procedures for Mettler-Toledo RC1e, HEL Simular, Syrris Atlas, etc. > [!NOTE] > The three operational modes of a reactive calorimeter are: > 1. **Isothermal** — Reactor temperature held constant; jacket temperature manipulated. > 2. **Isoperibolic** — Jacket temperature held constant; reactor temperature free to drift. > 3. **Adiabatic** — No heat exchange with the jacket; used for runaway studies. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principles — Structured Logic A reactive calorimeter functions by enforcing a **simultaneous solution** of two coupled thermal balances: one for the **reaction mass** and one for the **jacket fluid**. Through carefully controlled jacket temperature profiles, the heat flow between the reactor and jacket can be mathematically isolated. ### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic - **Step 1 — Initialize the reactor:** Load reactants, solvent, and any catalyst at the required starting temperature Tr,0. The jacket is pre-conditioned to a known temperature Tj,0. - **Step 2 — Start data acquisition:** Temperature sensors (typically Pt-100 RTDs with accuracy ±0.01 K) and mass flow measurements begin logging at a sampling rate of **1 Hz to 10 Hz**. - **Step 3 — Calibrate UA:** A known electrical heater input (Qcal) is applied to determine the overall heat transfer coefficient × area product (UA) at multiple temperatures and stirring rates. - **Step 4 — Initiate reaction:** Triggers include dosing, catalyst addition, mixing, or temperature ramp. - **Step 5 — Track heat release:** The system continuously computes qr(t) from the energy balance. - **Step 6 — Determine ΔHr:** Integrate qr(t) over the reaction time and divide by moles reacted. - **Step 7 — Extract kinetics:** Fit qr(t) data to a rate law (nth order, autocatalytic, etc.) to extract rate constant k(T) and activation energy EA. - **Step 8 — Runaway simulation:** Apply adiabatic closure to extrapolate the worst-case thermal excursion. ### 2.1.2 Why This Matters in KTU Examinations The examiner expects students to know that the RC's primary advantage is its **direct measurement** of heat flow at process-relevant scale. Unlike DSC (where only a few milligrams are heated) or ARC (where adiabatic conditions dominate), the RC preserves the **mixing, dosing, and mass-transfer realities** of an industrial reactor. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table compiles every equation you must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic. **All boundary conditions and units are KTU board-standard.** | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Definitions | Standard Units | |---|--------------|----------|----------------------|----------------| | 1 | Reactor Heat Balance | $q_r = m_r \cdot c_{p,r} \cdot \dfrac{dT_r}{dt} + U \cdot A \cdot (T_r - T_j)$ | qr: heat release rate, mr: mass, cp,r: sp. heat, dTr/dt: temp ramp rate, UA: heat transfer coefficient × area | W, kg, J/(kg·K), K/s, W/K | | 2 | Jacket Energy Balance | $U \cdot A \cdot (T_r - T_j) = m_j \cdot c_{p,j} \cdot \dfrac{dT_j}{dt} - \dot{Q}_{j,heater}$ | mj: jacket mass, cp,j: jacket sp. heat, dTj/dt: jacket ramp, Q̇j,heater: jacket heater power | W/K, kg, J/(kg·K), K/s, W | | 3 | Heat of Reaction | $\Delta H_r = -\dfrac{\int_{0}^{t_f} q_r(t)\, dt}{n_{reacted}}$ | nreacted: moles consumed, tf: final time | J/mol | | 4 | Adiabatic Temperature Rise | $\Delta T_{ad} = -\dfrac{\Delta H_r}{m_{tot} \cdot c_{p,tot}}$ | mtot: total mass, cp,tot: mean specific heat | K | | 5 | MTSR Definition | $MTSR = T_{process} + X_{ach} \cdot \Delta T_{ad}$ | Tprocess: operating temperature, Xach: achieved conversion at failure | K or °C | | 6 | Fractional Conversion | $X_A = 1 - \dfrac{n_A}{n_{A,0}}$ | nA: remaining moles, nA,0: initial moles | dimensionless | | 7 | Arrhenius Rate Law | $k(T) = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_A}{R \cdot T}\right)$ | A: pre-exponential, EA: activation energy, R: 8.314 J/(mol·K) | 1/s, J/mol, J/(mol·K), K | | 8 | Overall Rate Expression | $r_A = k(T) \cdot C_A^n$ | rA: reaction rate, CA: concentration, n: order | mol/(L·s), mol/L, — | | 9 | Runaway Criterion (Semenov) | $B = \dfrac{Q_{gen,max}}{Q_{rem,max}} = \dfrac{-\Delta H_r \cdot k(T_0) \cdot C_{A,0}^n}{U \cdot A \cdot T_0}$ | B: thermal runaway number | dimensionless | | 10 | TMRad Approximation | $\ln(TMR_{ad}) = a + \dfrac{b}{T}$ | a, b: empirical constants from ARC/adiabatic data | s, K | | 11 | Reactor Heat Capacity | $C_{p,reactor} = m_r \cdot c_{p,r} + m_{vessel} \cdot c_{p,vessel}$ | mvessel: vessel mass, cp,vessel: vessel specific heat | J/K | | 12 | Heat Release Per Unit Volume | $q_V = \dfrac{q_r}{V_r}$ | Vr: reactor working volume | W/m³ | ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering In a typical process safety lifecycle: - **Reaction Development Stage:** A chemist optimizes yield at the bench. The RC confirms the heat of reaction and identifies whether cooling demands are within the plant's capability. - **Process Design Stage:** Engineers scale up the reaction. RC data feeds the design of the plant reactor's cooling coil area, vent sizing, and emergency cooling capacity. - **HAZOP Stage:** MTSR values from RC experiments are used to determine which deviations can lead to runaway, directly informing independent protection layers (IPLs). - **Inherent Safety Design:** RC data supports the substitution of a solvent with a higher heat capacity (larger cp) to lower ΔTad, achieving passive risk reduction. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the RC Heat Balance Equation ### 3.1.1 The First Law of Thermodynamics Applied to the Reactor The energy balance on a perfectly mixed batch reactor (no inflow, no outflow) is: $$\text{Rate of energy accumulation} = \text{Rate of energy generation} - \text{Rate of energy removal}$$ Expressed mathematically: $$\frac{dU_{sys}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{gen} - \dot{Q}_{rem}$$ Expanding the accumulation term using $U_{sys} = m_r \cdot c_{p,r} \cdot T_r$ (assuming liquid-phase batch with constant cp,r): $$\frac{d(m_r \cdot c_{p,r} \cdot T_r)}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{gen} - \dot{Q}_{rem}$$ For a constant-mass system with constant cp,r: $$m_r \cdot c_{p,r} \cdot \frac{dT_r}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{gen} - \dot{Q}_{rem}$$ The heat removal term is given by Newton's law of cooling for a jacketed vessel: $$\dot{Q}_{rem} = U \cdot A \cdot (T_r - T_j)$$ By definition, the **heat release rate** qr equals the net heat generation within the system (positive for exothermic): $$q_r = \dot{Q}_{gen}$$ Substituting into the energy balance: $$m_r \cdot c_{p,r} \cdot \frac{dT_r}{dt} = q_r - U \cdot A \cdot (T_r - T_j)$$ Rearranging to isolate qr: $$\boxed{q_r = m_r \cdot c_{p,r} \cdot \frac{dT_r}{dt} + U \cdot A \cdot (T_r - T_j)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** The two terms on the right-hand side have distinct physical meanings: > 1. **Accumulation term** — $m_r \cdot c_{p,r} \cdot dT_r/dt$ captures the energy stored in the reaction mass as it heats (or cools). > 2. **Exchange term** — $U \cdot A \cdot (T_r - T_j)$ captures the energy transferred to the jacket (positive when Tr > Tj). > > In **isothermal mode**, dTr/dt = 0, simplifying to $q_r = U \cdot A \cdot (T_r - T_j)$. This is the simplest measurement scenario. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Adiabatic Temperature Rise (ΔTad) ### 3.2.1 Starting from the Heat of Reaction The heat of reaction is defined as the heat released per mole of limiting reactant consumed: $$\Delta H_r = \frac{Q_{released}}{n_{reacted}}$$ For a fully converted batch (X = 1), the total heat released is: $$Q_{released} = -\Delta H_r \cdot n_{A,0}$$ > **Sign convention note:** $\Delta H_r < 0$ for an exothermic reaction; the negative sign in front ensures Qreleased > 0. ### 3.2.2 Energy Conservation Under Adiabatic Conditions Under adiabatic closure (perfect insulation, no heat loss), all the released heat raises the temperature of the entire reactor contents: $$Q_{released} = (m_r \cdot c_{p,r} + m_{vessel} \cdot c_{p,vessel}) \cdot \Delta T_{ad}$$ Equating the two expressions for Qreleased: $$-\Delta H_r \cdot n_{A,0} = C_{p,reactor} \cdot \Delta T_{ad}$$ Solving for $\Delta T_{ad}$: $$\boxed{\Delta T_{ad} = -\frac{\Delta H_r \cdot n_{A,0}}{C_{p,reactor}} = -\frac{\Delta H_r \cdot C_{A,0} \cdot V_r}{m_{tot} \cdot c_{p,tot}}}$$ For dilute systems where mtot · cp,tot ≈ mr · cp,r: $$\Delta T_{ad} \approx -\frac{\Delta H_r}{m_r \cdot c_{p,r}} \quad \text{(per unit mass basis)}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of the MTSR Expression The **Maximum Temperature of Synthesis Reaction (MTSR)** is defined as the highest temperature the reaction mass can reach if cooling is suddenly lost (e.g., utility failure) at the worst possible moment (i.e., at the conversion that maximizes the remaining exothermic potential). Starting from a material balance on the limiting reactant A: $$n_A(t) = n_{A,0} \cdot (1 - X_A(t))$$ The unreacted moles (which can still release heat) are: $$n_{A,unreacted} = n_{A,0} \cdot (1 - X_{ach})$$ The remaining heat available for release is: $$Q_{remaining} = -\Delta H_r \cdot n_{A,unreacted} = -\Delta H_r \cdot n_{A,0} \cdot (1 - X_{ach})$$ Under adiabatic conditions after utility failure, this remaining heat raises the reactor temperature from Tprocess to MTSR: $$Q_{remaining} = C_{p,reactor} \cdot (MTSR - T_{process})$$ By definition, $C_{p,reactor} \cdot \Delta T_{ad} = -\Delta H_r \cdot n_{A,0}$ (full conversion case). Therefore: $$C_{p,reactor} \cdot (MTSR - T_{process}) = C_{p,reactor} \cdot \Delta T_{ad} \cdot (1 - X_{ach})$$ Dividing both sides by Cp,reactor: $$\boxed{MTSR = T_{process} + (1 - X_{ach}) \cdot \Delta T_{ad}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Many textbooks write MTSR = Tprocess + Xach · ΔTad. This is the **same** as Tprocess + (1 − Xach) · ΔTad only if Xach is interpreted as **unconverted fraction**. In the KTU standard convention, **Xach is the converted fraction**, so use $MTSR = T_{process} + (1 - X_{ach}) \cdot \Delta T_{ad}$ in your answer. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) ### Problem Statement A saponification reaction is carried out isothermally in an RC at Tr = 323.15 K (50 °C). The reactor has: - mr = 1.2 kg of reaction mixture - cp,r = 3200 J/(kg·K) - UA = 8.0 W/K - Jacket temperature Tj = 316.15 K - Initial moles of limiting reactant nA,0 = 0.5 mol - Final conversion Xf = 0.95 The RC measures a constant heat release rate of qr = 320 W during the reaction. ### Part (a) — Heat of Reaction Calculation The reaction was maintained isothermal, so $dT_r/dt = 0$. The total reaction time is found from the constant heat release: $$\Delta t = \frac{Q_{total}}{q_r} = \frac{-\Delta H_r \cdot n_{reacted}}{q_r}$$ Calculate nreacted: $$n_{reacted} = X_f \cdot n_{A,0} = 0.95 \times 0.5 = 0.475 \text{ mol}$$ The total heat released is: $$Q_{total} = q_r \cdot \Delta t$$ The heat of reaction (per mole) is: $$\Delta H_r = -\frac{Q_{total}}{n_{reacted}}$$ > **Note:** Without the time Δt, we can only express ΔHr in terms of qr and Δt. Suppose the reaction ran for **3600 s** (1 hour): $$Q_{total} = 320 \text{ W} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 1{,}152{,}000 \text{ J} = 1152 \text{ kJ}$$ $$\Delta H_r = -\frac{1{,}152{,}000}{0.475} = -2{,}425{,}263 \text{ J/mol} \approx -2425 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ ### Part (b) — Adiabatic Temperature Rise $$\Delta T_{ad} = -\frac{\Delta H_r \cdot n_{A,0}}{m_r \cdot c_{p,r}}$$ $$\Delta T_{ad} = -\frac{(-2{,}425{,}263) \times 0.5}{1.2 \times 3200}$$ $$\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{1{,}212{,}632}{3840} \approx 315.8 \text{ K}$$ ### Part (c) — MTSR Calculation If cooling is lost at the end of the reaction (Xach = 0.95): $$MTSR = T_{process} + (1 - X_{ach}) \cdot \Delta T_{ad}$$ $$MTSR = 323.15 + (1 - 0.95) \times 315.8$$ $$MTSR = 323.15 + 0.05 \times 315.8 = 323.15 + 15.79 = 338.94 \text{ K}$$ $$MTSR \approx 338.94 \text{ K} = 65.79 \text{ °C}$$ > [!NOTE] > The reactor would only rise by ~16 K if the cooling failed at high conversion — a manageable event. However, if cooling failed **early** (Xach = 0.1), then: > $MTSR = 323.15 + 0.9 \times 315.8 = 323.15 + 284.2 = 607.4$ K = **334.2 °C** — this would trigger solvent boiling and a major runaway. > This is why MTSR is **conversion-dependent** and must always be evaluated for the worst-case Xach. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Reactive Calorimeter The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional data flow** of a typical RC1-class instrument, showing the physical components and their information pathways. ```mermaid flowchart TB reactorNode["REACTOR VESSEL
T_r Measurement
V = 0.5 to 2.5 L"] jacketNode["JACKET SYSTEM
T_j Control Loop
Heat Transfer Fluid"] heaterNode["ELECTRICAL HEATER
Q_cal Input
Calibration Source"] probeTR["Pt100 PROBE T_r
Resolution 0.01 K"] probeTJ["Pt100 PROBE T_j
Resolution 0.01 K"] stirNode["MAGNETIC STIRRER
0 to 2000 rpm"] dosingNode["DOSING UNIT
Reagent Feed
Volumetric Pump"] ctrlNode["PROCESS CONTROLLER
PID Algorithm
Sample Rate 1 Hz"] balNode["ENERGY BALANCE ENGINE
q_r = m c_p dT/dt
Plus U A T_r minus T_j"] dataNode["DATA ACQUISITION
CSV and Time Series"] calcNode["KINETICS FITTING
Arrhenius k of T
Activation Energy"] mtsrNode["MTSR PREDICTOR
T plus Delta T_ad
Runaway Simulation"] reportNode["SAFETY REPORT
HAZOP Input
MTSR, TMR_ad"] reactorNode --- probeTR jacketNode --- probeTJ jacketNode --- heaterNode reactorNode --- stirNode reactorNode --- dosingNode probeTR --> ctrlNode probeTJ --> ctrlNode ctrlNode --> jacketNode ctrlNode --> balNode balNode --> dataNode dataNode --> calcNode calcNode --> mtsrNode mtsrNode --> reportNode ``` ## 4.2 Energy Flow Diagram of Heat Release and Removal The following Mermaid block illustrates the **two competing energy pathways** that the RC must separate mathematically. ```mermaid flowchart LR rxnNode["CHEMICAL REACTION
Heat Generation
q_r in Watts"] accNode["REACTOR ACCUMULATION
m_r times c_p,r
times dT_r per dt"] exchNode["JACKET EXCHANGE
U times A times
T_r minus T_j"] remNode["REMOVAL TO UTILITY
Cooling Water
Chilled Brine"] metrNode["MEASURED q_r
Net Heat Release
via Calorimetry"] rxnNode --> accNode rxnNode --> exchNode exchNode --> remNode accNode --> metrNode exchNode --> metrNode ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The chemical reaction acts as the source. The energy it generates is split into two sinks: (a) heating the reactor mass itself (the "accumulation" term, measured by dTr/dt), and (b) flowing through the jacket wall into the utility (the "exchange" term, measured via Tj and UA). The calorimeter algebraically sums these two sinks to back-calculate the source — qr. ## 4.3 Information Cascade from RC to Plant Safety Systems ```mermaid flowchart TB labNode["LAB RC EXPERIMENT
Bench Scale 1 L"] kineNode["KINETIC PARAMETERS
k of T, E_A, n"] deltaHNode["HEAT OF REACTION
Delta H_r in kJ per mol"] uaNode["UA VALUE
W per K"] mtsrNode["MTSR
Maximum Synthesis T"] hysysNode["PROCESS SIMULATOR
Aspen, HYSYS"] pfdNode["P and ID DESIGN
Reactor Cooling Coil"] hazopNode["HAZOP and LOPA
Independent Protection Layers"] opsNode["PLANT OPERATIONS
Alarm and Trip Settings"] labNode --> kineNode labNode --> deltaHNode labNode --> uaNode labNode --> mtsrNode kineNode --> hysysNode deltaHNode --> hysysNode uaNode --> pfdNode mtsrNode --> hazopNode hysysNode --> pfdNode pfdNode --> hazopNode hazopNode --> opsNode ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model] **Q: Define a reactive calorimeter. List any three of its key measured outputs.** **Model Answer:** A reactive calorimeter is a precision laboratory instrument used to measure the rate of heat release (qr) and total heat (Q) of a chemical reaction under controlled thermal conditions, while simultaneously determining reaction kinetics. The three key measured outputs are: 1. **Heat release rate** (qr in W) as a function of time. 2. **Overall heat of reaction** ($\Delta H_r$ in J/mol), obtained by time-integrating qr. 3. **Overall heat transfer coefficient × area** (UA in W/K), determined by electrical calibration. > [!NOTE] > A 3-mark answer must give the **definition** (1 mark) plus **three distinct outputs** (1 mark total for the list, with the third earning the third mark). Memorize UA, $\Delta H_r$, and qr(t) as the unbreakable trio. ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] **Q: Distinguish between isothermal and isoperibolic modes of operation in a reactive calorimeter.** **Model Answer:** | Mode | Definition | Heat Balance Simplification | Typical Use | |------|------------|------------------------------|-------------| | Isothermal | Reactor temperature Tr is held constant by manipulating Tj | $dT_r/dt = 0$ ⟹ $q_r = U \cdot A \cdot (T_r - T_j)$ | Accurate kinetic studies | | Isoperibolic | Jacket temperature Tj is held constant; Tr is free to vary | Both terms in the heat balance are non-zero | Simulates real plant behavior under constant utility temperature | In isothermal mode, the heat release rate is measured **directly** as the heat flowing to the jacket. In isoperibolic mode, the heat release must be **back-calculated** using both the dTr/dt term and the UA(Tr−Tj) term. **(3 marks for the table + brief explanation)** ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 ESE Module internal choice pattern: every Part B question is worth **14 marks**, and the student must answer **either OR**, not both. Each choice has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. ### Question Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **A(a): With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working principle of a Mettler-Toledo RC1-type reactive calorimeter. How is the heat release rate qr computed from the measured data?** **Model Answer:** **Construction:** The RC1 consists of a **jacketed glass or stainless-steel reaction vessel** (typically 1 L or 1.8 L), an **overhead stirrer** with torque measurement, a **Pt-100 temperature sensor** immersed in the reaction mass, a **second Pt-100 sensor** in the jacket fluid, an **electrical calibration heater** with known power Qcal, a **dosing system** for controlled reagent addition, and a **digital process controller** running a PID loop on the jacket temperature. **Working Principle:** The reaction mass is held under chosen thermal conditions (isothermal, isoperibolic, or adiabatic) by manipulating the jacket fluid temperature. The energy balance on the reactor is solved in real time. The calibration heater provides an in-situ measurement of UA at every temperature, eliminating the need for separate cold-flow experiments. **Computation of qr:** From the heat balance derived in Section 3.1: $$q_r = m_r \cdot c_{p,r} \cdot \frac{dT_r}{dt} + U \cdot A \cdot (T_r - T_j)$$ All terms on the right are directly measured: mr by weighing, cp,r from a calibration heat step, dTr/dt from successive Tr readings, UA from the calibration heater pulse, Tr and Tj from the two Pt-100 probes. **(7 marks: 3 for sketch description, 2 for working, 2 for equation with term identification)** #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A(b): A nitration reaction is carried out in a 1.5 L RC at 35 °C. The reactor contains 1.8 kg of reaction mixture with cp = 2900 J/(kg·K). The reaction has a heat of reaction ΔHr = −180 kJ/mol. The initial moles of the limiting reagent are 0.6 mol. Compute (i) the adiabatic temperature rise, and (ii) the MTSR if cooling is lost at 60% conversion.** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Adiabatic temperature rise calculation:** $$\Delta T_{ad} = -\frac{\Delta H_r \cdot n_{A,0}}{m_r \cdot c_{p,r}}$$ Substituting values: $$\Delta T_{ad} = -\frac{(-180{,}000) \times 0.6}{1.8 \times 2900}$$ $$\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{108{,}000}{5220}$$ $$\Delta T_{ad} \approx 20.69 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2 — MTSR calculation at Xach = 0.60:** $$MTSR = T_{process} + (1 - X_{ach}) \cdot \Delta T_{ad}$$ $$MTSR = (35 + 273.15) + (1 - 0.60) \times 20.69$$ $$MTSR = 308.15 + 0.40 \times 20.69$$ $$MTSR = 308.15 + 8.276$$ $$MTSR = 316.43 \text{ K} = 43.28 \text{ °C}$$ **Final answer:** ΔTad ≈ 20.69 K and MTSR ≈ 316.43 K (43.28 °C). **(7 marks: 2 marks for correct formula selection, 3 marks for substitution and numerical accuracy, 1 mark for unit conversion, 1 mark for final boxed answer)** ### Question Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **B(a): Discuss the role of a reactive calorimeter in establishing the Maximum Temperature of Synthesis Reaction (MTSR). Why is MTSR critical for designing emergency cooling systems?** **Model Answer:** The reactive calorimeter is the **primary experimental source** of the data needed to compute MTSR. The MTSR is the **worst-case temperature** the reaction mass can reach if all cooling is lost at the moment when the unreacted potential is at its maximum impact on plant safety. The RC provides: - **$\Delta H_r$** — the heat of reaction, computed from the integrated qr(t) curve. - **$m_r \cdot c_{p,r}$** — the heat capacity of the reactor contents, from mass and calibrated cp. - **$T_{process}$** — the operating temperature at the time of failure. - **$X_{ach}$** — the conversion at failure, obtained from the kinetics fit to qr(t) data. These four quantities combine as: $$MTSR = T_{process} + (1 - X_{ach}) \cdot \frac{-\Delta H_r \cdot n_{A,0}}{m_r \cdot c_{p,r}}$$ **MTSR is critical for emergency cooling design** because: 1. It sets the **upper bound** on the temperature the vessel internals, gaskets, and relief devices must withstand without catastrophic failure. 2. It defines the **set-point of high-temperature interlocks** in the safety instrumented system (SIS). 3. It determines whether the **maximum allowable working temperature (MAWT)** of the vessel is exceeded, triggering the need for redundant cooling. 4. It identifies the **time window** for operator intervention before runaway becomes irreversible. **(7 marks: 3 for RC's role in MTSR determination, 2 for the equation, 2 for the four criticality points)** #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **B(b): The thermal data from a reactive calorimeter run on a hydrogenation reaction are: $\Delta H_r$ = −95 kJ/mol, cp,r = 3500 J/(kg·K), mr = 2.0 kg, nA,0 = 1.0 mol. If the plant reactor has UA = 250 W/K and operates at T0 = 350 K with a first-order rate constant k(T0) = 4.5 × 10−4 s−1 and initial concentration CA,0 = 800 mol/m³, evaluate the Semenov thermal runaway number B. Comment on the runaway risk.** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Identify the Semenov runaway criterion formula:** $$B = \frac{Q_{gen,max}}{Q_{rem,max}} = \frac{-\Delta H_r \cdot k(T_0) \cdot C_{A,0}^n}{U \cdot A \cdot T_0}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Qgen,max (numerator):** For a first-order reaction (n = 1): $$Q_{gen,max} = -\Delta H_r \cdot k(T_0) \cdot C_{A,0}$$ $$Q_{gen,max} = 95{,}000 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 800$$ $$Q_{gen,max} = 95{,}000 \times 0.36 = 34{,}200 \text{ W/m}^3$$ **Step 3 — Compute Qrem,max (denominator):** $$Q_{rem,max} = U \cdot A \cdot T_0 = 250 \times 350 = 87{,}500 \text{ W}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the Semenov number B:** $$B = \frac{34{,}200}{87{,}500} \approx 0.391$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** Since **B = 0.391 < 1**, the maximum heat generation rate is less than the maximum heat removal rate. The system is **thermally stable** at the operating point, and runaway will not occur spontaneously. However, this analysis assumes a constant jacket temperature. In practice, jacket fouling, cooling water failure, or loss of agitation could push B above 1. Therefore, a **safety margin** must be retained, and high-temperature trips should be set below MTSR. **(7 marks: 2 for formula, 2 for substitution, 1 for arithmetic, 1 for unit check, 1 for correct interpretation and safety comment)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Sign convention trap:** Many students drop the negative sign on $\Delta H_r$ when computing $\Delta T_{ad}$ or B. Remember: for exothermic reactions, $\Delta H_r < 0$, and the negative sign in the formula cancels it to yield a positive result. The Qgen must be expressed as a **positive magnitude** in the Semenov criterion. > - **Unit trap:** CA,0 must be in mol/m³ (not mol/L) and k must be in s−1 for the W/m³ result. A 1 mol/L = 1000 mol/m³ conversion is often missed. > - **MTSR vs Tprocess:** Students often forget that MTSR is computed using **unconverted** moles (1 − Xach), not converted moles. This single sign error can shift the answer by 100 K or more. > - **MTSR ≠ adiabatic temperature rise:** These are different quantities. ΔTad assumes full conversion; MTSR accounts for partial conversion. Confusing them is a common 2-mark deduction. > - **Qgen,max vs Qgen,initial:** At the very start (X = 0), Qgen is at its maximum. As the reaction proceeds, Qgen decays. The Semenov criterion evaluates the **initial** worst case. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE** - **Reactive Calorimeter (RC)** = lab instrument measuring heat flow at near-plant conditions (0.5–2.5 L). - **Three operating modes:** Isothermal (Tr constant), Isoperibolic (Tj constant), Adiabatic (no exchange). - **Core heat balance equation:** $q_r = m_r \cdot c_{p,r} \cdot dT_r/dt + U \cdot A \cdot (T_r - T_j)$. **Memorize verbatim.** - **Heat of reaction** formula: $\Delta H_r = -\dfrac{\int q_r \, dt}{n_{reacted}}$. - **Adiabatic temperature rise:** $\Delta T_{ad} = -\dfrac{\Delta H_r \cdot n_{A,0}}{m_r \cdot c_{p,r}}$. - **MTSR formula:** $MTSR = T_{process} + (1 - X_{ach}) \cdot \Delta T_{ad}$ (KTU convention: Xach = converted fraction). - **Semenov runaway criterion:** B = Qgen,max / Qrem,max; **B > 1 implies thermal runaway**. - **TMRad:** Time to Maximum Rate under adiabatic conditions; obtained from ARC/adiabatic RC runs; relates to EA via $\ln(TMR_{ad}) = a + b/T$. - **UA calibration:** Done in-situ using a known electrical heater pulse; must be redone at multiple T and stirring speeds. - **Arrhenius law:** $k(T) = A \cdot \exp(-E_A / R T)$ with R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**. - **Fractional conversion:** $X_A = 1 - n_A / n_{A,0}$. - **RC vs DSC vs ARC:** RC = plant-scale, real concentrations; DSC = milligram-scale, idealized; ARC = adiabatic, runaway-focused. - **Sign convention:** Exothermic reactions have $\Delta H_r < 0$; the negative signs in formulas cancel this to give positive temperature rises. - **Always quote MTSR in Kelvin (or °C) with explicit unit.** Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units. - **Safety margin:** Plant cooling is sized for MTSR + ~20 K margin, with high-temperature trips set below MTSR. - **MTSR is conversion-dependent** — worst case is when failure occurs **early in the reaction** (Xach small, unconverted potential large). - **Reactive calorimeter data flows** into: kinetic model fitting, process simulation (Aspen/HYSYS), P&ID design, HAZOP/LOPA, and plant trip settings.

topics.title: Reaction System Screening Tool (RSST) - Principles of Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reaction System Screening Tool (RSST) - Principles of Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reaction System Screening Tool (RSST)** is a **low thermal inertia, pseudo-adiabatic calorimeter** developed by **Fauske & Associates, Inc. (FAI)** for the rapid screening of exothermic runaway reactions in **chemical process safety** design. It tracks the self-heating rate of a reactive chemical sample inside a thin-walled, low-mass containment vessel, while simultaneously recording the **pressure rise** generated by volatile decomposition products or gases.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (PESFT412 – Design for Safety, Module 3), the RSST is classified under the family of **adiabatic calorimetry instruments** that bridge the gap between simple **DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry)** screening and the more elaborate **VSP2 (Vent Sizing Package 2)** or **ARC (Accelerating Rate Calorimeter)** testing. Its defining feature is a **phi-factor (φ) between 1.04 and 1.25**, making it essentially *near-ideal adiabatic*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker with a Thermometer":**
> Imagine placing a small amount of food (the reactive chemical) inside a very thin, lightweight aluminium cup (the test cell). You put this cup into a heated oven (the heater jacket) that tracks and matches the cup's temperature perfectly. As soon as the food begins to cook and release heat (an exothermic reaction begins), the oven *instantly* raises its own temperature to match the cup, so **no heat escapes**. Because no heat is lost, the temperature inside the cup accelerates — sometimes explosively — revealing the **true runaway behaviour** of the reaction. A pressure gauge on top records the steam build-up.
> That is exactly what an RSST does: it **isolates a tiny reactive sample in a low-mass container**, follows its temperature and pressure, and tells the safety engineer **how violently the reaction would behave in a real 50,000-litre plant reactor**.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical RSST Value |
|---|---|---|
| Sample mass | $M_s$ | **0.5 g to 5 g** |
| Test-cell mass | $M_b$ | **≈ 1–2 g (thin-walled)** |
| Phi-factor | $\phi$ | **1.04 to 1.25** |
| Operating pressure | $P$ | up to **≈ 200 bar** |
| Sample heat capacity | $C_s$ | ≈ **1.5–2.5 J/g·K** |
| Cell heat capacity | $C_b$ | ≈ **0.5 J/g·K** |
| Temperature range | $T$ | **Ambient to 500 °C** |
| Self-heat detection limit | $dT/dt$ | **0.05 °C/min** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle — The Phi (φ) Factor

The **single most important parameter** governing any adiabatic calorimeter (RSST, ARC, VSP2) is the **thermal inertia factor φ**. It quantifies how *close* the system is to the ideal adiabatic limit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Inertia (Phi Factor)**
> $$\phi = \frac{\text{Total heat capacity of system}}{\text{Heat capacity of sample alone}} = \frac{M_s C_s + M_b C_b}{M_s C_s}$$
>
> $$\boxed{\phi = 1 + \frac{M_b C_b}{M_s C_s}}$$

Where:
- $M_s$ = sample mass (g)
- $C_s$ = specific heat capacity of sample (J/g·K)
- $M_b$ = mass of containment vessel (g)
- $C_b$ = specific heat capacity of vessel (J/g·K)

**Interpretation:**
- $\phi \to 1$ ⇒ **perfectly adiabatic** (no heat absorbed by container) ⇒ runaway behaviour is *fully realistic*.
- $\phi \gg 1$ ⇒ **highly non-adiabatic** ⇒ temperature rise is *damped* and *unrealistic* for plant scale.

Because the RSST uses an **extremely thin-walled, low-mass stainless-steel or titanium cell**, $M_b C_b \ll M_s C_s$, giving $\phi \approx 1.04$ – **the closest any commercial screening calorimeter gets to true adiabaticity**.

## 2.2 Corrected Adiabatic Temperature Rise

Since some heat is unavoidably absorbed by the cell, the observed temperature must be mathematically corrected to reflect what the **sample alone** would experience:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{ad,\,corrected} = \phi \cdot \Delta T_{observed}}$$

## 2.3 Self-Heat Rate and Arrhenius Kinetics

The **self-heat rate** $dT/dt$ detected by the RSST is the direct signature of an exothermic reaction. For a first-order decomposition:

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = \Delta T_{ad} \cdot A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

Where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (1/s)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/mol·K**
- $\Delta T_{ad}$ = adiabatic temperature rise (K)

## 2.4 Time to Maximum Rate (TMR / TMRad)

This is **THE** key safety metric — how long does a runaway have from the moment it is detected until it becomes uncontrollable?

$$\boxed{\text{TMR} = \frac{C_p \, R \, T^2}{\phi \, \Delta T_{ad} \, E_a}}$$

Where $C_p$ is the molar heat capacity of the reactant. **Shorter TMR ⇒ more hazardous reaction.**

## 2.5 Pressure Dynamics

The RSST simultaneously measures pressure rise $\Delta P$. The total pressure is decomposed into:

$$P_{total} = P_{initial} + \Delta P_{non-condensable} + \Delta P_{vapour}(T)$$

Using the **ideal gas law** for non-condensable gas generation:

$$\Delta P = \frac{n_{gas} R T}{V_{headspace}}$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi = 1 + \dfrac{M_b C_b}{M_s C_s}$ | Quantifies adiabaticity |
| 2 | $\Delta T_{ad,\,corr} = \phi \cdot \Delta T_{obs}$ | Sample-only temperature rise |
| 3 | $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \Delta T_{ad} \cdot A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Self-heat rate (Arrhenius) |
| 4 | $\text{TMR} = \dfrac{C_p R T^2}{\phi \, \Delta T_{ad} \, E_a}$ | Time to runaway maximum |
| 5 | $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Reaction rate constant |
| 6 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{n_{gas} R T}{V_{void}}$ | Non-condensable pressure rise |
| 7 | $\text{SADT} = T \text{ at TMR} = 7 \text{ days}$ | Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature |
| 8 | $P_{total} = P_i + \Delta P_{NC} + P_{vap}(T)$ | Total pressure decomposition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** RSST data feeds directly into:
> - **Relief-valve sizing** (per API 521 / DIERS methodology)
> - **Reactor vent-area calculations**
> - **HAZOP & LOPA studies** (defining worst-case scenarios)
> - **Storage & transportation hazard classification** (per UN recommendations)
> - **Process design limits** (safe operating temperature envelopes)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Balance Leading to the φ-Factor

Consider a sealed RSST test cell containing sample + cell. Under **adiabatic** conditions the heat released by the reaction stays in the system:

$$Q_{released} = -\Delta H_r \cdot (-dn_A)$$

This heat raises the temperature of **both** the sample and the container:

$$Q_{absorbed} = (M_s C_s + M_b C_b)\, \Delta T_{observed}$$

Equating (energy conservation):

$$(-\Delta H_r)\, dn_A = (M_s C_s + M_b C_b)\, \Delta T_{obs}$$

Dividing both sides by $M_s C_s$:

$$\frac{(-\Delta H_r)\, dn_A}{M_s C_s} = \left(1 + \frac{M_b C_b}{M_s C_s}\right)\, \Delta T_{obs}$$

$$\Delta T_{ad,\,sample} = \phi \cdot \Delta T_{observed}$$

This proves **why φ dilutes the observed temperature rise** when the cell absorbs energy.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Arrhenius Self-Heat Rate from RSST

For a single-step first-order decomposition $A \rightarrow B + \text{heat}$:

$$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k\, C_A \quad \text{where} \quad k = A\, e^{-E_a/RT}$$

The heat generation rate per unit mass:

$$\dot{q} = (-\Delta H_r)\, k\, C_A$$

Under adiabatic conditions all this heat goes into raising the temperature:

$$M_s C_s \frac{dT}{dt} = (-\Delta H_r)\, k\, C_A \cdot M_s$$

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{(-\Delta H_r)}{C_s}\, k\, C_A$$

But $(-\Delta H_r)/C_s = \Delta T_{ad}$ (the theoretical adiabatic rise), and in the **early stage** $C_A \approx C_{A0}$ (pseudo-zero-order):

$$\boxed{\frac{dT}{dt} = \Delta T_{ad} \cdot A\, e^{-E_a/RT}}$$

This equation is the **heart of RSST data analysis** — plotting $\ln(dT/dt)$ vs $1/T$ yields a straight line whose slope gives $-E_a/R$ and whose intercept gives $\ln(\Delta T_{ad} A)$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Time to Maximum Rate (TMR)

Starting from the self-heat rate:

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = \Delta T_{ad} \cdot A\, e^{-E_a/RT}$$

Separating variables:

$$dt = \frac{dT}{\Delta T_{ad} \cdot A\, e^{-E_a/RT}}$$

Integrating from the detection temperature $T_0$ to runaway temperature $T_f$:

$$TMR = \int_{T_0}^{T_f} \frac{e^{E_a/RT}}{\Delta T_{ad} \cdot A}\, dT$$

Using the standard approximation that $E_a/RT \gg 1$ (valid because runaway reactions have very high activation energies), the integrand is sharply peaked at $T_f$, and the integral evaluates to:

$$TMR \approx \frac{R\, T^2}{\phi\, \Delta T_{ad}\, E_a \cdot k(T)}$$

Applying $k(T) = A e^{-E_a/RT}$:

$$\boxed{\text{TMR} \approx \frac{C_p\, R\, T^2}{\phi\, \Delta T_{ad}\, E_a}}$$

This is the formula used in **every KTU-validated numerical problem** on adiabatic calorimetry.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A sample in an RSST has $M_s = 2.0$ g, $C_s = 2.0$ J/g·K, $M_b = 1.0$ g, $C_b = 0.5$ J/g·K. The observed runaway temperature rise is $\Delta T_{obs} = 180$ K. Calculate (a) φ-factor, (b) corrected adiabatic temperature rise, (c) TMR at $T = 423$ K given $E_a = 80$ kJ/mol and $C_p = 150$ J/mol·K.

**(a) Phi factor:**

$$\phi = 1 + \frac{M_b C_b}{M_s C_s} = 1 + \frac{1.0 \times 0.5}{2.0 \times 2.0} = 1 + \frac{0.5}{4.0} = 1.125$$

**(b) Corrected $\Delta T_{ad}$:**

$$\Delta T_{ad,\,corr} = \phi \cdot \Delta T_{obs} = 1.125 \times 180 = 202.5\, K$$

**(c) TMR:**

$$TMR = \frac{C_p R T^2}{\phi \Delta T_{ad} E_a} = \frac{150 \times 8.314 \times (423)^2}{1.125 \times 202.5 \times 80000}$$

Step-by-step:
- Numerator: $150 \times 8.314 \times 178929 = 2.232 \times 10^8$
- Denominator: $1.125 \times 202.5 \times 80000 = 1.8225 \times 10^7$
- $TMR = \dfrac{2.232 \times 10^8}{1.8225 \times 10^7} \approx 12.25\, \text{seconds}$

**Interpretation:** A runaway detected at 423 K will reach its maximum rate in **only 12 seconds** — clearly a *severely hazardous* reaction requiring emergency venting or inhibition.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the RSST

The diagram below shows the **block-level functional architecture** of the RSST, mapping every component and its information/energy flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Reactive Sample 0.5 to 5 g] --> B[Thin Walled Low Mass Test Cell]
    B --> C[Temperature Sensor Type K Thermocouple]
    B --> D[Pressure Transducer 0 to 200 bar]
    C --> E[Data Acquisition System DAQ]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Controller with Heat Wait Search Algorithm]
    F --> G[Heater Jacket with PID Control]
    G --> B
    E --> H[Output Temperature Profile]
    E --> I[Output Pressure Profile]
    E --> J[Derived Self Heat Rate dT by dt]
    J --> K[Arrhenius Plot ln dT by dt vs 1 by T]
    K --> L[Activation Energy E sub a]
    J --> M[TMR Calculation]
    H --> N[Onset Temperature T onset]
    I --> O[Peak Pressure P max]
```

## 4.2 Heat–Wait–Search (HWS) Operating Sequence

The RSST's signature operating mode — the **Heat–Wait–Search cycle** — is shown below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Heat Step Ramp oven to next target T at fixed rate 1 to 2 K per min] --> W
    W[Wait Step Hold T constant for equilibration period typically 15 to 30 min] --> S
    S[Search Step Monitor dT by dt for self heat above detection threshold 0.05 K per min] --> DEC{Exotherm detected?}
    DEC -- Yes --> STOP[Record T onset and continue adiabatic tracking]
    DEC -- No --> H
    STOP --> NXT[Move to next temperature step]
```

## 4.3 Information Flow from Sensor to Safety Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Raw Temperature Time Data] --> S2[Filter and Smooth 5 point moving average]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Numerical Derivative dT by dt]
    S3 --> S4[Plot Semi log dT by dt vs 1 by T]
    S4 --> S5[Linear Regression for E sub a]
    S5 --> S6[Safety Metrics T onset SADT TMRad Phi factor]
    S6 --> S7[Reactor Relief Sizing DIERS]
    S6 --> S8[HAZOP Hot Spot Identification]
    S6 --> S9[Storage Classification UN Test]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram — RSST vs ARC vs VSP2

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Screening Calorimeter"
        RSST[RSST Phi 1.04 to 1.25 Thin walled cell Low mass]
    end
    subgraph "Bench Scale Adiabatic"
        ARC[ARC Phi 1.5 to 2.5 Glass or metal cell Standard mass]
    end
    subgraph "Pilot Scale Adiabatic"
        VSP2[VSP2 Phi 1.05 to 1.15 True adiabatic with compensation heater]
    end
    RSST -- Faster -- SCN[Screening]
    ARC -- Detailed -- SCN
    VSP2 -- Scale up -- SCN
    RSST --> R1[Onset T and Pressure]
    ARC --> R2[Kinetic data E sub a]
    VSP2 --> R3[Vent sizing data]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "phi-factor" used in adiabatic calorimetry. Why is it significant in RSST?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **phi-factor (φ)** is defined as the ratio of the total thermal mass of the sample-and-container system to that of the sample alone:

$$\phi = \frac{M_s C_s + M_b C_b}{M_s C_s} = 1 + \frac{M_b C_b}{M_s C_s}$$

It is **significant in RSST** because the instrument uses an extremely thin-walled low-mass cell so that $\phi$ is very close to **1** (typically 1.04 – 1.25). This means the sample experiences **near-ideal adiabatic conditions** and the measured temperature/pressure rise realistically reflects what would happen in a large plant reactor.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the Heat–Wait–Search (HWS) operating mode of RSST?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
HWS is the **automatic temperature-programmed scanning sequence** used by the RSST to detect exothermic activity. It consists of three repeated steps:
1. **Heat** — the oven temperature is raised at a fixed rate (typically 1–2 °C/min) to the next target.
2. **Wait** — the temperature is held constant for ~15–30 minutes to equilibrate.
3. **Search** — the self-heat rate $dT/dt$ is monitored; if it exceeds the detection threshold (≈ 0.05 °C/min), an exotherm is recorded.

This mode **avoids masking weak exotherms** that would otherwise be hidden by the heating ramp.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** In RSST problems, students *commonly lose 2–3 marks* by:
> - Forgetting to apply the φ-correction to observed temperature rise.
> - Using $C_s$ in J/g·K but $E_a$ in J/mol without unit-conversion consistency.
> - Misnaming "self-heat rate" as "reaction rate" — they are linked but not identical.
> - Skipping the units in the TMR formula, which leads to dimensional errors.
>
> **Always state units, always apply φ-correction, always write the energy balance assumption.**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **principles of operation** and major components of the **Reaction System Screening Tool (RSST)**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain in detail the **phi-factor** in adiabatic calorimetry. Discuss how the low-φ design of the RSST makes it superior to conventional ARC for **reaction hazard screening**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 Marks**

The RSST is a **pseudo-adiabatic, low thermal-inertia calorimeter** whose operating principle rests on three pillars:

1. **Low-thermal-inertia containment:** A 0.5–5 g reactive sample is sealed inside a thin-walled (≈ 0.05 mm) spherical test cell of mass 1–2 g, giving $\phi \to 1$.
2. **Heat-Wait-Search scanning:** The oven uses the HWS algorithm to track the sample temperature and detect exothermic self-heating.
3. **Simultaneous T & P measurement:** A type-K thermocouple records sample temperature; a pressure transducer records gas/vapour pressure up to 200 bar.

**Major components** (refer to Section 4.1 block diagram):
- Test cell (sample container)
- Heater jacket (oven with PID control)
- Sample thermocouple
- Pressure transducer
- DAQ + controller (HWS algorithm)
- Output: $T(t)$, $P(t)$, $dT/dt(t)$

The detected exotherm yields **onset temperature $T_{onset}$**, **self-heat rate profile**, **peak pressure**, and (via Arrhenius plot) **$E_a$ and pre-exponential factor**.

**(b) — 7 Marks**

Derivation already shown in Section 3.1; here is the engineering narrative:

$$\phi = 1 + \frac{M_b C_b}{M_s C_s}$$

**Why low-φ matters:**
- When $\phi = 1$, **all** reaction heat stays in the sample → $\Delta T$ and $\Delta P$ are real.
- When $\phi = 2$ (typical ARC), half the heat goes into warming the cell → runaway appears *slower* and *cooler* than it really is → **under-prediction of hazard**.

**RSST vs ARC for screening:**

| Parameter | RSST | ARC |
|---|---|---|
| Typical φ | **1.04–1.25** | 1.5–2.5 |
| Sample size | 0.5–5 g | 1–10 g |
| Detection limit | 0.05 °C/min | 0.02 °C/min |
| Throughput | High (screening) | Lower (detailed) |
| Use | Onset + vent sizing | Full kinetic study |

**Conclusion:** RSST's low φ makes it ideal for **early-stage hazard screening** because it delivers plant-realistic onset temperatures and pressure rises using tiny samples, before committing to costly pilot-scale VSP2 tests.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice*

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **self-heat rate** of a first-order exothermic decomposition as measured by an RSST. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A sample in an RSST has $M_s = 3.0$ g, $C_s = 1.8$ J/g·K, $M_b = 1.2$ g, $C_b = 0.5$ J/g·K. The observed $\Delta T_{obs} = 150$ K and $E_a = 75$ kJ/mol. Compute (i) φ-factor, (ii) corrected adiabatic temperature rise, (iii) TMR at $T = 400$ K, taking $C_p = 140$ J/mol·K. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 Marks**

For first-order $A \to B + \text{heat}$:

$$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k C_A \quad ; \quad k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$$

Heat generation rate per unit mass:

$$\dot{q} = (-\Delta H_r)\, k\, C_A$$

Under adiabatic assumption, all heat raises sample temperature:

$$M_s C_s \frac{dT}{dt} = (-\Delta H_r)\, k\, C_A \cdot M_s$$

Rearranging, with $(-\Delta H_r)/C_s = \Delta T_{ad}$:

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = \Delta T_{ad} \cdot A\, e^{-E_a/RT}$$

Including the φ-correction for cell heat capacity:

$$\boxed{\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{\Delta T_{ad}}{\phi} \cdot A\, e^{-E_a/RT}}$$

This is the working equation that RSST data are fitted to using an Arrhenius plot.

**(b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) φ-factor:**

$$\phi = 1 + \frac{M_b C_b}{M_s C_s} = 1 + \frac{1.2 \times 0.5}{3.0 \times 1.8} = 1 + \frac{0.6}{5.4} = 1 + 0.1111 = 1.1111$$

**(ii) Corrected $\Delta T_{ad}$:**

$$\Delta T_{ad,\,corr} = \phi \cdot \Delta T_{obs} = 1.1111 \times 150 = 166.67\, K$$

**(iii) TMR at $T = 400$ K:**

$$TMR = \frac{C_p R T^2}{\phi\, \Delta T_{ad}\, E_a} = \frac{140 \times 8.314 \times (400)^2}{1.1111 \times 166.67 \times 75000}$$

Numerator: $140 \times 8.314 \times 160000 = 1.8623 \times 10^8$

Denominator: $1.1111 \times 166.67 \times 75000 = 1.3889 \times 10^7$

$$TMR = \frac{1.8623 \times 10^8}{1.3889 \times 10^7} \approx 13.41\, \text{seconds}$$

**Conclusion:** The reaction has a TMR of only **~13 s** at 400 K — a serious runaway hazard requiring immediate relief design.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — RSST Principles of Operations**

- [ ] **RSST** = *Reaction System Screening Tool*, a low-φ pseudo-adiabatic calorimeter by **Fauske & Associates (FAI)**.
- [ ] **Phi-factor** $\phi = 1 + \dfrac{M_b C_b}{M_s C_s}$ — RSST achieves **φ ≈ 1.04–1.25** (near-ideal adiabatic).
- [ ] **Corrected temperature rise:** $\Delta T_{ad,\,corr} = \phi \cdot \Delta T_{obs}$.
- [ ] **Heat–Wait–Search (HWS)** mode = repeated (Heat → Wait → Search) cycles with detection threshold ~**0.05 °C/min**.
- [ ] **Sample size:** 0.5–5 g; **cell:** thin-walled low-mass sphere; **pressure limit:** up to ~200 bar.
- [ ] **Self-heat rate (first order):** $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \Delta T_{ad} \cdot A\, e^{-E_a/RT}$.
- [ ] **TMR formula:** $\text{TMR} = \dfrac{C_p R T^2}{\phi\, \Delta T_{ad}\, E_a}$ — *shorter TMR ⇒ more hazardous*.
- [ ] RSST sits in the **calorimetry hierarchy:** DSC (screening) → **RSST (reaction hazards)** → ARC (kinetics) → VSP2 (vent design).
- [ ] Outputs feed **relief-valve sizing (DIERS)**, **HAZOP/LOPA**, and **UN transport classification**.
- [ ] **Always apply φ-correction** before using RSST data for plant-scale extrapolation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Controlling Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Controlling Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Controlling Parameters (Definition per KTU 2024 Syllabus, Module 3):**
> *Controlling Parameters* in the context of Design for Safety (DfS) are the **measurable, quantifiable, and adjustable variables** that govern the safety performance, reliability, and risk profile of a product, system, or process throughout its life cycle. These parameters act as the **primary inputs** that designers and safety engineers manipulate, monitor, and control to ensure that the design output remains within **acceptable safety thresholds** as defined by regulatory standards (e.g., ISO 12100, IEC 61508, OSHA).

In rigorous engineering terms, controlling parameters are the independent variables in the safety function:

$$ S = f(P_1, P_2, P_3, \ldots, P_n) $$

where $S$ is the overall safety performance index and $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$ represent the individual controlling parameters such as load, stress, temperature, voltage, speed, human factors, and environmental conditions.

### 1.2 Classification of Controlling Parameters

| Category | Examples | Governing Standard |
|----------|----------|--------------------|
| **Physical Parameters** | Load, Stress, Strain, Pressure | ISO 12100 |
| **Thermal Parameters** | Temperature, Heat Flux | IEC 60068 |
| **Electrical Parameters** | Voltage, Current, Resistance | IEC 61508 |
| **Environmental Parameters** | Humidity, Vibration, Dust | ISO 14001 |
| **Human Parameters** | Reaction Time, Ergonomics | ANSI Z365 |
| **Operational Parameters** | Speed, Cycle Time, Duty Cycle | ISO 13849 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 3 emphasizes that controlling parameters are not limited to physical/technical variables. **Human factors, organizational factors, and procedural parameters** are equally critical in modern safety design philosophy.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Thermostat Analogy:**
> Think of controlling parameters as the **dials on a thermostat** that controls room temperature. You have dials for *target temperature* (set-point), *sensitivity* (tolerance), *upper limit* (safety cutoff), and *response time* (damping). The room's actual temperature is a function of these settings. Similarly, in a safety-critical design, the *safety level* of a product is a function of multiple controlling parameters (load capacity, material strength, redundancy level, human-machine interface quality, etc.). Adjust any one dial, and the entire safety profile shifts.

**Real-World Example:** In designing a **pressure vessel**, the controlling parameters are:
- **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)** — the threshold beyond which failure occurs
- **Safety Valve Set Pressure** — the relief activation point
- **Material Yield Strength** — the structural limit
- **Corrosion Allowance** — the degradation margin
- **Design Temperature** — the operating envelope
- **Weld Joint Efficiency** — the manufacturing quality factor

Each of these is a *controlling parameter* that the designer must size, specify, and verify.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Framework

The theory of controlling parameters in Design for Safety rests on **three foundational pillars**:

### Pillar 1: The Safety Function Concept
Every engineering system has an implicit safety function:

$$ S_{total} = f(P_{design}, P_{material}, P_{operational}, P_{human}, P_{environment}) $$

The safety function is **multivariate**, meaning multiple parameters simultaneously govern the safety outcome. A change in any one parameter can cascade through the system.

### Pillar 2: Parameter Hierarchy (Criticality-Based)
Not all controlling parameters carry equal weight. The hierarchy is:

1. **Primary (Critical) Parameters** — Direct failure causation (e.g., load, stress, voltage)
2. **Secondary (Contributing) Parameters** — Influence failure probability (e.g., temperature, humidity)
3. **Tertiary (Contextual) Parameters** — Background conditions (e.g., cultural factors, training level)

### Pillar 3: The Safety Margin Principle
For every controlling parameter, a **safety margin** must be maintained:

$$ SM = \frac{P_{limit} - P_{operating}}{P_{operating}} \times 100\% $$

where $P_{limit}$ is the parameter's failure threshold and $P_{operating}$ is the nominal operating value. Industry standard minimum **SM = 1.5 to 4.0** depending on application criticality.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 — High-Yield Formula Reference Table**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Boundary Condition | Application |
|---|-------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Safety Factor (SF)** | $SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{\sigma_{working}}$ | $SF \geq 1.5$ (static), $\geq 4.0$ (dynamic) | Mechanical design |
| 2 | **Safety Margin (SM)** | $SM = \dfrac{P_{limit} - P_{operating}}{P_{operating}}$ | Expressed as % | All engineering |
| 3 | **Reliability Function** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate (1/hr) | Reliability engineering |
| 4 | **Hazard Rate** | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Bathtub curve model | Failure analysis |
| 5 | **Risk Score** | $Risk = Probability \times Severity$ | Score 1–25 (5×5 matrix) | Risk assessment |
| 6 | **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** | $RRF = \dfrac{Risk_{initial}}{Risk_{residual}}$ | $RRF \geq 1$ | Safety measure efficacy |
| 7 | **SIL Determination** | $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$ | IEC 61508, $PFD$ per demand | Functional safety |
| 8 | **Thermal Safety Limit** | $T_{max} = T_{ambient} + \Delta T_{critical}$ | $T$ in Kelvin or °C | Thermal design |
| 9 | **Human Error Probability** | $HEP = k \cdot e^{-s}$ | $s$ = slip index (Swain method) | Human factors |
| 10 | **MTBF Relation** | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours between failures | Maintainability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on LaTeX in Tables:** The vertical pipe symbol `\vert` or `\mid` is used in place of `\|` to preserve markdown table integrity per KTU formatting standards.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Controlling parameters are the **backbone of safety-critical design** in:

- **Aerospace:** Load factors, fatigue cycles, material degradation rates
- **Nuclear Industry:** Coolant flow rate, neutron flux, containment pressure
- **Automotive:** Crash energy absorption, airbag deployment timing, brake force
- **Medical Devices:** Dosage limits, electrical leakage current, biocompatibility indices
- **Process Industry:** Relief valve set pressure, LEL/UEL concentrations, ventilation rates

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" Behind Controlling Parameters:**
> Without explicitly identifying and quantifying controlling parameters, safety design becomes *reactive* (fixing failures after they occur) rather than *proactive* (preventing failures by design). Controlling parameters transform safety from an art into a **discipline of measurable engineering**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Deriving the Safety Factor from First Principles

The **Safety Factor (SF)** is the most fundamental controlling parameter in mechanical design for safety. Let us derive it rigorously.

### Step 1: Define the Failure Criterion

Every material has an ultimate stress limit $\sigma_{ultimate}$ beyond which fracture occurs. The design must ensure that the **working stress** $\sigma_{working}$ remains well below this limit.

$$\sigma_{working} = \frac{F_{applied}}{A_{cross-section}}$$

where $F_{applied}$ is the applied force (N) and $A_{cross-section}$ is the load-bearing area (m²).

### Step 2: Introduce the Safety Factor

The safety factor is defined as the ratio of the material's failure limit to the design's working stress:

$$SF = \frac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{\sigma_{working}}$$

### Step 3: Rearrangement for Design Use

For design, we need to find the required cross-sectional area given a known load and material. Rearranging:

$$A_{required} = SF \cdot \frac{F_{applied}}{\sigma_{ultimate}}$$

This is the **fundamental design equation** used in every KTU mechanical/civil safety design problem.

### Step 4: Incorporating Partial Safety Factors (Limit State Design)

In modern KTU 2024 curriculum following IS 456 / Eurocode philosophy, the safety factor is split into **partial safety factors**:

$$\gamma_m \cdot \sigma_{design} \leq \frac{\sigma_{characteristic}}{\gamma_c}$$

where:
- $\gamma_m$ = partial safety factor for material (typically **1.15** for steel, **1.5** for concrete)
- $\gamma_c$ = partial safety factor for load (typically **1.5** for dead load, **1.5** for live load)
- $\sigma_{characteristic}$ = material characteristic strength
- $\sigma_{design}$ = design stress

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A steel column must support a working load of **250 kN**. The ultimate tensile strength of the material is **410 MPa**. If a safety factor of **2.5** is required (per IS 800), find the minimum cross-sectional area.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Write down knowns.

$$F_{applied} = 250 \text{ kN} = 250{,}000 \text{ N}$$

$$\sigma_{ultimate} = 410 \text{ MPa} = 410 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$$

$$SF = 2.5$$

Step 2: Apply the design equation.

$$A_{required} = SF \cdot \frac{F_{applied}}{\sigma_{ultimate}}$$

Step 3: Substitute values.

$$A_{required} = 2.5 \cdot \frac{250{,}000}{410 \times 10^6}$$

Step 4: Compute.

$$A_{required} = 2.5 \cdot 6.0976 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{required} = 1.5244 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Step 5: Convert to mm².

$$A_{required} = 1524.4 \text{ mm}^2$$

Step 6: Interpretation. A circular cross-section would need a diameter of:

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot A_{required}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot 1524.4}{\pi}} \approx 44.05 \text{ mm}$$

So a standard **45 mm diameter** bar would be selected.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Risk Score Computation (5×5 Matrix Method)

The **Risk Score** is a critical controlling parameter in safety assessment.

### Step 1: Define Probability Categories (1 to 5)

| Score | Probability | Description |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | Rare | May occur in >10 years |
| 2 | Unlikely | May occur in 1–10 years |
| 3 | Possible | May occur in 1 year |
| 4 | Likely | May occur monthly |
| 5 | Almost Certain | May occur daily |

### Step 2: Define Severity Categories (1 to 5)

| Score | Severity | Description |
|-------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | Negligible | No injury |
| 2 | Minor | First aid injury |
| 3 | Moderate | Medical treatment |
| 4 | Major | Serious injury, hospitalization |
| 5 | Catastrophic | Fatality, mass damage |

### Step 3: Risk Score Formula

$$R = P \times S$$

where $P \in [1,5]$ and $S \in [1,5]$, giving $R \in [1,25]$.

### Step 4: Risk Acceptance Criteria

| Risk Score | Classification | Action Required |
|------------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1–4 | **Acceptable** | No action |
| 5–9 | **Tolerable** | Monitor, reduce if practicable |
| 10–15 | **Moderate** | Reduce risk, implement controls |
| 16–25 | **Unacceptable** | Halt operation, mandatory redesign |

### Step 5: Numerical Example

A machine has a hazard where:
- Probability of occurrence = 4 (Likely, monthly)
- Severity if it occurs = 3 (Moderate injury)

$$R = P \times S = 4 \times 3 = 12$$

This is in the **Moderate** band → risk reduction controls are mandatory.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Controlling Parameters on Safety Outcomes

| Controlling Parameter | ↑ Increase Effect on Safety | ↓ Decrease Effect on Safety | Mitigation Strategy |
|----------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| Safety Factor (SF) | Higher reliability, higher cost | Lower cost, higher risk | Optimize via cost-benefit |
| Redundancy Level | Fault tolerance increases | Single-point failure risk | Apply n+1 / 2oo3 voting |
| Material Strength | Higher load capacity | Brittle failure risk | Use ductile alloys |
| Inspection Frequency | Early fault detection | Operational downtime | Schedule-based CBM |
| Operator Training | Lower human error | Higher residual HEP | Continuous learning |
| Environmental Severity | Reduced margin | Lower reliability | Climate-controlled enclosures |
| Maintenance Quality | Longer MTBF | Higher breakdown rate | TPM implementation |

---
