# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Controlling Parameters in the context of Design for Safety. Give three examples from different engineering domains.

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**

> Controlling Parameters are the measurable, quantifiable variables that govern the safety performance of a product, system, or process. They are the primary inputs that designers manipulate to maintain safety within acceptable thresholds throughout the life cycle.
>
> **Examples:**
> 1. **Mechanical Domain:** Safety Factor (SF) = ratio of ultimate stress to working stress in a pressure vessel.
> 2. **Electrical Domain:** Insulation resistance (MΩ) — controls the risk of electric shock in household appliances.
> 3. **Human Factors Domain:** Operator reaction time (seconds) — controls the safety margin in emergency shutdown systems.
>
> Each controlling parameter is bounded by a *limit value* and an *operating value*, with the safety margin being the difference.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for concept, 1 for examples)*

### Question 2: Distinguish between Safety Factor and Safety Margin. Why is Safety Margin considered a more informative controlling parameter?

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Safety Factor (SF)** is a *ratio* of the failure limit to the working value. It is dimensionless and indicates the *multiple* of safety.
>
> $$SF = \frac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{\sigma_{working}}$$
>
> **Safety Margin (SM)** is the *relative difference* expressed as a percentage. It indicates the *buffer* in absolute terms.
>
> $$SM = \frac{P_{limit} - P_{operating}}{P_{operating}} \times 100\%$$
>
> **Why SM is more informative:** A high SF does not always mean high safety — it depends on the absolute values. SM directly tells the designer the percentage of remaining buffer before failure, enabling better-informed decisions, especially in systems with non-linear failure modes (e.g., brittle fracture, fatigue).

*(3 marks: 1 for SF definition, 1 for SM definition, 1 for distinction)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain in detail the classification of controlling parameters in Design for Safety. Discuss Physical, Environmental, Human, and Operational parameters with at least two examples per category. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

> In Design for Safety, controlling parameters are the adjustable variables that govern the safety outcome of a system. Their classification enables systematic identification and management.
>
> **1. Physical Parameters:** These govern the mechanical/structural integrity.
> - *Load (N):* The applied force on a structural member; beyond a threshold, failure occurs.
> - *Pressure (Pa):* In a pressure vessel, MAWP (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure) is the critical controlling parameter.
>
> **2. Environmental Parameters:** These define the ambient conditions affecting safety.
> - *Temperature (°C):* Controls material degradation rate; ASME BPVC specifies design temperature margins.
> - *Humidity (%):* Affects electrical insulation resistance; IEC 60068 mandates 95% RH testing.
>
> **3. Human Parameters:** These relate to the human-machine interface.
> - *Reaction Time (s):* ISO 13855 specifies 0.7s as average human reaction for emergency stops.
> - *Training Level (qualitative):* Higher training reduces Human Error Probability (HEP) per Swain's model.
>
> **4. Operational Parameters:** These define the usage pattern.
> - *Duty Cycle (%):* The fraction of time a machine operates; impacts thermal accumulation.
> - *Speed (rpm or m/s):* In a CNC machine, spindle speed affects kinetic energy and guarding requirements.
>
> **Conclusion:** A robust safety design must identify and control all four categories; ignoring any one leads to residual risk.

*(7 marks: 1 for introduction, 4 for category-wise elaboration with examples, 1 for interrelation, 1 for conclusion)*

#### (b) A lifting hook used in a crane is designed to lift a maximum load of 5 tonnes. The hook material has an ultimate tensile strength of 520 MPa. If the cross-sectional area at the critical section is 800 mm², calculate: (i) the working stress, (ii) the Safety Factor, and (iii) the Safety Margin. Comment on whether the design is safe by KTU/IS standards. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Maximum load $F = 5 \text{ tonnes} = 5000 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 = 49{,}050 \text{ N}$
- Ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{ultimate} = 520 \text{ MPa} = 520 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- Cross-sectional area $A = 800 \text{ mm}^2$

**(i) Working Stress:**

$$\sigma_{working} = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{49{,}050}{800} = 61.31 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 61.31 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Safety Factor:**

$$SF = \frac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{\sigma_{working}} = \frac{520}{61.31} = 8.48$$

**(iii) Safety Margin:**

$$SM = \frac{\sigma_{ultimate} - \sigma_{working}}{\sigma_{working}} \times 100\%$$

$$SM = \frac{520 - 61.31}{61.31} \times 100\% = 748.2\%$$

**Comment on Safety:**

> As per IS 800 and standard crane design codes (e.g., IS 3177), a minimum Safety Factor of **5.0** is required for lifting hooks under static loading, and **7.0 to 10.0** for dynamic/shock loading. The computed SF = 8.48 is within the **acceptable range for general lifting** but may be **marginal for dynamic shock loads**. For safety-critical applications, the designer should: (a) account for shock factor (typically 1.5× to 2.0×), and (b) consider fatigue loading if the hook is used in cyclic service.

*(7 marks: 1 for given data, 1 for working stress, 1 for SF, 1 for SM, 1 for comment on standards, 1 for design recommendations, 1 for final conclusion)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the process of identifying and managing controlling parameters in the safety design life cycle. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

> The systematic identification and management of controlling parameters follows a structured life-cycle approach:
>
> **Stage 1 — Hazard Identification:** Use techniques like HAZOP, FMEA, or What-If analysis to identify potential hazards in the system.
>
> **Stage 2 — Parameter Listing:** For each identified hazard, list all controlling parameters (physical, environmental, human, operational).
>
> **Stage 3 — Quantification:** Assign measurable values to each parameter (e.g., SF, temperature limits, reaction time).
>
> **Stage 4 — Limit Determination:** Establish the failure threshold for each parameter (e.g., $\sigma_{yield}$, $T_{max}$, $V_{max}$).
>
> **Stage 5 — Margin Calculation:** Compute Safety Margin for each parameter using:
>
> $$SM_i = \frac{P_{limit,i} - P_{operating,i}}{P_{operating,i}} \times 100\%$$
>
> **Stage 6 — Risk Scoring:** Compute risk score $R = P \times S$ for each hazard.
>
> **Stage 7 — Control Implementation:** Apply the *hierarchy of controls*: Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative → PPE.
>
> **Stage 8 — Verification and Validation:** Test the design against specified controlling parameter limits.
>
> **Stage 9 — Documentation and Review:** Document all parameters in the Safety Design File (SDF).
>
> **Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the complete Mermaid flow diagram of this process.)*

*(7 marks: 1 per major stage × 6 stages + 1 for diagram explanation)*

#### (b) A chemical reactor operates at 200°C with a maximum allowable temperature of 280°C. The pressure relief valve is set at 8 bar, and the design pressure is 5 bar. Calculate: (i) the Safety Margin for temperature, (ii) the Safety Margin for pressure, and (iii) determine if the design meets the typical industry standard of 25% minimum margin for process safety. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Operating temperature $T_{op} = 200°C$
- Maximum allowable temperature $T_{limit} = 280°C$
- Operating pressure $P_{op} = 5 \text{ bar}$
- Relief valve set pressure $P_{limit} = 8 \text{ bar}$
- Industry minimum margin = **25%**

**(i) Safety Margin for Temperature:**

$$SM_T = \frac{T_{limit} - T_{op}}{T_{op}} \times 100\%$$

$$SM_T = \frac{280 - 200}{200} \times 100\% = 40\%$$

**(ii) Safety Margin for Pressure:**

$$SM_P = \frac{P_{limit} - P_{op}}{P_{op}} \times 100\%$$

$$SM_P = \frac{8 - 5}{5} \times 100\% = 60\%$$

**(iii) Compliance Check:**

> - Temperature margin = 40% > 25% ✓ **PASSES**
> - Pressure margin = 60% > 25% ✓ **PASSES**
>
> **Conclusion:** The design satisfies the 25% minimum margin criterion for both controlling parameters. However, the designer should also verify:
> - That the **relief valve is correctly sized** for the full relieving capacity (API 520/521).
> - That **secondary containment** is provided per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM standard).
> - That **instrumentation and alarms** trigger at 80% of the limit values (i.e., 224°C and 6.4 bar).
>
> Both primary controlling parameters (T and P) are within safe design envelopes.

*(7 marks: 1 for temperature SM, 1 for pressure SM, 1 for comparison with standard, 1 for compliance conclusion, 1 each for additional verification points, 1 for final summary)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Most students lose 1–2 marks by mixing units (e.g., using MPa and N/mm² interchangeably without conversion, or forgetting to convert tonnes to Newtons via $g = 9.81$).
> 2. **Forgetting the "Comment" Section:** In 7-mark problems, the final *comment or recommendation* carries 1–2 marks. Students who stop at the numerical answer lose these.
> 3. **Confusing SF and SM:** Writing "Safety Margin = 8.48%" when they actually calculated the Safety Factor (which is dimensionless) is a very common error. Always recheck the formula before writing the final number.
> 4. **Ignoring Dynamic/Shock Loading:** In mechanical design problems, neglecting the shock factor is a frequent pitfall. Always state whether the load is *static* or *dynamic* and apply the appropriate SF.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Controlling Parameters**

- [x] **Definition:** Controlling Parameters = measurable variables governing safety performance.
- [x] **Categories:** Physical, Environmental, Human, Operational, System.
- [x] **Safety Factor:** $SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{\sigma_{working}}$ (dimensionless, ratio).
- [x] **Safety Margin:** $SM = \dfrac{P_{limit} - P_{operating}}{P_{operating}} \times 100\%$ (percentage).
- [x] **Hierarchy:** Primary (critical) > Secondary (contributing) > Tertiary (contextual).
- [x] **Risk Score:** $R = P \times S$, range 1–25 using 5×5 matrix.
- [x] **MTBF:** $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ (inverse of failure rate).
- [x] **Standard Minimum SF:** 1.5 (static), 4.0 (dynamic/shock).
- [x] **Standard Minimum SM:** 25% (process industry), 1.5× (mechanical).
- [x] **Key Standards:** ISO 12100, IEC 61508, ISO 13849, IS 800, ASME BPVC.
- [x] **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- [x] **Parameter Interaction:** Parameters are NOT independent — they cascade and amplify.
- [x] **Always include units** in the final answer — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.
- [x] **Always add a "Comment" or "Conclusion"** in numerical problems — it carries 1–2 marks.
- [x] **Consider dynamic/shock factors** in mechanical design — static analysis alone is insufficient.

> **End of Module 3 — Controlling Parameters Notes**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design for Safety (DfS) Applications** refer to the systematic integration of safety engineering principles, ergonomic considerations, human-factors analysis, and risk-reduction methodologies into the lifecycle of products, processes, plants, and engineered systems. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, applications translate theoretical safety models into engineered solutions that prevent occupational injuries, ill-health, and asset losses.

The term **"Applications"** in Module 3 consolidates the practical implementation of safety design tools such as **Hazard Identification (HAZID)**, **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**, **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)**, **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**, **Inherent Safer Design (ISD)**, **Hierarchy of Controls (HoC)**, and **Ergonomic/Human Factors Engineering** across diverse industrial sectors.

The principal engineering objective is governed by the **ALARP principle (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)** and the **Tolerable Risk** framework.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 emphasizes that every design decision must be justified using quantifiable risk metrics, the **Gutenberg Richter** analogy of risk reduction, and verification through standard codes like **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511**, **ISO 12100**, **OSHA 29 CFR 1910**, and **ILO-OSH 2001**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine designing a household pressure cooker:
- A **poorly designed** cooker has a single pressure release valve (single point of failure).
- A **safety-designed** cooker has: a spring-loaded primary valve, a backup gasket that softens under heat, a mechanical lock preventing opening under pressure, and a thermal fuse.

This is exactly what **"Applications of Design for Safety"** means — building **layers of engineered protection** into every artifact so that no single mistake, component failure, or human error results in harm.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Think of Design for Safety applications as the **"seatbelt + airbag + anti-lock braking + crumple zones + lane assist"** philosophy of a car. Each layer is an application of a specific safety principle working in concert.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following quantitative metrics are central to all safety design applications:

- **Fatal Accident Rate (FAR):** $\text{FAR} = \frac{\text{Number of Fatalities} \times 10^8}{\text{Total hours worked}}$
- **Lost Time Injury (LTI) Rate:** $\text{LTIR} = \frac{\text{Lost Time Injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Total hours worked}}$
- **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD):** Dimensionless probability, range $0 < \text{PFD} \leq 1$
- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL):** Discrete levels **1, 2, 3, 4** (per IEC 61508)
- **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF):** $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{1 - \text{PFD}}$ for PFD based systems
- **Acceleration of gravity:** $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$ (used in fall arrest and dynamic load calculations)
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \ \text{kPa}$ (used in pressure relief sizing)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Design-for-Safety Applications

The applications of Design for Safety are systematically grouped into six major engineering domains:

### A. Process Industry Applications
Applications in chemical, petrochemical, oil & gas, and pharmaceutical sectors where the design must prevent fires, explosions, and toxic releases. The central methodology is the **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** combined with **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)**.

### B. Construction & Civil Engineering Applications
Applications addressing fall protection, structural integrity, excavation safety, and crane/lift operations. Governed by **OSHA 1926** standards and Indian **National Building Code (NBC 2016)**.

### C. Manufacturing & Mechanical Applications
Applications covering machine guarding, lockout-tagout (LOTO), and robotic cell safety. Anchored by **ISO 12100** risk assessment and **EN 60204-1** electrical safety.

### D. Product & Consumer Goods Applications
Applications extending safety design into household appliances, automotive vehicles (per **ISO 26262**), and electronics (per **IEC 60950 / IEC 62368**).

### E. Ergonomic & Human Factors Applications
Applications of anthropometry, biomechanics, and cognitive ergonomics in workstation design, control panel layout, and human-machine interface (HMI) design.

### F. Transportation & Aerospace Applications
Applications of redundancy, fail-safe design, and probabilistic safety assessment (PSA) in aviation, rail, and marine domains.

## 2.2 The Hierarchy of Controls (HoC) — The Backbone of All Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Hierarchy of Controls is **THE** most frequently tested application in KTU examinations. Memorize the descending order of effectiveness.

The Hierarchy of Controls applied in every DfS context is (from **most effective to least effective**):

1. **Elimination** — Physically remove the hazard.
2. **Substitution** — Replace with a less hazardous material/process.
3. **Engineering Controls** — Guards, interlocks, ventilation.
4. **Administrative Controls** — Training, procedures, signage.
5. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — Last line of defense.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Many students mistakenly place PPE at the top. Always draw the **inverted triangle** with Elimination at the apex and PPE at the base.

## 2.3 Inherently Safer Design (ISD) — The Four Core Strategies

The applications of ISD, originally articulated by **Trevor Kletz**, are governed by four minimization/substitution principles:

- **Minimize** — Use smaller inventories of hazardous materials.
- **Substitute** — Replace hazardous with less hazardous substances.
- **Moderate** — Use less hazardous process conditions (lower T, P).
- **Simplify** — Design plants that are forgiving of operational errors.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units | Application Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fatal Accident Rate | $\text{FAR} = \dfrac{N_f \times 10^8}{H}$ | dimensionless | Process, Construction |
| 2 | LTI Rate | $\text{LTIR} = \dfrac{N_{LTI} \times 10^6}{H}$ | dimensionless | Manufacturing, Construction |
| 3 | Risk | $R = f \times C$ | mixed units | All domains |
| 4 | Risk Reduction Factor | $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{1 - \text{PFD}}$ | dimensionless | SIL Assignment |
| 5 | SIL PFD Range (1) | $10^{-2} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$ | probability | IEC 61508 |
| 6 | SIL PFD Range (2) | $10^{-3} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-2}$ | probability | IEC 61508 |
| 7 | SIL PFD Range (3) | $10^{-4} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$ | probability | IEC 61508 |
| 8 | SIL PFD Range (4) | $10^{-5} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-4}$ | probability | IEC 61508 |
| 9 | Fall Arrest Force | $F = m \times g$ | N | Construction |
| 10 | Stopping Distance | $d = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \mu g}$ | m | Machine Safety, Vehicle |
| 11 | Pressure Relief Orifice | $A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K \cdot P}$ | mm² | Process Safety |
| 12 | Tripod Stability | $\sum M_{\text{overturning}} \leq \sum M_{\text{restoring}}$ | N·m | Crane/Equipment |
| 13 | Reach Distance (Anthropometry) | $D_{reach} = 0.6 \times \text{Stature}$ | m | Ergonomics |
| 14 | Noise Dose | $D = \dfrac{C_1}{T_1} + \dfrac{C_2}{T_2} + \cdots$ | % | Industrial Hygiene |
| 15 | Ventilation Airflow | $Q = \dfrac{V}{t}$ | m³/s | Hazardous Atmospheres |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU:** The acronym **EMMS** = **E**liminate, **M**odify (Substitute + Engineering), **M**anage (Admin), **S**hield (PPE). Alternatively, **ESSEMAP** = E, S, S, E, M, A, P.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The applications of DfS underpin:
- **OSHA VPP (Voluntary Protection Programs)** — Star-rated worksites.
- **PSM (Process Safety Management)** under **29 CFR 1910.119** — Mandatory for highly hazardous chemicals.
- **SEVESO III Directive (EU)** — Industrial accident hazard control.
- **Indian Factories Act 1948, Section 6 & 7** — Fencing of machinery and worker safety.
- **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)** — The defining case study that made ISD and LOPA standard practice.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Safety Integrity Level (SIL) Determination from Risk Graph

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the most critical KTU computation question for Module 3. The Risk Graph method, codified in **IEC 61511**, determines the required SIL of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF).

**Step 1: Define the Unmitigated Risk Frequency ($f$)**
The unmitigated risk is the frequency of the hazardous event without any protection layer:
$$f_{u} = \text{Event frequency (per year)}$$

**Step 2: Modify Risk Using the Consequence Parameter ($C$)**
The consequence is classified into levels with assigned multipliers:

| Consequence | Description | Multiplier ($C_i$) |
|---|---|---|
| $C_A$ | Minor injury | 1 |
| $C_B$ | Serious injury (1 fatality) | 5 |
| $C_C$ | Multiple fatalities | 10 |
| $C_D$ | Catastrophic (mass fatality) | 50 |

**Step 3: Apply Occupancy Factor ($F$)** — number of people exposed
$$F = 1 \text{ to } 5$$

**Step 4: Apply Probability of Avoidance ($P$)** — operator intervention success rate
$$P = 0.1 \text{ to } 10$$

**Step 5: Demand Rate ($W$)** — frequency of exposure to the hazard
$$W = 0.1 \text{ to } 100 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 6: Compute Mitigated Event Frequency**
$$f_{m} = f_{u} \cdot C \cdot F \cdot P \cdot W \cdot \text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}}$$

**Step 7: Solve for $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}}$ to Achieve Tolerable Risk**
$$\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} \leq \dfrac{f_{\text{tolerable}}}{f_{u} \cdot C \cdot F \cdot P \cdot W}$$

**Step 8: Map the Calculated PFD to the SIL Table**
Use the standard IEC 61508 mapping:

| Calculated $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}}$ | SIL |
|---|---|
| $\geq 10^{-1}$ to $< 1$ | No SIL required |
| $\geq 10^{-2}$ to $< 10^{-1}$ | SIL 1 |
| $\geq 10^{-3}$ to $< 10^{-2}$ | SIL 2 |
| $\geq 10^{-4}$ to $< 10^{-3}$ | SIL 3 |
| $\geq 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$ | SIL 4 |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fall Arrest Force Computation for Construction Applications

This derivation is essential for **construction DfS applications** (scaffolding, high-rise work).

**Step 1: Define the falling mass including tools**
$$m = m_{\text{worker}} + m_{\text{tools}} \quad \text{(kg)}$$

**Step 2: Compute the free-fall energy at arrest**
The worker free-falls a distance $h$ before the lanyard engages:
$$E_{\text{kinetic}} = m g h \quad \text{(J)}$$

**Step 3: Apply the deceleration distance of the lanyard/energy absorber**
The energy absorber deforms by $d$ during arrest. The average arresting force is:
$$F_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{m g h}{d} + m g$$

**Step 4: Verify against the limit of $\mathbf{4 \ \text{kN}}$ (OSHA/EN 355 limit)**
The maximum arresting force must satisfy:
$$F_{\text{avg}} \leq 4000 \ \text{N}$$

**Step 5: Solve for the minimum energy absorber deformation $d$**
$$d \geq \dfrac{m g h}{F_{\text{limit}} - m g}$$

**Step 6: Worked Numerical Example**
Given: $m = 90$ kg (worker + tools), $h = 4$ m free-fall, $F_{\text{limit}} = 4000$ N.

$$d \geq \dfrac{90 \times 9.81 \times 4}{4000 - (90 \times 9.81)}$$

$$d \geq \dfrac{3531.6}{4000 - 882.9} = \dfrac{3531.6}{3117.1}$$

$$d \geq 1.133 \ \text{m}$$

**Conclusion:** The energy absorber must deform by at least **1.13 m** to keep the arresting force below the 4 kN limit.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Machine Safety Stopping Distance (ISO 13855)

**Step 1: Define inputs**
- $T_s$ = Total stopping time of the machine (s)
- $T_r$ = Response time of the protective device (s)
- $T_c$ = Machine reaction time (s)
- $v$ = Approach speed of the operator/limb (m/s)
- $K$ = Safety factor margin (typically 1.0 m or based on penetration depth)

**Step 2: Compute the minimum safety distance $S$**
$$S = v \cdot (T_s + T_r) + K$$

**Step 3: Substitute $T_s = T_r + T_c$**
$$S = v \cdot (T_c + 2 T_r) + K$$

**Step 4: Worked Example**
A press has $T_c = 0.1$ s, light curtain response $T_r = 0.02$ s, and operator approach speed $v = 1.6$ m/s. Safety margin $K = 0.1$ m.

$$S = 1.6 \times (0.1 + 2 \times 0.02) + 0.1$$

$$S = 1.6 \times 0.14 + 0.1 = 0.224 + 0.1 = 0.324 \ \text{m}$$

**Conclusion:** The light curtain must be placed at least **324 mm** from the hazard point.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hazard Control Method Selection

| Hazard Type | Best Application Method | Engineering Tool | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Toxic gas release | Inherently Safer Design | Minimize inventory | Kletz ISD |
| Fall from height | Engineering control | Guardrails, safety nets | OSHA 1926.501 |
| Rotating shaft entanglement | Engineering control | Fixed/permanent guard | ISO 12100 |
| Confined space | Administrative + PPE | Permit-to-work, SCBA | OSHA 1910.146 |
| Runaway reaction | Engineering control | BPCS + SIS layers | IEC 61511 LOPA |
| Noise exposure | Engineering control | Acoustic enclosure | ISO 1996 / NIOSH |
| Human error in HMI | Ergonomic design | Anthropometry, alarms | NUREG-0700 |
| Vehicle collision | Substitution + Engineering | Speed limiters, ABS | ISO 26262 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Application Architecture: Design for Safety Engineering Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Initiation and Concept Stage] --> B[Hazard Identification HAZID]
    B --> C[Preliminary Hazard Analysis PHA]
    C --> D[Risk Assessment using Risk Matrix]
    D --> E{Risk Tolerable?}
    E -->|No| F[Apply Hierarchy of Controls Elimination First]
    F --> G[Inherently Safer Design ISD Implementation]
    G --> D
    E -->|Yes| H[Detailed Design with Safety Standards]
    H --> I[SIL Determination via LOPA]
    I --> J[Engineering Control Selection ISO 12100]
    J --> K[Ergonomic and Human Factors Integration]
    K --> L[Verification and Validation Testing]
    L --> M[Pre Startup Safety Review PSSR]
    M --> N[Operations and Maintenance]
    N --> O[Change Management and MOC]
    O --> P[Incident Investigation and Feedback]
    P --> A
```

## 4.2 Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Process Design Layer IPL 1] --> L1[Basic Process Control System BPCS IPL 2]
    L1 --> L2[Operator Supervision IPL 3]
    L2 --> L3[Safety Instrumented System SIS IPL 4]
    L3 --> L4[Physical Protection Relief Valves IPL 5]
    L4 --> L5[Plant Emergency Response IPL 6]
    L5 --> L6[Community Emergency Response IPL 7]
    L6 --> OUT[Final Mitigated Risk Output]
```

```mermaid
    end

    C1[Consequence Slight] --> R1

```

## 4.4 Human-Machine Interface (HMI) Application Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[Operator] -->|Input Action| CP[Control Panel Designed per Anthropometry]
    CP -->|Signal| PLC[Programmable Logic Controller]

    PLC -->|Trip Signal| SIS[Safety Instrumented System]

    EV -->|Process Isolation| P[Process Plant]

```

```mermaid
    S1[Minimize Use Smaller Inventories of Hazardous Materials] --> OUT[Inherently Safer Plant]
    S2[Substitute Replace with Less Hazardous Substances] --> OUT
    S3[Moderate Use Less Hazardous Conditions Lower T and P] --> OUT
    S4[Simplify Design Forgiving of Operator Errors] --> OUT
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q: List any THREE applications of Inherently Safer Design (ISD) with one industrial example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | ISD Strategy | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Minimize** | Reducing inventory of methyl isocyanate (MIC) at the Bhopal plant from 90 tonnes to < 1 tonne would have prevented the 1984 disaster. |
| 2 | **Substitute** | Replacing solvent-based paints with water-based paints in automotive shops to eliminate VOC exposure. |
| 3 | **Moderate** | Operating a chlor-alkali process at atmospheric pressure and lower temperature instead of high pressure/temperature. |
| 4 | **Simplify** | Designing reactor cooling systems with gravity-driven flow instead of pump-dependent flow to eliminate pump failure as a failure mode. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** 1 mark per correct ISD principle + 1 mark for example. Total 3.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q: Differentiate between the PFD values of SIL 1 and SIL 3 with respect to their application in process industries.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SIL 1 | SIL 3 |
|---|---|---|
| PFD Range | $10^{-2} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$ | $10^{-4} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$ |
| Risk Reduction Factor | 10 to 100 | 1000 to 10000 |
| Application | Low hazard processes (e.g., fuel storage alarm) | High hazard processes (e.g., nuclear reactor shutdown, oil refinery furnace) |
| Cost Implication | Low | Very High (triple modular redundancy) |

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks (Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]**

**Q: (a) With a neat schematic, explain the Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) methodology as an application of Design for Safety in a process plant. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** is a semi-quantitative methodology used to evaluate the effectiveness of independent protection layers (IPLs) in reducing the frequency or consequence of a hazardous event.

**Schematic (refer to Section 4.2 above):** LOPA has **7 layers** — Process Design → BPCS → Operator → SIS → Physical Protection → Plant Emergency → Community Emergency.

**Step-by-step procedure:**

1. **Identify the Scenario** — Use HAZID/HAZOP team outputs.
2. **Determine the Initiating Event Frequency** $f_{IE}$ (e.g., pump failure: $10^{-1}$/yr).
3. **Identify IPLs** and assign their PFDs:

| Layer | Typical PFD |
|---|---|
| Process Design | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| BPCS | $10^{-1}$ |
| Operator | $10^{-1}$ to 1 |
| SIS (SIL 1) | $10^{-1}$ |
| Relief Valve | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| Bund/Dike | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ |

4. **Compute Mitigated Event Frequency:**
$$f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{IE} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_{i}$$

5. **Compare with Tolerable Frequency** — typically $10^{-6}$/yr for the public.

6. **Decision** — If $f_{\text{mitigated}} > f_{\text{tolerable}}$, add another IPL (usually upgrade SIS to SIL 2/3).

**Real-world application:** Bhopal (1984) — All 7 layers failed because of inadequate IPL design; LOPA would have flagged the absence of an SIS layer for MIC relief.

---

**(b) A reactor operates with a hazardous chemical release frequency of 0.1 per year. Three independent protection layers exist with PFDs of 0.1, 0.01, and 0.05. The tolerable frequency is $10^{-5}$/year. Determine whether additional safety measures are required. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Initiating Event Frequency: $f_{IE} = 0.1 \text{ per year}$
- $\text{PFD}_1 = 0.1$
- $\text{PFD}_2 = 0.01$
- $\text{PFD}_3 = 0.05$
- $f_{\text{tolerable}} = 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$

**Step 1: Compute the combined PFD of all layers**
$$\text{PFD}_{\text{total}} = \text{PFD}_1 \times \text{PFD}_2 \times \text{PFD}_3$$

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{total}} = 0.1 \times 0.01 \times 0.05$$

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{total}} = 5 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 2: Compute the mitigated event frequency**
$$f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{IE} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{total}}$$

$$f_{\text{mitigated}} = 0.1 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$f_{\text{mitigated}} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per year}$$

**Step 3: Compare with tolerable frequency**
$$f_{\text{mitigated}} = 5 \times 10^{-6} > f_{\text{tolerable}} = 10^{-5}? \text{ NO, it is LESS.}$$

Wait — verifying: $5 \times 10^{-6} = 0.5 \times 10^{-5}$, which is **less than** $10^{-5}$. So the mitigated frequency is **lower** than tolerable.

**Step 4: Decision**
Since $f_{\text{mitigated}} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per year} < f_{\text{tolerable}} = 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$, the design **meets the safety requirement** and **no additional safety measures are required**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently misjudge the comparison direction. Remember: the requirement is $f_{\text{mitigated}} \leq f_{\text{tolerable}}$. The smaller the mitigated frequency, the safer the system.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]**

**Q: (a) Explain the Hierarchy of Controls (HoC) and discuss its application in the design of an automotive manufacturing robot cell. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Hierarchy of Controls (HoC)** is a systematic framework used in safety engineering to select the most effective hazard control measures. It ranks interventions from most effective to least effective:

1. **Elimination** (most effective) — Remove the hazard entirely.
2. **Substitution** — Replace with a less hazardous alternative.
3. **Engineering Controls** — Physical guards, interlocks, light curtains.
4. **Administrative Controls** — Training, signage, procedures.
5. **PPE** (least effective) — Last line of defense.

**Application to an Automotive Robot Cell:**

| Control Level | Robot Cell Application |
|---|---|
| Elimination | Design the process to remove manual loading; use automated conveyors throughout. |
| Substitution | Replace heavy steel panels with light composites, reducing pinch-point forces. |
| Engineering | Install **physical perimeter fencing** with interlocked gates; **light curtains** at operator access; **emergency stop** buttons (Cat 1 / Cat 0 stops per EN 60204-1); **safety PLC** with dual-channel monitoring. |
| Administrative | Conduct lockout-tagout (LOTO) during maintenance; use **two-person verification** for cell entry. |
| PPE | Mandate **safety glasses, cut-resistant gloves, hearing protection**. |

**Justification (KTU):** Engineering controls MUST be exhausted before PPE because PPE depends on human compliance and can fail.

---

**(b) Calculate the safety distance $S$ for a hydraulic press light curtain system. The operator approach speed is 1.5 m/s, machine stopping time is 0.3 s, light curtain response time is 0.02 s, and penetration depth $K = 0.1$ m. Verify whether a 0.5 m placement is acceptable. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Approach speed $v = 1.5$ m/s
- Machine stopping time $T_c = 0.3$ s
- Light curtain response time $T_r = 0.02$ s
- Penetration depth $K = 0.1$ m
- Proposed placement $S_{\text{proposed}} = 0.5$ m

**Step 1: Apply the ISO 13855 safety distance formula**
$$S = v \times (T_c + T_r) + K$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**
$$S = 1.5 \times (0.3 + 0.02) + 0.1$$

**Step 3: Compute**
$$S = 1.5 \times 0.32 + 0.1$$

$$S = 0.48 + 0.1 = 0.58 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 4: Compare with proposed placement**
$$S_{\text{required}} = 0.58 \ \text{m} \quad \text{vs} \quad S_{\text{proposed}} = 0.50 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 5: Decision**
Since $S_{\text{proposed}} = 0.50 \ \text{m} < S_{\text{required}} = 0.58 \ \text{m}$, the proposed placement is **NOT acceptable**.

**Step 6: Recommendation**
The light curtain must be repositioned to at least **0.58 m** from the hazard, OR the machine stopping time must be reduced to:
$$T_c = \dfrac{S_{\text{proposed}} - K}{v} - T_r = \dfrac{0.50 - 0.1}{1.5} - 0.02 = 0.21 \ \text{s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget to ADD the penetration depth $K$ or use $T_s$ alone. Always use the ISO 13855 formula structure exactly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Applications**

- [ ] **ISD Four Pillars:** Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify (Kletz, 1978).
- [ ] **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE (inverted triangle, PPE at the bottom).
- [ ] **LOPA Layers:** Process Design (IPL 1) → BPCS (IPL 2) → Operator (IPL 3) → SIS (IPL 4) → Physical Protection (IPL 5) → Plant Emergency (IPL 6) → Community Emergency (IPL 7).
- [ ] **SIL ↔ PFD Mapping:** SIL 1 = $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 2 = $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 3 = $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$; SIL 4 = $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$.
- [ ] **ALARP Principle:** Risk must be reduced to "As Low As Reasonably Practicable" — economic, social, and technical factors considered.
- [ ] **Risk Equation:** $R = f \times C$ (frequency × consequence).
- [ ] **Safety Distance (ISO 13855):** $S = v \times (T_c + T_r) + K$.
- [ ] **Fall Arrest Force Limit:** $F_{\text{max}} = 4 \ \text{kN}$ (with energy absorber).
- [ ] **Key Standards to Memorize:** ISO 12100, ISO 13849, ISO 13855, IEC 61508, IEC 61511, OSHA 29 CFR 1910 & 1926, NBC 2016.
- [ ] **FAR Formula:** $\text{FAR} = (N_f \times 10^8)/H$ where $H$ = total man-hours.
- [ ] **LOPA Numerical:** Mitigated Frequency $f_m = f_{IE} \times \prod \text{PFD}_i$ — must be $\leq f_{\text{tolerable}}$.
- [ ] **Case Study:** Always mention **Bhopal 1984** for any process safety application question.
- [ ] **Ergonomic Reach Rule:** $D_{\text{reach}} = 0.6 \times \text{Stature}$ (approximately).
- [ ] **Permit-to-Work Systems:** Hot Work, Confined Space, Working at Height, Electrical Isolation.
- [ ] **Human Factors Hierarchy:** Displays → Controls → Environment → Procedures → Training.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** In any 14-mark question, allocate 3 minutes for a labeled diagram (e.g., LOPA layers, HoC pyramid, ISD schematic). Examiners award **2 marks minimum** for a well-drawn diagram even if the descriptive portion is incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Advantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PESFT412 – Design for Safety (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3)**, the term **"Advantages"** refers to the structured set of engineering, economic, operational, environmental, and socio-legal benefits obtained when safety is treated as a **primary design parameter** rather than as an add-on control measure. Specifically, it enumerates the benefits of adopting the **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** philosophy and the broader **Design for Safety (DfS)** lifecycle approach advocated by KTU under the NEP 2020 outcome-based framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Phrasing)**
> *"Advantages of Design for Safety refer to the long-term benefits — including risk reduction, lifecycle cost savings, regulatory compliance, environmental protection, and improved operational reliability — that are achieved by integrating hazard elimination and risk minimization principles at the conceptual and basic engineering design stages of any plant, process, product, or built environment."*

The advantages are formally classified under **four ISD pillars** (Kletz, 1978 — universally adopted in KTU syllabus):

1. **Minimize (Intensification)** — Use smaller inventories of hazardous materials.
2. **Substitute (Replacement)** — Replace hazardous substances with less hazardous ones.
3. **Moderate (Attenuation)** — Operate under safer process conditions (lower T, P, concentration).
4. **Simplify (Limitation of Effects)** — Design equipment and layouts that fail safely.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Belt + Airbag + Crumple Zone" Car**
>
> Imagine two car manufacturers designing a vehicle for accident safety:
> - **Manufacturer A (Reactive Safety):** Builds a normal car, then *adds* seat belts, airbags, and ABS as **retrofit accessories** after manufacture. The car is fundamentally the same — accidents still cause severe damage.
> - **Manufacturer B (Design for Safety):** Engineers the car *from scratch* with a **crumple zone**, **lower center of gravity**, **collapsible steering column**, and **side-impact beams** built **into the chassis**.
>
> **Manufacturer B's car is "inherently safer."** It absorbs crash energy by design, so the occupants are protected *even if* the airbags fail. This is the core idea of "Advantages of DfS" — safety is **built-in, not bolted-on**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Indicators (KPOs) & Standard Metrics

The advantages are quantified using these engineering metrics (highlighted in **bold** for KTU exam recall):

- **Expected Loss Reduction (ELR)** — measured in *fatalities/yr* or *₹/yr*.
- **Inherent Safety Index (ISI)** — dimensionless score (0 to 1) from the **ISI Metric (Edwards & Lawrence, 1993)**.
- **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** reduction — reduction in **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand)** values.
- **Life-Cycle Cost (LCC)** saving factor — typically **3× to 10×** the initial capital invested in safety.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Categorical Breakdown of Advantages

### A. Engineering & Technical Advantages
- **Elimination of failure modes:** Fewer components → fewer failure pathways.
- **Reduced inventory** of hazardous chemicals → smaller Hazardous Area (HA) classification zone.
- **Lower operating pressure/temperature** → thinner vessel walls → lower *capital expenditure*.
- **Improved process stability** and tighter control loops → better **product quality consistency**.

### B. Economic / Financial Advantages
- **Reduced CAPEX** on add-on safety instrumented systems (SIS), fire suppression, and PPE.
- **Reduced OPEX** on insurance premiums, inspections, and shutdowns.
- **Avoided loss** from incident-related production stoppage (typical industry loss ≈ **\$1M–\$100M per major incident**).
- **Longer asset life** due to less aggressive operating conditions.

### C. Regulatory & Legal Advantages
- **Easier compliance** with the **Factories Act 1948**, **OSHA**, **ISO 45001:2018**, and **ISO 14001:2015**.
- **Reduced litigation risk** and lower statutory penalty exposure.
- **Faster regulatory approvals** for greenfield projects (e.g., **MoEFCC Environmental Clearance** in India).

### D. Environmental & Social Advantages
- **Lower emissions**, smaller **carbon footprint**, and easier **ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance)** reporting.
- **Improved corporate reputation** and **community goodwill** — critical CSR (Corporate Social Responsibility) factor.
- **Enhanced worker morale and retention** — a documented human-factors benefit.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Use this table as the **last-page cheat sheet** when solving numerical problems on the cost-benefit analysis of DfS.

| # | Parameter / Formula | Symbol | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Expected Loss Reduction | $\Delta EL = EL_{\text{old}} - EL_{\text{new}}$ | ₹ / yr (or fatalities / yr) | Monetary or life-saving benefit per year after DfS implementation |
| 2 | Inherent Safety Index (ISI) | $ISI = \sum w_i \cdot s_i$ | dimensionless | Weighted sum of sub-indices (Chemical, Process, Inventory) |
| 3 | Probability of Failure on Demand | $PFD_{avg}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Reliability metric for a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) |
| 4 | Risk Reduction Factor | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ | dimensionless | How much risk is reduced by a protection layer |
| 5 | Life-Cycle Cost Saving | $\Delta LCC = CAPEX_{\text{saved}} + OPEX_{\text{saved}} \cdot T$ | ₹ | Total savings over project life $T$ (years) |
| 6 | Hazard Index (Fire / Explosion) | $HI = \dfrac{M \cdot H_c}{\text{Safe Threshold}}$ | dimensionless | Relative hazard magnitude of an inventory $M$ with heat of combustion $H_c$ |
| 7 | Pay-Back Period on DfS | $PB = \dfrac{C_{\text{DfS}}}{S_{\text{annual}}}$ | years | Time required for DfS investment to recover via annual savings |
| 8 | Safety Integrity Level | $SIL = -\log_{10}(PFD_{avg})$ | Levels 1–4 | KTU-mapped safety requirement level per IEC 61511 |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE (followed above):** The vertical pipe `|` symbol was replaced with `\vert` / `/` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. Use `\vert` in your own answer sheets.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These advantages are universally applied in:
- **Petrochemical & Refinery Design** (e.g., BPCL Kochi, CPCL Chennai).
- **Pharmaceutical Plant Design** (FDA-cGMP compliance).
- **Aerospace System Safety** (DO-178C, MIL-STD-882E).
- **Civil & Structural Engineering** (earthquake-resistant design per **IS 1893**).
- **Automotive Engineering** (Euro NCAP / GSR AIS-100 crash safety).
- **Nuclear Safety Design** (defence-in-depth per **AERB** guidelines).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantification of Expected Loss Reduction (ELR)

The **Expected Loss (EL)** before and after Design for Safety is defined as:

$$
EL = f \times C \times PFD_{avg}
$$

where:
- $f$ = Frequency of the hazardous event (events / year).
- $C$ = Consequence cost per event (₹ or fatalities).
- $PFD_{avg}$ = Average Probability of Failure on Demand of the protection layer.

### Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Identify the baseline (As-Is) Expected Loss.**

$$
EL_{\text{old}} = f_{\text{old}} \times C_{\text{old}} \times PFD_{old}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the post-DfS (To-Be) Expected Loss.**

After DfS, both the *frequency* and the *consequence* reduce, while *additional inherent safety layers* lower the effective $PFD$:

$$
EL_{\text{new}} = f_{\text{new}} \times C_{\text{new}} \times PFD_{new}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Expected Loss Reduction.**

$$
\Delta EL = EL_{\text{old}} - EL_{\text{new}}
$$

Substituting the two expressions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta EL &= (f_{\text{old}} \times C_{\text{old}} \times PFD_{old}) \; - \; (f_{\text{new}} \times C_{\text{new}} \times PFD_{new})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express as a fractional benefit ratio (B).**

$$
B = \dfrac{\Delta EL}{EL_{\text{old}}} = 1 - \dfrac{f_{\text{new}} \cdot C_{\text{new}} \cdot PFD_{new}}{f_{\text{old}} \cdot C_{\text{old}} \cdot PFD_{old}}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert benefit to monetary Pay-Back Period.**

$$
PB = \dfrac{C_{\text{DfS}}}{B \cdot EL_{\text{old}}}
$$

where $C_{\text{DfS}}$ is the upfront capital cost of the DfS intervention.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** If $B \to 1$, the DfS intervention eliminates virtually 100% of the expected loss — the **maximum inherent safety benefit** is achieved.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inherent Safety Index (ISI) Sub-Index Computation

The ISI metric (Edwards & Lawrence, 1993) breaks each advantage into a numerical sub-score. For the **Inventory Sub-Index ($ISI_{inv}$)**:

$$
ISI_{inv} = \dfrac{\log_{10}(M_{safe})}{\log_{10}(M_{actual})}
$$

where:
- $M_{safe}$ = Maximum permissible inventory (kg) per safe-handling threshold.
- $M_{actual}$ = Inventory currently held in the process.

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1 — Compute the safe inventory limit from regulatory tables** (e.g., **OSHA PEL**, **TCEQ**, or **Indian CPCB limits**):

$$
M_{safe} = \rho \cdot V_{safe}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the actual inventory of the unit operation:**

$$
M_{actual} = \rho \cdot V_{actual}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the logarithmic ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ISI_{inv} &= \dfrac{\log_{10}(\rho \cdot V_{safe})}{\log_{10}(\rho \cdot V_{actual})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine with weighted sub-indices** (Process, Chemical, Layout):

$$
ISI_{total} = w_1 \cdot ISI_{inv} + w_2 \cdot ISI_{proc} + w_3 \cdot ISI_{chem} + w_4 \cdot ISI_{layout}
$$

with the constraint:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i = 1
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When $ISI_{total} \geq 1$, the design is considered **inherently safer than the reference baseline** — this is the numerical advantage students are expected to compute.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for Advantages

| Advantage Category | Quantifiable Metric | KTU-Mapped Bloom's Level | Typical 3-mark Question |
|---|---|---|---|
| Risk Reduction | $\Delta EL$ | Apply (Level 3) | Compute ELR for a sample plant |
| Cost Saving | $\Delta LCC$ | Apply (Level 3) | Calculate 5-year payback |
| Reliability | $SIL$ | Analyze (Level 4) | Justify SIL upgrade |
| Compliance | Penalty avoided (₹) | Understand (Level 2) | List applicable Indian standards |
| Environmental | $CO_2$ tonnes avoided | Evaluate (Level 5) | ESG trade-off discussion |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Pillars of Design for Safety Advantages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DESIGN FOR SAFETY"] --> B["Minimize Inventory"]

    D --> K["ADV 6: Lower Energy Demand"]
    E --> L["ADV 7: Fewer Failure Modes"]
    E --> M["ADV 8: Safer Maintenance Access"]

    F --> N["INHERENTLY SAFER PLANT"]

    H --> N

    K --> N

    N --> O["QUANTIFIED OUTCOMES"]
```
