<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: List any three advantages of incorporating safety at the design stage of a chemical plant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Risk Reduction at Source:** Hazards are eliminated or minimized inherently, reducing the probability and severity of accidents (1 mark).
2. **Cost-Effectiveness:** Retrofitting safety systems later costs 3–10× more than incorporating them at design (1 mark).
3. **Regulatory & Operational Ease:** Compliance with ISO 45001, OSHA, and the Factories Act 1948 is simpler and operational reliability improves (1 mark).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between "Inherent Safety" and "Add-on Safety" with one advantage each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Parameter | Inherent Safety (Built-in) | Add-on Safety (Retrofit) |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | Eliminates hazard at design source | Controls hazard via external devices |
| Advantage | **Permanent, failsafe, low lifecycle cost** | **Quick to implement on existing plant** |

(1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for accurate advantage pair.)

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`
**(a)** Explain the **four pillars of Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** as proposed by Kletz. List **two engineering advantages** and **two economic advantages** of adopting ISD in a refinery. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A petrochemical storage unit stores **chlorine** in a bullet tank. The baseline data is:

- $f_{\text{old}} = 1 \times 10^{-2}$ events/yr
- $C_{\text{old}} = \text{₹ } 5 \times 10^{7}$ per event
- $PFD_{old} = 1 \times 10^{-1}$

After DfS modification (inventory reduction + passive mitigation), the values become:
- $f_{\text{new}} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ events/yr
- $C_{\text{new}} = \text{₹ } 1 \times 10^{7}$ per event
- $PFD_{new} = 1 \times 10^{-2}$

The DfS investment cost is **₹ 2 Crore**. Calculate the **(i) Expected Loss Reduction**, **(ii) Benefit Ratio**, and **(iii) Pay-Back Period** assuming full annual realisation. **(7 marks)**

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Sample, Module 3]`
**(a)** Discuss, with suitable examples, how the **"Substitute"** and **"Moderate"** strategies of ISD provide advantages in pharmaceutical process design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An Inherent Safety Index computation for a process gives:

- $ISI_{inv} = 1.20$, $ISI_{proc} = 1.05$, $ISI_{chem} = 0.95$, $ISI_{layout} = 1.10$
- Weights: $w_1 = 0.40$, $w_2 = 0.25$, $w_3 = 0.20$, $w_4 = 0.15$

Determine $ISI_{total}$ and interpret whether the design is inherently safer than the baseline. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A (a) — ISD Pillars & Advantages (7 marks)

**1. Four ISD Pillars (4 marks):**
- **Minimize (Intensification):** Reduce inventory of hazardous material. *Example:* Use on-site generation of chlorine instead of bulk storage.
- **Substitute (Replacement):** Replace a hazardous substance with a less hazardous one. *Example:* Replace benzene (carcinogenic) with cyclohexane or toluene.
- **Moderate (Attenuation):** Operate at lower temperature and pressure. *Example:* Distill under vacuum instead of at high pressure.
- **Simplify (Limitation of Effects):** Design equipment and layouts that fail safely. *Example:* Use spiral-wound gaskets that leak before rupture.

**2. Engineering Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Fewer instruments → smaller instrument error footprint.
- Lower inventory → smaller Hazardous Area (HA) zone → cheaper Ex-proof equipment.

**3. Economic Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Reduced CAPEX on firefighting systems and secondary containment.
- Reduced OPEX on insurance premiums (typical 15–30% saving).

---

### Solution to Question A (b) — Numerical ELR Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $f_{\text{old}} = 10^{-2}$ /yr; $C_{\text{old}} = 5 \times 10^{7}$ ₹; $PFD_{old} = 10^{-1}$
- $f_{\text{new}} = 10^{-3}$ /yr; $C_{\text{new}} = 10^{7}$ ₹; $PFD_{new} = 10^{-2}$
- $C_{\text{DfS}} = 2 \times 10^{7}$ ₹

**Step 1 — Compute $EL_{\text{old}}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EL_{\text{old}} &= f_{\text{old}} \times C_{\text{old}} \times PFD_{old} \\
&= 10^{-2} \times 5 \times 10^{7} \times 10^{-1} \\
&= 5 \times 10^{4} \;\text{₹/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $EL_{\text{new}}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EL_{\text{new}} &= f_{\text{new}} \times C_{\text{new}} \times PFD_{new} \\
&= 10^{-3} \times 10^{7} \times 10^{-2} \\
&= 10^{2} \;\text{₹/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Expected Loss Reduction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta EL &= EL_{\text{old}} - EL_{\text{new}} \\
&= 5 \times 10^{4} - 10^{2} \\
&= 4.99 \times 10^{4} \;\text{₹/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Benefit Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \dfrac{\Delta EL}{EL_{\text{old}}} = \dfrac{4.99 \times 10^{4}}{5 \times 10^{4}} \\
&= 0.998 \;\;(99.8\% \text{ benefit})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Pay-Back Period:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PB &= \dfrac{C_{\text{DfS}}}{\Delta EL} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{7}}{4.99 \times 10^{4}} \\
&\approx 400.8 \;\text{days} \;\approx\; 1.1 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The investment of ₹2 Crore is recovered in approximately **13 months** — a clear **engineering and economic advantage** of Design for Safety. **(7/7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question B (a) — Substitute & Moderate in Pharma (7 marks)

**Substitute Strategy (3.5 marks):**
- Replacing **diethyl ether** (highly flammable, peroxide-forming) with **dichloromethane** or **ethyl acetate** in extraction processes reduces fire and explosion risk.
- Replacing **chromic acid** (carcinogenic, Class 1B CMR) with **TEMPO-oxidation** or **electrochemical oxidation** reduces carcinogen exposure of operators.
- **Advantage:** Operator exposure drops below **TLV-TWA** limits, lowering occupational health surveillance cost.

**Moderate Strategy (3.5 marks):**
- Conducting hydrogenation at **lower temperature (60°C instead of 120°C)** using more active catalysts reduces runaway-reaction probability.
- Replacing **high-pressure steam heating** with **hot water heating** in API (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient) drying reduces explosion envelope.
- **Advantage:** Lower SIL requirement (SIL 2 instead of SIL 3) → saving on certified instrumentation cost.

---

### Solution to Question B (b) — ISI Total Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Apply weights:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ISI_{total} &= (0.40)(1.20) + (0.25)(1.05) + (0.20)(0.95) + (0.15)(1.10) \\
&= 0.480 + 0.2625 + 0.190 + 0.165 \\
&= 1.0975
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:**
Since $ISI_{total} = 1.0975 > 1$, the design is **inherently safer** than the reference baseline by approximately **9.75%**, confirming the **engineering advantage** of the chosen DfS configuration.

**Step 3 — Sub-Index Diagnosis:**
The lowest sub-index is **$ISI_{chem} = 0.95$** (below 1), indicating that **chemical substitution** is the weakest advantage area and should be the focus of further improvement.

**(7/7 marks)**

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in ELR:** Students frequently forget to keep *f* in events/yr, *C* in ₹/event, and *PFD* dimensionless — leading to a **1-mark deduction**.
> 2. **ISI Weight Constraint:** Failing to verify $\sum w_i = 1$ costs a full **1 mark** in numerical questions.
> 3. **Confusing "Minimize" with "Substitute":** These are *distinct* ISD pillars. Mixing them up in 14-mark answers loses up to **2 marks**.
> 4. **Pay-Back Unit Error:** Pay-back period is in **years** (or days), not months — but must be clearly stated.
> 5. **Writing "Zero Risk":** Inherent safety does *not* mean zero risk — examiners deduct marks for absolute statements; always write **"risk reduction"**, not **"risk elimination"**.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **four ISD pillars** are **Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify** — frequently tested as 3-mark definitions.
- ✅ Advantages span **Engineering, Economic, Regulatory, Environmental, and Social** categories.
- ✅ The **Expected Loss Reduction** formula $\Delta EL = EL_{\text{old}} - EL_{\text{new}}$ is the most-tested numerical.
- ✅ The **Inherent Safety Index (ISI)** must satisfy the weight sum constraint $\sum w_i = 1$.
- ✅ **Pay-Back Period** is computed as $PB = C_{\text{DfS}} / \Delta EL$ and typically ranges from **0.5 to 3 years** for well-designed DfS interventions.
- ✅ Always state **"risk reduction"**, never **"risk elimination"** — this is a high-frequency valuation trap.
- ✅ Connect every advantage to at least **one Indian regulation** (Factories Act 1948, OSHA 1970, ISO 45001:2018, IS 1893, AERB) for full marks in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ The **LCC (Life-Cycle Cost)** saving factor of **3×–10×** is a board-favourite numerical cite.
- ✅ **SIL levels** must always be quoted with the corresponding **$PFD_{avg}$** band (e.g., SIL 2 → $10^{-7} \leq PFD < 10^{-6}$).
- ✅ Use Mermaid / block diagrams in your answer sheets to visualise the ISD pillars — KTU examiners award up to **1 bonus mark** for clear schematic illustrations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Explosive Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Explosive Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Explosive Testing** is the systematic laboratory evaluation of substances, articles, and mixtures to determine their explosive behavior, sensitivity to external stimuli (impact, friction, heat, electrostatic discharge), thermal stability, and the appropriate hazard classification for transport, storage, and use, in accordance with internationally recognized protocols such as the **UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods – Manual of Tests and Criteria**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Explosive (UN Definition):** A solid or liquid substance (or mixture of substances) which is in itself capable by chemical reaction of producing gas at such a temperature and pressure and at such speed as to cause damage to the surroundings. Three classes exist: **(a) mass-explosive**, **(b) deflagrating**, and **(c) extremely insensitive substances (EIS)**.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Anchors)

| Term | Engineering Definition |
|------|------------------------|
| **Detonation** | Supersonic shock-driven reaction wave traveling at **1500–9000 m/s** through the explosive charge |
| **Deflagration** | Subsonic combustion wave (<**1000 m/s**) driven by heat transfer |
| **Sensitivity** | Minimum stimulus (impact, friction, heat) required to initiate reaction |
| **Brisance** | Shattering power of an explosive, proportional to detonation pressure |
| **TNT Equivalence** | Explosive power expressed as a fraction of TNT's energy output |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Fireworks Inspector":** Think of explosive testing like a quality inspector at a fireworks factory. Before shipping, every batch is **dropped from a specific height (impact sensitivity)**, **rubbed against a rough surface (friction sensitivity)**, **heated in a sealed oven (thermal stability)**, and **detonated in a controlled chamber (brisance test)**. The data tells the safety engineer *exactly* what hazard label to print on the box — just like the diamond inspector uses pressure and light tests to grade stones. Without these standardized tests, dangerous materials would be shipped blindly.

### 1.4 Reference Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Reference Constants (Bold for Emphasis)**
> - Standard TNT Heat of Detonation: **4184 kJ/kg** (≈ 1000 kcal/kg)
> - TNT Detonation Velocity: **6930 m/s** at ρ = 1.60 g/cm³
> - TNT Detonation Pressure (C-J): **21.0 GPa**
> - Speed of Detonation Reference Cord (PETN): **~6800 m/s**
> - Standard Lead Block Cavity: **61 mL** (Trauzl Test baseline)
> - BAM Fallhammer Drop Height Range: **10–1000 mm** (Impact Test)
> - Standard Atmosphere: **101.325 kPa**, 25 °C

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Explosive Tests

The UN Manual of Tests and Criteria organizes explosive assessment into **Eight Test Series**, each answering a specific safety question:

**Step 1 — Series 1 (Assignment to Class 1):**
Determines whether a substance possesses explosive properties through six sub-tests (gap test, Koenen test, time/pressure test, internal ignition, dropping weight, friction/wire).

**Step 2 — Series 2 (Insensitivity Acceptance):**
Confirms a substance is "too insensitive" to qualify as Class 1 (used for ammonium nitrate classification).

**Step 3 — Series 3 (Sensitivity Quantification):**
Quantifies response to mechanical stimuli — BAM Fallhammer (impact), BAM Friction apparatus, electrostatic discharge.

**Step 4 — Series 4 (Thermal Stability):**
Determines safe storage temperatures — 75 °C/48 h thermal stability, self-accelerating decomposition temperature (SADT) test.

**Step 5 — Series 5 (Self-Heating & Ignition):**
Evaluates bulk thermal run-away behavior in confined/unconfined stacks.

**Step 6 — Series 6 (Article Classification, Division 1.6):**
Determines whether an article can be classified as Extremely Insensitive Detonating Substance (EIDS).

**Step 7 — Series 7 (Article Classification, Division 1.4):**
Assesses whether an article presents only a minor explosion hazard.

**Step 8 — Series 8 (Liquid HE Desensitization):**
Tests ammonium nitrate emulsions, suspensions, and gels.

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters (Engineering Rationale)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Testing:**
> - **Impact tests** → safety during handling, transport vibrations
> - **Friction tests** → safety during pressing, machining, loading
> - **Thermal tests** → safety in tropical warehouses, accidental fires
> - **Detonation velocity** → enables blast-pressure prediction
> - **TNT equivalence** → universal yardstick for safety distance calculations (e.g., Q-D relationships in pyrotechnic siting)

### 2.3 The Six UN Hazard Divisions

| Division | Hazard Description | Example |
|----------|--------------------|---------|
| **1.1** | Mass Detonation (entire load detonates) | TNT, RDX, PETN |
| **1.2** | Projection hazard (few items detonate, fragments fly) | Detonators without two safety features |
| **1.3** | Mass Fire (radiant heat, minor blast) | Rocket motors, propellant powders |
| **1.4** | Minor hazard (effects confined to package) | Small arms ammunition |
| **1.5** | Very Insensitive (EIS / blasting agents) | ANFO emulsions |
| **1.6** | Extremely Insensitive Detonating Articles | Specialized shaped charges |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Critical for Numerical Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Note:** Vertical pipes in math expressions use `\vert` to avoid markdown table break.

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---------------------|---------|-------|
| 1 | **TNT Equivalence (mass basis)** | $TNT_{eq} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{det,sample}}{\Delta H_{det,TNT}} \times 100$ | % or fraction |
| 2 | **Trauzl Block Expansion** | $V_{exp} = V_{final} - 61\,\text{mL}$ | mL |
| 3 | **TNT Equivalence (Trauzl)** | $TNT_{eq} = \dfrac{V_{exp,sample}}{V_{exp,TNT}} \times 100$ | % |
| 4 | **Dautriche Detonation Velocity** | $D = \dfrac{L \cdot v_{cord}}{2\,d}$ | m/s |
| 5 | **Chapman-Jouguet Pressure** | $P_{CJ} = \dfrac{\rho_0 D^2}{\gamma + 1}$ | Pa |
| 6 | **Brisance Index (Hershel)** | $B = \rho_0 \cdot \dfrac{D^2}{1 + \rho_0/\rho_w}$ | relative |
| 7 | **Heat of Detonation (Kistiakowsky-Wilson)** | $Q = \dfrac{28.9b + 47.0(c - a) - \text{endothermic terms}}{\text{molecular mass}}$ | kcal/mol |
| 8 | **Equivalence of TNT (energy)** | $E_{charge} = TNT_{eq} \cdot W_{sample}$ | kg TNT |
| 9 | **Cube-Root Scaled Distance** | $Z = \dfrac{R}{E^{1/3}}$ | m/(kg)$^{1/3}$ |
| 10 | **Side-on Blast Overpressure (Hopkinson-Cranz)** | $\dfrac{P_s}{P_0} = f(Z)$ (using CONWEP charts) | Pa |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Explosive Testing Data Is Used:**
> 1. **Storage License Applications:** ISAH/CCOE and PESO (India) require test data
> 2. **Safety Distance Calculations:** Q-D equations by Wiss & Garofalo
> 3. **Blast-Resistant Building Design:** UFC 3-340-02 uses TNT equivalent weight
> 4. **Process Safety Management:** OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 explosive inventory thresholds
> 5. **Insurance Risk Assessment:** IME SLP-2 (US), HSE-UK safety documents

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — TNT Equivalence via Trauzl Lead Block

**Problem Statement:** A sample explosive weighing 10 g is detonated inside a standard lead block. The cavity volume after the test is measured as **315 mL**. For a 10 g TNT reference charge under identical conditions, the cavity volume is **300 mL**. Determine the TNT equivalence of the sample.

**Step 1: Calculate the net expansion volume for the sample.**

The Trauzl lead block has a standard initial cavity of **61 mL**. The net expansion is the final cavity volume minus the initial cavity.

$$
V_{exp,sample} = V_{final,sample} - V_{initial}
$$

$$
V_{exp,sample} = 315\,\text{mL} - 61\,\text{mL}
$$

$$
V_{exp,sample} = 254\,\text{mL}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the net expansion volume for the TNT reference.**

$$
V_{exp,TNT} = 300\,\text{mL} - 61\,\text{mL} = 239\,\text{mL}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the TNT equivalence formula.**

$$
TNT_{eq} = \frac{V_{exp,sample}}{V_{exp,TNT}} \times 100
$$

$$
TNT_{eq} = \frac{254}{239} \times 100
$$

$$
TNT_{eq} = 1.0627 \times 100 = 106.27\,\%
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

The sample explosive is **~6.27 % more powerful than TNT** on a per-gram basis. This result is common for RDX (TNT eq ≈ 115–120 %) and HMX (TNT eq ≈ 130 %).

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Detonation Velocity by Dautriche Method

**Problem Statement:** A Dautriche experiment is conducted to measure the detonation velocity of an unknown explosive charge. The two witness points on the donor cord are separated by **L = 100 mm**. The cord is laid across a soft lead witness plate, and the displacement of the two waves on the plate is measured as **d = 7.4 mm**. The detonation cord velocity is **v_cord = 6800 m/s**. Calculate the detonation velocity of the sample.

**Step 1: Understand the geometry.**

In the Dautriche method, the unknown detonation wave passes through two points on a high-velocity donor cord at distance L apart. The two cord-initiated shock waves meet at a midpoint but travel in opposite directions. The displacement of the meeting point on the witness plate, d, is related to the unknown detonation velocity D.

**Step 2: Apply the Dautriche relationship.**

$$
D = \frac{L \cdot v_{cord}}{2 \cdot d}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values.**

$$
D = \frac{100 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} \times 6800\,\text{m/s}}{2 \times 7.4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}}
$$

$$
D = \frac{0.100 \times 6800}{0.0148}
$$

$$
D = \frac{680}{0.0148}
$$

$$
D = 45\,945.95\,\text{m/s} \approx 45.95\,\text{km/s}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

A detonation velocity of approximately **46,000 m/s** (≈ 6.6× TNT's 6930 m/s) indicates an extremely high-performance explosive. Such high values are characteristic of high-density HMX or CL-20-based formulations under confinement. Standard pressed RDX typically exhibits **~8500 m/s**, while ANFO is **~3200 m/s**.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Chapman-Jouguet Detonation Pressure

**Problem Statement:** For an explosive charge with measured detonation velocity **D = 8500 m/s**, density **ρ₀ = 1.70 g/cm³**, and an assumed polytropic index **γ = 3.0**, compute the C-J detonation pressure.

**Step 1: Convert density to SI units.**

$$
\rho_0 = 1.70\,\text{g/cm}^3 = 1700\,\text{kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Chapman-Jouguet equation.**

$$
P_{CJ} = \frac{\rho_0 D^2}{\gamma + 1}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$
P_{CJ} = \frac{1700 \times (8500)^2}{3.0 + 1}
$$

$$
P_{CJ} = \frac{1700 \times 72\,250\,000}{4.0}
$$

$$
P_{CJ} = \frac{122\,825\,000\,000}{4.0}
$$

$$
P_{CJ} = 30.71 \times 10^9\,\text{Pa}
$$

$$
P_{CJ} \approx 30.7\,\text{GPa}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

The C-J pressure of **~30.7 GPa** indicates a high-performance secondary explosive. The standard reference values are: **TNT ≈ 21 GPa**, **RDX ≈ 33.5 GPa**, and **HMX ≈ 36.8 GPa**.

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Energy-Based TNT Equivalence (Mass Blast Equivalent)

**Problem Statement:** A process safety study involves a vessel containing **W = 500 kg** of a sample explosive. Laboratory tests indicate that the heat of detonation of the sample is **ΔH = 5480 kJ/kg**. If TNT's heat of detonation is **4184 kJ/kg**, compute the TNT equivalent mass and the resulting side-on blast overpressure at a distance **R = 200 m** using the cube-root scaling.

**Step 1: Calculate the mass-based TNT equivalence.**

$$
TNT_{eq} = \frac{\Delta H_{det,sample}}{\Delta H_{det,TNT}} \times 100
$$

$$
TNT_{eq} = \frac{5480}{4184} \times 100 = 130.97\,\%
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the equivalent TNT mass.**

$$
E = TNT_{eq} \times W = 1.3097 \times 500\,\text{kg} = 654.85\,\text{kg TNT}
$$

**Step 3: Compute scaled distance Z.**

$$
Z = \frac{R}{E^{1/3}} = \frac{200}{(654.85)^{1/3}}
$$

$$
Z = \frac{200}{8.692} = 23.01\,\text{m/kg}^{1/3}
$$

**Step 4: Read blast overpressure from the standard CONWEP curve.**

For **Z = 23 m/kg^(1/3)**, the side-on overpressure is approximately **P_s ≈ 9 kPa (1.3 psi)**. This is a level that typically causes **minor structural damage** (windows shatter, light injuries to personnel). The corresponding scaled impulse and positive-phase duration can be obtained from standard CONWEP charts.

**Step 5: Decision matrix for KTU.**

| Scaled Distance (m/kg^1/3) | Overpressure | Damage Description |
|-----------------------------|--------------|---------------------|
| $< 3$ | > 200 kPa | Total destruction |
| $3 - 6$ | 70–200 kPa | Heavy damage, reinforced concrete fails |
| $6 - 15$ | 20–70 kPa | Severe damage, framed buildings fail |
| $15 - 30$ | 7–20 kPa | Moderate damage, partial collapse |
| $> 30$ | < 7 kPa | Minor damage, broken windows |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 UN Test Series Decision Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete decision logic used to classify a substance as explosive under the UN Recommendations. It traces how the eight test series cascade through the classification procedure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Substance or Article] --> B[Series 1 - Explosive Properties Test]
    B --> C{Explosive?}
    C -- No --> D[Not Class 1 - Class 4 5 or 9]
    C -- Yes --> E[Series 2 - Too Insensitive?]
    E --> F{Insensitive?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Class 5.1 Oxidizer]
    F -- No --> H[Series 3 - Sensitivity Quantification]
    H --> I[Impact Friction ESD]
    H --> J[Series 4 - Thermal Stability]
    J --> K{Stable?}
    K -- No --> L[Reject or Modify Formulation]
    K -- Yes --> M[Series 6 or 7 - Article Classification]
    M --> N[Assign Division 1.1 1.2 1.3 1.4 1.5 or 1.6]
    N --> O[Final Hazard Classification]
    O --> P[Storage Transport Licensing]
```

### 4.2 Explosive Testing Workflow Block Diagram

The following diagram shows the standard workflow of an explosive testing laboratory from sample reception through final classification.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Sample Receipt and Documentation] --> R2[Visual and Chemical Characterization]
    R2 --> R3[Impact Test BAM Fallhammer]
    R2 --> R4[Friction Test BAM Apparatus]
    R2 --> R5[Thermal Stability 75C 48h]
    R2 --> R6[Koenen Test]
    R2 --> R7[Time Pressure Test]
    R2 --> R8[Detonation Velocity Dautriche]
    R2 --> R9[Trauzl Lead Block Test]
    R3 --> R10[Tabulate Sensitivity Data]
    R4 --> R10
    R5 --> R10
    R6 --> R10
    R7 --> R10
    R8 --> R11[Tabulate Performance Data]
    R9 --> R11
    R10 --> R12[UN Classification Determination]
    R11 --> R12
    R12 --> R13[Final Test Report and Safety Certificate]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Sensitivity vs Stability Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENS[Mechanical Sensitivity Group]
        S1[Impact Sensitivity]
        S2[Friction Sensitivity]
        S3[Electrostatic Sensitivity]
    end
    subgraph STAB[Thermal Stability Group]
        T1[SADT Determination]
        T2[Self Heating at 75C]
        T3[Koenen Confined Heating]
    end
    subgraph PERF[Performance Group]
        P1[Detonation Velocity]
        P2[Detonation Pressure]
        P3[Trauzl Expansion]
    end
    SENS --> DATA[(Test Database)]
    STAB --> DATA
    PERF --> DATA
    DATA --> CLASS{UN Division Decision}
    CLASS --> D1[Div 1.1 Mass Detonation]
    CLASS --> D2[Div 1.2 Projection]
    CLASS --> D3[Div 1.3 Mass Fire]
    CLASS --> D4[Div 1.4 Minor]
    CLASS --> D5[Div 1.5 EIS]
    CLASS --> D6[Div 1.6 EIDS]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]

**Q: Define explosive testing. List any four standard explosive tests with the specific property each measures.**

**Model Answer:**

**Explosive Testing** is the systematic laboratory evaluation of explosive substances and articles to determine their properties, sensitivity, stability, and hazard classification for safe handling, transport, and storage, in accordance with standardized protocols like the UN Manual of Tests and Criteria.

**Four Standard Tests:**

| Test | Property Measured |
|------|-------------------|
| **BAM Fallhammer Test** | Impact sensitivity (Joules of impact energy required for 50 % initiation) |
| **BAM Friction Test** | Friction sensitivity (Newton of frictional force) |
| **Koenen Test** | Sensitivity to heating under confinement (limiting orifice diameter in mm) |
| **Dautriche Method** | Detonation velocity in m/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (KTU 2024):** Students commonly confuse *sensitivity tests* (Series 3) with *performance tests* (Trauzl, Dautriche). Sensitivity answers the question *"how easily does it explode?"* while performance answers *"how strong is it once exploded?"*. **1-mark penalty** if these are interchanged.

---

#### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Q: Differentiate between detonation and deflagration. Why is this distinction critical for explosive classification?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Detonation | Deflagration |
|-----------|------------|--------------|
| **Wave Velocity** | 1500–9000 m/s (supersonic) | < 1000 m/s (subsonic) |
| **Propagation Mechanism** | Shock-driven chemistry | Heat transfer & diffusion |
| **Pressure Effect** | Produces shock wave (MPa–GPa) | Produces pressure rise (kPa–MPa) |
| **Typical Examples** | TNT, RDX, HMX, PETN | Black powder, propellants, pyrotechnics |
| **UN Division** | 1.1, 1.2, 1.6 | 1.3, 1.4 (mostly) |

**Critical Significance for Classification:**

The distinction is critical because the **UN Classification System uses propagation mode to assign hazard divisions**. Substances that undergo **detonation** receive Division 1.1 (mass-detonation hazard) and trigger the strictest storage distance rules, whereas substances that only **deflagrate** (Division 1.3 mass fire) may be stored in larger quantities with reduced inter-magazine separation. The same chemical compound (e.g., ammonium perchlorate) can shift divisions based on physical form (granular vs. pressed pellets) because of this very distinction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (1-mark loss):** Writing that "deflagration is slower" is incomplete. You must state the **mechanism** (heat transfer vs. shock) to earn full credit.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is mandatory. Choose **either Question A or Question B**.

---

#### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a) Explain the UN Test Series 1 through 4 in detail, including the type of stimulus assessed and the test apparatus used. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **UN Manual of Tests and Criteria** prescribes a four-stage hierarchical test protocol to determine whether a substance warrants Class 1 (Explosive) classification.

**Series 1 — Tests to Determine Explosive Properties:**

This initial series contains six sub-tests designed as screening experiments:

| Sub-Test | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| **1 (a) Gap Test** | Shock-passing capability |
| **1 (b) Koenen Test** | Heating under confinement |
| **1 (c) Time/Pressure Test** | Heating at constant pressure |
| **1 (d) Internal Ignition Test** | Thermal run-away in bulk |
| **1 (e) Drop Weight Test** | Impact sensitivity |
| **1 (f) Friction/Wire Test** | Friction sensitivity |

If **three or more positive results** are obtained, the substance is provisionally accepted as Class 1.

**Series 2 — Tests for Substances Too Insensitive:**

The objective is to confirm whether a substance is so insensitive that it does **not** warrant Class 1 classification. Such substances are typically reclassified as **Class 5.1 Oxidizers** (e.g., ammonium nitrate under standard conditions).

**Series 3 — Tests to Determine Sensitivity to Mechanical Stimuli:**

These tests quantitatively measure **impact sensitivity** and **friction sensitivity**:

- **BAM Fallhammer (Impact):** A 1–10 kg weight falls from variable height (10–1000 mm) onto a sample confined between steel anvils. The 50 % initiation height is reported.
- **BAM Friction Apparatus:** A sample is pressed against a roughened porcelain plate under a known hydraulic load while the plate reciprocates. Limiting load (N) for ignition is recorded.

**Series 4 — Tests to Determine Thermal Stability:**

The 75 °C / 48 hours test and SADT (Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature) test are used to establish safe storage temperatures. The sample must not undergo self-accelerating decomposition below 75 °C for normal Class 1 acceptance.

---

**(b) Describe in detail the procedure and result interpretation of the Koenen Test and the Trauzl Lead Block Test. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Koenen Test — Heating Under Confinement:**

The Koenen test determines whether a substance is sensitive to heating under confinement.

**Apparatus:**
- A steel tube (internal diameter 24 mm, length 75 mm, wall thickness 1 mm)
- A closing plate with a variable orifice diameter (1.0 to 20.0 mm)
- Four-burner gas ring capable of heating the tube to 800 °C in 30 seconds

**Procedure:**
1. Weigh **9 g** of sample and pack into the steel tube.
2. Close with the orifice plate of a chosen diameter.
3. Heat with the four-burner ring.
4. Observe whether the tube **fragments** into ≥ 4 pieces within 5 minutes.

**Result Interpretation:**
The test is repeated with progressively larger orifices. The **limiting diameter** (in mm) at which the result is still **positive ("+")** is reported. Larger limiting diameters indicate greater sensitivity to confined heating.

| Limiting Diameter (mm) | Sensitivity Class |
|------------------------|-------------------|
| $\geq$ 10 | Very Sensitive |
| 5–9 | Moderately Sensitive |
| 2–4 | Less Sensitive |
| $< 2$ | Insensitive (for heating) |

---

**Trauzl Lead Block Test:**

The Trauzl test measures the **working power (brisance/work)** of an explosive.

**Apparatus:**
- A cylindrical lead block (200 mm diameter × 200 mm height, mass ~ 70 kg)
- A central cavity of **25 mm diameter × 125 mm depth** (61 mL initial volume)
- Sand tamping column, detonator, lead block in wooden frame

**Procedure:**
1. Wrap **10 g** of test explosive in tin foil.
2. Insert into the cavity and place a No. 8 electric detonator on top.
3. Stem the cavity with dry sand.
4. Detonate and recover the lead block.
5. Measure the new cavity volume by water displacement.

**Result Interpretation:**
$$
V_{exp} = V_{final} - 61\,\text{mL}
$$
$$
TNT_{eq} = \frac{V_{exp,sample}}{V_{exp,TNT}} \times 100\,\%
$$

**Typical Values:** TNT = 100 %, RDX = 115–120 %, HMX = 130 %, ANFO = 70 %, Black powder = 30 %.

---

#### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a) Explain the concept of TNT equivalence. How is it determined using (i) heat of detonation, and (ii) Trauzl lead block test? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of TNT Equivalence:**

TNT equivalence is a **standardization method** that expresses the explosive energy output of any material as an **equivalent mass of TNT** (trinitrotoluene), which is used as the universal reference explosive.

**Why TNT?**
- TNT is a stable, well-characterized explosive with reproducible properties.
- Its detonation parameters (heat, velocity, pressure) are globally adopted as **reference values**.
- All blast-damage correlations (e.g., UFC 3-340-02) use TNT-equivalent mass as input.

**Mathematically:**
$$
TNT_{eq} = \frac{\text{Energy of Sample}}{\text{Energy of TNT}} \times 100\,\%
$$

**Method (i) — Heat of Detonation Basis:**

The heat released per unit mass is measured in a bomb calorimeter under inert (argon) atmosphere, simulating detonation products.

$$
TNT_{eq} = \frac{\Delta H_{det,sample}}{\Delta H_{det,TNT}} \times 100
$$

With **ΔH_TNT = 4184 kJ/kg**, a sample releasing 5480 kJ/kg yields:
$$
TNT_{eq} = \frac{5480}{4184} \times 100 = 130.97\,\%
$$

**Method (ii) — Trauzl Block Expansion Basis:**

Performed in a standard lead block (as described in 5.2 Question A above). The expansion volume is normalized against TNT's expansion under identical conditions.

$$
TNT_{eq} = \frac{V_{exp,sample}}{V_{exp,TNT}} \times 100\,\%
$$

**Comparison of Methods:**

| Aspect | Heat-of-Detonation | Trauzl Block |
|--------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Measures** | Total energy | Work output (gas expansion) |
| **Apparatus Cost** | Moderate (bomb calorimeter) | Low (lead blocks) |
| **Reproducibility** | ± 2 % | ± 5 % |
| **TNT Reference Value** | 4184 kJ/kg | 300 mL cavity for 10 g |

---

**(b) With the help of a neat sketch, describe the Dautriche method for measuring the detonation velocity of an explosive. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Dautriche Method** is a simple, indirect technique to measure the detonation velocity of an explosive charge by comparing wave arrival times along a high-velocity donor cord.

**Apparatus Required:**
- A known charge (initiator) and the unknown explosive charge separated by a barrier
- A high-velocity **detonating cord** (PETN cord with $v_{cord} \approx 6800$ m/s)
- Two reference points on the cord at known distance L apart
- A soft **lead or aluminum witness plate** placed at right angles to the cord

**Sketch (Reconstructed in Text Form):**

```
        Unknown Charge
              │
              ▼
        ┌─────────┐
        │  ●  Init ● │         ◄── Detonation cord
        │  ┌──┐    │         with two points L apart
        │  │L │    │
        │  └──┘    │
        └────┬─────┘
             │
      ───────┴────── Lead Plate
             ▲
             d (displacement)
```

**Procedure:**
1. Place the unknown charge so that its detonation wave initiates one end of the donor cord.
2. Pass the cord through two reference points L apart, then across a soft lead plate.
3. Detonate; the two cord-borne waves travel toward each other and meet at a point on the plate.
4. The meeting point is **displaced** from the geometric midpoint by a distance **d**.
5. Measure **d** from the witness plate indent.

**Derivation of Formula:**

The unknown detonation front passes between the two points in time $t = L/D$. In the same time, the cord waves travel a distance $v_{cord} \cdot t = v_{cord} \cdot L/D$ from each end. The displacement of the meeting point from the midpoint is:

$$
d = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{v_{cord} \cdot L}{D} - \frac{L}{2}\right)
$$

Solving for D:

$$
\boxed{D = \frac{L \cdot v_{cord}}{2 \cdot d}}
$$

**Numerical Example:**

Given L = 100 mm, d = 7.4 mm, v_cord = 6800 m/s:

$$
D = \frac{100 \times 6800}{2 \times 7.4} = 45\,946\,\text{m/s} \approx 45.95\,\text{km/s}
$$

**Interpretation:** The result indicates an extremely high-performance explosive (HMX-class).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Explosive Testing Questions (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Mixing up sensitivity vs. stability:** Impact/friction are *sensitivity*; thermal tests are *stability*. Cross-confusion = **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract the 61 mL baseline** in Trauzl problems. The net expansion is what matters. **−1 mark** per occurrence.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency in Dautriche method:** Always convert L and d to **meters** before computing D in m/s. **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Skipping the UN Division Number** in classification answers. The examiner specifically looks for "1.1 / 1.2 / 1.3 / 1.4 / 1.5 / 1.6" codes. Writing "Class 1" alone loses the **sub-classification mark**.
> 5. **Using TNT's heat of detonation as 1000 kcal/kg vs 4184 kJ/kg inconsistently.** Standardize on **kJ/kg** for KTU answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Explosive Testing — Final Rapid-Revision Bullets:**
> - **UN Test Series 1–8** form the international classification backbone (Series 1–4 are mandatory initial).
> - **BAM Fallhammer** measures impact sensitivity in J; **BAM Friction** measures friction in N.
> - **Koenen Test** reports the limiting orifice diameter (mm) — larger value = more sensitive.
> - **75 °C / 48 h thermal stability** is the standard thermal acceptance threshold.
> - **Trauzl Block Expansion** subtracts **61 mL** baseline; net expansion normalized to TNT gives **% TNT equivalence**.
> - **Dautriche Method:** $D = L \cdot v_{cord} / (2d)$ — measure L and d carefully in metres.
> - **TNT Reference Values (Memorize):** ΔH = **4184 kJ/kg**, D = **6930 m/s**, P_CJ = **21 GPa**.
> - **Chapman-Jouguet Pressure:** $P_{CJ} = \rho_0 D^2 / (\gamma + 1)$ — used for performance ranking.
> - **UN Divisions:** 1.1 (mass detonation), 1.2 (projection), 1.3 (mass fire), 1.4 (minor), 1.5 (EIS), 1.6 (EIDS).
> - **Detonation > 1500 m/s (shock-driven)**, **deflagration < 1000 m/s (heat-driven)** — know the mechanism difference.
> - **Cube-root scaling:** $Z = R/E^{1/3}$ used with CONWEP charts to predict blast overpressure at any distance.
> - **TNT Equivalence** can be computed from heat of detonation OR from Trauzl expansion; both are valid in KTU answers.
> - **Standard Reference Constants:** Atmospheric pressure = **101.325 kPa**; PETN cord velocity ≈ **6800 m/s**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deflagration Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Deflagration Test ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Deflagration** is a mode of combustion in which the flame front propagates through a combustible mixture at a velocity **less than the speed of sound** in the unreacted medium. The reaction zone advances primarily by **heat conduction and convection**, and the unburned gases are pushed forward by a pressure-driven flow ahead of the flame. The classic textbook velocity range is approximately **1 m/s to ~300–400 m/s**, with typical industrial deflagrations occurring in the range of **1–100 m/s**. A **Deflagration Test** is a standardized laboratory procedure used to determine the explosion characteristics of a flammable gas, vapor, or dust cloud when confined in a vessel. The test quantitatively measures: 1. **Maximum Explosion Pressure (P_max)** — in **bar(g)**. 2. **Maximum Rate of Pressure Rise ((dP/dt)_max)** — in **bar/s**. 3. **Deflagration Index (K_G for gases, K_St for dusts)** — in **bar·m/s**. These three parameters form the foundation of vent sizing, explosion protection design, and hazard classification as per **NFPA 68, EN 14491, VDI 3673, ASTM E1226, and ISO 6184**. ### 1.2 Deflagration vs. Detonation — The Critical Distinction | Property | Deflagration | Detonation | |----------|--------------|------------| | Flame speed | Subsonic (< 300 m/s typical) | Supersonic (1500–2000 m/s) | | Driving mechanism | Heat & mass transfer | Shock wave compression | | Pressure rise time | 10s of ms to seconds | Microseconds | | Pressure wave | Pressure pulse follows flame | Pressure wave leads flame | | Test vessel | 20 L sphere or 1 m³ | Detonation tube | | Relevance in safety | Vent design (NFPA 68) | Blast-resistant design (NFPA 69) | > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The deflagration test is concerned with **deflagration** events only. Detonations are studied using a different apparatus (detonation tube) and are typically a postgraduate-level topic in this course. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **long line of dominoes standing in a corridor**. - In **deflagration**, you push the first domino. It falls onto the next, transferring its energy by **contact** (heat transfer) — slowly and progressively. The chain reaction walks down the line at a walking pace. This is what happens in a gas cloud in a room: the flame front "walks" through the mixture. - In **detonation**, you inject a shock wave into the corridor. Every domino is hit simultaneously with enormous force — the wave moves faster than sound. This is the violent, supersonic analog. > **In a deflagration test**, we measure how "aggressively" the flame walks through the mixture, by recording how quickly the pressure builds up inside a closed test vessel. The faster the walk, the more violent the explosion, the larger the vent area we must provide. ### 1.4 Purpose of the Deflagration Test > [!IMPORTANT] > The deflagration test provides three engineering numbers that drive **every** subsequent design decision in explosion protection: > - **P_max** → tells the designer the *peak pressure* the enclosure must withstand or the vent must release. > - **(dP/dt)_max** → tells the designer how *fast* the pressure builds, which determines the *size* of the vent. > - **K_G or K_St** → a **scaled, vessel-independent** parameter that allows extrapolation to real industrial equipment. These outputs feed directly into **NFPA 68 vent sizing equations** and **EN 14491** design curves. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The 20-Litre Sphere Apparatus (Siwek Sphere) The **standard deflagration test apparatus** is the **20-litre spherical explosion chamber** developed by Siwek (originally at Ciba-Geigy) and now adopted worldwide. **Key components:** - **Spherical vessel** of internal volume **V = 20 L** (0.020 m³). Sphere chosen because it has **no geometric preferred direction** for flame propagation. - **Ignition source:** Chemical ignitor (typically **2 × 5 kJ igniters**, total 10 kJ) located at the **geometric centre**. Strong central ignition is critical to obtain a uniform, spherical flame front. - **Dust dispersion system:** A 0.6 L or 1 L dust dispersion cup pressurised with air (typically **20 bar(g)**) that injects the dust sample into the evacuated chamber just before ignition. - **Water-cooled jacket:** Maintains vessel wall at a controlled temperature (typically **20–25 °C**). - **Two piezoelectric pressure transducers** mounted flush with the inner wall, diametrically opposite. - **Data acquisition system** sampling at **≥ 1 kHz** to capture fast transients. - **Exhaust valve + vacuum pump** for cleaning between tests. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **20 L sphere** is the laboratory reference vessel. Its results are mathematically **scaled** to the **1 m³ vessel** using the **Cube Root Law (Bartknecht's Third Power Law)** to ensure that laboratory data are representative of large industrial enclosures. ### 2.2 Test Procedure (ASTM E1226 / ISO 6184 / VDI 3673) The test sequence is: 1. **Weigh the dust sample** (typically 5–10 g depending on dust type) and load it into the dispersion cup. 2. **Evacuate** the sphere to ~0.4 bar(a). 3. **Inject** the dust via a pulse of compressed air (delay of ~60 ms allows turbulence to decay to optimum). 4. **Fire** the chemical igniter (10 kJ). 5. **Record** the pressure-time curve. 6. **Compute** P_max (peak) and (dP/dt)_max (steepest slope). 7. **Repeat** for multiple concentrations to find the **most easily ignitable** (optimum) concentration. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes | |--------|----------|---------------------|-------|-------| | K_G | Gas deflagration index | $K_G = \left(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3}$ | **bar·m/s** | Gas test only | | K_St | Dust deflagration index | $K_{St} = \left(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3}$ | **bar·m/s** | Dust test only | | P_max | Max explosion pressure | Read from P–t curve | **bar(g)** | Pressure above atmospheric at peak | | P_red | Reduced pressure (with vent) | From P–t curve | **bar(g)** | Used in vent design | | (dP/dt)_max | Max rate of pressure rise | Max slope of P–t curve | **bar/s** | Critical for vent sizing | | (dP/dt)_P,max | Rate of pressure rise at P_max | Used in NFPA 68 cubic-law | **bar/s** | | | (dP/dt)_m | Reduced rate of pressure rise | Used in NFPA 68 (red. P)^(1/3) | **bar/s** | | > [!NOTE] > **KTU exam trick:** The symbol $K_G$ uses **G** for **Gases** and $K_{St}$ uses **St** for **Staub** (German word for **dust** — from VDI 3673). The numerical value and units are identical. ### 2.4 The Cube Root Law (Bartknecht's Law) The single most important equation in deflagration testing: $$\left(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = K_G \;\; \text{or} \;\; K_{St} = \text{constant for a given fuel–oxidiser mixture}$$ This means that **doubling** the volume does **not** double the rate of pressure rise — it increases it by a factor of $2^{1/3} \approx 1.26$. **Derivation logic (qualitative):** - The **flame front in a deflagration** propagates roughly as a sphere of radius $r(t) = S_u \cdot t$ (where $S_u$ is the burning velocity, in m/s). - The **burnt volume fraction** scales as $r^3 \propto t^3$. - The **rate of pressure rise** is proportional to the rate of mass consumption, which scales as $r^2 \cdot S_u \propto t^2$ — a *parabolic* relationship. - At the moment of maximum pressure rise, the flame is approximately at the vessel wall, so $r \propto V^{1/3}$ and the peak rate scales as $V^{2/3} / V = V^{-1/3}$. - Multiplying by $V^{1/3}$ removes the volume dependence, giving a **fuel-specific** constant. > [!IMPORTANT] > The Cube Root Law is **valid only for spherical or near-spherical vessels** and for **turbulent, but not severely obstructed**, deflagrations. It is the engineering bridge between the 20 L lab test and the 100 m³ industrial silo. ### 2.5 Explosion Severity Classes (VDI 3673 / EN 14491) The deflagration index $K_{St}$ is used to classify dusts into four classes: | Class | $K_{St}$ Range (bar·m/s) | Severity | Examples | |-------|-------------------------|----------|----------| | **St 0** | 0 | Non-explosible | Pure inorganic salts | | **St 1** | 0 < K_St ≤ 200 | Weak | Coal dust, sulphur, cocoa | | **St 2** | 200 < K_St ≤ 300 | Strong | Cellulose, wood dust | | **St 3** | > 300 | Very strong | Aluminium, magnesium, toner | > [!WARNING] > **KTU exam trap:** Many students confuse $K_{St}$ classes with **Explosion Groups** (IIA, IIB, IIC) for gases. These are **two different classification systems** — one is for *dusts* (St 1/2/3), the other for *gases* (IIA/IIB/IIC). They must never be mixed. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The deflagration test is the **starting point** for: - **NFPA 68 / EN 14491 vent area** calculations for elevators, silos, dust collectors, and cyclones. - **Explosion isolation** system selection (flameless venting, detonation arrestors, rotary valves). - **ATEX / IECEx equipment zoning** (Classifying hazardous areas into Zones 20/21/22). - **Selection of explosion protection equipment** rating (e.g., 10 bar(g) vs. 12 bar(g) rated enclosures). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Cube Root Law (Bartknecht Equation) **Step 1 — Assumptions.** We assume a closed spherical vessel of radius $R$ and volume $V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3$. A spherical flame front expands outward from the centre at a constant **fundamental burning velocity** $S_u$ (m/s). The combustion is isothermal to first order (the burnt gas temperature is roughly constant at the adiabatic flame temperature $T_{ad}$). **Step 2 — Flame radius as a function of time.** The flame front radius at time $t$ is: $$r_f(t) = S_u \cdot t$$ **Step 3 — Volume of burnt gas at time $t$.** The burnt volume (sphere of radius $r_f$) is: $$V_b(t) = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi \, r_f^3(t) = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi \, S_u^3 \, t^3$$ **Step 4 — Mass burned per unit time.** Differentiating $V_b$ with respect to time: $$\dfrac{dV_b}{dt} = 4\pi \, S_u^3 \, t^2$$ **Step 5 — Pressure rise rate from ideal gas law.** For an isothermal expansion of the burnt gas, the rate of pressure rise is proportional to the rate of increase of burnt volume: $$\dfrac{dP}{dt} \propto \dfrac{dV_b}{dt} = 4\pi \, S_u^3 \, t^2$$ **Step 6 — Maximum rate of pressure rise.** The maximum occurs when the flame reaches the vessel wall, $r_f = R$, i.e. at $t_{max} = R / S_u$. Substituting: $$\left(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \propto 4\pi \, S_u^3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{R}{S_u}\right)^2 = 4\pi \, S_u \cdot R^2$$ **Step 7 — Express in terms of volume.** Using $V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3$, so $R^2 = \left(\dfrac{3V}{4\pi}\right)^{2/3}$: $$\left(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \propto 4\pi \, S_u \cdot \left(\dfrac{3V}{4\pi}\right)^{2/3}$$ $$\left(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \propto V^{-1/3} \quad \text{(since $S_u$ is a fuel property)}$$ **Step 8 — Cube root scaling.** Multiplying both sides by $V^{1/3}$: $$\left(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = K_{St} = \text{constant}$$ This is Bartknecht's **Cube Root Law**. The constant $K_{St}$ depends only on the fuel, the oxidiser, and the initial turbulence intensity, **not** on the vessel volume. ### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example > **Problem (KTU Style):** A deflagration test on aluminium dust in a **20-litre sphere** gives $(dP/dt)_max = 950$ bar/s. Estimate $(dP/dt)_max$ expected in a **1 m³** industrial silo containing the same dust cloud. **Given:** - $V_1 = 20$ L $= 0.020$ m³ - $\left(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max,1} = 950$ bar/s - $V_2 = 1$ m³ **Step 1 — Compute $K_{St}$.** $$K_{St} = \left(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max,1} \cdot V_1^{1/3} = 950 \times (0.020)^{1/3}$$ $$(0.020)^{1/3} = (2 \times 10^{-2})^{1/3} \approx 0.2714$$ $$K_{St} = 950 \times 0.2714 \approx 257.8 \;\; \text{bar·m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Classify.** Since $K_{St} \approx 258 > 200$, the dust is **St 2 — Strong explosion**. **Step 3 — Compute expected (dP/dt)_max in 1 m³ silo.** $$\left(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max,2} = \dfrac{K_{St}}{V_2^{1/3}} = \dfrac{257.8}{(1)^{1/3}} = 257.8 \;\; \text{bar/s}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation.** The pressure rise rate in the larger vessel is **much lower** (257.8 bar/s) than in the 20 L sphere (950 bar/s). This is why **vent design based on small-vessel (dP/dt)_max values would be grossly over-conservative** — the Cube Root Law is essential for realistic scaling. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — KTU Pattern Numerical Questions | Problem Type | Key Formula | Trap to Avoid | |--------------|-------------|---------------| | Find $K_{St}$ from 20 L test | $K_{St} = (dP/dt)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3}$ | Convert L to m³ **before** taking cube root | | Scale (dP/dt) to a new volume | $(dP/dt)_2 = K_{St} / V_2^{1/3}$ | Use $V$ in **m³**, not L | | Classify dust | Compare $K_{St}$ with 200, 300 bar·m/s | St 1 / St 2 / St 3 boundaries | | Compare two tests | Cube root law gives ratio | Ratio is $V_1^{-1/3} : V_2^{-1/3}$ | | Vent area (introductory) | $A_v = f(K_{St}, P_{red}, P_{stat})$ | Full NFPA 68 formula is in Module 4 | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — 20-Litre Siwek Sphere Deflagration Test Rig ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[Dust Sample Cup
0.6 L Pressurised] --> B1[Quick-Opening Valve
Delay 60 ms] B1 --> C1[20 L Spherical Vessel
Stainless Steel] A2[Air Reservoir
20 barg] --> A1 A3[Vacuum Pump] --> C1 C1 --> D1[Piezo Pressure Sensor 1
Flush Mounted] C1 --> D2[Piezo Pressure Sensor 2
Flush Mounted] C1 --> E1[Chemical Igniter
2 x 5 kJ at Centre] C1 --> F1[Water Cooling Jacket
20 to 25 deg C] D1 --> G1[Data Acquisition
1 kHz Sampling] D2 --> G1 G1 --> H1[PC with P-t Curve
P max and dPdt max Extraction] H1 --> I1[Result
P max, K St, Explosion Class] ``` ### 4.2 Functional Flow — Deflagration Test Procedure ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Step 1: Weigh Dust
5 to 10 g] --> S2[Step 2: Load into Cup] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Evacuate Sphere
to 0.4 bar abs] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Disperse Dust
20 barg Air Pulse] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Ignition Delay
60 ms] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Fire Igniter
10 kJ Total] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Record P-t Curve] S7 --> S8[Step 8: Extract P max
and dPdt max] S8 --> S9[Step 9: Compute K St] S9 --> S10[Step 10: Vary Concentration
Find Optimum] S10 --> S11[Step 11: Repeat 3 Times
Average Result] ``` ### 4.3 Schematic — Typical Pressure–Time Curve from a Deflagration Test ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Pt["P-t Curve for Dust Deflagration"] direction LR P0[Point 0: t = 0
P = 0 barg
Ignition] P1[Point 1: t approx 30 ms
Flame Radius r = 0.05 m
Pressure Rising] P2[Point 2: t approx 60 ms
r = 0.10 m
dPdt Increasing] P3[Point 3: P max approx 9 to 10 barg
dPdt = Max
Flame at Wall] P4[Point 4: t approx 250 ms
Cooling Phase
P Decaying] P0 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 end ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Engineering Use of Deflagration Test Data ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Deflagration Test
20 L Sphere] --> B[Extract P max] A --> C[Extract dPdt max] C --> D[Compute K St] D --> E[Classify Dust St 1, 2, 3] B --> F[NFPA 68 Vent Sizing] D --> F E --> F F --> G[Vent Area A v] G --> H[Equipment Selection
Flameless Vent, Isolation] H --> I[Plant Explosion Protection Plan] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** *Define deflagration. How does it differ from detonation in terms of flame propagation mechanism?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Deflagration** is a combustion process in which the flame front propagates through a combustible mixture at a velocity **less than the speed of sound** in the unreacted medium. (1 mark) - The propagation is driven primarily by **heat transfer (conduction and convection)** from the burnt gases to the unburnt gases ahead of the flame. (1 mark) - **Detonation**, in contrast, propagates at **supersonic** velocity and is driven by a leading **shock wave** that compresses and ignites the mixture ahead of the flame. (1 mark) --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State Bartknecht's cube root law. Why is it important in deflagration testing?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - Bartknecht's cube root law states that for a given fuel, $(dP/dt)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = K_{St}$, a constant. (1 mark) - It shows that the **rate of pressure rise is inversely proportional to the cube root of the vessel volume**: $(dP/dt)_{max} \propto V^{-1/3}$. (1 mark) - It is important because it allows **scaling of deflagration test data** from the laboratory 20 L sphere to full-scale industrial equipment (silos, dust collectors, etc.) for vent sizing and explosion protection design. (1 mark) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **ESE Module 3 — Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3 Choice A]** > *(a)* Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of the **20-litre Siwek sphere apparatus** used for deflagration testing of dusts. Mention the relevant **ASTM / ISO / VDI standards**. (7 marks) > *(b)* A deflagration test on **coal dust** in a 20 L sphere gives $(dP/dt)_{max} = 425$ bar/s at the optimum concentration. (i) Calculate the deflagration index $K_{St}$. (ii) Classify the dust explosion severity. (iii) Predict the expected $(dP/dt)_{max}$ in a **2 m³** hopper handling the same coal dust. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) 20-Litre Siwek Sphere Apparatus (7 marks):** - **Construction:** Spherical stainless steel vessel of internal volume **20 L** (0.020 m³). (1 mark) Sphere chosen for **isotropic flame propagation**. - **Ignition source:** Two chemical igniters of **5 kJ each** (total 10 kJ) located at the **centre** of the sphere. (1 mark) Strong central ignition ensures a uniform, outwardly propagating spherical flame. - **Dust dispersion:** A pressurised dust cup (0.6 L) connected via a quick-opening valve. Air at 20 bar(g) disperses dust into the evacuated sphere. (1 mark) - **Pressure measurement:** Two **piezoelectric pressure transducers** mounted flush with the inner wall, diametrically opposite, connected to a high-speed data acquisition system (≥ 1 kHz). (1 mark) - **Cooling:** A water jacket maintains the wall at 20–25 °C, ensuring repeatable isothermal conditions. (1 mark) - **Auxiliary:** Vacuum pump for cleaning, exhaust valve, safety rupture disc. (1 mark) - **Standards:** ASTM E1226, ISO 6184, VDI 3673. (1 mark) **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **(i) Compute $K_{St}$ (3 marks):** $$K_{St} = \left(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = 425 \times (0.020)^{1/3}$$ $$(0.020)^{1/3} = 0.2714$$ $$K_{St} = 425 \times 0.2714 \approx 115.3 \;\; \text{bar·m/s}$$ **(ii) Classify (2 marks):** Since $0 < K_{St} \le 200$ bar·m/s, the coal dust is classified as **St 1 — Weak explosion severity**. **(iii) Predict $(dP/dt)_{max}$ in 2 m³ hopper (2 marks):** $$\left(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max,2} = \dfrac{K_{St}}{V_2^{1/3}} = \dfrac{115.3}{(2)^{1/3}} = \dfrac{115.3}{1.2599} \approx 91.5 \;\; \text{bar/s}$$ The pressure rise rate in the larger industrial hopper is **substantially lower** than in the 20 L test vessel — confirming the need to apply the cube root law for realistic design. --- > **ESE Module 3 — Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]** > *(a)* Discuss the **explosion severity classes** (St 0, St 1, St 2, St 3) as per VDI 3673. Give **two industrial dust examples** for each class. (7 marks) > *(b)* Derive **Bartknecht's cube root law** starting from the assumption of a spherically expanding flame front of constant burning velocity $S_u$. State clearly each assumption and intermediate result. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Explosion Severity Classes (7 marks):** - **St 0 — Non-explosible:** $K_{St} = 0$ bar·m/s. Dusts do not propagate explosion. Examples: pure sodium chloride, calcium carbonate. (1.5 marks) - **St 1 — Weak explosion:** $0 < K_{St} \le 200$ bar·m/s. Examples: coal dust, sulphur, cocoa powder. (1.5 marks) - **St 2 — Strong explosion:** $200 < K_{St} \le 300$ bar·m/s. Examples: cellulose, wood dust, flour. (1.5 marks) - **St 3 — Very strong explosion:** $K_{St} > 300$ bar·m/s. Examples: aluminium powder, magnesium powder, toner dust. (1.5 marks) - **Engineering implication:** Higher the class, the larger the vent area required and the more stringent the explosion protection measures (e.g., isolation, suppression). (1 mark) **(b) Derivation of Cube Root Law (7 marks):** - **Assumption 1:** Closed spherical vessel of volume $V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3$. (0.5 mark) - **Assumption 2:** Spherical flame front expands from the centre at constant burning velocity $S_u$. (0.5 mark) - **Step 1:** Flame radius $r_f(t) = S_u \cdot t$. (1 mark) - **Step 2:** Burnt volume $V_b(t) = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi S_u^3 t^3$. (1 mark) - **Step 3:** Differentiate: $dV_b/dt = 4\pi S_u^3 t^2$. (1 mark) - **Step 4:** At maximum, flame at wall: $r_f = R$, so $t_{max} = R / S_u$. (1 mark) - **Step 5:** $(dP/dt)_{max} \propto 4\pi S_u R^2 \propto V^{-1/3}$. (1 mark) - **Step 6:** Hence $(dP/dt)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = K_{St} = \text{constant}$. (1 mark) --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in deflagration test questions:** > 1. **Unit trap:** Forgetting to convert litres to **m³** *before* applying the cube root law. The 20 L sphere has $V = 0.020$ m³, **not** 20. Many students lose 1–2 marks here. > 2. **Class confusion:** Mixing up $K_{St}$ dust classes (St 1/2/3) with gas explosion groups (IIA/IIB/IIC). They are unrelated classification systems. > 3. **Confusing $P_{max}$ with reduced pressure $P_{red}$:** $P_{max}$ is from a **closed-vessel** test; $P_{red}$ is the *target* pressure when a vent is fitted. Confusing them invalidates vent sizing. > 4. **Ignoring the ignition energy:** For low-$K_{St}$ dusts, the standard **10 kJ igniter** is essential — quoting 1 kJ ignition in your answer marks you down because the test value would be artificially low. > 5. **Skipping the derivation steps:** A derivation question is worth **7 marks** for a reason. Skipping intermediate algebra means losing 3–4 marks even if the final equation is correct. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Deflagration Test** - ✅ **Deflagration** = subsonic combustion driven by **heat transfer**; velocity typically **1–300 m/s**. - ✅ **Detonation** = supersonic combustion driven by a **shock wave**; different test apparatus entirely. - ✅ The **20 L Siwek sphere** is the **standard laboratory apparatus** per ASTM E1226, ISO 6184, VDI 3673. - ✅ **Three key outputs:** $P_{max}$ (bar(g)), $(dP/dt)_{max}$ (bar/s), and $K_{St}$ (bar·m/s). - ✅ **Cube Root Law:** $\left(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = K_{St}$ — the **most-tested formula** in this topic. - ✅ **Always convert $V$ to m³** before applying the cube root law. - ✅ **Dust classes (VDI 3673):** St 1 (≤ 200), St 2 (200–300), St 3 (> 300) bar·m/s. - ✅ **Gas symbol:** $K_G$ ; **Dust symbol:** $K_{St}$ ; both have same units. - ✅ Deflagration test data feed into **NFPA 68** vent sizing, **EN 14491**, and **ATEX** zoning. - ✅ Ignition energy: **10 kJ** total (2 × 5 kJ chemical igniters) at the **geometric centre**. - ✅ Pressure transducers: **flush-mounted, diametrically opposite**, sample rate **≥ 1 kHz**. - ✅ **Limitation of cube root law:** valid for spherical vessels and non-severely obstructed deflagrations only. - ✅ **For detonation studies → use a detonation tube**, not the 20 L sphere. - ✅ Engineering outputs drive **vent area design, isolation system selection, and ATEX equipment zoning**.

topics.title: Detonation Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Detonation Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Detonation Test** refers to a suite of standardized experimental and analytical procedures used in safety engineering to determine the **detonation velocity ($D$)**, **detonation pressure ($P_{CJ}$)**, **detonation temperature ($T_{CJ}$)**, and **detonability (sensitivity to detonation)** of a reactive gas mixture, vapor cloud, dust, or condensed-phase explosive material.

In the context of **Process Equipment Safety Design (PESFT412)**, the detonation test is a critical experimental tool used to:
- Characterize worst-case explosion scenarios for **blast-resistant vessel design**.
- Generate input data for **Chapman-Jouguet (C-J) calculations** used in pressure vessel relief design.
- Determine **detonation cell size ($\lambda$)** for flame arrester and explosion protection system specification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the Design for Safety module, the detonation test is studied as a precursor to the **TNT Equivalency Method** and the **Blast Wave Parameter Method** used in facility siting and structural design.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **conga line of dominoes** stretching across a long corridor:
- If the first domino falls slowly and pushes the next one gently (subsonic flame front), this is a **deflagration** — a gentle push.
- If the first domino creates a shockwave that instantly slams into the next one at supersonic speed, with each domino helping drive the shockwave stronger, this is a **detonation** — the shockwave is *coupled* with the energy release.

A **Detonation Test** is essentially a "high-speed camera on the conga line" — it measures exactly how fast that supersonic wall of destruction travels, and how hard it slams into a target.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in safety design:** A detonation can produce pressures of **15–20 bar** in gases and over **200,000 bar** in condensed explosives, compared to 7–8 bar in a deflagration. This 30x difference determines whether you need a simple rupture disc or a multi-layered blast wall.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (Gas) | Typical Range (Condensed) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detonation Velocity | $D$ | **1,500 – 2,500 m/s** | **5,000 – 9,000 m/s** |
| Detonation Pressure (C-J) | $P_{CJ}$ | **15 – 20 bar** | **100 – 300 kbar** |
| Detonation Temperature | $T_{CJ}$ | **2,500 – 3,500 K** | **3,000 – 5,000 K** |
| Cell Size | $\lambda$ | **0.5 – 50 mm** | N/A |
| Density Ratio | $\rho_2/\rho_1$ | **1.6 – 2.0** | **1.3 – 1.4** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background: Chapman-Jouguet (C-J) Theory

The Detonation Test is interpreted using the **Chapman-Jouguet (C-J) theory**, which assumes a **one-dimensional, steady-state** detonation where the reaction zone is treated as a discontinuity (an infinitesimally thin shock). The C-J condition states that the detonation velocity is the **minimum possible velocity** that satisfies conservation of mass, momentum, and energy across the shock front.

### 2.1.1 Governing Conservation Laws (Rankine-Hugoniot Equations)

For a detonation front moving into an unreacted medium (state 1) and leaving behind reacted products (state 2):

**1. Mass Conservation:**

$$\rho_1 (D - u_1) = \rho_2 (D - u_2)$$

**2. Momentum Conservation:**

$$P_2 - P_1 = \rho_1 (D - u_1)(u_2 - u_1)$$

**3. Energy Conservation (Rankine-Hugoniot):**

$$h_2 - h_1 = \frac{1}{2}(P_2 - P_1)\left(\frac{1}{\rho_1} - \frac{1}{\rho_2}\right)$$

where:
- $D$ = Detonation velocity (m/s)
- $u$ = Particle velocity (m/s)
- $h$ = Specific enthalpy (J/kg)
- $\rho$ = Density (kg/m³)
- $P$ = Pressure (Pa)
- Subscripts 1, 2 = Unreacted medium and reacted products, respectively.

### 2.1.2 The Rayleigh Line and Hugoniot Curve

The **Rayleigh line** represents the locus of all thermodynamically admissible states that satisfy mass and momentum conservation. The **Hugoniot curve** represents all states satisfying energy conservation. The intersection of these two curves (at the **tangent point** where the Rayleigh line is tangent to the Hugoniot curve) is the **C-J point**, which uniquely determines the detonation velocity.

## 2.2 Why the C-J Condition? (The Physical "Why")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The C-J condition emerges from the physical requirement that the **reaction zone must be sonic with respect to the burnt gases**. This means the leading shock and the trailing expansion fan cannot overtake each other — the detonation is **self-sustaining**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = V_{CJ} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma^2 - 1}{2\gamma}}$ for strong detonation approximation | Simplified C-J velocity (ideal gas) | m/s |
| 2 | $P_{CJ} = \frac{\rho_1 D^2}{\gamma + 1}$ | C-J Detonation pressure (strong detonation) | Pa |
| 3 | $\rho_{CJ} = \rho_1 \frac{\gamma + 1}{\gamma}$ | C-J Density | kg/m³ |
| 4 | $T_{CJ} = \frac{2\gamma (\gamma - 1) T_0}{(\gamma + 1)^2} + \frac{Q}{c_v}$ | C-J Temperature (with heat release $Q$) | K |
| 5 | $M_{CJ} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma^2 - 1}{2\gamma}}$ | C-J Mach number (relative to burnt gas) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $u_{CJ} = \frac{D}{\gamma + 1}$ | Particle velocity at C-J plane | m/s |
| 7 | $E_{exp} = \frac{(P_{CJ} - P_0) V}{\gamma - 1}$ | Explosion energy available for blast work | J |
| 8 | $W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot m \cdot Q_{ex}}{Q_{TNT}}$ | TNT equivalent mass | kg |
| 9 | $Z = \frac{R}{\sqrt[3]{W_{TNT}}}$ | Scaled distance for blast correlation | m/kg^(1/3) |
| 10 | $\lambda = $ cell size | Used in DDT (Deflagration-to-Detonation) studies | m |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU Formula Pitfall:** In the simplified C-J formulas above, $\gamma$ refers to the **specific heat ratio of the burnt (reacted) products**, NOT the unreacted gas. Using the wrong $\gamma$ is the single most common error in detonation pressure calculations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The data from detonation tests directly feeds into:

1. **Blast-Resistant Building Design:** Determining the design overpressure $P_{so}$ for shelters, control rooms, and firewalls in facilities handling explosive materials.
2. **Explosion Venting & Suppression:** Establishing whether a process vessel can withstand a deflagration or requires a **detonation arrester**.
3. **Facility Siting (QRA):** Computing TNT equivalent $W_{TNT}$ for consequence analysis in CCPS / API RP 752/753 frameworks.
4. **Flame Arrester Specification:** Detonation cell size $\lambda$ determines the **Experimental Safe Gap (MESG)** and arrester mesh requirements.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: C-J Detonation Velocity for an Ideal Gas (Strong Detonation)

We start from the Rankine-Hugoniot jump conditions for a planar, steady detonation wave in a stoichiometric reactive ideal gas mixture.

### Step 1 — Apply Conservation of Mass Across the Shock Front

Let the detonation front move with velocity $D$ into a stationary unreacted gas ($u_1 = 0$). The mass flux is conserved:

$$\rho_1 D = \rho_2 (D - u_2)$$

Rearranging for the particle velocity $u_2$ in the burnt gas:

$$u_2 = D \left(1 - \frac{\rho_1}{\rho_2}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Apply Conservation of Momentum

The pressure jump across the front:

$$P_2 - P_1 = \rho_1 D u_2$$

Substituting $u_2$ from Step 1:

$$P_2 - P_1 = \rho_1 D^2 \left(1 - \frac{\rho_1}{\rho_2}\right) = D^2 \rho_1^2 \left(\frac{1}{\rho_1} - \frac{1}{\rho_2}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Define the Rayleigh Line

Define the parameter $j \equiv \rho_1 D = \rho_2 (D - u_2)$ (mass flux per unit area). Then the Rayleigh line in $P$–$v$ space (where $v = 1/\rho$ is specific volume) is:

$$P_2 - P_1 = j^2 (v_1 - v_2)$$

This is a straight line in the $P$–$v$ plane, passing through the initial state $(P_1, v_1)$ with slope $-j^2$.

### Step 4 — Apply the Hugoniot Energy Equation

For an ideal gas with constant $\gamma$, the specific internal energy is $e = c_v T = \frac{P v}{\gamma - 1}$. The Hugoniot equation for a detonation (with heat release $Q$ per unit mass) is:

$$\frac{P_2 v_2}{\gamma - 1} - \frac{P_1 v_1}{\gamma - 1} = Q + \frac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_2)(v_1 - v_2)$$

> This equation represents the conservation of energy across the shock, accounting for both mechanical work and the chemical energy release $Q$.

### Step 5 — Apply the C-J Condition (Tangency)

The C-J condition is the **tangency of the Rayleigh line to the Hugoniot curve** at point 2. Mathematically:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_{Rayleigh} = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_{Hugoniot}$$

This is equivalent to requiring that the **flow behind the C-J plane is sonic**, i.e., $D - u_2 = a_2$, where $a_2 = \sqrt{\gamma P_2 v_2}$ is the local sound speed in the burnt gas.

### Step 6 — Derive the C-J Mach Number

For the strong detonation limit ($P_2 \gg P_1$, valid for typical gas-phase detonations), the tangency condition yields:

$$M_{CJ}^2 = \frac{(\gamma - 1)^2}{2\gamma} \cdot \frac{P_2}{P_1}$$

In the high-pressure limit where $P_2/P_1 \to \infty$ (corresponding to a strong, fully self-sustained detonation), this reduces to:

$$M_{CJ}^2 = \frac{\gamma^2 - 1}{2\gamma}$$

### Step 7 — Express Detonation Velocity in Terms of Initial Sound Speed

Since the unreacted gas is at rest, the detonation velocity is related to the C-J Mach number (relative to the burnt gas) by:

$$D = a_1 \cdot M_D = a_1 \cdot M_{CJ} \cdot \frac{\gamma+1}{2}$$

Wait — this is an approximation step that requires careful handling. For a **strong detonation** in a gas initially at low pressure:

$$D = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1) Q}$$

This elegant result shows that the detonation velocity depends **only on the heat of reaction and the specific heat ratio**, not on the initial pressure or density (to first order).

### Step 8 — Compute Detonation Pressure

Substituting back into the momentum equation with $P_1 \ll P_2$:

$$P_{CJ} = \frac{\rho_1 D^2}{\gamma + 1}$$

### Worked Numerical Example: Stoichiometric Hydrogen-Air Detonation

**Given:**
- $\gamma = 1.4$ (typical for diatomic burnt gases)
- $Q = 2.42 \times 10^6$ J/kg (heat of reaction for 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O)
- $\rho_1 = 1.2$ kg/m³ (air at STP)

**Step 1:** Calculate detonation velocity using Step 7:

$$D = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1) Q} = \sqrt{2(1.4^2 - 1)(2.42 \times 10^6)}$$

$$\gamma^2 - 1 = 1.96 - 1 = 0.96$$

$$2 \times 0.96 \times 2.42 \times 10^6 = 4.6464 \times 10^6$$

$$D = \sqrt{4.6464 \times 10^6} \approx 2155.4 \text{ m/s}$$

> This matches the experimentally measured hydrogen-air detonation velocity of **~2,000–2,100 m/s** to within 5% — a good confirmation of C-J theory.

**Step 2:** Calculate C-J pressure:

$$P_{CJ} = \frac{\rho_1 D^2}{\gamma + 1} = \frac{1.2 \times (2155.4)^2}{2.4}$$

$$P_{CJ} = \frac{1.2 \times 4.646 \times 10^6}{2.4} = \frac{5.575 \times 10^6}{2.4} \approx 2.32 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{CJ} \approx 2.32 \text{ MPa} \approx 23.2 \text{ bar}}$$

**Step 3:** Compare to deflagration peak pressure (typically 7–8 bar):

$$\text{Detonation-to-Deflagration Pressure Ratio} = \frac{23.2}{7.5} \approx 3.1$$

> For higher hydrocarbons and condensed explosives, this ratio can exceed **30:1**, justifying the use of specialized detonation-resistant designs in chemical plants.

## 3.2 Derivation: TNT Equivalency from Detonation Energy

When a detonation test yields the total chemical energy release $E_{det}$ (in Joules), the equivalent mass of TNT is:

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot E_{det}}{Q_{TNT}}$$

where:
- $\eta$ = Empirical yield factor (0.05–0.10 typical for vapor cloud; 1.0 for pure explosive)
- $Q_{TNT} = 4.184 \times 10^6$ J/kg = Heat of detonation of TNT

**Example:** A stoichiometric propane-air cloud with $E_{det} = 5 \times 10^9$ J:

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{0.05 \times 5 \times 10^9}{4.184 \times 10^6} \approx 59.7 \text{ kg TNT}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define "detonation" and distinguish it from "deflagration" in the context of safety design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **detonation** is a supersonic combustion wave (Mach number typically 5–10) in which the flame front is coupled to a leading shock wave, with the shock wave compressing and igniting the unreacted medium, and the energy release from combustion reinforcing the shock. **Detonation velocity is typically 1,500–9,000 m/s** depending on the medium.

In contrast, a **deflagration** is a subsonic combustion wave (velocity < 100 m/s in gases) where flame propagation occurs primarily by thermal conduction and diffusion, without shock coupling. The peak pressure in a deflagration is limited to approximately 7–8 times the initial pressure, while a detonation can reach **15–20 bar in gases** and over 200 kbar in condensed explosives.

From a safety design perspective, the distinction is critical because a detonation scenario requires **detonation arresters, blast walls, and reinforced structures**, whereas a deflagration can be managed by simple venting per **NFPA 68**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Note (KTU 2024):** Award 1 mark for each of: (a) correct definition of detonation, (b) correct definition of deflagration, (c) correct numerical contrast and safety implication.

---

### Question 2: Chapman-Jouguet Condition `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: State and briefly explain the Chapman-Jouguet (C-J) condition. Why is it important in detonation test interpretation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Chapman-Jouguet (C-J) condition** states that at the C-J plane, the flow velocity of the burnt gases relative to the detonation front is **exactly sonic** (i.e., equal to the local speed of sound in the products). Mathematically:

$$D - u_{CJ} = a_{CJ}$$

This condition corresponds to the **tangency point** of the Rayleigh line and the Hugoniot curve in the $P$–$v$ plane.

It is important in detonation test interpretation because:
1. It **uniquely determines** the detonation velocity, pressure, density, and temperature from the thermodynamic properties of the unreacted gas and the heat of reaction.
2. It represents the **minimum self-sustained detonation velocity** — actual detonations observed in tests propagate at or above the C-J velocity.
3. It provides the **analytical foundation** for converting measured pressure/velocity data from detonation tests into design parameters (C-J pressure for vessel design, C-J velocity for blast correlation).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme** regulations, Part B questions on a single module are answered with internal choice. The two alternatives below are designed to be of comparable difficulty and mark distribution.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q (a):** With a neat sketch, describe the **experimental setup and procedure** for conducting a detonation test in a gaseous fuel-air mixture. Explain how **detonation velocity** is measured. **(7 marks)**

**(b):** A stoichiometric methane-air mixture ($\gamma = 1.35$, $Q = 2.8 \times 10^6$ J/kg, $\rho_1 = 1.18$ kg/m³) is detonated in a test tube. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
1. The C-J detonation velocity $D$.
2. The C-J detonation pressure $P_{CJ}$.
3. The TNT equivalent mass if a 50 kg methane cloud detonates completely, given $\eta = 0.05$ and $Q_{TNT} = 4.184 \times 10^6$ J/kg.

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The detonation test is conducted in a long, closed, high-strength steel tube, typically 10–50 m in length and 50–300 mm in diameter, designed to withstand the high pressures generated. The test setup has four main sections:

**1. Gas Preparation Section (2 marks):**
- Fuel gas and air (or oxygen) are metered through calibrated rotameters and mixed in a stainless-steel mixing chamber.
- A vacuum pump evacuates the tube to < 0.1 bar before filling to ensure atmospheric contamination is eliminated.
- The mixture is allowed to equilibrate for 15–30 minutes to ensure homogeneity; this is critical because any gradient in composition will cause the detonation to fail (cellular structure becomes irregular).

**2. Detonation Tube Section (2 marks):**
