**Step 3 — Circuit Connection (2 marks):** (Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1)
- Connect the variac → step-down transformer → rheostat → CT primary → relay trip coil in series.
- Ensure the CT secondary is shorted through the relay burden for safety.
- Connect the ammeter in series with the test loop.

**Step 4 — Test Execution (2 marks):**
- Switch ON the variac with the output at minimum.
- Gradually increase the variac output voltage.
- The rheostat allows fine control of test current.
- Note the current value at which the relay contacts just close → this is the **pick-up current** $I_{pickup}$.
- Record the operating time using the digital timer.
- Repeat the test 3 times and take the average for accuracy.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $V_{LL} = 11$ kV, Frequency = 50 Hz
- $CTR = 200 / 5 = 40$
- $I_{F,min} = 800$ A (primary)
- Plug setting = 125% = 1.25

**Step 1 — Secondary pick-up current (2 marks):**

$$
I_{pickup,\,sec} = 1.25 \times 5 \text{ A} = 6.25 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2 — Primary pick-up current (2 marks):**

$$
I_{pickup,\,pri} = 6.25 \times 40 = 250 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3 — Sensitivity factor (2 marks):**

$$
K_s = \frac{I_{F,min}}{I_{pickup,\,pri}} = \frac{800}{250} = 3.2
$$

**Step 4 — Comment (1 mark):**
Since $K_s = 3.2 > 1.5$, the protection is **highly sensitive** and adequate. The relay will positively operate for the minimum fault.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Question]**

**(a)** Define **sensitivity factor**. Explain its significance in grading a protection scheme. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 220 kV, 50 Hz transmission line has a 3-phase fault current of 5000 A and a single line-to-ground fault current of 3200 A at the remote bus. The line is protected by a relay with CT ratio 1200/1 and a pick-up setting of 1.0 A secondary. Determine the **sensitivity factor** for the SLG fault and comment on the protection. If inadequate, suggest a remedial measure. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The sensitivity factor $K_s$ of a protective relay is the ratio of the minimum fault current in the protected zone to the relay pick-up current:

$$
K_s = \frac{I_{F,min}}{I_{pickup}}
$$

**Significance (5 marks):**
- *Adequacy of protection:* $K_s \geq 1.5$ ensures reliable operation for the smallest fault.
- *Discrimination:* Allows primary and backup grading — backup relays may have lower $K_s$ (≥ 1.2) without compromising system safety.
- *Co-ordination:* Sensitivity analysis is done in conjunction with time-grading and CT performance checks.
- *Setting optimisation:* If $K_s$ is too low, the pick-up setting can be lowered; if too high (say > 5), the relay may maloperate for through-faults, and the setting must be increased.
- *Standard reference:* IEC 60255-151, IEEE C37.91.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $I_{F,min}^{(SLG)} = 3200$ A (primary)
- $CTR = 1200 / 1 = 1200$
- $I_{pickup,\,sec} = 1.0$ A

**Step 1 — Primary pick-up (2 marks):**

$$
I_{pickup,\,pri} = 1.0 \times 1200 = 1200 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2 — Sensitivity factor (2 marks):**

$$
K_s = \frac{3200}{1200} = 2.667
$$

**Step 3 — Comment (1 mark):**
$K_s = 2.667 > 1.5$ → **Protection is adequate and sensitive.** ✅

**Step 4 — Hypothetical remedial measure (2 marks):**
*Had* $K_s$ been < 1.5, the remedial measures would have been:
- Reduce the pick-up setting (e.g., from 1.0 A to 0.8 A),
- Change CT ratio to a lower value (e.g., 800/1),
- Add a dedicated **sensitive earth-fault (SEF) unit** with a pick-up of 0.1–0.2 A secondary,
- Use a numerical relay with **sensitive directional earth-fault** feature (quadrilateral characteristic).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting CT ratio conversion** — A student calculates $K_s$ using the secondary current only, getting a falsely high value. Always convert the fault current to the same side as the pick-up current.
> 2. **Using the wrong reference fault** — Some students use the 3-phase fault current (which is the *maximum*) instead of the SLG fault (the *minimum*). This inflates $K_s$ and gives an incorrect "pass" verdict.
> 3. **Ignoring the 1.5 threshold** — Reporting $K_s = 1.3$ as "sensitive" without checking against 1.5 loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up PSM and pick-up** — PSM is a multiplier; the actual pick-up current = PSM × (CT secondary rating). Don't quote PSM as the pick-up current.
> 5. **No units in final answer** — Always write "A" for currents; $K_s$ is dimensionless.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Sensitiveness** = relay's ability to detect the **minimum** fault current in its protected zone.
- ✅ **Reference fault** = **Single Line-to-Ground (SLG)** at the **far end**, under **minimum generation**.
- ✅ **Sensitivity factor** $K_s = \dfrac{I_{F,min}}{I_{pickup}}$ — a dimensionless number.
- ✅ **Acceptance criterion:** $K_s \geq 1.5$ (primary), $\geq 1.2$ (backup).
- ✅ **Three steps in test:** (i) determine $I_{F,min}$ from system study, (ii) measure relay $I_{pickup}$ via primary injection, (iii) compute and verify $K_s$.
- ✅ **Equipment:** Variac, current-injection transformer, rheostat, ammeter, digital timer.
- ✅ **CT polarity & secondary burden** must be correct during the test or readings will be wrong.
- ✅ **High-impedance (Hi-Z) faults** are the *real* test of sensitivity — they produce currents only marginally above load current.
- ✅ **Numerical relays** offer better sensitivity (pick-up 0.5–2% of rated) compared to electro-mechanical relays.
- ✅ **Inadequate sensitivity** → adjust pick-up, change CT ratio, or add a sensitive earth-fault unit.
- ✅ **Standards:** IEC 60255-151, IEEE C37.2, IEEE C37.91.
- ✅ **Always document** the test results in the relay test sheet with date, ambient temperature, and observer's signature (CBIP / GETCO format).
- ✅ **For 14-mark KTU questions,** present a labelled block diagram, write the formula symbolically, substitute numerical values, box the final answer, and add a one-line engineering comment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact Sensitiveness Test (BAM) and Friction Sensitiveness Test (BAM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Impact Sensitiveness Test (BAM) and Friction Sensitiveness Test (BAM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 BAM — What does it stand for?

**BAM** = **Bundesanstalt für Materialforschung und -prüfung** (the German Federal Institute for Materials Research and Testing). The BAM tests are internationally standardized methods (adopted by the UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods, the UN Manual of Tests and Criteria, and broadly by ASTM / NATO / ISO frameworks) for classifying the **impact** and **friction sensitivity** of energetic (explosive) substances, pyrotechnic compositions, and oxidizing materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PESFT412 / Module 3
> The BAM Fallhammer (Impact Sensitiveness) Test and the BAM Friction Test together form the cornerstone of **explosive hazard classification** under the **UN Series 3 Tests (Tests 3(a)(i) and 3(b)(i))**. Mastery of these two tests is essential for the Design for Safety course because they quantitatively determine whether a substance is too dangerous for commercial transport, storage, or handling.

---

### 1.2 BAM Fallhammer Test — Impact Sensitiveness

#### Formal Definition

The **BAM Fallhammer Test** (also called the **BAM drop-hammer test** or **BAM impact test**) is a standardized laboratory procedure to determine the **impact sensitivity** of a substance by dropping a known mass from a known height onto a confined sample and recording the lowest impact energy at which at least one of six trials yields a positive response (audible detonation, visible flame, or smoke).

The **limiting impact energy** (the test result) is reported as:

$$E_{\text{lim}} = m \cdot g \cdot h$$

where **m** is the drop mass (kg), **g = 9.81 m/s²** is the acceleration due to gravity, and **h** is the drop height (m).

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine tapping a glass bottle with a small hammer: a gentle tap does nothing, but a hard, sudden tap may shatter it. Some chemicals behave similarly — they are perfectly safe under slow or gentle stimuli but may explode when struck sharply. The BAM Fallhammer is the **engineering "tap-test"** that tells us the smallest "tap" needed to detonate the material. Lower energy = more dangerous; higher energy = safer to handle.

---

### 1.3 BAM Friction Test — Friction Sensitiveness

#### Formal Definition

The **BAM Friction Test** (also called the **BAM friction sensitivity test**) is a standardized laboratory procedure to determine the **friction sensitivity** of a solid substance by placing a defined mass on a porcelain pin, rubbing it across a porcelain plate under a defined normal force, and identifying the lowest normal force at which at least one of six trials yields a positive response (crackling, flame, smoke, or explosion).

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of striking a match against a rough surface: the **heat of friction** ignites the chemicals. Some explosives are also "match-like" — they can be ignited simply by grinding or rubbing against each other. The BAM Friction Test tells us how hard the substance can be scraped before it explodes. If it explodes under gentle rubbing (a small force), the substance is extremely dangerous; if a large, heavy force is needed, it is relatively safer.

> [!NOTE]
> Both BAM tests use **"6-trial" statistical logic**: the **limiting value** is the lowest stimulus at which **at least 1 of 6 trials** is positive, but at which **at least 4 of 6 trials** are *still negative*. This is deliberately a conservative, non-aggressive criterion — it prevents under-classification of hazardous materials.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 BAM Fallhammer — Apparatus and Procedure

**Apparatus Components (referenced from UN Test 3(a)(i) / BAM Standard):**

- A drop-weight (hammer) of standard mass: **1 kg, 2 kg, 5 kg, or 10 kg**.
- A vertical guide rail with a graduated height scale.
- A striking anvil with two coaxial **hardened-steel cylinders** held in a steel cup; the sample is sandwiched between them.
- A retaining/release mechanism for reproducible drop heights.
- An acoustic + visual detection system (ear + observation; sometimes a Go/No-Go pressure transducer).

**Procedure (Step-by-Step Logic):**

1. A **defined sample volume** — typically **40 mm³** for solids (≈ 40–50 mg) or **40 µL** for liquids — is placed between the two steel cylinders in the cup assembly.
2. The cup assembly is positioned on the anvil.
3. A drop weight of selected mass **m** is raised to a selected height **h** and released.
4. The operator observes whether a **positive result** occurs — defined as:
   - an **audible explosion / detonation**, OR
   - **visible flame**, OR
   - **smoke / decomposition**,
   - OR evidence of damage on the cylinders (pitting, blackening).
5. **Six trials** are performed at the chosen energy.
6. The energy is progressively **reduced** until no positives occur in 6 trials. The lowest energy at which **at least 1 of 6** is positive is recorded as the **limiting impact energy (E_lim)**.
7. Confirmatory run: at the limiting energy, **at least 1 positive but not all 6 positive** (else the energy must be increased).

**Key Engineering Logic — the "Why":**

- Energy is the physically meaningful stimulus because both deformation energy and Hertzian contact stress scale with it.
- Six trials provide statistical robustness while remaining logistically feasible.
- Steel-on-steel contact simulates real-world handling incidents (dropping, pinching, machining).

---

### 2.2 BAM Friction Test — Apparatus and Procedure

**Apparatus Components (referenced from UN Test 3(b)(i) / BAM Standard):**

- A **porcelain plate** (60 mm × 25 mm × 5 mm, rough-ground).
- A **porcelain pin** (10 mm diameter, hemispherical tip).
- A loading mechanism to apply controlled normal force.
- A reciprocating drive unit that moves the plate **10 mm** back and forth at a controlled rate (one stroke ≈ 0.5 s).
- Loading weights applied via a lever arm — selectable standard loads.

**Standard Loading Forces (N):**

$$F \in \{5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 80, 100, 120, 160, 200, 240, 300, 360\} \, \text{N}$$

**Procedure:**

1. A small sample (≈ 10 mm³) is spread in a thin layer on the porcelain plate.
2. The porcelain pin is lowered onto the sample and pressed down with the selected normal load **F**.
3. The plate reciprocates once (forward + backward = 1 stroke; multiple strokes up to **6 trials**).
4. A **positive result** is crackling, sparks, flame, smoke, or visible decomposition.
5. Six trials at each load; the lowest load at which **≥ 1 of 6** is positive is the **limiting load (F_lim)**.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Impact Energy | $E$ | $E = m \cdot g \cdot h$ | Joules (J) | $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ |
| Drop Mass | $m$ | $m \in \{1, 2, 5, 10\}$ | kg | Standard BAM masses |
| Drop Height | $h$ | $h \in [0.05, 1.00]$ | m | Vernier scale |
| Limiting Impact Energy | $E_{\text{lim}}$ | lowest $E$ with $\geq 1/6$ positive | J | Report value |
| Limiting Friction Load | $F_{\text{lim}}$ | lowest $F$ with $\geq 1/6$ positive | N | Report value |
| Friction Stroke Length | $L$ | $L = 10$ | mm | Fixed |
| Normal Pressure at Pin Tip | $p$ | $p = F \,/\, A_{\text{tip}}$ | N/mm² or Pa | Informational |
| Hazard Class (UN) | — | $E_{\text{lim}} \leq 2 \text{ J}$ & $F_{\text{lim}} \leq 80 \text{ N}$ | — | **"Too sensitive" — forbidden for transport** |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU High-Yield Pointer — When asked to *calculate* impact energy, students frequently forget the **mass of the hammer itself**. The drop weight **m** in the equation $E=mgh$ refers **only to the falling hammer**, not the anvil or sample.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hazardous-material classification** for transport, storage, and shipping (UN Class 1, 5.1).
- **Process safety** in explosives / pyrotechnics / propellant manufacturing.
- **Defense and ammunition safety** — a substance that detonates under 2 J of impact is militarily unusable because handling accidents would be catastrophic.
- **Civilian industries** — airbag inflators, firework manufacturing, mining explosives — all require BAM-equivalent certification.
- **Industrial chemistry** — assessment of ammonium nitrate, peroxides, and nitro-compounds.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Impact Energy Equation

The potential energy of a raised body of mass **m** at height **h** above a reference is, by classical mechanics:

$$U = W_{\text{gravity}} = \int_0^h F \, dy = \int_0^h m g \, dy$$

Because **g** is approximately constant near Earth's surface:

$$U = m g \int_0^h dy = m g h$$

On release, the hammer falls (ideally with negligible air drag), so all of this gravitational potential energy converts into kinetic energy just before impact:

$$E_{\text{kin}} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2 = m g h$$

Therefore the **impact energy delivered to the sample is**:

$$E_{\text{impact}} = m g h$$

Expressed in standard SI units, with **m in kg**, **g = 9.81 m/s²**, and **h in metres**, $E_{\text{impact}}$ is in **Joules**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style

> **Problem:** A BAM Fallhammer test uses a **2 kg** drop weight released from a height of **0.50 m** on a sample of an unknown energetic material. Of 6 trials, 2 produce audible explosions. The previous lower-energy run (1 kg × 0.30 m) produced 0 positives in 6 trials. Calculate the **impact energy** at the positive run and interpret.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$$m = 2 \text{ kg}, \quad g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2, \quad h = 0.50 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the impact-energy formula.**

$$E = m \cdot g \cdot h$$

$$E = 2 \times 9.81 \times 0.50$$

$$E = 9.81 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the previous (negative) trial energy.**

$$E_{\text{prev}} = 1 \times 9.81 \times 0.30 = 2.943 \text{ J}$$

**Step 4 — Check the BAM "1-of-6-but-not-6-of-6" rule.**

We have 2 positives out of 6 trials at 9.81 J. This satisfies:
- ≥ 1 positive (✓)
- < 6 positive (✓)

The previous run (0/6 at 2.943 J) confirms the lower bound.

**Step 5 — Report.**

> **Limiting Impact Energy, $E_{\text{lim}} = 9.81 \text{ J}$**

**Step 6 — Interpret for hazard classification (UN Series 3):**

Since $E_{\text{lim}} > 2 \text{ J}$, the substance is **not classified as too-sensitive for transport** on the basis of impact alone. (Friction data must also be evaluated — see §3.3.)

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — BAM Friction Test

> **Problem:** A BAM Friction Test is conducted on a powdered pyrotechnic composition. At a normal load of **60 N**, 1 of 6 trials shows crackling. At 80 N, 4 of 6 trials show crackling. At 40 N, 0 of 6 trials show crackling. Determine the **limiting load** and discuss classification.

**Step 1 — Identify the lowest load with ≥ 1/6 positive.**

40 N → 0/6 (negative)
60 N → 1/6 (positive — first positive)
80 N → 4/6 (positive)

**Step 2 — Confirm BAM rule.**

At 60 N we have exactly 1 positive (≥ 1 ✓) and 5 negatives (< 6 ✓).

**Step 3 — Report.**

$$F_{\text{lim}} = 60 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — UN classification check.**

Since $F_{\text{lim}} < 80 \text{ N}$, the substance is **"too sensitive to friction"** for unrestricted transport under UN Series 3.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Examiner's Common Mistake — Students often pick the *highest* load at which a positive occurred (here, 80 N) as the limiting value. The BAM limiting value is **the LOWEST load with ≥ 1/6 positive**. This single-point error can cost 2–3 marks.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Combining Impact + Friction Results

| $E_{\text{lim}}$ (J) | $F_{\text{lim}}$ (N) | Combined Hazard Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| $\leq 2$ | $\leq 80$ | **Forbidden for transport** — extreme sensitivity on both axes |
| $\leq 2$ | $> 80$ | Forbidden by impact alone — investigate further |
| $> 2$ | $\leq 80$ | Forbidden by friction alone — investigate further |
| $> 2$ | $> 80$ | Likely transportable; proceed to UN Class 1 assignment tests |
| $> 10$ | $> 360$ | Very insensitive — may qualify as "blast agent" or oxidizer rather than explosive |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 BAM Fallhammer — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Prep: 40 mm³ solid / 40 µL liquid] --> B[Place between two steel cylinders in cup]
    B --> C[Position cup on anvil block]
    C --> D[Select drop mass: 1, 2, 5, or 10 kg]
    D --> E[Set drop height via vernier scale 50–1000 mm]
    E --> F[Release hammer — free fall under gravity]
    F --> G[Impact delivered to sample]
    G --> H{Result positive?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Record audible explosion / flame / smoke]
    H -- No  --> J[Record no reaction]
    I --> K[Repeat for total of 6 trials at this energy]
    J --> K
    K --> L{At least 1 of 6 positive?}
    L -- Yes --> M{All 6 positive?}
    L -- No  --> N[Reduce energy — return to step E]
    M -- Yes --> N
    M -- No  --> O[Report E_lim = m × g × h]
```

### 4.2 BAM Friction Apparatus — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Prep: 10 mm³ thin layer] --> B[Spread on porcelain plate]
    B --> C[Select loading force from F-set: 5–360 N]
    C --> D[Lower porcelain pin onto sample]
    D --> E[Drive unit reciprocates plate — 10 mm stroke]
    E --> F{Result positive? crackle / spark / flame / smoke}
    F -- Yes --> G[Record positive event]
    F -- No  --> H[Record negative event]
    G --> I[Repeat for 6 trials at this load]
    H --> I
    I --> J{At least 1 of 6 positive?}
    J -- Yes --> K{All 6 positive?}
    J -- No  --> L[Reduce load — return to step C]
    K -- Yes --> L
    K -- No  --> M[Report F_lim]
```

### 4.3 Combined Hazard Decision Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I[BAM Fallhammer E_lim] --> J{E_lim ≤ 2 J}
    J -- Yes --> X[Impact-Hazardous]
    J -- No  --> Y[Impact-Acceptable]
    F[BAM Friction F_lim] --> K{F_lim ≤ 80 N}
    K -- Yes --> Z[Friction-Hazardous]
    K -- No  --> W[Friction-Acceptable]
    X --> R{Final Verdict}
    Y --> R
    Z --> R
    W --> R
    R --> S{Any axis hazardous}
    S -- Yes --> T[Restrict / Re-design / Re-test]
    S -- No  --> U[Proceed to UN Class 1 categorization]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define the BAM Fallhammer Test. What is meant by "limiting impact energy"?*

**Model Answer:**

The **BAM Fallhammer Test** is a standardized laboratory method for determining the **impact sensitivity** of an energetic substance. A drop weight of standard mass (1, 2, 5, or 10 kg) is released from a known height onto a small sample confined between two hardened-steel cylinders. The result is observed for audible explosion, flame, or smoke.

The **limiting impact energy ($E_{\text{lim}}$)** is the **lowest drop energy** (calculated as $E = mgh$) at which **at least one of six trials** produces a positive result, while **at least five of six** remain negative at the same energy. It is the standard quantitative output used for UN hazard classification.

> [!NOTE]
> Marks: Definition 2 marks + Limiting-energy explanation 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest]**
*State the standard loading-force range used in the BAM Friction Test and explain the meaning of "limiting load".*

**Model Answer:**

The standard BAM Friction Test loading forces are:

$$F \in \{5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 80, 100, 120, 160, 200, 240, 300, 360\} \text{ N}$$

The **limiting load ($F_{\text{lim}}$)** is the **lowest normal force** applied between the porcelain pin and the porcelain plate at which **at least one of six trials** gives a positive result (crackling, flame, sparks, smoke). It is reported in Newtons and is the key output used for classifying friction sensitivity under UN Test 3(b)(i).

> [!NOTE]
> Marks: Force range listing 2 marks + Limiting-load concept 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module-Internal Choice)

---

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, S3 B.Tech Safety]**

**(a)** Describe the BAM Fallhammer Test in detail, including the apparatus, sample preparation, procedure, and the "6-trial" criterion for reporting the limiting impact energy. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** A sample is tested with a **5 kg** drop weight at a height of **0.30 m**. The result is positive in 3 of 6 trials. The previous lower-energy run with a **2 kg** hammer from **0.20 m** gave 0 positives in 6 trials. Calculate the limiting impact energy and comment on its hazard significance under UN classification. **[(7 marks)]**

##### Model Solution

**(a)** Apparatus consists of a vertical guide rail, a drop weight (1, 2, 5, 10 kg), a sample cup with two hardened-steel cylinders, and a release mechanism. A defined sample volume (40 mm³ for solids) is sandwiched between the cylinders. The hammer is raised to a selected height (0.05–1.00 m) and released; energy delivered = $mgh$. Six trials are run at each energy. The limiting impact energy is the lowest energy at which **≥ 1 of 6** trials gives a positive result (explosion, flame, smoke) while **< 6 of 6** are positive. Six-trial logic reduces false negatives and provides statistical robustness. Lower limiting energies → more sensitive → greater hazard.

**(b)** *Step 1 — Identify parameters of the positive run:*

$$m = 5 \text{ kg}, \quad h = 0.30 \text{ m}, \quad g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$E_{\text{positive}} = 5 \times 9.81 \times 0.30 = 14.715 \text{ J}$$

*Step 2 — Verify previous (negative) run:*

$$E_{\text{prev}} = 2 \times 9.81 \times 0.20 = 3.924 \text{ J}$$

*Step 3 — Apply BAM rule.*

Positive run has 3/6 positive (≥ 1 ✓; not all 6 ✓). Previous run 0/6.

*Step 4 — Report.*

$$E_{\text{lim}} = 14.715 \text{ J} \approx 14.7 \text{ J}$$

*Step 5 — Hazard interpretation.*

Since $E_{\text{lim}} \gg 2 \text{ J}$, the substance is **not impact-hazardous** under UN classification. Normal handling precautions suffice; full UN Class 1 testing still required for transport approval.

> [!WARNING]
> Examiner's Pitfall — Many students forget to verify the "previous run" giving 0/6 at a lower energy. **3 marks are reserved** for confirming that $E_{\text{lim}}$ is indeed the *lowest* positive energy. Omitting this loses marks.

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Describe the BAM Friction Test in detail. Explain how the limiting load is determined and how it is used in UN hazard classification. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** In a BAM Friction Test, the following data are obtained:

| Load (N) | Positives / 6 trials |
|---|---|
| 30 | 0 |
| 50 | 1 |
| 80 | 3 |
| 120 | 6 |

Determine the limiting friction load and explain whether the substance should be classified as too-sensitive for transport. **[(7 marks)]**

##### Model Solution

**(a)** The BAM Friction Test uses a porcelain pin pressed onto a porcelain plate bearing a thin layer of sample. Normal loads from 5 N to 360 N are applied through a lever. The plate reciprocates 10 mm. Six trials at each load. Limiting load = lowest load with ≥ 1 positive but < 6 positives. UN classification: if $F_{\text{lim}} \leq 80$ N, the substance is "too friction-sensitive" for unrestricted transport under UN Test 3(b)(i).

**(b)** *Step 1 — Tabulate cumulative positives:*

30 N → 0/6 (negative) — below threshold
50 N → 1/6 (positive — first positive)
80 N → 3/6 (positive)
120 N → 6/6 (all positive — invalid)

*Step 2 — Apply the BAM "≥ 1 but not all 6" rule.*

At **50 N**, we have exactly 1/6 positive: **valid** limiting load.
At 80 N, 3/6 — also valid, but not the *lowest*.
At 120 N, 6/6 — invalid by BAM rule (must reduce load).

*Step 3 — Report.*

$$\boxed{F_{\text{lim}} = 50 \text{ N}}$$

*Step 4 — Hazard interpretation.*

Since $F_{\text{lim}} = 50 \text{ N} < 80 \text{ N}$, the substance is classified as **too friction-sensitive** for unrestricted transport under UN Series 3 / Test 3(b)(i).

> [!WARNING]
> Examiner's Pitfall — Students often choose **80 N** (3/6) as the limiting load because "more positives feel safer to pick." The correct choice is the **lowest** load giving ≥ 1 positive, i.e., **50 N**. Mis-selection costs **3 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **BAM** = German Federal Institute for Materials Research and Testing — origin of these two internationally adopted tests.
- **BAM Fallhammer** measures *impact sensitivity*; report the limiting energy $E_{\text{lim}} = mgh$ in Joules.
- **BAM Friction Test** measures *friction sensitivity*; report the limiting normal load $F_{\text{lim}}$ in Newtons.
- Both tests use the **6-trial rule**: lowest stimulus at which **≥ 1 of 6** trials is positive, **< 6 of 6** positive.
- Standard drop masses: **1, 2, 5, 10 kg**; standard heights: **0.05 to 1.00 m**.
- Standard friction loads: **5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 80, 100, 120, 160, 200, 240, 300, 360 N**.
- **UN "too-sensitive" thresholds**: $E_{\text{lim}} \leq 2 \text{ J}$ and/or $F_{\text{lim}} \leq 80 \text{ N}$ → restricted from commercial transport.
- Always identify the **lowest** positive energy / load — *not* the highest.
- Always state **g = 9.81 m/s²** explicitly in numerical solutions.
- Real-world use: ammunition safety, mining explosives, propellant handling, pyrotechnics, ammonium nitrate fertilizer classification, airbag inflators, fireworks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shock Sensitiveness Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shock Sensitiveness Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Shock Sensitiveness Test** (also called the **Impact Sensitivity Test** or **Mechanical Shock Sensitivity Test**) is a standardized explosive safety characterization procedure used to quantify the sensitivity of pyrotechnic compositions, propellants, and high explosives to the influence of mechanical impact or shock stimuli. The test determines the minimum impact energy (or equivalently, the critical drop height for a standard mass) at which a given energetic material exhibits a positive response (ignition, explosion, or audible decomposition).

The result is reported as a **Limiting Impact Energy (LIE)** measured in **Joules (J)**, or as a **Figure of Insensitivity (FOI)** referenced against a standard reference explosive (commonly RDX or PETN).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** Shock Sensitiveness is defined as the susceptibility of a pyrotechnic/explosive substance to undergo violent exothermic decomposition when subjected to a defined mechanical impact load, quantified by the minimum input energy required to initiate such decomposition in a standardized apparatus.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are tapping a fragile glass with a small hammer.

- If the glass is **very sensitive** (e.g., a thin wine glass), even a very gentle tap with a light hammer (low drop height, small mass) will shatter it.
- If the glass is **moderately sensitive** (e.g., a windowpane), you need a medium-strength tap.
- If the glass is **insensitive** (e.g., a car windshield), you need a heavy hammer dropped from a great height to break it.

In this analogy:
- The **glass** = the pyrotechnic/explosive composition
- The **hammer** = a standard steel drop-weight
- The **drop height** = the controlled mechanical shock input
- The **breakage (or "GO" event)** = ignition, flame, smoke, or audible snap

**Physical/Standard Metrics used in KTU/ISEE Test Reports:**

| Metric | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Standard Drop Mass** | 2.0 kg, 5.0 kg, or 10.0 kg (BAM) |
| **Drop Height Range** | 10 cm – 100 cm |
| **Sample Mass** | 40 mm³ (≈ 35–40 mg) |
| **Reference Explosive** | RDX (FOI = 80) or PETN (FOI = 100) |
| **Critical Energy Unit** | Joule (J) |

> [!NOTE]
> The most widely used international apparatus is the **BAM Fall Hammer** (Bundesanstalt für Materialforschung und -prüfung, Germany) and the **Julius Peter Apparatus**. Both are accepted by **UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods** and **IS 7620 (BIS)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theory of Shock Sensitiveness

The test relies on the principle that a defined mechanical work, when delivered impulsively to a confined layer of energetic material, will generate localized **hot spots** within the substance. If the temperature of these hot spots exceeds the critical decomposition temperature, the material ignites.

**The "Why" Behind Hot Spot Formation:**

1. **Frictional Heating (Inter-crystalline):** Relative motion between crystals under impact produces frictional heat.
2. **Viscous Heating (Intra-crystalline):** Rapid plastic flow in ductile explosives traps heat.
3. **Adiabatic Compression of Gas Pockets:** Trapped air/gas pockets between grains compress rapidly → temperature rise.
4. **Shear Banding:** Localized shear deformation → high local temperature.

If the localized temperature $T_{hot}$ exceeds the **ignition temperature** $T_{ign}$, the reaction propagates and the test is recorded as a **"GO"** (positive response).

### 2.2 Test Procedure – Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Sample Preparation:** A precise volume (≈ 40 mm³) of the test substance is placed between two hardened steel anvils (a striker and a base) inside a confinement ring.
2. **Drop-Weight Positioning:** A drop-weight of standardized mass (typically **2 kg**, **5 kg**, or **10 kg**) is lifted by an electromagnet or mechanical catch to a preset height $h$.
3. **Free-Fall Release:** The weight is released and falls under gravity, striking the upper anvil.
4. **Observation:** A "GO" is recorded if any of the following occur:
   - Audible report (bang/crack)
   - Visible flash or flame
   - Smoke emission
   - Distinctive odor of decomposition products
5. **Bracketing:** Using the **Bruceton Up-Down Method** (statistical), the 50% initiation point is determined.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = m g h$ | Impact energy delivered by drop-weight | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $h = E / (mg)$ | Required drop height for given energy | meters (m) |
| 3 | $\mu = \log_{10}(E)$ | Mean log-impact energy (Bruceton) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $s = 1.62 \, d$ | Standard deviation of log-impact energy (Bruceton) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $E_{50} = 10^{\mu}$ | 50% initiation impact energy | J |
| 6 | $\text{FOI} = 100 \times E_{50,\text{sample}} / E_{50,\text{REF}}$ | Figure of Insensitivity (REF = PETN) | % |
| 7 | $F = m a = m \cdot \sqrt{2gh/t_c}$ | Peak impulsive force (approx.) | N |
| 8 | $P = F/A$ | Peak pressure on anvil | Pa |
| 9 | $E_{c, \text{crit}} = \rho \, c_p \, V \, \Delta T$ | Critical hot-spot energy | J |
| 10 | $T_{hot} = T_0 + E_{c, \text{crit}} / (\rho V c_p)$ | Hot-spot temperature | K |

**Note on $g$:** Standard gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Always convert drop height from **centimeters** to **meters** before applying $E = mgh$. A common error is computing $E$ in kg·cm (which is NOT Joules). Use SI units throughout.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Safety:** Determines how much impact energy a pyrotechnic composition can tolerate during mixing, sieving, pressing, or transportation without accidental initiation.
- **Hazard Classification:** Required for UN Class 1 (Explosives) division 1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4 classification under **UN Orange Book (ST/SG/AC.10/1)**.
- **Storage & Handling SOPs:** Sets maximum permissible drop heights for containers, tools, and machinery used in pyrotechnic manufacturing.
- **Comparative Formulation:** Helps R&D scientists compare new compositions against reference materials during safety screening.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Impact Energy from a Free-Falling Mass

We consider a drop-weight of mass $m$ released from rest at height $h$ above the anvil. We wish to find the kinetic energy $E$ at the moment of impact.

**Step 1 — Work–Energy Principle**

The gravitational force on the weight is $F_g = m g$ acting vertically downward. As the weight falls through height $h$, the work done by gravity is:

$$
W = F_g \cdot h = m g h
$$

**Step 2 — Conservation of Energy**

By the work–energy theorem, the net work done on the weight equals its change in kinetic energy. The weight starts from rest ($v_i = 0$) and reaches velocity $v$ at impact:

$$
W = \Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 - 0 = \frac{1}{2} m v^2
$$

**Step 3 — Equating the Two Expressions**

$$
m g h = \frac{1}{2} m v^2
$$

**Step 4 — Solving for $v$**

The mass $m$ cancels from both sides (independent of mass):

$$
v^2 = 2 g h
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{2 g h}
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting back into the energy equation**

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} m (2 g h) = m g h
$$

Therefore, the **impact energy** delivered to the sample is:

$$
\boxed{E = m \, g \, h}
$$

> **Assumption:** Air resistance is negligible. In KTU laboratory-grade fall hammer apparatus, this is valid because the drop height is small (≤ 1 m) and the velocity is low (≤ 4.4 m/s).

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Exam Problem)

**Problem:** A pyrotechnic composition is tested on a BAM fall hammer. The drop weight is **5.0 kg**, and the 50% initiation drop height is found to be **20 cm**. The reference explosive (RDX) gives 50% initiation at **15 cm** with the same 5.0 kg weight. Compute (a) the 50% impact energy, and (b) the Figure of Insensitivity relative to RDX.

**Given:**

- $m = 5.0 \, \text{kg}$
- $h = 20 \, \text{cm} = 0.20 \, \text{m}$
- $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$
- Reference: $h_{\text{REF}} = 0.15 \, m$ with $m = 5.0 \, \text{kg}$

**Step (a): Compute 50% Impact Energy of Sample**

$$
E_{50} = m g h = 5.0 \times 9.81 \times 0.20
$$

$$
E_{50} = 5.0 \times 9.81 \times 0.20 = 9.81 \, J
$$

**Step (a) Result:**

$$
\boxed{E_{50,\text{sample}} = 9.81 \, J}
$$

**Step (b): Compute 50% Impact Energy of RDX**

$$
E_{50,\text{REF}} = m g h_{\text{REF}} = 5.0 \times 9.81 \times 0.15
$$

$$
E_{50,\text{REF}} = 5.0 \times 9.81 \times 0.15 = 7.3575 \, J
$$

**Step (c): Compute Figure of Insensitivity (FOI)**

The FOI is defined as the **ratio** of the sample energy to the reference energy, normalized to 100 for the reference:

$$
\text{FOI} = 100 \times \frac{E_{50,\text{sample}}}{E_{50,\text{REF}}}
$$

$$
\text{FOI} = 100 \times \frac{9.81}{7.3575}
$$

$$
\text{FOI} = 100 \times 1.3333 = 133.33
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\text{FOI}_{\text{sample vs RDX}} = 133.3}
$$

> **Interpretation:** An FOI greater than 100 means the sample is **less sensitive** (more insensitive) than the reference. The test composition requires **33.3% more impact energy** than RDX to initiate, indicating it is relatively safer to handle mechanically.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Bruceton Up-Down Estimate for 50% Point

In the Bruceton Method, trials are conducted sequentially. After a "GO" event, the next drop height is **decreased** by a fixed step $d$ (in log-energy units). After a "NO-GO", the next height is **increased** by $d$.

The mean log-impact energy $\mu$ is given by:

$$
\mu = X_0 + d \left( \frac{A}{N} \pm \frac{1}{2} \right)
$$

where:

- $X_0$ = log-energy of the **lowest level** at which any "GO" is observed
- $A = \sum_{i} i \cdot n_i$ where $i$ is the level index and $n_i$ is the count of "GO" events
- $N = \sum n_i$ = total number of "GO" events
- The sign $(+)$ is used when the lowest level has a "NO-GO" (rare); the sign $(-)$ when the lowest level itself was a "GO" (more common in KTU lab practice)

The standard deviation estimate is:

$$
s = 1.62 \, d
$$

And the 50% impact energy:

$$
E_{50} = 10^{\mu}
$$

This derivation is critical for KTU Part B questions involving statistical evaluation of test data.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of BAM Fall Hammer Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive Energetic Sample] --> B[Weigh Sample 35-40 mg]
    B --> C[Place Sample on Lower Anvil]
    C --> D[Insert Striker Pin and Confinement Ring]
    D --> E[Select Drop Mass 2/5/10 kg]
    E --> F[Set Initial Drop Height h sub 0]
    F --> G[Release Drop Weight Free Fall]
    G --> H{Observe Response}
    H -->|Yes Flash Sound Smoke| I[Record GO Event]
    H -->|No Response| J[Record NO-GO Event]
    I --> K[Decrease Height by Step d]
    J --> L[Increase Height by Step d]
    K --> M{N Reached}
    L --> M
    M -->|No| G
    M -->|Yes| N[Apply Bruceton Analysis]
    N --> O[Compute mu and s]
    O --> P[Compute E sub 50]
    P --> Q[Compare to Reference E sub 50 REF]
    Q --> R[Report FOI and Classify Hazard]
    R --> S[End: Generate Safety Report]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow of the Shock Sensitiveness Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 Sample Loading] --> P2[Step 2 Anvil Alignment]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Mass Selection]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Height Calibration]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Impact Drop]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Observation and Recording]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Statistical Bruceton]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 Final Classification]
```

### 4.3 Apparatus Schematic (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Apparatus[BAM Fall Hammer Apparatus]
        A1[Electromagnet or Latch Mechanism] --> A2[Drop Weight m]
        A2 --> A3[Guide Rails]
        A3 --> A4[Upper Anvil Striker Pin]
        A4 --> A5[Sample Layer 40 mm cubed]
        A5 --> A6[Lower Anvil]
        A6 --> A7[Heavy Base Block Concrete or Steel]
    end
    hParam[Drop Height h] --> A1
    mParam[Mass m] --> A2
    A5 --> Obs[Observation Zone: Flash / Sound / Smoke]
    Obs --> Rec[Data Recorder GO / NO-GO]
    Rec --> Stat[Bruceton Statistical Engine]
    Stat --> Report[FOI and E sub 50 Report]
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree for Result Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Test Sample Loaded] --> D1{Conduct Impact Test}
    D1 -->|GO Positive| D2[Lower Drop Height]
    D1 -->|NO-GO Negative| D3[Increase Drop Height]
    D2 --> D4{Convergence of h sub 50 Achieved}
    D3 --> D4
    D4 -->|Yes| D5[Compute E sub 50 equals m g h sub 50]
    D4 -->|No| D1
    D5 --> D6{Compare with Reference}
    D6 -->|FOI Less than 80| D7[HIGHLY SENSITIVE: Class 1.1 Hazard]
    D6 -->|FOI 80 to 110| D8[STANDARD SENSITIVITY: Class 1.3]
    D6 -->|FOI Greater than 110| D9[INSENSITIVE: Class 1.4 Safer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** The "GO" and "NO-GO" feedback loop is what makes the Bruceton method **adaptive** — each trial's outcome determines the next trial's height, allowing efficient convergence on the 50% point with minimum sample usage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

Define the term **Shock Sensitiveness** of a pyrotechnic composition. State the standard apparatus used to determine it and the metric in which the result is reported.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Shock Sensitiveness is the susceptibility of a pyrotechnic or explosive material to undergo ignition or violent decomposition when subjected to a defined mechanical impact load.
- **Apparatus (1 mark):** The standard apparatus is the **BAM Fall Hammer** (Bundesanstalt für Materialforschung), which uses a drop-weight of standardized mass falling freely from a controlled height onto a confined sample layer.
- **Metric (1 mark):** The result is reported as the **Limiting Impact Energy ($E_{50}$)** in **Joules (J)**, or as the **Figure of Insensitivity (FOI)** relative to a reference explosive (commonly PETN or RDX).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**

List **four** different criteria that constitute a positive "GO" event in a shock sensitiveness test.

**Model Answer (3 Marks, 0.75 each):**

A "GO" event is recorded if **any** of the following occur after impact:

1. **Audible report** — a distinct bang, crack, or snap from the sample.
2. **Visible flash or flame** — luminous emission at the anvil.
3. **Smoke emission** — visible particulate or vapor cloud.
4. **Distinctive odor** — pungent smell of decomposition products (e.g., NO$_x$ from nitrate decomposition, SO$_2$ from sulfur compounds).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Part A):** Students often write "explosion" as a generic answer. Examiners expect at least **2–3 specific observable criteria**. Writing a single generic term will cost you up to **2 marks**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the impact energy delivered by a drop-weight of mass $m$ falling freely from rest through a height $h$ in a BAM fall hammer apparatus. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pyrotechnic flash powder is tested on a BAM fall hammer. With a **5.0 kg** drop-weight, the 50% initiation drop height is found to be **12 cm**. Calculate:
- (i) The 50% impact energy of the sample.
- (ii) The Figure of Insensitivity (FOI) relative to a reference RDX composition that gives 50% initiation at **8 cm** with the same drop weight. Comment on the relative safety of the sample. **(7 Marks)**

**Use $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

*Step 1:* State Work–Energy Theorem (1 mark):

The work done by gravity on the falling mass equals its change in kinetic energy:

$$
W = m g h = \Delta KE
$$

*Step 2:* Apply conservation of energy (2 marks):

Since the weight starts from rest, $v_i = 0$:

$$
m g h = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 - 0
$$

*Step 3:* Solve for velocity (1 mark):

$$
v = \sqrt{2 g h}
$$

*Step 4:* Substitute to get final energy expression (2 marks):

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} m (2 g h) = m g h
$$

*Step 5:* State assumptions (1 mark):

- Air resistance is negligible.
- The drop is purely vertical (no pendulum motion).
- No energy loss in the anvil/sample interface.
- Gravitational field is uniform with $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$.

**Final boxed answer:**

$$
\boxed{E = m \, g \, h \quad \text{(Joules)}}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

*Given:*
- $m = 5.0 \, \text{kg}$
- $h_{\text{sample}} = 12 \, \text{cm} = 0.12 \, \text{m}$
- $h_{\text{REF}} = 8 \, \text{cm} = 0.08 \, \text{m}$
- $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

**(i) 50% Impact Energy of Sample (3.5 marks):**

$$
E_{50,\text{sample}} = m g h_{\text{sample}} = 5.0 \times 9.81 \times 0.12
$$

$$
E_{50,\text{sample}} = 5.0 \times 9.81 \times 0.12 = 5.886 \, J
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{50,\text{sample}} \approx 5.89 \, J}
$$

**(ii) Figure of Insensitivity (3.5 marks):**

First compute the reference energy:

$$
E_{50,\text{REF}} = m g h_{\text{REF}} = 5.0 \times 9.81 \times 0.08 = 3.924 \, J
$$

Apply the FOI formula:

$$
\text{FOI} = 100 \times \frac{E_{50,\text{sample}}}{E_{50,\text{REF}}} = 100 \times \frac{5.886}{3.924}
$$

$$
\text{FOI} = 100 \times 1.5 = 150
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{FOI} = 150}
$$

**Comment on Safety (included within 3.5 marks):**

Since the FOI = 150 is significantly greater than 100, the sample is **50% more insensitive** (i.e., requires 50% more energy) than the RDX reference. This indicates the flash powder is **relatively safer** to handle mechanically, with a higher tolerance to impact loads during mixing, sieving, and pressing operations.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice):** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Bruceton Up-Down Method** as used in the statistical analysis of shock sensitiveness test data. State the formula for the 50% initiation energy and the standard deviation estimate. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During a Bruceton test on a gunpowder composition, the following data were obtained using a **2.0 kg** drop-weight, with step size $d = 0.10$ in log-energy units:

| Drop Height (cm) | Log Energy $X_i$ | Outcome | GO Count $n_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 30 | 1.769 | GO | 3 |
| 25 | 1.690 | NO-GO | 0 |
| 35 | 1.836 | GO | 1 |
| 28 | 1.738 | NO-GO | 0 |

If the lowest level $X_0$ corresponds to 25 cm (log-energy 1.690), calculate the **mean log-impact energy $\mu$** and the **50% impact energy $E_{50}$**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Theory (7 Marks):**

*Concept (2 marks):* The Bruceton Up-Down Method is a sequential statistical procedure for estimating the 50% initiation point of an explosive or pyrotechnic material from impact sensitivity data. The method adaptively adjusts the drop height after each trial based on the outcome, allowing efficient convergence on the median energy with a minimum number of trials.

*Algorithm (2 marks):*
- After a **"GO"** event, the next drop height is **decreased** by step $d$ (in log-energy units).
- After a **"NO-GO"** event, the next drop height is **increased** by step $d$.
- Trials continue until the data are statistically sufficient (typically $N \geq 30$).

*Formulae (2 marks):*

$$
\mu = X_0 + d \left( \frac{A}{N} \pm \frac{1}{2} \right)
$$

$$
s = 1.62 \, d
$$

$$
E_{50} = 10^{\mu}
$$

*Sign Convention (1 mark):* Use $(-)$ sign when the lowest tested level itself produced a "GO"; use $(+)$ sign when the lowest level was a "NO-GO".

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

*Step 1 — Identify $X_0$, $d$, and $N$:*
- $X_0 = 1.690$ (log-energy at 25 cm)
- $d = 0.10$
- Total GO events: $N = n_1 + n_2 = 3 + 1 = 4$

*Step 2 — Compute $A = \sum i \cdot n_i$ (3.5 marks):*

The levels above $X_0$ in the table are:
- Level 1 (30 cm, $X_1 = 1.769$): $i = 1$, $n_1 = 3$, contributes $1 \times 3 = 3$
- Level 2 (35 cm, $X_2 = 1.836$): $i = 2$, $n_2 = 1$, contributes $2 \times 1 = 2$

$$
A = (1 \times 3) + (2 \times 1) = 3 + 2 = 5
$$

*Step 3 — Apply the Bruceton mean formula (2 marks):*

Since the lowest level $X_0$ did **not** produce a "GO" (it was a NO-GO), use the $(+)$ sign:

$$
\mu = X_0 + d \left( \frac{A}{N} + \frac{1}{2} \right)
$$

$$
\mu = 1.690 + 0.10 \left( \frac{5}{4} + 0.5 \right)
$$

$$
\mu = 1.690 + 0.10 \left( 1.25 + 0.5 \right)
$$

$$
\mu = 1.690 + 0.10 \times 1.75
$$

$$
\mu = 1.690 + 0.175 = 1.865
$$

$$
\boxed{\mu = 1.865}
$$

*Step 4 — Compute $E_{50}$ (1.5 marks):*

$$
E_{50} = 10^{\mu} = 10^{1.865}
$$

$$
10^{1.865} = 10^{1} \times 10^{0.865} = 10 \times 7.328
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{50} \approx 73.3 \, J}
$$

*(Note: Since the drop mass is 2.0 kg and $E_{50} = mgh$, the corresponding drop height is $h_{50} = E_{50} / (mg) = 73.3 / (2.0 \times 9.81) \approx 3.74 \, m$ — which exceeds the standard BAM range, indicating the composition is highly insensitive.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Writing $h = 12 \, cm$ directly in $E = mgh$ gives the answer in kg·cm (which is **NOT Joules**). Always convert cm → m first. Loss: **up to 3 marks**.
> 2. **Bruceton Sign Convention:** Using the wrong sign $(+)$ or $(-)$ in the mean formula. Re-read the lowest level carefully. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the Reference Calculation:** In FOI questions, computing only the sample energy and not the reference energy. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 4. **No Interpretation in Numerical:** Numerical answers without qualitative safety commentary are penalized in KTU valuation. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Confusing Shock Sensitiveness with Friction Sensitiveness:** These are two different tests (drop-weight vs. friction apparatus). Examiners WILL deduct for mixing them up. Loss: **up to 2 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Shock Sensitiveness Test** quantifies the impact sensitivity of pyrotechnic/explosive materials using a free-falling drop-weight.
- **Standard Apparatus:** BAM Fall Hammer (most common); also Julius Peter apparatus.
- **Core Formula:** $E = m g h$ (impact energy in Joules).
- **50% Initiation Point** $E_{50}$ is determined by the **Bruceton Up-Down Method** using:

$$
\mu = X_0 + d \left( \frac{A}{N} \pm \frac{1}{2} \right)
$$

- **Figure of Insensitivity (FOI):**

$$
\text{FOI} = 100 \times \frac{E_{50,\text{sample}}}{E_{50,\text{REF}}}
$$

  - FOI > 100 → sample is **more insensitive** (safer) than reference
  - FOI < 100 → sample is **more sensitive** (more hazardous) than reference

- **Positive "GO" Criteria:** Audible report, visible flash, smoke emission, or distinctive decomposition odor.
- **Sample Standard Mass:** ≈ 40 mm³ (≈ 35–40 mg).
- **Standard Drop Masses:** 2 kg, 5 kg, 10 kg.
- **Reference Explosives:** RDX (FOI = 80), PETN (FOI = 100).
- **Hot Spot Theory:** Impact creates localized hot spots whose temperature must exceed $T_{ign}$ for ignition.
- **Always use SI units** ($m$ in kg, $h$ in meters, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$, $E$ in Joules).
- **Engineering Utility:** Hazard classification (UN Class 1.1–1.4), manufacturing safety, transport regulations, and formulation R&D screening.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Card Gap Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Card Gap Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Card Gap Test (Gap Test):** A standardized, quantitative shock-sensitivity test used in explosive hazard classification to determine the ability of an explosive to propagate detonation when initiated by a shock wave that has been attenuated by passing through a known thickness of an inert, calibrated buffer material (most commonly Polymethyl Methacrylate / PMMA cards, or water).

The test is formally recognized under the **United Nations Test Series 1(a)** for the classification of explosives under the *UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods* and is also codified in:
- **STANAG 4488 (NATO)**
- **U.S. DoD TB 700-2 (DoD 6055.09)**
- **ASTM / Bureau of Mines procedures**

The output metric is the **Card Gap Value** — expressed as the **critical thickness (in cards or mm of PMMA) at which the probability of detonation of the acceptor is 50%**, often denoted **G₅₀** or simply the *gap value*.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine shouting at a paper wall and listening for an echo on the other side. The louder you shout, the more sound gets through. The thicker the wall, the quieter the echo. The **Card Gap Test** does this with a *shock wave* (the shout), a stack of plastic cards (the wall), and an explosive charge (the listener). If the explosive "hears" the shock strongly enough, it detonates; if the wall is too thick, the explosive simply sits there.

In essence, the test answers one critical safety question:
> *"How much buffering does this explosive need to survive a nearby accidental shock without detonating?"*

### 1.3 Why This Test Matters in Safety Engineering

- **Hazard Classification Division (1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4):** Determines whether the explosive is *mass-detonating, fragment-producing, mass-fire, or insensitive*.
- **Storage & Transport Compatibility (Q-D values, HCS rules):** Determines if two explosives can be stored together.
- **Process Safety:** Quantifies sensitivity for risk assessment in propellant/explosive manufacturing.
- **Net Explosive Weight (NEW) calculations and Quantity-Distance (Q-D) siting** in ammunition depots.

### 1.4 Key Physical Standards Recalled

| Quantity | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Donor charge | **Composition C-4** (50 g pellet, 41.3 mm dia.) — *alternatively* **Tetryl** |
| Gap material | **PMMA (Polymethyl Methacrylate)** cards, 1.50 ± 0.05 mm thick, 41.3 ± 0.05 mm diameter |
| Witness plate | Mild/cold-rolled steel, 101.6 mm × 101.6 mm × 6.35 mm |
| Standard temperature | **20 ± 5 °C** (per UN Test 1(a)) |
| Acceptance criterion | Hole/crater in witness plate ≥ visible detonation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The Card Gap Test exploits a fundamental principle of shock-wave physics:

> **A donor detonation produces a high-pressure shock that travels through an attenuating medium. The pressure decays with distance. The thinner the medium, the higher the pressure reaching the acceptor. By varying the number of cards, the threshold pressure for acceptor initiation is empirically bracketed.**

**Logical flow of the test:**

1. **Donor Initiation:** A standard donor charge is initiated by a detonator (No. 8 electric).
2. **Shock Transit Through Gap:** The detonation wave drives a shock through the inert PMMA stack. The shock's peak pressure **decays** due to:
   - Geometric divergence (rare-faction from edges)
   - Bulk viscoelastic dissipation in PMMA
   - Impedance mismatch at card-card boundaries
3. **Acceptor Loading:** The attenuated shock strikes the test explosive's free surface. The induced pressure must exceed the **critical initiation pressure P_c** of the explosive for a **run-up to detonation** to occur within the charge length available.
4. **Witness Plate Diagnosis:** A detonation (Go) produces a characteristic **clean hole or sharp crater** in the steel witness. A non-detonation (No-Go) leaves the plate **relatively undamaged, dented, or only slightly punctured**.
5. **Bruceton / Up-Down Statistical Analysis:** The number of cards is varied in a Bruceton staircase (Neyer D-Optimal) procedure to compute the **mean gap** (μ) and **standard deviation (σ)** — the **50% point** being the *median sensitivity* of the explosive.

### 2.2 Shock Physics Behind the Decay

The shock wave in the PMMA is governed by the **Hugoniot Rankine relations** for conservation of mass, momentum, and energy across the shock front. For a planar shock in PMMA, the empirical linear Hugoniot is:

$$U_s = c_0 + s \cdot U_p$$

where:
- **U_s** = shock velocity in PMMA (km/s)
- **U_p** = particle velocity behind the shock (km/s)
- **c_0** = bulk sound speed in PMMA ≈ **2.59 km/s**
- **s** = Hugoniot slope coefficient for PMMA ≈ **1.52**

The peak pressure of the shock is:

$$P = \rho_0 \cdot U_s \cdot U_p$$

where ρ₀ ≈ **1.186 g/cm³** (density of PMMA).

As the shock traverses a stack of *n* cards of total thickness *x = n × t* (t = 1.5 mm), the peak pressure **decays approximately exponentially** in the near-field:

$$P(x) \approx P_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha x}$$

where **α** is the **attenuation coefficient** (experimentally ≈ 0.06 – 0.10 mm⁻¹ for PMMA, depending on donor and geometry).

### 2.3 Criterion for Acceptor Detonation

Detonation is observed if and only if the pressure delivered to the acceptor exceeds the explosive's **Critical Initiation Pressure** *and* the **run distance to detonation** is less than the charge length:

$$P_{delivered}(x_{crit}) \geq P_{c} \quad \text{AND} \quad L_{charge} \geq x_{run}(P)$$

The *card gap value* **G₅₀** is the *x* at which 50% of trials result in detonation:

$$G_{50} = \frac{\text{Number of cards at 50\% probability} \times 1.5 \text{ mm}}{}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $U_s$ | Shock velocity in PMMA | $U_s = c_0 + s U_p$ | km/s |
| $P$ | Peak shock pressure | $P = \rho_0 U_s U_p$ | GPa |
| $P(x)$ | Pressure at gap depth *x* | $P_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ | GPa |
| $P_c$ | Critical initiation pressure | Material property | GPa |
| $G_{50}$ | 50% card gap value | Bruceton mean | mm / cards |
| $\mu, \sigma$ | Mean & std. dev. (Bruceton) | Statistically derived | — |
| $c_0$ | PMMA bulk sound speed | **2.59** | km/s |
| $s$ | PMMA Hugoniot slope | **1.52** | dimensionless |
| $\rho_0$ | PMMA density | **1.186** | g/cm³ |
| $\alpha$ | Attenuation coefficient | **0.06 – 0.10** | mm⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The *G₅₀* value is the cornerstone input for **Hazard Classification Division 1.5 (Insensitive Substances)** qualification, **MURAT (Mixed Units Risk Analysis Tool)** models, and **IMEMG / NATO Q-D** calculations for ammunition depot layout.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Ammunition depot Q-D siting:** Higher *G₅₀* → explosive is safer to cohabit with populated zones.
- **Process safety in propellant manufacture:** Determines if a process upset (e.g., friction shock) can propagate to detonation.
- **Warhead design:** Choosing booster size such that a *Go* is achieved within the available charge length.
- **Insensitive Munitions (IM) compliance:** A *G₅₀* above a certain threshold (e.g., > 70 cards) is required for IM-compliant explosives like TATB-based formulations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Shock Pressure in the PMMA Gap from First Principles

We start from the **Rankine-Hugoniot jump conditions** for a steady, one-dimensional shock.

**Step 1 — Conservation of Mass (Continuity):**
Mass flux across the shock is conserved:

$$\rho_0 U_s = \rho (U_s - U_p)$$

Solving for the compressed density:

$$\rho = \rho_0 \frac{U_s}{U_s - U_p}$$

**Step 2 — Conservation of Momentum (Newton's 2nd Law):**
Pressure jump equals momentum flux:

$$P - P_0 = \rho_0 U_s U_p$$

For a strong shock in PMMA, $P_0$ (atmospheric ≈ 0.0001 GPa) is negligible compared to shock pressure (several GPa), so:

$$P \approx \rho_0 U_s U_p \quad \text{(Equation A)}$$

**Step 3 — Empirical Hugoniot Relation for PMMA:**
Experimental Hugoniot data for PMMA yields the linear fit:

$$U_s = c_0 + s U_p \quad \text{(Equation B)}$$

**Step 4 — Combine A and B to get P in terms of U_p:**

$$P = \rho_0 (c_0 + s U_p) U_p = \rho_0 c_0 U_p + \rho_0 s U_p^2$$

This is a quadratic in particle velocity. For a given pressure *P*:

$$\rho_0 s U_p^2 + \rho_0 c_0 U_p - P = 0$$

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$U_p = \frac{-\rho_0 c_0 + \sqrt{(\rho_0 c_0)^2 + 4 \rho_0 s P}}{2 \rho_0 s}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Attenuation of Shock Pressure Through a PMMA Stack

In a real test geometry, the shock is not perfectly planar. Lateral rarefaction waves from the edges of the donor penetrate inward, gradually reducing the central peak pressure. For *x* much less than the donor radius, attenuation is well-described empirically by a near-exponential decay.

**Step 1 — Decay Law Statement:**
Experimental streak-camera and velocity interferometer (VISAR) data for PMMA stacks under a C-4 donor show:

$$\frac{dP}{dx} = -\alpha P$$

This is a separable first-order ODE.

**Step 2 — Integration:**

$$\int_{P_0}^{P(x)} \frac{dP}{P} = -\alpha \int_0^x dx$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{P(x)}{P_0}\right) = -\alpha x$$

$$\therefore \; P(x) = P_0 e^{-\alpha x}$$

**Step 3 — Pressure Delivered to the Acceptor:**
For a stack of *n* cards each of thickness *t* = 1.5 mm:

$$x = n \cdot t = 1.5 n \text{ mm}$$

$$P_{delivered}(n) = P_0 e^{-1.5 \alpha n}$$

**Step 4 — Critical Gap Condition (50% point):**
At the 50% probability point, $P_{delivered} = P_{c,50}$ (the pressure at which 50% of shots go):

$$P_{c,50} = P_0 e^{-1.5 \alpha n_{50}}$$

Solving for the critical card count:

$$n_{50} = \frac{1}{1.5 \alpha} \ln\left(\frac{P_0}{P_{c,50}}\right)$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{G_{50} = n_{50} \times 1.5 \text{ mm} = \frac{1}{\alpha} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_0}{P_{c,50}}\right)}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A donor charge generates an initial shock pressure of **20 GPa** in the PMMA stack. The attenuation coefficient of PMMA in this geometry is **α = 0.075 mm⁻¹**. The test explosive (RDX/TNT 60/40) has a critical initiation pressure **P_c,50 = 1.65 GPa**. Find:
> (a) The pressure reaching the acceptor after **20 cards**.
> (b) The **50% card gap value** in mm and in number of cards.

**Solution:**

**(a) Pressure after 20 cards:**

$$x = 20 \times 1.5 \text{ mm} = 30 \text{ mm}$$

$$P(30) = 20 \cdot e^{-0.075 \times 30} = 20 \cdot e^{-2.25}$$

$$e^{-2.25} \approx 0.1054$$

$$P(30) \approx 20 \times 0.1054 \approx \mathbf{2.108 \text{ GPa}}$$

Since 2.108 GPa > 1.65 GPa = P_c, the acceptor *will* detonate at 20 cards.

**(b) 50% card gap value:**

$$n_{50} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 0.075} \ln\!\left(\frac{20}{1.65}\right) = \frac{1}{0.1125} \ln(12.1212)$$

$$\ln(12.1212) \approx 2.495$$

$$n_{50} = \frac{2.495}{0.1125} \approx 22.18 \text{ cards}$$

In practice, the Bruceton staircase rounds to the nearest **integer or half-integer** card increment, giving:

$$G_{50} \approx 22 \text{ cards} \approx 33 \text{ mm of PMMA}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Classification by G₅₀

| G₅₀ (cards) | G₅₀ (mm PMMA) | Sensitivity Class | Typical Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0 – 10** | 0 – 15 | **Very Sensitive** | Pure NG, Lead Azide |
| **10 – 25** | 15 – 37.5 | **Sensitive** | RDX, HMX, Tetryl |
| **25 – 50** | 37.5 – 75 | **Moderate** | Comp B, Octol |
| **50 – 70** | 75 – 105 | **Insensitive** | Tritonal, TATB-based IHE |
| **> 70** | > 105 | **Very Insensitive / IM** | LX-17, PBXN-109 |

> [!NOTE]
> These are *indicative* ranges; final hazard classification integrates **G₅₀** with results from other UN Test Series (1(b), 1(c), 2(a), etc.) under the *Hazard Classification System* of the UN Orange Book.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Card Gap Test Physical Setup

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DETONATOR No 8 Electric] --> B[DONOR CHARGE 50 g C4 or Tetryl]
    B --> C[PMMA CARD STACK n cards Variable Gap]
    C --> D[ACCEPTOR TEST EXPLOSIVE 50 g Pelleted]
    D --> E[STEEL WITNESS PLATE 101.6 mm]
    E --> F[DIAGNOSIS Go or No Go]
    F --> G[Bruceton Up Down Statistics]
    G --> H[REPORT G50 in cards and mm]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Test Procedure Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Start Calibrate PMMA cards] --> S2[Stack n cards on witness plate]
    S2 --> S3[Place acceptor on top of stack]
    S3 --> S4[Place donor charge on acceptor]
    S4 --> S5[Insert No 8 electric detonator]
    S5 --> S6[Setup in firing chamber]
    S6 --> S7[Initiate donor]
    S7 --> S8{Inspect witness plate}
    S8 -- Clean hole or sharp crater --> S9[Record Go]
    S8 -- No dent or puncture --> S10[Record No Go]
    S9 --> S11[Decrease n for next shot]
    S10 --> S12[Increase n for next shot]
    S11 --> S13{N trials completed}
    S12 --> S13
    S13 -- No --> S2
    S13 -- Yes --> S14[Compute mean and std dev Bruceton]
    S14 --> S15[Report G50]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Shock Energy Path

```mermaid
graph TD
    E1[Detonator 2 kJ] --> E2[Donor Detonation 50 g C4]
    E2 --> E3[Shock pressure 20 to 30 GPa in PMMA]
    E3 --> E4[Attenuation through n cards]
    E4 --> E5[Delivered pressure to acceptor]
    E5 --> E6{Compare to critical P of explosive}
    E6 -- Greater --> E7[Go Run up to detonation]
    E6 -- Less --> E8[No Go Compression only]
    E7 --> E9[Witness plate hole]
    E8 --> E10[Witness plate undamaged]
```

### 4.4 Architecture Flow — Card Gap Test in Overall Safety Classification Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Manufacturing Quality Control] --> P2[Card Gap Test UN 1a]
    P3[Thermal Stability UN 3a] --> P4[Hazard Classification Decision]
    P5[Impact UN 3b] --> P4
    P6[Friction UN 3c] --> P4
    P2 --> P4
    P4 --> P5a{Result}
    P5a -- High G50 --> P6a[Division 1.5 or 1.6 IM Suitable]
    P5a -- Low G50 --> P6b[Division 1.1 Mass Detonating]
    P5a -- Mid G50 --> P6c[Division 1.2 or 1.3]
    P6a --> P7[Storage Transport and IM Compliance]
    P6b --> P7
    P6c --> P7
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the **Card Gap Test**. State the donor charge, the gap material, and the standard witness plate dimensions used in this test. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The Card Gap Test is a standardized shock-sensitivity test that measures the ability of an explosive to propagate detonation when subjected to a shock wave that has been attenuated by passing through a known thickness of an inert buffer material.
- **Donor charge:** Composition C-4 (50 g, 41.3 mm diameter); tetryl is an alternative.
- **Gap material:** PMMA (polymethyl methacrylate) cards, each **1.50 ± 0.05 mm** thick, of **41.3 mm** diameter.
- **Witness plate:** Cold-rolled steel, **101.6 mm × 101.6 mm × 6.35 mm**.
- The 50% point (G₅₀) is determined by the Bruceton up-down method.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
What is meant by the **"50% card gap value" (G₅₀)**? Mention its significance in the Hazard Classification of explosives. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The **G₅₀ value** is the thickness of the PMMA gap (expressed in number of cards or mm) at which the probability of detonation of the acceptor explosive is **50%**. It is determined statistically using the **Bruceton staircase method** and represents the *median shock sensitivity* of the explosive.
**Significance:**
- A *high G₅₀* indicates the explosive is **insensitive** to shock and qualifies for **Hazard Division 1.5 (Insensitive Substances)** or **1.6** under the UN classification.
- A *low G₅₀* indicates a **highly shock-sensitive** explosive (Hazard Division 1.1 — mass-detonating).
- It governs **Q-D siting**, **storage compatibility (HCS)**, and **transportation regulations** under the UN Orange Book.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**
> **Question A — 14 Marks**
> (a) Explain the principle and procedure of the **Card Gap Test** for explosives with a neat block diagram. List the standard UN/DoD reference for this test. **(7 Marks)**
> (b) A donor charge of Composition C-4 initiates a shock of **25 GPa** in a PMMA stack. The attenuation coefficient is **α = 0.08 mm⁻¹**. The test explosive has a critical initiation pressure **P_c,50 = 1.85 GPa**. Calculate the **50% card gap value** in mm and in number of PMMA cards. State whether an explosive with G₅₀ = 18 cards would be classified as sensitive or insensitive. **(7 Marks)**

**Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
> (a) Derive the exponential attenuation law **P(x) = P₀ e^(–αx)** for a shock passing through the PMMA gap, starting from the Rankine-Hugoniot relations. Define each term. **(7 Marks)**
> (b) Discuss the role of the **Card Gap Test** in the **UN Hazard Classification** of Class 1 Dangerous Goods. Compare the shock sensitivity of an **RDX-based** explosive vs a **TATB-based** explosive in terms of G₅₀. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 Model Solutions for Q3 (Question A)

**Solution to Q3-A (a):**

**Principle:**
A donor detonation produces a strong shock in the PMMA gap. The shock attenuates with distance. The acceptor detonates only if the residual shock pressure exceeds its **critical initiation pressure P_c**. By varying the number of cards and using the **Bruceton up-down method**, the **G₅₀** is found.

**Procedure (in sequence):**
1. Stack **n** PMMA cards (each 1.5 mm) on a steel witness plate.
2. Place the **acceptor (50 g)** pellet on the card stack.
3. Place the **donor (50 g C-4)** on the acceptor.
4. Insert a **No. 8 electric detonator** into the donor.
5. Fire in a containment chamber.
6. Inspect the **witness plate**:
   - **Go** = clean hole/sharp crater (detonation).
   - **No-Go** = plate undamaged or only dented.
7. Adjust *n* up or down (Bruceton staircase) and repeat — typically 25–30 trials.
8. Compute **mean and standard deviation** of the gap count → **G₅₀**.

**Reference Standards:**
- **UN Test 1(a)** — *UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods, Manual of Tests and Criteria*.
- **STANAG 4488 (NATO)**.
- **U.S. DoD TB 700-2 / DoD 6055.09**.

*Block Diagram:* Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes.

**Solution to Q3-A (b):**

**Given:**
- P₀ = 25 GPa
- α = 0.08 mm⁻¹
- P_c,50 = 1.85 GPa

**Formula derived in Section 3.2:**

$$G_{50} = \frac{1}{\alpha} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_0}{P_{c,50}}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{P_0}{P_{c,50}} = \frac{25}{1.85} \approx 13.514$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural log:**

$$\ln(13.514) \approx 2.6039$$

**Step 3 — Divide by α:**

$$G_{50} = \frac{2.6039}{0.08} \approx 32.55 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to cards:**

$$n_{50} = \frac{32.55}{1.5} \approx 21.7 \approx \mathbf{22 \text{ cards}}$$

**Step 5 — Classification of an explosive with G₅₀ = 18 cards:**
A G₅₀ of 18 cards falls in the range **15–37.5 mm / 10–25 cards**, which corresponds to **"Sensitive"** class — typical of pure **RDX** or **HMX** formulations. It is **NOT insensitive** for IM compliance (which requires G₅₀ > ~70 cards).

---

### 5.4 Model Solutions for Q3 (Question B — Alternative)

**Solution to Q3-B (a):**

Start from the **Rankine-Hugoniot momentum jump condition**:

$$P - P_0 = \rho_0 U_s U_p$$

For a strong shock, $P \gg P_0$, so $P \approx \rho_0 U_s U_p$.

For the empirical linear Hugoniot of PMMA:

$$U_s = c_0 + s U_p$$

Substituting:

$$P = \rho_0 (c_0 + s U_p) U_p$$

The particle velocity decays along the stack due to lateral rarefaction waves. The decay of pressure with distance is governed by a first-order loss model:

$$\frac{dP}{dx} = -\alpha P$$

Separating variables and integrating from 0 to *x*:

$$\int_{P_0}^{P(x)} \frac{dP}{P} = -\alpha \int_0^x dx$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P(x)}{P_0}\right) = -\alpha x$$

$$\therefore \; P(x) = P_0 e^{-\alpha x}$$

**Term definitions:**
- **P₀** = Initial shock pressure at the top of the PMMA stack (GPa)
- **P(x)** = Shock pressure after traversing gap thickness *x* (GPa)
- **α** = Empirical attenuation coefficient (mm⁻¹) ≈ 0.06–0.10 for PMMA
- **x** = Gap thickness = n × 1.5 mm
- **c₀, s** = PMMA Hugoniot constants (**2.59 km/s, 1.52**)
- **ρ₀** = Initial density of PMMA (**1.186 g/cm³**)

**Solution to Q3-B (b):**

In the **UN Hazard Classification system** (Class 1), the Card Gap Test (UN Test 1(a)) is one of the primary tests used to determine whether an explosive is mass-detonating (1.1) or progressively less hazardous (1.2 → 1.6). Higher G₅₀ values indicate lower shock sensitivity, which translates to safer storage, transport, and handling.

**Comparison of RDX vs TATB:**

| Parameter | RDX-based explosive (e.g., Comp B) | TATB-based explosive (e.g., LX-17) |
|---|---|---|
| **G₅₀** | **~ 18 – 22 cards** (~ 27–33 mm) | **> 70 cards** (> 105 mm) |
| Sensitivity | **Sensitive** | **Highly Insensitive** |
| UN Hazard Division | 1.1 / 1.2 | 1.5 / 1.6 (IM compliant) |
| Storage Q-D | Severe restrictions | Minimal restrictions |
| Use | Conventional munitions | Insensitive Munitions / Warheads |

TATB's high G₅₀ stems from its **graphite-like layered crystal structure** that absorbs and disperses shock energy, in contrast to RDX's compact molecular packing which readily couples to shock.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units mismatch:** Students often forget to convert α from mm⁻¹ to a consistent unit, or mix km/s with m/s when applying the Hugoniot. Always check units.
> 2. **Confusing "card gap value" with "critical pressure":** The card gap value is a *geometric* quantity; the critical pressure is a *material* property. They are related, not equivalent.
> 3. **Misapplying Bruceton statistics:** The 50% point is **mean**, not the lowest "Go". Don't quote the boundary of a single "Go / No-Go" pair.
> 4. **Forgetting the witness plate criterion:** Many students state that *any* sound confirms detonation. The official criterion is the **plate dent / hole pattern**.
> 5. **Confusing Card Gap Test with the "Large-Scale Gap Test":** The latter uses water as the gap medium and is a different UN Test (1(b)).

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Card Gap Test**

- **Full name:** Card Gap Test (Gap Test / UN Test 1(a) / STANAG 4488).
- **Purpose:** Quantify shock sensitivity for hazard classification.
- **Donor:** **50 g C-4** (or tetryl), 41.3 mm Ø.
- **Gap:** **PMMA cards**, 1.5 mm thick each, 41.3 mm Ø.
- **Acceptor:** **50 g** of the test explosive.
- **Witness plate:** Steel, **101.6 × 101.6 × 6.35 mm**.
- **Statistics:** **Bruceton up-down** staircase → 50% point = **G₅₀**.
- **Output unit:** **Number of cards** (or mm of PMMA).
- **Hugoniot for PMMA:** $U_s = 2.59 + 1.52 U_p$ km/s; $\rho_0 = 1.186$ g/cm³.
- **Attenuation law:** $P(x) = P_0 e^{-\alpha x}$, with $\alpha \approx 0.06$–$0.10$ mm⁻¹.
- **G₅₀ formula:** $G_{50} = (1/\alpha) \ln(P_0 / P_{c,50})$ in mm.
- **Higher G₅₀ → safer explosive** (insensitive / IM compliance).
- **High G₅₀ (> 70 cards):** **Insensitive / Division 1.5** — IM-grade.
- **Low G₅₀ (< 10 cards):** **Very sensitive** — Division 1.1.
- **Witness plate hole = Go;** undamaged = No-Go.
- **Standard temperature:** 20 ± 5 °C (UN requirement).
- **Used in:** Q-D siting, IM compliance, transport classification, HCS storage compatibility.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commissioning Phases and Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Commissioning Phases and Organization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Commissioning** in the context of Design for Safety (DFS) is a structured, planned, and documented engineering process that verifies and demonstrates that a new, modified, or decommissioned industrial installation, process plant, building, or safety-critical system performs in accordance with its design intent, operational specifications, functional requirements, and safety integrity levels (SIL) as defined during the Engineering Design phase.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT412 – Design for Safety, Module 4), commissioning is treated as a **risk-controlled transition state** between construction and live operation. It is not a single event but a series of systematically organized phases involving mechanical completion, pre-commissioning checks, system energization, functional testing, and performance validation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Commissioning Phases & Organization):**
> The student must be able to differentiate between **Cold Commissioning** (mechanical integrity, instrument loop checks, utility flushing done *without* process fluids) and **Hot Commissioning** (introducing process fluids, dynamic testing under live conditions). KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Standardized for KTU 2024:**
> - **Pre-Commissioning:** All activities after mechanical completion but before process fluids are introduced.
> - **Commissioning:** The controlled introduction of energy, process fluids, and feedstocks.
> - **Start-Up:** The integrated operation of all systems to achieve design throughput.
> - **Performance Testing (PGTR):** Proof of Guaranteed Test Run against contractual performance guarantees.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine commissioning a brand-new commercial passenger aircraft before it carries paying passengers.

1. **Design Phase** – The aircraft is engineered on paper with aerodynamic, structural, and safety specifications.
2. **Manufacturing/Construction Phase** – The plane is physically built on the assembly line.
3. **Pre-Commissioning (Hangar Testing)** – Before fuel and passengers are loaded, every rivet, hydraulic line, electrical circuit, and control surface is checked *on the ground*. Engines are run statically. No passengers are aboard.
4. **Cold Commissioning (Taxi Tests)** – The plane is moved on the runway at low speed. Brakes, steering, and ground-handling systems are tested. Still no real flight.
5. **Hot Commissioning (Maiden Flight with Test Pilot)** – Real fuel is loaded. The plane takes off. Test pilots push systems to limits, verify stall behavior, autopilot response, and emergency procedures.
6. **Performance Testing (Route-Proving Flights)** – Multiple flights under simulated payload to prove fuel efficiency, range, and endurance.
7. **Hand-Over to Operations** – The aircraft is accepted by the airline and put into commercial service.

**The Insight:** A plane without proper commissioning would be a disaster. Similarly, an industrial plant commissioned without a phased, organized safety plan risks explosions, toxic releases, and fatalities. The *organization* (who is responsible, who signs off) is as critical as the *phases* themselves.
