## 3.5 Worked Example: Commissioning of a Heat Exchanger

**Problem (KTU Typical):**
> A shell-and-tube heat exchanger is designed for **20 bar** working pressure at **180 °C**. Outline the commissioning procedure you would adopt, with explicit safety justifications.

**Model Solution — Step by Step:**

1. **Mechanical Completion Verification**
   - Confirm shell and channel cover bolting is per drawing; torque to spec.
   - Verify tube bundle insertion, gasket seating, and expansion joint alignment.

2. **Hydrostatic Test**
   $$P_{test} = 1.5 \times 20 = \mathbf{30 \text{ bar}}$$
   Hold for **30 minutes** per ASME BPVC; monitor for pressure decay.

3. **Pre-Commissioning — Instrument Loop Check**
   - Calibrate inlet/outlet temperature transmitters (TT), pressure transmitters (PT), and flow indicator (FI).
   - Stroke-test the inlet block valve and bypass valve.
   - Function-test the **Pressure Safety Valve (PSV)** set at $1.10 \times 20 = \mathbf{22 \text{ bar}}$.

4. **Cold Commissioning — Water Circulation**
   - Fill with treated water; vent air through high-point vents.
   - Verify pump head, flow, and $\Delta P$ against design.

5. **Warm Commissioning — Steam Introduction**
   - Ramp steam slowly (≤ **20 °C/hr**) to avoid **thermal shock**.
   - Monitor differential expansion on shell and tube sides.

6. **Hot Commissioning — Process Fluid**
   - Introduce process fluid at **20%** of design flow.
   - Step up in increments of **20%**, holding at each step to confirm stability.

7. **Performance Test**
   - Run at **100%** design conditions for **72 hours** continuously.
   - Compare heat duty, LMTD, and overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ against design values.

8. **PSSR & Handover**
   - Confirm all commissioning records signed.
   - Issue **Mechanical Completion Certificate**, **Pre-Commissioning Certificate**, and **Commissioning Certificate** to Operations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Process Commissioning Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **gated, sequential flow** of the entire Process Commissioning cycle. Note the **alpha-prefixed node identifiers** (e.g., `phaseA`, `gateA1`) and **plain uppercase text labels** (no bold or italics inside quotes), as mandated.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START NEW PLANT PROJECT]) --> phaseA[PHASE 1 MECHANICAL COMPLETION]
    phaseA --> phaseA1[DIMENSIONAL CHECKS]
    phaseA1 --> phaseA2[HYDROSTATIC TEST 1.5 P]
    phaseA2 --> phaseA3[NDT AND PMI VERIFICATION]
    phaseA3 --> gateA1{MC GATE 100 PERCENT SIGN OFF}
    gateA1 -- NO --> phaseA
    gateA1 -- YES --> phaseB[PHASE 2 PRE COMMISSIONING]
    phaseB --> phaseB1[INSTRUMENT LOOP CHECK]
    phaseB1 --> phaseB2[SIS AND ESD FUNCTIONAL TEST]
    phaseB2 --> phaseB3[MOTOR BUMP AND ROTATION CHECK]
    phaseB3 --> phaseB4[AIR OR STEAM BLOWING]
    phaseB4 --> gateB1{PRE COMM GATE SIGN OFF}
    gateB1 -- NO --> phaseB
    gateB1 -- YES --> phaseC[PHASE 3 COMMISSIONING]
    phaseC --> phaseC1[COLD UTILITY TRIAL]
    phaseC1 --> phaseC2[WARM STEAM OR HOT OIL]
    phaseC2 --> phaseC3[HOT PROCESS FLUID INTRODUCTION]
    phaseC3 --> gateC1{COMMISSIONING GATE SIGN OFF}
    gateC1 -- NO --> phaseC
    gateC1 -- YES --> phaseD[PHASE 4 PERFORMANCE TEST]
    phaseD --> phaseD1[72 TO 168 HOUR CONTINUOUS RUN]
    phaseD1 --> phaseD2[CAPACITY AND QUALITY VERIFICATION]
    phaseD2 --> gateD1{GTP PASS GATE}
    gateD1 -- NO --> phaseC
    gateD1 -- YES --> phaseE[PHASE 5 PSSR AND HANDOVER]
    phaseE --> phaseE1[HAZOP RE VERIFICATION]
    phaseE1 --> phaseE2[OPERATOR TRAINING COMPLETION]
    phaseE2 --> phaseE3[EMERGENCY DRILL EXECUTION]
    phaseE3 --> phaseE4[DOCUMENTATION DOSSIER ISSUE]
    phaseE4 --> gateE1{PSSR FINAL GATE}
    gateE1 -- NO --> phaseE
    gateE1 -- YES --> opsA([OPERATIONS HANDOVER])
    opsA --> stopA([COMMERCIAL PRODUCTION BEGINS])

    style startA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style stopA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style gateA1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    style gateB1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    style gateC1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    style gateD1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    style gateE1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.2 Process Safety Interlock Verification Architecture

The block diagram below illustrates the **layered verification of safety interlocks** during commissioning, mapped per **ISA 84 / IEC 61511** layers of protection.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    processVar[PROCESS VARIABLE PT TT FT] --> sisLayer1[LAYER 1 SIS SENSOR]
    sisLayer1 --> logicSolver[LOGIC SOLVER PLC AND DCS]
    logicSolver --> sisLayer2[LAYER 2 SIS FINAL ELEMENT ESD VALVE]
    sisLayer2 --> actuate[ACTUATION STOP OR ISOLATE]

    processVar --> bpcLayer[LAYER 3 BPCS REGULATORY CONTROL]
    processVar --> alarmLayer[LAYER 4 ALARM AND OPERATOR ACTION]
    processVar --> reliefLayer[LAYER 5 RELIEF DEVICE PSV AND RD]
    processVar --> passiveLayer[LAYER 6 PASSIVE PROTECTION CONTAINMENT]

    actuate --> verifyRecord[COMMISSIONING VERIFICATION RECORD]
    bpcLayer --> verifyRecord
    alarmLayer --> verifyRecord
    reliefLayer --> verifyRecord
    passiveLayer --> verifyRecord
    verifyRecord --> pssrFinal[PSSR SIGN OFF GATE]

    style verifyRecord fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style pssrFinal fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    style actuate fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Commissioning Stakeholder RACI Matrix

The diagram below shows the **RACI** (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) structure for a typical commissioning team.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cMgr[COMMISSIONING MANAGER] --> cLead[COMMISSIONING LEAD]
    cLead --> mechLead[MECHANICAL LEAD]
    cLead --> elecLead[ELECTRICAL LEAD]
    cLead --> instLead[INSTRUMENTATION LEAD]
    cLead --> procLead[PROCESS LEAD]
    cLead --> hseLead[HSE LEAD]
    cLead --> opsLead[OPERATIONS LEAD]

    cMgr --> hseMgr[HSE MANAGER PSMR APPROVAL]
    cMgr --> projDir[PROJECT DIRECTOR ACCOUNTABLE]

    mechLead --> craftTeam1[CRAFT TEAMS]
    elecLead --> craftTeam2[ELECTRICIANS]
    instLead --> craftTeam3[INSTRUMENT TECHNICIANS]
    procLead --> processEng[PROCESS ENGINEERS]
    hseLead --> safetyOff[Safety Officers]
    opsLead --> operators[OPERATORS TRAINEES]

    style cMgr fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style hseMgr fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style projDir fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style cLead fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The diagram above directly answers the common question *"Discuss the roles and responsibilities of personnel involved in process commissioning."* Reproducing a RACI-style block diagram earns **5 to 6 marks** in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Define Process Commissioning. List its five phases in the correct order.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Process Commissioning is the structured, documented sequence of activities undertaken to verify that a newly constructed process plant is installed correctly, operates as per design intent, and is safe for the introduction of process fluids before commercial operation.

The five phases in order are:
1. **Mechanical Completion (MC)**
2. **Pre-Commissioning**
3. **Commissioning (Cold, Warm, Hot)**
4. **Performance Testing (GPT)**
5. **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR) and Handover**

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for sequenced list)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: What is the Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)? Why is it considered a mandatory gate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The PSSR is the **final, formal, multidisciplinary safety review** conducted **immediately before** the introduction of process fluids into a new or modified plant. It confirms that:
- All construction and commissioning procedures are complete and signed off.
- All HAZOP, SIL, and HAZAN recommendations have been closed out.
- Operators are trained and competent.
- Emergency response plans, EOPs, and PPE are in place.
- As-built documentation matches installed plant.

It is **mandatory** under **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM)** and is treated as a **Pass/Fail gate** — no plant may commence operation without a signed PSSR certificate.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for elements + 1 mark for regulatory citation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q: A new ammonia plant has completed mechanical completion. As the Lead Commissioning Engineer:**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **Pre-Commissioning activities** you would carry out, with reference to safety instrumented systems and ESD valves. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the sequence of **Cold, Warm, and Hot Commissioning**, and justify why the introduction of process fluids must be staged rather than simultaneous. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Pre-Commissioning Activities (7 marks):**

Pre-commissioning verifies every system individually, **without** process fluids, using only utilities (instrument air, nitrogen, cooling water). The activities are:

1. **Instrument Loop Checking (2 marks)** — Every loop from sensor (TT, PT, FT) through transmitter, marshalling cabinet, DCS I/O, and final control element is checked end-to-end. Calibration certificates are issued.

2. **Safety Instrumented System (SIS) Verification (2 marks)** — Each safety function is functionally tested in accordance with **ISA 84 / IEC 61511**. The **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFDavg)** is verified against the required SIL rating, and proof test intervals are logged.

3. **ESD Valve Stroke Testing (2 marks)** — Every Emergency Shutdown valve is stroked in both directions. The **trip time** (typically < 2 seconds for fast ESDs) is recorded. Partial-stroke testing is also verified for valves that cannot be fully shut during normal operation.

4. **Piping Cleaning — Air/Steam Blowing (1 mark)** — Piping is blown through with air at **≥ 18.3 m/s** or steam at **≥ 30 m/s** to remove debris. Targets are visually clean at the discharge.

**(b) Cold, Warm, Hot Commissioning Sequence (7 marks):**

| Stage | Fluid | Purpose |
|-------|-------|---------|
| **Cold** | Instrument air, N₂, cooling water | Verify mechanical rotation, control logic, alarm responses, ESD trips |
| **Warm** | Steam, hot oil | Verify thermal expansion, PSV set pressure operation, heat tracing |
| **Hot** | Actual process feed (natural gas, H₂, N₂ in ammonia plant) | Verify steady-state performance vs. design |

**Justification for Staging (3 marks):**

- **Safety:** Sudden introduction of hot, reactive, or flammable fluids can cause **runaway reactions**, **thermal shock**, and **vapor cloud explosions**. Staging allows operators to isolate any fault with minimal inventory at risk.
- **Diagnostic Clarity:** If a problem arises at 20% flow, it is much easier to diagnose than at 100% flow. Staging provides **incremental learning** about the plant's behaviour.
- **Equipment Protection:** Rotating equipment, refractory linings, and catalyst beds require **gradual thermal and pressure ramp-up** (e.g., ≤ **20 °C/hr** for refractory) to avoid mechanical damage.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Q: (a)** What is the **hydrostatic test pressure** for a pressure vessel designed for **12 bar** at **200 °C**? Show the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of the **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** in preventing catastrophic start-up incidents. Cite at least **two historical case studies**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Hydrostatic Test Pressure Derivation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $P_{design} = 12 \text{ bar}$, $T_{design} = 200 \text{ °C}$.

The hydrostatic test pressure combines the **material strength gain at ambient** (factor **1.25**) with the **test safety margin** (factor **1.20**).

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{test} &= 1.25 \times 1.20 \times P_{design} \\
         &= 1.50 \times 12 \text{ bar} \\
         &= \mathbf{18 \text{ bar}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Justification of each factor:**

- **1.25 × factor:** At ambient test temperature, the allowable stress per ASME BPVC is up to 1.25 times the allowable stress at design temperature.
- **1.20 × factor:** Even at the higher material strength, a safety margin is retained because the vessel is unsupported by normal operating stresses (fatigue, creep) and any defect must be detected without yielding the base material.

*(2 marks derivation + 2 marks factor justification + 1 mark numerical answer + 2 marks test procedure: hold time, decay check, water quality)*

**(b) PSSR and Historical Case Studies (7 marks):**

The **PSSR** is the final, formal, multidisciplinary safety review conducted before the introduction of process fluids. It is the **last formal opportunity** to catch missing safeguards, untrained operators, or undocumented procedures.

**Case Study 1 — Texas City Refinery Explosion (2005) (3 marks):**
- **15 workers killed, 180 injured.**
- The raffinate splitter was started up with a **level control valve isolated** and a **bypass line open**, allowing a slug of hot liquid to enter the tower.
- A **PSSR was inadequate**; the start-up procedure did not require verification of level instrumentation, and operators were not fully trained.
- **Lesson:** A robust PSSR must verify that all **interlocks, alarms, and operator competencies** are confirmed **before** start-up.

**Case Study 2 — Buncefield Oil Storage Depot, UK (2005) (2 marks):**
- **43 injured, £1 billion loss.**
- A petrol storage tank was overfilled due to a defective level-indicating system that had **not been functionally tested** during commissioning.
- **Lesson:** Loop checks and **independent high-level alarms** must be verified and recorded during pre-commissioning.

**Synthesis (2 marks):**
Both disasters demonstrate that a **diligent PSSR** — verifying that every instrument, valve, alarm, and procedure is in place — would have prevented the incident. The PSSR is not a paperwork formality; it is a **life-safety gate**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Do NOT write "commissioning" as a single phase.** KTU awards full marks only when you explicitly distinguish **MC, Pre-Commissioning, Cold/Warm/Hot, GPT, and PSSR** as **separate, gated stages**.
> 2. **Do NOT forget the Hold Points.** Each gate must have a named **sign-off authority** (Commissioning Manager + HSE Manager).
> 3. **Do NOT skip the hydrostatic factor derivation.** Writing only "$1.5 \times P_{design}$" loses 2 marks; the **$1.25 \times 1.20$** layering is what the examiner is testing.
> 4. **Do NOT state generic "training was given."** Be specific: **minimum 40 hours per operator**, with documented competency assessment.
> 5. **Do NOT confuse SIS test interval with maintenance interval.** The **proof test interval** is governed by SIL PFDavg calculations, not by standard maintenance schedules.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Process Commissioning is the **gated, sequential** verification of a plant from construction to commercial operation.
- **Five Phases:** **MC → Pre-Commissioning → Commissioning (Cold, Warm, Hot) → GPT → PSSR & Handover**.
- **Hold Points:** Every phase has a **mandatory sign-off gate** — no skipping.
- **Hydrostatic Test Pressure:** $\mathbf{P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{design}}$ (derived as $1.25 \times 1.20 \times P_{design}$).
- **Pneumatic Test Pressure:** $\mathbf{P_{test} = 1.25 \times P_{design}}$.
- **Air Blowing Minimum Velocity:** $\mathbf{18.3 \text{ m/s}}$ (≈ 60 ft/s).
- **Steam Blowing Minimum Velocity:** $\mathbf{30 \text{ m/s}}$.
- **Motor Bump Test Duration:** $\mathbf{\leq 2 \text{ seconds}}$.
- **Vibration Limit (Rotating Equipment):** $\mathbf{V_{rms} \leq 4.5 \text{ mm/s}}$ (ISO 10816-3).
- **PSV Set Pressure:** $\mathbf{P_{set} \leq 1.10 \times MAWP}$.
- **SIS Proof Test Interval Effect:** $\mathbf{PFD_{avg} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2}}$.
- **SIS SIL Bands (PFDavg):** SIL 1: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 2: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 3: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$.
- **PSSR:** Final gate, mandatory under **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119**.
- **Operator Training:** **Minimum 40 hours/shift** before process fluid introduction.
- **Critical Standards:** IEC 61511, ISA 84, ASME BPVC, ISO 10816, API RP 1D.
- **Historical Lessons:** Texas City 2005, Buncefield 2005, BP Deepwater Horizon 2010 — **all** involve **commissioning failures**.
- **Key Personnel:** Commissioning Manager, HSE Manager, Process Lead, Mechanical/Electrical/Instrumentation Leads, Operators.
- **Mandatory Documentation:** As-built drawings, calibration certificates, loop folders, PSSR dossier, training records.
- **Single Most Important Exam Sentence:** *"Commissioning is not a paperwork exercise; it is the last chance to prevent a catastrophic start-up incident."*

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** Begin every commissioning question by drawing the **five-phase gated flow** (even as a simple ASCII chart). This anchors your answer, demonstrates structured thinking, and **earns 1 to 2 easy marks** before you even start writing technical content.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commissioning Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Commissioning Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commissioning** is the **systematic process of verifying, testing, and validating** that an installed industrial facility, plant, equipment, or safety system is built according to design specifications, functions correctly, and is safe to operate under both normal and abnormal conditions **before** being handed over for routine production.

In the context of **Design for Safety (PESFT412 – Module 4)**, **Commissioning Problems** refer to the **unexpected technical, procedural, organizational, and human-factor failures** that arise *during the transitional phase* between construction completion and full operational startup. These problems often act as **latent triggers** for major industrial accidents (e.g., runaway reactions, explosions, toxic releases, fires, electrocution, mechanical disintegration).

The internationally accepted framework for managing commissioning safety is **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSSR – Pre-Startup Safety Review)** and **CCPS Guidelines for Process Safety** during commissioning and startup.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy – "The New Car Break-In Period"**
>
> Imagine you buy a brand-new car. The manufacturer does not simply hand you the keys and say, *"Drive at 200 km/h on a mountain highway in the rain."* Instead, there is a **commissioning phase**: the dealer checks the brakes, the tire pressure, the oil level, the alignment, and the airbags. A test driver (a *commissioning engineer*) takes it through a sequence: idle → low speed → highway → emergency braking.
>
> **Commissioning Problems** are what happens when:
> - The brake fluid line was not properly bled (residual air pockets → brake failure).
> - The fuel line was connected to the windshield washer reservoir (wrong line-up).
> - The airbag was never tested (silent latent failure).
> - The mechanic forgot to torque the wheel nuts (mechanical disintegration).
>
> In a chemical plant, the *stakes* are dramatically higher: a wrongly connected valve can release a tonne of chlorine gas. Commissioning problems are therefore the **"last line of defense"** before the system enters live service, and Design for Safety insists that this phase be engineered with the same rigor as the operating phase.

## 1.3 Why Commissioning is a High-Risk Phase

Statistical evidence from major accident databases (e.g., **HID – Hazardous Incident Database**, **eMARS – Major Accident Reporting System**, **Marsh & McLennan 100 Largest Losses**):

> **Roughly 30–40% of all major process industry losses** occur during **commissioning, startup, and shutdown (CSS)** phases — periods when the plant is **not yet at steady-state**, procedures are still being refined, and operators are not yet familiar with the unit.

- Commissioning is a **dynamic, non-steady-state operation**.
- The plant is being subjected to **first-of-its-kind stress** (first pressure, first temperature, first flow).
- **Incomplete loops** (e.g., instrumentation not yet calibrated) mean operators are effectively "flying blind."
- **Simultaneous work** (construction, testing, vendor commissioning) creates physical and procedural conflicts.

## 1.4 Standard KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology

| Term | KTU 2024 Definition |
|---|---|
| **Pre-Commissioning** | All checks performed *without* introducing process fluids (mechanical completion, NDT, loop checks on air). |
| **Commissioning** | Introduction of process fluids/utilities and functional verification of the system. |
| **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** | Formal, documented review confirming the plant is safe to introduce hazardous materials. |
| **Management of Change (MOC)** | Formal process to review and authorize any deviation from the original commissioning procedure. |
| **Mechanical Completion (MC)** | Confirmation that all construction activities are finished and the system is physically ready for testing. |
| **Ready for Commissioning (RFC)** | Formal handover certificate from construction to commissioning team. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always distinguish between **Pre-Commissioning** (dry/static) and **Commissioning** (dynamic/with process fluids). Examiners award marks when you correctly identify the phase in which a particular problem occurs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Commissioning Problems

KTU 2024 expects students to classify commissioning problems along **four orthogonal axes**:

### Axis 1: By Engineering Discipline
1. **Mechanical Problems** — misalignment, wrong material, gasket failure, dead-heading of pumps, thermal expansion mismatch.
2. **Electrical Problems** — phase reversal, incorrect earthing, missing relays, wrong cable sizing, arc-flash incidents during energization.
3. **Instrumentation & Control Problems** — transmitter ranged incorrectly, I/P converter stuck, control valve installed backward (air-to-open vs. air-to-close), interlocks bypassed.
4. **Process Problems** — dead legs, trapped gas pockets, wrong sequence of line flushing, vacuum collapse, thermal shock.
5. **Safety System Problems** — relief valves sized at 110% set but installed at 80% set; PSV inlet block-valve left closed; SIS bypassed for "temporary" testing; fire-water system uncharged.
6. **Procedural / Organizational Problems** — simultaneous work permits not coordinated; handover between shifts lost information; emergency response plan not in place.

### Axis 2: By Root Cause
- **Design error** carried forward into commissioning (e.g., pump NPSHa < NPSHr discovered only at first start).
- **Construction/Installation error** (e.g., pipe support missing → first hot run causes sag and rupture).
- **Documentation error** (P&ID not updated after field change; line-list mismatch).
- **Human factor** (operator fatigue during night commissioning; untrained contractor).
- **Inadequate procedure** (no HAZOP for commissioning sequence).

### Axis 3: By Consequence Severity
- **Near-miss** (no injury/damage but reveals a latent hazard).
- **Equipment damage** (e.g., dry-run of a centrifugal pump destroys the mechanical seal in 8 seconds).
- **Minor injury** (sprain, burn, electrical shock).
- **Major accident** (toxic release, fire, explosion, fatality).

### Axis 4: By Phase of Occurrence
- Mechanical completion walk-down.
- Energization (first power-on).
- Utility introduction (instrument air, nitrogen, steam).
- Process fluid introduction (first hydrocarbon, first acid).
- Steady-state performance test.
- Hand-over to operations.

## 2.2 The "Hierarchy of Commissioning Defects"

```
LAYER 4: Procedural / Organizational  (e.g., unclear PSSR, no MOC)
LAYER 3: Commissioning Procedure Error (e.g., wrong sequence, missing step)
LAYER 2: Installation Defect           (e.g., misaligned coupling, wrong gasket)
LAYER 1: Design Defect                (e.g., inadequate NPSH margin)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A defect at **Layer 1 (Design)** is the most expensive to fix during commissioning (already fabricated), while a **Layer 4** defect is the cheapest. **Design for Safety philosophy** aims to push defect discovery as far *left* (early in the lifecycle) as possible — i.e., detect it in **HAZOP/3D model review** rather than on the day of first hydrocarbon introduction.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **high-yield quantitative tools** examiners expect KTU students to apply when analysing commissioning problems. Memorize the symbols and units.

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $NPSH_{a} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_{vapor}}{\rho g} \pm h_{static} - h_{friction}$ | Available NPSH at pump suction (commissioning critical) | **m of liquid** |
| 2 | $NPSH_{a} \geq NPSH_{r} + \text{Safety Margin}$ | Pump cavitation criterion; KTU typical margin: **0.5 – 1.0 m** | m |
| 3 | $\Delta P_{relief} = P_{set} \times 1.10$ (or 1.05 for fire case) | Pressure Relief Valve sizing basis (API 520/521) | bar |
| 4 | $Q_{min,\,flush} = \frac{V_{pipe}}{t_{flush}}$ | Minimum flushing flowrate to achieve target velocity (typically **1.5–3.0 m/s**) | m³/s |
| 5 | $t_{purge} = \frac{V}{Q} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C_{initial}}{C_{final}}\right)$ | Time to purge a vessel from one O₂/concentration to another (used in air-to-fuel changeover) | s |
| 6 | $P_{hydrotest} = 1.5 \times P_{design}$ | Hydrostatic test pressure (ASME B31.3) | bar |
| 7 | $T_{thermal\,shock} \leq \Delta T_{max}$ (typically 100 °C for carbon steel) | Maximum allowable temperature differential across a vessel wall to avoid brittle crack | °C |
| 8 | $E_{silon} = \dfrac{\sigma_{allow}}{\sigma_{actual}}$ | Stress ratio for structural integrity during commissioning loads | dimensionless |
| 9 | $LOTO_{sequence} = \{E, L, T, V, B\}$ | Lock-Out Tag-Out sequence: **Energy isolation, Lock, Tag, Verify zero, Bleed/Block** | — |
| 10 | $Risk = Severity \times Probability$ | Standard KTU risk-matrix expression used in PSSR | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Safety Rule (LaTeX):** In KTU answer scripts, never use ASCII pipe `|` inside a math expression. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value or "divides". The same convention is preserved in these notes to ensure PDF rendering integrity.

## 2.4 The 7 Most Common "KTU-Recurring" Commissioning Problem Types

Below are the seven problem archetypes that appear most often in KTU 2024 scheme questions and in real incident reports. Each is mapped to its **cause → mechanism → consequence → control**.

1. **Wrong Material of Construction (MOC) Installed**
   - *Cause:* Pipe spool fabrication error, undocumented substitution.
   - *Mechanism:* First exposure to process fluid causes corrosion/embrittlement.
   - *Consequence:* Leak, rupture, fire.
   - *Control:* **Material traceability log** + PMI (Positive Material Identification) at site receipt.

2. **Pump Dead-Heading / Cavitation**
   - *Cause:* Discharge block valve closed at first run; suction flooded but not vented.
   - *Mechanism:* Rapid energy dissipation → local boiling → mechanical seal damage.
   - *Consequence:* Pump seizure, hot oil release, fire.
   - *Control:* **First-run checklist** verifying all valves per the *line-up diagram*; minimum-flow recycle line commissioned *before* main run.

3. **Valve Line-Up Errors (Reverse-Acting / Air-Failure Action)**
   - *Cause:* Control valve installed upside down or air-failure position not matching safety philosophy.
   - *Mechanism:* Loss of instrument air causes valve to *open* instead of *close* on a hazardous line.
   - *Consequence:* Uncontrolled flow of toxic/flammable material.
   - *Control:* **Valve line-up register** reviewed at HAZOP; air-failure position tagged physically.

4. **Interlock / Bypass of Safety Instrumented System (SIS)**
   - *Cause:* "Temporary" bypass installed to facilitate commissioning and never removed.
   - *Mechanism:* A process deviation occurs but the interlock is silent.
   - *Consequence:* Overpressure, runaway reaction, explosion.
   - *Control:* **Bypass register** with time-limit, approval matrix, and **mandatory removal** before RFC.

5. **Air-to-Hydrocarbon Changeover (Residual Oxygen)**
   - *Cause:* Inadequate purging before introducing flammable gas.
   - *Mechanism:* Fuel + O₂ inside flammable range → ignition source → explosion.
   - *Consequence:* Vessel explosion, fatality.
   - *Control:* **Purge calculation** $t_{purge} = (V/Q) \cdot \ln(C_i/C_f)$; verify < 1% O₂ before hydrocarbon introduction.

6. **Energization of Electrical System without Coordination**
   - *Cause:* Multiple contractors working on adjacent MCC; phase rotation untested.
   - *Mechanism:* Phase reversal rotates a motor in wrong direction; ungrounded system gives shock.
   - *Consequence:* Arc flash, motor damage, personnel electrocution.
   - *Control:* **LOTO permit system** + phase-rotation check + pre-energization checklist (IEEE 1584).

7. **Handover / Communication Failure Between Shifts**
   - *Cause:* Verbal-only handover, no logbook entry.
   - *Mechanism:* Critical isolation (e.g., PSV inlet block-valve) re-opened by next shift unaware of status.
   - *Consequence:* Loss of overpressure protection; latent failure.
   - *Control:* **Formal shift handover document** with status of every critical safety device.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These concepts are not merely academic — they are the **operational backbone** of:

- **Oil & Gas EPC Projects** (Pre-Startup Safety Reviews in refineries).
- **Pharmaceutical Plant Validation** (FDA / GAMP commissioning protocols).
- **Nuclear Power Plant Outage Management** (10 CFR 50.59 commissioning reviews).
- **Aerospace Test Stand Commissioning** (range-safety and failure-mode checks).
- **Smart Factory Commissioning (Industry 4.0)** — digital twin verification of physical asset before energization.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 – Course Outcome (CO) Mapping:**
> - **CO4 (Design and evaluate safety systems for industrial processes):** Directly assessed by the PSSSR / commissioning safety case.
> - **CO5 (Apply HAZOP and LOPA to commissioning scenarios):** HAZOP of the *commissioning sequence* is a Module 4 high-weightage item.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations You Must Be Able to Reproduce in the Exam

### Derivation 1: Purge Time for Air-to-Fuel Changeover (Laminar/Plug-Flow Assumption)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem framing:** A 10 m³ reactor is being commissioned. It currently contains air (21% O₂). The plant intends to introduce methane. To stay below the **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL = 5% methane in air)**, the air must first be replaced with nitrogen until O₂ falls below **1%**, and *then* methane is introduced. Find the minimum nitrogen purge time if the nitrogen flowrate is **200 Nm³/h** at standard conditions. Assume plug flow (well-mixed, conservative).

**Step 1 — State the governing equation.**

For a well-mixed vessel with continuous inflow of nitrogen, the oxygen concentration decays exponentially:

$$
C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{\left(-\dfrac{Q}{V} \cdot t\right)}
$$

where:
- $C(t)$ = oxygen concentration at time $t$ (volume fraction)
- $C_0$ = initial oxygen concentration = **0.21**
- $Q$ = volumetric nitrogen flowrate (same units as $V$)
- $V$ = vessel volume
- $t$ = purge time (same units as $V/Q$)

**Step 2 — Solve for t.**

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{C(t)}{C_0} = e^{-\frac{Q t}{V}}
$$

Taking natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{C(t)}{C_0}\right) = -\frac{Q t}{V}
$$

$$
t = \frac{V}{Q} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C(t)}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.** Convert units to a common base: $Q = 200 \;\text{Nm}^3/\text{h}$, $V = 10 \;\text{m}^3$, $C_0 = 0.21$, $C(t) = 0.01$.

$$
t = \frac{10 \;\text{m}^3}{200 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{0.21}{0.01}\right)
$$

Compute the logarithm first:

$$
\ln(21) \approx 3.0445
$$

Compute the volumetric time constant:

$$
\frac{10}{200} = 0.05 \;\text{h}
$$

Therefore:

$$
t = 0.05 \times 3.0445 = 0.1522 \;\text{h}
$$

Convert to minutes:

$$
t = 0.1522 \times 60 = 9.13 \;\text{minutes}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply engineering safety factor.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU / industry practice requires a **minimum of 5–7 volumetric changes** *or* a 1.5–2× safety factor on the calculated time, whichever is larger.

$$
t_{design} = 2 \times 9.13 = 18.3 \;\text{minutes} \;\;\approx\;\; 19 \;\text{min}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_{purge,\,recommended} \;\geq\; 19 \;\text{minutes}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check.**

Total volume exchanged = $Q \cdot t = 200 \times 0.305 = 61 \;\text{m}^3$, i.e. **6.1 vessel volumes**. This satisfies the 5–7 volume rule of thumb. ✔

---

### Derivation 2: Pressure Relief Valve Set-Pressure Margin During Commissioning

**Given:** A vessel is designed for $P_{design} = 10 \;\text{bar g}$. API 520/521 mandates that the *first* pressure relief valve shall be set at:

$$
P_{set,\,primary} = 1.10 \times P_{design} = 11.0 \;\text{bar g}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify overpressure scenario.**

The most severe credible overpressure during commissioning is **"external fire on an isolated vessel"** (vessel block-valved in, no flow, all heat goes into the inventory). API 520 permits the fire-case set-pressure to be **5% above MAWP**:

$$
P_{set,\,fire} = 1.05 \times P_{MAWP} = 1.05 \times 10 = 10.5 \;\text{bar g}
$$

**Step 2 — Required accumulation.**

Accumulation = (Set pressure) − (Design pressure):

$$
\text{Accumulation}_{\text{fire}} = 10.5 - 10.0 = 0.5 \;\text{bar} \;\;=\; 5\%
$$

This is within the API 521 fire-case allowance of 21% accumulation if the vessel is on true fire relief sizing, but for a *single* PRV, 5% is the standard.

**Step 3 — Why this matters during commissioning.**

During commissioning, the *block-valves* at PSV inlet/outlet are often locked open as a step of **Mechanical Completion**. Forgetting to lock them open is a **classic KTU exam problem**:

$$
\text{Pressure relief path status at handover} = \{PSV_{in} : OPEN,\; PSV_{out} : OPEN,\; Locked\}\;\checkmark
$$

If *any* of the three is missing, the overpressure protection is compromised.

---

### Derivation 3: Pump Cavitation Check at First Run (NPSH)

**Given:** A centrifugal pump must deliver water at $Q = 50 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h}$ from a suction vessel 4 m below pump centerline. Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 1.013 \;\text{bar a}$, water at 30 °C has $P_{vap} = 0.0425 \;\text{bar a}$, suction line friction + fittings head loss = 0.8 m, density $\rho = 996 \;\text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \;\text{m/s}^2$. Manufacturer's pump curve gives $NPSH_{r} = 2.5 \;\text{m}$ at the duty point. **Determine if the pump will cavitate on first start.**

**Step 1 — Write the NPSH-available formula.**

$$
NPSH_{a} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_{vap}}{\rho g} + h_{static} - h_{friction}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term.**

Atmospheric-to-vapor head:

$$
\frac{P_{atm} - P_{vap}}{\rho g} = \frac{(1.013 - 0.0425) \times 10^{5}}{996 \times 9.81} = \frac{97050}{9771} \approx 9.93 \;\text{m}
$$

Static suction head (vessel is *below* the pump → positive suction head):

$$
h_{static} = +4.0 \;\text{m}
$$

Friction loss:

$$
h_{friction} = 0.8 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum them up.**

$$
NPSH_{a} = 9.93 + 4.0 - 0.8 = 13.13 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the cavitation criterion.**

$$
NPSH_{a} \geq NPSH_{r} + \text{margin}
$$

$$
13.13 \;\text{m} \;\geq\; 2.5 + 1.0 = 3.5 \;\text{m}\;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** The pump has a margin of:

$$
\text{Margin} = 13.13 - 2.5 = 10.63 \;\text{m} \;\;\gg\;\; 1.0 \;\text{m (required)}
$$

**Result:** Pump will not cavitate on first run. ✔

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam pitfall:** Students often forget to *convert bar to Pa* ($1\;\text{bar} = 10^5\;\text{Pa}$) and get a numerical result an order of magnitude wrong. Always include units in every step.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Linking Each Commissioning Problem to its Engineering Control

| # | Problem | Phase of Discovery | Quantitative Test | Primary Control | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wrong MOC | Mechanical completion | PMI (x-ray / OES) | Material traceability log | **5** |
| 2 | Pump cavitation | First run | NPSH calculation | Line-up checklist | **7** |
| 3 | Reverse-acting valve | Pre-commissioning loop test | Air-failure test | Valve line-up register | **7** |
| 4 | Bypassed interlock | PSSR | SIS logic simulation | Bypass register + MOC | **10** |
| 5 | Air-hydrocarbon changeover | Utility→process | O₂ analyzer | Purge time calculation | **10** |
| 6 | Electrical phase reversal | Energization | Phase-rotation meter | Pre-energization checklist | **5** |
| 7 | Handover loss | All phases | Logbook audit | Formal handover SOP | **3** |

## 3.3 Worked-Through Case (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Case:** A distillation column is being commissioned. The reflux drum is being purged with nitrogen before introduction of hot hydrocarbon. The operator reports that **after 12 minutes of purge, the O₂ analyzer reads 4.5%**. The commissioning procedure specifies a target of **< 1% O₂** before hydrocarbon introduction. The nitrogen flow is $Q = 150 \;\text{Nm}^3/\text{h}$ and the drum volume is $V = 6 \;\text{m}^3$. Initial O₂ is atmospheric (21%).

**(a) Determine the expected O₂ concentration at 12 minutes if the purge is operating correctly.** (7 marks)

**(b) Identify the most likely commissioning problems and recommend corrective actions.** (7 marks)

---

### Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — State the model.**

$$
C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-\frac{Q t}{V}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the dimensionless time group.**

$$
\frac{Q t}{V} = \frac{150 \times (12/60)}{6} = \frac{150 \times 0.2}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5.0
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the exponential.**

$$
C(12\,\text{min}) = 0.21 \times e^{-5.0}
$$

$$
e^{-5.0} \approx 0.00674
$$

$$
C(12\,\text{min}) = 0.21 \times 0.00674 = 0.001415 = 0.1415\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the actual reading.**

$$
\text{Expected: } 0.14\% \;\;\text{vs.}\;\; \text{Actual: } 4.5\%
$$

The actual reading is **~32× higher** than expected. The purge is *not* working as designed.

---

### Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Root-cause hypothesis (each one is a mark-worthy point):**

1. **Nitrogen flowrate is actually lower than indicated** (e.g., blocked filter, partially closed isolation valve, faulty flowmeter). **Action:** Verify flow at the inlet rotameter; check filter ΔP; check upstream block valve position.

2. **The O₂ analyzer is incorrectly located** (e.g., sample line tapping is in a dead leg, not in active flow path). **Action:** Move the sample probe into the *flowing* gas stream; verify response time of analyzer.

3. **The drum has an unaccounted leak path** (e.g., open vent to atmosphere, leaking sample point, defective PRD). **Action:** Perform a leak-test on the drum; isolate all vents; re-pressurize and monitor decay.

4. **Volume is larger than expected** (e.g., the drum is connected to downstream piping that was not isolated). **Action:** Physically isolate drum from column with a spectacle blind; re-evaluate $V$.

5. **Temperature gradient / stratification** — cold nitrogen stratifies at the bottom; O₂ analyzer is at the top. **Action:** Recirculate N₂ via a small blower until uniform, then verify.

**Corrective Action Decision Tree:**

> 1. *Stop* hydrocarbon introduction. (Maintain safe state.)
> 2. *Raise* a Management of Change (MOC) form.
> 3. *Investigate* using the four hypotheses above in order of cost (cheapest first).
> 4. *Re-test* to confirm $C < 1\%$ O₂.
> 5. *Document* the deviation, root cause, and corrective action in the PSSR file.
> 6. *Authorize* next step only after sign-off by Commissioning Manager + HSE Lead + Operations Lead.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Verdict on (b):** Students who only list "increase nitrogen flow" lose 4 marks. The full marks require **systematic root-cause analysis** of all 5 possible reasons, *prioritised* by ease of verification, with an **MOC / PSSR documentation step**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Commissioning Lifecycle Flow

The following **Mermaid flowchart** captures the canonical sequence of activities in a safely-managed commissioning campaign, with embedded decision gates that act as "go / no-go" barriers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mechanical Completion MC] --> B[Pre-Commissioning Walk-down]
    B --> C{Punch List Closed?}
    C -- No --> C1[Issue Punch Items to Construction] --> B
    C -- Yes --> D[Utilities Energisation]
    D --> E[Loop Checks on Air]
    E --> F[Instrument Calibration]
    F --> G[Inert Purge Verification]
    G --> G1{O2 less than 1 percent}
    G1 -- No --> G2[Halt: Investigate Purge Failure] --> G
    G1 -- Yes --> H[Process Fluid Introduction]
    H --> I[First Run Performance Test]
    I --> J[Pre-Startup Safety Review PSSR]
    J --> K{PSSR Signed Off?}
    K -- No --> J1[Close PSSR Action Items] --> J
    K -- Yes --> L[Hand-over to Operations]
    L --> M[Steady-State Operation]
```

> **Reading guide:** Each diamond (`{}`) is a *barrier* that must be passed before proceeding. If a barrier fails, the workflow loops back. The **PSSR** is the most critical barrier and is mandated by OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 for all covered process plants.

---

## 4.2 Layered Defence Model (Bow-Tie) for a Commissioning Incident

This **block-level functional architecture flow** maps how Design for Safety barriers are *expected* to function during a representative commissioning incident: **"Pump started against closed discharge block-valve → dead-head → seal failure → hydrocarbon release."**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph THREAT
        T1[Closed Discharge Block Valve at First Pump Start]
    end
    subgraph PREVENTION
        P1[Line-up Diagram Verification] --> P2[Pre-Start Checklist Sign-off] --> P3[Minimum-Flow Bypass Line Active]
    end
    subgraph TOP_EVENT[TOP EVENT Dead-Heading and Seal Failure]
        TE1[Mechanical Seal Overheats]
    end
    subgraph MITIGATION
        M1[Seal Flush Plan Active] --> M2[Local Fire Detection] --> M3[Block Valve Closure Procedure] --> M4[Emergency Shutdown ESD]
    end
    subgraph CONSEQUENCE
        C1[Hydrocarbon Spray] --> C2[Pool Fire] --> C3[Personnel Injury Asset Loss]
    end
    T1 --> P1
    T1 --> P2
    T1 --> P3
    P3 --> TE1
    TE1 --> M1
    TE1 --> M2
    TE1 --> M3
    TE1 --> M4
    M4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
```

> **Reading guide:** This is a **bow-tie** representation. The left side (Threat → Top Event) shows preventive barriers; the right side (Top Event → Consequence) shows mitigative barriers. In *good* Design for Safety, **at least three independent barriers** must exist on each side. A *commissioning problem* typically arises when **one or more of these barriers is bypassed, missing, or not yet commissioned**.

---

## 4.3 Root-Cause Tree for "O₂ Above Target During Purge" (Used in Worked Example of §3.3)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Oxygen above 1 percent at end of purge] --> B1[Low actual nitrogen flow]
    A --> B2[Higher effective volume than design]
    A --> B3[Oxygen analyzer reading wrong]
    A --> B4[System leak / open vent]
    B1 --> C1[Blocked inlet filter]
    B1 --> C2[Partially closed block valve]
    B1 --> C3[Flow meter miscalibrated]
    B2 --> D1[Connected piping not isolated]
    B2 --> D2[Internal trays / demister holdup]
    B3 --> E1[Sample line in dead leg]
    B3 --> E2[Sensor drift / not calibrated]
    B4 --> F1[Open atmospheric vent]
    B4 --> F2[Leaking PRD or sample valve]
    B4 --> F3[Flange gasket failed]
```

> **Reading guide:** Use this tree when investigating *any* commissioning deviation. The principle is to **enumerate all plausible causes** and **verify/invalidate each** in order of *lowest cost first*.

---

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Commissioning Documentation Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Execution Plan] --> B[Commissioning Procedure]
    B --> C[Pre-Commissioning Checklist]
    B --> D[Commissioning Procedure Step-wise]
    B --> E[HSE Plan for Commissioning]
    E --> E1[HAZOP of Commissioning Sequence]
    E --> E2[JSA per Step]
    E --> E3[Emergency Response Plan]
    B --> F[Pre-Startup Safety Review PSSR]
    F --> F1[MC Verification]
    F --> F2[Loop Check Records]
    F --> F3[Punch List Closure]
    F --> F4[SIS Validation Reports]
    F --> F5[Operator Competency Matrix]
    F --> G[Hand-over Dossier]
    G --> G1[As-Built Drawings]
    G --> G2[Vendor Manuals]
    G --> G3[Operating Procedures Draft 1]
    G --> G4[Maintenance Procedures Draft 1]
```

> **Reading guide:** In a KTU 14-mark answer, citing **three or more of these documents by name** (e.g., "HAZOP of commissioning sequence", "Operator Competency Matrix", "SIS validation report") demonstrates awareness of the *system-of-systems* approach expected at the B.Tech level.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define "Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)". List any four items verified during a PSSR.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** is a formal, documented review conducted **before** the introduction of hazardous materials (or before plant startup) to confirm that the installation is in **safe operating condition** and that all **design, construction, and commissioning safeguards** are in place and functional.

> **Any four items verified (½ mark each = 2 marks):**
> 1. **Mechanical completion** of all equipment as per design.
> 2. **Loop checks and instrument calibration** completed.
> 3. **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** logic validated; no outstanding bypasses.
> 4. **Operating procedures and emergency response plans** approved and personnel trained.
> 5. **Punch list** of all outstanding construction items closed or risk-assessed.
> 6. **Pre-commissioning records** (e.g., NDT, pressure tests, flushing, drying) reviewed.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is a "Bypass Register"? Why is it critical during commissioning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A **Bypass Register** is a **controlled log** maintained during commissioning that records **every safety interlock, alarm, or trip that has been temporarily bypassed** (e.g., for testing). Each entry includes: tag number, reason, duration, approver, and *mandated date of removal*.

> **Why critical (2 marks):**
> 1. **Prevents latent unsafe state** — bypasses left in place at hand-over are a *direct* cause of major process accidents (e.g., Texas City Refinery, 2005).
> 2. **Provides MOC traceability** — auditors can reconstruct what was disabled, why, by whom, and when.
> 3. **Enables safe hand-over** — the operations team is explicitly informed of any device currently bypassed so they can manage the temporary risk envelope.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned to Dec 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, describe the **six phases of a typical industrial plant commissioning cycle**, identifying the *key safety activity* performed at each phase. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **vertical cylindrical vessel** of internal volume $V = 8 \;\text{m}^3$ is being purged with nitrogen at a flowrate of $Q = 100 \;\text{Nm}^3/\text{h}$ to remove air before introduction of a flammable hydrocarbon. The vessel is initially filled with atmospheric air (21% O₂). Calculate the **time required** to bring the O₂ concentration below **1%** (the safe limit) using a well-mixed model, and recommend a **design purge time** with an appropriate safety factor. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (a) — 7 marks**

| Phase | Activity | Key Safety Activity |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Mechanical Completion (MC)** | All construction finished; physical checks | **Punch list walk-down**; verify no open lines to atmosphere |
| **2. Pre-Commissioning (Static)** | Hydrotest, NDT, instrument air loop checks, insulation | **Pressure test to 1.5 × design pressure**; LOTO register opened |
| **3. Energisation** | First power-on of electrical, instrument air, nitrogen | **LOTO permit**; **pre-energisation checklist**; phase-rotation check |
| **4. Commissioning (Dynamic — Dry)** | Functional tests on air/nitrogen, no process fluid | **SIS logic simulation**; interlock trip test |
| **5. Commissioning (Dynamic — Wet)** | Introduction of process fluids, first-run performance | **HAZOP of commissioning sequence**; **purge verification** (O₂ < 1%) |
| **6. Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR) & Hand-over** | Final review; transfer to operations | **PSSR sign-off**; **bypass register closure**; **operator competency check** |

> **Marking note (½ mark per correct safety activity link; ½ mark for clarity of block diagram):** A labelled block diagram with these six phases and the safety activities earns the full 7 marks.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Identify governing model (1 mark).**

For a well-mixed vessel with continuous gas inflow:

$$
C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-\frac{Q t}{V}}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for t (2 marks).**

$$
t = \frac{V}{Q} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C(t)}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Plug in values (2 marks).**

$$
t = \frac{8}{100} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{0.21}{0.01}\right)
$$

$$
\ln(21) \approx 3.0445
$$

$$
t = 0.08 \times 3.0445 = 0.2436 \;\text{h}
$$

Convert to minutes:

$$
t = 0.2436 \times 60 = 14.6 \;\text{minutes}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply design safety factor (1 mark).**

Industry and KTU-accepted practice: **design purge time = 2 × theoretical** (or minimum **5 vessel volumes**).

Total volumes exchanged = $Q \cdot t / V = 100 \times 0.487 / 8 = 6.1$ volumes ✔.

Recommended:

$$
\boxed{\,t_{design} = 2 \times 14.6 = 29.2 \;\text{minutes} \;\;\approx\;\; 30 \;\text{minutes}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering recommendation (1 mark).**

> "Hold the purge for **30 minutes**, then verify with a **calibrated O₂ analyzer** at the vessel's *highest point* (where air would tend to stratify). **Only proceed** to hydrocarbon introduction when the analyzer reads < 1% O₂ at the top of the vessel and a sample of the exit gas confirms the same."

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned to May 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** Explain the **"Hierarchy of Commissioning Defects"** (4 layers) and illustrate with a **worked example** of how a defect at *Layer 1 (Design)* could propagate undetected until commissioning. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **centrifugal pump** is to be commissioned. The pump is rated for $NPSH_{r} = 3.2 \;\text{m}$ at the design flow of $60 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h}$. The suction source is a tank with the **pump centerline 3 m below** the liquid surface. Atmospheric pressure = 1.013 bar a, water temperature = 40 °C ($P_{vap} = 0.0735 \;\text{bar a}$), suction line and fitting losses total $0.9 \;\text{m}$, $\rho = 992 \;\text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \;\text{m/s}^2$. **Determine if the pump will cavitate on first start**, and recommend **two engineering controls** to be added to the commissioning procedure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B (a) — 7 marks**

**The Four Layers (2 marks):**

1. **Layer 1 – Design Defect** (e.g., pump selected with insufficient NPSH margin).
2. **Layer 2 – Installation Defect** (e.g., pump not properly aligned, coupling misaligned).
3. **Layer 3 – Commissioning Procedure Error** (e.g., discharge valve not verified open before start).
4. **Layer 4 – Procedural / Organizational Defect** (e.g., shift handover missing isolation status).

**Worked Example of Layer 1 Propagation (5 marks):**

> A pump was **specified by the process engineer** with $NPSH_{r} = 2.5 \;\text{m}$. The **site conditions** (hot summer, atmospheric pressure at altitude) actually give $NPSH_{a} = 2.3 \;\text{m}$. This is a *Layer 1* design defect.
>
> During **mechanical completion**, this defect is *not* detected because the walk-down checks the pump physically, not the thermodynamic margin.
>
> During **pre-commissioning loop checks on air**, the issue is *still* hidden — air has very low vapor pressure, so no cavitation.
>
> **At first run with water**, the operator hears a rattling noise. The pump loses suction and is shut down.
>
> **Root cause analysis (post-event) reveals** the design margin was insufficient for summer conditions. The fix is *expensive* — either replace the impeller, raise the suction tank, or throttle flow.
>
> **Lessons:** The defect could have been caught earlier (and far more cheaply) at the **HAZOP / design review** stage by computing $NPSH_{a}$ explicitly, *or* by a **cold-loop test with hot water** before full commissioning.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Compute NPSH-available (3 marks).**

$$
NPSH_{a} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_{vap}}{\rho g} + h_{static} - h_{friction}
$$

Atmospheric-to-vapor head:

$$
\frac{(1.013 - 0.0735) \times 10^{5}}{992 \times 9.81} = \frac{93950}{9731.5} \approx 9.65 \;\text{m}
$$

Static suction head (tank *above* pump → positive):

$$
h_{static} = +3.0 \;\text{m}
$$

Friction loss:

$$
h_{friction} = 0.9 \;\text{m}
$$

Sum:

$$
NPSH_{a} = 9.65 + 3.0 - 0.9 = 11.75 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply cavitation criterion (2 marks).**

$$
NPSH_{a} \geq NPSH_{r} + 1.0 \;\text{m (KTU safety margin)}
$$

$$
11.75 \;\text{m} \;\geq\; 3.2 + 1.0 = 4.2 \;\text{m}\;\;\checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** Pump will *not* cavitate. Margin = $11.75 - 3.2 = 8.55 \;\text{m}$ (very comfortable).

**Step 3 — Two engineering controls to add to the commissioning procedure (2 marks).**

1. **Mandatory first-run checklist** that verifies (a) suction valve fully open, (b) pump primed / flooded, (c) discharge block valve initially closed, (d) minimum-flow recycle line open *before* motor start.
2. **Continuous vibration and bearing-temperature monitoring** during the first 4 hours of run, with auto-trip setpoints, to detect any incipient cavitation or mechanical defect.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Common Mark-Loss Areas):**
> 1. **Confusing pre-commissioning with commissioning** (3-mark loss) — pre-commissioning has *no process fluid*.
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversion** in NPSH (bar to Pa) — 4-mark loss.
> 3. **Omitting the safety factor** in purge-time calculation — 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Listing "increase N₂ flow" without root-cause analysis** in O₂-in-purge case — 4-mark loss.
> 5. **Ignoring the bypass register** as a control for SIS commissioning — 3-mark loss.
> 6. **Failing to mention operator training / competency** in PSSR — 2-mark loss.
> 7. **Mixing up "relief set pressure"** (110% MAWP) and "fire-case set pressure" (105% MAWP) — 3-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **A. Definitions You Must Memorize**
> - Pre-Commissioning = no process fluid.
> - Commissioning = process fluid introduced, functional verification.
> - PSSR = formal safety review before startup.
> - Bypass Register = controlled log of temporarily disabled interlocks.
> - MOC = Management of Change (any deviation from approved design).
> - LOTO = Lock-Out Tag-Out (energy isolation procedure).
> - HAZOP of Commissioning Sequence = systematic review of each commissioning step for hazards.

> **B. The 7 Cardinal Commissioning Problems**
> 1. Wrong MOC installed.
> 2. Pump cavitation / dead-heading.
> 3. Reverse-acting / wrong air-failure valve.
> 4. Bypassed safety interlock.
> 5. Incomplete purge (air + hydrocarbon = explosion).
> 6. Phase reversal on motor energization.
> 7. Handover / communication failure.

> **C. The 4 Critical Safety Documents**
> 1. **Commissioning Procedure** (with HAZOP at every step).
> 2. **Bypass Register** (every bypass logged with expiry).
> 3. **PSSR Report** (final go/no-go).
> 4. **As-Built Drawings + Dossier** (for the operations team).

> **D. The 5 Formulas You Must Be Able to Apply**
> 1. $NPSH_{a} = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_{vap}}{\rho g} + h_{static} - h_{friction}$
> 2. $t_{purge} = \dfrac{V}{Q} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_t}\right)$
> 3. $P_{set,\,primary} = 1.10 \times P_{design}$
> 4. $P_{set,\,fire} = 1.05 \times P_{MAWP}$
> 5. $Risk = Severity \times Probability$ (for PSSR risk-ranking)

> **E. The 3 Engineering Ethics / Professional Points**
> 1. **"Temporary" bypasses are never temporary** — they must have a *fixed* removal date and a *named* approver.
> 2. **The commissioning team is responsible to the *future* operations team** — hand-over documentation is a *moral* obligation, not just paperwork.
> 3. **If a parameter reads "off-design", stop** — never silence an alarm to "make it run" (Flixborough 1974 lesson).

> **F. KTU 2024 Exam Day Strategy**
> - For 3-mark questions: define + 2 short points.
> - For 7-mark sub-questions: definition (1) + diagram (2) + 2 key points (2) + example (1) + conclusion (1).
> - For 14-mark questions: structure into 2 halves; each half follows the 7-mark template.
> - Always end a long answer with a **safety recommendation** or **control measure** — this is where KTU examiners consistently award the "balance marks".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Post-commissioning Documentation, Plant Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Post-Commissioning Documentation — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Post-Commissioning Documentation** refers to the complete, certified, and legally binding set of records, drawings, manuals, certificates, and operational data packages that are compiled, verified, and archived **after** a process plant (or any engineered facility) has been successfully commissioned and handed over from the project execution team to the operations and maintenance team.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this documentation is treated as a **live asset register** — meaning it is not a static one-time deliverable, but a continuously updated record that supports the entire operational life of the plant (often 25–30 years for hydrocarbon/petrochemical plants).

The core objectives of post-commissioning documentation are:

- **Legal Compliance:** Satisfy statutory requirements under the Factories Act 1948 (India), the Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC, 1989), the Public Liability Insurance Act 1991, and the National Disaster Management Act 2005.
- **Operational Continuity:** Provide the operations team with verified as-built information to run the plant safely.
- **Maintenance Reference:** Enable predictive, preventive, and corrective maintenance scheduling.
- **Audit Trail:** Create an evidence chain for any incident investigation, insurance claim, or regulatory audit.

## 1.2 Plant Inspection — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plant Inspection** is the systematic, planned, and documented examination of process equipment, piping, structures, instrumentation, and safety systems — conducted during the operational phase — to verify continued mechanical integrity, fitness-for-service, and compliance with applicable codes (e.g., ASME, API, IS, BS standards).

Plant inspection is a **mandatory activity** under the Indian Boilers Regulations 1950 (for fired equipment), the Static and Mobile Pressure Vessels (Unfired) Rules 1981, and is reinforced by international codes such as:
- **API 510** — Pressure Vessel Inspection Code (in-service)
- **API 570** — Piping Inspection Code
- **API 653** — Tank Inspection Code
- **ASME PCC-2** — Repair of Pressure Equipment

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The House Handover:** Imagine you buy a newly constructed house. The builder gives you a **handover folder** containing: the as-built floor plan (not the design plan, but the *actual* built one), the electrical wiring diagram, the water-line schematic, the list of fixtures with model numbers, warranties for the elevator and water heater, the structural engineer’s certificate, and the fire-safety NOC. Without this folder, you cannot maintain the house, claim warranty, or sell it legally. **Post-commissioning documentation is exactly this handover folder — but for a multi-crore chemical plant.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The Car Service Logbook:** When you buy a car, the manufacturer gives you a service schedule (every 10,000 km: oil change; every 40,000 km: brake pads; every 100,000 km: timing belt). The mechanic inspects, marks results, and stamps the logbook. Plant inspection works the same way — except the "car" is a reactor worth ₹50 crores operating at 100 bar and 450°C, and missing a service entry can cost lives, not just engine life.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Codes Used

Key inspection and documentation metrics used across the industry:

- **Inspection Interval (T):** Measured in **months** or **operating hours**, typically ranging from 12 to 60 months.
- **Remaining Life (R):** Measured in **years**, calculated from corrosion rate data.
- **Probability of Failure (POF):** Dimensionless ratio, scale 1–5 (API RBI methodology).
- **Consequence of Failure (COF):** Categorical A (low) to E (very high) per API 581.
- **Risk = POF × COF:** Used to prioritize inspection resources.
- **Standard reference documents:** P&ID, PFD, Plot Plan, Equipment Datasheets, Isometric Drawings, Line List, Tie-in List, Loop Diagrams, Cause & Effect Matrix.
- **Compliance codes:** ASME Section VIII (vessels), ASME B31.3 (process piping), API 510, API 570, TEMA (heat exchangers), NACE MR0175 (sour service materials).

---

**Key Takeaway for Section 1:**
Post-commissioning documentation is the *legal and engineering memory* of the plant, while plant inspection is the *continuous health-check* mechanism. Together they form the backbone of **Asset Integrity Management (AIM)** and **Process Safety Management (PSM)**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchy of Post-Commissioning Documents

The documentation is structured in a strict hierarchy, often called the **Document Deliverable Index (DDI)** or **Master Document Register (MDR)**. The hierarchy (from top-tier to bottom-tier) is:

### Tier 1 — Strategic & Legal Documents
- Plant Operating License / Factory License
- Environmental Clearance (EC) and Consent to Operate (CTO) from State Pollution Control Board
- Hazardous Material Inventory Statement
- Emergency Response and Disaster Management Plan (ERDMP) — mandatory under MSIHC rules
- Insurance Policy and Asset Register

### Tier 2 — Design and Engineering Records
- **As-Built P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram)** — *the single most important document for safety*
- Process Flow Diagram (PFD) with material and energy balances
- Plot Plan and Hazardous Area Classification Drawing
- Equipment Datasheets (with actual tested performance data)
- Piping Isometric Drawings and Line List
- Cause & Effect Matrix and ESD Logic Diagram
- Instrument Loop Diagrams and Hook-up Drawings

### Tier 3 — Vendor and Manufacturing Records
- Material Test Certificates (MTC) — as per EN 10204 / DIN 50049
- Welding Procedure Specification (WPS) and Procedure Qualification Record (PQR)
- Welder Qualification Test Certificates
- Radiographic and Ultrasonic Examination Reports
- Pressure Test Certificates (hydrostatic / pneumatic)
- Nameplate data and unique equipment tag numbers

### Tier 4 — Commissioning and Performance Records
- Pre-commissioning Checklists (PCC)
- Commissioning Procedures and Test Reports
- Performance Test Certificates and Guarantee Test Results
- Site Acceptance Test (SAT) reports for instrumentation
- Punch List (deficiency list) and its closure status

### Tier 5 — Operational and Maintenance Manuals
- Operating Manual (start-up, normal operation, shut-down, emergency shut-down — ESD)
- Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) — task-specific
- Maintenance Manual with recommended spares list
- Lubrication Schedule and Calibration Records
- Training manuals for operators

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In Part A questions, students are frequently asked: *"List any six post-commissioning documents"* or *"What is the importance of as-built P&ID?"* — memorize the **5-tier hierarchy** above.

## 2.2 Types of Plant Inspection

Plant inspection is classified along multiple axes:

### A. By Timing
- **Baseline Inspection:** Conducted immediately after commissioning to record the initial "as-built" condition. This becomes the benchmark for all future degradation comparisons.
- **Periodic (Routine) Inspection:** Conducted at fixed intervals (e.g., every 24 or 48 months) as per statutory code.
- **Turnaround Inspection:** Conducted during planned plant shut-downs.
- **Emergency Inspection:** Triggered by an incident, alarm, or process upset.

### B. By Methodology
- **Visual Inspection (VT):** The first and most fundamental — 60–70% of all defects are detected visually.
- **Surface NDT:** Dye Penetrant Test (DPT), Magnetic Particle Test (MPT).
- **Volumetric NDT:** Radiographic Test (RT), Ultrasonic Test (UT), Eddy Current Test (ECT).
- **Specialized NDT:** Acoustic Emission, Thermography, Phased Array UT, Time-of-Flight Diffraction (TOFD).

### C. By Risk Approach
- **Time-Based Inspection (TBI):** Fixed calendar interval.
- **Risk-Based Inspection (RBI):** Interval determined by POF and COF (per API 580 / API 581).
- **Condition-Based Inspection (CBI):** Triggered by real-time monitoring data (corrosion coupons, online corrosion probes, vibration sensors).

## 2.3 Risk-Based Inspection (RBI) — The Core Analytical Framework

RBI is the **most exam-relevant** inspection methodology in the KTU 2024 syllabus. It uses a 5×5 matrix approach (or sometimes 5×4):

- **POF (Probability of Failure):** Rate from **1 (very low)** to **5 (very high)**.
- **COF (Consequence of Failure):** Rate from **A (low)** to **E (very high)**, based on safety, environmental, and production impact.

$$\text{Risk Level} = \text{POF Category} \times \text{COF Category}$$

Inspection intervals are then derived:

$$T_{\text{inspect}} = T_{\text{base}} \times f_{\text{POF}} \times f_{\text{COF}}$$

where:
- $T_{\text{base}}$ is the statutory baseline interval (e.g., 48 months for pressure vessels per Indian rules).
- $f_{\text{POF}}$ is the POF adjustment factor (**1.5 for high POF, 1.0 for medium, 0.6 for low**).
- $f_{\text{COF}}$ is the COF adjustment factor (**1.3 for high COF, 1.0 for medium, 0.7 for low**).

## 2.4 Corrosion Rate and Remaining Life Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the most frequently asked numerical problem in KTU examinations** — please master the derivation in Section 3.

The **uniform corrosion rate** is given by:

$$CR = \frac{\Delta t}{T_{\text{op}}} = \frac{t_{\text{original}} - t_{\text{measured}}}{T_{\text{op}}}$$

where:
- $CR$ = corrosion rate in **mm/year** (key unit to remember).
- $\Delta t$ = thickness loss in **mm**.
- $t_{\text{original}}$ = nominal / as-built thickness in **mm**.
- $t_{\text{measured}}$ = current measured thickness in **mm**.
- $T_{\text{op}}$ = operating time since installation or last inspection in **years**.

The **minimum required thickness** (per ASME Section VIII) for a cylindrical shell under internal pressure is:

$$t_{\min} = \frac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6P} + c$$

where:
- $P$ = design pressure in **MPa**.
- $R$ = inside radius in **mm**.
- $S$ = allowable stress of material at design temperature in **MPa**.
- $E$ = joint efficiency (0.70 for spot-examined, 1.00 for fully radiographed).
- $c$ = corrosion allowance in **mm**.

The **remaining service life** is then:

$$R_{\text{life}} = \frac{t_{\text{measured}} - t_{\min} - c_{\text{future}}}{CR}$$

where $c_{\text{future}}$ is the future corrosion allowance reserved for the next inspection cycle.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Corrosion Rate | $CR = \Delta t \,/\, T_{\text{op}}$ | **mm/year** |
| Min. Required Thickness | $t_{\min} = (P \cdot R) \,/\, (S \cdot E - 0.6P) + c$ | **mm** |
| Remaining Life | $R_{\text{life}} = (t_{\text{measured}} - t_{\min} - c_{\text{future}}) \,/\, CR$ | **years** |
| RBI Risk | $\text{Risk} = \text{POF} \times \text{COF}$ | Dimensionless (1 to 25) |
| RBI Inspection Interval | $T_{\text{inspect}} = T_{\text{base}} \times f_{\text{POF}} \times f_{\text{COF}}$ | **months** |
| Hydrostatic Test Pressure | $P_{\text{test}} = 1.30 \times P_{\text{design}}$ (for vessels) | **MPa or barg** |
| Pneumatic Test Pressure | $P_{\text{test}} = 1.10 \times P_{\text{design}}$ (more hazardous) | **MPa or barg** |
| Coverage of NDT (UT) | $\text{Cov} = (\text{Scanned length} \,/\, \text{Total weld length}) \times 100$ | **%** |

> [!NOTE]
> The hydrostatic test factor **1.30** and pneumatic factor **1.10** are very commonly asked as direct one-mark questions in KTU Part A.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Petrochemical & Refining:** RBI is mandatory for FCC units, hydrocrackers, and crude distillation columns where wall loss can lead to fires and toxic releases.
- **Pharmaceutical:** Documentation supports FDA 21 CFR Part 11 (electronic records) and GxP compliance.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler inspection follows the Indian Boilers Act; tube failure analysis is the largest cause of forced outages.
- **Aerospace & Defense:** AS9100D and NADCAP requirements demand exhaustive post-manufacturing and post-test documentation chains.
- **Offshore Oil & Gas:** HSE Case, ALARP demonstration, and Verification Schemes all rely on inspection data to prove barrier integrity.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Corrosion Rate and Remaining Life Calculation (14-Mark Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A distillation column shell has the following data:
> - Original (as-built) thickness: $t_{\text{original}} = 32 \text{ mm}$
> - Thickness measured during inspection after 8 years: $t_{\text{measured}} = 28.5 \text{ mm}$
> - Design pressure: $P = 2.0 \text{ MPa}$
> - Inside radius: $R = 1500 \text{ mm}$
> - Allowable stress: $S = 138 \text{ MPa}$
> - Joint efficiency: $E = 0.85$ (spot radiographed)
> - Corrosion allowance: $c = 3.0 \text{ mm}$
> - Future corrosion allowance reserved: $c_{\text{future}} = 1.5 \text{ mm}$
>
> **Find:** (a) Corrosion rate, (b) Minimum required thickness, (c) Remaining service life.

### Part (a) — Corrosion Rate

**Step 1:** Identify the thickness loss.

$$\Delta t = t_{\text{original}} - t_{\text{measured}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$\Delta t = 32 - 28.5 = 3.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the corrosion rate formula.

$$CR = \frac{\Delta t}{T_{\text{op}}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the operating time (8 years).

$$CR = \frac{3.5}{8} = 0.4375 \text{ mm/year}$$

**Step 5:** Present the result with units and significant figures.

$$\boxed{CR \approx 0.44 \text{ mm/year}}$$

### Part (b) — Minimum Required Thickness

**Step 1:** Use the ASME Section VIII formula for cylindrical shell.

$$t_{\min} = \frac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6P} + c$$

**Step 2:** Compute the denominator first.

$$S \cdot E - 0.6P = 138 \times 0.85 - 0.6 \times 2.0$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate each term.

$$S \cdot E = 138 \times 0.85 = 117.3 \text{ MPa}$$

$$0.6P = 0.6 \times 2.0 = 1.2 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4:** Subtract.

$$S \cdot E - 0.6P = 117.3 - 1.2 = 116.1 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the numerator.

$$P \cdot R = 2.0 \times 1500 = 3000 \text{ MPa}\cdot\text{mm}$$

**Step 6:** Divide and add corrosion allowance.

$$t_{\min} = \frac{3000}{116.1} + 3.0$$

$$t_{\min} = 25.84 + 3.0 = 28.84 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7:** Round up for safety (engineering practice).

$$\boxed{t_{\min} \approx 28.85 \text{ mm}}$$

### Part (c) — Remaining Service Life

**Step 1:** Apply the remaining life formula.

$$R_{\text{life}} = \frac{t_{\text{measured}} - t_{\min} - c_{\text{future}}}{CR}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the numerator.

$$t_{\text{measured}} - t_{\min} - c_{\text{future}} = 28.5 - 28.85 - 1.5$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate.

$$= -1.85 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4:** Interpret the negative result.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Interpretation:** The numerator is **negative**, which means the measured thickness has already fallen **below** the minimum required thickness plus the reserved future corrosion allowance. The vessel is **NOT fit for continued service**. Immediate actions required: (i) engineering assessment for fitness-for-service (per ASME PCC-1 or API 579), (ii) reduce operating pressure, (iii) re-inspect within 6 months, or (iv) plan for replacement / repair.

**Step 5:** Compute the numerical remaining life (which is now meaningless because of negative numerator, but the algebraic answer is).

$$R_{\text{life}} = \frac{-1.85}{0.4375} = -4.23 \text{ years}$$

**Step 6:** Present the engineering conclusion.

$$\boxed{R_{\text{life}} < 0 \implies \text{Vessel is unsafe for continued operation at design pressure. Immediate corrective action required.}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — RBI Inspection Interval Calculation (7-Mark Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** For a pressure vessel in a hydrocarbon service:
> - Statutory baseline inspection interval: $T_{\text{base}} = 48 \text{ months}$
> - POF Category = 3 (Medium)
> - COF Category = D (High — toxic and flammable release)
> - $f_{\text{POF}} = 1.0$, $f_{\text{COF}} = 1.3$
>
> **Find:** (a) Risk category, (b) Revised inspection interval.

### Part (a) — Risk Category

**Step 1:** Use the risk matrix.

$$\text{Risk} = \text{POF} \times \text{COF}$$

**Step 2:** Map categories to numerical values. POF 3 = 3, COF D = 4 (using A=1 to E=5 mapping).

$$\text{Risk} = 3 \times 4 = 12$$

**Step 3:** Classify.

$$\text{Risk} = 12 \implies \text{Medium-High Risk} \implies \text{Inspection priority: 2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Risk Level: Medium-High (12)}}$$

### Part (b) — Revised Inspection Interval

**Step 1:** Apply the RBI interval formula.

$$T_{\text{inspect}} = T_{\text{base}} \times f_{\text{POF}} \times f_{\text{COF}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$T_{\text{inspect}} = 48 \times 1.0 \times 1.3$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate.

$$T_{\text{inspect}} = 62.4 \text{ months}$$

**Step 4:** Round down to a practical inspection cycle.

$$\boxed{T_{\text{inspect}} \approx 60 \text{ months} \, (5 \text{ years})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** Interestingly, when both POF and COF are high, RBI may actually *shorten* the interval (use $f < 1$ to reduce $T_{\text{base}}$ for very high risk), and *extend* the interval for low-risk equipment. This is the **key advantage** of RBI over rigid time-based inspection — it optimizes resources.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right NDT Method

This matrix is a **high-yield** answer for the question *"Discuss various NDT methods and their applications."*

| NDT Method | Detects | Best for | Cannot Detect | Cost Factor |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Visual (VT)** | Surface defects, corrosion, leaks | All equipment, first-pass | Subsurface flaws | 1× (lowest) |
| **Dye Penetrant (DPT)** | Surface cracks, porosity | Non-magnetic materials (SS, Al) | Subsurface flaws | 2× |
| **Magnetic Particle (MPT)** | Surface & near-surface cracks | Ferromagnetic materials | Non-ferrous, deep flaws | 3× |
| **Radiographic (RT)** | Internal & surface voids, cracks | Welds, castings | Planar defects parallel to beam | 5× |
| **Ultrasonic (UT)** | Internal cracks, laminations, thickness | Thick sections, in-service | Thin sections, very rough surfaces | 4× |
| **Eddy Current (ECT)** | Surface cracks, conductivity changes | Tubing, heat exchanger tubes | Ferromagnetic thick sections | 4× |
| **Acoustic Emission (AE)** | Active crack growth | Pressure testing, in-service monitoring | Static (non-growing) flaws | 6× |
| **Phased Array UT (PAUT)** | Precise defect sizing & imaging | Critical welds, nozzle joints | Requires trained analysts | 8× |
| **TOFD (Time of Flight Diffraction)** | Through-thickness cracks | Critical welds, dissimilar joints | Surface-breaking defects | 7× |
| **Thermography (IR)** | Hot spots, insulation defects | Electrical panels, refractory | Internal flaws in opaque solids | 3× |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: Why 1.3× for Hydrostatic Test

The hydrostatic test pressure multiplier **1.3** is derived from a combination of:
- A safety factor of **1.0** (no extra load beyond design, but verified at full membrane stress).
- A material stress ratio of approximately **1.10** (test stress / design allowable stress for ductile materials).
- An additional engineering cushion of **0.20** to account for fabrication defects.

The result is rounded to **1.3** in major codes (ASME Section VIII, IS 2825, EN 13445). For the more hazardous **pneumatic test** (using gas instead of liquid), the multiplier is reduced to **1.1** because gas stores far more energy and a failure is explosive — so the test pressure is held lower but the risk control is higher.

$$P_{\text{hydro}} = 1.3 \times P_{\text{design}}$$

$$P_{\text{pneumatic}} = 1.1 \times P_{\text{design}}$$
