# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Post-Commissioning Documentation Workflow

The following Mermaid block maps the **complete workflow** from project handover to operational records, including the inter-relationships between the five tiers of documents.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Project Engineering Phase] --> A2[Construction Phase]
    A2 --> A3[Pre-Commissioning]
    A3 --> A4[Commissioning and SAT]
    A4 --> A5[Mechanical Completion Certificate]
    A5 --> B1[Tier 1: Legal and Statutory]
    A5 --> B2[Tier 2: Design and Engineering]
    A5 --> B3[Tier 3: Vendor and Manufacturing]
    A5 --> B4[Tier 4: Commissioning Records]
    A5 --> B5[Tier 5: Operating and Maintenance]
    B1 --> C1[Asset Integrity Management System]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Periodic Plant Inspection]
    C1 --> D2[RBI Assessment and Planning]
    C1 --> D3[Operating Crew Training]
    D1 --> E1[Inspection Records and Reports]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Document Update and Re-issue]
    F1 --> C1
    D1 --> G1[Defect Detection and Repair]
    G1 --> H1{Fitness for Service?}
    H1 --> I1[Yes: Continue Operation]
    H1 --> I2[No: Derate, Repair, or Replace]
    I2 --> F1
```

**Explanation of Nodes (for the student):**
- The chart shows that documentation is **not a one-way street** — the right-hand loop (E1 → F1 → C1) shows that inspection findings *update* the documentation register, which is the essence of the **living document** concept.
- The diamond node `H1` represents the **fitness-for-service decision** (per API 579 / ASME PCC-1).
- The block `I2` leads back to the documentation update, closing the integrity loop.

## 4.2 Plant Inspection Methodology Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Equipment Entering Inspection Phase] --> P2{Equipment Type?}
    P2 --> P3[Pressure Vessel]
    P2 --> P4[Piping Circuit]
    P2 --> P5[Storage Tank]
    P2 --> P6[Heat Exchanger]
    P2 --> P7[Rotating Equipment]
    P3 --> Q1[Refer API 510]
    P4 --> Q2[Refer API 570]
    P5 --> Q3[Refer API 653]
    P6 --> Q4[Refer TEMA and ASME]
    P7 --> Q5[Refer API 686 and ISO 13373]
    Q1 --> R1[Baseline Visual and UT Thickness Survey]
    Q2 --> R1
    Q3 --> R1
    Q4 --> R1
    Q1 --> R2[NDT Selection per Defect Type]
    Q2 --> R2
    Q3 --> R2
    Q4 --> R2
    R1 --> S1[Corrosion Rate Calculation]
    R2 --> S1
    S1 --> T1[Remaining Life Estimation]
    T1 --> U1{Risk Acceptable?}
    U1 --> V1[Yes: Schedule Next Inspection]
    U1 --> V2[No: Trigger RBI or FFS Study]
    V2 --> W1[Define Repair or Replacement]
    W1 --> X1[Update Master Inspection Plan]
    V1 --> X1
```

**Explanation:**
- The left branch (P2 → P3–P7) shows the **code-based classification** — the first decision the inspector must make is which governing code applies.
- The middle path (R1 → S1 → T1) shows the **standard analytical chain** — measure, calculate, predict.
- The decision diamond U1 represents the **critical safety gate** that triggers RBI or Fitness-For-Service studies.

## 4.3 Risk-Based Inspection (RBI) Decision Matrix Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Equipment Item] --> B[Identify Failure Modes]
    B --> C[Estimate Probability of Failure POF]
    C --> D[POF 1 to 5 Scale per API 581]
    B --> E[Estimate Consequence of Failure COF]
    E --> F[COF A to E Scale considering Safety, Env, Cost]
    D --> G[Multiply POF and COF]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Risk Level?}
    H --> I[Risk 1 to 4: Low — Extend Interval]
    H --> J[Risk 5 to 9: Medium — Standard Interval]
    H --> K[Risk 10 to 15: High — Reduce Interval]
    H --> L[Risk 16 to 25: Very High — Immediate Action]
    I --> M[Update RBI Plan and Inspection Database]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> N[Inspect Now and Derate or Replace]
    N --> M
```

**Explanation:**
- This Mermaid chart visually represents the **5×5 RBI matrix** in a process flow form.
- The four risk bands (I, J, K, L) correspond to the four **inspection strategies** in industry: relaxed, standard, tightened, and immediate-action.

## 4.4 Asset Integrity Management (AIM) Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sub1[Plant Operations]
    sub2[Maintenance Execution]
    sub3[Inspection and NDT]
    sub4[Engineering and RBI]
    sub5[Document Control and Records]
    sub6[HSE and Compliance]
    sub7[Management Review and KPI]
    sub1 --> sub3
    sub2 --> sub3
    sub3 --> sub4
    sub4 --> sub5
    sub5 --> sub6
    sub6 --> sub7
    sub7 --> sub1
    sub7 --> sub2
    sub4 --> sub2
    sub5 --> sub1
```

**Explanation:**
- This is a **closed-loop integrity management cycle** consistent with ISO 55000 (Asset Management) and API 580.
- The plant operations and maintenance generate data; inspection converts it to evidence; engineering converts evidence to risk-based plans; documents provide the audit trail; HSE verifies legal compliance; management reviews the entire system using KPIs like **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** and **Inspection Backlog %**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

> *"What is the significance of the as-built P&ID in post-commissioning documentation? List any four key differences between a design P&ID and an as-built P&ID."*

**Model Answer:**

The **as-built P&ID (Piping and Instrumentation Diagram)** is the single most important post-commissioning document because it represents the *actual* installed configuration of the process plant, including all field modifications, red-marks, and tie-ins made during construction and commissioning. It is the master reference used by operations, maintenance, safety, and inspection teams for the entire life of the plant.

**Four key differences between design P&ID and as-built P&ID:**

| Aspect | Design P&ID | As-Built P&ID |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Purpose** | Used for engineering and procurement | Used for operations, maintenance, and inspection |
| **Modifications** | Shows the original intended design | Incorporates all field changes, red-marks, and deviations |
| **Update Frequency** | Issued once with revisions during design | Continuously updated throughout plant life |
| **Approval** | Approved by Lead Process Engineer | Re-approved by Process, Operations, and Safety after commissioning |

The as-built P&ID is **legally binding** and is used in MOC (Management of Change) reviews, HAZOP revalidation, and incident investigations.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> *"Explain the difference between hydrostatic testing and pneumatic testing of pressure vessels. Why is pneumatic testing performed at a lower pressure multiplier (1.1×) compared to hydrostatic (1.3×)?"*

**Model Answer:**

**Hydrostatic testing** uses a liquid (usually water) as the test medium, while **pneumatic testing** uses a gas (air or nitrogen). Hydrostatic testing is the **preferred and default** method because liquid is incompressible — if the vessel fails, the released energy is limited and the failure mode is a slow leak, not an explosive rupture.

Pneumatic testing is used only when hydrostatic testing is impractical (e.g., very large vessels, equipment that cannot be dried, or vessels with very thin corrosion allowances where the test water could cause damage).

The pressure multipliers are:

$$P_{\text{hydro}} = 1.30 \times P_{\text{design}}$$

$$P_{\text{pneumatic}} = 1.10 \times P_{\text{design}}$$

The **pneumatic multiplier is lower (1.1×)** because gas is highly compressible and stores enormous strain energy. A pneumatic failure is **explosive and catastrophic** — the released energy follows the adiabatic expansion equation $E = P \cdot V / (\gamma - 1)$, which can be 200 to 1000 times greater than the equivalent hydrostatic failure. Therefore, the test pressure is deliberately reduced to **1.1×** to limit the stored energy, and additional safety precautions (blast walls, exclusion zones, remote read-out) are mandatory during pneumatic testing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Main Question]**

> *(a)* Explain in detail the **five-tier hierarchy of post-commissioning documentation** with examples of documents in each tier. Why is this hierarchy important from a safety and statutory compliance perspective? **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> *(b)* A pressure vessel has the following inspection data: $t_{\text{original}} = 30 \text{ mm}$, $t_{\text{measured}} = 25 \text{ mm}$ after 5 years, $P = 1.5 \text{ MPa}$, $R = 1200 \text{ mm}$, $S = 120 \text{ MPa}$, $E = 1.0$, $c = 2.0 \text{ mm}$, $c_{\text{future}} = 1.0 \text{ mm}$. Calculate: (i) Corrosion rate, (ii) Minimum required thickness, (iii) Remaining life. Comment on fitness for service. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

The five-tier hierarchy of post-commissioning documentation is:

**Tier 1 — Strategic and Legal Documents (1 Mark)**
These are the highest-level documents required by law. Examples: Factory License under the Factories Act 1948, Consent to Operate from the State Pollution Control Board under the Water and Air Acts, Hazardous Material Inventory under MSIHC Rules 1989, and the Emergency Response and Disaster Management Plan (ERDMP). Without these documents, the plant **cannot legally operate**.

**Tier 2 — Design and Engineering Records (1.5 Marks)**
These are the technical drawings and specifications that describe *what* was built. Examples: As-Built P&ID, PFD, Equipment Datasheets, Plot Plan, Hazardous Area Classification Drawing, Cause & Effect Matrix. These are used daily by operations and safety teams for troubleshooting, MOC, and HAZOP studies.

**Tier 3 — Vendor and Manufacturing Records (1.5 Marks)**
These document the *quality* of fabrication. Examples: Material Test Certificates (MTC), Welding Procedure Specification (WPS), Welder Qualifications, Radiographic and Ultrasonic Examination Reports, Hydrostatic Test Certificates, and Nameplate data. They are essential for traceability in case of material failure.

**Tier 4 — Commissioning and Performance Records (1.5 Marks)**
These prove that the plant was *verified* to work. Examples: Pre-commissioning Checklists, Commissioning Procedures, Site Acceptance Test (SAT) reports, Performance Test Results, and the Punch List (with closure status). These serve as the legal evidence that the plant met design intent at handover.

**Tier 5 — Operational and Maintenance Manuals (1.5 Marks)**
These guide the *day-to-day running* of the plant. Examples: Operating Manual, Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), Maintenance Manual, Lubrication Schedule, Calibration Records, and Training Manuals. They are continuously updated as the plant evolves.

**Importance of the hierarchy (Bonus 0.5 Mark for coherence):**
The hierarchy ensures that **every layer of plant life is documented** — from license to lubrication. From a safety perspective, the lower tiers (3 and 4) provide *evidence* of integrity, while the upper tiers (1 and 2) define *legal and operational boundaries*. During an incident, the investigator walks down the tiers: Tier 1 (was the plant legal to operate?), Tier 2 (was the design safe?), Tier 3 (was the fabrication sound?), Tier 4 (was it commissioned correctly?), Tier 5 (were SOPs followed?).

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- $t_{\text{original}} = 30 \text{ mm}$
- $t_{\text{measured}} = 25 \text{ mm}$
- $T_{\text{op}} = 5 \text{ years}$
- $P = 1.5 \text{ MPa}$
- $R = 1200 \text{ mm}$
- $S = 120 \text{ MPa}$
- $E = 1.0$ (fully radiographed)
- $c = 2.0 \text{ mm}$
- $c_{\text{future}} = 1.0 \text{ mm}$

**(i) Corrosion Rate (2 Marks):**

$$\Delta t = 30 - 25 = 5 \text{ mm}$$

$$CR = \frac{5}{5} = 1.0 \text{ mm/year}$$

**(ii) Minimum Required Thickness (3 Marks):**

$$t_{\min} = \frac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6P} + c$$

Compute denominator:

$$S \cdot E - 0.6P = 120 \times 1.0 - 0.6 \times 1.5 = 120 - 0.9 = 119.1 \text{ MPa}$$

Compute numerator:

$$P \cdot R = 1.5 \times 1200 = 1800 \text{ MPa}\cdot\text{mm}$$

Divide and add corrosion allowance:

$$t_{\min} = \frac{1800}{119.1} + 2.0 = 15.11 + 2.0 = 17.11 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Remaining Life (2 Marks):**

$$R_{\text{life}} = \frac{t_{\text{measured}} - t_{\min} - c_{\text{future}}}{CR}$$

$$R_{\text{life}} = \frac{25 - 17.11 - 1.0}{1.0} = \frac{6.89}{1.0} = 6.89 \text{ years}$$

**Fitness-for-Service Comment (Bonus 0.5 Mark):**
The vessel has a **remaining life of approximately 6.89 years** at the current corrosion rate. The vessel is **fit for continued service**, but the corrosion rate of 1.0 mm/year is **high** (typical industry average is 0.1 to 0.3 mm/year). **Recommendations:** (a) Reduce the next inspection interval from the statutory 48 months to 24 months, (b) Investigate the root cause of the high corrosion rate (corrosion coupons, process fluid analysis, NDT of similar equipment), (c) Consider corrosion-inhibitor injection or material upgrade if the cause is fluid-side attack.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Question]**

> *(a)* Discuss the various **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** methods used during plant inspection. Compare any four methods in terms of principle, application, advantages, and limitations. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> *(b)* Explain the concept of **Risk-Based Inspection (RBI)** per API 580. Using a 5×5 POF–COF matrix, demonstrate how inspection intervals are revised for a vessel with: $T_{\text{base}} = 60$ months, POF Category = 4, COF Category = E, $f_{\text{POF}} = 0.7$, $f_{\text{COF}} = 0.6$. What is your conclusion? **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**NDT Methods — Overview (2 Marks):**
Non-Destructive Testing is a group of analysis techniques used to evaluate the properties of a material, component, or system without causing damage. The common NDT methods used during plant inspection are:

1. **Visual Testing (VT)**
2. **Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT)**
3. **Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)**
4. **Radiographic Testing (RT)**
5. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)**
6. **Eddy Current Testing (ECT)**
7. **Acoustic Emission (AE)**
8. **Phased Array UT (PAUT)**
9. **Time-of-Flight Diffraction (TOFD)**
10. **Thermography (IR / Infrared)**

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Method | Principle | Application | Advantage | Limitation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Visual (VT)** | Direct eye examination, often with borescope | All equipment, first-line check | Fast, low cost, no special prep | Detects only surface defects |
| **Dye Penetrant (DPT)** | Capillary action draws dye into surface cracks | Non-magnetic materials (SS, Al, Cu) | Simple, low cost, very sensitive to surface cracks | Cannot detect subsurface defects |
| **Magnetic Particle (MPT)** | Magnetic field disturbed by surface/subsurface flaws | Ferromagnetic materials only | Detects surface and near-surface defects rapidly | Not for non-ferrous, requires demagnetization |
| **Ultrasonic (UT)** | High-frequency sound waves reflect from internal flaws | Thick sections, welds, in-service thickness | Detects deep and subsurface flaws, gives thickness | Requires skilled operator, rough surfaces limit use |
| **Radiographic (RT)** | X-rays or gamma rays pass through material; defects shown on film | Welds, castings, critical joints | Permanent record, detects internal flaws | Radiation hazard, slow, expensive, two-side access needed |
| **Eddy Current (ECT)** | Induced eddy currents disturbed by defects | Heat exchanger tubes, thin-wall tubing | Fast, no couplant needed | Limited depth, for conductive materials only |

**Conclusion on Method Selection (2 Marks):**
The choice of NDT method depends on the **material**, **type of defect suspected**, **equipment geometry**, and **in-service vs. shutdown condition**. A modern inspection campaign typically uses a **combination** — e.g., VT for first pass, DPT for confirmation of surface cracks, UT for thickness mapping, and PAUT/TOFD for critical welds. The "right method" is dictated by the **fitness-for-service objective**, not the inspector's familiarity.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Concept of RBI per API 580 (3 Marks):**
Risk-Based Inspection (RBI) is a risk-management methodology that integrates **engineering analysis**, **inspection planning**, and **resource allocation** to optimize plant integrity. Per API 580, RBI is based on the principle that inspection effort should be **proportional to risk** — high-risk equipment is inspected more frequently and with more rigorous methods, while low-risk equipment can have extended intervals.

The two key parameters are:
- **POF (Probability of Failure):** Likelihood that a given failure mode will occur. Categories 1 (very low) to 5 (very high). Determined by damage factors (corrosion, fatigue, creep, etc.), inspection history, and operating conditions.
- **COF (Consequence of Failure):** Severity of outcome if failure occurs. Categories A (low) to E (very high), based on safety impact (injuries, fatalities), environmental impact (toxic release, fire), and production loss.

**Risk Matrix (1 Mark):**
The product of POF and COF gives the **Risk Level (1 to 25)**, which maps to one of four inspection strategies:
- **Risk 1–4 (Low):** Relaxed interval
- **Risk 5–9 (Medium):** Standard interval
- **Risk 10–15 (High):** Tightened interval
- **Risk 16–25 (Very High):** Immediate action / replacement

**Numerical Calculation (3 Marks):**

Given: $T_{\text{base}} = 60$ months, POF = 4, COF = E = 5, $f_{\text{POF}} = 0.7$, $f_{\text{COF}} = 0.6$.

**Step 1:** Compute Risk.

$$\text{Risk} = \text{POF} \times \text{COF} = 4 \times 5 = 20$$

**Step 2:** Risk Level.

$$\text{Risk} = 20 \implies \text{Very High Risk} \implies \text{Immediate action required}$$

**Step 3:** Compute revised inspection interval.

$$T_{\text{inspect}} = T_{\text{base}} \times f_{\text{POF}} \times f_{\text{COF}}$$

$$T_{\text{inspect}} = 60 \times 0.7 \times 0.6 = 25.2 \text{ months}$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion.

> [!WARNING]
> The revised interval is only **25.2 months** (≈ 2 years) — but in the Very High Risk band, RBI recommends **immediate inspection plus an engineering assessment for derating or replacement**. The interval of 25.2 months applies *after* the immediate action. The $f$ values being less than 1 reflect that **high-risk equipment is inspected more frequently**, not less. The numerical $T_{\text{inspect}}$ is the **maximum allowable interval** — not a target. The actual next inspection should be scheduled as soon as practical.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Costing Students 2–3 Marks Each):**
>
> 1. **Unit Error in Corrosion Rate:** Students frequently compute $CR$ in mm/month or forget to convert the operating time to years. Always state the unit clearly as **mm/year**.
> 2. **Confusing POF and COF Categories:** POF is **1 to 5** (probability), COF is **A to E** (consequence). In RBI calculations, map COF letters to numbers: A=1, B=2, C=3, D=4, E=5.
> 3. **Forgetting the Corrosion Allowance $c$:** Students often drop the $+c$ term in the $t_{\min}$ formula, leading to a *less* conservative thickness — this is dangerous and is heavily penalized.
> 4. **Negative Remaining Life Interpretation:** When $R_{\text{life}} < 0$, students write the negative number without explaining that **the vessel is unfit for service** and that an **engineering assessment (FFS)** is required.
> 5. **Mixing Up Hydrostatic and Pneumatic Multipliers:** Memorize: **Hydro = 1.3×**, **Pneumatic = 1.1×**. Reverse answer is a guaranteed zero in that part.
> 6. **Confusing RBI with CBI:** RBI is **risk-based** (uses POF and COF), while CBI is **condition-based** (uses real-time sensor data). They are **not** the same.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist as your final-day revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

### A. Post-Commissioning Documentation — Key Points
- **Five tiers** (Legal → Design → Vendor → Commissioning → Operations) — must know at least 3 examples in each.
- **As-built P&ID** is the single most important document — used in MOC, HAZOP, and incident investigation.
- **Material Test Certificates (MTC)** per EN 10204 (3.1, 3.2) are the foundation of traceability.
- **Commissioning certificates** include Mechanical Completion, SAT, Performance Test, and Punch List closure.
- **Statutory compliance** is mandatory under the Factories Act, MSIHC Rules, and Indian Boilers Regulations.

### B. Plant Inspection — Key Points
- **Baseline inspection** sets the benchmark; all future inspections compare against it.
- **Visual Testing (VT)** detects 60–70% of defects — always do it first.
- **UT thickness mapping** is the primary tool for corrosion rate calculation.
- **API 510, 570, 653** are the governing codes for vessels, piping, and tanks respectively.
- **Hydrostatic test** = 1.3× design; **Pneumatic test** = 1.1× design (with extra safety measures).
- **Inspection intervals** can be revised by **RBI** using $T_{\text{inspect}} = T_{\text{base}} \times f_{\text{POF}} \times f_{\text{COF}}$.

### C. Essential Formulas (Memorize These Three)
1. $CR = \Delta t \,/\, T_{\text{op}}$ → **mm/year**
2. $t_{\min} = (P \cdot R) \,/\, (S \cdot E - 0.6P) + c$ → **mm**
3. $R_{\text{life}} = (t_{\text{measured}} - t_{\min} - c_{\text{future}}) \,/\, CR$ → **years**

### D. RBI Quick-Reference
- **POF:** 1 to 5 scale — based on damage mechanisms and operating severity.
- **COF:** A to E scale — based on safety, environment, and production.
- **Risk = POF × COF:** 1–4 Low, 5–9 Medium, 10–15 High, 16–25 Very High.
- **High Risk → Shorten Interval:** $f < 1$ in the interval formula.
- **Low Risk → Extend Interval:** $f > 1$ in the interval formula.

### E. Common NDT Pairings (Memorize for "Compare" Questions)
- **Ferromagnetic + Surface crack → MPT**
- **Non-magnetic (SS, Al) + Surface crack → DPT**
- **Internal flaw in weld → RT or UT**
- **Tube bundle inspection → ECT**
- **In-service thickness → UT (with couplant)**
- **Critical weld, full volumetric → PAUT or TOFD**
- **Active crack growth during test → Acoustic Emission**

### F. Examiners' Favorite Trap Topics
- "What is the difference between RBI and CBI?" — answer must mention **real-time sensors** vs. **risk scoring**.
- "Why is pneumatic testing rarely used?" — answer: **explosive failure energy**, requires blast walls and remote operation.
- "What happens if measured thickness is less than $t_{\min}$?" — answer: **immediate shut-down, FFS study, derate or repair** — never *continue* as-is.
- "What document is updated first after a field change?" — answer: **As-built P&ID with red-mark**, followed by MOC approval.

### G. Connection to Higher Modules and Industry Practice
- This topic links directly to **Module 5** (Hazard Analysis and Risk Management) and **Module 6** (Emergency Planning and Response).
- In industry, the entire framework described here is implemented as an **Integrity Management System (IMS)** or **Plant Integrity System (PIS)**, audited annually under ISO 55001 and API SIFE (Senior Inspector of Fixed Equipment) certification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When a question asks you to *"discuss"* or *"explain in detail"*, structure your answer in **bullet points with sub-headings** (e.g., *Definition, Types, Procedure, Advantages, Limitations, Examples*). KTU examiners reward **structured, well-labeled answers** with 1 to 2 extra marks compared to a single-paragraph response.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Vessel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT412 - DESIGN FOR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pressure Vessel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pressure Vessel** is a closed container (cylindrical, spherical, or of any other geometric shape) that is designed to hold gases, vapors, liquids, or a combination of these at pressures substantially different (higher or lower) from the ambient atmospheric pressure. The primary design intent, from a **Design for Safety** perspective, is to contain the working fluid reliably under all anticipated operating, transient, and failure conditions without loss of structural integrity, thereby protecting human life, the environment, and physical assets.

According to the **Indian Standard IS 2825:1969** (reaffirmed 2014) and the **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section VIII**, a pressure vessel is typically defined as a container with:
- A design pressure exceeding **0.1 MPa (1 bar) gauge**, AND
- An internal volume greater than **0.025 m³ (25 liters)** containing a fluid (gas, vapor, or liquid under pressure).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** In *Design for Safety*, a pressure vessel is not merely studied as a mechanical structure but as a **high-consequence asset**. Its failure can result in catastrophic consequences — loss of life, environmental contamination, and major economic loss. Hence, design follows codified, prescriptive, and performance-based safety principles.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a balloon. The stretched rubber skin of the balloon is under tension because the air inside pushes outward in all directions. If you add more air, the rubber stretches more, and eventually it bursts.

A pressure vessel works on the **same physical principle**, but instead of fragile rubber, it uses **engineered metal shells (carbon steel, stainless steel, alloy steels)** that are designed with specific thicknesses, geometries, weld qualities, and safety margins to handle enormous internal forces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy:** Think of a pressure vessel as a "**fortress**" that must hold back a relentless enemy (the pressurized fluid). Every wall, every rivet, every welded seam is part of a **defense system**. The Design for Safety engineer is the **architect of that fortress**, ensuring it never falls.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Atmospheric Pressure:** $p_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$
- **Design Pressure ($P$):** Maximum expected working pressure + safety margin
- **Design Temperature ($T$):** Maximum expected metal temperature during operation
- **Allowable Stress ($S$):** The maximum permissible stress the material can safely carry
- **Joint Efficiency ($E$):** A factor (between 0 and 1) that accounts for weld quality
- **Corrosion Allowance ($C_A$):** Extra thickness provided to compensate for material loss over the design life (typically **1.0 mm to 3.0 mm**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Examinations:** Always express pressure in **MPa (N/mm²)** and dimensions in **mm** when using stress formulas. The standard wall-thickness formulas assume **consistent units** — a common source of numerical errors in exams.

## 1.4 Classification of Pressure Vessels

Pressure vessels are classified based on multiple criteria relevant to safety design:

| Classification Basis | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **By Wall Thickness Ratio** | Thin-walled ($D/t \geq 20$) | Hoop stress dominates; simple formulas used |
| | Thick-walled ($D/t < 20$) | Radial stress significant; Lamé's equations required |
| **By Shape** | Cylindrical | Most common — boilers, storage tanks |
| | Spherical | Highest strength-to-volume ratio; ideal for high pressure |
| | Conical/Torispherical | Used as end closures (heads) |
| **By Service** | Fired (heated) | Boilers, superheaters — high thermal stresses |
| | Unfired | Compressed air tanks, chemical reactors |
| **By Temperature** | Cryogenic ($T < -29°C$) | Liquefied gases (LNG, LOX) — requires special materials |
| | Ambient ($T = -29°C$ to $345°C$) | Standard carbon steel vessels |
| | High-temperature ($T > 345°C$) | Creep becomes a concern |
| **By Hazard** | Lethal substances | Highest code category (Class I) |
| | Non-lethal substances | Lower code category |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stresses in Thin-Walled Cylindrical Pressure Vessels

For a thin-walled cylindrical vessel of **internal diameter $D$**, **wall thickness $t$**, and **internal pressure $p$**, the following stresses develop in the shell wall.

### 2.1.1 Circumferential (Hoop) Stress — $\sigma_h$

This is the stress acting along the **circumference** of the cylinder, generated by the radial expansion of the cylindrical shell under internal pressure.

**Physical Reasoning:**
- The internal pressure pushes outward on the cylindrical wall in all radial directions.
- Consider a longitudinal section through the cylinder. The internal pressure acting on the projected area is balanced by the tensile force in the two wall sections on either side.
- The projected area of the internal pressure on a half-cylinder of length $L$ is: $A = D \cdot L$
- The resisting cross-sectional area of the two wall sections is: $A_{wall} = 2 \cdot t \cdot L$

By **force equilibrium** (projected-area method):

$$\sum F_x = 0 \implies p \cdot D \cdot L = \sigma_h \cdot 2 \cdot t \cdot L$$

Solving for $\sigma_h$:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$$

### 2.1.2 Longitudinal (Axial) Stress — $\sigma_l$

This is the stress acting along the **axis** of the cylinder, generated by the pressure acting on the end closure (head) of the vessel.

**Physical Reasoning:**
- The internal pressure acts on the end cap of area $A_{cap} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$.
- This force is resisted by the cylindrical wall acting in a circumferential ring of cross-section $A_{wall} = \pi D \cdot t$.

By **force equilibrium**:

$$p \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \sigma_l \cdot \pi D \cdot t$$

Solving for $\sigma_l$:

$$\sigma_l = \frac{p \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$$

### 2.1.3 Key Observation — Hoop Stress is **TWICE** the Longitudinal Stress

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$\boxed{\sigma_h = 2 \cdot \sigma_l}$$
>
> This fundamental relationship has a **profound safety implication**: **Longitudinal seams (girth welds) carry half the stress of circumferential seams (longitudinal welds)**. Therefore, in pressure vessel design, **circumferential (longitudinal) welds must be of higher quality** — typically 100% radiographed, while girth welds may be spot-checked.

### 2.1.4 Maximum Shear Stress in Cylindrical Wall

Using **principal stress analysis** where $\sigma_h$ and $\sigma_l$ are the in-plane principal stresses and the radial stress $\sigma_r \approx 0$ for thin walls:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} = \frac{p \cdot D}{8 \cdot t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For thin-walled pressure vessels, **yielding is governed by the maximum shear stress theory (Tresca criterion)** when ductile materials are used. This is the basis for the **formula for required wall thickness**.

## 2.2 Stresses in Spherical Pressure Vessels

For a thin-walled spherical vessel, by symmetry, the hoop and longitudinal stresses are **equal**:

$$\sigma_{sphere} = \frac{p \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why spherical vessels are preferred for high pressure:** A sphere has the **minimum surface area for a given volume**, which means **less material** is needed to contain a given pressure. The hoop stress in a sphere is exactly half that of a cylinder of the same diameter and pressure — making spheres inherently safer and more efficient.

## 2.3 Thick-Walled Cylinders — Lamé's Equations

When the wall thickness is significant ($D/t < 20$), the assumption of uniform stress distribution across the wall thickness is no longer valid. The **radial stress $\sigma_r$** becomes significant and varies through the wall thickness.

**Lamé's equations** (developed in 1852) give the radial and hoop stresses at any radius $r$ within the wall of a thick cylinder:

$$\sigma_r = \frac{p_i \cdot a^2 - p_o \cdot b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \frac{a^2 \cdot b^2 \cdot (p_o - p_i)}{r^2 \cdot (b^2 - a^2)}$$

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{p_i \cdot a^2 - p_o \cdot b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{a^2 \cdot b^2 \cdot (p_o - p_i)}{r^2 \cdot (b^2 - a^2)}$$

Where:
- $p_i$ = internal pressure
- $p_o$ = external pressure (typically 0 for internal pressure vessels, or atmospheric for vacuum vessels)
- $a$ = inner radius
- $b$ = outer radius
- $r$ = radius at the point of interest ($a \leq r \leq b$)

**Boundary conditions:**
- At $r = a$ (inner surface): $\sigma_r = -p_i$
- At $r = b$ (outer surface): $\sigma_r = -p_o$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for KTU Board Exams.** The most common questions ask for required thickness, stress analysis, or safety factor calculations.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use in KTU Exams |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p D}{2 t}$ | Hoop stress (thin cylinder) | Stress analysis questions |
| 2 | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p D}{4 t}$ | Longitudinal stress (thin cylinder) | Stress analysis questions |
| 3 | $\sigma_{sphere} = \dfrac{p D}{4 t}$ | Stress in spherical vessel | Comparing cylinder vs sphere |
| 4 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{p D}{8 t}$ | Maximum shear stress (thin cylinder) | Failure theory questions |
| 5 | $t = \dfrac{p D}{2 S E - 1.2 p} + C_A$ | Required thickness (ASME, circumferential) | Design thickness calculation |
| 6 | $t = \dfrac{p D}{4 S E - 0.8 p} + C_A$ | Required thickness (ASME, longitudinal) | Design thickness calculation |
| 7 | $\sigma_r, \sigma_\theta$ (Lamé) | Thick cylinder stresses | Thick-walled vessel problems |
| 8 | $\sigma_v = \sqrt{\sigma_h^2 + \sigma_l^2 - \sigma_h \sigma_l}$ | Von Mises equivalent stress | Combined stress analysis |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{working}}$ | Factor of safety (yield-based) | Safety verification |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{\sigma_{working}}$ | Factor of safety (ultimate-based) | Safety verification |

**Notation key:**
- $p$ = internal design pressure
- $D$ = internal diameter
- $t$ = wall thickness
- $S$ = allowable stress (from code, depends on material and temperature)
- $E$ = joint efficiency (weld quality factor, typically 0.70 to 1.00)
- $C_A$ = corrosion allowance
- $\eta$ (or $FoS$) = Factor of Safety

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these equations matter in industry:**
> 1. **Boilers in Power Plants** — Cylindrical shells with hemispherical heads operate at 10–25 MPa and 540°C. Hoop stress is the critical design driver.
> 2. **LPG Storage Tanks** — Spherical vessels at 1.6 MPa store propane/butane. The spherical shape minimizes material cost and weld length.
> 3. **Nuclear Reactor Pressure Vessels (RPV)** — Massive thick-walled cylinders (300+ mm wall) at 15–17 MPa. Lamé's equations are essential here because of high $D/t$ ratios.
> 4. **Compressed Air Receivers** — Standard thin-walled cylinders in every industrial facility. Failure of these is a common industrial accident.
> 5. **Submarines and Hyperbaric Chambers** — External pressure vessels. Same formulas apply with $p$ interpreted as the pressure differential.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Hoop Stress (Circumferential Stress)

### Statement
Derive the expression for the circumferential (hoop) stress in a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel of internal diameter $D$, wall thickness $t$, and internal pressure $p$.

### Given
- Internal pressure: $p$
- Internal diameter: $D$
- Wall thickness: $t$ (with $t \ll D$, i.e., thin-walled)
- Length of vessel: $L$

### Assumptions
1. The wall is thin ($D/t \geq 20$), so the stress is assumed uniform across the wall thickness.
2. The material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law.
3. The pressure is static and uniformly distributed.
4. End effects are neglected (we examine a section far from the heads).

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Imagine the cylindrical vessel is cut by a **longitudinal (axial) plane**, exposing a half-section of length $L$.

**Step 2:** Identify the forces acting on this half-section:
- The internal pressure pushes outward on the **projected (diametrical) area** of the half-cylinder.
- The two wall sections (on either side of the cut) resist this force through tensile stress.

**Step 3:** Calculate the projected area of the internal pressure:

$$A_{projected} = D \cdot L$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the resisting cross-sectional area of the two cut wall sections:

$$A_{wall} = 2 \cdot t \cdot L$$

**Step 5:** Apply **force equilibrium** in the horizontal direction. The net force on the fluid inside the half-cylinder must be balanced by the tensile force in the walls:

$$p \cdot A_{projected} = \sigma_h \cdot A_{wall}$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$p \cdot (D \cdot L) = \sigma_h \cdot (2 \cdot t \cdot L)$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $L$ from both sides:

$$p \cdot D = \sigma_h \cdot 2 \cdot t$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $\sigma_h$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}}$$

This is the **hoop stress** — the principal stress acting circumferentially in the cylindrical shell.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Longitudinal Stress (Axial Stress)

### Statement
Derive the expression for the longitudinal (axial) stress in a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel of internal diameter $D$, wall thickness $t$, and internal pressure $p$.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Imagine the vessel is cut by a **transverse (radial) plane**, separating one end cap from the cylindrical body.

**Step 2:** The internal pressure acts on the **end cap area** (a circle of diameter $D$):

$$A_{cap} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 3:** The total force exerted by the internal pressure on the end cap:

$$F_{pressure} = p \cdot A_{cap} = p \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 4:** This force is resisted by the cylindrical wall acting as a **circumferential ring** of cross-section:

$$A_{ring} = \pi D \cdot t$$

**Step 5:** Apply **force equilibrium** in the axial direction:

$$F_{pressure} = \sigma_l \cdot A_{ring}$$

Substituting:

$$p \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \sigma_l \cdot \pi D \cdot t$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $\pi D$ from both sides:

$$p \cdot \frac{D}{4} = \sigma_l \cdot t$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $\sigma_l$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_l = \frac{p \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Lamé's Equations for Thick Cylinders

### Statement
Derive the radial and hoop stress distributions in a thick-walled cylindrical pressure vessel subjected to internal pressure $p_i$ and external pressure $p_o$.

### Assumptions
1. Plane strain conditions (long cylinder, end effects neglected).
2. Axisymmetric loading and geometry.
3. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic.
4. The cylinder is composed of two coaxial cylinders, but we treat the wall as a continuum.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Set up cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$. By symmetry, displacement $u$ depends only on $r$. The strains are:

$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{du}{dr}, \quad \varepsilon_\theta = \frac{u}{r}$$

**Step 2:** The equilibrium equation in the radial direction (with no body forces) is:

$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Use Hooke's law for plane strain (where $\sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_r + \sigma_\theta)$ for closed-end cylinder, but we use plane stress here for simplicity):

$$\sigma_r = \frac{E}{1 - \nu^2}(\varepsilon_r + \nu \varepsilon_\theta), \quad \sigma_\theta = \frac{E}{1 - \nu^2}(\varepsilon_\theta + \nu \varepsilon_r)$$

**Step 4:** After algebraic manipulation (substituting strains into stresses and then into the equilibrium equation), the general solution takes the form:

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}, \quad \sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the **boundary conditions**:
- At $r = a$ (inner radius): $\sigma_r = -p_i$
- At $r = b$ (outer radius): $\sigma_r = -p_o$

This gives the system:

$$-p_i = A - \frac{B}{a^2}, \quad -p_o = A - \frac{B}{b^2}$$

**Step 6:** Subtract the two equations to solve for $B$:

$$-p_i + p_o = -\frac{B}{a^2} + \frac{B}{b^2} = B \left( \frac{1}{b^2} - \frac{1}{a^2} \right)$$

$$B = \frac{(p_o - p_i) \cdot a^2 \cdot b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute back to find $A$:

$$A = \frac{p_i \cdot a^2 - p_o \cdot b^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

**Step 8:** Combine to obtain Lamé's equations:

$$\boxed{\sigma_r = \frac{p_i \cdot a^2 - p_o \cdot b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \frac{a^2 \cdot b^2 \cdot (p_o - p_i)}{r^2 \cdot (b^2 - a^2)}}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_\theta = \frac{p_i \cdot a^2 - p_o \cdot b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{a^2 \cdot b^2 \cdot (p_o - p_i)}{r^2 \cdot (b^2 - a^2)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of boundary conditions:**
> - At $r = a$: $\sigma_r = \dfrac{p_i a^2 - p_o b^2}{b^2-a^2} - \dfrac{b^2(p_o - p_i)}{b^2-a^2} = \dfrac{p_i(a^2 - b^2) - p_o(b^2 - b^2)}{b^2-a^2} = -p_i$ ✓
> - At $r = b$: $\sigma_r = \dfrac{p_i a^2 - p_o b^2}{b^2-a^2} - \dfrac{a^2(p_o - p_i)}{b^2-a^2} = \dfrac{p_i a^2 - p_o a^2 - p_o(b^2-a^2)}{b^2-a^2} = -p_o$ ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Theories Applied to Pressure Vessels

| Failure Theory | Formula | Applicability | Material Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Maximum Principal Stress (Rankine)** | $\sigma_1 \leq \sigma_{allow}$ | Conservative; brittle failure | Cast iron, ceramics |
| **Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca)** | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} \leq \dfrac{S_y}{2 \cdot FoS}$ | Ductile, simple to apply | Carbon steel, mild steel |
| **Distortion Energy (Von Mises)** | $\sigma_v = \sqrt{\sigma_h^2 + \sigma_l^2 - \sigma_h \sigma_l} \leq \dfrac{S_y}{FoS}$ | Most accurate for ductile | Ductile alloys |
| **Strain Energy (Haigh)** | More complex, rarely used in vessel design | Ductile with mean stress effects | Special applications |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 2024 Exams:** The most commonly expected failure criterion is the **maximum shear stress (Tresca)** theory, because the bi-axial stress state in a thin-walled cylinder is well-suited to it. The Von Mises criterion may be asked in design verification questions.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Cylinder Thickness Calculation

**Problem:** A cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of **1000 mm** and operates at an internal pressure of **3 MPa**. The material is carbon steel with an allowable stress of **138 MPa** and the joint efficiency is **0.85**. The corrosion allowance is **1.5 mm**. Calculate the required wall thickness (circumferential).

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the governing formula (ASME, circumferential stress governs):

$$t = \frac{p \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 1.2 \cdot p} + C_A$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$t = \frac{3 \cdot 1000}{2 \cdot 138 \cdot 0.85 - 1.2 \cdot 3} + 1.5$$

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator:

$$2 \cdot 138 \cdot 0.85 = 234.6$$
$$1.2 \cdot 3 = 3.6$$
$$\text{Denominator} = 234.6 - 3.6 = 231.0$$

**Step 4:** Compute the thickness:

$$t = \frac{3000}{231.0} + 1.5 = 12.99 + 1.5 = 14.49 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5:** Apply engineering rounding (round up to the next standard plate thickness — typically 16 mm):

$$t_{selected} = 16 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6:** Verify the actual working stress:

$$\sigma_{actual} = \frac{p \cdot D}{2 \cdot t_{selected}} = \frac{3 \cdot 1000}{2 \cdot 16} = 93.75 \text{ MPa}$$

This is well below the allowable stress of 138 MPa, confirming a **safe design** with adequate margin.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a pressure vessel as per IS 2825. List any four types of pressure vessels based on shape.**

**Model Answer:**

A pressure vessel, as defined in **IS 2825:1969**, is a closed container designed to hold fluids (gases, vapors, or liquids) at pressures substantially different from ambient pressure, typically with a design pressure exceeding **0.1 MPa** and a volume greater than **0.025 m³**.

**Four types based on shape:**
1. **Cylindrical** — most common; used for boilers, air receivers, storage tanks.
2. **Spherical** — most efficient; used for LPG, high-pressure gas storage.
3. **Conical** — used as transition sections or hoppers.
4. **Torispherical** — combination of spherical and cylindrical sections; used as end heads.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain why the hoop stress in a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel is twice the longitudinal stress. State the safety implication.**

**Model Answer:**

In a thin-walled cylinder of diameter $D$ and thickness $t$ under internal pressure $p$:
- **Hoop stress:** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ (resists the force on the diametrical projected area)
- **Longitudinal stress:** $\sigma_l = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$ (resists the force on the end cap)

Therefore, $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l$ because the **diametrical projected area is twice the end-cap area** for the same length.

**Safety implication:** The **longitudinal welds (which carry the hoop stress) are the most critical** and must be of higher quality than girth welds. ASME requires **100% radiographic examination of longitudinal seams**, while girth welds may receive **spot radiography**.

---

## **Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)** — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (Option 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expressions for circumferential and longitudinal stresses in a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of **750 mm** and a wall thickness of **12 mm**. The internal pressure is **4 MPa**. Calculate:
1. The hoop stress and longitudinal stress.
2. The maximum shear stress.
3. The factor of safety if the yield strength of the material is **240 MPa**.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Derivation:** *(Refer to Section 3.1 and 3.2 of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation.)*

**Final expressions:**
$$\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t}, \quad \sigma_l = \frac{pD}{4t}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D = 750$ mm
- $t = 12$ mm
- $p = 4$ MPa = 4 N/mm²
- $S_y = 240$ MPa

**Step 1: Hoop stress**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{4 \times 750}{2 \times 12} = \frac{3000}{24} = 125 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Longitudinal stress**

$$\sigma_l = \frac{4 \times 750}{4 \times 12} = \frac{3000}{48} = 62.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Maximum shear stress (Tresca)**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} = \frac{125 - 62.5}{2} = 31.25 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Factor of safety based on yield**

$$\eta = \frac{S_y}{\sigma_h} = \frac{240}{125} = 1.92$$

**Result:** Factor of Safety = **1.92**, which is **adequate** for static pressure applications (typically $\eta \geq 1.5$ to 2.0 is acceptable per ASME).

---

### **Question B (Option 2 / Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of Lamé's equations, explain the stress distribution in a thick-walled cylindrical pressure vessel. Why is the maximum hoop stress at the inner surface?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A thick-walled cylinder has an inner radius of **100 mm** and an outer radius of **150 mm**. It is subjected to an internal pressure of **50 MPa** with no external pressure. Calculate the radial and hoop stresses at:
1. The inner surface ($r = 100$ mm)
2. The outer surface ($r = 150$ mm)
3. The mid-wall ($r = 125$ mm)

Verify that the maximum hoop stress occurs at the inner surface.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Lamé's Equations Explanation:**

For a thick-walled cylinder, the stress varies through the wall thickness. Lamé's equations (derived from equilibrium, compatibility, and Hooke's law) give:

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}, \quad \sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2}$$

where $A$ and $B$ are constants determined from boundary conditions.

The **maximum hoop stress occurs at the inner surface** because:
- The term $\dfrac{B}{r^2}$ is **maximum when $r$ is minimum** (i.e., at the inner radius $r = a$).
- Adding $A$ to the maximum value of $\dfrac{B}{r^2}$ gives the maximum $\sigma_\theta$.
- Physically, the inner surface experiences the highest tangential stretching because it directly resists the internal pressure.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $a = 100$ mm, $b = 150$ mm
- $p_i = 50$ MPa, $p_o = 0$

**Lamé constants:**

$$A = \frac{p_i a^2 - p_o b^2}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{50 \times 100^2 - 0}{150^2 - 100^2} = \frac{500000}{12500} = 40 \text{ MPa}$$

$$B = \frac{a^2 b^2 (p_o - p_i)}{b^2 - a^2} = \frac{100^2 \times 150^2 \times (0 - 50)}{12500} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^8 \times (-50)}{12500} = -9 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa·mm}^2$$

**Case 1: At inner surface ($r = 100$ mm)**

$$\sigma_r = 40 - \frac{-9 \times 10^5}{100^2} = 40 - (-90) = 40 + 90 = -50 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark \text{ (equals } -p_i\text{)}$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 40 + \frac{-9 \times 10^5}{100^2} = 40 + (-90) = 40 - 90 = -50 \text{ MPa}$$

Wait — let me recompute. With $B = -9 \times 10^5$:

$$\sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2} = 40 + \frac{-9 \times 10^5}{10000} = 40 - 90 = -50 \text{ MPa}$$

Hmm, this should be a **large tensile (positive) value**. Let me recheck the sign of $B$.

Actually, the standard form of Lamé's equations is:
$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{p_i a^2 - p_o b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{a^2 b^2 (p_o - p_i)}{r^2 (b^2 - a^2)}$$

With $p_o - p_i = 0 - 50 = -50$:

$$\sigma_\theta \big|_{r=a} = 40 + \frac{100^2 \times 150^2 \times (-50)}{100^2 \times 12500} = 40 + \frac{150^2 \times (-50)}{12500} = 40 + \frac{-1125000}{12500} = 40 - 90 = -50$$

This still gives -50, which suggests a sign error. Let me carefully redo:

For internal pressure only ($p_o = 0$):

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{p_i a^2 b^2}{r^2 (b^2 - a^2)} = \frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2} \left( 1 + \frac{b^2}{r^2} \right)$$

At $r = a = 100$:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{50 \times 10000}{12500} \left( 1 + \frac{22500}{10000} \right) = 40 \times (1 + 2.25) = 40 \times 3.25 = 130 \text{ MPa}$$

At $r = b = 150$:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{50 \times 10000}{12500} \left( 1 + \frac{22500}{22500} \right) = 40 \times (1 + 1) = 40 \times 2 = 80 \text{ MPa}$$

At $r = 125$ (mid-wall):

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{50 \times 10000}{12500} \left( 1 + \frac{22500}{15625} \right) = 40 \times (1 + 1.44) = 40 \times 2.44 = 97.6 \text{ MPa}$$

For radial stress:

$$\sigma_r = \frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \frac{p_i a^2 b^2}{r^2 (b^2 - a^2)} = \frac{p_i a^2}{b^2 - a^2} \left( 1 - \frac{b^2}{r^2} \right)$$

At $r = a = 100$:

$$\sigma_r = 40 \times (1 - 2.25) = 40 \times (-1.25) = -50 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

At $r = b = 150$:

$$\sigma_r = 40 \times (1 - 1) = 0 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark \text{ (equals } -p_o = 0\text{)}$$

At $r = 125$:

$$\sigma_r = 40 \times (1 - 1.44) = 40 \times (-0.44) = -17.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Location | $\sigma_r$ (MPa) | $\sigma_\theta$ (MPa) |
|---|---|---|
| Inner surface ($r=100$) | $-50$ | $+130$ (max) |
| Mid-wall ($r=125$) | $-17.6$ | $+97.6$ |
| Outer surface ($r=150$) | $0$ | $+80$ |

**Verification:** The hoop stress is **maximum at the inner surface (130 MPa)** and decreases toward the outer surface — confirming the theoretical prediction. The design of thick-walled cylinders is governed by this **maximum hoop stress at the inner surface**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Mixing MPa and N/mm² carelessly. Remember: 1 MPa = 1 N/mm². Stress formulas require **consistent units**.
> 2. **Confusing $\sigma_h$ and $\sigma_l$:** Many students invert these. Remember: **hoop stress governs the wall thickness** (it's twice the longitudinal stress).
> 3. **Forgetting Corrosion Allowance:** In thickness design problems, the $C_A$ term is often missed — this is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Sign Convention in Lamé's Equations:** $\sigma_r$ is **compressive (negative)** at the inner surface, while $\sigma_\theta$ is **tensile (positive)**. Getting these signs wrong leads to wrong safety checks.
> 5. **Joint Efficiency Default:** If not given in the problem, **do not assume $E = 1$** unless the problem says "seamless" or "fully radiographed." Default to $E = 0.85$ for spot-checked welds.
> 6. **Thin vs. Thick Wall Check:** Always verify $D/t \geq 20$ before applying thin-wall formulas. If not, use Lamé's equations.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

**Core Definitions:**
- ☐ Pressure vessel = closed container for fluids at pressure > 0.1 MPa, volume > 0.025 m³ (per IS 2825).
- ☐ Thin-walled: $D/t \geq 20$; Thick-walled: $D/t < 20$.

**Critical Formulas (must memorize):**
- ☐ $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ (hoop/circumferential)
- ☐ $\sigma_l = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$ (longitudinal/axial)
- ☐ $\sigma_{sphere} = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$
- ☐ $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{pD}{8t}$ (Tresca for thin cylinder)
- ☐ $\sigma_v = \sqrt{\sigma_h^2 + \sigma_l^2 - \sigma_h \sigma_l}$ (Von Mises)
- ☐ Lamé's equations for thick cylinders
- ☐ $t = \dfrac{pD}{2SE - 1.2p} + C_A$ (ASME thickness, circumferential)

**Key Ratios & Relationships:**
- ☐ $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l$ (fundamental — always!)
- ☐ Sphere has half the hoop stress of cylinder → preferred for high pressure
- ☐ Maximum hoop stress in thick cylinder occurs at the **inner surface**

**Design for Safety Principles:**
- ☐ Factor of Safety $\eta \geq 1.5$ to 2.0 for static pressure (per ASME)
- ☐ Joint efficiency $E$: 1.0 (seamless), 0.95 (100% radiographed), 0.85 (spot-checked), 0.70 (no radiography)
- ☐ Corrosion allowance $C_A$: typically 1–3 mm
- ☐ Pressure relief devices (PSV/PRV) are **mandatory** safety features — never omit in design
- ☐ Hydrostatic test pressure = $1.3 \times$ design pressure (for strength verification)
- ☐ Codes: ASME Sec VIII, IS 2825, PD 5500, EN 13445

**Safety Critical Points:**
- ☐ Longitudinal welds are **more critical** than girth welds (carry 2× stress)
- ☐ Nozzle openings create **stress concentrations** — reinforcement pads may be required
- ☐ Brittle fracture is a major concern at low temperatures — use Charpy V-notch testing
- ☐ Creep becomes significant above $T \approx 345°C$ — short-term tests not sufficient
- ☐ Fatigue is critical for vessels under cyclic pressure (e.g., reciprocating compressor surge)

**Exam Strategy:**
- ☐ Always state **assumptions** (thin-walled, plane stress, axisymmetric) before solving.
- ☐ Show **free body diagrams** in derivations for full marks.
- ☐ Verify **units** at every step.
- ☐ For thick-walled problems, present results in a **summary table** for clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pressure Piping System** is a network of pipes, fittings, valves, flanges, gaskets, bolts, supports, and associated equipment designed to convey fluids (liquids, gases, vapours, slurries, or two-phase mixtures) at pressures and temperatures that differ appreciably from ambient conditions, and whose failure could result in hazardous consequences to personnel, property, or the environment. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (PESFT412 – Design for Safety), a pressure piping system is treated as a *primary pressure-containing component* whose integrity is governed by design codes such as **ASME B31.3 (Process Piping)**, **ASME B31.1 (Power Piping)**, and **IS 15490** (Indian Standard for unfired pressure vessels/piping).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PESFT412 – Module 4):**
> *A pressure piping system is an assembly of components (pipes, fittings, flanges, valves, expansion joints, supports) subject to internal or external pressure, designed, fabricated, and tested in accordance with a recognized code to ensure structural integrity under all anticipated operating, transient, and failure conditions without loss of containment.*

### 1.2 Constituents of a Pressure Piping System

| Component | Functional Role in Safety Design |
|-----------|----------------------------------|
| **Pipe (Straight Run)** | Primary pressure boundary; designed per hoop-stress formula |
| **Fittings (Elbows, Tees, Reducers)** | Direction/size change; introduce flexibility & stress intensification |
| **Flanges & Gaskets** | Bolted joints; leak-tight sealing with controlled bolt-up |
| **Valves** | Flow isolation & emergency shut-down (ESD) |
| **Pipe Supports / Hangers / Restraints** | Limit sag, vibration, thermal growth; protect against nozzle overload |
| **Expansion Joints / Loops** | Absorb thermal expansion; reduce stress at equipment connections |
| **Insulation & Tracing** | Process control & personnel protection (burn/scald) |
| **Pressure Relief Devices (PSV/PRD)** | Last line of defence against overpressure |
| **Instrumentation (PT, TT, FT, ESD valves)** | Detection of deviation & initiation of protective action |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bloodstream of a Plant"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine a chemical plant as a **human body**. The **pressure piping system** is its **circulatory system (arteries and veins)**. Just as blood is pumped under pressure by the heart through vessels that branch and reach every organ, process fluids are pumped by compressors/pumps through pipes that branch to every reactor, heat exchanger, and storage tank.
>
> - A **ruptured artery** (pipe burst) is fatal — equivalent to a **catastrophic loss of containment** leading to fire, explosion, or toxic release.
> - **Cholesterol deposits** in arteries (corrosion, erosion) weaken them slowly — analogous to **wall-thickness loss** that must be detected by inspection.
> - **Bypasses and stents** in medicine are like **relief valves and expansion loops** — engineered safety features that release pressure when needed.
> - **Stethoscopes and blood-pressure cuffs** are like **pressure transmitters and emergency shut-down valves** — instruments that detect abnormal conditions and trigger protective action.
>
> Just as a cardiologist designs vessels to withstand surges, a **Design-for-Safety engineer** designs the piping to withstand pressure surges, thermal cycling, vibration, and external damage.

### 1.4 Why Pressure Piping Demands Special Attention in Process Safety

Piping systems are uniquely hazardous because:

1. **High Length-to-Volume Ratio:** A single 6-inch line of length 200 m can release tonnes of hydrocarbon in minutes.
2. **Large Inventory Under Pressure:** Multiply cross-sectional area × length × pressure → enormous stored mechanical energy (≈ P × V).
3. **Multiple Failure Modes:** Rupture, leak, fire escalation, jet fire, vapour cloud explosion (VCE), boiling liquid expanding vapour explosion (BLEVE).
4. **Interface with Equipment:** Pipe failures can escalate to vessel failures and vice-versa (domino effect).

> [!WARNING]
> **Major Industry Lessons:**
> - **Flixborough Disaster (1974, UK):** Failure of a temporary "bypass" pipe (hose with bellows) caused a massive VCE killing 28 people. Root cause: lack of engineering design for the temporary piping.
> - **BP Texas City Refinery (2005):** A "safe" raffinate splitter tower over-pressurized; liquid came out of a vent stack → VCE → 15 fatalities.
> - **Chevron Richmond Refinery (2012):** Sulfuric acid leak from corroded pipe — a single pinhole caused a major community impact.
> - These incidents highlight why **Design for Safety of Piping** is a compulsory module in PESFT412.

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Allowable Stress (S):** 1/3 of ultimate tensile strength OR 2/3 of yield strength (whichever is lower) at design temperature — **units: MPa or psi**.
- **Design Pressure (P):** Maximum sustained operating pressure + incidental surge allowance (typically 10 % margin over MAWP) — **units: bar, MPa, or psig**.
- **Design Temperature:** Maximum sustained metal temperature under operating conditions — **units: °C or °F**.
- **Corrosion Allowance (CA):** Extra wall thickness reserved for expected metal loss over design life — **typical: 1.5–3.0 mm**.
- **Quality Factor (E):** Joint efficiency factor from Table A-1B of ASME B31.3 — **0.60 to 1.00**.
- **Standard Pipe Sizes (NPS):** Nominal Pipe Size, **1/8″ to 42″** in common process use.
- **Safety Factor (n):** Implicit in code; for ductile materials, S already incorporates a factor of ~3 on UTS.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Design Philosophy (The "5 Pillars" of Safe Piping)

A pressure piping system in **Design for Safety** is engineered through five hierarchical, non-negotiable steps:

### Pillar 1 — Process & Fluid Characterization
- Identify fluid (toxic, flammable, corrosive, two-phase).
- Determine **Design Pressure (P_design)**, **Design Temperature (T_design)**, and **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)**.
- Calculate **required relief capacity** and select pressure safety valves (PSV).

### Pillar 2 — Material Selection
- Carbon steel (A106 Gr.B, A53, A333), low-temp carbon steel, alloy steels (A335 P11/P22/P91), stainless (A312 TP304/316), CRA, non-metallics (HDPE, GRE, PTFE-lined).
- **Material selection matrix:** Strength vs. corrosion resistance vs. temperature vs. cost vs. weldability.

### Pillar 3 — Wall-Thickness & Pressure Design
- Compute **pressure design thickness (t)** of straight pipe per ASME B31.3, §304.1.
- Apply **corrosion allowance**, **mill tolerance (12.5 %)** and verify: `t_actual ≥ t + CA`.

### Pillar 4 — Flexibility, Support & Stress Analysis
- Compute **thermal expansion** of the system.
- Apply **Stress Intensification Factors (SIF, `i`)** for fittings and **Flexibility Factors (K)**.
- Verify **sustained, occasional, and expansion stress ranges** vs. allowable (per ASME B31.3 §302).

### Pillar 5 — Fabrication, Inspection, Testing & Integrity Assurance
- Welding procedure qualification (WPS/PQR/WPQ).
- NDE (RT, UT, MT, PT) % per fluid service.
- Hydrostatic test pressure = **1.5 × design pressure** (minimum).
- Document the **Piping Safety Audit File (PSAF)** — a KTU/CCPS concept.

## 2.2 The Two Governing Failure Criteria

Pressure piping can fail in **two primary modes**, each with its own safety basis:

| Failure Mode | Governing Equation | Code Basis | Why |
|--------------|-------------------|------------|-----|
| **Hoop Burst (Membrane)** | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 1.2 \, P}$ | ASME B31.3 §304.1.2 (straight pipe under internal pressure) | Prevents gross rupture by limiting membrane stress to allowable (S·E). |
| **Collapse / Vacuum / External Pressure** | $t = D \left[ \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{P_o \cdot D}{S \cdot E} \right) \right]^{0.5}$ (estimate) | ASME B31.3 §304.1.3 | Prevents buckling under vacuum or external pressure. |
| **Thermal Expansion Fatigue** | $S_{E} = S_{A} = f(1.25\,S_c + 0.25\,S_h)$ | ASME B31.3 §302.3.5 | Prevents low-cycle fatigue from thermal cycling. |
| **Sustained (Weight + Pressure)** | $S_L = S_h \le S_{hot}$ | ASME B31.3 §302.3.2 | Prevents creep / sag in hot lines. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is THE high-yield formula bank for PESFT412 Module 4 examinations. Memorize and re-derive these — they appear almost every year.**

| # | Formula (Latex) | Description / Use | Units |
|---|-----------------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 1.2 \, P}$ | Pressure design thickness of **straight pipe** (ASME B31.3) | mm |
| 2 | $P = \dfrac{2 \cdot S \cdot E \cdot t}{D - 1.2 \, t}$ | Internal pressure capacity (rearranged) | bar / MPa |
| 3 | $t_{nom} = t + CA$ | Required nominal thickness (incl. corrosion allowance) | mm |
| 4 | $t_{actual} = t_{nom} - \tfrac{1}{2} \times \text{mill tol}$ | Effective thickness after mill tolerance (12.5 % of t) | mm |
| 5 | $P_{test} = 1.5 \cdot P_{design} \cdot \dfrac{S_{test}}{S_{design}}$ | Hydrostatic test pressure | bar |
| 6 | $i$ (SIF) — Elbow 90° long radius | $i = \dfrac{0.9}{h^{2/3}}$ for in-plane, $i = \dfrac{0.75}{h^{2/3}}$ out-of-plane (Markl) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $K$ (Flexibility Factor, long-radius 90° elbow) | $K = \dfrac{1.65}{h}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal expansion (free) of pipe | m |
| 9 | $S_E = i \cdot M / Z$ (expansion stress range) | Bending stress from thermal expansion | MPa |
| 10 | $S_{allowable\,exp} = S_A = f(1.25\,S_c + 0.25\,S_h)$ | Allowable expansion stress range | MPa |
| 11 | $S_h = (i \cdot M_i)/Z$ ; $S_c = (M_c)/Z$ | Sustained & occasional stress (sustained = weight + pressure) | MPa |
| 12 | $S_L = S_h \le S_{hot} = \min(\tfrac{2}{3}S_y,\, \tfrac{1}{3}S_u)$ at T_design | Sustained stress check | MPa |
| 13 | Reinforcement of opening: $A_{req} = d \cdot t$ | Required area of reinforcement (ASME B31.3 §304.5) | mm² |
| 14 | Branch connection: $d_b = \min(2 \cdot t_b,\, 2 \cdot t_p, \, d + 2 \cdot t_n)$ | Branch effective diameter | mm |
| 15 | $P_{allow} = \dfrac{2 \cdot S \cdot E \cdot t}{D - 1.2\,t}$ | MAWP of the as-built pipe | MPa |
| 16 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Thrust force on a pipe end / bend (unrestrained) | N |
| 17 | $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{t}$ | Hoop stress (thin-wall) | MPa |
| 18 | $\sigma_{axial} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{2 t}$ | Longitudinal stress (closed ends) | MPa |
| 19 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2 E_{mod}}{(1 - \nu^2)} \left( \dfrac{t}{D} \right)^3$ (external) | Buckling pressure (vacuum) — Bresse / Bryan | MPa |
| 20 | $E_{mod}$ for steel ≈ **200 GPa (2 × 10⁵ MPa)** | Modulus of elasticity | MPa |

> **Where (in formulas):**
> - `P` = internal design pressure (MPa) ; `D` = outside diameter (mm) ; `t` = pressure design thickness (mm)
> - `S` = allowable stress at design temperature (MPa) ; `E` = quality factor (0.6 to 1.0)
> - `CA` = corrosion allowance (mm) ; `α` = linear coefficient of thermal expansion (1/°C)
> - `M` = bending moment (N·mm) ; `Z` = section modulus (mm³) ; `h` = flexibility characteristic = `t·R / r²`
> - `i` = stress intensification factor ; `K` = flexibility factor ; `R` = bend radius ; `r` = mean pipe radius
> - `d` = branch opening diameter ; `t_b` = branch thickness ; `t_p` = header thickness
> - `S_c, S_h, S_e` = cold (ambient) sustained, hot sustained, and expansion stresses (MPa)
> - `S_u` = ultimate tensile strength ; `S_y` = yield strength (both at T_design)
> - `ν` = Poisson's ratio (steel = 0.3) ; `E_mod` = modulus of elasticity

## 2.4 Engineering Utility Across Industries

| Industry | Code (typical) | Key Piping Concern |
|----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Oil & Gas (Onshore) | ASME B31.3 / B31.4 | Hydrocarbon fire, two-phase |
| Offshore Platforms | ASME B31.3 + API 570 | Wave, vibration, marine corrosion |
| Refineries | ASME B31.3 | High-T hot lines, naphthenic corrosion |
| Petrochemical | ASME B31.3 | Toxic & reactive services |
| Power (Thermal) | ASME B31.1 | High-T creep, steam-line flexibility |
| Nuclear | ASME B31.1 / B&PV III | Seismic, quality factor E = 1.0, full RT |
| Cryogenic (LNG) | ASME B31.3 + EN 13480 | Low-temp embrittlement, austenitic SS |
| Water (industrial) | ASME B31.4 / AWWA | Surge, water hammer |
| Compressed Air / N₂ | ASME B31.3 | Moisture, oxygen hazard |
| Pharmaceutical | ASME B31.3 + ASME BPE | Sanitary, electropolish, low residuals |

## 2.5 Decision Logic: When to Use B31.3 vs B31.1

> **ASME B31.3 (Process Piping):** — Used for fluids in the **process industries** (refining, chemical, pharmaceutical). Most common code in chemical engineering practice.
> **ASME B31.1 (Power Piping):** — Used for **boiler external piping**, power stations, and high-pressure steam.
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU):** If the question mentions *"refinery," "chemical plant," "hydrocarbon," "heat exchanger," "reactor feed/discharge,"* → use **B31.3**. If it mentions *"boiler," "turbine," "main steam," "feedwater,"* → use **B31.1**.
> Default in PESFT412 = **B31.3**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hoop Stress in Thin-Walled Pipe (Membrane Theory)

Consider a straight cylindrical pipe of inside radius $r_i$ and wall thickness $t$, with $r_i / t > 10$ (thin-wall assumption). Cut the pipe longitudinally along a length $L$ and isolate a free body of width $2\theta$.

**Step 1 — Free-Body Setup**

The cut surface exposes the **internal pressure $P$** acting on the projected area $2 \cdot r_m \cdot L \cdot \sin\theta$, where $r_m = r_i + t/2$ is the mean radius.

**Step 2 — Equilibrium of Hoop Force**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum F_{\text{hoop}} &= 0 \\[4pt]
2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L \cdot \sin\theta &= P \cdot (2 \cdot r_m \cdot L) \cdot \sin\theta \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad \sigma_h &= \frac{P \cdot r_m}{t} = \frac{P \cdot D}{2t} \quad \text{(using } D \approx 2 r_m \text{ for thin wall)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Longitudinal Stress (Closed-End)**

For a pipe with closed ends, the same pressure on the end cap produces an axial tensile force $F = P \cdot \pi r_i^2$. Dividing by the wall cross-section $\pi (r_o^2 - r_i^2) \approx 2 \pi r_m t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_L &= \frac{P \cdot r_i^2}{2 r_m t} \;\;\approx\;\; \frac{P \cdot r_m}{2 t} = \frac{\sigma_h}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Code Allowable (Code-Based Design Thickness)**

Setting the hoop stress to allowable $S \cdot E$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P \cdot D}{2 t} &= S \cdot E \\[4pt]
P \cdot D &= 2 t \cdot S \cdot E \\[4pt]
t &= \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot E}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Refinement to Account for Secondary Stress Concentration in the Wall**

The ASME B31.3 committee introduced a numerical factor 1.2 (or 0.4 in imperial form) in the denominator to correct the linearization. This converts the simple $2SE$ in the denominator to a more conservative $(2SE - 1.2P)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{allow} \cdot t &\rightarrow (S \cdot E - 0.6 P) \cdot 2 t \quad \text{(approx.)} \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad t &= \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 1.2 P} \quad \text{(final ASME B31.3 formula)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "1.2 P" in the denominator?**
> It is a **second-order correction** that recognizes hoop stress is mildly amplified by the local wall thinning, especially for thick walls. Including it makes the design margin uniform across both thin and thick pipes.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stress Intensification Factor (Markl's Method)

When a pipe carries a bending moment $M$, the stress at a fitting (elbow, tee, miter bend) is amplified. Markl in 1947–1955 experimentally showed that the **amplification ratio `i`** depends on a *flexibility characteristic*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{t \cdot R}{r^2} = 4 \cdot \frac{t \cdot R}{D^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $R$ = bend radius, $r$ = mean pipe radius, $t$ = wall thickness, $D$ = outside diameter.

Markl's empirical curve fit gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{\text{in-plane}} &= \frac{0.9}{h^{2/3}} \\[4pt]
i_{\text{out-of-plane}} &= \frac{0.75}{h^{2/3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Flexibility factor (K)** (how much easier a fitting deflects than a straight pipe):

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{\text{in-plane}} &= \frac{1.65}{h} \\[4pt]
K_{\text{out-of-plane}} &= \frac{1.5}{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stress at the fitting under bending moment $M$ and section modulus $Z$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= i \cdot \frac{M}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Design for Safety:**
> At an elbow, the *real* stress can be **2–4× higher** than in a straight pipe of the same diameter and thickness. Ignoring the SIF is one of the most common causes of pipe-cracking failures in service.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Expansion Stress Range (B31.3 §302.3.5)

The expansion stress range is the **difference between the maximum and minimum moments** experienced by the pipe as it cycles between ambient (cold) and operating (hot) conditions. Code allows a higher stress than sustained because it occurs only a limited number of times (low-cycle fatigue basis, not creep).

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_E &= \frac{i \cdot M}{Z} \quad \text{(expansion stress at fitting)} \\[4pt]
S_{A,allow} &= f(1.25\,S_c + 0.25\,S_h)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pass criterion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_E \le S_{A,allow}
\end{aligned}
$$

For carbon steel with $S_c$ and $S_h$ close to $S$ at ambient and hot temperatures, the allowable is typically $\approx 1.25 S_c + 0.25 S_h$. For example, if $S_c = 138$ MPa (A106 Gr.B at 38 °C) and $S_h = 124$ MPa (at 200 °C):

$$
S_{A,allow} = 1.25 (138) + 0.25 (124) = 172.5 + 31 = 203.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Reinforcement of Branch Connection (Area Replacement Method, B31.3 §304.5)

When a hole is cut in a header pipe to receive a branch, the load-carrying material is **weakened**. The code requires the **area removed** (at the opening) to be **replaced** by **excess material** in (a) the header, (b) the branch, and (c) any reinforcing pad or saddle.

**Area removed (required reinforcement):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= d \cdot t \quad \text{(where } d = \text{effective branch diameter, } t = \text{header thickness)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Areas available (within reinforcement zone, height $L_r = 2.5 \cdot t$ from the weld):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_2 &= (2 \cdot L_r - d) \cdot (t - t_{min}) \quad \text{(excess in header)} \\[4pt]
A_3 &= 2 \cdot L_r \cdot (t_b - t_{b,min}) \quad \text{(excess in branch)} \\[4pt]
A_4 &= \text{area of any reinforcing pad} \\[4pt]
A_5 &= \text{area of weld metal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pass criterion (design for safety):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_2 + A_3 + A_4 + A_5 \ge A_1
\end{aligned}
$$

If the sum is less than $A_1$, **either thicken the header, increase branch wall, or weld a reinforcing pad (saddle) around the branch**.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Wall Thickness for External Pressure (Vacuum / Collapse)

For long, thin cylinders under external pressure $P_o$, ASME B31.3 uses a chart-based method (geometric factor `B`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &= \frac{4 \cdot B}{3 \cdot (D_o / t)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $B$ is obtained from ASME BPVC §II-D Chart 3-1 (or a similar formula for the elastic/plastic region). The required thickness is found by iteration:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:}&\;\; \text{Guess } t = t_{\text{internal}}/2 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2:}&\;\; L / D_o,\; D_o / t \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{find } B \text{ from chart} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3:}&\;\; P_a = \frac{4B}{3(D_o/t)} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4:}&\;\; \text{If } P_a \ge P_o \Rightarrow \text{OK, else increase } t
\end{aligned}
$$

For elastic buckling (long, thin pipe), the simplified Bryan formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cr} &= \frac{2 E_{mod}}{1 - \nu^2} \left( \frac{t}{D} \right)^3
\end{aligned}
$$

For steel ($E_{mod} = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cr} \approx 226 \cdot GPa \cdot \left( \frac{t}{D} \right)^3
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Standard Question Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example 3.6.1 — Wall Thickness Calculation**
> A 6-inch Schedule-40 carbon steel pipe (A106 Gr.B) conveys methane at 38 bar and 65 °C. Header outside diameter $D = 168.28$ mm, nominal wall $t_n = 7.11$ mm. Corrosion allowance = 1.5 mm. Quality factor $E = 1.0$ (seamless). Allowable stress at 65 °C for A106 Gr.B = $S = 138$ MPa. Find the **required schedule** and **verify the pipe is safe**.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert design pressure to MPa:

$$
P = 38 \;\text{bar} \times 0.1 = 3.8 \text{ MPa}
$$

Step 2 — Calculate pressure design thickness:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 1.2 P} \\[4pt]
t &= \frac{3.8 \times 168.28}{2 \times 138 \times 1.0 - 1.2 \times 3.8} \\[4pt]
t &= \frac{639.46}{276 - 4.56} = \frac{639.46}{271.44} = 2.356 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Add corrosion allowance:

$$
t_{req} = 2.356 + 1.5 = 3.856 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 4 — Add mill tolerance (12.5 % of nominal):

$$
t_{nom} = 3.856 + 0.125 \times t_{nom} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0.875 \cdot t_{nom} = 3.856 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; t_{nom} = 4.41 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 5 — Compare with Sch-40 ($t_n = 7.11$ mm):

$$
t_n = 7.11 \text{ mm} \;\; \ge \;\; 4.41 \text{ mm} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\;\textbf{SCH-40 IS ADEQUATE}\;\;\checkmark
$$

Step 6 — MAWP of as-built pipe (verification):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{allow} &= \frac{2 \cdot S \cdot E \cdot (t_n - CA)}{D - 1.2 (t_n - CA)} \\[4pt]
P_{allow} &= \frac{2 \times 138 \times 1.0 \times (7.11 - 1.5)}{168.28 - 1.2(7.11 - 1.5)} \\[4pt]
P_{allow} &= \frac{1548.96}{168.28 - 6.732} = \frac{1548.96}{161.55} = 9.59 \text{ MPa} = 95.9 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Sch-40 has 152 % over-design margin (95.9 / 3.8 = 2.52×). **Piping is safe with comfortable margin.** This is typical — schedule is bumped up to next commercial size to allow for downrating due to CA, mill tolerance, and future inspection needs.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Design for Safety Decision Flowchart Logic

| Process Step | Input / Question | Output / Decision | KTU Code Reference |
|--------------|------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | What is the fluid? | Determine fluid service class (Normal, Category M, High Pressure) | B31.3 §A1 |
| 2 | What is the design pressure? | Set $P_d$ = MAWP + margin (or max sustained + surge) | B31.3 §302.2 |
| 3 | What is the design temperature? | Choose material & corresponding $S(T)$ | B31.3 §A1 |
| 4 | What is the pipe size, D? | Pick NPS from line sizing (ΔP, velocity) | — |
| 5 | Calculate $t$ | $t = PD/(2SE - 1.2P)$ | B31.3 §304.1.2 |
| 6 | Add CA, mill tolerance | $t_{nom}$ | — |
| 7 | Pick Schedule ≥ $t_{nom}$ | Confirm t_actual ≥ t_req | — |
| 8 | Check fittings, branches | Apply SIF `i`, K, and reinforcement check | B31.3 §304.5 |
| 9 | Thermal expansion | Compute $\Delta L$, $S_E$, compare to $S_{A,allow}$ | B31.3 §302.3.5 |
| 10 | Supports | Set spans, anchor locations | B31.3 §321 |
| 11 | Relief | Verify PSV sized for overpressure | API 521 / 520 |
| 12 | Test | Hydrotest 1.5 × P_design | B31.3 §345 |
| 13 | Inspect & NDE | Establish RBI plan (RT/UT % per service) | API 570 / 510 |
| 14 | Document | Generate **Piping Safety Audit File (PSAF)** | KTU PESFT412 §4 |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — Constituents & Code Basis of a Pressure Piping System

> A *block-level functional architecture* of the pressure piping system, mapping every component to its safety function. (Adapted to Mermaid since complex physical drawings are out of scope.)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Process Source<br>Reactor or Pump<br>Provides Pressure] --> B[Pipe R1<br>Straight Run<br>Hoop-Stress Design t=PD / 2SE-1.2P]
    B --> C[Fitting F1<br>Elbow 90 Degree<br>SIF i and K applied]
    C --> D[Valve V1<br>Isolation or ESD<br>SIF i applied]
    D --> E[Fitting F2<br>Tee or Branch<br>Reinforcement Check A2+A3+A4+A5 >= A1]
    E --> F[Pipe R2<br>Process Header<br>Sustained Stress Check Sh]
    F --> G[Equipment E1<br>Heat Exchanger or Vessel<br>Nozzle Load Check]
    G --> H[Fitting F3<br>Expansion Loop or Bellows<br>Thermal Expansion]
    H --> I[Valve V2<br>Pressure Safety Valve PSV<br>Last Line of Defence]
    I --> J[Flare Stack<br>Safe Disposal]
    F -.->|Branch| K[Pipe R3<br>Instrument or Drain]
    E --> L[Support S1<br>Spring Hanger or Guide]
    H --> M[Support S2<br>Anchor or Restraint]
```
